meta.description: Authorship per Sanskrit text: roles separated (karika/bhasya/vrtti/tika/compiler/redactor/translator), with attribution status. Triangulated from GRETIL/DSBC header, secondary literature, and web.

texts.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 9
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (composite, drawn from multiple seer families)
texts.primary_author: Multiple ṛṣi-composers (traditional, per Anukramaṇī); no single author

authors.name: various ṛṣi families (Bhṛgus, Kaśyapas, Aṅgirasas, and others; one seer ascribed per hymn by the Anukramaṇī)
anonymous redactor/compiler (Soma Pavamāna collection arranged for the Udgātṛ)

texts.basis: GRETIL header: only editorial credits (rev./conv. Detlef Eichler; digitized Van Nooten & Holland; ed. Aufrecht 1877); no ṛṣi lines in e-text. Web (Wikipedia/scholarly): unlike the family books (2-7), M9 is a thematic Soma-Pavamāna collection compiling hymns from many seer families across compositional periods, fused by a redactor; Anukramaṇī ascribes a seer to each hymn.
texts.note: Composite: many distinct traditional ṛṣi-composers (not one family), plus anonymous redactorial arrangement. Current 'composite, multiple seer families' is accurate; flagged conflated to record distinct roles.

texts.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 10
texts.primary_author: Multiple ṛṣi-composers (traditional, per Anukramaṇī); latest stratum; no single author

authors.name: various ṛṣi families and individual poets (latest stratum of the Ṛgveda; one seer ascribed per hymn by the Anukramaṇī)

texts.basis: GRETIL header: editorial credits only (Eichler; Van Nooten & Holland; Aufrecht 1877), no ṛṣi ascriptions in e-text. Web/scholarly: M10 (with M1) is the latest and longest book, compositions by many poets of various families, appended to the earlier collection.
texts.note: Composite of many distinct traditional authors plus redaction; latest stratum. Flagged conflated.

texts.title: Ṛgveda 9
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (composite, drawn from multiple seer families)
texts.primary_author: Multiple ṛṣi-composers (traditional, per Anukramaṇī); no single author

authors.name: various ṛṣi families (Bhṛgus, Kaśyapas, Aṅgirasas, and others; one seer ascribed per hymn by the Anukramaṇī)

texts.basis: Same text as rv_hn09u (Maṇḍala 9), alternate GRETIL transcription. Header gives editorial credits only; no ṛṣi lines. Web/scholarly: thematic Soma-Pavamāna collection compiling hymns from many seer families.
texts.note: Duplicate of Maṇḍala 9 (different e-text encoding). Composite of many distinct traditional ṛṣi-composers plus anonymous redaction; flagged conflated.

texts.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 7
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Vasiṣṭha and his family)
texts.primary_author: Vasiṣṭha and the Vāsiṣṭha family of ṛṣis (traditional ascription)

authors.name: Vasiṣṭha and the Vāsiṣṭha (Maitrāvaruṇi) family of ṛṣis

texts.basis: GRETIL header: editorial credits only (Eichler; Van Nooten & Holland; Aufrecht 1877). Web/scholarly: M7 is a 'pure' family book — every hymn ascribed to a composer of the Vasiṣṭha family per the Anukramaṇī.
texts.note: Pure family book ascribed traditionally to one ṛṣi lineage (Vasiṣṭhas), not a single individual; multiple poets within a single family, so not flagged as multi-author conflation. Traditional ascription, not securely historical.

texts.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 3
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Viśvāmitra and his family)
texts.primary_author: Viśvāmitra and the Vaiśvāmitra family of ṛṣis (traditional ascription)

authors.name: Viśvāmitra and the Vaiśvāmitra (Kuśika) family of ṛṣis

texts.basis: GRETIL header: editorial credits only (Eichler; Van Nooten & Holland; Aufrecht 1877). Web/scholarly: M3 is a 'pure' family book — every hymn ascribed to a composer of the Viśvāmitra family per the Anukramaṇī.
texts.note: Pure family book ascribed traditionally to the Viśvāmitra lineage (includes the Gāyatrī, RV 3.62.10). Family ascription, not one individual; traditional rather than secure.

texts.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 1
texts.primary_author: Multiple ṛṣi-composers (traditional, per Anukramaṇī); later stratum; no single author

authors.name: various individual poets and bardic families (notably the Kaṇvas) — one seer ascribed per hymn-group by the Anukramaṇī

texts.basis: GRETIL header: editorial credits only (Eichler; Van Nooten & Holland; Aufrecht 1877), no ṛṣi ascriptions in e-text. Web/scholarly: M1 (with M10) is among the latest and longest books; first part is six groups of hymns by individual poets (many Kāṇva), the older second part nine groups by poets of various bardic families.
texts.note: Composite of many distinct traditional authors across families plus redaction; later stratum. Flagged conflated (multiple distinct lineages, unlike the single-family books 2-7).

texts.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 6
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Bharadvaja and the Bārhaspatya Aṅgirasas)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally the Bharadvāja / Bārhaspatya Aṅgirasa ṛṣi family; multiple distinct seers)

authors.name: Bharadvāja Bārhaspatya (and the Bārhaspatya Aṅgirasa lineage)
Barend A. van Nooten & Gary B. Holland

texts.basis: GRETIL header: text based on Aufrecht (1877), digitized by van Nooten & Holland, revised by Eichler; no individual ṛṣi names in header. Web (Wikipedia/Anukramaṇī tradition): Maṇḍala 6 is the Bharadvāja family book, hymns ascribed to Bharadvāja and the Bārhaspatya Aṅgirasas — many distinct seers, not one author. Secondary-literature corpus (Buddhist-focused) had no relevant Vedic attribution scholarship.
texts.note: Family book: composed by a lineage of distinct ṛṣis (traditionally Bharadvāja & Bārhaspatya Aṅgirasas), no single historical author. Editorial chain (Aufrecht→van Nooten/Holland→Eichler) is redactional, not authorial.

texts.title: Ṛgveda 1
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally many distinct ṛṣis of multiple families)

authors.name: multiple ṛṣis of various families (e.g. Madhucchandas Vaiśvāmitra, Kaṇva, Aṅgiras, et al.)
Barend A. van Nooten & Gary B. Holland

texts.basis: GRETIL header: based on Aufrecht (1877), digitized by van Nooten & Holland, revised by Eichler; no ṛṣi names in header. Tradition (Anukramaṇī): Maṇḍala 1 is a heterogeneous late book whose 191 hymns are ascribed to many different ṛṣis across several families — by definition multi-author.
texts.note: Maṇḍala 1 conflates hymns by numerous distinct seers of different lineages; no single author. Editorial chain is redactional.

texts.title: Ṛgveda 4
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to the Gautama/Vāmadeva family of ṛṣis)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally Vāmadeva Gautama and the Gautama family; multiple distinct seers)

authors.name: Barend A. van Nooten & Gary B. Holland

texts.basis: GRETIL header: Aufrecht (1877) base, digitized by van Nooten & Holland, revised by Eichler; no ṛṣi names. Web confirms Maṇḍala 4 is the Vāmadeva family book, hymns ascribed to Vāmadeva Gautama and family — a lineage of distinct seers. NB this is a duplicate text-file of the same maṇḍala as rv_hn04u (different GRETIL filename).
texts.note: Family book by the Gautama/Vāmadeva ṛṣi lineage; multiple distinct seers, no single author. Same content as item rv_hn04u. Editorial chain redactional.

texts.title: Ṛgveda
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally hundreds of distinct ṛṣis across all ten maṇḍalas)

authors.name: hundreds of distinct ṛṣis of the seer-families (Gṛtsamada, Viśvāmitra, Vāmadeva, Atri, Bharadvāja, Vasiṣṭha, Kaṇva, Aṅgiras, et al.)
Barend A. van Nooten & Gary B. Holland

texts.basis: GRETIL header: complete Ṛgveda (maṇḍalas 1-10) based on Aufrecht (1877), digitized by van Nooten & Holland, revised by Eichler. The entire saṃhitā conflates the work of many seer-families over generations; traditional redaction ascribed to Vyāsa but composition is multi-author. Maximal conflation case.
texts.note: Whole-Ṛgveda file: conflates the entire corpus of ṛṣi-families across 10 maṇḍalas; no single author. Traditional redaction credited to Vyāsa (legendary). Editorial chain redactional.

texts.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 5
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to the Atri family of ṛṣis)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally the Atri ṛṣi family; multiple distinct seers)

authors.name: Barend A. van Nooten & Gary B. Holland

texts.basis: GRETIL header: Aufrecht (1877) base, digitized by van Nooten & Holland, revised by Eichler; no ṛṣi names. Web confirms Maṇḍala 5 is the Atri family book, hymns ascribed to the Atri (Ātreya) lineage — multiple distinct seers.
texts.note: Family book by the Atri ṛṣi lineage; multiple distinct seers, no single author. Editorial chain redactional.

texts.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 4
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Vāmadeva Gautama and family)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally Vāmadeva Gautama and the Gautama family; multiple distinct seers)

authors.name: Barend A. van Nooten & Gary B. Holland

texts.basis: GRETIL header: Aufrecht (1877) base, digitized by van Nooten & Holland, revised by Eichler; no ṛṣi names. Web confirms Maṇḍala 4 is the Vāmadeva family book, hymns ascribed to Vāmadeva Gautama and family — a lineage of distinct seers. Duplicate maṇḍala-4 file alongside rv_04_u.
texts.note: Family book by the Gautama/Vāmadeva ṛṣi lineage; multiple distinct seers, no single author. Same content as item rv_04_u. Editorial chain redactional.

texts.title: Ṛgveda 5
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to the Atri family of ṛṣis)
texts.primary_author: Multiple ṛṣis (anthology); traditionally the Atri family (Ātreyas)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: only editorial/institutional attribution (Aufrecht 1877; digitized by Ananthanarayana & Lehman), no ṛṣi names. Web: maṇḍala 5 is a 'family book' traditionally ascribed to the Atri clan; per-hymn poets recorded in Kātyāyana's Sarvānukramaṇī, but Oldenberg/Mayrhofer judge only the family-level tradition reliable.
texts.note: Maṇḍala 5 is an anthology of hymns by many distinct Ātreya poets over generations — one title conflates numerous authors; family ascription traditional, individual-hymn ascriptions (Anukramaṇī) of doubtful historicity.

texts.title: Ṛgveda 6
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Bharadvāja and the Bārhaspatya Aṅgirasas)
texts.primary_author: Multiple ṛṣis (anthology); traditionally the Bharadvāja family (Bārhaspatya Aṅgirasas)

authors.name: Bharadvāja family (Bārhaspatya Aṅgirasas) — many individual ṛṣis

texts.basis: GRETIL header: editorial attribution only (Aufrecht 1877; Ananthanarayana & Lehman; verified vs Grassmann), no ṛṣi names. Web: maṇḍala 6 is a family book traditionally ascribed to the Bharadvāja/Aṅgirasa clan; individual poets in the Anukramaṇī, but only family-level tradition deemed reliable (Oldenberg/Mayrhofer).
texts.note: Anthology of hymns by many distinct Bhāradvāja poets — one title conflates numerous authors; family ascription traditional.

texts.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 8
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (hymns largely attributed to the Kāṇva and Āṅgirasa families)
texts.primary_author: Multiple ṛṣis (mixed anthology); largely Kāṇva and Āṅgirasa families
texts.basis: GRETIL header: editorial attribution only (Aufrecht 1877; Ananthanarayana & Lehman), no ṛṣi names. Web: maṇḍala 8 is NOT a single-family 'family book' — it is a mixed/later collection dominated by Kāṇva and Āṅgirasa poets, with the Vālakhilya inserts; per-hymn poets in the Anukramaṇī.
texts.note: Mixed anthology (Kāṇvas + Āṅgirasas, incl. Vālakhilya hymns) — distinct from the pure family books 2-7; conflates many authors across families.

texts.title: Ṛgveda: Maṇḍala 2
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Gṛtsamada and his family)
texts.primary_author: Multiple ṛṣis (anthology); traditionally Gṛtsamada Śaunahotra and family

authors.name: Gṛtsamada (Śaunahotra) and family — several individual ṛṣis

texts.basis: GRETIL header: editorial attribution only (Aufrecht 1877; Ananthanarayana & Lehman), no ṛṣi names. Web: maṇḍala 2 is the shortest family book, traditionally ascribed to Gṛtsamada and his descendants; per-hymn poets in the Anukramaṇī, only family-level tradition reliable.
texts.note: Anthology of hymns by Gṛtsamada and family poets — one title conflates multiple authors; family ascription traditional. (Duplicate maṇḍala-2 entry, see rv_02_u.)

texts.title: Ṛgveda 3
texts.primary_author: Multiple ṛṣis (anthology); traditionally the Viśvāmitra family (Kauśikas)

authors.name: Viśvāmitra (Gāthina/Kauśika) and family — many individual ṛṣis

texts.basis: GRETIL header: editorial attribution only (Aufrecht 1877; Ananthanarayana & Lehman), no ṛṣi names. Web: maṇḍala 3 is a family book traditionally ascribed to Viśvāmitra and his clan (contains the Gāyatrī mantra 3.62.10); per-hymn poets in the Anukramaṇī, only family-level tradition reliable (Oldenberg/Mayrhofer).
texts.note: Anthology of hymns by Viśvāmitra-clan poets — one title conflates many authors; family ascription traditional.

texts.title: Ṛgveda 2
texts.primary_author: Multiple ṛṣis (anthology); traditionally Gṛtsamada Śaunahotra and family

authors.name: Gṛtsamada (Śaunahotra) and family — several individual ṛṣis

texts.basis: GRETIL header: editorial attribution only (Aufrecht 1877; Ananthanarayana & Lehman), no ṛṣi names. Web: maṇḍala 2, shortest family book, traditionally ascribed to Gṛtsamada and his descendants; per-hymn poets in the Anukramaṇī, only family-level tradition reliable.
texts.note: Anthology of hymns by Gṛtsamada and family poets — one title conflates multiple authors; family ascription traditional. (Same maṇḍala as rv_hn02u, alternate digitization.)

texts.title: Ṛgveda 7
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (hymns attributed to Vasiṣṭha and his family)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally the Vasiṣṭha family of ṛṣis)
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editors/inputters (Aufrecht 1877 ed.; Ananthanarayana & Lehman e-text), no authorial attribution. Secondary lit (History of civilisation in ancient India: hymns 'are ascribed to the Vasishthas'; Understanding Mantras: Vasiṣṭha family) supports the traditional family ascription for Maṇḍala VII.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_History of civilisation in ancient India_.txt; secondary:cleaned_Understanding Mantras_Harvey P. Alper, 1989.txt
texts.note: A 'family book' (gotra-maṇḍala): Maṇḍala VII traditionally composed by Vasiṣṭha and descendants. No single historical author; ascription is to a lineage of seers, not one person.

texts.title: Ṛgveda 10
texts.primary_author: anonymous (composite; many families/seers, latest stratum of the RV)
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editors/inputters (Aufrecht; Ananthanarayana & Lehman), no attribution. Secondary lit (Silk; Maharatnakuta study; Daya Krishna; Trevor Ling) repeatedly identifies Maṇḍala X as the latest, appended, composite stratum drawn from many sources/families.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Origins and early History of Maharatnakuta Tradition_Silk, 1994.txt; secondary:cleaned_Contrary Thinking_Daya Krishna et al., 2011.txt
texts.note: Latest layer of the Ṛgveda; not a family book. Many traditional seer-ascriptions in the Anukramaṇī but no unifying author; genuinely anonymous/composite.

texts.title: Sāmaveda-Saṃhitā
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Kauthuma śākhā; verses largely drawn from the Ṛgveda)
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives only e-text editor Anshuman Pandey (1998) and the Kauthuma śākhā; no authorial attribution and no named edition. Secondary lit (Dessein, Samyuktābhidharmahṛdaya: Sāmaveda verses 'taken from'/derived from the Ṛgveda) confirms it is a liturgical re-arrangement (saman melodies) of mostly Ṛgvedic ṛc, hence a compilation rather than original composition.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Samyuktaabhidharmahrdaya. Heart of Scholasticism. II_Bart Dessein, 1999.txt
texts.note: Kauthuma recension. Saṃhitā is a chant-book: the great majority of its verses are Ṛgvedic, set to sāman melodies; compiler(s) anonymous, no single seer.

texts.title: Atharvaveda-Saṃhitā
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic ṛṣis; ascribed to Atharvan and Aṅgiras)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Śaunaka recension; traditionally Atharvan and Aṅgiras)
texts.basis: GRETIL/TITUS header names only the transliteration (Orlandi, Pisa 1991; collated Roth & Whitney 1856) and inputters/redactors (Petr, Vavrousek; Gippert; Griffiths; Kubisch); no authorial attribution. Secondary lit (Trevor Ling on 'Atharvan hymns'; Understanding Mantras: fire-priests = atharvans) supports the traditional two-fold ascription (Atharvāṅgirasaḥ).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_History of religion East and West_Trevor Ling.txt; secondary:cleaned_Understanding Mantras_Harvey P. Alper, 1989.txt
texts.note: Śaunaka (Saunaka) recension, unaccented base. Name Atharvāṅgirasaḥ reflects ascription to two priestly lineages (Atharvan = auspicious/healing, Aṅgiras = hostile/sorcery hymns); no single historical author.

texts.title: Atharvaveda-Saṃhitā
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Śaunaka recension, accented; traditionally Atharvan and Aṅgiras)
texts.basis: Same e-text family as avs___u but ACCENTED (avs_acu). GRETIL/TITUS header names only Orlandi transliteration (Pisa 1991), Roth & Whitney 1856 collation, and inputters/redactors (Petr, Vavrousek, Gippert, Griffiths, Kubisch); no authorial attribution. Traditional ascription to Atharvan/Aṅgiras per secondary lit.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_History of religion East and West_Trevor Ling.txt
texts.note: Accented Śaunaka recension (duplicate of the AV Saṃhitā with Vedic accents). Same anonymous/traditional status as the unaccented item; ascribed to Atharvan and Aṅgiras lineages.

texts.title: Śukla-Yajurveda

authors.name: Yājñavalkya (legendary/traditional founder of the White YV school)

texts.basis: source_url is null; edition cited is Weber's Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā (Mādhyandina & Kāṇva, with Mahīdhara's comm., 1852) — Weber and Mahīdhara are editor/commentator, not the Saṃhitā's author. Secondary lit (Lindquist, In Search of a Sage) treats Yājñavalkya as the traditional founder of the White Yajurveda 'said to have received it from the Sun', and as a literary/'mythical' figure, i.e. not a documented historical author.
texts.source: tradition; secondary:cleaned_IN SEARCH OF A SAGE. YAJNAVALKYA AND ANCIENT INDIAN LITERARY MEMORY_Steven Edward Lindquist.txt
texts.note: White (Śukla) Yajurveda = Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā. Yājñavalkya is the legendary eponymous founder/redactor (received from Sūrya/Āditya), not an attestable author; the Saṃhitā of ritual formulae is itself anonymous. Mahīdhara (commentary) and Weber (editor) are separate roles, not authors of the text.

texts.title: Paippalāda-Saṃhitā
texts.basis: GRETIL header credits only modern editors/inputters (Dipak Bhattacharya ed.), no individual author. Gonda 1975 treats it as one of the two AV recensions (Paippalāda vs Śaunakīya), a school corpus with no named author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt | gretil-header
texts.note: Traditional ascription to the Paippalāda śākhā of the Atharvaveda; collective/anonymous Vedic transmission, not an individual.

texts.title: Maitrāyaṇī-Saṃhitā
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Maitrāyaṇīya śākhā of the Black Yajurveda)
texts.basis: Edition is von Schroeder (modern editor); a Saṃhitā of the Maitrāyaṇīya branch of the Black Yajurveda. School/collective text with no named author in tradition or scholarship.
texts.source: tradition | secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt
texts.note: Named after the Maitrāyaṇīya school (eponym Maitri/Mitra), not an attested author; anonymous Vedic Saṃhitā.

texts.title: Pañcaviṃśabrāhmaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header lists only data-enterers (Kümmel, Griffiths, Kobayashi), no author. Gonda 1975: the text is named after its promulgator Tāṇḍya/Tāṇḍin, 'of whose authorship there is no evidence in the text itself.' The edition's Sāyaṇa commentary is a separate later work, not in this e-text.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt | gretil-header
texts.note: = Tāṇḍya-Mahābrāhmaṇa. Eponym Tāṇḍya/Tāṇḍin is a legendary promulgator, not a demonstrable author. Sāyaṇa's commentary (in the print edition) is by a distinct 14th-c. author but is not part of this Brāhmaṇa e-text.

texts.title: Gopatha-Brāhmaṇa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Gopatha-Brāhmaṇa, sole Brāhmaṇa of the Atharvaveda)
texts.basis: Edition Mitra & Vidyābhūṣaṇa (modern editors). Secondary lit (White 2009; Padoux 1990; Gonda) characterizes it as a very late Atharvaveda Brāhmaṇa largely compiled from earlier passages; no named author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sinister Yogis_David Gordon White, 2009.txt | secondary:cleaned_Vac...Andre Padoux, 1990.txt
texts.note: Same work as the Gaastra-edition entry below (Bibliotheca Indica vs Leiden 1919). 'Gopatha' is the school/eponym, not an attested individual author; widely judged a late composite of borrowed passages.

texts.title: Gopathabrāhmaṇa
texts.basis: Gaastra 1919 edition (modern editor). Identical work to gopthbru (different edition). Traditional eponymous ascription to 'Gopatha' is not a real author; scholarship treats it as a late composite Brāhmaṇa.
texts.note: Duplicate title of the preceding item (Gaastra ed. 1919). 'Attributed to Gopatha' is a traditional eponym, not a demonstrable author; remains anonymous.

texts.title: Kauṣītaki-Brāhmaṇa [Śāṅkhāyana-Brāhmaṇa]
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (associated with the sage Kauṣītaki and his school Śāṅkhāyana)
texts.basis: GRETIL title page: 'Kausitaki-Brahmana (or Sankhayana-Brahmana)' — the two names denote the same Ṛgvedic Brāhmaṇa. Gonda 1975 likewise pairs 'Kausitaki (Śāṅkhāyana)' as one school's text. Edition by Sreekrishna Sarma (modern editor); no individual author.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt
texts.note: Kauṣītaki and Śāṅkhāyana are alternative names for the same Brāhmaṇa, not two distinct authors — eponymous sage/school, not a conflation of authors. Anonymous Vedic school text.

texts.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 13
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the school of Yājñavalkya)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (compiled); traditionally Yājñavalkya (kāṇḍa 13 belongs to the Yājñavalkya section, books 11-13)

authors.name: Sāyaṇa / Harisvāmin / Dvivedaganga (commentary extracts in the edition)

texts.basis: GRETIL header names only data-inputters (Ananthanarayana & Lehman), no author. Lindquist (In Search of a Sage) places ŚBM books 1-5 and 11-13 in the 'Yājñavalkya sections' vs books 6-10 under Śāṇḍilya; the text as a whole is anonymous/compiled by various editors. Web (Wikipedia/grokipedia) confirms traditional ascription of the Śukla-Yajurveda brāhmaṇa to Yājñavalkya and Eggeling's two-part structure. Weber edition carries extracts from three distinct commentators.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_IN SEARCH OF A SAGE. YAJNAVALKYA...Lindquist; gretil-header; web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shatapatha_Brahmana
texts.note: Kāṇḍa 13 = Yājñavalkya section (books 11-13). Underlying brāhmaṇa is anonymous, multiply-redacted; 'Yājñavalkya' is a traditional/legendary authority not a real single author. Conflation flagged: the title ŚB conflates the Yājñavalkya authority (1-5,11-13) and the Śāṇḍilya authority (6-10), plus the edition's three commentators (Sāyaṇa, Harisvāmin, Dvivedaganga).

texts.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 1
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (the work as a whole is attributed to Yājñavalkya; Kāṇḍas 1-5 closely associated with him)
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only data-inputters (Ananthanarayana, Lehman), no author claim. Gonda (Vedic Literature, ŚBM I-V more closely connected, Yājñavalkya quoted as chief authority; redaction of the whole associated with him) and Mylius (Kāṇḍas I-V have Yājñavalkya as teacher). Web (Eggeling-derived) confirms Yājñavalkya dominant in first five books. Edition (Weber) carries commentary extracts of three named scholars.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt; secondary:cleaned_Geschichte der altindischen Literatur_Klaus Mylius_2003 (1983).txt; gretil-header; web:en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shatapatha_Brahmana
texts.note: Kāṇḍa 1 falls in the Yājñavalkya layer (I-V). Base text anonymous school compilation, traditionally ascribed to Yājñavalkya. Edition conflates distinct commentators Sāyaṇa, Harisvāmin, Dvivedagaṅga.

texts.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa
texts.primary_author: Anonymous (Vājasaneyin school compilation; layers: Yājñavalkya, Kāṇḍas I-V; Śāṇḍilya, Kāṇḍas VI-X)
texts.basis: Gonda: ŚBM is not a unity; in I-V Yājñavalkya is the chief authority and the redaction of the whole is associated with him, while Books VI-X (Agnicayana) have Śāṇḍilya as chief authority, with differences in geography, style and mythology pointing to difference of authorship/redaction. Mylius: 'mehrere Werke in ein einziges großes Kompendium zusammengeflossen' — Kāṇḍas I-V teacher Yājñavalkya, VI-X (fire-altar) Śāṇḍilya. Web confirms Yājñavalkya in first five, Śāṇḍilya in 6-9, scarcely explicable except by difference of authorship.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt; secondary:cleaned_Geschichte der altindischen Literatur_Klaus Mylius_2003 (1983).txt; web:en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shatapatha_Brahmana
texts.note: Whole-work record: conflates the Yājñavalkya-school layer (I-V) and the Śāṇḍilya layer (VI-X); concludes with Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad in Kāṇḍa XIV. Plus three distinct commentators in the edition. Fundamentally anonymous compilation with traditional school ascriptions.

texts.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 4
texts.basis: Gonda and Mylius: Kāṇḍa 4 lies within the Yājñavalkya-associated layer (I-V), in which Yājñavalkya is the chief doctrinal authority. GRETIL header gives no author. Web (Eggeling tradition) confirms Yājñavalkya prominent in first five books.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt; secondary:cleaned_Geschichte der altindischen Literatur_Klaus Mylius_2003 (1983).txt; gretil-header
texts.note: Kāṇḍa 4 (Agniṣṭoma material) in the Yājñavalkya layer (I-V). Anonymous base text with traditional Yājñavalkya ascription; edition conflates three named commentators.

texts.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 10
texts.primary_author: Anonymous (Vājasaneyin school; Agnirahasya layer — tradition: Śāṇḍilya)
texts.basis: Gonda: Book X (Agnirahasya, the mystery of the fireplace) has Śāṇḍilya as the chief authority; the Śāṇḍilya books (VI-X) differ from the Yājñavalkya books in authority, geography and style. Mylius: VI-X (Feueraltarschichtung/Agnicayana) have Śāṇḍilya as teacher. Confirms current_author. GRETIL header no author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt; secondary:cleaned_Geschichte der altindischen Literatur_Klaus Mylius_2003 (1983).txt; gretil-header
texts.note: Kāṇḍa 10 = Agnirahasya; Śāṇḍilya is the chief authority (distinct from the Yājñavalkya layer). Anonymous base with traditional Śāṇḍilya ascription; edition conflates three named commentators. Current_author already correct.

texts.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 11
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the school of Yājñavalkya)
texts.primary_author: Anonymous (Vājasaneyin school; later/addenda books — tradition: Yājñavalkya)
texts.basis: Gonda: the later books (XI-XIV) were completed after the Yājñavalkya books; Yājñavalkya 'plays a prominent part in ŚBM. XI'. Mylius: Kāṇḍa XI gives résumés and treats the animal sacrifice, part of the addenda following the main two layers. GRETIL header no author. School-of-Yājñavalkya ascription confirmed.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt; secondary:cleaned_Geschichte der altindischen Literatur_Klaus Mylius_2003 (1983).txt; gretil-header
texts.note: Kāṇḍa 11 (résumés / paśubandha) — Yājñavalkya prominent again; an addenda book completed after the core layers. Anonymous base, traditional Yājñavalkya-school ascription; edition conflates three named commentators. Current_author already correct.

texts.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 14
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the school of Yājñavalkya)
texts.primary_author: Anonymous (Vājasaneyin school; concludes with Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad — Yājñavalkyakāṇḍa = BAU 3-4)
texts.basis: Mylius: Kāṇḍa XIV describes the Pravargya ceremony (adhyāyas 1-3) and its adhyāyas 4-9 in fact constitute the Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad. Gonda: BAU constitutes the concluding portion (14, 4ff.); Yājñavalkya is mentioned 108 times in the BAU and BAU 3-4 = the Yājñavalkyakāṇḍa, a continuation of his ŚB discussions. GRETIL header no author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Geschichte der altindischen Literatur_Klaus Mylius_2003 (1983).txt; secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt; gretil-header
texts.note: Kāṇḍa 14 is itself layered: Pravargya (adh. 1-3) + Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad (adh. 4-9), whose books 3-4 are the Yājñavalkyakāṇḍa. Anonymous base, strong traditional Yājñavalkya association; edition conflates three named commentators.

texts.title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 8
texts.basis: GRETIL header (input Ananthanarayana/Lehman) gives no author, no school, and crucially NO commentary text — it is mūla only, so Sāyaṇa/Harisvāmin/Dvivedaganga of the Weber edition are absent. Secondary lit treats the SB as an anonymous Brāhmaṇa of the White Yajurveda; Kāṇḍas 6-10 (the Agnirahasya/Śāṇḍilya-kāṇḍa) are traditionally 'proclaimed by' Śāṇḍilya as a teacher-authority, not authored by him.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 3 - Potter 1981.txt
texts.note: Anonymous śruti. Traditional ascription of Kāṇḍas 6-10 to Śāṇḍilya is a proclaimer/teacher attribution, not authorship. No commentator conflation in the e-text (mūla-only).

texts.title: The Khila Verses of the Ṛgveda
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Scheftelowitz 1906, input Tokunaga) names no author; notes individual hymns carry ṛṣi-ascriptions (Bharadvāja, Pṛṣadhra etc.) in their anukramaṇī. Secondary lit (Understanding Mantras / Alper; Ludvik) cites the Khilāni as the 'Apokryphen des Rgveda', a heterogeneous appendix collection, not a single author's work.
texts.note: Anonymous collection of apocryphal supplementary hymns. Individual hymns ascribed to various ṛṣis in the anukramaṇī, but that is a per-hymn liturgical convention, not unified authorship; not flagged as a single-title conflation of distinct authors.

texts.title: Śāṅkhāyana-Āraṇyaka
texts.primary_author: anonymous (śruti; Śāṅkhāyana/Kauṣītaki school of the Ṛgveda)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Bhim Dev 1980 + Keith adhyāyas 7-15; input Kümmel) names no author/sage in a colophon; text is associated with the Śāṅkhāyana (Kauṣītaki) school and internally names teachers like Māṇḍūkeya. The e-text is the Āraṇyaka itself, no commentary attached.
texts.note: Anonymous śruti Āraṇyaka of the Śāṅkhāyana/Kauṣītaki śākhā. School name is eponymous of a tradition, not an established individual author. No commentator conflation.

texts.title: Chāndogya-Upaniṣad
texts.basis: GRETIL header: mūla text extracted from a commented version, checked vs Limaye-Vadekar 1958 and TITUS; the commentary (Śaṅkara) is NOT included in this file. No single sage is named as author; text is dialogic (teacher-student exchanges). Secondary lit treats it as an anonymous Upaniṣad of the Sāmaveda (part of the Chāndogya-Brāhmaṇa tradition).
texts.note: Anonymous śruti Upaniṣad embedded in the Sāmaveda's Chāndogya-Brāhmaṇa. Mūla-only extraction, so no Śaṅkara-bhāṣya conflation in the e-text.

texts.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (closely associated with the sage Yājñavalkya)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Kāṇva recension, mūla extracted from the version with commentary 'ascribed to Śaṅkara' — but only the mūla is in this file; checked vs Limaye-Vadekar 1958/TITUS. Yājñavalkya appears as the principal SPEAKER in the dialogues, not as a redactional author. Secondary lit (multiple) consistently frames Yājñavalkya as the central teacher-figure of the text, not its composer.
texts.note: Anonymous śruti Upaniṣad forming part of the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa (White Yajurveda), Kāṇva recension. Traditional association with Yājñavalkya is a dialogue/teacher attribution, not authorship. Śaṅkara-bhāṣya not present (mūla-only); no conflation.

texts.title: Chāndogya-Upaniṣad
texts.basis: Same GRETIL file/source as SA_GV05_chup___u (duplicate work item, same source_url): mūla extracted from a commented version, checked vs Limaye-Vadekar 1958/TITUS, commentary not included. Anonymous Sāmaveda Upaniṣad.
texts.note: Duplicate of SA_GV05_chup___u (identical source_url). Anonymous śruti Upaniṣad; mūla-only, no Śaṅkara-bhāṣya conflation.

texts.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 5
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Kāṇva recension 'with the commentary ascribed to Saṃkara'; mūla is anonymous Vedic prose (traditionally linked to Yājñavalkya, a literary persona per Lindquist). Potter EIP v3 p.647: Śaṅkara's authorship of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka-bhāṣya is undisputed. The GRETIL file conflates an anonymous Vedic mūla with Śaṅkara's bhāṣya.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 3 - Potter 1981.txt; secondary:cleaned_IN SEARCH OF A SAGE. YAJNAVALKYA...Lindquist
texts.note: Two distinct strata: anonymous Vedic mūla + Śaṅkara's bhāṣya (genuine/undisputed). 'Yājñavalkya' is a traditional ascription/literary persona, not a documented author of this adhyāya.

texts.title: Atharvaprāyaścittāni
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Julius von Negelein; e-text by Arlo Griffiths & R. Grünendahl) gives no author/compiler. Colophon 'ity atharvavede vaitānasūtre prāyaścittaprakaraṇam samāptam' places it as an expiation section in the Atharvavedic ritual/Pariśiṣṭa tradition, an anonymous Vedic ancillary (sūtra/pariśiṣṭa) work.
texts.note: Anonymous Atharvavedic ritual/Pariśiṣṭa text; no named author or compiler. Negelein is the modern editor only.

texts.title: Chāndogya-Upaniṣad (Śaṅkarabhāṣya)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'with the commentary ascribed to Saṃkara'; Sanskrit colophon 'śrīmacchaṅkarabhagavatpādācāryaviracitaṃ bhāṣyam'. Potter EIP v3 p.647: 'No one seems anxious to question the person's authorship of the Bhāsyas on the Brhadaranyaka and Chandogya Upaniṣads' — i.e. genuine Śaṅkara, unlike many disputed works ascribed to him.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 3 - Potter 1981.txt
texts.note: This item is the bhāṣya work: Śaṅkara's commentary is secure/undisputed, but the title embeds the anonymous Vedic mūla too; flagged as conflated since it bundles mūla + bhāṣya.

texts.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 6
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Kāṇva recension 'with the commentary ascribed to Saṃkara'. Mūla anonymous Vedic prose (Yājñavalkya = traditional/legendary persona per Lindquist). Potter EIP v3 p.647: Śaṅkara's Bṛhadāraṇyaka-bhāṣya undisputed.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 3 - Potter 1981.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Vedic mūla + Śaṅkara's bhāṣya (genuine). Yājñavalkya is a traditional ascription, not a verifiable author.

texts.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad
texts.basis: GRETIL header (adhyāya 1 / full BĀU): 'Kanva recension with the commentary ascribed to Samkara'; ref system 'BrhUPBh = Samkara's Bhasya'. Mūla anonymous; Tuck notes scholars speak of 'the author(s) of the Brhadaranyaka Upanisad'. Potter EIP v3 p.647: Śaṅkara's bhāṣya undisputed; vārttika on it is by Sureśvara (separate, not in this file).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 3 - Potter 1981.txt; secondary:cleaned_Isogesis...Tuck 1987.txt
texts.note: Compilatory anonymous Vedic mūla (multiple hands, per Tuck) + Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. Yājñavalkya = traditional persona. Sureśvara's vārttika is on Śaṅkara's bhāṣya but is a distinct work not bundled here.

texts.title: Chāndogya-Upaniṣad
texts.primary_author: anonymous Vedic seers (mūla); Śaṅkara (bhāṣya, in source file)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (same file as the Śaṅkarabhāṣya item): mūla is one of the oldest, anonymous Sāmaveda Upaniṣads (Potter EIP v3 p.9672). The file bundles the anonymous mūla with the commentary 'ascribed to Saṃkara' (colophon 'śrīmacchaṅkarabhagavatpādācāryaviracitaṃ bhāṣyam'), whose Śaṅkara authorship is undisputed (Potter p.647).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 3 - Potter 1981.txt
texts.note: Mūla anonymous (Vedic seers), but the cited source file conflates it with Śaṅkara's genuine bhāṣya; flagged conflated. Mūla attribution itself unchanged (anonymous).

texts.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Kanva recension with the commentary ascribed to Samkara' — file contains both mula and Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. The mula is an anonymous Vedic composition of the White-Yajurveda (Vājasaneyin/Kāṇva) school; tradition links the Yājñavalkya-kāṇḍa (incl. adhyāya 2) to the sage Yājñavalkya as eponymous/internal speaker, not as a historical author (Olivelle 1998). Śaṅkara's BAU-bhāṣya is among his securely genuine works (cited as such throughout secondary lit).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Early Upanisads. Annotated Text and Translation_Patrick Olivelle, 1998.txt
texts.note: Single GRETIL file conflates anonymous Vedic mula with Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. Yājñavalkya is a traditional/eponymous figure (dialogue speaker), not the historical composer.

texts.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 4
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Kāṇva recension with commentary ascribed to Śaṅkara; file contains mula + Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. Mula is anonymous Vedic composition (White Yajurveda); adhyāya 4 belongs to the Yājñavalkya-kāṇḍa where Yājñavalkya is the internal speaker (Olivelle 1998), an eponymous/traditional association rather than historical authorship. Śaṅkara's BAU-bhāṣya is securely genuine.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Early Upanisads. Annotated Text and Translation_Patrick Olivelle, 1998.txt
texts.note: Conflation of anonymous mula and Śaṅkara bhāṣya; Yājñavalkya is traditional dialogue speaker, not author.

texts.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 3
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Kāṇva recension with Śaṅkara's commentary; file = mula + bhāṣya. Adhyāya 3 is the famous Yājñavalkya dialogue chapter (the brahmodya at Janaka's court); Yājñavalkya is the internal protagonist, an eponymous/traditional ascription, not the historical composer (Olivelle 1998). Mula anonymous; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya genuine.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Early Upanisads. Annotated Text and Translation_Patrick Olivelle, 1998.txt
texts.note: Conflation: anonymous Vedic mula + Śaṅkara bhāṣya. Yājñavalkya = traditional speaker of adhyāya 3 dialogues, not author.

texts.title: Aitareya-Upaniṣad
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the seer Aitareya Mahidāsa)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Mula text extracted from the commented version' — mula only, no commentary. Anonymous Vedic composition embedded in the Aitareya Āraṇyaka (Ṛgveda). Olivelle 1998 (glossary): 'Mahidāsa Aitareya. The teacher after whom the Aitareya Brāhmaṇa, Āraṇyaka [and Upaniṣad] are named' — i.e. an eponymous figure, not a documented author. Gonda 1975 notes the Aitareya Brāhmaṇa is by tradition 'handed down ... as the work of one person, Mahidāsa', i.e. a traditional ascription.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Early Upanisads...Olivelle 1998.txt; secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt
texts.note: Mula-only file; truly anonymous. Aitareya Mahidāsa is an eponymous/traditional namesake of the school, not a historical author.

texts.title: Aitareya-Upaniṣad
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the seer Aitareya Mahidāsa)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: file includes mula (AitUp_) and the bhāṣya ascribed to Śaṅkara (AitUpBh_). Mula is the anonymous Aitareya-Upaniṣad of the Ṛgveda; Mahidāsa Aitareya is the eponymous teacher of the school, not a documented author (Olivelle 1998). The Aitareya-Upaniṣad-bhāṣya is among the bhāṣyas traditionally ascribed to Śaṅkara.
texts.note: Conflation: anonymous Vedic mula + Śaṅkara bhāṣya. Eponymous traditional namesake (Mahidāsa) is not the author; bhāṣya ascribed to Śaṅkara.

texts.title: Mānava-Gṛhyasūtra [Maitrāyaṇīyamānava-Gṛhyasūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: sūtra text alone, no commentary; ed. R.H. Sastri, GOS 35, 1926; attributed to the Maitrāyaṇīya (Mānava) śākhā of the (Black) Yajurveda. Gṛhyasūtras are anonymous śrauta/gṛhya school productions; the name 'Mānava' designates the school/Manu-lineage tradition, not a named historical author. Secondary lit (DeCaroli 2004) cites the Mānava Gṛhyasūtra as a school text without an individual author.
texts.note: Anonymous Mānava/Maitrāyaṇīya school sūtra text, mula only; 'Mānava' is a school designation, not a personal author.

texts.title: Āśvalāyana-Gṛhyasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Stenzler 1864, input Tokunaga) names no historical author, only the Āśvalāyana school; Gonda's Vedic Literature treats it as a Ṛgvedic gṛhyasūtra of the Āśvalāyana/Śākala tradition. Āśvalāyana is the eponymous reputed sūtrakāra, not an attested individual.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt
texts.note: Eponymous school founder; single sūtra text, no verse/commentary conflation. Companion to Āśvalāyana-Śrautasūtra.

texts.title: Kauśika-Sūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Bloomfield 1889 / 1st adhyāya Bahulkar 1990; recension by Arlo Griffiths) names no historical author beyond editorial work. Gonda's Vedic Literature: the Kauśika-Sūtra is the Atharvavedic ritual sūtra, compiled later than the (Vaitāna) śrauta work it relates to; ascribed to Kauśika. Secondary hits for 'Kausika' in Buddhist texts (Vasubandhu's Kauśika gotra) are unrelated.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Vedic Literature. Samhitas and Brahmanas_Jan Gonda, 1975.txt
texts.note: Atharvaveda (Śaunaka) Kalpasūtra; traditional ascription to Kauśika, historically uncertain. No multi-author structure in the edition.

texts.title: Vārāha-Gṛhyasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Raghu Vira 1982, input Hellwig) names no author; the text is the gṛhyasūtra of the Vārāha branch of the Black Yajurveda (Maitrāyaṇīya tradition). No individual sūtrakāra is named; 'Vārāha' designates the śākhā, not a person.
texts.note: School-affiliated, genuinely anonymous; traditional label 'Vārāha' is a śākhā name, not a personal ascription.

texts.title: Śāṅkhāyana-Śrautasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Hillebrandt 1885-1899, input Tokunaga): anonymous text of the Śāṅkhāyana school of the Ṛgveda. Web (wisdomlib/Oxford Reference): Śāṅkhāyana is the reputed author of the Śāṅkhāyana Śrauta- and Gṛhya-sūtras; the underlying doctrine is referred to a Kauṣītaki (patronymic for a teacher/teacher-lineage) of the Kauṣītaki śākhā.
texts.note: Eponymous reputed author; doctrine traced to Kauṣītaki teacher-lineage. Single śrauta sūtra, no verse/commentary conflation.

texts.title: Kauthuma-Gṛhyasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Suryakanta 1956, input J.S. Nair, Sansknet): no author named; colophon 'kauthumagṛhyaṃ samāptam'. Web: the Kauthuma school of the Sāmaveda principally follows the Gobhila-Gṛhyasūtra; the text labelled 'Kauthuma-Gṛhya' is a school-affiliated minor/supplementary gṛhya text with no named author ('nothing is known' of the school's sūtra authors).
texts.note: Anonymous, Kauthuma-śākhā label only; not to be conflated with Gobhila-Gṛhyasūtra (the main Kauthuma gṛhya manual). Single text, no commentary layer.

texts.title: Āpastamba-Śulbasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (checked vs Bürk ZDMG 55/56, Sansknet input): no author named; text 'extracted from the commented version', Āpastamba school of the Black Yajurveda (Taittirīya). The Śulbasūtra is one part of the larger Āpastamba Kalpasūtra corpus, traditionally ascribed to Āpastamba.
texts.note: Eponymous ascription to Āpastamba (geometry/altar-construction sūtra). GRETIL etext is the bare sūtra extracted from a commented version; the commentary (e.g. Kapardisvāmin/Sundararāja) is NOT included, so no conflation in this record.

texts.title: Śāṅkhāyana-Gṛhyasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Oldenberg ed., input Tokunaga) gives no colophon naming an author. Web/Oxford Reference: Śāṅkhāyana is the reputed/eponymous author of the Śāṅkhāyana Śrauta- and Gṛhya-sūtras, also called Kauṣītaki-Gṛhyasūtra; Śāṅkhāyana and Kauṣītaki are teachers (or series of teachers) of the same Ṛgvedic school.
texts.note: Eponymous/legendary teacher, not a securely individual historical author; school text of the Kauṣītaki/Śāṅkhāyana Ṛgvedins. Closely parallel to the Kauṣītaki-Gṛhyasūtra.

texts.title: Vaitāna-Śrautasūtra [Vaitānasūtra]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Atharvaveda Śaunaka Śrautasūtra); commentary Ākṣepānuvidhi by Somāditya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vishva Bandhu ed., input Arlo Griffiths) names the commentary Ākṣepānuvidhi by Somāditya; root sūtra carries no author. Web: Vaitānasūtra is the Śrautasūtra of the Atharvaveda Śaunaka school (Kauśikasūtra being its Gṛhyasūtra); the name 'Vaitāna' is taken either from an eponymous ṛṣi (Vaitāyana) or from the opening sūtra, not a known historical individual.
texts.note: Edition/file conflates two distinct authorial layers: anonymous Atharvavedic root Śrautasūtra + Somāditya's commentary Ākṣepānuvidhi. Root not attributable to a real person (eponymic/first-word name).

texts.title: Āśvalāyana-Śrautasūtra
texts.primary_author: Āśvalāyana (traditional eponym, Ṛgveda; pupil of Śaunaka)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Ramanarayana Vidyaratna ed., Bibliotheca Indica 49, input Tokunaga) gives no colophon naming an author. Standard tradition ascribes the Śrauta- and Gṛhyasūtras of the Āśvalāyana school (Ṛgveda) to Āśvalāyana, traditionally a pupil of Śaunaka.
texts.note: Eponymous teacher of the Āśvalāyana Ṛgvedic school; traditional ascription, not a documented individual author. No commentary bundled in this file.

texts.title: Aṣṭādhyāyī
texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes the sūtras to Pāṇini; the e-text is the sūtra-pāṭha EXTRACTED from the Kāśikāvṛtti of Vāmana & Jayāditya (Aryendra Sharma ed.). The authorship of the sūtras themselves is Pāṇini; Vāmana/Jayāditya are only the source of the reconstructed sūtra text, not authors of it.
texts.note: Editorial provenance: sūtra-pāṭha lifted from the Kāśikā commentary, not a directly transmitted Pāṇini MS; this is a text-constitution note, not a change of authorship. Sūtras securely Pāṇini's. (paninipu = sūtras in text order.)

texts.title: Aṣṭādhyāyī
texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes the sūtras to Pāṇini; same data as paninipu but reorganized (sūtras arranged for index/reference). Source again the Kāśikāvṛtti of Vāmana & Jayāditya (Aryendra Sharma ed.), input Mari Minamino.
texts.note: Duplicate of the Aṣṭādhyāyī, alphabetically/index-ordered variant (paniniiu). Sūtras securely Pāṇini's; Kāśikā only the source of the extracted text. Text-constitution note only.

texts.title: Āpastamba-Gṛhyasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL file bundles root + two commentaries with explicit colophons: 'iti śrīharadattaviracitāyāṃ gṛhyasūtravṛttāvanākulāyāṃ...' (Haradatta's Anākulāvṛtti) and 'iti śrīsudarśanācāryakṛte gṛhyatātparyadarśane...' (Sudarśanācārya). Root Gṛhyasūtra has no in-text author; traditionally ascribed to Āpastamba, eponym of the Āpastamba school (Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda, Taittirīya).
texts.note: Single title conflates three distinct authorial layers: traditional/eponymic Āpastamba mūla + Haradatta's Anākulā(vṛtti) + Sudarśanācārya's Gṛhyatātparyadarśana(vyākhyā). Root attribution is traditional, not a documented individual.

texts.title: Kaivalya-Upaniṣad
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only the edition (Ramamaya Tarkaratna 1872) and digitizers (Pandey, Gippert); colophon 'ity atharvavede kaivalyopaniṣat samāptā' attributes the text to the Atharvaveda tradition, no personal author. A later sectarian (Śaiva) Upaniṣad; secondary lit cites it doctrinally but names no author.
texts.note: Colophon ascribes to Atharvaveda, no human author. Genuinely anonymous; a late Śaiva Upaniṣad.

texts.title: Jaimini-Gṛhyasūtra
texts.current_author: attributed to the Jaiminīya school (trad. Jaimini)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Jaiminīya school of the Sāmaveda; traditionally ascribed to Jaimini)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Willem Caland 1922, input Sansknet; header notes '[no source given]' for original authorship. Secondary lit (Sayers, Feeding the Dead) states it is a text 'in the Jaiminīya school of the Sāma Veda tradition' (per Caland) — a school product, not a securely identified author. Traditional eponymous ascription to the sage Jaimini.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Feeding the Dead. Ancestor Worship in Ancient India_Matthew R. Sayers (2013).txt
texts.note: Jaiminīya/Sāmaveda school composition; 'Jaimini' is an eponymous/traditional founder ascription, not a verified author.

texts.title: Śira-Upaniṣad [Atharvaśira-Upaniṣad]
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only the edition (Ramamaya Tarkaratna 1872) and digitizer (Oliver Hellwig); colophon 'ity atharvavede śiraupaniṣat samāptā' attributes it to the Atharvaveda, no personal author.
texts.note: Colophon ascribes to Atharvaveda; genuinely anonymous Śaiva-leaning Atharvan Upaniṣad.

texts.title: Kaṭha-Upaniṣad
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Kaṭha/Kāṭhaka school of the Black Yajurveda)
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives only the edition (Patrick Olivelle, Early Upaniṣads 1998) and the Vienna student input team; no author named. A classical Upaniṣad of the Kaṭha branch; the figure Naciketas is a narrative character, not the author. Secondary lit treats it as anonymous.
texts.note: Anonymous; associated with the Kaṭha (Kāṭhaka) school. Naciketas is a dramatis persona, not the composer.

texts.title: Īśa-Upaniṣad [Īśāvāsyopaniṣad]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (final adhyāya of the Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā, Śukla Yajurveda)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: mūla checked vs Limaye & Vadekar 1958; header also notes the file is the mūla, with a commentary 'ascribed to Śaṅkara' associated in the edition tradition. The work item is the mūla only (Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā 40), which is anonymous. Śaṅkara is the commentator on a separate bhāṣya, not author of the mūla — not conflated within this work item.
texts.note: Mūla is anonymous (= VS 40). Śaṅkara is a later commentator (bhāṣya), distinct from the root text and outside this item's scope; no conflation in the mūla itself.

texts.title: Śvetāśvatara-Upaniṣad
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to the sage Śvetāśvatara)
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editions (Goendaka/Gita Press; Bhakti Sudhakar) and digitizer (Jan Brzezinski); colophon 'iti śvetāśvataropaniṣat sampūrṇā'. The name Śvetāśvatara occurs within the text (6.21) as the teacher to whom it is ascribed; scholarship treats this as an eponymous/traditional ascription, not a verified historical author.
texts.note: Traditional eponymous ascription to sage Śvetāśvatara (self-named at 6.21); a Black Yajurveda theistic Upaniṣad, effectively anonymous in authorship.

texts.title: Śvetāśvatara-Upaniṣad
texts.basis: GRETIL header: mūla only, input by S.H., checked vs Limaye & Vadekar 1958; colophon/verse 6.21 names 'śvetāśvataro' as the sage who received/transmitted the teaching (legendary eponym, not a historical author). Secondary lit treats it as an anonymous classical Upaniṣad.
texts.note: Eponymous sage Śvetāśvatara named in 6.21 is a traditional/legendary ascription; actual composition anonymous. GRETIL file is mūla only, no commentary.

texts.title: Āpastamba-Dharmasūtra
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (Āpastamba school of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Ikari/Kano, Kyoto project) gives text basis Bühler BSS LIV & L with KSS variants; names 'aapastamba' as school/eponym, no individual author. Dharmasūtra of the Āpastamba branch of the Taittirīya (Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda); school-corporate composition.
texts.note: Traditional eponymous ascription to Āpastamba (school founder); text is a corporate sūtra of the Āpastamba caraṇa. Mūla only on GRETIL.

texts.title: Īśa-Upaniṣad [Īśāvāsyopaniṣad]
texts.basis: GRETIL header/content: file 'isupsb_u' bundles the anonymous mūla Īśopaniṣad (Kāṇva, checked vs Limaye & Vadekar 1958) WITH the bhāṣya whose closing colophon reads 'iti śrīmat...śrīśaṅkarabhagavataḥ kṛtā īśāvāsyopaniṣadbhāṣyaṃ sampūrṇam' — i.e. commentary ascribed to Śaṅkara. Two distinct authors in one title.
texts.note: Single title conflates anonymous mūla Upaniṣad + Śaṅkara's bhāṣya (commentary). Colophon ascribes bhāṣya to Śaṅkara (traditional but standard ascription). Roles separated.

texts.title: Vaiśeṣikasūtra
texts.primary_author: Kaṇāda (traditional; alias Ulūka, gotra Kaśyapa) — composite redaction
texts.basis: GRETIL header: mūla only (input Nozawa Masanobu), no Candrānanda commentary despite the cited GOS 136 edition; no authorial colophon. Secondary lit: founder 'Ulūka Kaṇāda' = Kāśyapa (so named by Praśastapāda/Uddyotakara); web/Wikipedia: extant sūtra is a composite text redacted around the turn of the CE, doctrines older.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Traditional/legendary ascription to sage Kaṇāda (Ulūka, atom-eater). Extant text is a composite redaction (c. turn of CE), so single-author attribution is conventional, not historical. GRETIL file is mūla only — Candrānanda's vṛtti from the edition is NOT included.

texts.title: Ṛgvidhāna
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. M.S. Bhat (Vedic Tantrism), input Tokunaga; text refers to 'śaunakaḥ'. Secondary/web: traditionally ascribed to Śaunaka (one of the ten 'Śaunakasmṛti' works); scholars treat it as a later post-Vedic ritual/magical manual, attribution traditional/eponymous rather than a secure historical author.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Śaunaka (Vedic school eponym). Metrical manual of Ṛgvedic mantra-application; regarded as later/post-Vedic. No individual historical author securely established.

texts.title: Gautama-Dharmasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: text input Watase, eds. Gokhale (ASS 61, 1910) & Stenzler 1876; 'not proof-read'; no formal authorial colophon naming Gautama. Traditionally the 'Institutes of Gautama', oldest extant Dharmasūtra, linked to the Sāmaveda; corporate/school composition.
texts.note: Traditional eponymous ascription to Gautama; no historical individual author. GRETIL note explicitly: no formal colophon naming him. Mūla only.

texts.title: Baudhāyana-Dharmasūtra
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (Baudhāyana school of the Taittirīya Yajurveda)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Baudhāyana school of the Taittirīya/Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: mūla Dharmasūtra, no individual author; ed. Hultzsch/Pandeya (Govinda Svāmin's Vivaraṇa cited but not in this e-text). Secondary lit (Bronkhorst, Olivelle's Dharmasūtra Parallels context) treats it as an anonymous school text, one of the oldest dharmasūtras. Eponym 'Baudhāyana' is the sūtra-school tradent, not a historical single author.
texts.note: Traditionally ascribed to the sage/teacher Baudhāyana (school eponym); composite school redaction. This e-text is mūla only; Govinda Svāmin's Vivaraṇa not included.

texts.title: Arthaśāstra
texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes to Kauṭilya (=Viṣṇugupta/Cāṇakya), ed. Kangle 1969. Secondary: Basham notes the author called variously Kauṭilya/Cāṇakya/Viṣṇugupta; Bronkhorst (2011) 'several authors have contributed' and Kauṭilya compiling earlier teachers' Arthaśāstras; Szántó notes a late final redaction; Trautmann (1971) statistical study argues composite/multi-author evolution, casting doubt on single Mauryan authorship.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Buddhism in the Shadow of Brahmanism_Johannes Bronkhorst, 2011.txt
texts.note: Traditional single author Kauṭilya disputed; text is a composite stratified work (Trautmann statistical analysis). Conflation: nominal author vs. earlier sourced ārya-teachers vs. later redactor.

texts.title: Gautama-Dharmasūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (mūla, ascribed to Gautama); Maskarin (Maskarī) — bhāṣya
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Gautama: Dharmasūtra, Adhyāyas 1-3, with Maskari's commentary (Gautamasmṛtibhāṣya)'; colophon 'iti maskarīye gautamabhāṣye ...adhyāyaḥ'. This e-text combines the anonymous mūla sūtra (eponym Gautama, school tradent) with Maskarin's bhāṣya — two distinct hands. Secondary: dharmasūtra of the Sāmaveda tradition, anonymous school text.
texts.note: current_author records only Gautama; this e-text conflates anonymous mūla (ascribed Gautama) + Maskarin's bhāṣya. Colophon names Maskarin. Mislabeled 'Gautama-Gṛhyasūtra' on Sansknet.

texts.title: Dharmaskandha
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Śāriputra in Sanskrit/Tibetan sources, to Maudgalyāyana in Chinese sources)
texts.primary_author: traditional: Śāriputra (Sanskrit/Tibetan tradition) / Maudgalyāyana (Chinese tradition); actually anonymous Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma
texts.basis: GRETIL header: no traditional colophon; teachings framed 'evam uktaṃ bhagavatā'; ed. Dietz 1984 (Gilgit fragments), Matsuda 1986. Secondary (Mejor 1991, citing Yaśomitra/Tibetan list): 'Dharmaskandhasya ārya-Śāriputraḥ kartā'; Analayo 2014 same; Gethin (1998) and Patt (1993) note the Chinese tradition ascribes it to Mahā-Maudgalyāyana. One of the seven Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma works; actual author unknown.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakosa and the Commentaries Preserved in the Tanjur_Marek Mejor, 1991_OCR.txt
texts.note: Two divergent traditional ascriptions (Śāriputra vs Maudgalyāyana) for a single anonymous canonical Abhidharma text — competing legendary attributions, not multiple historical authors of distinct parts, so conflation_flag=false.

texts.title: Praśna-Upaniṣad
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Praśna-Upaniṣad with Śaṅkara's Bhāṣya'; reference system distinguishes mūla (PrUp_n.n) from commentary (PrUpBh_n.n); 'text of the commentary is not proofread'. The e-text combines the anonymous Atharvavedic Upaniṣad (attached to Pippalāda school) with Śaṅkara's bhāṣya — two distinct authors.
texts.note: current_author=anonymous omits the Śaṅkara bhāṣya present in THIS file (the 'bu' variant). Conflates anonymous mūla + Śaṅkara commentary.

texts.title: Praśna-Upaniṣad
texts.primary_author: anonymous (mūla Upaniṣad of the Atharvaveda, Pippalāda school)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Mula text extracted from commented version' — root text only, no commentary; ed. Limaye & Vadekar 1958, PrUp 6.4 completed per Bibliotheca Indica. Anonymous Atharvavedic (Pippalāda) Upaniṣad, traditionally part of the principal Upaniṣad corpus.
texts.note: Mūla-only variant (contrast prasupbu which adds Śaṅkara's bhāṣya). Genuinely anonymous; no conflation.

texts.title: Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives no author (input by J. Samuels; std ed. Limaye & Vadekar). But the file CONTENT conflates two distinct works: the 12 anonymous prose mantras of the Vedic Upaniṣad AND 29 kārikās (the Āgama-prakaraṇa of Gauḍapāda's Māṇḍūkya-kārikā/Āgamaśāstra), ending 'oṅkāro vidito yena sa munirnetaro janaḥ' — the final verse of Gauḍapāda's first prakaraṇa. Secondary lit (Richard King, Early Advaita Vedanta; 'Literature about the Philosophical Systems') firmly attributes the kārikās to Gauḍapāda.
texts.source: gretil-header + content; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Title says 'Upaniṣad' but file interleaves the anonymous Upaniṣad with Gauḍapāda's kārikās (29 vv. = Āgama-prakaraṇa). Upaniṣad proper is anonymous Vedic; kārikās are by Gauḍapāda (c. 6th c. CE).

texts.title: Vāsiṣṭha-Dharmasūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Vasiṣṭha school; pseudepigraphically ascribed to sage Vasiṣṭha)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Führer ed., BSPS 23; input M. Fushimi, checked F. Enomoto) names no author; notes only the legendary sage Vasiṣṭha via the title. No real human author known; the dharmasūtra is a school text traditionally ascribed to the eponymous sage.
texts.note: Eponymous/pseudepigraphic ascription to sage Vasiṣṭha; actual composition anonymous (Vasiṣṭha dharma school). No colophon naming a person.

texts.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 4. Kiṣkindhākāṇḍa
texts.basis: GRETIL header lists 'Author: Vālmīki' (Baroda Critical Edition, vol. IV; input Tokunaga, rev. J. Smith/T. Neill). Scholarly consensus treats Vālmīki as the traditional/legendary attributed poet of a text that grew by oral-redactional layers; Kiṣkindhākāṇḍa is part of the core books (II–VI), unlike the later Bāla- and Uttara-kāṇḍas.
texts.note: Traditional single-author ascription to Vālmīki; text is composite/redacted. This book (4) belongs to the older core, not the later-added first/last kāṇḍas.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Udyogaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header carries the traditional invocation ('vyāsaṃ vasiṣṭhanaptāram') ascribing the work to Vyāsa; Critical Edition (Sukthankar/De, BORI 1940; e-text BORI 1999). Scholarship treats Vyāsa as the legendary author of a multi-layered, redactionally composite epic.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to (legendary) Vyāsa; actually anonymous/composite epic redacted over centuries.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Sabhāparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header = Critical Edition (Sukthankar ed., BORI 1944) with traditional Vyāsa invocation. Vyāsa is the legendary attributed author; text is anonymous/composite.
texts.note: Same as other parvans: traditional/legendary ascription to Vyāsa, composite anonymous epic.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Śalyaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header = Critical Edition (Dandekar ed., BORI 1961) with traditional Vyāsa invocation. Legendary ascription to Vyāsa; anonymous/composite text.
texts.note: Traditional/legendary ascription to Vyāsa; composite anonymous epic redacted over centuries.

texts.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 3. Araṇyakāṇḍa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Baroda crit. ed., Divanji) names no author beyond editor; the Vālmīki-Rāmāyaṇa is traditionally ascribed to the poet Vālmīki. Secondary lit (Mylius, Geschichte der altindischen Literatur) treats Book III Araṇyakāṇḍa as part of the core epic but the work as a whole is a stratified composite with later accretions/interpolations (Books 1 partly and 7 latest); Araṇyakāṇḍa belongs to the older core but still shows redactional layering.
texts.note: Traditional single-author ascription to Vālmīki retained; scholarly view = composite oral-poetic transmission, not a single historical author. Not a conflation of named authors, so conflation_flag=false; redactional layering noted.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Āraṇyakaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header (BORI crit. ed., Sukthankar; Tokunaga/Smith digitization) names no author; the text itself internally ascribes composition to Vyāsa (Satyavatī-suta). Scholarly consensus: the Mahābhārata is an anonymous multi-author composite accreted over centuries; the BORI critical edition reflects this stratified transmission rather than a single author.
texts.note: Vyāsa is the legendary/traditional author; actual authorship anonymous and composite. Single parvan of one epic — not a conflation of distinct named authors.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Karṇaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header (BORI crit. ed., Vaidya, founded by Sukthankar; Tokunaga/Smith digitization) names no author. Internal/traditional ascription to Vyāsa. Scholarly consensus: anonymous composite, multi-author accretion; Karṇaparvan (Book 8) is part of the older battle-books core.
texts.note: Same as other Mbh parvans: legendary author Vyāsa, real authorship anonymous/composite. Not a conflation of distinct named authors.

texts.title: Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet input; checked vs Limaye/Vadekar 1958) gives no author. GRETIL content verified = mula only, the 12 prose mantras (MandUp_1..12), oṃ ity etad akṣaram... to ...ya evaṃ veda. It does NOT include Gauḍapāda's kārikās (Āgamaśāstra), although the edition note flags it as the mula from the commented version. Anonymous classical Upaniṣad.
texts.note: Verified the file contains only the anonymous Upaniṣad mula, NOT Gauḍapāda's Mānḍūkyakārikā/Āgamaśāstra — so no conflation here. The full commented work would conflate (anonymous Upaniṣad + Gauḍapāda's kārikās + Śaṅkara's bhāṣya), but the GRETIL text is mula only.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Ādiparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header (BORI crit. ed., Sukthankar 1933; Tokunaga/Smith digitization) names no author; text internally ascribes composition to Vyāsa: 'tapasā brahmacaryeṇa vyasya vedaṃ sanātanam / itihāsam imaṃ cakre puṇyaṃ satyavatīsutaḥ'. Critically edited composite; scholarly consensus = anonymous multi-author accretion.
texts.note: Current_author already captures the dual traditional-Vyāsa / anonymous-composite picture. Ādiparvan (Book 1) is the frame book; legendary author Vyāsa, real authorship anonymous. Not a conflation of distinct named authors.

texts.title: Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Aṣṭasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (DSBC/Nagarjuna Institute input; Vaidya 1960 ed.; missing portion restored from Karashima 2013, input by K. Wille) names no author — only editors/contributors. Secondary lit (Whitehead, Sinicization of Buddhism) treats the Aṣṭasāhasrikā as the earliest Prajñāpāramitā text (c. 1st c. BCE), an anonymous composite Mahāyāna sūtra. The Vaidya edition is published WITH Haribhadra's Āloka commentary, but the GRETIL bsu049 file is the sūtra mula only.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra (canonically 'spoken by the Buddha' / pseudo-buddhavacana but treated as anonymous composite). Edition pairs it with Haribhadra's Āloka, but the e-text is the root sūtra alone — no author conflation in the file itself. Restored missing portion from Karashima noted as editorial, not authorial.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Droṇaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header names traditional author Vyāsa, electronic text from BORI critical edition 1999, entered by Tokunaga et al., revised by John Smith; no scribal colophon in e-text. Critical ed. of book 7 by S. K. De (founded by Sukthankar). Scholarship treats the epic as anonymous, multi-layered, orally transmitted composite work only legendarily ascribed to Vyāsa.
texts.note: Traditional single-author ascription to Vyāsa is legendary; actual text is an anonymous composite. Critical edition redactor: S. K. De (BORI).

texts.title: Patna Dharmapada
texts.basis: GRETIL header: editor Margaret Cone (JPTS 13, 1989), input Tatsushi Tamai; no individual author; opening homage to the Three Jewels; colophon 'samāptā dhammapadā... gāthāśatāni pañca dve ca gāthe' (502 verses). Secondary lit (Norman 1983; Skilling 1997 'On the School-affiliation of the Patna Dharmapada'; Almogi ed.) treats it as an anonymous canonical Dharmapada recension; Skilling attributes the recension's SCHOOL affiliation to the Sāṃmatīyas (not the Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda), in slightly Sanskritized Middle Indic.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Almogi_ed._Evolution of scriptures, formation of canons_the Buddhist case.txt
texts.note: No author; canonical anthology. Skilling (1997) ascribes school to Sāṃmatīya; ms. discovered by Rāhula Sāṅkṛtyāyana in Tibet, ~12th-c. Eastern Indian script. Editor Cone; transcriber Tamai.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Virāṭaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header names traditional author Vyāsa; e-text from BORI critical edition 1999 (entered Tokunaga et al., revised John Smith). Critical ed. of book 4 by Raghu Vira (first fascicule N. B. Utgikar), BORI 1936. Scholarship treats epic as anonymous composite, only legendarily Vyāsa.
texts.note: Vyāsa ascription legendary; anonymous composite. Critical edition redactor: Raghu Vira / Utgikar (BORI).

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Bhīṣmaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header names traditional author Vyāsa; e-text from BORI critical edition 1999 (entered Tokunaga et al., revised John Smith). Critical ed. of book 6 by S. K. Belvalkar (founded by Sukthankar), BORI 1947. The Bhagavadgītā is embedded in this book (BORI 6.23-40) but is itself part of the same anonymous epic redaction, not a separately attributed author. Scholarship: anonymous composite, legendarily Vyāsa.
texts.note: Vyāsa ascription legendary; anonymous composite. Bhagavadgītā embedded here (also anonymous, traditionally Vyāsa). Critical edition redactor: S. K. Belvalkar (BORI).

texts.title: Abhisamācārikadharma
texts.basis: GRETIL header: digital edition of the Abhisamācārika-Dharma of the Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravādins; ed. Abhisamācārika-Dharma Study Group, Institute for Comprehensive Studies of Buddhism, Taisho University; cites Ms., Jinananda ed. (J.), Chinese (Ch.). No individual author. Secondary lit (Karashima 2012; Hirakawa 1982) treats it as an anonymous Vinaya (monastic-conduct) text of the school, included with related Bhikṣuṇī-Vinaya material.
texts.note: Anonymous Vinaya text of the Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda; no individual author. Critical ed. by Taishō Study Group; earlier ed. Jinananda.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header explicitly states the sūtra text is EXTRACTED FROM ŚAṄKARA'S Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya (input by Sansknet) — so this e-text physically conflates two authors' works: the root sūtras of Bādarāyaṇa as embedded in/transmitted by Śaṅkara's commentary. Secondary lit: Bādarāyaṇa is the traditional (and standard scholarly) author of the Brahma/Vedāntasūtra (Mysticism & Phil. Analysis, Katz ed.: 'Bādarāyaṇa, the presumed author'); the sūtras themselves cite Bādarāyaṇa and others (Jaimini etc.) as named authorities. Bronkhorst (Mīmāṃsā and Vedānta, 2007) discusses and rejects Sureśvara's minority remark on Brahmasūtra authorship as standing alone and uncontradicted-by-others; Bādarāyaṇa traditionally identified with Vyāsa.
texts.note: Conflation: e-text is sūtras (Bādarāyaṇa) extracted from Śaṅkara's bhāṣya — two distinct authors in one source file. Sūtras cite Bādarāyaṇa/Jaimini as authorities; Bādarāyaṇa traditionally = Vyāsa. Minority ascription (Sureśvara) rejected by Bronkhorst.

texts.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 3. Araṇyakāṇḍa
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Valmiki (traditional attribution)'; e-text input by M. Tokunaga, rev. O. Hellwig (with Southern-recension corrections). Scholarship treats the epic as orally transmitted and multiply redacted, not the work of a single historical Vālmīki; the kāṇḍa is part of the BORI/Baroda critical edition (Divanji 1963).
texts.note: Traditional single-author ascription to Vālmīki over composite, multiply-redacted material; not a multi-author work in the commentary sense (no bundled commentary). Critical edition is editorial reconstruction (Divanji/Bhatt).

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Sauptikaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Vyāsa (traditional attribution)'; e-text (C) BORI Pune 1999, entered by M. Tokunaga et al., rev. J. Smith. The epic is internally framed as composed by Vyāsa but is a composite, orally transmitted text; the parvan is part of the BORI critical edition.
texts.note: Traditional intra-narrative ascription to Vyāsa over anonymous composite material. Constituted text = BORI critical edition (editorial, not authorial). No bundled commentary, so no author conflation within the file.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra
texts.primary_author: Bādarāyaṇa (traditional; identified with Vyāsa in tradition)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet project input): traditional author Bādarāyaṇa, also called Vyāsa; plain sūtra text, NO commentary bundled (not the Śāṅkarabhāṣya despite the edition cited). Secondary lit (Bronkhorst ed. 2007, Mīmāṃsā and Vedānta) discusses Brahmasūtra authorship and sūtra-vs-commentary issues; tradition equates Bādarāyaṇa with Vyāsa, scholarship treats this identification as legendary and the sūtras as possibly containing earlier strata.
texts.note: Sūtra text attributed to Bādarāyaṇa; traditional equation with Vyāsa is legendary. The cited edition is the Śāṅkarabhāṣya, but this GRETIL file contains only the bare sūtras (no Śaṅkara commentary) — no kārikā/bhāṣya conflation in the actual e-text.

texts.title: Bhikṣuṇī-Karmavācanā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: anonymous ancient Buddhist ceremonial (karmavācanā) text; critical edition by Michael Schmidt (1993) from Bodleian MS Sansk. c. 25 (R), made available by his courtesy. No personal author named in the text.
texts.note: Anonymous Vinaya ordination formulary (bhikṣuṇī upasaṃpadā). Schmidt is modern editor, not author. No traditional personal ascription.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Strīparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Vyāsa (attributed)'; e-text (C) BORI Pune 1999, entered by M. Tokunaga et al., rev. J. Smith. Internally framed as Vyāsa's composition; composite anonymous epic material. Constituted text = BORI critical edition (Sukthankar/Dandekar 1956, vol. 12).
texts.note: Same status as Sauptikaparvan: traditional intra-narrative ascription to Vyāsa over anonymous composite material; editorial critical text. No bundled commentary.

texts.title: Śālistambasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: no author/colophon naming a composer; ends 'āryaśālistambaṃ nāma mahāyānasūtraṃ saṃpūrṇam' (names the text, not an author). Input by Sanskrit Buddhist Input Project (DSBC), ed. P.L. Vaidya 1961 (Skt. reconstructed from quotations/Tibetan). As a Mahāyāna sūtra it is traditionally buddhavacana (word of the Buddha), i.e. anonymous; sometimes cited via Nāgārjuna's commentary but the sūtra itself is unattributed.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra; traditionally buddhavacana. No personal author. A separate commentary is ascribed to Nāgārjuna but is NOT part of this root-sūtra file (no conflation within the e-text).

texts.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 2. Ayodhyākāṇḍa
texts.primary_author: Vālmīki (traditional; part of the unitary core, Books 2-6)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Southern recension, Krishnacharya ed.) names Vālmīki traditionally, no individual colophon author. Secondary lit + web: Books 2-6 form the 'more or less unitary' original core ascribed to Vālmīki; the epic is a layered oral-literary work with later redaction, not a single fixed author.
texts.note: Ayodhyākāṇḍa belongs to the original core (Books 2-6); traditional Vālmīki authorship, multi-hand transmission/redaction acknowledged.

texts.title: Brahmabindu-Upaniṣad
texts.basis: GRETIL/Tarkaratna Atharvana-Upanishads edition: no author. Potter's Encyclopedia lists it only among minor Upaniṣads carrying later commentaries (Nārāyaṇa's, Śaṃkarānanda's Dīpikā) - i.e. the root text itself is unattributed. Web: one of the five Bindu Upaniṣads attached to the Atharvaveda; also transmitted as Amṛtabindu Upaniṣad; authorship unknown.
texts.source: secondary:Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies Vol.1 - Potter | web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amritabindu_Upanishad
texts.note: Anonymous minor Upaniṣad; = Amṛtabindu Upaniṣad in some anthologies; commentaries (Nārāyaṇa, Śaṃkarānanda) are separate later works, not part of this e-text.

texts.title: Madhyamakaśālistambasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya, Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha): opens 'namo buddhāya', colophon 'madhyamaka-śālistambasūtraṃ samāptam', no human author named - presented as buddhavacana. Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, p.176): the Śālistamba-sūtra teaches dependent origination; a Madhyamika-setting recension exists; two COMMENTARIES (by Kamalaśīla and another) exist - those are separate authored works, not this root sūtra.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical sūtra; not by Nāgārjuna. Authored commentaries (Kamalaśīla et al.) are distinct texts, not conflated into this root e-text.

texts.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 1. Bālakāṇḍa
texts.current_author: Vālmīki (traditional); Bālakāṇḍa largely a later addition
texts.basis: GRETIL (Baroda critical ed., Bhatt) ascribes traditionally to Vālmīki. Secondary/web consensus: Bālakāṇḍa (Book 1) and Uttarakāṇḍa (Book 7) are later additions absent from oldest mss, composed in a later linguistic/theological period (Rāma as Viṣṇu avatāra), later than the unitary core Books 2-6. So mostly NOT by the original Vālmīki layer.
texts.note: Book 1 is largely a later accretion (with Book 7); traditional Vālmīki ascription, but scholarship treats it as later anonymous composition. Not a multi-author single-title conflation in the colophon sense, so conflation_flag=false.

texts.title: Prātimokṣasūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya recension; ed. Lokesh Chandra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Klaus Wille input, from Lokesh Chandra's Gilgit fragment, WZKSO 4, 1960; Mūlasarvāstivāda recension): anonymous Vinaya text, no original author named. Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, item 9, p.176/n) confirms the 'Unpublished Gilgit fragment of the Prātimokṣa-sūtra introduced by L. Chandra, WZKSO IV' - matching this exact edition; Prātimokṣa-sūtras are school-specific anonymous Vinaya recensions.
texts.note: Anonymous Vinaya recension (Mūlasarvāstivāda Gilgit fragment); Lokesh Chandra is editor of the fragment, not author. No personal author exists for the text itself.

texts.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 5. Sundarakaṇḍa
texts.primary_author: Vālmīki (traditional; part of the unitary core, Books 2-6)
texts.basis: GRETIL (Baroda critical ed., Jhala) ascribes traditionally to Vālmīki. Secondary/web: Sundarakāṇḍa (Book 5) is part of the unitary original core (Books 2-6) attributed to Vālmīki, as opposed to the later-added Books 1 and 7; epic is layered with later redaction.
texts.note: Sundarakāṇḍa belongs to the original core (Books 2-6); traditional Vālmīki authorship with acknowledged multi-hand transmission.

texts.title: Nyāyasūtra 5.1
texts.basis: GRETIL header titles it 'Gautama: Nyayasutra 5.1, with Nyayasutrabhasya by Vatsyayana' and the e-text content interleaves NyS_5,1.x sūtras with NySBh_5,1.x bhāṣya prose (two distinct authors in one file). Edition is the Nyāyadarśana with Vātsyāyana's Bhāṣya (Tarkatirtha 1944). Secondary lit (Matilal; Tillemans; Bilimoria) consistently treats the Nyāyasūtras as Akṣapāda Gautama's with the foundational Bhāṣya by Pakṣilasvāmin Vātsyāyana.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_DHARMAKIRTI'S PRAMANAVARTTIKA...Tillemans 2000.txt; cleaned_Mind, language, and world...Matilal.txt
texts.note: current_author lists only Gautama; the file actually conflates root sūtras (Akṣapāda Gautama) with Vātsyāyana's Nyāyabhāṣya. Book 5 (jāti/nigrahasthāna) is among the strata some scholars regard as later, but here authorship roles are the salient point.

texts.title: Kāśyapaparivartasūtra [Kāśyapaparivarta]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Staël-Holstein 1926 ed.) gives no author and the e-text has no composer/scribe colophon — it is a Mahāyāna sūtra of the Ratnakūṭa class. Secondary lit (Pagel, Bodhisattvapiṭaka 1995) treats it as an early anonymous Ratnakūṭa sūtra and discusses its early date of composition; a separate Sthiramati-ascribed ṭīkā exists but is not this text.
