texts.note: Canonical anonymous Mahāyānasūtra (Ratnakūṭa). No traditional personal author; spoken by/attributed to the Buddha in tradition. Sthiramati's ṭīkā on it is a distinct work.

texts.title: Gurupañcāśikā
texts.current_author: Aśvaghoṣa (traditional attribution; the tantric content postdates the famous 1st-2nd c. poet, so authorship is pseudepigraphic/uncertain)
texts.primary_author: Aśvaghoṣa (tantric; not the kāvya poet — pseudepigraphic)
texts.basis: DSBC e-text colophon: 'kṛtiriyaṃ mahācāryāśvaghoṣasya' (work of the great master Aśvaghoṣa). Secondary lit: Ruegg (Literature of the Madhyamaka 1981) explicitly distinguishes the author of the Gurupañcāśikā from the kāvya poet; Nakamura, Wayman ('the tantric Aśvaghoṣa'), and Samuel note its Tantric (guru-devotion) character, which postdates the 1st-2nd c. poet. An undated commentary by Vāpilladatta (9th-10th c.) exists (van der Kuijp).
texts.source: gretil-header(colophon) + secondary:cleaned_Literature of the Madhyamaka_Ruegg_1981.txt; cleaned_Untying the Knots in Buddhism_Wayman_1997.txt
texts.note: Colophon ascribes to Aśvaghoṣa but this is a tantric guru-devotion text; scholarship distinguishes this 'tantric Aśvaghoṣa' from the famous poet — pseudepigraphic. Vāpilladatta wrote a commentary (not part of this verse text).

texts.title: Saundarananda Mahākāvya
texts.basis: DSBC e-text (Johnston 1928 ed.) attributes to Aśvaghoṣa. Secondary lit: Eltschinger ('Aśvaghoṣa and His Canonical Sources') notes the colophons of the Buddhacarita and Saundarananda secure Aśvaghoṣa's authorship; Loukota and Brunnhölzl cite 'Aśvaghoṣa's Saundarananda' as standard. This is the genuine 1st-2nd c. CE kāvya poet — a securely attributed work.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Eltschinger_Aśvaghoṣa and His Canonical Sources II.txt
texts.note: Securely Aśvaghoṣa (the kāvya poet); attribution supported by the poem's own colophons. Single author, no commentary conflated in this text.

texts.title: Saundarananda
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'Asvaghosa: Saundarananda' (Matsunami 1980, based on Johnston 1928). Sarga colophons (e.g. 'Saundaranande mahākāvye ... prathamaḥ sargaḥ') name the work. Secondary lit (Eltschinger) ties authorship to Aśvaghoṣa via the colophons of his two mahākāvyas. Same text/author as SA_T13 (different e-text source).
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Eltschinger_Aśvaghoṣa and His Canonical Sources II.txt
texts.note: Duplicate work of SA_T13 from a different e-text edition; securely the kāvya poet Aśvaghoṣa. Single author.

texts.title: Vajrasūcī
texts.basis: DSBC e-text colophon ascribes to Aśvaghoṣa ('kṛtiriyaṃ siddhācāryāśvaghoṣapādānām'). Secondary lit: Nakamura (1980) — Sanskrit text ascribes to Aśvaghoṣa, the Chinese version ascribes to Dharmakīrti, and citations of later works argue against the poet's authorship; Nariman (1919) — Chinese (Fa-hsien) calls it a work of Dharmakīrti, ascription 'not improbable'; Samuels treats authorship as uncertain. Anti-caste polemic; content overlaps Yogācārabhūmi chuan 61.
texts.source: gretil-header(colophon) + secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism...Nakamura, 1980.txt; cleaned_Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism_Nariman_1919.txt
texts.note: Genuinely disputed: Sanskrit colophon = Aśvaghoṣa; Chinese version = Dharmakīrti. Even the Aśvaghoṣa ascription is likely not the 1st-2nd c. poet given later citations. Not a within-text conflation (single composition), so conflation_flag=false.

texts.title: Saundarananda
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Asvaghosa: Saundarananda' (Johnston ed., DSBC input). Secondary: Nakamura (Indian Buddhism) and Snellgrove (Indo-Tibetan Buddhism, via Johnston's translation) treat it as a genuine Aśvaghoṣa mahākāvya on the conversion of Nanda; verses cited as Aśvaghoṣa's in Janaśrayī Chandoviciti. Single authorship undisputed.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Single-author mahākāvya. Aśvaghoṣa's two kāvyas (Saundarananda, Buddhacarita) are the earliest extant Sanskrit kāvya. No multi-author structure.

texts.title: Buddhacarita
texts.basis: GRETIL header credits Aśvaghoṣa (Johnston 1935 ed., Cowell 1893 variants). E-text grep shows colophons run through canto 14 only (does NOT contain cantos 15-28). Secondary (Nariman, Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism): the surviving Sanskrit is 'old and genuine'; the concluding portion (later cantos) was supplied by the copyist Amṛtānanda in the early 9th cent. — but that completion is absent from this e-text. Johnston/Snellgrove: cantos i-xiv from original Sanskrit; xv onward survive only via Tibetan/Chinese.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism_Nariman G K_1919.txt
texts.note: This e-text = genuine Aśvaghoṣa Sanskrit, cantos 1-14 only. The spurious Sanskrit cantos 15-28 (Amṛtānanda's medieval completion) are NOT present here, so no conflation. The full Buddhacarita transmission DOES conflate Aśvaghoṣa + Amṛtānanda, but not this redaction.

texts.title: Dramatic Fragments (buddhist)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Lüders, Bruchstücke buddhistischer Dramen 1911; ed. Klaus Wille) groups three distinct dramas from Turfan SHT fragments: (1) an allegorical drama with Buddhi/Dhṛti/Kīrti — author unattributed; (2) the Śāriputraprakaraṇa, explicitly Aśvaghoṣa, with colophon SHT 57c 'śāriputraprakaraṇe navamo 'ṅkaḥ ... āryyāśvaghoṣasya kṛtiḥ' (a work of the noble Aśvaghoṣa); (3) a drama 'presumedly with a hetaera' — author unattributed.
texts.note: Single title conflates >=2 distinct authors: only the Śāriputraprakaraṇa is colophon-attributed to Aśvaghoṣa; the other two dramatic fragments are anonymous/unidentified. Current_author already hedges, but record now separates roles explicitly.

texts.title: Buddhacarita
texts.basis: GRETIL header credits Aśvaghoṣa (Johnston 1935 ed.; DSBC/Sanskrit Buddhist Input Project; minor 5.77 correction by Arlo Griffiths). E-text grep shows colophons through canto 14 only — does not include cantos 15-28. Same genuine Aśvaghoṣa Sanskrit as asvbc_1u; this is a parallel/DSBC encoding of the same Johnston text.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism_Nariman G K_1919.txt
texts.note: Duplicate Buddhacarita encoding (cantos 1-14, genuine Aśvaghoṣa). Amṛtānanda's later completion (cantos 15-28) NOT in this file -> no conflation flag for this redaction.

texts.title: Kāśyapaparivartasūtra [Kāśyapaparivarta]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition Vorobyova-Desyatovskaya/Karashima/Kudo 2002 (romanized text + facsimiles), input Klaus Wille; a fragmentary scholarly reconstruction from multiple MSS (SI P/2, SI P/85A, Hoernle 143, SHT I 374). It is a canonical Mahāyāna sūtra (Buddha-Kāśyapa dialogue), no human author. Secondary (Pagel, Bodhisattvapiṭaka; Nakamura): one of the oldest Ratnakūṭa sūtras, attested in the Lokakṣema corpus — anonymous scripture.
texts.note: Anonymous early Mahāyāna sūtra (Ratnakūṭa no. 43 in collection). Buddhavacana, no traditional named human author. Sanskrit commentary (ascribed to Vasubandhu/Sthiramati) exists separately but is NOT part of this sūtra e-text.

texts.title: Garbha-Upaniṣad
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Ramamaya Tarkaratna, Atharvana-Upanishads (Bibliotheca Indica 76, 1872); digital input Oliver Hellwig. No author named; text's own colophon reads 'paippalādaṃ mokṣaśāstram', associating it with the Paippalāda/Atharvaveda school. Secondary (Eliot, Hinduism and Buddhism vol. III) discusses it as a minor Upaniṣad giving a psychophysiological/embryological account — treated as anonymous Vedic-genre text.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Hinduism and Buddhism. An Historical Sketch - Volume III_Charles Eliot, 1921.txt
texts.note: Anonymous minor (Atharvaveda/sāmānya) Upaniṣad. Internal colophon links it to the Paippalāda recension as a 'mokṣaśāstra'; no individual or ṛṣi authorship. Sometimes traditionally grouped with Pippalāda but no secure ascription.

texts.title: Carakasaṃhitā
texts.current_author: Agniveśa (redacted by Caraka; later supplemented by Dṛḍhabala)
texts.primary_author: Agniveśa (mūla compilation); Caraka (redactor, 79 chs); Dṛḍhabala (restorer/supplementer, 41 chs); Cakrapāṇidatta (Āyurvedadīpikā commentary)
texts.basis: GRETIL header/content: 'tantrasya kartā prathamam agniveśo' names Agniveśa as first creator; teaching lineage Ātreya>Bharadvāja>Agniveśa; Cakrapāṇidatta's Āyurvedadīpikā embedded inline ('kriyate cakradattena ṭīkāyurvedadīpikā'). Web (wisdomlib, Internet Archive Gulabkunverba ed., Wikipedia): Agniveśa compiled, Caraka redacted 79 chs, Dṛḍhabala restored/supplemented remaining 41 chs from lost portions; three authorial strata.
texts.note: Multi-stratum: Agniveśa (orig.) / Caraka (redaction of 79 chs) / Dṛḍhabala (supplemented 41 chs incl. last part of Cikitsāsthāna + Kalpa/Siddhi). GRETIL e-text also carries Cakrapāṇidatta's Āyurvedadīpikā commentary, a separate author. Current_author omitted Cakrapāṇidatta and did not flag conflation.

texts.title: Nyāyasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header names author Gautama/Gotama/Akṣapāda; no editorial attribution statement beyond SANSKNET input. Standard scholarship treats Akṣapāda Gautama as the traditional author of the sūtras, though the extant text is a compiled/stratified collection redacted over centuries (the named author is conventional).
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Akṣapāda Gautama. The sūtra text itself is layered (later additions debated by scholars), but no distinct named co-author; Vātsyāyana's Bhāṣya is NOT included in this e-text, so no conflation here.

texts.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 2. Ayodhyākāṇḍa
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Vālmīki as author; edition P.L. Vaidya (Baroda critical ed., Vol. II Ayodhyākāṇḍa 1962), input Tokunaga rev. John Smith. Traditional single ascription to Vālmīki; the epic is a multi-layered oral-redactional composition, but no distinct named author for this kāṇḍa.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Vālmīki. This is the critically edited text (Baroda); the Bālakāṇḍa and Uttarakāṇḍa are widely held to be later additions, but Ayodhyākāṇḍa is part of the older core. No co-author/commentary in this e-text.

texts.title: Buddhist dramatic fragments (Turfan): Śāriputraprakaraṇa and other dramas
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Lüders, Bruchstücke buddhistischer Dramen, Berlin 1911; input Klaus Wille): the manuscript preserves three Buddhist dramas; the Śāriputraprakaraṇa (=Śāradvatīputraprakaraṇa) colophon explicitly ascribes it to Aśvaghoṣa: 'āryyasuvarṇṇākṣiputrasyāryyāśvaghoṣasya kṛtiś śāradvatīputraprakaraṇaṃ samāptaṃ'. The allegorical drama (Buddhi/Dhṛti/Kīrti) and the third (courtesan) drama are not securely attributed.
texts.note: Colophon names Aśvaghoṣa son of Suvarṇākṣī for the Śāriputraprakaraṇa. The two other fragmentary dramas in the same Turfan find are anonymous (sometimes conjecturally linked to Aśvaghoṣa but not by colophon). Workitem had empty title/author; resolved.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 11
texts.primary_author: Vyāsa (traditional, mūla); with commentaries by Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header/content (input Gaudiya Grantha Mandira; ed. cf. Pansikar, Śrīmadbhagavadgītā with eight commentaries): chapter 11 (Viśvarūpadarśana) base text traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa within the Mahābhārata, accompanied by four named commentaries: Śrīdhara (Subodhinī), Madhusūdana (Gūḍhārthadīpikā), Viśvanātha (Sārārthavarṣiṇī), Baladeva.
texts.note: E-text conflates the mūla (traditionally Vyāsa; really anonymous/composite epic interpolation) with four distinct historical commentators. Current_author noted Vyāsa/anonymous but did not list the commentators or flag conflation.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 10
texts.primary_author: Vyāsa (traditional, mūla); with commentaries by Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header/content (same series as BhG 11; ed. cf. Pansikar): chapter 10 (Vibhūtiyoga) base text traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, accompanied by the same four named commentaries: Śrīdhara (Subodhinī), Madhusūdana (Gūḍhārthadīpikā), Viśvanātha (Sārārthavarṣiṇī), Baladeva.
texts.note: Same structure as BhG 11: mūla (traditionally Vyāsa; really anonymous/composite) plus four distinct commentators. Current_author did not list commentators or flag conflation.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 1
texts.primary_author: Vyāsa (traditional, mūla); commentaries by Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha, Baladeva
texts.basis: GRETIL header: mūla ascribed to Kṛṣṇa-dvaipāyana (Vyāsa), 'direct utterance of the Lord'; edition is the Bhagavadgītā WITH four named commentaries (Śrīdhara Subodhinī, Madhusūdana Gūḍhārthadīpikā, Viśvanātha Sārārthavarṣiṇī, Baladeva). Secondary lit (Buddhist Studies in honour of Walpola Rahula; Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies) treats the Gītā as composite/interpolated within the MBh, not a single author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Buddhist Studies in honour of Walpola Rahula_Balasooriya et. al., ed (1980) (A).txt
texts.note: Title file is a multi-commentary edition: root Gītā (trad. Vyāsa, scholarly anonymous/composite) plus four distinct commentators (Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha, Baladeva). These distinct authors are conflated under one title.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 12
texts.primary_author: Vyāsa (traditional, mūla); commentaries by Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha, Baladeva
texts.basis: GRETIL header for ch.12 names the same four commentators (Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha, Baladeva); root text traditionally Vyāsa. Same multi-commentary edition (Pansikar, Eight Commentaries) as ch.1.
texts.note: Same structure as Bhagavadgītā 1: root (trad. Vyāsa / scholarly composite) conflated with four distinct commentators. Chapter 12 (Bhakti-yoga) of the same edition.

texts.title: Prātimokṣasūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Sarvāstivāda canonical Prātimokṣa); ed. Georg von Simson
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Prātimokṣasūtra der Sarvāstivādins', input by Georg von Simson (Turfan fragments, Sanskrittexte aus den Turfanfunden 11). Secondary lit (Schmidt, Schlussteil des Prātimokṣasūtra der Sarvāstivādins 1989; Puri, Buddhism in Central Asia; Banerjee, Sarvāstivāda Literature) confirms this is the SARVĀSTIVĀDA (not Mūlasarvāstivāda) recension from the Turfan finds. Monastic code = anonymous canonical compilation, no single author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Schlussteil des Pratimoksasutra der Sarvastivadins_Klaus T. Schmidt, 1989.txt
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Vinaya text. CORRECTION: this von Simson/Turfan edition is the Sarvāstivāda recension, NOT Mūlasarvāstivāda as the current record states. von Simson is editor (recorded as translator-role for digital edition).

texts.title: Prātimokṣasūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Mūlasarvāstivāda Prātimokṣa); ed. Anukul Chandra Banerjee
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Mūlasarvāstivāda recension, ed. Anukul Chandra Banerjee (Calcutta 1954; first IHQ 29, 1953), input by Klaus Wille. Secondary lit (Banerjee, Sarvāstivāda Literature) frames it as canonical Vinaya. Monastic code = anonymous communal compilation.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Vinaya text, Mūlasarvāstivāda recension (Banerjee ed.). Distinct from the von Simson Sarvāstivāda edition (prmosu_u). Banerjee recorded as editor.

texts.title: Vimalakīrtinirdeśa [Vimalakīrtinirdeśasūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header names no author/translator, only the Study Group ed. (Potala MS, Tokyo 2006) and inputter Klaus Wille. Secondary lit (Nakamura, Indian Buddhism — composed ca. 1st c. AD; Pagel, Bodhisattvapiṭaka — discusses terminus ad quem) treats it as an anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra of uncertain composition date, no named author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra. Sanskrit edition from Potala MS (Study Group on Buddhist Sanskrit Literature). No traditional single-author ascription.

texts.title: Vimalakīrtinirdeśa (Vimalakīrtinirdeśasūtra)
texts.basis: Same work and same Study Group edition (Potala MS, Tokyo 2006) as vimkn_u, here in the corpustei/TEI-HTML transformation. Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra per secondary lit (Nakamura; Pagel).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Duplicate digital encoding (TEI/corpustei) of the same anonymous Vimalakīrtinirdeśa. No named author.

texts.title: Nādabindu-Upaniṣad
texts.primary_author: anonymous (minor/yoga Upaniṣad of the Atharvaveda tradition)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Ramamaya Tarkaratna, Atharvana-Upanishads 1872) makes no authorship claim; no colophon names a composer. Upaniṣadic śruti is traditionally ascribed to no human author; nominally counted among yoga Upaniṣads.
texts.note: Anonymous śruti; no individual author. Edition is by Ramamaya Tarkaratna (editor, not author).

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 3
texts.primary_author: Vyāsa (Gītā, traditional); with commentaries by Śrīdhara Svāmin, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header confirms the file bundles the BhG mūla text with four distinct commentaries by Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha, Baladeva. Gītā itself traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa (a Mahābhārata interpolation); each commentary is by a separate historical author.
texts.note: Single title conflates root Gītā (Vyāsa, traditional) + 4 independent commentators (Śrīdhara Svāmin ~14c; Madhusūdana Sarasvatī 16c; Viśvanātha Cakravartin & Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa, Gauḍīya, 17-18c).

texts.title: Vimalakīrtinirdeśasūtra [Vimalakīrtinirdeśa]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Āryavimalakīrtinirdeśo nāma mahāyānasūtram; DSBC/CIHTS reconstruction (Joshi & Pāsādika 1981). Opens 'evaṃ mayā śrutam'; no colophon names a composer. Canonical Mahāyāna sūtra presented as buddhavacana.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra (composed ~1-2c CE). No human author; presented as the Buddha's word. Editors Joshi & Pāsādika, not authors.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 2
texts.primary_author: Vyāsa (Gītā, traditional); with commentaries by Śrīdhara Svāmin, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: Same Bhagavadgītā-4comm corpus (Sadhale, 11 Commentaries print). GRETIL header lists the BhG verse plus four commentaries by Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha, Baladeva. Root Gītā ascribed to Vyāsa traditionally; commentaries each by a distinct author.
texts.note: Same conflation as BhG 3: root verses (Vyāsa, traditional) + 4 separate commentators bundled under one title.

texts.title: Śālistambakakārikā [Āryaśālistambakakārikā]
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'āryanāgārjunaviracitā āryaśālistambakasūtrakārikā samāptā' (ascribes to Nāgārjuna). Schoening 1991 (Śālistamba-sūtra and its Indian Commentaries) states the ŚK 'is ascribed to Nāgārjuna' — i.e. a traditional ascription, not securely his; the kārikās versify the anonymous Śālistamba-sūtra.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Salistamba-sutra and its Indian Commentaries_Jeffrey Davis Schoening, 1991.txt
texts.note: Colophon names Nāgārjuna but attribution is traditional/disputed in scholarship (Schoening). Underlying Śālistamba-sūtra it versifies is itself anonymous; that base sūtra is not in this kārikā record.

texts.title: Nyāyasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Gautama: Nyayasutra, with Vatsyayana's Nyayabhasya'; colophon 'iti vātsyāyanīye nyāyabhāṣye...'. The file is the sūtra plus Vātsyāyana's bhāṣya. Ganeri 2001 (Proper Work of Reason): Nyāyasūtra by Gautama Akṣapāda, redacted 1st-2nd c. AD, with Bhāṣya of Vātsyāyana.
texts.note: File conflates two distinct authors: Gautama Akṣapāda (root sūtra, traditional/composite redaction) and Vātsyāyana Pakṣilasvāmin (Nyāyabhāṣya, ~5c). current_author lists only Gautama, missing the bhāṣyakāra.

texts.title: Prātimokṣasūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Mahāsāṅghika recension; scribe: Śākyabhikṣu Śrī Vijayabhadra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Pachow & Mishra 1956): Mahāsāṅghika recension, NOT Mūlasarvāstivāda as item metadata states. Canonical Vinaya text with no individual author. Colophon 'śākyabhikṣu śrīvijayabhadralikhitamidam'. Prebish (Buddhist Monastic Discipline) reads this colophon as the MS having been COPIED by Bhikṣu Vijaya Bhadra (scribe), not authored — 'This was written by Śrīvijayabhadra, a monk of Śākya'.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Buddhist Monastic Discipline. Sanskrit Pratimoksa Sutras of Mahasamghikas and Mulasarvastivadins_Charles S. Prebish, 1996-2002.txt
texts.note: Recension correction: GRETIL = Mahāsāṅghika, not Mūlasarvāstivāda. Colophon names Vijayabhadra as scribe/copyist only, not author; work is canonical and anonymous.

texts.title: Prātimokṣasūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Lokottaravāda-Mahāsāṅghika recension; scribe: Śākyabhikṣu Śrī Vijayabhadra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (N. Tatia 1976, TSWS 16; input Klaus Wille): Lokottaravāda-Mahāsāṅghika recension, explicitly named in colophon 'āryamahāsāṃghikānāṃ lokottaravādināṃ madhyoddeśikānāṃ'. Canonical Vinaya text, anonymous. Same scribal colophon 'śākyabhikṣuśrīvijayabhadralikhitam idam' as the Pachow/Mishra recension — Vijayabhadra is copyist (Prebish), not author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Buddhist Monastic Discipline. Sanskrit Pratimoksa Sutras of Mahasamghikas and Mulasarvastivadins_Charles S. Prebish, 1996-2002.txt
texts.note: Lokottaravāda-Mahāsāṅghika recension (specific sub-school of the same Mahāsāṅghika Prātimokṣa). Anonymous canonical text; Vijayabhadra is scribe only.

texts.title: Nāṭyaśāstra, Vol. 3
texts.primary_author: Bharata Muni (legendary; composite/anonymous mūla); Abhinavagupta (Abhinavabhāratī commentary)
texts.basis: Edition (Ramakrishna Kavi, GOS 124) = Nāṭyaśāstra mūla WITH Abhinavabhāratī commentary by Abhinavaguptācārya — two distinct authors. Mūla traditionally ascribed to sage Bharata, but scholars hold it a compilation of several hands (or a legendary/school name; 'Bharata' may = 'actor', or acronym bhāva/rāga/tāla); Bharat Gupt & Vatsyayan argue single-compiler style. Commentary (vol. covers chs. 19-27) is Abhinavagupta's, securely attributed.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Buddhism. Critical Concepts in Religious - Volume 3_Paul Williams_A.txt; web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natya_Shastra
texts.note: Conflates two authors: composite/legendary Bharata (mūla) + Abhinavagupta (Abhinavabhāratī ṭīkā). Edition title masks the commentary author.

texts.title: Nāṭyaśāstra, Vol. 1
texts.primary_author: Bharata Muni (legendary; composite/anonymous mūla); Abhinavagupta (Abhinavabhāratī commentary)
texts.basis: Edition (Ramakrishna Kavi, GOS 36, chs. 1-7) = Nāṭyaśāstra mūla WITH Abhinavabhāratī commentary by Abhinavaguptācārya — two distinct authors. Mūla traditionally ascribed to Bharata Muni but widely regarded as composite/anonymous (legendary figure or school); commentary is Abhinavagupta's.
texts.note: Conflates composite/legendary Bharata (mūla) + Abhinavagupta (Abhinavabhāratī ṭīkā). Current_author omits the commentator entirely.

texts.title: Acintyastava
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Lindtner, Nagarjuniana 1982; input R. Mahoney): attributed to Nāgārjuna, colophon 'ity acintyastavaḥ samāptaḥ'. One of the four Catuḥstava hymns. Brunnhölzl (Luminous Heart) calls its authorship 'undisputed'; Lindtner places the Catuḥstava among Nāgārjuna's genuine works. However, authenticity of the Catuḥstava as a group is debated in scholarship (questions of authenticity persist; some hymns doubted). Acintyastava & Lokātītastava are the most widely accepted as genuine.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Luminous Heart. Third Karmapa..._Karl Brunnholzl, 2009.txt; web:https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF00208526
texts.note: Attributed to Nāgārjuna; among the more securely accepted Catuḥstava hymns, but the group's authenticity is debated. Status kept as disputed per current_author.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 4 (with Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (SANSKNET project input): mūla Brahmasūtra by Bādarāyaṇa, bhāṣya (Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya) by Śaṅkara — two distinct authors. Both attributions traditional/secure within Vedānta. NB: the fetched GRETIL HTML transformation displayed adhyāya 1, though item targets adhyāya 4.
texts.note: Two-author work: Bādarāyaṇa (sūtra, traditionally identified with Vyāsa) + Śaṅkara (bhāṣya). Already correctly split in current_author; conflation_flag set since one title bundles both.

texts.title: Dharmadhātustava
texts.basis: No source_url. Tibetan tradition (Karmapa, Brunnhölzl) cites it consistently as 'Nāgārjuna's Dharmadhātustava'. Web (Zhen Liu 2015 edition / Liu 2012) shows attribution to Nāgārjuna only since 11th c.; modern scholarship disputes it on doctrinal grounds (strong Tathāgatagarbha influence) and Liu shows it was not thought Nāgārjuna's before mid-8th c.
texts.note: Traditional Madhyamaka ascription to Nāgārjuna; modern scholarship questions it (Tathāgatagarbha content); not attributed to Nāgārjuna before mid-8th c. per Zhen Liu.

texts.title: Aśokāvadāna
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Mukhopadhyaya 1963 ed., DSBC input) names no author; it is an anonymous narrative legend, the Aśoka cycle preserved as Avadāna 29 of the Divyāvadāna. Secondary lit (Rotman, Divine Stories) treats it as an anonymous compiled avadāna with no individual author.
texts.note: Anonymous compilation; no author named in header or colophon. Part of the Divyāvadāna corpus.

texts.title: Prasādapratibhodbhava [Śatapañcāśatka]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Shackleton Bailey 1951 ed., input Hartmann) names Mātṛceṭa. Secondary lit: Nance diss confirms author Mātṛceṭa (2nd-3rd c.); the Sanskrit colophon (Sāṃkṛtyāyana ms.) attributes it to Mātṛceṭa, while the Tibetan translation INcorrectly attributes it to Aśvaghoṣa.
texts.note: Secure as Mātṛceṭa per Sanskrit colophon; Tibetan tradition misattributes to Aśvaghoṣa. Title also debated (Prasādapratibhodbhava / Śatapañcāśatka).

texts.title: Kalpanāmaṇḍitikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Lüders 1926 ed., input Wille) attributes to Kumāralāta; Kizil ms. colophons read 'āryakaukāmāralātāyāṃ kalpanāmaṇḍitikāyāṃ' (the noble Taxilan monk Kumāralāta). Secondary lit: Loukota diss and Nakamura both note the Chinese tradition (Kumārajīva's tr., T201) ascribed it to Aśvaghoṣa as Sūtrālaṃkāra; Lüders (1926) established Kumāralāta authorship from the colophons; Lévi (1927) saw it as Kumāralāta's reworking of an Aśvaghoṣa work, now discounted.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Loukota_The Goods that Cannot Be Stolen; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism_Nakamura 1980
texts.note: Secure as Kumāralāta (colophons). Historically misattributed to Aśvaghoṣa in the Chinese tradition (as 'Sūtrālaṃkāra'); true title Dṛṣṭāntapaṅkti/Kalpanāmaṇḍitikā.

texts.title: Mīmāṃsāsūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (B.D. Basu ed., SBH vol. 27; Sansknet input) names Jaimini as author; no translator named in the e-text though the edition includes Mohan Lal Sandal's translation. Secondary lit uniformly refers to the work as 'Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra(s)', the foundational sūtra of Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā ascribed to the eponymous Jaimini.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Jaimini, the system's eponymous founder, as is normal for a sūtra collection. Translator Mohan Lal Sandal is a distinct (modern) role per the edition, not in the e-text.

texts.title: Anaparāddhastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Hartmann 1988 ed., compiled by Hartmann from Lévi 1929, Schlingloff 1955, etc.) and the colophon in the text explicitly read 'anaparāddhastotraṃ samāptaṃ kṛtir ācāryasthaviramātṛceṭasya' (the work of ācārya sthavira Mātṛceṭa). Secondary lit corroborates Mātṛceṭa as a major Buddhist stotra author (often discussed alongside Aśvaghoṣa fragments).
texts.note: Secure; colophon explicitly names ācārya sthavira Mātṛceṭa. Edition is a modern compilation of Ostturkistan fragments by Hartmann.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1 (with Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Bādarāyaṇa: Brahmasūtra with Śaṃkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya'; file contains both root sūtras (BBs_1,1.x) and Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. Secondary (Bronkhorst, Mīmāṃsā and Vedānta 2007) discusses the authorship of the Brahmasūtra: traditionally Bādarāyaṇa, but Sureśvara even ascribed the sūtras to Jaimini, a view contradicted by Padmapāda — so the mūla authorship is traditional, not secure. Śaṅkara's bhāṣya is securely attributed (with later commentaries Bhāmatī of Vācaspati, Ratnaprabhā of Govindānanda etc.).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Mimamsa and Vedanta. Interaction and Continuity_Johannes Bronkhorst, ed., 2007.txt
texts.note: Two distinct authors: sūtra (Bādarāyaṇa, traditional; ascription disputed already by Sureśvara/Jaimini) and bhāṣya (Śaṅkara, secure). This file = Adhyāya 1.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 3 (with Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya)
texts.basis: Same work/file as Adhyāya 1 (shared source_url): GRETIL header names Bādarāyaṇa for the sūtras and Śaṅkara for the Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya; both present in text. Bādarāyaṇa's authorship of the mūla is traditional and was contested in antiquity (Sureśvara ascribed it to Jaimini, per Bronkhorst 2007), while Śaṅkara's commentary is secure.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Mimamsa and Vedanta. Interaction and Continuity_Johannes Bronkhorst, ed., 2007.txt
texts.note: Two distinct authors: sūtra (Bādarāyaṇa, traditional) and bhāṣya (Śaṅkara, secure). This file = Adhyāya 3.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Aśvamedhikaparvan
texts.basis: BORI critical edition (Dandekar, gen. ed. Sukthankar, 1960) of one parvan of a composite, multi-layered epic with no single historical author; tradition ascribes the whole MBh to Vyāsa (Kṛṣṇa Dvaipāyana). Bendall's catalogue notes the Āśvamedhikaparvan circulated with Nīlakaṇṭha's commentary (a separate, later commentator, not part of this e-text). No basis to name a single composer.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Bendall_Catalogue of the Sanskrit manuscripts in the British museum.txt; tradition
texts.note: Composite epic stratum; legendary author Vyāsa. Effectively anonymous/collective. Nīlakaṇṭha's bhāṣya exists in mss tradition but is not in this root e-text.

texts.title: Rāmāyaṇa: 7. Uttarakāṇḍa
texts.current_author: Vālmīki (traditional); widely regarded as a later addition
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally Vālmīki; widely held a later addition)
texts.basis: Baroda critical edition (U.P. Shah 1975). The Uttarakāṇḍa is traditionally ascribed to Vālmīki but is widely regarded by scholars as a later addition to the original poem (along with the Bālakāṇḍa), i.e. not by the original poet of kāṇḍas 2–6; tradition itself (skt-en-mono note) preserves the Vālmīki legend. No identifiable individual author for this later stratum.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Vālmīki retained as legendary; scholarship treats book 7 as a later accretion, so the historical composer of this kāṇḍa is anonymous.

texts.title: Lokātītastava
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Lindtner, Nagarjuniana 1982; input R. Mahoney) attributes it to Nāgārjuna; colophon 'iti lokātītastavaḥ samāptaḥ'. Candrakīrti's Prasannapadā cites it as the ācārya's (Nāgārjuna). However, the authenticity of the Catuḥstava (Lokātīta-, Niraupamya-, Acintya-, Paramārtha-stava) is debated; some manuscripts/scholars question Nāgārjuna's authorship (cf. Tucci, Lindtner, Tsuda). Wayman (Buddhist Insight) and Hopkins (Meditation on Emptiness) quote it as Nāgārjuna's.
texts.note: One of the Catuḥstava hymns. Traditionally Nāgārjuna and cited as his by Candrakīrti, but Catuḥstava authenticity is scholarly disputed; single author, no conflation.

texts.title: Suhṛllekha
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'ācāryanāgārjunaviracitaḥ Suhṛllekhaḥ', based on Pema Tendzin's CIHTS 2002 edition; colophon 'iti suhṛllekha samāptā'. This e-text contains ONLY Nāgārjuna's root verses (the printed edition also has Mahāmati's Vyaktapadā ṭīkā, but that commentary is NOT in this GRETIL file). Tradition firmly ascribes the verse-epistle to Nāgārjuna; Hahn (Prajñāśataka 1990) notes the Suhṛllekha among Nāgārjuna's didactic works while observing that its same-authorship with the Ratnāvalī is not externally provable.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Hundert Strophen von der Lebensklugheit. Nagarjunas Prajnasataka_Michael Hahn, 1990.txt
texts.note: Root verses by Nāgārjuna (no conflation in this file; Mahāmati's ṭīkā present in the edition but excluded from the e-text). Strongly attributed by tradition though the broader 'which Nāgārjuna' question persists.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Mausalaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header (BORI crit. ed., Tokunaga/J.Smith) carries no human author; internal narration 'vaiśaṃpāyana uvāca'. Olivelle (Between the Empires, 2006) calls the Mausalaparvan 'one of the later portions of the epic'. Epic is anonymous-composite, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa.
texts.note: Traditional/legendary author Vyāsa, internal narrator Vaiśaṃpāyana; in reality an anonymous late stratum of a composite text. No distinct named multiple authors, so not flagged conflation.

texts.title: Daśabhūmikasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. P.L. Vaidya 1967, input DSBC): no human author; presented as a sūtra, teaching spoken by Vajragarbha Bodhisattva. Secondary refs distinguish the SŪTRA from later commentaries (Daśabhūmika-vibhāṣā/-śāstra ascribed to Nāgārjuna; Daśabhūmika-śāstra/vyākhyāna ascribed to Vasubandhu) — those are separate works, not the sūtra itself.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra; speaker Vajragarbha is internal, not author. Commentaries by Nāgārjuna/Vasubandhu are distinct texts (not this item).

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Āśramavāsikaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header (BORI crit. ed. vol.19, ed. Belvalkar; entered Tokunaga, rev. J.Smith): no human author; internal narration 'vaiśaṃpāyana uvāca'. Anonymous-composite epic traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa.
texts.note: Traditional/legendary author Vyāsa, internal narrator Vaiśaṃpāyana; anonymous-composite in fact. No distinct named multiple authors, so not flagged conflation.

texts.title: Daśabhūmīśvaro nāma mahāyānasūtraratnarājastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL '108 Buddhist Stotras' (based on J.S. Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha 1994, input DSBC): text no. 26 listed by title only, no author given. Title = praise (stotra/ratnarāja) of the Daśabhūmīśvara (=Daśabhūmika) sūtra; the Daśabhūmīśvara is itself an anonymous vaipulya sūtra (Saha 1966). Web search found no named author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Buddhism and Buddhist literature in central Asia_Saha 1966
texts.note: Anonymous; a stotra/extract praising the Daśabhūmīśvara-sūtra. No author in the edition or scholarship.

texts.title: Larger Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. S. Zacchetti 2005; input K. Wille; ms LPG = GBM 175-675): edition of the SŪTRA, no human author. Wikipedia/scholarship: anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra, an expanded recension of the Larger PP urtext (Watanabe). The Nāgārjuna attribution belongs to the COMMENTARY (Da zhidu lun/*Mahāprajñāpāramitopadeśa), disputed by Lamotte — not this sūtra.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra, composite/expanded text. Da zhidu lun commentary (ascribed Nāgārjuna, disputed) is a separate work, not flagged here.

texts.title: Abhidharmāmṛta
texts.basis: Edition: Sanskrit RECONSTRUCTION by Shanti Bhikshu Sastri (1953) from the Chinese (Abhidharmāmṛta-śāstra, T 1553); no Sanskrit original survives. Nakamura (Indian Buddhism 1980): 'The Abhidharmāmṛta-śāstra is ascribed to Bhadanta Ghoṣaka', a great Sarvāstivāda philosopher named in the Mahāvibhāṣā. Crystal Mirror (Tarthang Tulku 1991): Ghoṣaka = author of the Abhidharmāmṛta-śāstra.
texts.note: Attribution to Ghoṣaka is traditional/well-accepted ('ascribed to'), not strictly verified. The Sanskrit text is a modern reconstruction; original extant only in Chinese (T 1553). Added the reconstructor as translator role; hence changed=true.

texts.title: Harivaṃśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya BORI crit. ed.) names only editors/transliterators, no author. Secondary (Warder, Winternitz, Studholme) treats it as an anonymous khila/supplement to the Mahābhārata composed by an unknown poet; traditional epic ascription to Vyāsa is legendary.
texts.note: Anonymous supplement (khila) to the Mahābhārata; only legendary ascription to Vyāsa. No author named in colophon/header.

texts.title: Udānavarga
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Bernhard ed.) names no human author; verses are utterances of the Buddha compiled thematically into 29 vargas. Secondary (Nakamura: 'a legend that the Udānavarga was compiled by Dharmatrāta'; Dhammajoti; Puri) report only a traditional/legendary ascription to (Bhadanta) Dharmatrāta. Hirakawa notes several distinct persons named Dharmatrāta, so the identification is uncertain.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Sarvāstivāda anthology of canonical verses; compilation only legendarily ascribed to Dharmatrāta (a name borne by several persons). Traditional, not secure.

texts.title: Lalitavistara [Lalitavistarasūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header explicitly states anonymous original authorship; canonical Mahāyāna/Vaipulya sūtra; colophons end parivartas without naming an author. Secondary (Nariman: present redaction of an older Hīnayāna text; Kragh: 'anonymous Lalitavistara Sūtra') confirm anonymity with redactional history.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism_Nariman G K_1919.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Vaipulya sūtra; a redaction of an earlier (Hīnayāna) life-of-the-Buddha text. No human author or traditional named ascription; status sharpened to anonymous.

texts.title: Pañcatantra
texts.basis: GRETIL header repeats the prelude's claim that the brahmin Viṣṇuśarman composed the five tantras for the sons of King Amaraśakti. Secondary (Olivelle 1999; Dan Martin) note Viṣṇuśarman is the name given in the prelude/frame; the actual transmitted recensions are anonymous redactions (Schopen on later redactors), so the named author is essentially traditional/legendary.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Pancatantra. The Book of India's Folk Wisdom_Patrick Olivelle, 1999.txt
texts.note: Viṣṇuśarman is the frame-story author named in the prelude; historically the work is an anonymous, multiply-redacted compilation. Traditional ascription, not secure single authorship.

texts.title: Mūlamadhyamakakārikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya ed.) attributes the root verses to Nāgārjuna; this file contains only the kārikās (27 chapters), no commentary. Secondary (Nakamura, Warrick, Eckel) uniformly attribute the MMK to Nāgārjuna (2nd c.). Other works ascribed to 'Nāgārjuna' are disputed, but the MMK is his foundational, securely attributed text.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Wise One in the Prasannapada_M Warrick - 1983_OCR.txt
texts.note: Root verses only (no bhāṣya/ṭīkā in this file). Securely Nāgārjuna; commentaries (Akutobhayā, Prasannapadā, etc.) are separate works by others and not present here.

texts.title: Pañcatantra
texts.basis: Same GRETIL source file (vispancu) as SA_GK22_vispancu_e; header repeats prelude claim that Viṣṇuśarman composed the five tantras. Secondary (Olivelle 1999; Dan Martin; Schopen) treat Viṣṇuśarman as the prelude/frame author and the transmitted text as an anonymous, redacted compilation.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Pancatantra. The Book of India's Folk Wisdom_Patrick Olivelle, 1999.txt
texts.note: Duplicate Pañcatantra item (same source_url). Viṣṇuśarman is a traditional/frame-narrative author; underlying recension is anonymous. Traditional attribution confirmed.

texts.title: Nyāyasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Gautama: Nyayasutra with Vacaspatimisra's Nyayavarttikatatparyatika', checked against Anantalal Thakur ed. 1996. Body interleaves the Nyāyasūtra (NyS_ markers) with Vācaspatimiśra's Tātparyaṭīkā (the dominant layer, header colophon 'vācaspatimiśraviracitāyāṃ nyāyavārttikatātparyaṭīkāyāṃ') which itself glosses Uddyotakara's Vārttika ('uddyotakareṇa', 'vārtikakṛt'). Secondary lit confirms the 4-layer stack: sūtra (Gautama) > bhāṣya (Vātsyāyana) > vārttika (Uddyotakara) > tātparyaṭīkā (Vācaspatimiśra, c.840-960 CE).
texts.note: current_author names only Gautama; the file actually conflates the sūtra with Vācaspatimiśra's Tātparyaṭīkā (and presupposes Uddyotakara's vārttika, Vātsyāyana's bhāṣya). Root sūtras securely traditional to Gautama; commentary securely Vācaspatimiśra.

texts.title: Vigrahavyāvartanīkārikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'Nagarjuna: Vigrahavyāvartanī-kārikā'; file is kārikā ONLY (71 verse units NagVvy_01-71, no vṛtti), Lindtner ed. 1982 (Nagarjuniana). Westerhoff (Dispeller of Disputes 2010, 'Question of Authenticity') reports VV is part of the Yukti-corpus, ascribed to Nāgārjuna by Sanskrit/Chinese/Tibetan colophons and by Bhāviveka, Candrakīrti, Śāntarakṣita; accepted as genuine by Lindtner and Ruegg. Doubt raised only by Tola & Dragonetti (1998), which Westerhoff rebuts.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Dispeller of Disputes. Nagarjunas Vigrahavyavartani_Jan Westerhoff, tr., 2010.txt
texts.note: Kārikā only (auto-vṛtti not in this file). Traditional/widely-accepted Nāgārjuna attribution; minority doubt (Tola-Dragonetti) noted but rejected by mainstream. A hypothesis that the first ~20 objection-verses are by an opponent (VV = two authors) is mentioned by Westerhoff but he rejects it; not treated as conflation here.

texts.title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā] 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'Nagarjuna: Ratnavali, 2', based on Tucci ed. (JRAS 1934/1936), input R. Mahoney. Ratnāvalī (Rājaparikathāratnāvalī) is a core member of the Yukti-corpus regularly ascribed to Nāgārjuna (Westerhoff 2010, citing Ruegg 1981; in some lists replaced by *Vyavahārasiddhi). Single-author verse epistle to a king; no commentary in this file.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Dispeller of Disputes. Nagarjunas Vigrahavyavartani_Jan Westerhoff, tr., 2010.txt
texts.note: Part 2 of the Ratnāvalī (Tucci edition divided the text). Securely-traditional Nāgārjuna attribution; no conflation. 'Ratnamālā' is an alternate title; Rājaparikathāratnāvalī / Rājaparikathāratnamālā.

texts.title: Vigrahavyāvartanī
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'Nagarjuna: Vigrahavyāvartanī', Vaidya ed. (Madhyamakaśāstra, BST 10, 1960, pp.277-295); file is kārikā ONLY (verses Vv_1-72, no vṛtti), ends 'iti //'. Same work as nagvvy_u, different edition. Yukti-corpus, ascribed to Nāgārjuna by colophons and by Bhāviveka/Candrakīrti/Śāntarakṣita; accepted by Lindtner, Ruegg; minority doubt (Tola-Dragonetti) rebutted by Westerhoff.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Dispeller of Disputes. Nagarjunas Vigrahavyavartani_Jan Westerhoff, tr., 2010.txt
texts.note: Vaidya-edition copy of the VV (kārikā only; the author's own svavṛtti is not in this file). Traditional Nāgārjuna; minority authenticity doubt noted but not adopted. Duplicate-work of SA_T04_nagvvy_u.

texts.title: Madhyamakaśāstra
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'Nagarjuna: Madhyamakasastra (Karika only!)', Vaidya ed. (BST 10, 1960). Explicitly kārikā only - NO Prasannapadā (Candrakīrti) and NO Akutobhayā. This is the Mūlamadhyamakakārikā, the foundational work taken as Nāgārjuna's by definition (Westerhoff 2010).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Dispeller of Disputes. Nagarjunas Vigrahavyavartani_Jan Westerhoff, tr., 2010.txt
texts.note: = Mūlamadhyamakakārikā, kārikā only (no commentary in this file, so no Candrakīrti/Buddhapālita/Bhāviveka layer). Secure Nāgārjuna. Header explicitly flags 'Karika only!', so no conflation.

texts.title: Niraupamyastava
texts.current_author: Nāgārjuna (traditionally attributed; part of the Catuḥstava)
texts.basis: source_url is a directory (file not directly fetched). Lindtner ed. 1982 (orig. Tucci JRAS 1932). Tucci (Minor Buddhist Texts I, 1956) and Lindtner identify the Niraupamyastava as the 2nd of the four hymns of the Catuḥstava (Lokātīta, Niraupamya, Acintya, Paramārtha) traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna; a verse-by-verse Sanskrit commentary (Catuḥstavasamāsārtha, by an unknown later pandit) confirms it as 2nd in sequence. Lindtner lists the Catuḥstava among Nāgārjuna's genuine works.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Minor Buddhist Texts. Part I _Giuseppe Tucci, 1956.txt; secondary:cleaned_Emptiness, the emptiness of emptiness..._Jeongeun Park, 2008.txt
texts.note: One of the four Catuḥstava hymns (the 2nd). Traditionally Nāgārjuna; authorship of the stava corpus is less certain than the kārikā corpus (only confidence medium). NOT a conflation, but note the separate Catuḥstavasamāsārtha commentary is by an anonymous later author (not part of this hymn file).

texts.title: Vigrahavyāvartanī
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Nāgārjuna (ed. Yonezawa); ms colophon names only the scribe Śrīdha(r)makīrti, not the author. Secondary lit (de Jong; Steinkellner) places VV in the small accepted core of genuine Nāgārjuna works; Bhattacharya ed. titled 'with the Author's Own Commentary'. The work comprises kārikās + Nāgārjuna's own svavṛtti (same author), so not a multi-author conflation.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Brief History of Buddhist Studies in Europe and America_J. M. de Jong, 1987.txt; secondary:cleaned_Nyingma School of Tibetan Buddhism...txt
texts.note: kārikā + svavṛtti both by Nāgārjuna; colophon names scribe Śrīdharmakīrti only. Authenticity widely accepted, hence 'traditional' rather than 'disputed'.

texts.title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā] 3
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Tucci ed.) names Nāgārjuna. Steinkellner: 'Nāgārjuna is the author of the Ratnāvalī - and, actually, we do not have serious reasons to doubt this'. Generally accepted as genuine Nāgārjuna (also called Rājaparikathāratnamālā). This item is one chapter-portion of the same Tucci edition.
texts.note: Chapter/book division of the Ratnāvalī; single author Nāgārjuna, authenticity widely accepted.

texts.title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā] 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Tucci ed., conv. R. Mahoney) names Nāgārjuna, Book 1. Steinkellner: no serious reasons to doubt Nāgārjuna's authorship. Same edition split by book.
texts.note: Book 1 of the Ratnāvalī; single author Nāgārjuna.

texts.title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā]
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC text (Vaidya ed., Buddhist Sanskrit Texts 10) attributes to Nāgārjuna. Steinkellner and general scholarly consensus accept Nāgārjuna's authorship of the Ratnāvalī (Rājaparikathāratnamālā). Single-author advice-poem to a king.
texts.note: Vaidya BST-10 version of the full Ratnāvalī; single author Nāgārjuna.

texts.title: Upāyahṛdaya
texts.primary_author: uncertain (Nāgārjuna ascription argued by Kajiyama; doubted by others)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Tucci reconstruction from Chinese) gives NO author. Secondary lit: Bronkhorst cites Kajiyama 'On the authorship of the Upāyahṛdaya'; Burton (Emptiness Appraised) notes 'Kajiyama has argued at length that the Upāyahṛdaya... is actually by Nāgārjuna', while 'there is evidence for both interpretations' (Bhattacharya doubts). Extant only in Chinese; a pre-Diṅnāga logic/debate manual.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Buddhistische Lehre_Johannes Bronkhorst, 2000.txt; secondary:cleaned_Emptiness Appraised A Critical Study of Nagarjunas Philosophy_David Burton 2001_OCR.txt
texts.note: Nāgārjuna attribution defended by Kajiyama but contested (Bhattacharya); no author in the text itself. Status: disputed.

texts.title: Yogasūtra
texts.primary_author: Patañjali (sūtra + bhāṣya as the Pātañjalayogaśāstra); 'Vyāsa' bhāṣya-ascription likely later/spurious
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Āgāśe, input Maas) presents the Ānandāśrama edition bundling Yogasūtra + Vyāsabhāṣya + Vācaspatimiśra's Tattvavaiśāradī ṭīkā + Bhoja's Rājamārtaṇḍa. The edition therefore conflates multiple authors/roles. Maas (in White, 'Yoga in Practice') argues sūtra + Bhāṣya form a single composition by a single author (the Pātañjalayogaśāstra, c.325-425 CE), implying the legendary 'Vyāsa' attribution of the bhāṣya is later/unreliable; Vācaspatimiśra (~9-10c) and Bhojadeva (11c) are distinct later commentators. Patañjali-the-grammarian identity is itself debated.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_White_Yoga in Practice.txt; secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse...txt; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 2 - Potter 1977.txt
texts.note: Edition conflates sūtra (Patañjali) + bhāṣya (trad. 'Vyāsa', per Maas same author as sūtra) + ṭīkās by Vācaspatimiśra and Bhojadeva — distinct authors, hence conflation_flag=true. 'Vyāsa' ascription pseudepigraphic/legendary.

texts.title: Śārdūlakarṇāvadāna
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Mūlasarvāstivāda avadāna; Avadāna 33 of the Divyāvadāna)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Mukhopadhyaya ed. 1954) identifies it as Avadāna 33 of the Divyāvadāna; no author/scribe colophon. The Divyāvadāna is an anonymous Mūlasarvāstivāda anthology of avadāna narratives with no single named author or compiler.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Divine Stories Divyavadana, Part 2_Andy Rotman, 2017.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist narrative; part of the Divyāvadāna collection. No traditional individual ascription; redactional origin in Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya tradition.

texts.title: Dramatic Fragments (Buddhist) [incl. Aśvaghoṣa's Śāriputraprakaraṇa]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Lüders, Bruchstücke buddhistischer Dramen, 1911; ed. Klaus Wille): the collection groups several dramatic manuscript fragments. The Śāriputraprakaraṇa is explicitly Aśvaghoṣa's per its colophon (SHT 57): 'āryyasuvarṇṇākṣiputrasyāryyāśvaghoṣasya kṛtiś śāradvatīputrapprakaraṇaṃ samāptaṃ'. Nariman (1919) confirms the nine-act drama colophon names Aśvaghoṣa unequivocally. Other fragments in the file are anonymous/unattributed dramas.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism_Nariman G K_1919.txt
texts.note: Title is a modern editorial collection conflating distinct works: Śāriputraprakaraṇa is securely Aśvaghoṣa (kṛti per colophon); accompanying allegorical/dramatic fragments are by unknown author(s).

texts.title: Mānava-Dharmaśāstra [Manusmṛti]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (typed by M. Yano & Y. Ikari): the e-text is the mūla Manusmṛti verses only (Kullūka's Manvartha-muktāvalī and Medhātithi cited only as collation sources for the edition, not included as commentary text). The work is pseudepigraphically/traditionally ascribed to the mythical lawgiver Manu; scholarly consensus treats it as an anonymous composite redacted (c. 2nd c. BCE–3rd c. CE).
texts.note: Mūla text only despite edition title naming Kullūka's commentary. Traditional ascription to Manu is legendary; no historical author. Kullūka Bhaṭṭa is the commentator (not present in this e-text).

texts.title: Yogasūtra [with Vyāsabhāṣya]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Ānandāśrama ed.; input M. Tokunaga): file is Yogasūtra together with the Yogasūtrabhāṣya (no Vācaspati tīkā in this file). Sūtras traditionally by Patañjali; bhāṣya traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. Modern scholarship (Maas 2006/2013) argues sūtra+bhāṣya are one work (Pātañjalayogaśāstra) by a single author Patañjali; separate Vyāsa authorship is first asserted only in the 14th-c. Sarvadarśanasaṅgraha.
texts.note: current_author 'Patañjali' omits the bhāṣya layer. Two-author transmission (Patañjali sūtra + Vyāsa bhāṣya) is the traditional model but its single-vs-double authorship is genuinely disputed (Maas).

texts.title: Yogasūtra with Bhāṣya [Pātañjalayogaśāstra]
texts.current_author: Patañjali (sūtras); Bhāṣya attributed to Vyāsa (or Patañjali himself)
texts.primary_author: Patañjali (sūtra + bhāṣya as Pātañjalayogaśāstra); bhāṣya traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa
texts.basis: GRETIL header explicitly labels this the 'Pātañjalayogaśāstra' (ed. Āgāśe, Ānandāśrama; input/proofread by Philipp A. Maas). Maas's edition embodies the thesis that sūtra+bhāṣya form one unified text by Patañjali, against the traditional ascription of the bhāṣya to Vyāsa. Colophons call the work Pātañjala-yogaśāstra-sāṃkhyapravacana. Bhāṣya-to-Vyāsa is the late (post-14th-c.) traditional view.
texts.note: Same Pātañjalayogaśāstra dispute. current_author already flags Vyāsa-vs-Patañjali; recording roles explicitly. Maas (editor of this e-text) argues single authorship (Patañjali); traditional view splits sūtra/Patañjali vs bhāṣya/Vyāsa.

texts.title: Yogasūtra
texts.primary_author: Patañjali (sūtra; constituted text by Ashok Aklujkar)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: mūla Yogasūtra (root aphorisms only, four pādas), constituted text by Ashok Aklujkar based on Kashi Sanskrit Series 85 with other eds consulted; commentaries (bhāṣya, vivaraṇa) only consulted, not printed. Sūtras securely attributed to Patañjali by tradition and scholarship.
texts.note: Mūla-only text; Patañjali authorship of the sūtras is secure/standard. No bhāṣya present, so no Vyāsa conflation here. Aklujkar is modern editor (text-constitution), noted as redactor.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Mahāprasthānikaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL: BORI critical edition (Bks 17-18), no human author claimed; this is book 17 of the epic. Secondary (skt-en-mono): the Mbh is a multi-layered composite 'composed at different points in time', traditionally remembered as composed by 'the son of Satyavati' (Vyāsa), an ascription the Naiyāyikas/Mīmāṃsakas debate; impossible to unbundle its layers.
texts.note: Single short parvan of a vast anonymous redacted epic; legendary ascription to Vyāsa is traditional, not historical. Anonymous in substance.

texts.title: Vyākaraṇamahābhāṣya [Mahābhāṣya]
texts.basis: GRETIL: Kielhorn/Abhyankar ed., 'Vyākaraṇa-Mahābhāṣya of Patañjali'. Secondary (Candragomin & the Bodhisattva Vow, Tatz): 'Patañjali composed the Mahābhāṣya'; widely cited as Patañjali's c. 2nd-cent-BCE grammar commentary.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Candragomin and the Bodhisattva Vow_Mark Joseph Tatz, 1978.txt
texts.note: The bhāṣya is Patañjali's own authored work. It embeds and discusses Pāṇini's sūtras and Kātyāyana's vārttikas (the 'munitraya' triad), but those are quoted/commented material, not co-authorship of this titled text; hence not flagged as conflation.

texts.title: Mahāsannipātaratnaketudhāraṇīsūtra [Ratnaketuparivarta, Ratnaketudhāraṇīsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL (Dutt/Gilgit ed.): presented as a Mahāyāna sūtra with no human author; speaker is the Buddha; chapter colophons read 'mahāyānasūtrād ratnaketu... parivarto nāma'. Secondary (Drewes; Brunnhölzl) and web (84000 toh138) treat it as an anonymous canonical Mahāyāna sūtra/dhāraṇī of the Mahāsannipāta collection.
texts.source: gretil-header; web:https://84000.co/translation/toh138; secondary:cleaned_Drewes_Mahayana Sutras in Recent Scholarship.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra (this ed. = parivartas 1-6, 10-11 from Gilgit mss). Buddhavacana convention; no traditional individual author.

texts.title: Chandoviciti
texts.primary_author: Mitradhara (per Turfan colophon); attribution to Patañjali unsupported
texts.basis: GRETIL (Schlingloff, Texte zur Sanskritmetrik, Turfan): text names its composer 'mitradhareṇa ... kṛtiḥ cchandovicitiḥ' (the Chandoviciti, the composition by Mitradhara). Web: this Turfan metrics manual (not later than c. 500 CE) is attributed in the ms to Mitradhara; elsewhere 'chandoviciti' is also a generic term for the vedāṅga of metrics and a title sometimes loosely linked to Daṇḍin/Patañjali. The 'Patañjali' ascription is not supported by this edition's text.
texts.source: gretil-header; web:https://gretil.sub.uni-goettingen.de/gretil/corpustei/transformations/html/sa_chandoviciti.htm; secondary:cleaned_Buddhism in Central Asia_B.N. Puri.txt
texts.note: Current 'attributed to Patañjali' is wrong/unsupported for this specific Turfan text; its own colophon names Mitradhara. Title also overlaps with the generic metrics discipline (chandoviciti = a vedāṅga), a source of confusion. Disputed.

texts.title: Mahāsannipātaratnaketudhāraṇīsūtra [Ratnaketuparivarta, Ratnaketudhāraṇīsūtra]
texts.basis: Same work as SA_K10_bsu024_u but Kurumiya 1978 edition (full Sanskrit text). Anonymous canonical Mahāyāna sūtra of the Mahāsannipāta; Buddhavacana, no human author. Secondary (Drewes lists Ratnaketuparivarta among Mahāyāna sūtras; Brunnhölzl cites Mahāsannipātaratnaketudhāraṇī).
texts.source: gretil-header; web:https://84000.co/translation/toh138; secondary:cleaned_Drewes_Mahayana Sutras in Recent Scholarship.txt
texts.note: Duplicate title of the Ratnaketu (Kurumiya complete ed.). Anonymous; no individual or traditional author.

texts.title: Yuktiṣaṣṭikākārikā
texts.basis: GRETIL (B. Kumar reconstruction): colophon 'āryanāgārjunamukhaniḥsṛtam śāstram idam' (this treatise issued from the mouth of noble Nāgārjuna). Secondary (Lopez, Study of Svatantrika; Hopkins): the Yuktiṣaṣṭikā is one of Nāgārjuna's Five Collections of Reasoning (yukti-corpus). The vṛtti is Candrakīrti's, but that is a separate work not in this edition.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Study of Svatantrika_Donald S Lopez, 2016.txt
texts.note: This edition = kārikās only (Nāgārjuna). Candrakīrti's Yuktiṣaṣṭikāvṛtti is a distinct commentary not included here; 'Sūryakīrti' in the digital header refers to the Tibetan translator, not the author. Securely Nāgārjuna.

texts.title: Śūnyatāsaptati
texts.basis: GRETIL header: author Nāgārjuna, ed. Sempa Dorje 1985 (DSBC). Colophon: 'śūnyatāsaptatervṛttiḥ ācāryanāgārjunapādakṛtā samāptā'. Secondary (Nakamura, Lindtner): 73 root kārikās securely Nāgārjuna's; the Śūnyatāsaptativṛtti is the so-called auto-commentary whose Nāgārjuna authorship is scholarly debated (Lindtner accepts it among genuine works; others doubt the svavṛtti).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Root verses = Nāgārjuna (secure). E-text presents verses with vṛtti colophon ascribing the vṛtti to Nāgārjuna; the auto-commentary's authenticity is disputed in scholarship. Peking ed. has 73 verses.

texts.title: Catuḥśatikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header opens 'Āryadevasya ... upalabdhagranthabhāgāḥ' (recovered text-portions of Āryadeva), ed. Bhagchandra Jain 1971 (DSBC). Secondary (Nakamura): Catuḥśataka = 400 kārikās, 16 chapters x 25, most important work of Āryadeva; commented by Candrakīrti (Catuḥśatakaṭīkā) and Dharmapāla. Sanskrit largely lost; H.P. Shastri recovered fragments mixed with Candrakīrti's commentary.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Author Āryadeva secure. This e-text = only the recovered Sanskrit fragments (upalabdhagranthabhāgāḥ), full text survives in Tibetan. Candrakīrti's ṭīkā is a separate commentary, not included here.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 8
texts.primary_author: anonymous/composite base text (trad. Vyāsa); commentaries by Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha, Baladeva
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Gaudiya Grantha Mandira): BhG ch.8 with the four commentaries of Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha and Baladeva. Base Gītā traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa but is anonymous/composite (part of MBh Bhīṣmaparvan). Secondary confirms commentators: Madhusūdana Sarasvatī (16c Advaita), Viśvanātha Cakravartin (Sātārthadarśinī, Gauḍīya), Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa (Gauḍīya ṭīkā), Śrīdhara Svāmin.
texts.note: Single file conflates the anonymous Gītā verses with four distinct commentators from different schools/centuries (Śrīdhara; Madhusūdana Sarasvatī 16c Advaita; Viśvanātha Cakravartin Gauḍīya; Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa Gauḍīya). Roles separated.

texts.title: Harivaṃśa (appendix)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: critical edition by P.L. Vaidya, BORI 1969; no explicit authorship statement. These are the khila/appendix (Appendix I) passages — supplementary material drawn from manuscript families (K, Ñ, V, B, D, T, G, M), i.e. modern editorial compilation of insertions, not a single authorial voice. Harivaṃśa traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa.
texts.note: Appendix/khila = composite manuscript-variant insertions, inherently multi-source/anonymous; traditional ascription to Vyāsa noted only as tradition. Critical compilation by Vaidya (editor/redactor, not author).

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Śāntiparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header: BORI critical edition, book 12 Śāntiparvan ed. S.K. Belvalkar; e-text by Tokunaga et al., rev. John Smith. Text invokes Vyāsa (Dvaipāyana) as source ('dvaipāyanauṣṭhapuṭaniḥsṛtam'), the traditional ascription. Scholarly consensus: MBh is a composite, multi-author/layered text; Śāntiparvan especially regarded as a late, heavily expanded didactic stratum.
texts.note: Anonymous/composite epic; traditional ascription to Vyāsa is legendary. Belvalkar = critical editor (redactor of the constituted text), not author. Śāntiparvan is itself an accreted didactic layer.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 5
texts.primary_author: anonymous/composite base text (trad. Vyāsa); commentaries by Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha, Baladeva
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Gaudiya Grantha Mandira): BhG ch.5 with the four commentaries of Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha and Baladeva; underlying Gītā text = Bhīṣmaparvan of MBh, ed. Belvalkar, BORI 1947 (per workitem edition note). Base Gītā anonymous/composite, traditionally Vyāsa. Same four distinct commentators as ch.8.
texts.note: Same conflation as BhG 8: anonymous Gītā verses + four distinct commentators (Śrīdhara; Madhusūdana Sarasvatī; Viśvanātha Cakravartin; Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa) of different schools/dates. Roles separated.

texts.title: Triskandhasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (DSBC/Nagarjuna Institute input, ed. M.B. Shakya 2000) names no author/editor as composer; colophon only reads āryatriskandhamahāyānasūtraṃ samāptam with no personal name. Secondary lit (Pagel Bodhisattvapiṭaka, Lopez) treats it as an anonymous Mahāyāna confession sūtra (triskandhaka liturgy).
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra (āryatriskandha confession liturgy); buddhavacana by tradition, no individual author. Editor of e-text edition = M.B. Shakya (not the author).

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 7
texts.primary_author: anonymous/composite (mūla, trad. Vyāsa); with commentaries by Śrīdhara Svāmin, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Bhagavadgita 7 with the commentaries of Sridhara, Madhusudana, Visvanatha and Baladeva' (Gaudiya Grantha Mandira e-text); content interleaves four named commentaries. Secondary lit confirms distinct works: Śrīdhara's Subodhinī, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī's Gūḍhārthadīpikā (16th c. Advaitin), Viśvanātha Cakravartin's ṭīkā/Sārārthavarṣiṇī (17th c.), Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa's Bhagavadgītābhāṣya (18th c.). Mūla Gītā = part of MBh Bhīṣmaparvan, anonymous/composite, traditionally Vyāsa.
texts.note: Single title conflates the anonymous mūla Gītā (trad. Vyāsa) with FOUR distinct commentators across Advaita and Gauḍīya/Vaiṣṇava traditions (16th-18th c.). Separate the mūla from each commentary.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 4
texts.primary_author: anonymous/composite (mūla, trad. Vyāsa); with commentaries by Śrīdhara Svāmin, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: Same edition as bhg4c07u (Gaudiya Grantha Mandira; commentaries of Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha, Baladeva). GRETIL header names all four commentators; content interleaves them. Secondary lit (Lit. about Philosophical Systems; Freschi & Maas) attests each as a distinct author and work. Mūla Gītā anonymous/composite (MBh Bhīṣmaparvan), trad. Vyāsa.
texts.note: Conflates anonymous mūla Gītā (trad. Vyāsa) + four distinct commentators (Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī 16th c., Viśvanātha Cakravartin 17th c., Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa 18th c.).

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Svargārohaṇaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header (BORI critical ed., e-text Muneo Tokunaga et al., rev. John Smith): internal narrative ascribes composition to Vyāsa ('maharṣir bhagavān vyāsaḥ kṛtvemāṃ saṃhitāṃ purā' 18,005.046; 'kṛṣṇadvaipāyanaḥ prabhuḥ ... saṃdarbhaṃ bhāratasya' 18,005.041), transmitted by Vaiśaṃpāyana. No personal author colophon; scholarly consensus = anonymous, layered/composite epic with traditional Vyāsa ascription.
texts.note: Book 18 of the MBh. Traditional ascription to Vyāsa stated within the text itself; actually anonymous/composite. Tokunaga/Smith are e-text encoders, not authors.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 9
texts.primary_author: anonymous/composite (mūla, trad. Vyāsa); with commentaries by Śrīdhara Svāmin, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: Same Gaudiya Grantha Mandira edition with commentaries of Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha, Baladeva; GRETIL header names the four commentators, content interleaves them. Secondary lit confirms each as a distinct historical author/work. Mūla Gītā anonymous/composite (MBh Bhīṣmaparvan), trad. Vyāsa.
texts.note: Conflates anonymous mūla Gītā (trad. Vyāsa) + four distinct commentators (Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa).

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 6
texts.primary_author: anonymous/composite (mūla, trad. Vyāsa); with commentaries by Śrīdhara Svāmin, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: Same Gaudiya Grantha Mandira edition (commentaries of Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha, Baladeva); GRETIL header names the four commentators; content interleaves them. Secondary lit attests each as a distinct author/work. Mūla Gītā anonymous/composite (MBh Bhīṣmaparvan), trad. Vyāsa.
texts.note: Conflates anonymous mūla Gītā (trad. Vyāsa) + four distinct commentators (Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa).

texts.title: Yogācārabhūmi: Śrāvakabhūmi
texts.primary_author: Asaṅga (traditional, as compiler/author of the Yogācārabhūmi)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: modern critical ed. (Śrāvakabhūmi Study Group; conv. Klaus Wille), no traditional colophon. Secondary lit (Nakamura; Davidson/esoteric survey; Pagel): Śrāvakabhūmi is a section of the encyclopedic Yogācārabhūmi traditionally ascribed to Asaṅga, but Chinese tradition ascribes the whole to Maitreya, and Schmithausen (1969) argues it is a composite multi-stratum text not by a single hand.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism.txt
texts.note: Traditional Asaṅga attribution retained but flagged: Yogācārabhūmi is composite (Maitreya ascription in Chinese canon; Asaṅga as compiler/redactor; layered strata per Schmithausen). Edition is a modern Study Group reconstruction, no medieval colophon.

texts.title: Yogācārabhūmi: Śrāvakabhūmi
texts.primary_author: Asaṅga (traditional, as compiler/author of the Yogācārabhūmi)
texts.basis: Same text as srabhu_u but Shukla (1973) edition, titled 'Śrāvakabhūmi of Ācārya Asaṅga'. Edition title asserts Asaṅga authorship; secondary lit treats Yogācārabhūmi as composite (Maitreya/Asaṅga/layered redaction, Schmithausen).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Duplicate Śrāvakabhūmi (Shukla ed.). Edition title names Asaṅga, but composite/multi-stratum status and rival Maitreya ascription mean attribution is disputed, not secure.

texts.title: Pāṃśupradāna-avadāna
texts.basis: GRETIL header: diplomatic ed. from Gilgit Buddhist Manuscripts (Raghu Vira & Lokesh Chandra), transliteration Klaus Wille, cf. Divyāvadāna (Cowell & Neil); no author/scribe colophon. Secondary lit (Strong; Deeg): Pāṃśupradāna-avadāna is the first story of the Aśoka-cycle of the Divyāvadāna, an anonymous compiled avadāna.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Deeg_Legend and cult.txt; secondary:cleaned_Translation and Study of the Purnavadana_Tatelman, 1988.txt
texts.note: Anonymous; opening 'Avadāna of the Donation of Dirt' of the Aśoka-cycle within the Divyāvadāna / Mūlasarvāstivāda corpus. No personal author.

texts.title: Bhaiṣajyavastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: from Vinayavastvāgama of the Mūlasarvāstivādin (Vastu 6), ed. N. Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts vol. III, input Klaus Wille; no author colophon. Secondary lit (Schopen; Prebish, Survey of Vinaya Literature): Bhaiṣajyavastu is a section of the corporately compiled Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Survey of Vinaya Litterature_Prebish, 1994.txt; secondary:Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks_Schopen, 1997.txt
texts.note: Anonymous; a vastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, compiled corporately within the saṅgha, no individual author.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 2 (with Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya)
texts.basis: GRETIL header confirms two layers: mūla sūtra by Bādarāyaṇa and bhāṣya (Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya) by Śaṅkara; input by Sansknet Project. (Header legacy label says 'Adhyāya 1', a file-header artifact; item title is Adhyāya 2; both are the same mūla+bhāṣya pairing.)
texts.note: Two distinct authors: root sūtra Bādarāyaṇa (traditional Vedānta-sūtra author), commentary Śaṅkara. Bādarāyaṇa's authorship is itself traditional; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya authorship secure. Correctly conflated.

texts.title: Meghasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL source URL 404 (file moved); edition is Cecil Bendall, 'The Megha-Sūtra', JRAS n.s. 12 (1880). Web (Bendall/JRAS; ancient-buddhist-texts.net): Mahāmegha(sūtra) is an anonymous Mahāyāna/dhāraṇī sūtra (64 parivartas), translated into Chinese 397-439, no named author.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna 'Great Cloud' sūtra; Buddhavacana by tradition, no personal author. GRETIL URL returned 404.

texts.title: Vinayaviniścaya Upāliparipṛcchā [Vinayaviniścaya upāliparipṛcchā sūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Vinayaviniścaya upāliparipṛcchā', ed. Nalinaksha Dutt (Bodhisattva Prātimokṣa Sūtra 1931); no author named, colophon 'iti bodhisattvaprātimokṣāḥ' only. Secondary (Pagel, Bodhisattvapitaka; Python, Vinayaviniscayasutra) treats it as a canonical Mahāyāna sūtra of collective/anonymous authorship, framed as the Buddha answering Upāli.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Bodhisattvapitaka.  Its doctrines, practices and their position in Mahayana literature_Ulrich Pagel, 1992.txt
texts.note: Buddhavacana sūtra; no human author. Nalinaksha Dutt is the modern editor, not author.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā
texts.primary_author: anonymous/composite (traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa as part of the Mahābhārata)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: constituted text from BORI critical edition of the Mahābhārata (Bhīṣmaparvan, Belvalkar), entered by Tokunaga et al., revised by John Smith; no individual author. The Gītā is an embedded section of the Mbh, traditionally ascribed to the legendary Vyāsa; modern scholarship treats it as anonymous/composite epic material.
texts.note: Vyāsa is a traditional/legendary ascription for the whole Mahābhārata; the Gītā itself is anonymous composite verse. Belvalkar/Smith are editors only.

texts.title: Prajñāpāramitāstotra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Rāhulabhadra: Prajñāpāramitāstotra', ed. Michael Hahn 1988, input Hartmann; attribution consistent throughout. Secondary (Conze, Buddhist Scriptures; Hookham; Leaman) routinely cite the stotra as Rāhulabhadra's; this is his best-known work. Tradition conflates Rāhulabhadra with the siddha Saraha and links him to Nāgārjuna's circle (Joshi; Willson), but the stotra attribution itself is stable.
texts.note: Attribution to Rāhulabhadra secure in the textual tradition; only his identity/dates (= Saraha?) and his floruit are debated, not authorship of this stotra.

texts.title: Nyāyasūtra + Bhāṣya
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Gautama: Nyāyasūtra, with Vātsyāyana's Nyāyabhāṣya', ed. Taranatha Nyaya-Tarkatirtha et al., input Univ. of Tokyo Indology Student Team. Two distinct authors in one e-text: the root sūtras ascribed to Gautama/Akṣapāda (traditional founder of Nyāya) and the bhāṣya to Vātsyāyana (Pakṣilasvāmin), centuries later. Standard sūtra+bhāṣya layering by different persons.
texts.note: One title conflates root-sūtra author (Gautama/Akṣapāda, traditional) and commentator (Vātsyāyana). current_author already separates the roles; flagged conflation. Gautama himself is a traditional/legendary attribution.

texts.title: Pratītyasamutpādahṛdayavyākhyāna
texts.primary_author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (disputed; probable author Śuddhamati)
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC header and colophon claim 'ācārya nāgārjunakṛtam' / 'ācārya nāgārjunakṛtaṃ pratītyasamutpādahṛdayavyākhyānaṃ samāptam'. But secondary literature disputes this: Dragonetti ("...kārikā and vyākhyāna attributed to Nāgārjuna") and Lusthaus state 'the probable author was Śuddhamati'; Potter's Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophy notes 'this Nāgārjuna's authorship of the present text has been controverted'; Scherrer-Schaub/Gokhale debate authenticity (only 5 of 7 kārikās 'authentic'). The vyākhyāna is the auto-commentary on the kārikā; both go to the same (disputed) author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 8  Buddhist Philosophy 100 to 350 A.D_Karl H. Potter (1999).txt; secondary:cleaned_Philosophical investigation of the Ch’eng wei-shih lun ..._Dan Lusthaus, 1989.txt
texts.note: Colophon ascribes to Nāgārjuna, but attribution is controverted; Dragonetti/Lusthaus propose Śuddhamati. Treated as disputed/pseudepigraphic rather than secure. Kārikā+vyākhyāna are one author's work (auto-commentary), so not a multi-author conflation.

texts.title: Catuṣpariṣatsūtra
texts.basis: No source_url; edition Ernst Waldschmidt, Das Catuṣpariṣatsūtra (3 vols, 1952-62), a critical reconstruction from Turfan/Central Asian Sanskrit fragments. Secondary (Nakamura, Indian Buddhism; Gethin; Frauwallner) treats it as a canonical sūtra forming part of the (Mūla)Sarvāstivāda Vinaya, on the founding of the Buddhist order — buddhavacana, anonymous collective authorship, no named author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Earliest Vinaya and the Beginnings of Buddhist Literature_Erich Frauwallner_1956.txt
texts.note: Canonical sūtra, part of Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya; no human author. Waldschmidt is the modern critical editor/reconstructor from Turfan manuscripts, not author.

texts.title: Svastyayanagāthā
texts.basis: DSBC e-text gives no author (only project input). Secondary: Eimer/Germano 'Many Canons of Tibetan Buddhism 2' lists 'Svastyayana-gāthā' (Tib. bde legs su gyur pa'i tshigs su bcad pa) among canonical benedictory verse texts with no named author; the term svastyayana denotes auspicious/blessing rites (Bhattacharyya, Sutherland). No author attested in any source.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical benedictory verse-text (svastyayana = rite of welfare/blessing); transmitted in Tibetan canon without authorial ascription. DSBC source_url was only the project homepage; direct page 404.

texts.title: Bhavasaṅkrāntisūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC colophon: 'āryabhavasaṅkrāntir nāma mahāyānasūtraṃ saṃpūrṇam' — anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra, no author. Secondary: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (p.6037f) treats Bhavasaṃkrānti-sūtra as a sūtra (Chinese 大乗破有論), distinct from the Bhavasaṃkrānti-śāstra. The accompanying śāstra (NOT this item) is attributed to Nāgārjuna (Ramanan; Aiyaswami Shastri ed. title 'Nāgārjuna's Bhavasaṅkrānti Śāstra').
texts.note: The SŪTRA itself is anonymous canonical scripture. NB the cited edition (Aiyaswami Shastri 1938) bundles the anonymous sūtra WITH Nāgārjuna's separate Bhavasaṅkrānti-śāstra — that edition-title conflates two distinct authorial entities, but this work-item is the sūtra (anonymous), so no work-internal conflation.

texts.title: Bhikṣuṇīvinaya
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Gustav Roth 1970 (Tibetan Sanskrit Works 12); no author, school = Ārya-Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravādin. Secondary: Edith Nolot, Règles de discipline des nonnes bouddhistes (1991) explicitly states the Lokottaravādin Bhikṣuṇīvinaya 'obviously does not' form 'a datable harmonious whole' — i.e. a composite, anonymous monastic compilation. Hirakawa 1982 likewise treats it as collective school literature.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Regles de Discipline des Nonnes Bouddhistes_Edith Nolot, 1991.txt
texts.note: Anonymous composite Vinaya of the Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda; no individual author — collective/redactional school text. School ascription confirmed; not single-authored.

texts.title: Daśasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed./restoration Sten Konow 1941 (first two chapters), input K. Wille; no author stated. Prajñāpāramitā sūtra literature — anonymous Mahāyāna scripture. Secondary references treat it as one of several Prajñāpāramitā recensions/versions, never single-authored.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Prajñāpāramitā sūtra (10,000-line version); Konow's text is a scholarly restoration of chs. 1-2, no authorial ascription.

texts.title: Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-4 [Śatasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Takayasu Kimura 2014, input K. Wille; no author. The Śatasāhasrikā (100,000-line Perfection of Wisdom) is anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra literature; this is editorial section II-4 of Kimura's edition.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Prajñāpāramitā sūtra; 'II-4' is Kimura's editorial volume division, not an authorship distinction.

texts.title: Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-3 [Śatasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Takayasu Kimura 2010, input K. Wille; no author. Anonymous Mahāyāna Prajñāpāramitā sūtra (100,000-line); section II-3 of Kimura's edition.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Prajñāpāramitā sūtra; 'II-3' is an editorial volume division (Kimura), not an authorial part.

texts.title: Mahābhārata: Anuśāsanaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header (BORI critical ed., Dandekar) names editor only, no individual author. Secondary lit (Bronkhorst 2011) calls the Anuśāsanaparvan 'commonly regarded a late portion' of the epic; Olivelle notes such books are 'very late additions'. Traditional pan-epic ascription to Vyāsa is legendary, not historical.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Buddhism in the Shadow of Brahmanism_Johannes Bronkhorst, 2011.txt; gretil-header
texts.note: Anonymous oral-composite epic book; late stratum of the MBh. Vyāsa ascription is traditional/legendary only.

texts.title: Saṅghabhedavastu 2
texts.basis: GRETIL/Gnoli edition: 17th and last section (vastu) of the Vinaya of the Mūlasarvāstivādin, from the Gilgit MS. No individual author; canonical monastic-corporate compilation. Schopen (Bones, Stones) treats the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya as 'a fairly late compilation' (Lamotte/Panglung).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks..._Gregory Schopen, 1997.txt; gretil-header
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Vinaya section; Gnoli is editor, not author.

texts.title: Vaiśeṣikasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet input) identifies the text as the Vaiśeṣikasūtra of Kaṇāda (cited 'KVs'); this e-text is the sūtra text only, NOT Candrānanda's Vṛtti named in the edition citation.
