Secondary lit (Ganeri 2001; Tillemans 2000; Jacobsen 2012) uniformly ascribes the sūtras to Kaṇāda (sage name, also Kaśyapa/Ulūka), traditionally dated 2nd c. BCE–2nd c. CE.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Philosophy in Classical India_Jonardon Ganeri, 2001.txt; gretil-header
texts.note: Authorship by 'Kaṇāda' is a traditional sage-ascription (no independent biography). NB: GRETIL e-text contains only the sūtras, not Candrānanda's commentary cited in the edition string.

texts.title: Yogācārabhūmi: Laukikamārga
texts.primary_author: Asaṅga (traditional redactor); composite work of multiple authors
texts.basis: GRETIL source_url actually points to the Śrāvakabhūmi e-text (srabhusu.htm, Shukla ed.), a sub-section of the YBh. Schmithausen (1969:812, via 'three natures and three phases theory') presents strong arguments that the Yogācārabhūmi as a whole is 'the work of a number of authors over a period of time'; Asaṅga 'may have been the final redactor' rather than sole author. Chinese tradition (Paramārtha, 506 CE) ascribes parts of the Yogācāra corpus to Maitreya. Davidson likewise notes the layered authorship.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_three natures and three phases theory.txt; gretil-header
texts.note: Composite/stratified text: traditional Asaṅga ascription overstates single authorship. Multiple anonymous Yogācāra hands over time; Asaṅga as final redactor; legendary Maitreya ascription for parts. Flagged as conflated.

texts.title: Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL/Vaidya ed. in Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha; a Prajñāpāramitā sūtra with no human author, traditionally buddhavacana. Secondary lit references concern later commentaries (Maitreya-ascribed kārikās with Vasubandhu/Asaṅga bhāṣya, T#1511/T#235) — those are SEPARATE works, not this root sūtra.
texts.note: Root sūtra is anonymous. Do not conflate with the (separately authored) Vajracchedikā commentary kārikās ascribed to Maitreya/Asaṅga with Vasubandhu's bhāṣya — those are distinct texts.

texts.title: Bhikṣuṇīvinaya
texts.basis: GRETIL/Roth edition: Bhikṣuṇī-Vinaya of the Ārya-Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravādin, incl. Bhikṣuṇī-Prakīrṇaka. Canonical monastic-corporate text, no individual author; Roth is editor. Nakamura (1980) and Nolot (1991, French tr.) treat it as a school recension with no known author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism..._Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Regles de Discipline des Nonnes Bouddhistes_Edith Nolot, 1991.txt; gretil-header
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Vinaya of a specific school recension; Gustav Roth is the editor, not author.

texts.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā I [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editor Takayasu Kimura and transcriber Klaus Wille; no author/scribe colophon in text. Web/scholarship: Mahāyāna Prajñāpāramitā sūtras are anonymous compilations, no known composer.
texts.note: Anonymous Prajñāpāramitā sūtra; traditionally buddhavacana. Modern critical ed. Kimura 2007/2009. No individual author; no conflation (single sūtra text, not root+commentary).

texts.title: Bhikṣukarmavākya
texts.basis: GRETIL header names editor A.C. Banerjee (IHQ 1949) and SBC Input Project; colophon reads only 'samāptaṃ karmavākyam' with no author/scribe. A liturgical Vinaya karmavācanā formulary, not an authored work.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist monastic ordination ritual text (karmavācanā); no composer named. Ed. Banerjee. No conflation.

texts.title: Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-2 [Śatasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editor Takayasu Kimura (Tokyo 2010) and transcriber K. Wille; no internal author/scribe colophon. Largest Prajñāpāramitā sūtra, anonymous Mahāyāna compilation.
texts.note: Anonymous Prajñāpāramitā sūtra; traditionally buddhavacana. No individual author; no conflation.

texts.title: Mahāvadānasūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous ((Mūla)Sarvāstivāda Dīrghāgama sūtra; buddhavacana by tradition)
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editor Takamichi Fukita (Göttingen 2003), a reconstruction from Turfan/N. Turkestan manuscripts; no author/scribe colophon. Web: belongs to the Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda Dīrghāgama, an anonymous canonical āgama sūtra.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical sūtra (the 'Great Discourse on the Lineage', on the seven past buddhas), Sarvāstivāda Dīrghāgama. Critical reconstruction by Fukita. No individual author; no conflation.

texts.title: Suvikrāntavikrāmiparipṛcchā [Suvikrāntavikrāmiparipṛcchāprajñāpāramitāsūtra, Sārdhadvisāhasrikā prajñāpāramitā]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Mahāyāna Prajñāpāramitā sūtra; buddhavacana by tradition)
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editor P.L. Vaidya (Mithila Institute 1961) and SBC Input Project; no author/scribe colophon. The text is a dialogue sūtra (Buddha and bodhisattva Suvikrāntavikrāmin), an anonymous shorter Prajñāpāramitā.
texts.note: Anonymous Prajñāpāramitā sūtra (a.k.a. Sārdhadvisāhasrikā); traditionally buddhavacana. No individual author; no conflation.

texts.title: Sarvajñatākāradhāraṇī [Karuṇāpuṇḍarīka(sūtra)]
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editor Isshi Yamada (SOAS 1968) and SBC Input Project; no internal author/scribe colophon. Web: Karuṇāpuṇḍarīka ('White Lotus of Compassion') is an anonymous Mahāyāna scripture (poss. ca. 3rd c. CE); Mahāyāna sūtras are effectively anonymous, no known composer.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra; traditionally buddhavacana. The Sarvajñatākāradhāraṇī is an embedded dhāraṇī section within the Karuṇāpuṇḍarīka. No individual author; no conflation.

texts.title: Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-1 [Śatasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only the modern editor Takayasu Kimura (Tokyo 2009) and digitizer Klaus Wille; no author/colophon attribution. Prajñāpāramitā sūtras are anonymous canonical Mahāyāna scripture (buddhavacana), with a long anonymous redactional history (100,000-line recension).
texts.note: Sūtra, not an authored work; presented as buddhavacana. Editor Kimura is editorial role only, not author.

texts.title: Mahāvadānasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editor Takamichi Fukita (Göttingen 2003, based on Turfan/N. Turkestan MSS); no author or colophon attribution. The Mahāvadānasūtra is a canonical sūtra (Skt parallel to Pali Mahāpadānasutta, DN 14) on the seven past Buddhas; anonymous buddhavacana transmitted in the (Mūla)sarvāstivāda Dīrghāgama.
texts.note: Reconstructed from Turfan manuscripts; complex anonymous transmission, no single author. Fukita is editor only.

texts.title: Bhadracaryādeśanā
texts.primary_author: anonymous; traditionally ascribed to (the bodhisattva) Samantabhadra
texts.basis: GRETIL page (n2971u) returned 404, so header not directly read; edition by K. Watanabe (1912) and P. L. Vaidya (Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha I, BST 17, 1961). Web/secondary (Encyclopedia of Buddhism; Britannica; Hamar on Buddhāvataṃsaka): the Bhadracarī(praṇidhāna) = Āryasamantabhadracaryāpraṇidhānarāja, the closing verses of the Gaṇḍavyūha/Buddhāvataṃsaka, where they are recited by the bodhisattva Samantabhadra. No human author; anonymous canonical Mahāyāna verse, very widely transmitted as an independent praṇidhāna/dhāraṇī.
texts.note: Traditional in-text speaker is Samantabhadra (legendary, not historical authorship). Effectively anonymous. Title also = Bhadracarīpraṇidhāna; concludes Gaṇḍavyūha-sūtra.

texts.title: Dūtavākya
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives no in-text author (editions Ramachandra Iyer 1981, Devadhar 1962; e-text Y. Codet). One of the 13 'Trivandrum plays' published by T. Ganapati Sastri 1912 — none of which names an author in the body; ascription to Bhāsa rests on external grounds and is the long-debated 'Bhāsa problem' (alternatives proposed: Śaktibhadra, a Pallava/Pāṇḍya king; possibly multiple hands / later redaction). Majority opinion accepts Bhāsa but evidence is inconclusive.
texts.note: Current_author 'Bhāsa' stated flatly; status is in fact disputed/traditional (Trivandrum-plays authorship problem). Single attributed author, so no conflation.

texts.title: Svapnavāsavadatta
texts.basis: GRETIL header (e-text M. Ahlborn) gives no author; colophon only 'iti svapnanāṭakam avasitam'. The flagship Trivandrum play (Ganapati Sastri, Trivandrum SS 1912); the plays carry no author name internally, so ascription to Bhāsa is external and is the classic disputed 'Bhāsa problem'. Secondary lit (Thapar, Penguin History: 'dates for Bhasa are controversial') and wisdomlib confirm the attribution is the prevailing but unproven view.
texts.note: Best-known of the disputed Trivandrum plays; attribution traditional/disputed rather than secure. Single author, no conflation.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 4 (with Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī)
texts.current_author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Vācaspati Miśra (Bhāmatī sub-commentary); Śaṅkara (underlying bhāṣya)
texts.primary_author: Vācaspati Miśra (Bhāmatī ṭīkā); Śaṅkara (bhāṣya); Bādarāyaṇa (mūla sūtra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header explicitly describes a layered text: Bādarāyaṇa = composer of the Brahmasūtra (mūla); Śaṅkara = bhāṣyakāra (Brahmasūtrabhāṣya); Vācaspati Miśra = author of the Bhāmatī, a sub-commentary on Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. This single GRETIL title therefore conflates three distinct authors across three textual layers (sūtra / bhāṣya / ṭīkā). Edition Bakre/Pansikar, Nirnaya-Sagar 1934 (with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā and Ānandagiri's Nyāyanirṇaya present in the printed work).
texts.note: Genuine multi-author conflation (cf. Mahāyānasūtrālaṅkāra pattern): mūla Bādarāyaṇa, bhāṣya Śaṅkara, Bhāmatī Vācaspati Miśra. Current_author already lists the three roles; matches.

texts.title: Satyasiddhiśāstra [Tattvasiddhiśāstra]
texts.primary_author: Harivarman (original author); N. Aiyaswami Sastri (Sanskrit reconstruction from Chinese)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: author Harivarman, ed. N. Aiyaswami Sastri 1975. Secondary (Nakamura 1980, p.~2794): 'Satyasiddhi-śāstra by Harivarman, whose Sanskrit original is lost and only the Chinese translation is available.' Web (Britannica, Wikipedia Tattvasiddhi): no Indic ms survives; translated to Chinese by Kumārajīva 411; Sanskrit text RESTORED/retranslated from Kumārajīva's Chinese by N. Aiyaswami Sastri. So the e-text Sanskrit is a 20th-c. reconstruction, not the original.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt | web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tattvasiddhi | gretil-header
texts.note: Original composition by Harivarman (c. 250-350 CE) is secure as authorship; but the extant/edited SANSKRIT is a modern reconstruction by N. Aiyaswami Sastri from Kumārajīva's Chinese (original Sanskrit lost). Roles separated: author (Harivarman) vs translator (Kumārajīva) vs reconstructor/editor (Aiyaswami Sastri).

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 3 (with Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī)
texts.current_author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Vācaspati Miśra (Bhāmatī sub-commentary); Śaṅkara (underlying bhāṣya)
texts.basis: GRETIL header confirms the Bhāmatī, a commentary by Vācaspati Miśra on Śaṅkara's Brahmasūtrabhāṣya (which itself comments on Bādarāyaṇa's sūtras). Three-tier authorship: mūla-sūtra (Bādarāyaṇa, traditional), bhāṣya (Śaṅkara), sub-commentary Bhāmatī (Vācaspati Miśra).
texts.note: Three distinct authors across textual layers. Bādarāyaṇa's authorship of the sūtras is itself traditional/legendary; Śaṅkara and Vācaspati Miśra attributions are secure. The Bhāmatī is the Adhyāya-3 layer foregrounded here.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 4 (Śaṅkara's bhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
texts.basis: GRETIL TEI file = Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra with Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya and Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā(-vyākhyā) sub-commentary (fetch model saw an Adhyāya-1 excerpt but the file/edition is the Ratnaprabhā layer; metadata specifies Adhyāya 4). Three-tier authorship matches the Nirnaya-Sagar 1934 edition.
texts.note: Three distinct authors across layers: sūtra (Bādarāyaṇa, traditional), bhāṣya (Śaṅkara), sub-commentary Ratnaprabhā (Govindānanda, c. 16th c.). Sister-volume to item 253 (Bhāmatī) from same printed edition.

texts.title: Pravrajyāvastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Pravrajyāvastu (Vastu 1) of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu; ed. from C. Vogel & K. Wille editions (1984-2002) of Gilgit/Turfan fragments; cf. Gilgit Ms ed. N. Dutt. No individual author; canonical Vinaya school text with no personal ascription.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Vinaya text, collectively transmitted/redacted within the Mūlasarvāstivāda school. Editors Vogel/Wille and Dutt are modern philologists (Sanskrit-fragment reconstruction), not authors. No traditional individual ascription.

texts.title: Nāṭyaśāstra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: text self-identifies as 'bharatamunipraṇītaṃ nāṭyaśāstram' (composed by sage Bharata). Scholarly consensus: 'Bharata' is a traditional/legendary eponym for a composite, multi-layered text accreted over centuries (c. 200 BCE - 300 CE); not a single historical author. Kāvyamālā vulgate; adhyāyas 17, 31, 32, 34 not included in this e-text.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to the sage Bharata; widely regarded as a composite work without a single historical author. Kept as traditional (not conflated): the layering is anonymous accretion, not distinct named authors playing different roles.

texts.title: Mahāyānaviṃśikā [Mahāyānaviṃśati]
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'mahāyānaviṃśikā kṛtir āryanāgārjunapādānāṃ' (a composition of the noble Nāgārjuna). Ed. G. Tucci, Minor Buddhist Texts I, 1956. Web (Wikipedia/SEP context): the large corpus ascribed to 'Nāgārjuna' is, by modern consensus, not by one author or era; the Mahāyānaviṃśikā falls among the disputed minor works, not the securely-attributed core.
texts.note: Colophon ascribes to ārya-Nāgārjuna, but attribution to the historical Madhyamaka Nāgārjuna is disputed by modern scholarship; one of the many spurious/uncertain 'Nāgārjuna' minor texts. Retained as disputed rather than pseudepigraphic since no specific alternative author is established.

texts.title: Suśrutasaṃhitā
texts.primary_author: Suśruta (legendary core); Nāgārjuna (redactor/pratisaṃskartā); + Uttaratantra by a later hand; Ḍalhaṇa (Nibandhasaṅgraha commentary)

authors.name: Nāgārjuna (medical redactor; not the Madhyamaka philosopher)

texts.basis: Edition = Jādavji Trikamji Ācārya ed. with Ḍalhaṇa's Nibandhasaṅgraha. GRETIL source_url 404s (page moved/renamed). Secondary lit (Buddhism in Kerala, P.C. Alexander 1949) names Nāgārjuna as redactor of the Suśrutasaṃhitā with 'Bhadanta' appellation; web (Wikipedia/wisdomlib) confirms composite layering: original 'Vṛddha Suśruta' core, redaction by Nāgārjuna (per Ḍalhaṇa, ~3rd-4th c. CE), and the Uttaratantra added later as a supplement.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Buddhism in Kerala_P. C. Alexander, 1949.txt | web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sushruta_Samhita | edition-metadata
texts.note: Classic composite/conflation: legendary surgeon Suśruta (Sūtra-Sthāna etc.) + redaction ascribed to Nāgārjuna (pratisaṃskartā, per Ḍalhaṇa) + later-added Uttaratantra; this edition bundles Ḍalhaṇa's Nibandhasaṅgraha commentary (12th c.). The 'Nāgārjuna' here is the medical/alchemical Nāgārjuna, distinct from the Madhyamaka founder.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 3 (Śaṅkara's bhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (this file) explicitly names three layers: mūla = Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra; bhāṣya = Śaṅkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya; subcommentary = Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhāvyākhyā. Input by Sansknet project; header states text NOT proof-read. Edition cited (Bakre/Pansikar, Nirnaya-Sagar 1934) also contains Bhāmatī (Vācaspati) and Nyāyanirṇaya (Ānandagiri), but THIS e-text file carries only Ratnaprabhā.
texts.note: Three distinct authors across textual layers. Sūtra traditionally ascribed to Bādarāyaṇa (= Vyāsa in tradition); bhāṣya securely Śaṅkara; Ratnaprabhā by Govindānanda (16th c.). Bhāmatī/Nyāyanirṇaya named in the print edition are NOT in this digital file.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 16
texts.primary_author: Bhagavadgītā (mūla, trad. Vyāsa); commentaries by Śrīdhara Svāmin, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Gaudiya Grantha Mandira input) names the chapter-16 verses with commentaries of Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha and Baladeva. Body confirms all four commentaries. Gītā mūla itself anonymous/traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa.
texts.note: Single title bundles 4 distinct commentators on the Gītā verses; mūla anonymous (traditional Vyāsa ascription). Commentators: Śrīdhara (14th c.), Madhusūdana (16th c.), Viśvanātha & Baladeva (Gauḍīya, 17th-18th c.).

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 14
texts.primary_author: Bhagavadgītā (mūla, trad. Vyāsa); commentaries by Śrīdhara Svāmin, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Gaudiya Grantha Mandira input) lists chapter-14 verses with commentaries of Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha and Baladeva; body confirms all four. Gītā mūla anonymous/traditionally Vyāsa.
texts.note: Same structure as other Gītā-chapter files: mūla (anonymous/Vyāsa-ascribed) plus 4 distinct named commentators conflated under one title.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 15
texts.primary_author: Bhagavadgītā (mūla, trad. Vyāsa); commentaries by Śrīdhara Svāmin, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Gaudiya Grantha Mandira input) lists chapter-15 verses with commentaries of Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Viśvanātha and Baladeva; body confirms all four. Gītā mūla anonymous/traditionally Vyāsa.
texts.note: Mūla (anonymous/Vyāsa-ascribed) plus 4 distinct named commentators conflated under one title.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 17
texts.primary_author: Bhagavadgītā (mūla, trad. Vyāsa); commentaries by Śrīdhara Svāmin, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Gaudiya Grantha Mandira input) names chapter-17 verses with commentaries of Śrīdhara Svāmin, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Viśvanātha and Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa; body confirms all four.
texts.note: Mūla (anonymous/Vyāsa-ascribed) plus 4 distinct named commentators conflated under one title.

texts.title: Mahākarmavibhaṅga
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Buddhavacana; traditionally a discourse of the Buddha)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Sylvain Lévi 1932; input Klaus Wille) names no author; colophon 'karmavibhaṅgasūtraṃ samāptam'; presents as Buddha discourse (bhāṣita). Cited in secondary lit (Lamotte's Mahāprajñāpāramitāśāstra tr.) as a sūtra, not an authored treatise.
texts.note: Canonical sūtra-genre text, no named author; traditional ascription is Buddhavacana. Edited/translated by Sylvain Lévi (editor, not author).

texts.title: Pratītyasamutpādādivibhaṅganirdeśasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. P.L. Vaidya, Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha 1961) names no author; opens 'evaṃ mayā śrutam', framed as taught by bhagavān at Śrāvastī; colophon names no author. Standard sūtra/Buddhavacana.
texts.note: Canonical sūtra; traditional ascription Buddhavacana. Vaidya is editor only.

texts.title: Pravāraṇāvastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Pravāraṇāvastu, Vastu 3 of the Vinayavastu of the Mūlasarvāstivādin, reconstituted by Jin-il Chung (1998) from Gilgit/Turfan mss. Anonymous canonical monastic code; no colophon author.
texts.note: Canonical Vinaya section; Jin-il Chung is editor/reconstructor, not author.

texts.title: Upasaṃpadājñapti
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ordination procedures (upasaṃpadā) of the Mūlasarvāstivādins; based on Jin-il Chung, Handbuch für die buddhistische Mönchsordination (2011). Anonymous canonical text, no colophon author.
texts.note: Canonical Vinaya ordination formulary; editors (Jinananda; Jin-il Chung) are not authors.

texts.title: Śayanāsanavastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Śayanāsanavastu, 15th section of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya; ed. R. Gnoli 1978 from Gilgit ms (Dutt). Anonymous canonical monastic code; input by Seishi Karashima. No author colophon.
texts.note: Canonical Vinaya section; Gnoli is editor, not author.

texts.title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṅkāra
texts.basis: GRETIL header credits kārikās to Asaṅga and bhāṣya to Vasubandhu, distinguishing kārikā vs bhāṣya per chapter colophons (e.g. 'mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāre ... prathamaḥ'). Secondary lit (Thurman 2004 tr.; second GRETIL-related ed.) consistently titles it 'By Maitreyanātha/Āryāsaṅga, together with its Commentary (Bhāṣya) by Vasubandhu' — the kārikās ascribed to Maitreya(nātha) and transmitted via Asaṅga, bhāṣya by Vasubandhu.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Universal Vehicle Discourse Literature (Mahayanasutralamkara) by Maitreyanatha-Aryasanga.. (Bhasya) by Vasubandhu_Robert Thurman, 2004.txt
texts.note: Multi-author: root kārikās attributed to Maitreya(nātha) (Asaṅga in some traditions / as redactor-transmitter), prose bhāṣya by Vasubandhu. GRETIL header alone names Asaṅga for the kārikās; the Maitreya ascription and the verse/commentary split are the key conflation. Current_author already captures the three roles.

texts.title: Upasampadājñapti
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya tradition; edited by B. Jinananda)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya tradition); ed. Jin-il Chung / B. Jinananda
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Chung ed. 2011, underlying Jinananda 1961): a Mūlasarvāstivāda ordination manual (jñapti), no individual author named; colophon merely 'upasaṃpādajñaptiḥ samāptaḥ'. Canonical Vinaya material of corporate/anonymous transmission.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Vinaya ordination formulary; modern editors Chung and Jinananda are editors, not authors.

texts.title: Śayanāsanavastu
texts.basis: Edition (Gnoli 1978, Gilgit MS): 15th section of the Mūlasarvāstivādin Vinaya. Canonical Vinaya vastu of corporate/anonymous authorship; no individual ascribed. DSBC text from the same tradition.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Vinaya section (vastu); Gnoli is the modern editor.

texts.title: Nagaropamasūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Saṃyuktāgama sūtra); ed. Bongard-Levin, Boucher, Fukita, Wille

authors.name: G. Bongard-Levin, D. Boucher, T. Fukita, K. Wille

texts.basis: GRETIL header / edition (1996): an apotropaic sūtra from the Saṃyuktāgama, reconstructed from Central Asian (Turfan, Pelliot/Stein) Sanskrit manuscripts. Canonical sūtra, no individual author; spoken-by-the-Buddha framing.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical āgama sūtra; the four named scholars are editors/reconstructors.

texts.title: Parāśaradharmasaṃhitā [Parāśarasmṛti]: Kāṇḍas 1 & 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Islampurkar/Bhandarkar, Bombay Skt Series): the file is the smṛti WITH Mādhava's ṭīkā. The mūla smṛti is traditionally (pseudepigraphically) ascribed to the sage Parāśara; the commentary is the Parāśaramādhavīya by Mādhava. Web (ZDMG, academia.edu, bvparishat): the commentator Mādhava is Mādhavācārya, kulaguru/minister of Bukka I, mid-14th c. Vijayanagara, who as renunciant took the name Vidyāraṇya (debated as distinct from Sāyaṇa).
texts.note: Title conflates two distinct authors: legendary sage Parāśara (mūla, traditional/pseudepigraphic) vs historical 14th-c. Mādhava=Vidyāraṇya (ṭīkā). Separated as roles.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā 18
texts.primary_author: Vyāsa (traditional, mūla); Śrīdhara Svāmin, Viśvanātha Cakravartin, Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa (commentaries)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Gaudiya Grantha Mandira e-text): the file bundles the Gītā mūla with the commentaries of Śrīdhara (Subodhinī), Viśvanātha (ṭīkā) and Baladeva (bhāṣya) — header notes Madhusūdana effectively absent. Secondary lit (Karl Potter bibliography) confirms these as three distinct Vaiṣṇava commentators (Śrīdhara Svāmin ~14th c.; Viśvanātha Cakravartin & Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa, Gauḍīya, 17th-18th c.). The Gītā mūla itself is traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, in fact an anonymous/composite portion of the Mahābhārata.
texts.note: One file conflates the mūla (Vyāsa/anonymous) and three separate commentators from different centuries; separated by role.

texts.title: Divyāvadāna
texts.primary_author: anonymous (compiled; draws on Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya); ed. P. L. Vaidya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya 1959): anonymous avadāna anthology; colophons name only individual avadānas (e.g. Koṭikarṇāvadāna), not an author. Secondary lit (Rotman, Divine Stories 2017): repeatedly speaks of 'the compiler of the Divyāvadāna' who borrowed from the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya — a single anonymous compiler drawing on canonical material.
texts.note: Anonymous compilation; an unnamed compiler assembled pre-existing (largely Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya) narratives. Not a single named author.

texts.title: Sabhikasaugataśāsanapravrajyāvratacaraṇaparivarta
texts.basis: This is a parivarta (the Sabhika story) of the Mahāvastu-Avadāna, a Vinaya-affiliated work of the Lokottaravāda branch of the Mahāsāṃghika. Secondary lit (Nakamura 1980: ~5 strata of enlargement; Klimburg-Salter: 'several compilers working over a long period'; Gyatso: older strata 2nd-3rd c.) treats it as an anonymous, multi-stratum compilation, not a single-author work. Ed. Senart 1882-97.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Kingdom of Bamiyan...Klimburg-Salter, 1989.txt
texts.note: Chapter of the Mahāvastu; no individual author. Traditional ascription is collective to the Lokottaravādins/Mahāsāṃghika; compiled over centuries in multiple strata. current_author 'anonymous/traditional' is accurate.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1 (Śaṅkara's bhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
texts.basis: GRETIL header explicitly names three layers: Bādarāyaṇa = mūla Brahmasūtra; Śaṅkara = Sārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya; Govindānanda = Ratnaprabhāvyākhyā sub-commentary; digitized by Sansknet project. Secondary lit (Sinha 1938: 'Govindānanda elaborates the argument of Śaṅkara'; Pollock/World Philology: Govindānanda of Bengal) confirms Govindānanda Sarasvatī as a later (post-13th c.) sub-commentator. Ed. Bākre, Nirnaya Sagar 1934.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Realism_Jadunath Sinha, 1938.txt; secondary:cleaned_World Philology...Pollock 2015.txt
texts.note: Three distinct authors across three centuries: sūtra (Bādarāyaṇa), bhāṣya (Śaṅkara, ~8th c.), Ratnaprabhā sub-commentary (Govindānanda Sarasvatī, later medieval, Bengal). current_author already separates the roles correctly.

texts.title: Saddharmasmṛtyupasthānasūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (heterogeneous Buddhist compilation, Greater Gandhāra milieu)
texts.basis: Daniel Stuart (the editor of this very text, A Less Traveled Path, 2015) explicitly calls the Saddharmasmṛtyupasthānasūtra 'historically and textually heterogeneous, like many other authorless' Buddhist sūtras, with material from 3rd-century yoga-practitioner circles in Greater Gandhāra. Anonymous sūtra-genre work; no individual author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Stuart_Yogācāra Substrata? ...Third-Century Yoga Practitioners in Greater Gandhāra.txt
texts.note: Editor Stuart classes it as 'authorless' and heterogeneous. Anonymous canonical sūtra. current_author 'anonymous/traditional' accurate.

texts.title: Āryasaṃghāṭasūtra
texts.basis: A Mahāyāna sūtra of the dhāraṇī/buddhavacana genre, preserved among the Gilgit manuscripts (von Hinüber & Kudo eds.). As buddhavacana it bears no human authorial attribution; no scholarly attribution to any individual author exists. Schopen (1978, Gilgit Buddhism) treats it among the anonymous Gilgit sūtra corpus.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_BHAISAJYAGURU-SUTRA AND THE BUDDHISM OF GILGIT_Gregory Schopen, 1978.txt; edition (von Hinüber & Kudo)
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra; framed as buddhavacana, no individual author. current_author 'anonymous/traditional' accurate.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 4 (with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
texts.basis: Same Bākre 1934 edition (Brahmasūtra-Śāṅkarabhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā), Adhyāya 4. GRETIL transformation URL 404s now, but item is the Adhyāya-4 counterpart of brssgo1u: three layers — Bādarāyaṇa (sūtra), Śaṅkara (bhāṣya), Govindānanda Sarasvatī (Ratnaprabhā sub-commentary). Govindānanda confirmed as later medieval Bengali sub-commentator (Pollock 2015; Sinha 1938).
texts.source: gretil-header (parallel brssgo1u); secondary:cleaned_World Philology...Pollock 2015.txt; secondary:cleaned_Indian Realism_Jadunath Sinha, 1938.txt
texts.note: Three distinct authors (sūtra/bhāṣya/sub-commentary). current_author already lists all three roles; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya is the primary commentarial layer, Govindānanda glosses it.

texts.title: Karmavibhaṅgopadeśa
texts.basis: Nakamura 1980: 'the Karmavibhaṅgopadeśa is a commentary on the Mahākarmavibhaṅga.' Edited (with the Mahākarmavibhaṅga) by Sylvain Lévi, Paris 1932; this e-text is from Vaidya's BST 17 (1961) reprint. GRETIL header reports a closing line 'jayatu saddharmaḥ | ityāha bhikṣā(u) śrutasomā' — a devotional/scribal subscription naming a Bhikṣu Śrutasoma, but this is not established by scholarship (Lévi) as the author; the work is treated as an anonymous Nepalese commentary on the Mahākarmavibhaṅga.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey...Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; gretil-header; web:https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/journal-of-the-royal-asiatic-society/article/abs/...Levi-1932
texts.note: Anonymous upadeśa (commentary) on the Mahākarmavibhaṅga. The colophon name 'Bhikṣu Śrutasoma' appears in the e-text subscription but is best read as a scribal/devotional formula, not a secure authorial attribution; not in any role recognized by Lévi/Nakamura. Kept anonymous; traditional ascription noted.

texts.title: Dharmasamuccaya [Saddharmasmṛtyupasthānakārikā]
texts.primary_author: Avalokitasiṃha (compiler of the verses); anonymous (underlying Saddharmasmṛtyupasthānasūtra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header/opening line names the monk Avalokitasiṃha as the one by whom the work was 'samudbhāvitaḥ' (compiled). Web (Heidelberg DhS edition project) confirms the Dharmasamuccaya is a collection of verse extracts (strophes) drawn from the anonymous Saddharmasmṛtyupasthānasūtra, with Avalokitasiṃha responsible for the compilation. So the verses are excerpted canonical/sūtra material; Avalokitasiṃha is the anthologist/compiler, not the original author.
texts.note: Colophon/opening: 'bhikṣuṇā avalokitasiṃhena samudbhāvitaḥ'. Two distinct roles: anonymous source sūtra (Saddharmasmṛtyupasthāna) vs Avalokitasiṃha the compiler of the verse extracts. Flagged as conflation rather than single author.

texts.title: Tantrākhyāyika 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Schreiner) gives no author, only the e-text editor. Secondary (Olivelle, Pañcatantra, OUP 1999, 'Sources, Author, and Date'): the Pañcatantra's Prelude names its author as the octogenarian Brahmin Viṣṇuśarman, but there is no external/independent evidence; Edgerton holds the ascription is fictitious ('no hint anywhere as to the true name or station of the author'), and some southern/SE-Asian recensions name the author as Vasubhāga. Tantrākhyāyika is Hertel's oldest (Kashmirian) recension of the Pañcatantra.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Pancatantra. The Book of India's Folk Wisdom_Patrick Olivelle, 1999.txt | gretil-header
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Viṣṇuśarman is from the text's own frame-prelude; scholars (Edgerton) regard it as a literary/fictitious attribution; competing name Vasubhāga in S. recensions. Effectively anonymous; recorded as pseudepigraphic.

texts.title: Tantrākhyāyika 1
texts.basis: Same as Tantrākhyāyika 2. GRETIL header (Schreiner) names only the e-text editor. Olivelle (1999): Prelude ascribes authorship to Viṣṇuśarman, but Edgerton judges this fictitious; Vasubhāga named in southern recensions; no independent evidence for a historical author. This is part 1 of Hertel's Kashmirian recension (oldest Pañcatantra).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Pancatantra. The Book of India's Folk Wisdom_Patrick Olivelle, 1999.txt | gretil-header
texts.note: Pseudepigraphic frame-attribution to Viṣṇuśarman; genuinely anonymous folk-narrative compilation. Same status as Tantrākhyāyika 2.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 2 (with Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī)
texts.current_author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Vācaspati Miśra (Bhāmatī sub-commentary); Śaṅkara (underlying bhāṣya)
texts.primary_author: Vācaspati Miśra (Bhāmatī ṭīkā); Śaṅkara (bhāṣya); Bādarāyaṇa (mūla sūtra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header explicitly: 'Vācaspati: Bhāmatī (a commentary on Śaṃkara's Brahmasūtrabhāṣya)'; Śaṅkara named as author of the Brahmasūtrabhāṣya being glossed; the maṅgala verse 'brahmasūtrakṛte tasmai vyāsāya...' invokes Vyāsa/Bādarāyaṇa as author of the sūtras. Three-tier text: mūla sūtra (Bādarāyaṇa) → bhāṣya (Śaṅkara) → Bhāmatī sub-commentary (Vācaspati Miśra). Current_author already captures all three correctly.
texts.note: Genuine multi-author stratification: Bādarāyaṇa (root sūtra), Śaṅkara (bhāṣya), Vācaspati Miśra (Bhāmatī ṭīkā). Display lead = Vācaspati Miśra (the actual composing author of this Bhāmatī text). Conflation flagged; matches current attribution.

texts.title: Bodhisattvabhūmi
texts.primary_author: Asaṅga (traditional; as compiler/redactor of the Yogācārabhūmi corpus)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: the text identifies itself as part of the Yogācārabhūmi ('yogācārabhūmau'), ed. N. Dutt 1966 as 'the XVth section of Asangapada's Yogācārabhūmi'. Secondary/web: the YBh is now widely held (Schmithausen 1987; Stanford Encyclopedia; YBh-corpus scholarship) to be a COMPOSITE compilation of heterogeneous layers composed over time, traditionally ascribed to Asaṅga (Indo-Tibetan tradition) or to Maitreya (East Asian tradition); Asaṅga is best regarded as compiler/redactor rather than sole author. Bodhisattvabhūmi is one self-contained section within this corpus.
texts.note: Sole attribution 'Asaṅga' is the traditional Indo-Tibetan ascription; East Asian tradition ascribes the YBh to Maitreya, and modern scholarship treats the YBh as a composite/compilation, so Asaṅga's role is compiler/redactor of pre-existing materials. Status downgraded to disputed; single (composite) section so no conflation flag.

texts.title: Śarīrārthagāthā
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (compiled within the Yogācārabhūmi, ascribed to Asaṅga)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Enomoto 1989, input Wille): 'Asanga: Śarīrārthagāthā of the Cintāmayībhūmi', described as 'A Collection of Canonical Verses in the Yogācārabhūmi'. Secondary (Enomoto 1989; bibliography in 'Literature about the Philosophical Systems'): the Śarīrārthagāthā is a COLLECTION of pre-existing canonical (āgama/sūtra) verses embedded in the Cintāmayī-bhūmi of the YBh — i.e. the gāthās themselves are anonymous canonical material, with the YBh-compiler (traditionally Asaṅga) as collector. The YBh itself is a composite work (see Bodhisattvabhūmi entry).
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt | web:https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/yogacara/
texts.note: Two roles conflated: anonymous canonical (āgama) verses vs the compiler (YBh-redactor, traditionally Asaṅga, low confidence) who anthologized them. Not an original composition. Conflation flagged.

texts.title: Yogācārabhūmi: Samāhitā Bhūmi
texts.primary_author: Asaṅga (traditional, Tibetan); Maitreya (traditional, Chinese) — composite compilation
texts.basis: Delhey ed. (this chapter) treats it as part of the YBh Basic Section; modern scholarship (per WebSearch; Stanford SEP, encyclopediaofbuddhism) holds the Yogācārabhūmi is a composite, multi-layered compilation by numerous authors reaching final redaction ~4th c., not a single-author work. Tibetan tradition ascribes to Asaṅga, Chinese canon to Maitreya. Bhattacharya/Calcutta and Silk Road survey ('composed by Asanga and Vasubandhu') reflect the traditional/composite ascriptions.
texts.source: web:https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/yogacara/; web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yogacarabhumi-sastra; secondary:cleaned_Silk Road Journey with Xuanzang_Sally Wriggins, 2004.txt
texts.note: Composite text with chronological layers; traditional Asaṅga (Tib) vs Maitreya (Chin). Not securely single-authored; flagged as conflated redactional layers rather than one named author.

texts.title: Abhisamayālaṅkāranāmaprajñāpāramitopadeśaśāstra
texts.current_author: attributed to Maitreyanātha (Tibetan tradition: revealed by Maitreya to Asaṅga)
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives internal colophon 'kṛtir āryamaitreyanāthasya' (the work of noble Maitreyanātha) and 'oṃ namo maitreyanāthāya'; source unknown. This e-text is the kārikā/root verse text only (no bhāṣya). Snellgrove and Mipham list it 'by Maitreya/Asanga'; Nakamura notes the early commentators Ārya-Vimuktisena and Haribhadra (8th c.). Maitreyanātha's historicity disputed in modern scholarship (human teacher vs. revelatory device of Asaṅga).
texts.note: Root verses (kārikā) only; colophon names Maitreyanātha. Traditional ascription (revealed to Asaṅga); commentaries by Vimuktisena/Haribhadra are separate works not included here.

texts.title: Nārāyaṇīya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Schreiner ed.) names no author; text = MBh 12.321-339, an anonymous epic section. Edited within Schreiner's Nārāyaṇīya-Studien (Grünendahl, Malinar, Oberlies, Schreiner as editors of the volume). Anonymous redactional layer of the Mokṣadharma; traditionally part of the Vyāsa-ascribed Mahābhārata.
texts.note: Anonymous epic passage (MBh 12.321-339); editors named are modern scholars, not authors. Traditional ascription to Vyāsa noted separately.

texts.title: Abhisamayālaṅkārāntaḥpātināṃ (saṃketānāṃ nāmāni)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (scholastic glossary of Abhisamayālaṅkāra technical terms)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Tripathi ed., Sarnath 1977) frames the content as the well-spoken āgama of Maitreyanātha — i.e. it glosses/lists the technical terms (saṃketa) falling within the Abhisamayālaṅkāra; a concluding verse is attributed to Ācārya Haribhadra. The glossary itself names no compiler; it is a derivative scholastic list of AA terminology, not a work by Maitreya.
texts.note: Anonymous scholastic glossary of AA terms; header invokes Maitreyanātha (the source treatise) and quotes Haribhadra, but no compiler is named. Not authored by Maitreya.

texts.title: Yogācārabhūmi: Kleśa section
texts.primary_author: Asaṅga (traditional, Tibetan); Maitreya (traditional, Chinese) — composite compilation
texts.basis: Kleśa section of the Yogācārabhūmi (Bhattacharya, Calcutta 1957, 'The Yogācārabhūmi of Ācārya Asaṅga'). As with the Samāhitā Bhūmi, modern scholarship treats the YBh as a composite, multi-author compilation with chronological layers (~4th c. final redaction); traditional Asaṅga (Tibetan) vs Maitreya (Chinese) attributions. No single secure author.
texts.note: Empty current_author resolved: part of the composite Yogācārabhūmi. Traditional Asaṅga/Maitreya; modern view = multi-layered compilation. Flagged conflated.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 2 (Śaṅkara's bhāṣya with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
texts.basis: GRETIL TEI header confirms three distinct named authors: Bādarāyaṇa (Brahmasūtra mūla), Śaṃkara (Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya), Govindānanda (Ratnaprabhāvyākhyā); input by Sansknet Project, no print edition cited in header. (Header metadata label reads Adhyāya 1, but the work is one layered text across adhyāyas.) Three roles by three different people across two millennia.
texts.note: Three distinct authors: mūla-sūtra (Bādarāyaṇa), bhāṣya (Śaṅkara), sub-commentary Ratnaprabhā (Govindānanda). Already separated in current_author; confirmed by header. Conflated by single title.

texts.title: Abhisamayālaṅkāra
texts.current_author: attributed to Maitreyanātha (Tibetan tradition: Maitreya, redacted by Asaṅga)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition is Tripathi's 'Abhisamayālaṅkāravṛttiḥ Sphuṭārthā (with the kārikā)' — i.e. kārikā PLUS the Sphuṭārthā vṛtti. Opening ascribes kārikā to Āryamaitreyanātha. Secondary: Snellgrove lists Abhisamayālaṅkāra '(by Maitreya/Asanga)'; Obermiller and Lopez identify the Sphuṭārthā as Haribhadra's 'Clear Meaning' commentary. Current_author names only Maitreya/Asaṅga, omitting Haribhadra.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Abhisamayalamkara_E. Obermiller, 1933.txt; cleaned_Study of Svatantrika_Donald S Lopez, 2016.txt; cleaned_Snellgrove_Indo-Tibetan Buddhism.txt
texts.note: Text = root kārikā (Maitreya, traditionally redacted by Asaṅga) + Haribhadra's Sphuṭārthā vṛtti (c. late 8th c.). Distinct authors for distinct layers; current_author covers only the kārikā layer.

texts.title: Jātakamālā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Hanisch ed.) attributes to Āryaśūra. Secondary: Nariman notes the Chinese translation names Āryaśūra as author; Hahn's work on Haribhaṭṭa/Gopadatta places them 'in the succession of Āryaśūra', confirming Āryaśūra as the recognized author of the Jātakamālā.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism_Nariman G K_1919.txt; cleaned_Sanskrit Texts from Giuseppe Tucci's Collection. Part I_Francesco Sferra, ed, 2008.txt
texts.note: Single-author kāvya jātaka collection. Attribution to Āryaśūra (c. 4th c.) is secure across editions and traditions.

texts.title: Madhyāntavibhāgakārikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Maitreya (attrib.): Madhyantavibhagakarika' — kārikā ALONE, no bhāṣya/ṭīkā included (colophon 'samāptā madhyāntavibhāgakārikāḥ'). Secondary confirms the famous multi-author complex (kārikā=Maitreyanātha, bhāṣya=Vasubandhu, ṭīkā=Sthiramati), but THIS file is the verses only, so no conflation here.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Jnanagarbha on the Two Truths_Malcolm David Eckel.txt; cleaned_Indian Buddhism_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: This GRETIL file is the kārikā layer only (Vasubandhu's bhāṣya and Sthiramati's ṭīkā are separate texts), so single attribution stands; Maitreya ascription is traditional, transmitted via Asaṅga.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1 (with Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī)
texts.current_author: Bādarāyaṇa (mūla); Vācaspati Miśra (Bhāmatī sub-commentary); Śaṅkara (underlying bhāṣya)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Vācaspati: Bhamati (A Commentary on Śaṅkara's Brahmasūtrabhāṣya)'. Three-layered text: mūla Brahmasūtra (Bādarāyaṇa), bhāṣya (Śaṅkara), Bhāmatī sub-commentary (Vācaspati Miśra). Secondary confirms Vācaspati's Bhāmatī as Advaita sub-commentary on Śaṅkara.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Sabdapramana_Purusottama Bilimoria.txt; cleaned_Dignaga's Philosophy of Language_Ole Holten Pind, 2009.txt
texts.note: Genuine three-author stratification (Bādarāyaṇa sūtra / Śaṅkara bhāṣya / Vācaspati Bhāmatī). current_author already lists all three; conflation flagged for completeness. Roles refined.

texts.title: Jātakamālā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya ed.): opening 'āryaśūraviracitā bodhisattvāvadānamālāparaparyāyā' — composed by Āryaśūra (alt. title Bodhisattvāvadānamālā). Secondary (Nariman) notes Chinese translation names Āryaśūra. Same work as ajm_u, different edition.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism_Nariman G K_1919.txt
texts.note: Vaidya edition of the same Āryaśūra Jātakamālā; colophon-style opening names Āryaśūra. Secure single authorship.

texts.title: Sāṃkhyakārikā [Sāṃkhyasaptati]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition = 'Sāṃkhyakārikā: Māṭharācāryaviracita-Māṭharavṛtti-sahitā' — i.e. Īśvarakṛṣṇa's kārikā TOGETHER WITH Māṭhara's vṛtti (reference system distinguishes ISk_n verses from SkMv_n commentary). Secondary (Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism; Cross Currents in Early Buddhism; Frauwallner) treats the Māṭhara-vṛtti as a (relatively late, ascription-debated) commentary on the kārikā, alongside Yuktidīpikā and Gauḍapāda-bhāṣya.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism_Teun Goudriaan, ed, 1992.txt; cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: File conflates kārikā (Īśvarakṛṣṇa) with the Māṭharavṛtti (ascribed to Māṭhara/Māṭharācārya, a later commentary of uncertain date). current_author names only Īśvarakṛṣṇa, omitting the commentary author.

texts.title: Vaikhānasa Dharmasūtra 1–3 [Vaikhānasa Smārtasūtra 8-10]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to the sage Vikhanas)
texts.basis: GRETIL header names no author; presents the Vaikhānasa Smārtasūtra of the Black Yajurveda (ed. Caland 1927), input by Hikita. No colophon author; tradition ascribes the school's sūtras to the eponymous sage Vikhanas.
texts.note: School text; legendary ascription to Vikhanas (eponym of the Vaikhānasa school). Editor W. Caland.

texts.title: Jñānālokālaṃkāra sūtra [Sarvabuddhaviṣayāvatārajñānālokālaṃkāra nāma mahāyānasūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editors (Kimura/Otsuka/Kimura/Takahashi 2004); text is a Mahāyāna sūtra with the Buddha teaching, Mañjuśrī as interlocutor. Manuscript colophon names only a scribe (Cāṇḍoka) and a date under King Gopāla. Cited as a tathāgatagarbha source by the Ratnagotravibhāga.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra; colophon is a scribal copy-note, not authorship. Quoted in RGV/RGVV.

texts.title: Tarkaśāstra
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (sometimes attributed to Vasubandhu or his school)
texts.primary_author: uncertain (Chinese tradition ascribes to Vasubandhu; scholars doubt it; Sanskrit reconstructed by Tucci)
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives no author (reconstructed from Chinese via Tucci 1929). Chinese Ru-shi-lun (T1633) traditionally ascribed to Vasubandhu (Mejor, Steinkellner, Fa-tsang/Vorenkamp), but Tucci and Williams hold it is by an unknown pre-Diṅnāga Buddhist author, not Vasubandhu; Coseru/Matilal note Tucci's Sanskrit reconstruction.
texts.note: Attribution to Vasubandhu disputed; Tucci/Williams: unknown pre-Diṅnāga author. The Sanskrit is Tucci's reconstruction from Chinese (translator role).

texts.title: Mīmāṃsāsūtra Shabara-Bhāṣya
texts.primary_author: Jaimini (sūtra); Śabara (bhāṣya), incorporating an earlier Vṛttikāra layer (MS 1.1.1-5)

authors.name: Vṛttikāra (anonymous; old commentator, sometimes assoc. with Upavarṣa)

texts.basis: GRETIL header: Jaimini = sūtra, Śabara = bhāṣya (diplomatic ed. by Pohlus from 6 editions). Secondary lit (Matilal; Potter EIP vol.3; Dunne) and web confirm Śabara's Bhāṣya embeds the 'Vṛttikāragrantha' (esp. on MS 1.1.1-5) deriving from an earlier anonymous Vṛttikāra, often linked to Upavarṣa though the identity is disputed.
texts.note: Sūtra=Jaimini, bhāṣya=Śabara, plus an embedded older Vṛttikāra stratum (MS 1.1.1-5); Vṛttikāra's identity (Upavarṣa?) disputed.

texts.title: Viṃśatikā vijñaptimātratāsiddhi [Viṃśikāvṛtti] kārikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'kṛtir iyam ācāryavasubandhoḥ'; kārikās and prose vṛtti integrated. Secondary lit (Bruhacs; Buswell Encyclopedia; Kochumuttom) confirms the Viṃśatikā has an auto-commentary (vṛtti) by Vasubandhu himself.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Vasubandhu’s Argument Against Atomism in the Twenty Verses_Lorand Bruhacs, 2008.txt
texts.note: Both kārikā and vṛtti by Vasubandhu (autocommentary); single author, not a conflation despite kārikā/vṛtti roles.

texts.title: Nāmaliṅgānuśāsana [Amarakoṣa, Amarakośa], Kāṇḍa 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header and the text's own colophon (1.11.607-608): 'ity amarasiṃhakṛtau nāmaliṅgānuśāsane' identify Amarasiṃha as author. This GRETIL file is the mūla (kāṇḍa 1) only, without Kṣīrasvāmin's commentary.
texts.note: Author named in colophon as Amarasiṃha. Mūla text only (no commentary in this file).

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 3 (with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Bādarāyaṇa: Brahmasūtra with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhāvyākhyā, a subcommentary on Śaṃkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya'. Three distinct layers confirmed; mūla by Bādarāyaṇa, bhāṣya by Śaṅkara, Ratnaprabhā by Govindānanda.
texts.note: Three-author stratified work: sūtra (Bādarāyaṇa, traditional) / bhāṣya (Śaṅkara) / Ratnaprabhā gloss (Govindānanda, c.16th c.). Adhyāya 3 portion.

texts.title: Mīmāṃsāsūtrabhāṣya
texts.primary_author: Śabara (Śabarasvāmin) (bhāṣya); Jaimini (sūtra); incorporating an older Vṛttikāra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Sabara: Mimamsasutrabhasya' on 'Jaimini's Mimamsasutra'. Secondary lit (Bilimoria; web/wisdomlib, Hindupedia): Śabara repeatedly quotes and incorporates an older Vṛttikāra (assoc. Upavarṣa); Frauwallner's Materialien edition (the edition used) specifically isolates these pre-Śabara layers.
texts.note: Bhāṣya by Śabara on Jaimini's sūtras, but the bhāṣya embeds an earlier Vṛttikāra's commentary (named only as Vṛttikāra; often linked to Upavarṣa) — genuine multi-author stratification, the editorial focus of Frauwallner 1968.

texts.title: Yogācārabhūmi
texts.current_author: Asaṅga (traditional; East Asian tradition ascribes to Maitreya; modern scholarship sees it as a layered compilation)
texts.primary_author: Asaṅga (traditional Indo-Tibetan); Maitreya (East Asian tradition); modern: layered compilation
texts.basis: DSBC page has no colophon attribution (header fields blank; text ends '...yogācārabhūmau ... bhūmiḥ prathamā samāptā'). Secondary lit: Bhattacharya ed. titles it 'of Ācārya Asaṅga' (Nakamura); Chinese tradition ascribes Maitreya's five texts incl. Yogācārabhūmi (Cha); modern scholarship treats it as a layered/composite compilation rather than single-authored.
texts.source: dsbc-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism...Nakamura,1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Study of Dharmadharmatavibhaga...John Younghan Cha.txt
texts.note: Attribution genuinely split across traditions: Asaṅga (Indo-Tibetan), Maitreya (East Asian), composite-redaction (modern). This GRETIL/DSBC chunk = Pañcavijñānakāyasamprayuktā Bhūmi (1st bhūmi).

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 2 (with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Bādarāyaṇa: Brahmasūtra with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhāvyākhyā, a subcommentary on Śaṃkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya'. Same stratified source file as Adhyāya 3.
texts.note: Three-author stratified work: sūtra (Bādarāyaṇa) / bhāṣya (Śaṅkara) / Ratnaprabhā (Govindānanda). Adhyāya 2 portion.

texts.title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra
texts.basis: GRETIL/Bagchi ed. titles it 'Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra of Asaṅga with its Svavṛtti'. Secondary lit (Cha; Hopkins): kārikās ascribed to Maitreya in tradition ('Maitreya in his Ornament...'), bhāṣya to Vasubandhu (Lévi ed.); Asaṅga as transmitter/redactor. Edition file is kārikā + svavṛtti.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Study of Dharmadharmatavibhaga...John Younghan Cha.txt; secondary:cleaned_Meditation on Emptiness_Hopkins.txt
texts.note: Gold-standard conflation: kārikā (Maitreya), bhāṣya/svavṛtti (Vasubandhu), redaction/transmission (Asaṅga). Bagchi edition title ascribes whole to Asaṅga — a simplification.

texts.title: Triśatikāyāḥ prajñāpāramitāyāḥ kārikāsaptatiḥ [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtraśāstrakārikā]
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'kṛtir iyam āryāsaṅgapādānām iti' (this work is by the venerable Asaṅga). Secondary (Nakamura 1980): the kārikās (頌) ascribed to Asaṅga; Vasubandhu wrote the prose commentary, but THIS edition (Tucci, kārikā-saptati) contains the verses only — single author.
texts.note: Kārikā-only text, securely Asaṅga per colophon. Vasubandhu's prose bhāṣya on the same root is a separate work and not included here, so no conflation in this item.

texts.title: Abhidharmasamuccaya
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Asaṅga as author (ed. Pradhan 1950; Gokhale fragments 1947). Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, 1980), Snellgrove and Karmay/Nagano all cite it as Asaṅga's work. This GRETIL file is the mūla (root) text only; a separate bhāṣya (often associated with Sthiramati / unknown) is NOT part of this file, so no in-file conflation.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt + secondary:cleaned_Snellgrove_Indo-Tibetan Buddhism.txt
texts.note: Root text securely Asaṅga; bhāṣya (Sthiramati/anon.) is a separate work not in this file.

texts.title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra
texts.basis: GRETIL header oversimplifies to 'Asaṅga' but the file contains kārikā + bhāṣya. Web (Wikipedia, Encyclopedia of Buddhism, AIBS Treasury of Buddhist Sciences ed.) and secondary lit confirm classic multi-author structure: kārikā ascribed to Maitreya(nātha), transmitted via Asaṅga; prose bhāṣya by Vasubandhu; sub-comms by Sthiramati. Scholarly dispute (Ui, Tucci vs. those identifying Maitreya=Asaṅga) over the kārikā author.
texts.note: Gold-standard conflation: kārikā Maitreya(nātha)/Asaṅga, bhāṣya Vasubandhu, redaction Asaṅga. current_author already separates roles; GRETIL header (just 'Asaṅga') is wrong.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1 (with Govindānanda's Ratnaprabhā)
texts.basis: GRETIL header explicitly names all three: Bādarāyaṇa (Brahmasūtra mūla), Śaṅkara (Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya), Govindānanda (Ratnaprabhā[-vyākhyā] sub-commentary). Secondary lit (Freschi & Maas, Adaptive Reuse) confirms Govindānanda's Bhāṣyaratnaprabhā as a gloss on Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. Brahmasūtra mūla authorship traditionally Bādarāyaṇa (Vyāsa); historically composite/disputed but conventionally Bādarāyaṇa.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse_Aspects of Creativity in South Asian Cultural History.txt
texts.note: Three-author stack: sūtra Bādarāyaṇa, bhāṣya Śaṅkara, Ratnaprabhā ṭīkā Govindānanda (post-Śaṅkara). Matches current_author.

texts.title: Mahāyānasaṃgraha
texts.basis: DSBC header gives no explicit author but text is the root Mahāyānasaṃgraha; secondary lit unanimously attributes it to Asaṅga (Verdu 1981 'most important work of Asaṅga'; Keenan; Lamotte's Somme du Grand Véhicule d'Asaṅga). No extant Skt original — DSBC is a romanized restoration (input Miroj Shakya). The bhāṣya by Vasubandhu and upanibandhana by Asvabhāva are SEPARATE works, not in this root-text file, so no in-file conflation.
texts.source: dsbc-header + secondary:cleaned_Philosophy of Buddhism. A Totalistic Synthesis_Alfonso Verdu, 1981.txt + secondary:cleaned_Keenan_Dharmapala's Yogacara Critique.txt
texts.note: Root saṃgraha = Asaṅga (secure). DSBC text is a Skt restoration from Tibetan/Chinese (no extant Skt original). Bhāṣya by Vasubandhu is a separate work.

texts.title: Abhidharmakośakārikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Vasubandhu (kārikā; ed. Pradhan/Haldar 1975; Gokhale 1946). This file is the verse kārikā only (the auto-commentary bhāṣya is a separate file, also Vasubandhu). Frauwallner's 'two Vasubandhus' theory (1951) once assigned the Kośa to a distinct Vasubandhu, but this is no longer tenable (Schmithausen et al.); single authorship now standard.
texts.note: Securely Vasubandhu. Historical 'two Vasubandhu' dispute (Frauwallner) abandoned. File is kārikā only; auto-bhāṣya is a separate text.

texts.title: Nāmaliṅgānuśāsana [Amarakoṣa], Kāṇḍa 3
texts.basis: GRETIL header (input Sathaye & Ganesan 1997) names Amarasiṃha. Secondary lit (Lopez, Study of Svatantrika: 'sixth-century Buddhist lexicographer Amarasimha'; Speyer, Avadānaśataka: 'Amarasimha, who was a Buddhist') confirms. NOTE: the edition citation mentions Kṣīrasvāmin's Amarakośodghāṭana commentary, but THIS GRETIL file (amark3_u) is the bare mūla text of Kāṇḍa 3 without commentary — so no in-file conflation with the commentator.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Study of Svatantrika_Donald S Lopez, 2016.txt + secondary:cleaned_Avadanacataka...J. S. Speyer.txt
texts.note: Securely Amarasiṃha (trad. Buddhist lexicographer). File is mūla text only; Kṣīrasvāmin commentary cited in edition note is NOT present in this file.

texts.title: Nāmaliṅgānuśāsana [Amarakoṣa, Amarakośa], Kāṇḍa 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header and the file's own colophon ('ityamarasiṃhakṛtau nāmaliṅgānuśāsane dvitīyaḥ kāṇḍo') ascribe the lexicon to Amarasiṃha. Lopez calls him 'the sixth-century Buddhist lexicographer Amarasimha'; Lessing & Wayman confirm Amarasimha as author. Note: this GRETIL file is the root kośa (kāṇḍa 2) only; the commentary Amarakośodghāṭana of Kṣīrasvāmin named in the edition is NOT in this file.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Study of Svatantrika_Donald S  Lopez, 2016.txt
texts.note: Colophon names Amarasiṃha. Author's biography legendary (linked to Vikramāditya's 'nine gems'); identity secure as the kośa's author. Root text only here, not Kṣīrasvāmin's ṭīkā.

texts.title: Sarvajñatākāradhāraṇī [Karuṇāpuṇḍarīka(sūtra)]
texts.basis: GRETIL header names no author; the text is a Buddhist dhāraṇī extracted from / associated with the Karuṇāpuṇḍarīkasūtra, edited by Isshi Yamada (1968), input by Klaus Wille. As canonical Mahāyāna scriptural material it has no human author.
texts.note: Anonymous dhāraṇī text; Yamada is editor, not author. No traditional personal ascription.

texts.title: Viṣṇudharma
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. R. Grünendahl 1983-89) names no author. Secondary lit (Inden/Ali/Walters; skt-en-mono citing Grünendahl pt 3) treats it as an anonymous Vaiṣṇava dharma/purāṇic text, internally framed with Mārkaṇḍeya as 'author'/narrator, date uncertain (3rd-11th c. CE). Composite anonymous tradition.
texts.note: Anonymous; internal narrator-frame ascribes it to Mārkaṇḍeya (a literary/traditional device, not real authorship). Distinct from Viṣṇudharmottarapurāṇa.

texts.title: Viṃśatikākārikā (Vijñaptimātratāsiddhi)
texts.basis: GRETIL file bsa023_u is the Viṃśatikākārikā (twenty verses), part of Tripathi's ed. of the Vijñaptimātratāsiddhi prakaraṇadvayam; final colophon 'viṃśatikākārikāḥ samāptāḥ'. This file contains ONLY the kārikā, not Vasubandhu's own vṛtti. Chatterjee (1980) states the prakaraṇadvayam = Viṃśatikā-kārikā and Triṃśikā-kārikā composed by Vasubandhu. Verses securely Vasubandhu's.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Vasubandhu’s Vijnapti-Matrata-Siddhi. With Sthiramati's Commentary_K. N. Chatterjee, 1980.txt
texts.note: Workitem title/author were blank; resolved as Vasubandhu's Viṃśatikākārikā. Only the 20 kārikā are in this file (no vṛtti, no Sthiramati ṭīkā), so single-author, not conflated.

texts.title: Mahāvastu-avadāna
texts.basis: GRETIL header self-identifies the work as belonging to the Āryamahāsāṃghika Lokottaravāda Madhyadeśika Vinayapiṭaka ('āryamahāsāṃghikānāṃ lokottaravādināṃ...mahāvastuye ādi'). Nakamura attributes its compilation to the Lokottaravādins; Tatelman notes it 'was compiled' over time. A composite, multi-layered anonymous corpus with no single named author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey...Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Tatelman_The Legend of the Buddha's Wife...txt
texts.note: Anonymous communal/sectarian compilation (Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda Vinaya). Layered/redacted over centuries; no individual author — collective tradition, not a conflation of named authors.

texts.title: Abhidharmakośabhāṣya Chapter 9
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Vasubandhu and marks kārikās in bold vs the bhāṣya prose — both by Vasubandhu (the auto-commentary on his own verses). Nakamura and others uniformly credit both the Abhidharmakośa(kārikā) and its bhāṣya to Vasubandhu. Chapter 9 (Pudgalaviniścaya / refutation of the self) is prose-only and has no kārikās, but is still Vasubandhu's bhāṣya.
texts.note: Single author for both verse and commentary (Vasubandhu's auto-bhāṣya), so NOT a conflation — unlike the Maitreya/Vasubandhu split of the MSA. Ch.9 is the appended ātmavāda-pratiṣedha, prose only.

texts.title: Kāmasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Vatsyayana, Kamasutram', digitalized by Mizue Sugita, based on the 1900 Nirnayasagara ed. with Yasodhara's commentary, but the e-text contains ONLY the Kamasutra mula, not the Jayamangala. Bendall catalogue: 'Vatsyayana, son of Mallanaga'. Basham, Potter EIP: attributed to sage Vatsyayana, early centuries CE. Yasodhara (Jayamangala tika) is a separate, much later commentator and is NOT in this e-text.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Bendall_Catalogue of the Sanskrit manuscripts in the British museum.txt
texts.note: Mula only; the cited edition merely USED Yasodhara's Jayamangala commentary, which is not part of this e-text, so no conflation. Full name Vatsyayana Mallanaga (Mallanaga = patronymic/personal name).

texts.title: Kāmasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL/TEI header (input Jean Fezas, 2020): mula text by Vatsyayana, 'Interpretive markup: none'; no Jayamangala present. Same secure tradition as the parallel item; Vatsyayana son of Mallanaga (Bendall catalogue).
texts.note: Mula-only TEI e-text; Yasodhara's Jayamangala (cited in edition metadata) not included, so no conflation.

texts.title: Pañcaskandhaprakaraṇa
texts.basis: Brunnholzl (Luminous Heart) and Karmay/Nagano repeatedly cite the work as 'Vasubandhu's Pancaskandhaprakarana' (D4059); Sthiramati wrote a separate Pancaskandhakavaibhasya and Gunaprabha a commentary, none of which are in this prakarana e-text. A distinct same-titled Pancaskandhaprakarana by Candrakirti exists (Lindtner, AO 40) but is a different work.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Luminous Heart. Third Karmapa on Consciousness, Wisdom, and Buddha Nature_Karl Brunnholzl, 2009.txt
texts.note: Root prakarana by Vasubandhu; do not confuse with Candrakirti's homonymous text or with Sthiramati's vaibhasya.

texts.title: Triṃśikāvijñaptikārikā [Triṃśikākārikā]
texts.basis: Standard ascription: Vasubandhu's 30 verses (Levi ed. 1925, Vimsatika et Trimsika). Chatterjee (Vijnapti-Matrata-Siddhi) notes Vasubandhu composed the Trimsika-karika, on which ten commentaries were written (Sthiramati's bhasya being the famous one). This e-text is the karika alone, not Sthiramati's bhasya.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Vasubandhu's Vijnapti-Matrata-Siddhi. With Sthiramati's Commentary_K. N. Chatterjee, 1980.txt
texts.note: Karika (root verses) by Vasubandhu only; Sthiramati's Trimsikabhasya is a separate work, not present here, so no conflation.

texts.title: Trisvabhāvanirdeśa
texts.basis: Traditionally ascribed to Vasubandhu (Anacker, Seven Works; Nakamura; Steinkellner Festschrift notes the Skt ms). However Notz, Lexikon des Buddhismus, explicitly: 'dem aelteren Vasubandhu zugeschrieben... Die Autorschaft des Vasubandhu gilt indes als nicht endgueltig gesichert' (authorship not definitively secured). Text unavailable in Chinese (Kapstein), survives only Tibetan + a Skt ms.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Lexikon des Buddhismus. Grundbegriffe – Traditionen – Praxis_Klaus-Josef Notz, 2001.txt
texts.note: Ascribed to Vasubandhu but authorship explicitly not definitively secured per Notz; downgraded from secure to disputed. Likely the (older) Yogacara Vasubandhu if genuine.

texts.title: Bodhicittotpādasūtraśāstra
texts.basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism: 'The Bodhicittotpada-sastra ascribed to Vasubandhu, whose Sanskrit original is lost'; authorship is ascribed but 'One scholar doubted its authenticity, believing it was composed in China.' Hirakawa index: T1659 attributed to Vasubandhu (天親). The Sanskrit is a modern reconstruction by Shantibhiksu Shastri (Visva-Bharati Annals 1949) from the Chinese, not an original.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Attributed to Vasubandhu but authenticity doubted (possibly Chinese composition); Skt original lost, e-text is a reconstruction. Current 'Vasubandhu (attributed)' is accurate; status sharpened to pseudepigraphic.

texts.title: Madhyāntavibhāgabhāṣya [Madhyāntavibhāgakārikābhāṣya]
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: root kārikās ascribed to Maitreyanātha, bhāṣya by Vasubandhu; colophon 'kṛtir ācāryabhadantavasubandhoḥ'. Secondary (John Younghan Cha; Nakamura via Cha): Maitreya credited with the kārikās, traditionally transmitted via Asaṅga, Vasubandhu wrote the commentary.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Study of Dharmadharmatavibhaga...Vasubandhu's Commentary_John Younghan Cha.txt
texts.note: Classic Maitreya/Asaṅga/Vasubandhu Yogācāra layering. current_author 'Vasubandhu' covers only the bhāṣya; kārikās are by Maitreya(nātha), with transmission associated with Asaṅga.

texts.title: Pañcaśatikāprajñāpāramitā [Pañcaśatikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: Anonymous Mahāyāna Prajñāpāramitā sūtra ('Perfection of Wisdom in 500 Lines'); like all PP sūtras it bears no human author and is presented as buddhavacana. Web (encyclopedic/scholarly sources on Prajñāpāramitā) confirm no named author; edition is text-critical (Li Xuezhu & Fujita).
texts.note: Canonical anonymous sūtra; no source_url e-text header. Traditionally buddhavacana; uses Yogācāra trisvabhāva terminology but not author-attributable.

texts.title: Vaikhānasamantrapraśna
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the Vaikhānasa school of Vikhanas)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Vaikhānasa school; traditionally ascribed to Vikhanas / sages of the Kāśyapa-Atri-Marīci lineage)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: input by H. J. Resnick; title 'Śrī-vaikhānasa-mantra-praśnāṣṭake'; internal attributions to Kāśyapa ('iti kāśyapaḥ') and colophon-type references to the Vaikhānasa ṛṣi lineage (kāśyapāvatsāra-naiddhruva). The corpus is foundational scripture of the Vaikhānasa school traditionally traced to the sage Vikhanas.
texts.note: Composite Vaikhānasa ritual scripture; no single historical author. Traditional ascription to Vikhanas / Kāśyapa-lineage sages; effectively anonymous school text.

texts.title: Yājñavalkyasmṛti
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to the sage Yājñavalkya)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ascribed to Yogīśvara Yājñavalkya, with Mitākṣarā commentary of Vijñāneśvara (the commentary is a separate work, not in this bare-text e-text). Secondary (Lindquist, 'In Search of a Sage': Yājñavalkya is a legendary/literary figure 'credited with' the legal treatise; the Vedic seer is not its author). Web (Rocher, Wikipedia): a dharma scholarly tradition/compendium quoting Manu etc., not a single-author composition.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_IN SEARCH OF A SAGE. YAJNAVALKYA...Steven Edward Lindquist.txt | web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y%C4%81j%C3%B1avalkya_Sm%E1%B9%9Bti
texts.note: Pseudepigraphic-by-tradition: ascribed to the legendary sage Yājñavalkya; actual author(s) unknown, likely a redacted compendium. Mitākṣarā of Vijñāneśvara is a distinct commentarial work, absent from this e-text.

texts.title: Pāśupatasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL e-text is the bare sūtra only (no Lakulīśa or Kauṇḍinya named in header; flagged needs-proofreading). Secondary (N. N. Bhattacharyya, 'History of the Tantric Religion': 'The name of its author is not known but it was commented by Rāśīkara Kauṇḍinya'). Pāśupata tradition attributes the sūtra to Lakulīśa (legendary founder); Kauṇḍinya's Pañcārthabhāṣya (5th c.) is a separate commentary not present in this e-text.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion_N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Lakulīśa is legendary/uncertain; scholarship says author unknown. Distinguish root sūtra (this text) from Kauṇḍinya's Pañcārthabhāṣya commentary, which is NOT in this e-text.

texts.title: Pudgalavastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Pudgalavastu = Vastu 12 of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu, ed. N. Dutt from Gilgit MSS; colophon 'pudgalavastu samāptam'. Secondary (Prebish, 'Survey of Vinaya Literature'): canonical vinaya section, anonymous collective/redacted monastic-disciplinary text, no individual author.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical vinaya chapter (Mūlasarvāstivāda); communal/redacted, traditionally buddhavacana. N. Dutt is the modern editor, not author.

texts.title: Kośāmbakavastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: no individual author; canonical Vastu 9 of Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu, ed. N. Dutt, input K. Wille; colophon only 'kośāmbakavastu samāptam'. Secondary lit (Schopen; Gnoli) treats the MSV as a late anonymous communal compilation/redaction.
texts.note: Canonical Vinaya section; no named author. Traditionally word of the Buddha; anonymous redactors. Modern editor N. Dutt only.

texts.title: Mahāparinirvāṇasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: critical edition by E. Waldschmidt (Berlin 1950-51), input K. Wille; no authorship claim, Waldschmidt's role purely editorial. Secondary lit (Nakamura) describes the sūtra as compiled, not authored.
texts.note: Sanskrit (Mūlasarvāstivāda) recension of the canonical sūtra; anonymous communal composition, traditionally buddhavacana. Not the Mahāyāna Mahāparinirvāṇa-mahāsūtra. Editor Waldschmidt only.

texts.title: Adhikaraṇavastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 16th section of the Mūlasarvāstivādin Vinaya, ed. R. Gnoli (Roma 1978), input S. Karashima/proof K. Wille; colophon 'adhikaraṇavastu samāptam', no author. MSV treated as anonymous late compilation in secondary lit.
texts.note: Canonical Vinaya section; no named author. Modern editor R. Gnoli only.

texts.title: Cīvaravastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: monastic legal text (Vinayavastu) of the Mūlasarvāstivādin school, ed. N. Dutt, input K. Wille with corrections to MS; no individual author named. Consistent with secondary lit on the anonymous MSV redaction.
texts.note: Canonical Vinaya section; no named author. Modern editor N. Dutt only.

texts.title: Poṣadhasthāpanavastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Vinayavastu section, ed. N. Dutt (part 3), input K. Wille; no authorship attribution, teachings framed as spoken by bhagavān (Buddha) but no historical author. Anonymous communal text.
texts.note: Canonical Vinaya section; no named author. Traditionally buddhavacana. Modern editor N. Dutt only.

texts.title: Saṅghabhedavastu 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 17th and last section of the Mūlasarvāstivādin Vinaya, ed. R. Gnoli with T. Venkatacharya (Roma 1977-78), input Karashima/Wille; no author. Secondary lit (Gnoli intro; Almogi vol.; Bapat) speaks of 'redactors'/'compiler' of the Saṅghabhedavastu and of the MSV as a fairly late compilation.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Gilgit manuscript of Sanghabhedavastu (Mulasarvastivadin). Part I_Raniero Gnoli, 1977.txt
texts.note: Part I of canonical Vinaya section; anonymous redactors/compiler, no individual author. Modern editor R. Gnoli only.

texts.title: Mahāmeghasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL e-text edition (Bendall 1880, 'The Megha-sūtra', JRAS) names only the editor, no original author. Web (Wikipedia/Tsadra/Encyclopedia of Buddhism): a Mahāyāna tathāgatagarbha-type sūtra; Mahāyāna sūtras are effectively anonymous compiled literature; known only via Chinese tr. by Dharmakṣema (5th c.) and Tibetan tr. by Surendrabodhi/Yeshe Dé (9th c.).
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra; no traditional personal author. Source GRETIL htm (n443u) returned 404 at fetch time; edition cites only editor Bendall.

texts.title: Karmavastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Karmavastu of the Vinayavastvagama of the Mūlasarvāstivādin' (Vastu 10 of the Vinayavastu); edition N. Dutt (Gilgit Mss vol. III), input Klaus Wille, corrections vs. ms. No author named. Canonical Vinaya = collective monastic/school redaction, not a single author.
texts.note: Section (vastu 10) of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu; canonical, anonymous. Editor Dutt, transcriber Wille are modern, not authors.

texts.title: Abhidharmakośabhāṣya
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Vasubandhu credited for both kārikās (verses, shown bold, 'VAkK') and bhāṣya (commentary); best reading on Pradhan ed. with corrections by Hackett/Lusthaus. Secondary (Nakamura 1980, Indian Buddhism, p.~2686): 'The main body of this work consists of kārikās, and Vasubandhu himself wrote a com[mentary]' — i.e. auto-commentary. Yaśomitra's Sphuṭārthā is a separate vyākhyā (sub-commentary), not part of this file.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Two roles (root kārikā + auto-bhāṣya) but SAME author Vasubandhu, so not a multi-author conflation. Yaśomitra (vyākhyā) is a distinct later author but is not included in this edition.

texts.title: Kaṭhinavastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Kaṭhinavastu of the Vinayavastvagama of the Mūlasarvāstivādin (= Vastu 8 of the Vinayavastu)'; edition Matsumura 1996; source ms. GBM; input Klaus Wille. Colophon: 'kaṭhinavastu samāpta // //'. No author named. Canonical Vinaya = collective monastic/school redaction.
texts.note: Section (vastu 8) of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu; canonical, anonymous. Colophon names no author/scribe; Matsumura/Wille are modern editors.

texts.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-III [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition Takayasu Kimura 1986, input Klaus Wille (Dec 2006); reference-only file, no original author named. Prajñāpāramitā sūtras are anonymous compiled Mahāyāna scripture transmitted across recensions of varying sizes.
texts.note: Vols II-III of the 25,000-line Perfection of Wisdom; canonical Mahāyāna sūtra, anonymous. Kimura/Wille are modern editor/transcriber.

texts.title: Ṛgvedavedāṅgajyotiṣa (Ārca/Ṛk recension of the Vedāṅgajyotiṣa)
texts.basis: No source_url (not in GRETIL htm corpus at given path). Edition Kuppanna Sastry & Sarma 1985 (INSA), 'Vedāṅga Jyotiṣa of Lagadha in its Ṛk and Yajus Recensions'. Web (Wikipedia/WorldCat/Internet Archive): traditionally attributed to the sage Lagadha; survives in two recensions (Ṛk = 36 verses, Yajus = 44). The Yajus recension appears edited a few centuries later than the Ṛk on linguistic grounds; both ascribed to Lagadha by tradition.
texts.note: This item = the Ṛk (Ārca) recension specifically (the earlier of the two). Lagadha is a legendary/traditional ascription, not securely a historical individual; treated as traditional, confidence medium. Yajus recension is a related but later-edited parallel, not conflated here.

texts.title: Pāṇḍulohitakavastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editor (Yamagiwa) and inputter (K. Wille); no original author. Text is Buddhavacana ('bhagavān āha'), part of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinayavastu — a collectively transmitted/redacted monastic corpus, not an authored work.
texts.note: Section of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya (Vinayavastu); traditionally Buddhavacana. Edited from Gilgit MS by N. Yamagiwa (2001); no individual author.

texts.title: Gaṇḍavyūhasūtra [Gaṇḍavyūha]
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC header (ed. P. L. Vaidya 1960) names no author; canonical Mahāyāna sūtra presented as revealed doctrine. Secondary lit (Nakamura, Indian Buddhism; Fontein; Hamar Huayan studies) treats it as the final/culminating book of the Avataṃsaka, a composite text 'issued from different hands'.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Anonymous; composite/gradual composition, culminating chapter of the Buddhāvataṃsaka. Sudhana's pilgrimage. No single author.

texts.title: Sāṃkhyakārikā [Sāṃkhyasaptati]
texts.current_author: Īśvarakṛṣṇa (kārikās), with the bhāṣya of Gauḍapāda
texts.basis: GRETIL header: kārikās by Īśvarakṛṣṇa, bhāṣya by Gauḍapāda (Colebrooke/Wilson tr.). Web (IEP; Wikipedia; researchgate Suvarṇasaptati study): the Gauḍapādabhāṣya attribution is disputed — Satchidanandendra rejects it; scholars debate whether this Gauḍapāda is the Māṇḍūkyakārikā Gauḍapāda (Śaṅkara's parama-guru); commentary is one of a family of five related Sāṃkhya commentaries.
texts.note: Two distinct authors conflated under one title: root verses = Īśvarakṛṣṇa; bhāṣya = 'Gauḍapāda' (identity/authenticity disputed, not securely the Māṇḍūkya Gauḍapāda).

texts.title: Poṣadhavastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editor (Hu-von Hinüber 1994) and inputter (K. Wille); no original author. Buddhavacana ('bhagavān āha'); part of the Mūlasarvāstivādin Vinayavastu, a collectively transmitted monastic corpus.
texts.note: Section of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya governing the Poṣadha/uposatha observance; traditionally Buddhavacana; no individual author.

texts.title: Amaruśataka
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'amarukaviracitam', with commentary (Rasikasaṃjīvinī) of Arjunavarmadeva; footnotes also cite Vemabhūpāla and Rudramadeva. Secondary (skt-en-mono: 'Amaruka, reputed author'; anthologies ascribe some verses elsewhere; interpolation noted). Web: legendary/disputed authorship (king Amaru; ironically ascribed to Śaṅkara via the body-entering legend); multiple recensions (~72 core verses common), suggesting an anthology-like collection.
texts.note: Edition conflates the verse-collection (traditionally Amaru/Amaruka, legendary, possibly an anthology — recensions diverge; some verses attributed to others) with Arjunavarmadeva's commentary. Verse authorship traditional/uncertain.

texts.title: Sāṃkhyakārikā [Sāṃkhyasaptati]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: kārikās by Īśvarakṛṣṇa with the Māṭharavṛtti (Māṭharācārya), ed. V. P. Śarmā 1970; colophon verses (ISk 70-73) give Sāṃkhya transmission (Kapila > Āsuri > Pañcaśikha > Īśvarakṛṣṇa). Web (Oxford JHS / researchgate Suvarṇasaptati study): consensus that the extant Māṭharavṛtti is the LATEST of five related commentaries, datable 9th c. onward — its early/Māṭhara authorship is disputed.
texts.note: current_author lists only Īśvarakṛṣṇa but this edition conflates root kārikā (Īśvarakṛṣṇa) with the Māṭharavṛtti commentary (ascribed to Māṭhara; extant version late/disputed, possibly post-9th c.).

texts.title: Sāṃkhyakārikā [Sāṃkhyasaptati]
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Īśvarakṛṣṇa as author; transmission verses 70-71 give lineage paramarṣi/Kapila -> Āsuri -> Pañcaśikha -> Īśvarakṛṣṇa who condensed it into verse. Secondary lit (Potter bibliography) uniformly cites 'Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā', with separate later commentaries (Māṭharavṛtti, Jayamaṅgalā, Yuktidīpikā, Gauḍapādabhāṣya, Suvarṇasaptati) by other hands. GRETIL e-text = the kārikā text itself.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Digital file is the kārikā only (author Īśvarakṛṣṇa); the named edition merely bundles separate later commentaries (Māṭharavṛtti, Jayamaṅgalā), which are NOT by Īśvarakṛṣṇa. No conflation in the e-text itself.

texts.title: Viṣṇu-Purāṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL text: narrative frame has 'śrī-parāśara uvāca', Parāśara teaching Maitreya, and section colophons read 'iti śrī-viṣṇu-purāṇe...'. Parāśara is the legendary in-text narrator/speaker, the traditional ascription; scholarship treats the Purāṇa as an anonymous, multi-layered redactional compilation, not the work of a single historical author.
texts.note: Parāśara is a legendary/framing ascription (sage, narrator to Maitreya), not historical authorship; composite anonymous text. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Pāramitāsamāsa
texts.basis: DSBC/GRETIL header gives no colophon author. Tradition ascribes it to Āryaśūra. Secondary lit (Richardson, Shalu Murals diss): Carol Meadows argues doctrinal divergences between the Jātakamālā and the Pāramitāsamāsa imply DIFFERENT authorship, i.e. the Pāramitāsamāsa-Āryaśūra may not be the famous Jātakamālā-Āryaśūra (whose own identity is itself debated vs Aśvaghoṣa/Mātṛceṭa). Meadows 1986 is the standard ed./study.
texts.source: secondary:Richardson_Shalu Murals_diss vol 1.txt; secondary:Hahn_Haribhaṭṭa and Gopadatta; web:https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/6fbb728a8305d4d98e8612d11ff2c8ae459cd200
