texts.note: Single-author work ascribed to Āryaśūra; DISPUTED whether this Āryaśūra = the Jātakamālā author (Meadows: likely different). Not a multi-author conflation.

texts.title: Suvarṇavarṇāvadāna
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Braarvig input from Roy 1971 ed.) explicitly describes it as 'an unknown Mahāyāna(avadāna) text from Tibet' of unknown authorship; no traditional colophon naming an author/scribe. An anonymous avadāna; Tibetan translation by Dharmaśrībhadra and Rin chen bzaṅ po (translators, not original author).
texts.note: Genuinely anonymous avadāna; no author known. Tibetan translators Dharmaśrībhadra + Rin chen bzaṅ po noted as translators only. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Viṃśatikā vijñaptimātratāsiddhi [Viṃśikāvṛtti]
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'viṃśatikā vijñaptimātratāsiddhiḥ | kṛtir iyam ācāryavasubandhoḥ'. Header marks kārikās in bold within the prose vṛtti. Secondary lit (Chatterjee 1980; Das 2012): Vasubandhu composed the Viṃśatikā-kārikā and HIMSELF wrote the vṛtti (autocommentary). So both verse and commentary are by one author — an autocommentary, not separate hands.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:Vasubandhu's Vijnapti-Matrata-Siddhi (Chatterjee 1980).txt; secondary:Concept of consciousness in yogacara (Das 2012).txt
texts.note: kārikā + vṛtti both by Vasubandhu (autocommentary), so single author in two roles — NOT a conflation despite verse/commentary structure. ('Two Vasubandhus' Frauwallner debate concerns dating/identity generally, not split authorship of this text.)

texts.title: Pārivāsikavastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Wille input from Dutt's Gilgit Manuscripts ed.): a section (vastu) of the Mūlasarvāstivādin Vinayavastu; no individual author identified. Closing 'pārivāsikavastu samāptam' is a section-end marker, not an authorial colophon. Canonical monastic-discipline literature is a collective, anonymous redactional product of the school.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Vinaya section of the Mūlasarvāstivāda school; no individual author. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Arthaviniścayasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya, Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha) names no author; colophon 'ārya-arthaviniścayo nāma dharmaparyāyaḥ samāptaḥ', no individual author/scribe. Presented as discourse of the Buddha. This Vaidya edition is the sūtra text alone (no commentary).
texts.note: Canonical Mahāyāna sūtra, anonymous (Buddhavacana). Traditionally word of the Buddha; no human author named. Distinct from the Samtani edition which adds Vīryaśrīdatta's commentary.

texts.title: Arthaviniścayasūtra
texts.basis: Edition is Samtani 1971 'The Arthaviniścaya-sūtra & its Commentary (Nibandhana)'. Secondary lit (Nakamura 1980; Mejor 1991; Warder 2000) states the Nibandhana commentary was written by Bhikṣu Vīryaśrīdatta of Nālanda, 8th c. (reign of Dharmapāla 770-810), per its colophon. The sūtra itself is anonymous Buddhavacana.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakosa and the Commentaries Preserved in the Tanjur_Marek Mejor, 1991_OCR.txt; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism_Anthony Kennedy Warder, 2000.txt
texts.note: Title conflates two distinct works: anonymous sūtra + Vīryaśrīdatta's Nibandhana commentary (8th c. Nālanda). The bare 'anonymous' attribution misses the named commentator embedded in this edition.

texts.title: Sāṃkhyakārikā [Sāṃkhyasaptati]
texts.basis: GRETIL header credits the Sāṃkhyakārikā to Īśvarakṛṣṇa (secure) and the Jayamaṅgalā commentary to 'Śaṃkara-ārya'. This is the H. Sarma 1926 edition of the Jayamaṅgalā (Sāṃkhyasaptatiṭīkā). Web/secondary: Īśvarakṛṣṇa's authorship of the kārikā is secure; the Jayamaṅgalā's self-ascription to Śaṅkara(ārya) — implying Ādi Śaṅkara — is widely doubted, not counted among Śaṅkara's authentic works.
texts.source: gretil-header; web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samkhyakarika; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 4 - Potter 1987.txt
texts.note: Two distinct authors. Root verses = Īśvarakṛṣṇa (secure). Commentary = 'Śaṅkara-ārya', a disputed/doubtful attribution (likely not Ādi Śaṅkara). current_author lists only the kārikā poet, omitting the commentator who is the actual edited text.

texts.title: Raghuvaṃśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header: base text (Kashmirian recension) by Kālidāsa as read by Vallabhadeva, the commentator (Raghupañcikā). Edition = Goodall & Isaacson 2003, 'The Raghupañcikā of Vallabhadeva, the earliest commentary on the Raghuvaṃśa'. Secondary (Epperson) dates Vallabhadeva's Northern/Kashmirian commentary to ca. 10th c.
texts.note: Poet Kālidāsa (secure) plus commentator Vallabhadeva (Raghupañcikā, earliest extant comm., ~10th c. Kashmir). This e-text embeds the Kashmirian text as transmitted within Vallabhadeva's commentary; current_author omits the commentator.

texts.title: Meghadūta
texts.basis: GRETIL header (De/Raghavan ed.) names Kālidāsa; closing colophon reads 'iti kālidāsaviracitaṃ meghadūtaṃ samāptam'. Single-author lyric poem; no commentary embedded in this e-text.
texts.note: Securely Kālidāsa per colophon. Plain text of the poem only (no commentary).

texts.title: Abhijñānaśākuntala
texts.basis: GRETIL header (M.R. Kale ed.) names Kālidāsa as author. Plain drama text (Devanāgarī recension); no commentary embedded. Kālidāsa's authorship of the play is uncontested.
texts.note: Securely Kālidāsa. Bare play text (Kale recension); commentary not embedded in this e-text.

texts.title: Devatāsūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (canonical Buddhist sūtra; ed. A. Mette)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: anonymous canonical sūtra from Gilgit manuscripts (GBM 1542.5-1545.3), opens 'evaṃ mayā śrutam', no author/scribe colophon; ed. A. Mette 1981, input Klaus Wille. Secondary lit (Kudo, Karmavibhaṅga quotation notes) treats it as an anonymous canonical sūtra cited elsewhere.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist canonical sūtra; no traditional named author. Edition/input figures noted as editor/redactor roles, not composers.

texts.title: Raghuvaṃśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header: root kāvya by Kālidāsa (sargas 1-6 in this file); critical edition is of Vallabhadeva's Raghupañcikā, the earliest (Kashmirian, ~10th c.) commentary, ed. Goodall & Isaacson 2003. Secondary lit confirms Vallabhadeva's Northern commentary dated 10th c.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_(Meghadūta commentary studies, Vallabhadeva 10th c.)
texts.note: Root verses securely Kālidāsa; the edition this e-text derives from is Vallabhadeva's commentary, a distinct author/role. Conflation: poet vs commentator.

texts.title: Meghadūta
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Meghadūta by Kālidāsa with the commentary of Vallabhadeva, ed. Eugen Hultzsch (RAS 1911), input Asko Parpola. Secondary lit places Vallabhadeva's Northern commentary at ~10th c.
texts.note: Root poem securely Kālidāsa; e-text edition bundles Vallabhadeva's commentary (distinct author). Conflation: poet vs commentator.

texts.title: Meghadūta
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Meghadūta by Kālidāsa, based on M.R. Kale's edition which carries Mallinātha's commentary (Sañjīvanī). Secondary lit dates Mallinātha to ~14th-15th c. (Southern/Northern commentary tradition).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_(Meghadūta commentary study, Mallinātha 14th-15th c.)
texts.note: Root poem securely Kālidāsa; Kale edition is the one with Mallinātha's 14th-c. commentary (distinct author). Conflation: poet vs commentator.

texts.title: Ṛtusaṃhāra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: attributed to Kālidāsa, ed. Kālidāsagranthāvalī 1980, input O. Hellwig; header takes no stance. Secondary lit: 'The authorship of the Rtusamhara is doubtful'; also ascribed to Kumāradāsa in the anthology Śārṅgadhara-paddhati; 'about the Ṛtusaṃhāra there is a difference of opinion'.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Kālidāsa is widely doubted; alternative ascription to Kumāradāsa in Śārṅgadhara-paddhati. Status: disputed, not secure.

texts.title: Kumārasambhava
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Kumārasambhava by Kālidāsa, input Utz Podzeit (this file = cantos I-VIII, the authentic core, marked Ks_1.1 etc.). Secondary lit: cantos IX-XVII are 'believed not to be his' (later continuation), so the genuine portion is cantos I-VIII.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Kale Introduction (cantos IX-XVII not genuine)
texts.note: Cantos I-VIII securely Kālidāsa. The non-genuine cantos IX-XVII (later author) are not in this e-text, so no conflation here; flagging the authenticity boundary for the title generally.

texts.title: Raghuvaṃśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header = Scharpé's Kālidāsa-Lexicon text of the Raghuvaṃśa, ascribed to Kālidāsa. Secondary lit (Winternitz, de Bary, Olson, skt-en-mono) uniformly cites 'Raghuvaṃśa of Kālidāsa' as the standard mahākāvya; Kālidāsa's name is given in the colophons. (By contrast the Ṛtusaṃhāra is noted as of doubtful authorship, not the Raghuvaṃśa.)
texts.note: Single-author mahākāvya, attribution secure. The edition is a text-only extract from a lexicon, no commentary bundled.

texts.title: Pāśupatasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL/edition based on Ananthakrishna Sastri's Pāśupatasūtras with Pañcārthabhāṣya of Kauṇḍinya (TSS 143). Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra; Goudriaan/Padoux Festschrift; Watson, Self's Awareness) treats Lakulīśa (Lakulin) as the founder-teacher to whom the sūtras are ascribed, the bhāṣya being by Kauṇḍinya. This work-item is the SŪTRA text, not the commentary.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra; secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism (Goudriaan)
texts.note: Sūtra traditionally ascribed to Lakulīśa, founder of the Pāśupata/Lakulīśa-Pāśupata school; legendary/traditional ascription, not documentarily secure. Pañcārthabhāṣya commentary (by Kauṇḍinya) is a SEPARATE text and not part of this sūtra item — no conflation within this record.

texts.title: Āptamīmāṃsā [Devāgamastotra]
texts.basis: GRETIL = Gajādhara Lal Jain ed., Āptamīmāṃsā of Samantabhadra (1914). Secondary lit (Ganeri, Philosophy in Classical India; Matilal/Ganeri/Tiwari, Character of Logic in India: 'Samantabhadra, seventh century. Author of Āptamīmāṃsā'; Vidyabhusana, Mediaeval School: extolled as author of the Devāgama; Pandey, Buddhist Conception of Omniscience) uniformly credit the Jain logician Samantabhadra. The Āptamīmāṃsā = the Devāgamastotra (kārikās).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Character of Logic in India; secondary:cleaned_History of the Mediaeval School of Indian Logic
texts.note: Root kārikās by Samantabhadra. Later commentaries (Akalaṅka's Aṣṭaśatī, Vidyānanda's Aṣṭasāhasrī) are separate works and not part of this text item; no conflation.

texts.title: Saddharmapuṇḍarīkasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL = Vaidya ed. (BST 6). Traditionally a sermon of Śākyamuni Buddha (legendary ascription). Modern scholarship (Karashima, 'Who composed the Lotus Sutra'; web/encyclopedia consensus) treats it as an anonymous composite work redacted in stages by multiple unknown authors over c. 1st–2nd c. CE (Tamura's three strata).
texts.note: Anonymous; traditional ascription to the Buddha (Śākyamuni). Composite/stratified by multiple anonymous redactors, but no NAMED distinct authors to enumerate, so conflation_flag=false (no identifiable conflated persons). Anonymous authors stand collectively.

texts.title: Bodhisattvaprātimokṣasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL = Nalinaksha Dutt's edition (IHQ VII, 1931), an editio princeps from manuscript; Dutt is the EDITOR, not author. A Mahāyāna prātimokṣa sūtra with no named composer; traditionally buddhavacana. Secondary lit gives no individual author attribution.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Prebish_Text and Tradition in the Study of Buddhist Ethics.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra (buddhavacana by tradition). Dutt = modern editor only. No conflation of distinct authors.

texts.title: Ratnagotravibhāga [Mahāyānottaratantraśāstra / Uttaratantra]
texts.current_author: Maitreya (verses, per Tibetan tradition) / Sāramati (per Chinese tradition); commentary ascribed to Asaṅga — disputed
texts.primary_author: Maitreya (kārikā, Tibetan tradition); Asaṅga (vyākhyā, Tibetan tradition) / Sāramati (whole work, Chinese tradition)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Johnston ed.): the text combines ślokas with prose vyākhyāna — colophon 'ratnagotravibhāge mahāyānottaratantraśāstre ... ślokārthasaṃgrahavyākhyānataḥ samāptaḥ', i.e. root verses + embedded commentary. Tibetan tradition: verses by Maitreya, prose commentary by Asaṅga. Chinese tradition: whole attributed to Sāramati (Jianyi); Zhiyi 6th c. Takasaki argues the commentator is Sāramati. Johnston earlier (mis)attributed to Sthiramati. Saka fragment names Maitreya as author of the mūla verses. Nakamura: Saramati c.350–450 likely composer per Chinese tradition.
texts.note: Classic multi-author/conflation case (parallel to Mahāyānasūtrālaṅkāra). The single GRETIL title bundles kārikās + vyākhyā by different ascribees: per Tibetan tradition kārikā=Maitreya, vyākhyā=Asaṅga; per Chinese tradition the whole=Sāramati. Attributions unresolved/disputed across traditions; roles separated here.

texts.basis: Work item is empty: no title, no current_author, no edition, no source_url, source_corpus=unknown. ID pattern (K14 'sf259' fragment) suggests an unidentified Buddhist Sanskrit manuscript fragment; nothing identifiable to attribute.
texts.note: Empty record; no metadata to triangulate. Treated as anonymous/unidentified fragment.

texts.title: Śrīghanācārasaṃgraha
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Mahāsāṃghika root saṃgraha); reconstructed by Sanghasena Singh from Jayarakṣita's ṭīkā
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC header: original composer not identified; this text was reconstructed by Sanghasena Singh from Jayarakṣita's Sphuṭārthā Śrīghanācārasaṃgrahaṭīkā. Prebish, Survey of Vinaya Literature Vol.1 (l.1606,2591): the Sphuṭārthā Śrīghanācārasaṃgrahaṭīkā by Jayarakṣita is a commentary ON the Śrīghanācārasaṃgraha (a Mahāsāṃghika Vinaya summary); ṭīkā ed. Sanghasena, TSWS XI, Patna 1968.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Survey of Vinaya Literature Vol.1_Charles S. Prebish, 1994.txt
texts.note: Multi-layer: anonymous root saṃgraha (Mahāsāṃghika), ṭīkā by Jayarakṣita, modern reconstruction by Sanghasena Singh. Root author distinct from commentator Jayarakṣita.

texts.title: Saṃghāṭasūtra [Saṅghāṭasūtra]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Mahāyāna sūtra; framed as discourse of the Buddha)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Canevascini 1993, input Peters/Wille): no human author; presented as a Mahāyāna sūtra spoken by the Buddha (Bhagavān) to the bodhisattva Sarvaśūra. Standard sūtra self-attribution to the Buddha, no historical composer named.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra; traditional speaker = Buddha, interlocutor Sarvaśūra. No human authorship.

texts.title: Matsya-Purāṇa
texts.primary_author: anonymous; traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa (narrated by Sūta)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Calcutta/Caukhamba 1954, input Hellwig): no human author stated; internal framing 'viṣṇunā yadudīritam' presents content as divine teaching narrated by Sūta to the ṛṣis. Like all purāṇas, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa; actually a composite anonymous compilation.
texts.note: Traditional Vyāsa ascription (low confidence); in reality an anonymous, multi-layered compilation. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Ekādaśamukha
texts.primary_author: anonymous (dhāraṇī text; framed as revealed by Avalokiteśvara)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. N. Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts I, pp.33-40): no human author; the eleven-faced (Avalokiteśvara) dhāraṇī is framed as spoken by Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva to the Buddha at Śrāvastī. Liturgical dhāraṇī material, no historical composer.
texts.note: Anonymous Avalokiteśvara dhāraṇī (Ekādaśamukha/Eleven-Faced); traditional speaker = Avalokiteśvara.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1, Pāda 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Gopālakṛṣṇācārya, Madras 1900): the e-text integrates three textual levels — mūla Brahmasūtra by Bādarāyaṇa, Madhva's bhāṣya, and Jagannāthayati's ṭippaṇī (dīpikā). Edition title itself: 'Jagannātha-yati-kṛta-ṭippaṇī-saṃvalita-Madhva-Bhāṣya-sametāni'. Web: Jagannāthayati a Dvaita commentator within Madhva's tradition. Bādarāyaṇa traditionally identified with Vyāsa.
texts.note: One title conflates three distinct authors: root sūtra (Bādarāyaṇa), bhāṣya (Madhva), ṭippaṇī (Jagannāthayati). current_author lists only the root sūtra author.

texts.title: Śyāmalādaṇḍaka
texts.primary_author: Kālidāsa (traditionally attributed; spurious per modern scholarship)
texts.basis: GRETIL header & colophon (Kāvyamālā ed., Durgaprasad & Parab) name 'mahākaviśrīkālidāsakṛtaṃ Śyāmalādaṇḍakam' and repeat it in the colophon. Web (Britannica/EBSCO, Wikipedia Kalidasa): the Śyāmalādaṇḍaka is among ~20 spurious works wrongly attributed to Kālidāsa; multiple 'Kālidāsa' authors theory.
texts.note: Edition asserts Kālidāsa, but the attribution is traditional/spurious; a devotional stotra-daṇḍaka to Śyāmalā (Mātaṅgī), not securely by the classical Kālidāsa. current_author already flags 'traditionally attributed'.

texts.title: Śiṣyalekha (alternate)
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'kṛtirācāryacandragomipādasya' (the work of Ācārya Candragomin), Minaev ed. Secondary lit (de Jong; Buddhist epistolary tradition) consistently ascribes the Śiṣyalekha (verse epistle) to Candragomin.
texts.note: Verse letter of instruction to a pupil; attribution to Candragomin (c. 5th-7th c.) is uncontested. Distinct from Candragomin's grammatical works.

texts.title: Bhavasaṅkrāntiṭīkā
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon present whole as 'Maitreyanāthakṛtā Bhavasaṅkrāntiṭīkā' / 'paṇḍitamaitreyanāthakṛtaḥ' and read it as a unified ṭīkā. BUT a ṭīkā embeds the root verses it comments on: Aiyaswami Sastri's 1938 Adyar edition is titled 'Bhavasaṅkrānti Sūtra and Nāgārjuna's Bhavasaṅkrānti Śāstra with the Commentary of Maitreyanātha' (web:worldcat/archive.org/siteofenlightenment PDF). Secondary lit (Nakamura 1980; Ramanan 1966) attributes the Bhavasaṅkrānti-śāstra (root) to Nāgārjuna; the commentary to Maitreyanātha (4th c.).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Nagarjuna's Philosophy..._K. Venkata Ramanan, 1966, 1987.txt; web:https://www.worldcat.org/title/oclc/959252
texts.note: Two distinct authors conflated: root Bhavasaṅkrānti (kārikā/śāstra) ascribed to Nāgārjuna; the ṭīkā/bhāṣya by Maitreyanātha. Both attributions themselves debated, but the multi-author root-vs-commentary structure is the key correction. current_author named only the commentator.

texts.title: Nidānasaṃyukta
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Tripāṭhī ed. 1962, Turfan finds; input Karashima) makes no authorship claim. It is a collection of 25 canonical sūtras (Nidāna-saṃyukta) of the Saṃyukta-āgama; canonical buddhavacana with no named author, transmitted/redacted within a Sarvāstivāda lineage.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical āgama text (Turfan Sanskrit fragments); traditionally buddhavacana. No individual author; matches current 'anonymous/traditional'.

texts.title: Jātakamālā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Michael Hahn & K. Wille) attributes throughout to Haribhaṭṭa; no divergent colophon. Secondary lit (Hahn, 'Haribhaṭṭa and Gopadatta: Two Authors in the Succession of Āryaśūra'; de Jong 1987; Wood 2005) treats Haribhaṭṭa as the author, active early 5th c., modelling his work on Āryaśūra's Jātakamālā.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Brief History of Buddhist Studies in Europe and America_J. M. de Jong, 1987.txt; secondary:cleaned_Buddha and the Shape of Belief..._Leela Aditi Wood, 2005.txt
texts.note: Distinct from Āryaśūra's (Ārya Śūra's) more famous Jātakamālā, which Haribhaṭṭa took as model. Author secure (Haribhaṭṭa); transmission partly via Tibetan, edition reconstructed by Hahn.

texts.title: Āgamaśāstra 1: Āgamaprakaraṇa
texts.primary_author: Gauḍapāda (kārikā, book 1); Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad mantras (anonymous śruti, interleaved); Śaṅkara (commentary)
texts.basis: GRETIL e-text interleaves three layers: the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad mantras (MandUp_, anonymous śruti), Gauḍapāda's kārikās (MandUpK_), and Śaṅkara's commentary (MandUpC_/MandUpKC_), checked vs Limaye-Vadekar (mūla) and Kathavate (kārikās). Secondary lit (R. King 1995, 'Early Advaita Vedanta and Buddhism'; V. Bhattacharya 1943) debates the unity of authorship of the Gauḍapādīya-kārikā/Āgamaśāstra: Bhattacharya's thesis is the GK is composite, four separate works, with the fourth prakaraṇa especially distinct (possibly a different, Buddhist-influenced hand). Book 1 (Āgamaprakaraṇa) is the prakaraṇa most tied to the Māṇḍūkya mantras and most plausibly by 'Gauḍapāda'.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Early Advaita Vedanta and Buddhism. Mahayana Context of  Gaudapadiya-karika_Richard King, 1995.txt
texts.note: This file (Āgamaprakaraṇa, book 1) conflates: anonymous Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad śruti mantras + Gauḍapāda's kārikās + Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. Authorship of GK as a whole disputed (Bhattacharya/King: composite, four prakaraṇas possibly distinct authors); 'Gauḍapāda' itself a quasi-legendary paramaguru figure. Book 1 attribution to Gauḍapāda is the least contested of the four.

texts.title: Viṣṇu-Purāṇa
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Parāśara, related to Vyāsa)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally narrated by Parāśara to Maitreya)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ascribed to the legendary sage Parāśara, who narrates to Maitreya; ed. M. M. Pathak (critical ed.), input Peter Schreiner. Colophons read 'iti śrīviṣṇupurāṇe' with no historical author. Purāṇa is an anonymous, multi-layered compilation conventionally framed as Parāśara's narration.
texts.note: Traditional/legendary ascription to Parāśara is a narrative frame, not historical authorship; text is an anonymous redacted compilation.

texts.title: Dharmasaṃgraha
texts.basis: GRETIL header and colophon claim authorship: 'iti nāgārjunapādaviracito 'yaṃ dharmasaṃgrahaḥ samāptaḥ'. Secondary lit (Nariman 1919: 'passes for his production'; Westerhoff 2010: 'attributed to Nāgārjuna'; Joshi) treat ascription as traditional only. Web/Lindtner regard it as questionable/inauthentic; multiple 'Nāgārjunas' confound attribution.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Dispeller of Disputes. Nagarjunas Vigrahavyavartani_Jan Westerhoff, tr., 2010.txt
texts.note: Colophon names Nāgārjuna but scholars (Lindtner) consider it spurious; a short glossary of Buddhist technical terms attributed to one of several 'Nāgārjunas'.

texts.title: Avikalpapraveśadhāraṇī [Avikalpapraveśadhāraṇīsūtra]
texts.basis: Canonical Mahāyāna dhāraṇī-sūtra (buddhavacana), no source_url; reconstructed Sanskrit (Matsuda / D. Bhattacharya) from Tibetan-Sanskrit witnesses. Like all sūtras it carries no human author; conventionally framed as the word of the Buddha.
texts.note: Anonymous sūtra; no authorial colophon. Traditionally buddhavacana.

texts.title: Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra [Avalokiteśvaraguṇa-Kāraṇḍavyūha(sūtra)]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Vaidya, Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha): no traditional author named; titled Avalokiteśvaraguṇa-Kāraṇḍavyūha. Secondary lit (Obeyesekere 2004, Shakya 1994, Tuladhar-Douglas) dates composition to/after 4th c., anonymous; distinct from the later verse recast Guṇakāraṇḍavyūha (GKV) often confused with it.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra (prose recension); not to be conflated with the later verse Guṇakāraṇḍavyūha, a separate title.

texts.title: Citralakṣaṇa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (frame ascribed to Nagnajit; chapters ascribed variously to Nagnajit, Viśvakarman/Brahmā, Prahlāda)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Nagnajit is a character (recipient of instruction from Brahmā/Viśvakarman), not clearly the composer; Sanskrit original lost, survives in Tibetan (ed./tr. A. C. Sastri 1987 from Laufer). Ruegg: 'le premier chapitre est attribué à Nagnajit' — only ch.1 to Nagnajit; web (Shastri) notes the three chapters are ascribed to Nagnajit, Viśvakarman and Prahlāda, and the Nagnajit attribution may be erroneous or partial.
texts.note: Title conflates a multi-chapter work whose chapters bear different (legendary) ascriptions — only ch.1 to Nagnajit per Ruegg; Nagnajit may be the narrative recipient, not author. Original Sanskrit lost; extant via Tibetan.

texts.title: Yamasmṛti
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to the sage Yama)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Jürgen Neuß, South Indian recension, MA thesis FU Berlin 1999): dialogue of ṛṣayaḥ asking and Yamaḥ answering; ascribed to the divine sage Yama, no historical author. Multiple recensions/manuscripts (12 mss for adhyāya 1, a second adhyāya in a single Grantha ms) indicate an anonymous smṛti compilation.
texts.note: Legendary ascription to the god Yama; an anonymous Dharmaśāstra smṛti transmitted in variable recensions.

texts.title: Carmavastu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: section 5 (Carmavastu) of the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinayavastu, ed. N. Dutt from Gilgit MSS; colophon only 'Carmavastu samāptam', no named author; input by Klaus Wille. Prebish, Survey of Vinaya Literature, lists Carmavastu as the 5th vastu (leather/shoes, Śroṇa Koṭīviṃśa story) of the canonical Vinayavastu — an anonymous monastic compilation, traditionally Buddhavacana.
texts.note: Canonical Vinaya text, no individual author; traditionally ascribed to the Buddha/monastic transmission. Modern editor N. Dutt; digital input K. Wille.

texts.basis: No title, no current_author, no source_url. Edition note: Klaus Wille SHT-type Turfan/Sanskrithandschriften aus den Turfanfunden fragment edition. SHT fragments are anonymous unidentified manuscript scraps; Wille is the modern editor, not the author.
texts.note: Unidentified Turfan/SHT Sanskrit fragment (sf258); no work-title or author recoverable. Editor Klaus Wille.

texts.title: Subhāṣitaratnakaraṇḍakathā
texts.current_author: attributed to Āryaśūra (also given as Śūra/Aśvaghoṣa; uncertain)
texts.primary_author: Āryaśūra (per colophon; identity/dating uncertain — distinct from the classical Jātakamālā Āryaśūra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. M. Hahn 1983, input K. Wille): colophon reads 'kṛtir ācāryaśūrasya' = composition of Āryaśūra; 27 narrative sections + 6 pāramitā chapters, a late Buddhist puṇyaśāstra. Web (Nariman ch.5, Wikipedia/JIP): Āryaśūra is frequently confused with Aśvaghoṣa and Mātṛceṭa, and famous names were freely attached to anonymous works; this late text is not by the classical Jātakamālā Āryaśūra, hence attribution is uncertain.
texts.note: Colophon ascribes to Āryaśūra; alternative ascriptions Śūra/Aśvaghoṣa; Hahn treats it as a late text — identity of this 'Āryaśūra' and the rival Aśvaghoṣa ascription leave attribution uncertain, but a single author (not a conflation of roles).

texts.title: Brahmasūtra: Adhyāya 1, Pāda 3
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Bādarāyaṇa: Brahmasūtra, with Bhāṣya and Dīpikā' — three layers: root sūtra by Bādarāyaṇa, bhāṣya by Madhva (Ānandatīrtha), ṭippaṇī/dīpikā by Jagannātha-yati; edited by Gopālakṛṣṇācārya (Madras 1900). Bādarāyaṇa traditionally identified with Vyāsa.
texts.note: Single GRETIL title bundles three distinct authors across three layers (sūtra/bhāṣya/dīpikā); current_author lists only Bādarāyaṇa — the Madhva bhāṣya and Jagannātha dīpikā are separate authors.

texts.basis: No title, no current_author, no source_url. Edition note: Klaus Wille SHT-type Turfan/Sanskrithandschriften aus den Turfanfunden fragment edition. Anonymous unidentified manuscript fragment; Wille is modern editor.
texts.note: Unidentified Turfan/SHT Sanskrit fragment (sf107); no work-title or author recoverable. Editor Klaus Wille.

texts.title: Saṃghāṭa Sūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Ārya Saṃghāṭa Sūtra, critical edition by Oskar von Hinüber from Gilgit Sanskrit MSS (Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon / Nagarjuna Institute). Text opens 'namaḥ sarvabuddhabodhisattvebhyaḥ'; no human author colophon — a Mahāyāna sūtra presented as the word of the Buddha. Von Hinüber is the modern editor.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra, traditionally Buddhavacana; no individual author. Critical editor Oskar von Hinüber.

texts.title: Triratnasaundaryagāthā
texts.basis: DSBC header names only modern input personnel (Miroj Shakya 2018, Nagarjuna Institute / University of the West); no traditional author/translator. Colophon 'iti śrītriratnasaundaryagāthāyāṁ prakṛtiratnaṁ samāptam' marks completion only, no composer named. No mention in local secondary literature; web search returns only the DSBC catalogue entry with no authorship.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist devotional gāthā in praise of the Three Jewels; colophon gives no author/scribe. No traditional ascription found.

texts.title: Mārkaṇḍeya-Purāṇa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa; narrated by sage Mārkaṇḍeya)
texts.basis: GRETIL header reads 'śrīmanmaharṣivedavyāsapraṇītaṃ śrīmārkaṇḍeyapurāṇam' — traditional ascription to Veda-Vyāsa; chapter colophons frame narration by sage Mārkaṇḍeya (to Jaimini, via the talking birds). Standard purāṇic pseudo-authorship: actual composition is anonymous, multi-layered, by unknown hands over centuries. Local literature confirms Mārkaṇḍeya as legendary narrator (son of Mṛkaṇḍu), not historical author.
texts.note: Conventional Vyāsa ascription is legendary, not a real conflation of distinct historical authors; text is anonymous composite. Mārkaṇḍeya = in-text narrator persona.

texts.basis: Untitled SHT-type Sanskrit manuscript fragment (sf253) edited by Klaus Wille from the Turfan finds (Sanskrithandschriften aus den Turfanfunden); no title, no source_url, no colophon. Such Turfan fragments are anonymous canonical/scriptural fragments of unidentified or partially identified Buddhist works; the GRETIL/SHT corpus credits only the modern editor (Wille).
texts.note: Anonymous unidentified Buddhist manuscript fragment; Wille is editor, not author. No work-title recoverable from item data.

texts.title: Ṣaṇmukhīdhāraṇī
texts.basis: DSBC text is the dhāraṇī itself (within a Dhāraṇīsaṃgraha collection; input Miroj & Milan Shakya 2018); no author/translator in header or colophon, as expected for canonical scripture (buddhavacana). Web (84000 / Toh 526, 916) confirms the root dhāraṇī is anonymous; only its later commentary (Ṣaṇmukhīdhāraṇīvyākhyāna, Toh 3989) is ascribed to Vasubandhu, and a subcommentary (IOL Tib J 430) to Jñānadatta — but those are separate works, NOT part of this root text.
texts.note: Root dhāraṇī anonymous. Commentarial attributions (Vasubandhu vyākhyāna; Jñānadatta subcommentary) belong to distinct texts not present here — so no conflation within this item.

texts.title: Pāḍatāḍitaka
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'śrīmahākaviśyāmilakaviracitaṃ pādatāḍitakam'; colophon 'iti kaver udīcyasya viśveśvaradattaputrasya āryaśyāmilakasya kṛtiḥ pādatāḍitakaṃ nāma bhāṇaḥ samāptaḥ' names Śyāmilaka, son of Viśveśvaradatta. Local secondary literature (Schokker 1966 ed.; Dezső & S. Vasudeva, Quartet of Causeries/Caturbhāṇī; Verardi; Olivelle; von Hinüber) uniformly attributes this bhāṇa to Śyāmilaka, dated 2nd half of 5th c. CE (Kashmir). One source notes a variant naming 'Saumillaka'.
texts.note: Single-author bhāṇa; secure attribution to Śyāmilaka. Title spelled 'Pāḍatāḍitaka' in item = Pādatāḍitaka. Variant author-name Saumillaka noted but Śyāmilaka standard.

texts.title: Ekādaśamukhahṛdaya
texts.primary_author: anonymous (canonical dhāraṇī of the Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (input Somadeva Vasudeva, from N. Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts I, 1939) names no author/translator; manuscript has no authorial colophon. As Buddhist scripture (the 'heart'/hṛdaya dhāraṇī of Ekādaśamukha Avalokiteśvara) it is anonymous buddhavacana. Local literature references the Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara sūtra/spells (Xuanzang) as a scriptural cult text, not an authored treatise.
texts.note: Anonymous dhāraṇī text from the Gilgit corpus; S. Vasudeva is e-text inputter only. No author/scribe in colophon; traditional buddhavacana.

texts.title: Prāṇāgnihotra (Prāṇāgnihotra Upaniṣad)
texts.basis: sanskritdocuments header lists only 'Vedic tradition' as author and Sunder Hattangadi as transliterator/encoder (no classical author). Secondary (Potter bibliography) confirms it as one of the minor Atharva/Kṛṣṇayajurveda Upaniṣads with traditional commentaries: Upaniṣad-brahmayogin's commentary in the 108-Upaniṣad Adyar collection and Nārāyaṇa's Dīpikā.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt | web:https://sanskritdocuments.org/doc_upanishhat/pranagni.itx
texts.note: Root Upaniṣad anonymous (traditional Buddha-word equivalent: 'Vedic'/śruti). Commentators are distinct later authors: Upaniṣad-brahmayogin (Adyar 108) and Nārāyaṇa (Dīpikā); these are separate roles, not conflated into one title here.

texts.title: Samādhirājasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya 1961, BST 2) names no author, only editor/inputters; text framed as Buddha-word (Candrapradīpa-sūtra). Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, 1980): compiled prior to 557 CE, probably posterior to Kaniṣka; dialogue text. Web (Wikipedia / P.A. Roberts): no identifiable author; very likely a compilation of earlier shorter works, no Sanskrit MS earlier than 6th c.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt | web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samadhiraja_Sutra
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra, also called Samādhirāja-candrapradīpa-sūtra; layered compilation, no single human author. Traditionally Buddha-word.

texts.basis: Work item has empty title and empty current_author; source_url is the generic SuttaCentral root (https://suttacentral.net/) with no edition. No identifiable text to triangulate, so no author can be determined without inventing one.
texts.note: Unresolvable: no title, no edition, generic portal URL. id 'SA_K10_uvs_sc' suggests a SuttaCentral-sourced item but the specific text is not specified. Left anonymous; no traditional ascription available.

texts.title: Boudhatantrokta Siddhauṣadhi Saṅgraha
texts.basis: DSBC page (canon-text 811/3006) lists only Miroj Shakya (input/proofreader), Nagarjuna Institute (supplier), University of the West (sponsor); no original author/scribe and no printed critical edition. Title itself ('Bauddhatantrokta Siddhauṣadhi Saṅgraha' = 'collection of accomplished/efficacious medicines stated in the Buddhist tantras') and internal references to the Caṇḍamahāroṣaṇa-tantra mark it as a compiled tantric materia-medica anthology. No secondary-literature match found.
texts.note: A saṅgraha (compilation) drawing material from Buddhist tantras (esp. Caṇḍamahāroṣaṇa-tantra); compiler unnamed. Anonymous; current_author already empty so no meaningful change.

texts.title: Āmoghapāśanāmahṛdayasūtra [Āryāmoghapāśanāmahṛdayasūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Dhīḥ 38, 2004, ed. CIHTS; input Klaus Wille) names no author; text is a dhāraṇī/hṛdaya sūtra framed as Buddha-word spoken via Avalokiteśvara. No colophon naming a composer. No secondary attribution to a human author found.
texts.note: Anonymous Avalokiteśvara dhāraṇī sūtra. Same underlying text as SA_K14_bsu002_u but a different edition (CIHTS/Dhīḥ vs NIEM/Shakya); not multi-author, so no conflation.

texts.title: Āmoghapāśanāmahṛdaya sūtra [Ārya amoghapāśahṛdaya nāma mahāyānasūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. M.B. Shakya, NIEM Nepal 1988; via DSBC/Nagarjuna Institute) names only the modern editor and digitizers, no original author; presented as Buddha-word sūtra, no colophon naming a composer.
texts.note: Anonymous Avalokiteśvara dhāraṇī sūtra; M.B. Shakya is editor, not author. Same text as SA_K14_amoghapu in a different edition.

texts.title: Nāradasmṛti
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to the sage Nārada)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: text based on Lariviere's critical ed. (1989); editor named, but no human author — only the legendary sage Nārada as traditional ascription. Dharmaśāstra of composite/anonymous authorship; Nārada is a divine eponym (pseudepigraphic), not a historical writer.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to sage Nārada is eponymous/pseudepigraphic; actual compiler unknown. Edited (not authored) by R. W. Lariviere.

texts.title: Bṛhaspatismṛti
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Bṛhaspati); modern reconstruction by K. V. Rangaswami Aiyangar
texts.primary_author: anonymous (ascribed to Bṛhaspati); reconstructed by K. V. Rangaswami Aiyangar
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Brhaspatismrti (reconstructed)', ed. K. V. Rangaswami Aiyangar (GOS 85, 1941); only the Vyavahārakāṇḍa is present, the other six kāṇḍas excluded. The original smṛti survives only in citations; Aiyangar reconstructed it from quotations. Ascribed to the divine teacher Bṛhaspati (eponymous).
texts.note: Text does not survive as a unit; it is a modern reconstruction (Aiyangar) of an anonymous dharma text under the eponym Bṛhaspati. Only Vyavahārakāṇḍa digitised.

texts.title: Saddharmalaṅkāvatārasūtra [Laṅkāvatārasūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya ed. 1963): no human author; presented as a sūtra spoken by the Buddha Śākyamuni to Mahāmati. Secondary lit (Nakamura, Indian Buddhism) notes several versions and that the sūtra was compiled (4th c.), i.e. a composite/redacted scriptural text but anonymous, not attributable to named authors.
texts.note: Composite/compiled Mahāyāna sūtra (multiple recensions, interpolated Sagāthakam etc.), but redactors are unknown; remains anonymous Buddhavacana, not a conflation of named authors.

texts.title: Hastavālaprakaraṇavṛtti
texts.current_author: attributed to Āryadeva (or Dignāga; authorship disputed)
texts.primary_author: Dignāga (probable; kārikā and auto-vṛtti) — Tibetan tradition ascribes to Āryadeva
texts.basis: Secondary: Nakamura (Indian Buddhism) — Tibetan tradition ascribes to Āryadeva but 'must be wrong' as the work sets forth Vijñaptimātra/Representation-Only; Amar Singh — Tibetan colophon = Āryadeva, but Hattori/Frauwallner doubt this, content is Sautrāntika/Yogācāra (Dignāga). Web (Ruegg via search): likely Dignāga; Chinese tradition ascribes to Dignāga, Tibetan to Āryadeva. The verses (kārikā) and the vṛtti (auto-commentary) are by the same hand.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dign%C4%81ga
texts.note: Disputed Dignāga (Chinese tradition, most scholars) vs Āryadeva (Tibetan colophon). Single author for both kārikā and vṛtti — not a multi-author conflation. Sanskrit is a Tucci reconstruction from Tib/Chi.

texts.title: Kātyāyanasmṛti [Kātyāyanasmṛtisāroddhāra]
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Kātyāyana); reconstruction by P. V. Kane
texts.primary_author: anonymous (ascribed to Kātyāyana); reconstructed by P. V. Kane
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'reconstructed' Kātyāyanasmṛti on vyavahāra, ed. P. V. Kane (1933). The smṛti survives only via citations; Kane reconstructed/extracted (sāroddhāra) the vyavahāra verses. Ascribed to the sage/grammarian-eponym Kātyāyana; no historical author secured.
texts.note: Modern reconstruction (Kane) of a lost anonymous dharma text under the eponym Kātyāyana; pseudepigraphic ascription, Kane as redactor/compiler.

texts.title: Abhidharmadīpakārikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: author 'Vimalamitra(?)' with a question mark = uncertain; kārikās extracted from the commented version (Jaini ed., 1959). Secondary (Mejor, Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakośa and the Commentaries): Jaini argues the author is unknown — Saṅghabhadra unlikely, and Vimalamitra or Iśvarasena were only 'suggested' as potential authors; conventionally cited as the anonymous 'Dīpakāra'. The kārikā and its Vibhāṣāprabhāvṛtti are by the same anonymous author; this item is only the kārikā.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakosa and the Commentaries Preserved in the Tanjur_Marek Mejor, 1991_OCR.txt; gretil-header
texts.note: Author genuinely unknown ('Dīpakāra'); Vimalamitra/Iśvarasena/Saṅghabhadra are unverified scholarly guesses (Jaini rejects identification). GRETIL's 'Vimalamitra(?)' is tentative. Kept anonymous, not invented.

texts.title: Abhidharmadīpakārikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives author as 'Vimalamitra(?)' with explicit question mark and notes this file is kārikās 1-383 only. Anacker (Vasubandhu, Three Aspects) states 'Dīpakāra' is the name assigned to the author of the *anonymous* Abhidharmadīpa; the figure is a Neo-Vaibhāṣika polemicist against Vasubandhu's Kośa. Potter, Cox, Kwan all refer to him only as 'the Dīpakāra'.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Vasubandhu. Three Aspects. Study of a Buddhist Philosopher_Stefan Anacker, 1970.txt
texts.note: Author genuinely unknown; 'Dīpakāra' = epithet meaning 'author of the Dīpa'. Vimalamitra is a tentative, questioned identification only. GRETIL file = kārikās only (full bhāṣya Vibhāṣāprabhāvṛtti is separate); kārikā and vṛtti are by the same anonymous author per Jaini.

texts.title: Nāradasmṛti
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to the sage Nārada)
texts.basis: GRETIL header names no historical author, only Lariviere as critical editor (1989) and Y. Ikari as input. Dharmaśāstra convention: a smṛti is pseudonymously attributed to a legendary sage (here Nārada); the actual composition is by anonymous redactors and survives in distinct recensions (Lariviere edited the 'vulgate'/critical text).
texts.note: Legendary ascription to the sage Nārada; no individual human author. Text exists in multiple recensions; current_author already correct.

texts.title: Triṃśikāvijñaptibhāṣya [Triṃśikābhāṣya]
texts.basis: GRETIL title is 'Vasubandhu's Triṃśikāvijñaptikārikā with Sthiramati's Triṃśikāvijñaptibhāṣya'; closing colophon reads 'triṃśikāvijñaptibhāṣyaṃ samāptam || kṛtir ācāryasthiramateḥ ||' (work of master Sthiramati). Nakamura and Mejor confirm the bhāṣya is Sthiramati's, commenting on Vasubandhu's 30 root verses.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: The edited text embeds both works: root kārikā by Vasubandhu, bhāṣya by Sthiramati. current_author 'Sthiramati' is correct for the bhāṣya but conflates the two roles; recorded both.

texts.title: Sukhāvatyamṛtavyūha [Sukhāvatī(amṛta)vyūhasūtra], the Smaller Sukhāvatīvyūha
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya, Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha 1961) names only the editor; the text is a canonical Mahāyāna sūtra with no author or translator named. Traditionally framed as buddhavacana.
texts.note: Anonymous sūtra; no individual author. current_author already correct.

texts.title: Ālambanaparīkṣāvṛtti
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: vṛtti is 'ācāryadiṅnāgakṛtā' (composed by master Diṅnāga) — i.e. Diṅnāga's own autocommentary on his Ālambanaparīkṣā kārikā. Ganeri and Aiyaswami Sastri (edition title) confirm kārikā + vṛtti both by Diṅnāga; Dharmapāla wrote a *separate* sub-commentary (in the printed edition but NOT in this GRETIL file).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Philosophy in Classical India. The Proper Work of Reason_Jonardon Ganeri, 2001.txt
texts.note: Single author Diṅnāga for both kārikā and vṛtti (svavṛtti). The Sanskrit is a restoration from Tibetan/Chinese (Aiyaswami Sastri 1942). Dharmapāla's commentary, named in the edition title, is a distinct work not contained in this file.

texts.title: Bhaiṣajyaguruvaidūryaprabharājasūtra
texts.basis: DSBC page (text from Vaidya, Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha 1961) names no author or translator; canonical Mahāyāna sūtra, traditionally buddhavacana.
texts.note: Anonymous sūtra; no individual author. current_author already correct.

texts.title: Prajñāpāramitāpiṇḍārtha
texts.basis: GRETIL header reads 'Ācāryadignāgaviracitaḥ' and the closing colophon 'kṛtir ācāryadignāgapādānām', attributing the verse-summary to Dignāga. Tucci's editio princeps (JRAS 1947, 'Minor Sanskrit Texts on the Prajñāpāramitā: 1. The Prajñāpāramitāpiṇḍārtha of Diṅnāga') and secondary references (Amar Singh; Keenan) treat it as Dignāga's work. Single-author verse text, no commentary embedded.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Heart of Buddhist Philosophy. Dinnaga and Dharmakirti_Amar Singh, 1984.txt; web:Tucci 1947
texts.note: Colophon names Dignāga; attribution undisputed. Editor/translator Tucci is editorial only, not an author role for the Sanskrit text.

texts.title: Pañcaskandhakavibhāṣā
texts.basis: The Pañcaskandhakavibhāṣā is Sthiramati's commentary on Vasubandhu's root prakaraṇa, the Pañcaskandhaka, which it incorporates and explains. Secondary lit is unanimous: Gokhale, 'The Pañcaskandhaka by Vasubandhu and the commentary by Sthiramati' (lists both authors); Kramer 2013 critical edition (Sthiramati's PSKV); Stuart and Sharf both pair 'Vasubandhu's Pañcaskandhaka and Sthiramati's Pañcaskandhakavibhāṣā'. The GRETIL e-text per the Kramer edition is the commentary.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt (Gokhale 175.11.1); secondary:cleaned_Stuart_Yogācāra Substrata.txt; secondary:cleaned_Candragomin and the Bodhisattva Vow_Tatz.txt
texts.note: Two distinct authors by role: root mūla Pañcaskandhaka by Vasubandhu, vibhāṣā commentary by Sthiramati. Current_author 'Sthiramati' correctly names the commentator but omits the root-text author Vasubandhu whose work the title comments on.

texts.title: Śiva-Purāṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Venkateśvara Steam Press ed., input Jun Takashima 2001) names only editor/inputter, no author. Like all Mahāpurāṇas the Śiva-Purāṇa is a composite, anonymously compiled and redacted text traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa; no historical author. Modern scholarship treats it as anonymous/accreted.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Vyāsa is legendary, applied generically to all Purāṇas; the work is anonymous and multi-layered. Current_author already records this correctly.

texts.title: Yogasūtrabhāṣya
texts.current_author: attributed to Vyāsa (Vedavyāsa); possibly Patañjali himself
texts.primary_author: Patañjali (modern consensus, as part of Pātañjalayogaśāstra); traditionally Vyāsa (Vedavyāsa)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Āgāśe ed., ĀnSS 47) labels it 'Śrīmadvyāsabhāṣya', the traditional ascription to Vyāsa. But Maas's thesis (summarized in White, Yoga in Practice, pp.1200-1208) holds the sūtra+bhāṣya form a single composition (Pātañjalayogaśāstra) by one author, Patañjali (c.400 CE); the earliest ascription of the bhāṣya to 'Vedavyāsa' appears only c.1100 CE in Vācaspatimiśra. Web confirms (Freschi, Sahapedia, Wujastyk): 'Vyāsa' is a later/legendary author for what is Patañjali's own auto-commentary.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_White_Yoga in Practice.txt; secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse.txt; web:elisafreschi.com; web:sahapedia.org
texts.note: Disputed/pseudepigraphic: traditional ascription to Vyāsa (Vedavyāsa) is post-1100 CE and rejected by Maas, who argues the bhāṣya is Patañjali's own (unified Pātañjalayogaśāstra). Not a multi-author conflation but a contested single attribution. Current_author already flags both possibilities.

texts.title: Bhaiṣajyaguruvaidūryaprabharājasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya, Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha 1961) and colophon 'āryabhaiṣajyaguruvaidūryaprabharājaṃ nāma mahāyānasūtram' name no author, as expected for a Mahāyāna sūtra. Secondary lit (Nakamura) discusses Chinese translators (Śrīmitra, Xuanzang) and possible Central Asian origin, but no Indian author is known.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra; only later translators (Buddha) named, not authors. Current_author correct.

texts.title: Buddhist Yoga Manual
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Schlingloff, Ein buddhistisches Yogalehrbuch 1964; repr. Hartmann/Röllicke 2006; input Klaus Wille) names only editors. The text is a fragmentary Turfan/Kučā (Qïzïl) birch-bark manuscript with no surviving colophon; title and authorship unknown. Web (GRETIL/scholarly) and secondary refs (Davidson; Bronkhorst; Analayo) treat it as anonymous, authored by an unnamed Sarvāstivādin practitioner-yogin, c. 7th c. or earlier.
texts.source: gretil-header; web:gretil byogalbu; secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism.txt; secondary:cleaned_Buddhism in the Shadow of Brahmanism_Bronkhorst, 2011.txt
texts.note: No colophon survives; author genuinely unknown. Schlingloff and Hartmann/Röllicke are modern editors, not authors. Current_author correct.

texts.title: Bṛhaspatismṛti: Vyavahārakāṇḍa
texts.primary_author: Bṛhaspati (legendary/eponymous); historical author anonymous; text reconstructed by K. V. Rangaswami Aiyangar
texts.basis: GRETIL header: reconstructed text (Aiyangar 1941), opens '[śrī brhaspatismṛtiḥ]', no historical author claimed — traditional eponymous ascription to the sage/deity Bṛhaspati. Mylius (Geschichte der altindischen Literatur): the Bṛhaspatismṛti survives only fragmentarily, closely modeled on the Manusmṛti; Jolly translated the fragments (SBE). Sanderson cites it as a normative dharmaśāstra. The work is a smṛti = eponymous, historically anonymous.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Geschichte der altindischen Literatur_Klaus Mylius_2003 (1983).txt
texts.note: Eponymous dharmaśāstra ascribed to sage/deity Bṛhaspati; real author unknown; text is a modern reconstruction from scattered citations (nibandha quotations) by Aiyangar. Vyavahārakāṇḍa = juridical section.

texts.title: Adbhutadharmaparyāya [Adbhutadharmaparyāyasūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition from Bentor 1988 (3 incomplete Gilgit mss), input Jens-Uwe Hartmann; makes no authorship claim — presented as a Buddhist sūtra without human author, per genre convention. Traditionally Buddhavacana (word of the Buddha). Bentor's title itself ('The Redactions of...') signals a multi-recension transmission but no single named composer.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna/Buddhist sūtra; traditional ascription to the Buddha (Buddhavacana). Multiple Gilgit redactions; editor Bentor reconstructed the text.

texts.title: Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition by Harrison & Watanabe 2006 (Schøyen Collection), input Klaus Wille; no individual author attributed, presented as established canonical text; ms breaks off at section 16c with no colophon. Web (Wikipedia/Buddha-Nature): the Diamond Sūtra is traditionally attributed to the Buddha (Buddhavacana), not an individual author; Kumārajīva is a translator (into Chinese), not author.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna Prajñāpāramitā sūtra; traditional ascription to the Buddha. No author named in the Sanskrit edition; Kumārajīva etc. are translators only.

texts.title: Ḍākārṇavatantra (Śrīḍākārṇavamahāyoginītantrarāja), Chapter 15; and Jayasena's Ratnapadmarāganidhi (meditation manual)
texts.basis: OAPEN/Sugiki 2022 (title + WebSearch): the volume critically edits TWO distinct works — (1) the Ḍākārṇava ch. 15, an anonymous Buddhist Saṃvara-tradition tantra/scripture compiled ~early 12th c. in eastern India (no individual author; āgama/revealed scripture), and (2) Jayasena's Ratnapadmarāganidhi, a 12th-c. meditation manual (sādhana for the Heruka maṇḍala of Ḍākārṇava 15) authored by the named author Jayasena. Sugiki is the modern editor/translator. The item id literally chains 'Āgama-Jayasena' confirming two authorship layers.
texts.note: CONFLATION: one edition bundles an anonymous revealed tantra (scripture, no author) with Jayasena's named meditation manual. Distinct authorship roles. current_author was empty; now resolved to two distinct situations.

texts.title: Abhidharmasamuccayabhāṣya
texts.primary_author: Bhāṣya: disputed — Buddhasiṃha + Sthiramati (Chinese tradition) or Jinaputra (Tibetan tradition); on the root Abhidharmasamuccaya by Asaṅga
texts.basis: GRETIL header: only editorial provenance (Tatia 1976), no author named. Web (Wikipedia Sthiramati/Abhidharma-samuccaya): the bhāṣya is attributed to Sthiramati in the Chinese tradition and to Jinaputra in the Tibetan; per Chinese tradition Buddhasiṃha wrote the commentary and Sthiramati added the root text to form the Vyākhyā; the bhāṣya is textually close to Sthiramati's Triṃśikābhāṣya. Secondary (Warder, Indian Buddhism): root Abhidharmasamuccaya = Asaṅga; 'Jinaputra: Commentary on Abhidharmasamuccaya (Tibetan)'. Nakamura/Sferra also note the layered transmission (Jinaputra; 'additional leaf').
texts.note: Layered/disputed: root verses+prose Abhidharmasamuccaya by Asaṅga; the bhāṣya itself variously ascribed — Buddhasiṃha (composer) with Sthiramati (redactor combining root+bhāṣya into vyākhyā) in Chinese tradition vs Jinaputra in Tibetan. Distinct roles separated.

texts.title: Dharmadhātuvāgīśvaramaṇḍalastotra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (tantric stotra of the Dharmadhātuvāgīśvara maṇḍala)
texts.basis: No source_url. Edition note: stotra of the Dharmadhātuvāgīśvara-maṇḍala, a maṇḍala described in Abhayākaragupta's Niṣpannayogāvalī (ed. B. Bhattacharyya, GOS 109, 1949). The stotra is a liturgical hymn to the maṇḍala deities with no named author; Abhayākaragupta is the author of the Niṣpannayogāvalī (the maṇḍala-description text), not necessarily of this stotra. No attribution of the stotra to an individual found.
texts.note: Anonymous tantric praise-hymn (stotra) for the Dharmadhātuvāgīśvara maṇḍala. Associated with Abhayākaragupta's Niṣpannayogāvalī only as the source describing the maṇḍala; no individual author of the stotra attested.

texts.title: Sukhāvatīvyūha
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition by P.L. Vaidya, Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgrahaḥ 1961; no author named, closes with standard ye dharmā formula, no scribal/authorial colophon. Secondary lit (Williams et al., Nakamura) treats the Larger Sukhāvatīvyūha as an early, collectively-composed anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra. Note: the Sukhāvatīvyūhopadeśa attributed to Vasubandhu is a SEPARATE commentary, not this sūtra.
texts.note: Larger Sukhāvatīvyūha. Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra, trad. spoken by the Buddha. Vaidya = modern editor only. Do not conflate with Vasubandhu's Sukhāvatīvyūhopadeśa.

texts.title: Sukhāvatyamṛtavyūha (Sukhāvatī[amṛta]vyūhasūtra), the Smaller Sukhāvatīvyūha
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Max Müller & Nanjio 1883, annotations by U. Wogihara, data entry Y. Fujita; no author named. Smaller Sukhāvatīvyūha is a short anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra (Amitābha/Pure Land), traditionally buddhavacana.
texts.note: Smaller (Shorter) Sukhāvatīvyūha. Anonymous; editors Müller/Nanjio modern, Wogihara annotator. Not authored by any named person.

texts.title: Brahmasandhāna
texts.basis: No edition, no source_url in workitem. No hits in reference-literature/ (ag search empty). Web searches return no scholarly attribution for any text titled Brahmasandhāna; results are confined to unrelated Brahma* works (Brahmasūtra, Brahmayāmala, etc.). No named author can be established.
texts.note: Obscure/minor title (likely a short Śaiva/yoga work, 'joining with brahman'). Genuinely unknown authorship; current_author was empty, now explicitly anonymous. No traditional ascription located.

texts.title: Saptaśatikā prajñāpāramitā [Saptaśatikaprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. P.L. Vaidya 1961; no author/translator/scribe named. Secondary lit (Pagel, Bodhisattvapiṭaka 1995) describes the Saptaśatikā Prajñāpāramitā as composed by a 'circle of monks' (collective/anonymous), grouped with Śatasāhasrikā; Wayman discusses its meditative content but names no author.
texts.note: 700-line Perfection of Wisdom (Mañjuśrī) sūtra. Anonymous Mahāyāna scripture, trad. buddhavacana; Vaidya = modern editor.

texts.title: Āryānityatāsūtra
texts.basis: DSBC canon text (source_url placeholder, no edition). Short canonical Buddhist sūtra on impermanence (anityatā); no author. Mahāyāna/canonical sūtras of this type are anonymous and traditionally spoken by the Buddha (cf. SuttaCentral parallels sf299).
texts.note: Noble Sūtra on Impermanence. Anonymous buddhavacana; 'ārya' is honorific, not an author. No named composer or translator established.

texts.title: Candrajñānāgama (Candrajñāna)
texts.basis: One of the 28 Śaiva Siddhāntāgamas (a pratiṣṭhātantra). Traditional theology ascribes it to divine revelation (Sadāśiva > Ananta > ... > ṛṣis), i.e. no human author. Edition: V. Dwivedi (Skt text), R. Ghose (Eng tr., notes), Varanasi 1995. wisdomlib confirms classification and divine-authorship (pratisaṃhitā) tradition.
texts.note: Śaiva Siddhānta Āgama (Kriyāpāda & Caryāpāda). Revealed scripture, no human author; Dwivedi = editor, Ghose = translator. Traditional ascription is to Śiva (Sadāśiva) via lineage.

texts.title: Samādhirājasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (input Kazuhiro Shimizu, Gilgit ms.) gives no author or colophon, only Buddha-discourse framing. Secondary lit (reference-literature) treats it as an anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra known to Candrakīrti, used to elucidate ultimate meaning. Web confirms Mahāyāna sūtra, no individual author.
texts.note: Anonymous compiled Mahāyāna sūtra (Buddhavacana); cited as 'Samādhirāja' already by Candrakīrti. No named human author.

texts.title: Dhvajāgrakeyūrā-dhāraṇī
texts.basis: GRETIL header (DSBC input, proof Milan Shakya) states 'Source unknown', no author, no colophon naming a composer. Secondary lit notes the dhāraṇī was *translated* into Tibetan by Jinamitra, Dānaśīla and Ye-śes-sde, i.e. transmitted as anonymous Buddhavacana with no Indian author.
texts.note: Anonymous protective dhāraṇī, Buddha to Śakra. Tibetan translators Jinamitra/Dānaśīla/Ye-śes-sde named in transmission, not as authors.

texts.title: Dhvajāgrakeyūrā-dhāraṇī
texts.basis: GRETIL header: diplomatic edition by Paolo Giunta of MS Tucci 3.2.16 (Sferra, Manuscripta Buddhica 1, 2008); colophon 'āryadhvajāgrakeyūrā nāma dhāriṇī samāptā' marks completion, names no author. Framed as Buddha's teaching to Śakra. Same anonymous text as dhvajkeu, here a different MS witness/edition.
texts.note: Anonymous dhāraṇī (Buddhavacana). Giunta is the modern editor, not author. Duplicate of dhvajkeu via a distinct manuscript (Tucci 3.2.16).

texts.title: Yamasmṛti
texts.primary_author: ascribed to Yama (traditional/pseudepigraphic); historical author anonymous
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Neuß critical ed. of South-Indian recension, 78 vv.) gives no historical author; text is a dialogue with Yama as authoritative speaker answering ṛṣis. Web/dharmaśāstra references list Yamasmṛti as a smṛti 'ascribed to Yama' among the metrical dharmaśāstra authors—a divine/sage ascription typical of smṛti, with no recoverable human author.
texts.note: Smṛti pseudonymously ascribed to the god/sage Yama; actual author anonymous. Multiple recensions exist (Neuß edits the 78-verse South-Indian one).

texts.title: (untitled in metadata; large K14 biographical/avadāna sūtra, very heavy Mahāvastu parallels)
texts.basis: No GRETIL URL/title in workitem; corpus meta.json has empty title/author. Parallel data (matches/SA_K14_n3013u): self-category K14 and ~9,562/21,164 parallels with the Mahāvastu (mhvastuu), plus Divyāvadāna, Avadānaśataka and Vinaya texts. Pattern fits a large anonymous Vinaya/avadāna biographical compilation (Mahāvastu-type, possibly Lalitavistara/Saṅghabhedavastu). All candidates are anonymous compilations.
texts.note: Identity uncertain (text id n3013, title not recorded; GRETIL page 404). Overwhelmingly Mahāvastu-parallel. Whatever the precise work, it is an anonymous multi-layered Buddhist compilation; no individual author.

texts.title: Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Schopen's ed. of the Gilgit ms. (Gómez & Silk 1989), input Klaus Wille; no author, colophon only 'vajracchedikā prajñāpāramitā samāptāḥ'. Text framed as Buddha's discourse to Subhūti. Secondary lit treats it as anonymous sūtra (Diamond Sutra), not an authored work.
texts.note: Anonymous Prajñāpāramitā sūtra (Buddhavacana, Buddha to Subhūti). Schopen/Wille are modern editor/inputter, not authors.

texts.title: Sukhāvatīvyūha
texts.basis: GRETIL header (data entry Y. Fujita) gives no author/colophon. Müller-Nanjio edition is anonymous canonical scripture. Web (encyclopedic/scholarly) confirms the (Larger/Smaller) Sukhāvatīvyūha is an anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra composed in NW India / Gandhāra c. 1st-2nd c. CE, no individual author.
texts.note: Buddhavacana; no human author by genre. Current 'anonymous/traditional' is correct.

texts.title: Madhyamakahṛdaya
texts.basis: Edition = Lindtner, 'Madhyamakahrdayam of Bhavya'. Secondary lit (Eckel; Ruegg; Apple; Sferra) uniformly ascribes the Madhyamakahṛdayakārikā to Bhavya/Bhāviveka (c. 500-570), with the prose Tarkajvālā as his own autocommentary. This item is the kārikā text only.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Jnanagarbha on the Two Truths_Eckel.txt; secondary:cleaned_Sanskrit Texts from Giuseppe Tucci's Collection Part I_Sferra.txt
texts.note: Verses (this text) by Bhāviveka; the separate autocommentary Tarkajvālā (not this item) is also his. Name also spelled Bhavya/Bhāvaviveka. Secure.

texts.title: Pratisarākalpadhāraṇī
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Rinpoche & Pandey, Dhīḥ 28; input K. Wille) names no author; colophon reads only 'āryapratisarākalpadhāraṇī samāptā'. Secondary lit (Nakamura; Nariman) treats Mahāpratisarā as one of the five Pañcarakṣā dhāraṇīs — anonymous protective-spell scripture, no individual author.
texts.note: Part of the Pañcarakṣā (Mahāpratisarā) dhāraṇī corpus; anonymous by genre. Current attribution correct.

texts.title: Viṣṇudharmottara-Purāṇa: Atharvavidhikathana [Atharvavidhāna]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (purāṇa); Dūrgāmohana Bhaṭṭācārya (modern editor of this passage)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: based on Bombay 1912 ed., passage adapted/reconstituted ('pratisaṃskṛtam') by Dūrgāmohana Bhaṭṭācārya in D. Bhattacharyya's Atharvaveda study; input Shilpa Sumant. Colophon frames it as Mārkaṇḍeya/Rāma dialogue (Puṣkara speaking) — traditional scripture. Secondary lit (Loizzo) notes 'the author of the Viṣṇudharmottarapurāṇa' as anonymous, by 6th c. CE.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Candrakirti and Moon-flower of Nalanda_Loizzo 2001.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Vaiṣṇava purāṇa. The named modern scholar Dūrgāmohana Bhaṭṭācārya is the editor/reconstructor of this Atharva chapter, not the purāṇa's author.

texts.basis: Work item is empty: no title, no current_author, no edition, source_url null, source_corpus 'unknown'. Nothing to triangulate; identifier SA_K10_sf284_sc (coll K10) provides no attributable content.
texts.note: Stub/empty record; author cannot be determined from available data. Treated as anonymous/unknown.

texts.title: Madhyāntavibhāgaṭīkā Chapter 1
texts.basis: Edition = Yamaguchi, 'Madhyāntavibhāgaṭīkā ... de Sthiramati'. Secondary lit (Nakamura: 'Madhyāntavibhāgatīkā de Sthiramati'; Brunnhölzl: Sthiramati's ṭīkā comments on Vasubandhu's MAV-bhāṣya, which in turn explains Maitreya's kārikā; Eckel indexes the Madhyāntavibhāga under Maitreyanātha 'See Vasubandhu'). The ṭīkā text embeds and is structured around all three layers.
texts.note: Three-layer Yogācāra text like the Mahāyānasūtrālaṅkāra: root kārikās = Maitreya(nātha), bhāṣya = Vasubandhu, ṭīkā (this work) = Sthiramati. Current 'Sthiramati' names only the sub-commentator; flagged as conflation and roles separated.

texts.title: Madhyāntavibhāgaṭīkā (Yamaguchi)
texts.basis: GRETIL/Yamaguchi ed. titled 'Madhyantavibhagatika de Sthiramati'; secondary (John Younghan Cha; Pandeya 1999 'Kārikās of Maitreya, Bhāṣya...') confirms the layered structure: Maitreya kārikā, Vasubandhu bhāṣya, Sthiramati ṭīkā (sub-commentary on the bhāṣya).
texts.note: ṭīkā is Sthiramati's, but the work as transmitted embeds Maitreya's kārikā and Vasubandhu's bhāṣya (root + bhāṣya by different authors); the ṭīkā itself is securely Sthiramati's.

texts.title: Madhyāntavibhāgaṭīkā
texts.basis: Yamaguchi 1934 ed. is 'Madhyantavibhaga-tika de Sthiramati'; Pandeya 1971/1999 ed. presents kārikās of Maitreya + bhāṣya of Vasubandhu + ṭīkā of Sthiramati together. Same triple-layer Yogācāra structure as the Yamaguchi item.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Jewels of the Middle Way_James B Apple, 2019.txt (Pandeya 1999 ref)
texts.note: Sthiramati's ṭīkā secure; title conflates Maitreya (kārikā) and Vasubandhu (bhāṣya) as embedded layers.

texts.title: Avadānaśataka
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Speyer/Vaidya eds., input Klaus Wille) gives no author. Secondary (Speyer ed. vol.2; Tatelman; Winternitz): anonymous compiled avadāna collection (oldest extant in Skt, ~2nd c. CE per Speyer). Colophon styles it 'Sugatabhāṣitam' (i.e. spoken by the Buddha) and adds 'prakāśitam' with the name of an ācārya Thandīśvara.
texts.note: No human author claimed; traditional ascription is to the Buddha (Sugatabhāṣita). Colophon names ācārya Thandīśvara as one who 'brought it to appearance' (prakāśitam) — possibly redactor/promulgator, not original author; insufficient to assert authorship.

texts.title: Daśabalasūtra
texts.basis: DSBC source URL mis-resolves to the Abhidharmakośa, so no usable e-text header. Secondary (Puri, Buddhism in Central Asia): canonical Sarvāstivāda āgama sūtra, reconstructed by Waldschmidt from Turfan/Central Asian Brāhmī fragments; no named author.
texts.note: Canonical sūtra (Buddhavacana); anonymous. Waldschmidt is modern critical editor, not author. Source_url returns wrong text (Abhidharmakośa).

texts.title: Ekottarāgama [Ekottarāgamasūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. C. B. Tripathi 1995, Gilgit fragments; input Klaus Wille) gives no author/colophon. Secondary (Nariman; Frauwallner): Ekottarāgama is the Sanskrit canonical āgama corresponding to the Pali Aṅguttaranikāya — anonymous collective monastic transmission.
texts.note: Canonical āgama (counterpart of Aṅguttaranikāya); anonymous Buddhavacana. Tripathi is modern editor of Gilgit fragments, not author.

texts.title: Skanda-Purāṇa
texts.basis: No source_url. Critical edition (Bakker, Bisschop, Yokochi et al., Brill 2014) and Skandapurāṇa Project scholarship: anonymous composite Śaiva Purāṇa originally composed c. 6th-7th c. CE; traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa as with all Purāṇas. Multiple recensions/layers, no single historical author.
texts.note: Anonymous/composite; traditional ascription to Vyāsa (legendary). Bakker/Bisschop/Yokochi are modern critical editors. Multi-layered redaction, but not distinct named authors -> conflation_flag false.

texts.title: Vajrayāna darśana mīmāṁsā
texts.primary_author: anonymous (modern composition; no named author in DSBC)
texts.basis: DSBC content page (832/2959): no author named; only input by Bibek Shakya, proofread by Miroj Shakya (2018), supplied by Nagarjuna Institute, sponsored by Univ. of the West. Appears to be a modern scholarly Sanskrit composition on Vajrayāna philosophy, not a classical text. Web search found no attributed author.
texts.note: Modern (likely 20th-21st c.) Sanskrit composition; DSBC leaves author blank. Bibek/Miroj Shakya are data-entry/proofreader, not authors.

texts.title: Nyāyapraveśakasūtra
texts.primary_author: Śaṅkarasvāmin (Chinese tradition; disciple of Dignāga) / Dignāga (Tibetan tradition)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (reference-literature) repeatedly cites 'Nyāyapraveśa of Śaṅkarasvāmin' and 'Samkarasvamin's Nyayapravesa', noting it summarizes Dignāga's logic. Web (ancient-buddhist-texts.net appendix; SEP; wisdomlib): Chinese tradition + Xuanzang's translation lineage attribute to Śaṅkarasvāmin (pupil of Dignāga); Tibetan colophon attributes to Dignāga himself. Genuine disputed attribution between teacher and disciple.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:https://ancient-buddhist-texts.net/English-Texts/Manimekhalai/S9-Appendix.htm
texts.note: Same dispute as current_author. Chinese/East Asian transmission = Śaṅkarasvāmin; Tibetan colophon = Dignāga. Not a multi-author conflation, but a single-work disputed ascription.

texts.title: Maitreyavyākaraṇa [Maitreyavyākaraṇasūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (bsu009): Ārya Maitreyavyākaraṇaṃ, ed. N. Dutt (Gilgit Mss IV); colophon 'maitreyavyākaraṇaṃ samāptaṃ'. No traditional author named; presents as prophetic discourse on the future Buddha Maitreya, compiled from earlier sources. Standard buddhavacana sūtra without authorial attribution.
texts.note: Anonymous prophetic sūtra (vyākaraṇa). No personal author; traditionally Buddha-word. Multiple recensions exist (verse and prose) but no distinct named authors to separate.

texts.title: Mahāpratisarāmahāvidyārājñī
texts.basis: GRETIL header (mahpratu): critical edition by Gergely Hidas (2012, Śata-Piṭaka 636), input by Hidas. Colophon: 'mahāpratisarāyā mahāvidyārājñyā rakṣāvidhānakalpo vidyādharasyāyaṃ samāptaḥ'. A dhāraṇī/amulet scripture; no human author — Buddha-word. Hidas is modern editor, not author.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist dhāraṇī text (one of the Pañcarakṣā). Hidas = editor only. Distinct edition from SA_K12_mpratpru (Iwamoto/Wille) of essentially the same text.

texts.title: Narasiṃha-Purāṇa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Vaiṣṇava upapurāṇa; traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (narsippu): ed. Siddheswar Jena (1987), transliteration by Peter Schreiner (Tübingen Purāṇa Project). No named author. Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra; iconography studies) treats it as the Vaiṣṇava Narasiṃha Purāṇa, a (upa)purāṇa of disputed date (Coburn early; Willis later). Purāṇas are conventionally ascribed to Vyāsa but are anonymous compilations. NB: distinct from the Telugu poet Errana's 'Narasimha Purana' mentioned in History of the Andhras — that is a separate vernacular work, not this Sanskrit text.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Sanskrit Vaiṣṇava (upa)purāṇa, traditional ascription to Vyāsa. Do NOT conflate with Errana's Telugu Narasimha Purana (different author/language).

texts.title: Mahāpratisarā-vidyārājñī [Mahāpratisarādhāraṇī(sūtra)]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (mpratpru): based on Y. Iwamoto's ed. (Pañcarakṣā, Kyoto 1937), Unicode input by Klaus Wille. Frame: teachings spoken by the bhagavān (Buddha) on the Mahāpratisarā vidyārājñī. Colophons name kalpas ('āryamahāvidyārājño mahāpratisarāyāḥ prathamakalpaḥ samāptaḥ'), no human author.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist dhāraṇī text; Iwamoto = editor, Wille = digitizer. Same work-family as SA_K12_mahpratu (Hidas ed.), different edition/recension.

texts.title: Bhavasaṃkrāntiparikathā
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'ācārya āryanāgārjunakṛta bhavasaṅkrāntiparikathā sampūrṇā' — ascribes to Āryanāgārjuna. Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, 1980): śāstra 'attributed to Nāgārjuna', school uncertain. Lindtner (Nagarjuniana 1987): lists it within the corpus but flags it as so 'contaminated or hopelessly corrupt' that no archetype is recoverable; falls in the may-or-may-not-be-genuine category, and a recension (Abodhabodhaka) is ascribed to Advayavajra.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Nāgārjuna preserved in colophon, but authenticity is disputed in scholarship (Lindtner). Same text also transmitted as Abodhabodhaka under Advayavajra. Kept current attribution, status downgraded to disputed.

texts.title: Sphuṭārthā Śrīghanācārasaṃgrahaṭīkā
texts.basis: GRETIL header and closing verses: 'iti jayarakṣite(na) nibadhya ṭīkāṃ ...' and 'suśrīghanācārasusaṃgrahasya kṛtvā hi ṭīkāṃ ...' — Jayarakṣita composed this commentary (ṭīkā) on the Śrīghanācārasaṃgraha, which is itself his work (a self-commentary / auto-ṭīkā). Confirmed in secondary refs (Williams, Buddhism Critical Concepts; Derrett) as 'Jayarakṣita's hybrid Sanskrit commentary'.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:Williams_Buddhism_Critical_Concepts_v7; secondary:Derrett_Monastic masturbation in Pali texts
texts.note: Both the root Śrīghanācārasaṃgraha and the ṭīkā are by Jayarakṣita (auto-commentary). Two roles, single author — not a conflation.

texts.title: Mārīcīnāmadhāraṇī
texts.basis: GRETIL header: input by Klaus Wille; ed. Dhīḥ 42 (2006). Colophon: 'āryamārīcī nāma dhāraṇī samāptā' — names no author or scribe. Canonical dhāraṇī (Āryamārīcī), traditionally 'spoken by the Buddha', not an authored composition.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical dhāraṇī; no author claimed in edition or colophon. Buddhavacana by tradition.

texts.title: Mahāśītavatī vidyārājñī
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Yutaka Iwamoto, Kleinere Dhāraṇī-Texte 1937; input Klaus Wille. Colophon: 'āryamahāśītavatī nāma vidyārājñī samāptā' — no author/scribe named. Canonical vidyā/dhāraṇī text, by tradition Buddhavacana.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical vidyārājñī (dhāraṇī); no author in edition or colophon.

texts.title: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara: Adhyāya 4
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'śrīmādhavapraṇīto jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistaraḥ' and 'iti śrīmādhavīye jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistare ...' — both the metrical nyāyamālā (kārikās) and the prose vistara commentary are by Mādhava. Web (ZDMG; Wikipedia Vidyāraṇya/Sāyaṇa): Mādhava traditionally identified with the sannyāsin Vidyāraṇya (14th c. Vijayanagara), brother/associate of Sāyaṇa; the Mādhava=Vidyāraṇya=Sāyaṇa identifications are debated but the Mīmāṃsā Nyāyamālāvistara is consistently ascribed to Mādhava/Vidyāraṇya.
texts.note: Single author (verse nyāyamālā + own prose vistara = auto-commentary, one person). Traditional identification of Mādhava with Vidyāraṇya; relation to Sāyaṇa is scholarly debated — flagged in note, not as a conflation.

texts.title: Mahāsudarśanāvadāna
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. H. Matsumura 1988; input Klaus Wille from Gilgit Buddhist Manuscripts (GBM). Colophon: 'mahāsudarśanāvadānaḥ samāpta' — no individual author/scribe named. Collected narrative (avadāna) of unattributed canonical origin.
texts.note: Anonymous avadāna narrative; no author in edition or colophon.

texts.title: Bauddhadharme tathā purāṇeṣu karuṇā
texts.basis: Edition field states this is a modern Sanskrit essay edited/authored by Phra Suntharnadhammathada, published in DSBC. DSBC page header names only project personnel (input: Milan Shakya; proofreader: Miroj Shakya; Nagarjuna Institute / University of the West) and no separate original author, consistent with Phra Suntharnadhammathada being the composing author.
texts.note: Modern comparative essay ('Compassion in Buddhism and the Purāṇas'); author per edition metadata, not classical. Shakyas are DSBC inputters/proofreaders, not the author.

texts.title: Rāṣṭrapālasūtra fragment (SuttaCentral SF 101)
texts.basis: Edition field identifies this as SuttaCentral Sanskrit fragment SF 101 = Rāṣṭrapālasūtra fragment ed. Hisashi Matsumura. The Rāṣṭrapālasūtra is canonical Buddhist sūtra literature (traditionally Buddhavacana), with no named human author; Matsumura is the modern editor of the fragment, not its author. Web (SuttaCentral SF 101; Matsumura 1985 'development of the Rāṣṭrapālasūtra') confirms editorial role.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical sūtra; Matsumura is editor (recorded under translator/editor role). Traditional ascription: Buddhavacana.

texts.title: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara: Adhyāya 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Mādhava: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara ... with Mādhava's prose commentary'; opening verse 'śrīmādhavapraṇīto'. Both the metrical nyāyamālā (kārikās) and the prose vistara are by the single author Mādhava — no author conflict. Web: Mādhava is widely identified with the Advaitin sannyāsin Vidyāraṇya (brother of Sāyaṇa), though that identification is debated (Sāyaṇa-vs-Mādhava authorship discussion in scholarship).
texts.note: Single author for verses + commentary (not a multi-author conflation). Mādhava=Vidyāraṇya identification traditional but scholarly debated.

texts.title: Vajrapāṇināmāṣṭottaraśatastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (ed. Janardan Shastri Pandey 1994) header: 108-stotra collection input by SBC Input Project; individual stotras occasionally credit authors (e.g. no.1 Mātṛceṭa, no.6 Sarvajñamitra) but stotra no. 96 (Vajrapāṇi 108-names hymn) carries no author name in header or colophon.
texts.note: Anonymous devotional aṣṭottaraśata-nāma stotra; no colophon author for entry no. 96. Current 'anonymous/traditional' confirmed.

texts.title: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara: Adhyāya 3
texts.basis: Same work/edition as Adhyāya 2. GRETIL header attributes both the metrical nyāyamālā verses and the prose vistara commentary to the single author Mādhava ('śrīmādhavapraṇīto'). Web confirms traditional but debated identification of Mādhava with Vidyāraṇya.
texts.note: Single author for verses + self-commentary; no conflation. Adhyāya 3 of the same Mādhava work.

texts.title: Kirātārjunīya
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Bhāravi: Kirātārjunīya', text as commented on by Mallinātha (word-division per Mallinātha's recension), data entry Harunaga Isaacson 2020. Only the base mahākāvya is reproduced here — Mallinātha's Ghaṇṭāpatha commentary is NOT included (informed editing only). Bhāravi's authorship of this mahākāvya is securely established in the tradition and scholarship.
texts.note: Base text only; Mallinātha (commentator) used for word-division but commentary not present, so no commentary author to add. Bhāravi secure.

texts.title: Dharmadhātugarbhavivaraṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Āryadharmadhātugarbhavivaraṇam', author given as Ācārya Nāgārjunapāda; colophon notes review/refinement by the Indian upādhyāya Jñānagarbha (translation-team role). Secondary lit: Marek Mejor, 'The Ārya-dharma-dhātu-garbha-vivaraṇa ascribed to Nāgārjuna' (title itself flags ascription); Lindtner Nagarjuniana lists it among works whose authenticity is doubtful; Brunnhölzl (In Praise of Dharmadhātu, n.300) notes the Derge Tengyur DHDBU (D4101) attributed to Nāgārjuna 'neither comments on dharmadhātu nor garbha', questioning the attribution.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_In Praise of Dharmadhatu...Brunnhölzl 2008.txt; secondary:cleaned_Nagarjuniana...Lindtner 1987.txt; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems...txt
texts.note: Traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna but widely doubted; survives chiefly in Tibetan transmission (Jñānagarbha associated with translation/revision). Treat attribution as disputed/dubious rather than secure.

texts.title: Maṃjuśrīpārājikā [Mañjuśrīpārājikā]
texts.basis: DSBC source_url resolved to a different text (Caṇḍamahāroṣaṇatantra), so header unusable. Secondary lit: Gray & Overbey (eds.), Tantric Traditions in Transmission and Translation (2016), pp. 159 and 5101, list the Mañjuśrīpārājikā among Newar Buddhist ritual texts; web (Newar Buddhism studies): subtitle 'Rules of Conduct expounded by the Buddha to Mañjuśrī', used to define the Vajrācārya's role. No human author named; the 'Mañjuśrī' in the title is the bodhisattva interlocutor, not the composer.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Tantric traditions in transmission and translation_Gray_Overbey_2016_a.txt; web:https://books.openedition.org/editionsehess/24678
texts.note: Pseudo-canonical Newar Buddhist ritual/disciplinary text; framed as Buddha's discourse to Mañjuśrī. Traditional ascription to Buddha/Mañjuśrī is a literary frame; actual author anonymous.

texts.title: Śukavākya
texts.basis: No source_url or edition provided. No occurrence in reference-literature/ (ag for 'Sukavakya/Śukavākya' returned nothing). Web search returned no identifiable text/author. Title ('Speech/Sayings of the Parrot') suggests a minor didactic/nīti or anthology piece, possibly Śuka-frame material; no author can be established.
