texts.note: Genuinely unidentified minor text; no scholarly or e-text attribution found. Leaving anonymous; do not invent an author.

texts.title: Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Brahmanda-Purana, Part 1 (38 Adhyayas)', Venkatesvara Steam Press ed., input by SANSKNET; no individual author named, only edition/input data. Purāṇas are by genre anonymous compilations conventionally ascribed to Vyāsa; a multi-layered composite text without a single historical author.
texts.note: Anonymous composite Purāṇa; pan-Purāṇic traditional ascription to Vyāsa as compiler/arranger. No real single author.

texts.title: (SHT fragment SF 273, Catuṣpariṣatsūtra / Turfan corpus)
texts.basis: No source_url. Edition: Waldschmidt et al., Sanskrithandschriften aus den Turfanfunden, fragment SF 273, identified as Catuṣpariṣat-/Turfan corpus. Secondary lit (Nakamura, Indian Buddhism 1980): the Catuṣpariṣatsūtra belonged to the Dīrghāgama of the Mūlasarvāstivādins; edited by Ernst Waldschmidt (Das Catuṣpariṣatsūtra). A canonical āgama sūtra (buddhavacana), not an authored composition.
texts.note: Manuscript fragment of a canonical Mūlasarvāstivāda sūtra (Catuṣpariṣatsūtra); anonymous canonical text edited by Waldschmidt. No author; editor = Waldschmidt et al. (modern).

texts.title: Padārthadharmasaṃgraha
texts.basis: GRETIL header: author Praśastapāda (text also known as Praśastapādabhāṣya), ed. V. P. Dvivedin with Śrīdhara's Nyāyakaṇḍalī; input Tokunaga/Muroya. Secondary lit broadly concordant: Ganeri (Philosophy in Classical India) 'Padārthadharmasaṃgraha aka Praśastapādabhāṣya, by Praśastapāda'; Matilal & Ganeri (Epistemology, Logic and Grammar) discuss when Praśastapāda wrote his PDS; dated ~5th-6th c. CE. Though conventionally called a 'bhāṣya' on the Vaiśeṣikasūtra (Kaṇāda), it is in fact an independent systematic treatise authored by Praśastapāda.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Philosophy in Classical India_Ganeri_2001.txt; secondary:cleaned_Epistemology, Logic and Grammar...Matilal_Ganeri_2005.txt
texts.note: Secure single authorship: Praśastapāda. The Padārthadharmasaṃgraha (=Praśastapādabhāṣya) is an independent treatise, not a verse-by-verse gloss; Kaṇāda is the underlying sūtra author and Śrīdhara wrote the later Nyāyakaṇḍalī sub-commentary (not part of this work). No conflation in the text itself.

texts.title: Nirvāṇayogottara
texts.basis: Secondary lit (cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt) cites it as a revealed Śaiva scripture quoted by later authors ('As has been declared in venerable Nirvāṇayogottara'; listed among Tantras alongside Kālottara, Svacchandabhairava); a MS noted in a Central Library catalogue. No human author named; treated as āgama/revelation. No source_url, no GRETIL header.
texts.note: Revealed Śaiva Tantra of the Kālottara/Niśvāsa group; no human composer. Traditionally Śiva-uttered scripture.

texts.title: Avalokiteśvarāṣṭottaraśatanāmastotra
texts.basis: DSBC canonical-text stub URL gives no author; web search found no attested composer. The aṣṭottaraśatanāma ('108 names') stotra is a stock devotional genre transmitted in the Buddhist canon without named authorship; related Avalokiteśvara 108-names material survives in Taishō (T20 no.1054) as anonymous canonical liturgy.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna liturgical 108-names hymn; no traditional individual ascription. Canonical/devotional, not authored by a named poet.

texts.title: Aṃśumatkāśyapāgama
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to revelation; assoc. with sage Kāśyapa)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śaiva Siddhānta Āgama; traditionally ascribed to sage Kāśyapa)
texts.basis: Edition note: Muktabodha e-text M00198, a Śaiva Siddhānta Āgama with no critical printed edition. Secondary lit (cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt) calls the Aṃśumat(tantra) 'another late authority' quoted for pratiṣṭhā/prāyaścitta; Bühnemann notes it gives ~20 names; Bendall catalogues it among Āgamas. Treated as revealed scripture; the 'Kāśyapa' element is a legendary sage-ascription, not historical authorship.
texts.note: Śaiva Siddhānta Āgama (Aṃśumadbheda/Aṃśumat); traditionally revealed, sage Kāśyapa legendary. Quoted as a late authority on iconometry/pratiṣṭhā.

texts.title: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara: Adhyāya 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header explicitly: 'śrīmādhavapraṇīto jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistaraḥ' (composed by Mādhava); based on Sivadatta ed., Anandasrama Sanskrit Series 24, Poona 1892. Secondary lit (Potter EIP vol.1; Olivelle, Dharma-Studies) consistently attributes it to Mādhava = Vidyāraṇya (14th c. Vijayanagara). Identification Mādhava=Vidyāraṇya is standard.
texts.note: Single author secure; colophon names Mādhava. Mādhava/Vidyāraṇya equation standard in scholarship (Potter, Olivelle).

texts.title: Viṣṇudharmottara-Purāṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editor (Bombay 1912 ed., input Reinhold Grünendahl), no author. Secondary lit (Loizzo; Revire) dates the author/compiler of the Viṣṇudharmottarapurāṇa to ~6th c. CE but treats it as an anonymous, multi-layered compiled encyclopedic Purāṇa (khaṇḍas on art, astronomy, dharma).
texts.note: Anonymous compiled Purāṇa (~6th c. CE core), encyclopedic; no individual author. Composite redaction over time but no distinct named authors to separate.

texts.title: Caṇḍikādaṇḍakastotra
texts.basis: DSBC stub URL gives no author; web search yielded no attested composer for this specific daṇḍaka-meter Caṇḍikā stotra. Note: Bāṇa's well-known goddess hymn is the Caṇḍīśataka (different, śataka not daṇḍaka), so the Bāṇa attribution does NOT transfer to this daṇḍakastotra. No named author established.
texts.note: Anonymous daṇḍaka-meter Caṇḍikā hymn; distinct from Bāṇa's Caṇḍīśataka. No secure individual ascription; do not conflate with Bāṇa.

texts.title: Ākāśagarbhanāmāṣṭottaraśatastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL 108-stotra collection (ed. J.S. Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha 1994, stotra no. 3). Colophon reads 'śrī ākāśagarbhanāmāṣṭaśatādhyeṣaṇāstotraṃ saṃpūrṇam' and names no author; a 108-name (aṣṭottaraśata) devotional praise of the bodhisattva Ākāśagarbha, of the kind transmitted without human attribution.
texts.note: No author in colophon; anonymous Buddhist nāmāṣṭottaraśata stotra. Editor Janardan Shastri Pandey is editor only, not author.

texts.basis: Work item carries no title and no current_author; source_url is only the generic GRETIL Buddhist-literature directory (1_sanskr/4_rellit/buddh/), not a specific text page. The id fragment 'sf137_sc' does not resolve to an identifiable title or filename, so no e-text header, secondary literature, or web source can be triangulated.
texts.note: Unidentifiable placeholder item: no title, no resolvable URL. No author asserted to avoid invention; status uncertain (effectively anonymous).

texts.title: Mahācakravartināmāṣṭottaraśatastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL bst-108u (108 Buddhist stotras, ed. J.S. Pandey 1994, stotra no. 44). A 108-name (aṣṭottaraśata) praise text of the same anonymous nāmāṣṭottaraśata genre as the other entries in the collection; no author named in header or colophon.
texts.note: Anonymous nāmāṣṭottaraśata stotra; Pandey is editor only. No human author transmitted.

texts.title: Vākyapadīya 1.1-3.7
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. W. Rau 1977; input S. Vasudeva 2002) attributes the kārikā/mūla text to Bhartṛhari; secondary literature (Matilal, Collected Essays) treats the Vākyapadīya as Bhartṛhari's foundational work, quoted already by Dignāga. This file is the kārikā text only (no vṛtti).
texts.note: Mūla kārikā only, securely Bhartṛhari. (The early Vṛtti on books 1-2, whose colophon names 'Harivṛṣabha', is debated as to whether it is Bhartṛhari's own; but that commentary is NOT contained in this text, so no conflation here.)

texts.title: Maitreyavyākaraṇa [Maitreyavyākaraṇasūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: text constituted from three Mss (Gilgit ed. Majumder 1959; Calcutta ed. Lévi 1932; Kathmandu ed. Ishigami 1989; data entry J.-U. Hartmann). It is a Mahāyāna sūtra (Ārya-Maitreya-vyākaraṇa) framed as the Buddha's prophecy of the future Buddha Maitreya, with no human author. Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, p.180 n.40) notes two Sanskrit versions plus Tibetan/Chinese (Chinese tr. by Kumārajīva), versions compared by Ishigami.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna prophecy-sūtra; the Calcutta colophon name 'bhikṣu Puṇyakīrti' (a monk 'from the Chinese lands') is a scribe/copyist, not the author. Editors named are modern editors only.

texts.title: Vākyapadīya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (vakyp_au, ed. W. Rau 1977) gives the kārikā text of the Vākyapadīya (Trikāṇḍī) by Bhartṛhari. Secondary literature (Matilal) confirms Bhartṛhari's authorship; the work is cited by Dignāga, fixing it before c. 5th c. CE.
texts.note: Kārikā/mūla text, securely Bhartṛhari. Same authorship subtlety as the other Vākyapadīya entry: the disputed Vṛtti (colophon 'Harivṛṣabha') and Helārāja's ṭīkā on Kāṇḍa III are separate works not contained here, so no conflation.

texts.title: Mahāsāhasrapramardanī
texts.primary_author: anonymous (canonical Mahāyāna dhāraṇī-sūtra; spoken by the Buddha)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Iwamoto ed. 1937, input K. Wille): text is anonymous, frames itself as a Buddhavacana Mahāyāna sūtra (one of the Pañcarakṣā); colophon 'āryamahāsāhasrapramardanī nāma mahāyānasūtraṃ samāptam' names no human author. Secondary lit (Nakamura) treats it as an anonymous Pañcarakṣā protective sūtra.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Pañcarakṣā sūtra; no compiler/author named. Editor Iwamoto, input Klaus Wille.

texts.title: Bhṛgu-Saṃhitā
texts.primary_author: Bhṛgu (legendary/traditional ascription); historically anonymous Vaikhānasa compilation
texts.basis: GRETIL header: a Vaikhānasa-tradition text 'proclaimed by Bhṛgu' (bhṛguproktāyāṃ saṃhitāyāṃ in colophons); header makes no historical authorship claim. The ascription to the sage Bhṛgu is the framing convention of the Vaikhānasa āgama corpus, i.e. legendary, with actual composition by anonymous Vaikhānasa compilers.
texts.note: Traditional/legendary ascription to ṛṣi Bhṛgu; not a historical author. Vaikhānasa ritual saṃhitā, anonymous redactors. Text not proof-read per header.

texts.title: Śatakatraya: 1. Nītiśataka, 2. Śṛṅgāraśataka, 3. Vairāgyaśataka
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. J.K. Brzezinski): notes many extant editions with different numberings and many interpolated verses, needs critical reading. Web (Wikipedia/Kosambi): D.D. Kosambi's critical edition (377 mss, 1945-48) found only a kernel of ~200 verses common to all recensions (N vs S recensions); the rest accrete by recension, so the three śatakas are partly an anthology/floating-verse corpus. Identity of the poet with the grammarian-philosopher Bhartṛhari of the Vākyapadīya is itself disputed.
texts.note: Core verses ascribed to Bhartṛhari (traditional), but recensions add many non-authorial/interpolated verses (Kosambi: ~200-verse common kernel). Poet-vs-grammarian identity disputed. Conflation = single 'Bhartṛhari' title masking accreted multi-hand subhāṣita material.

texts.title: Saṅgītamālikā
texts.basis: Edition: J.S. Pandey (ed.), Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha, MLBD 1994 — a collection of Buddhist hymns (buddhas, bodhisattvas, ḍākinīs, deities) preserved in Nepalese mss. No author is recorded for the Saṅgītamālikā in DSBC or in web/secondary sources; many items in that collection are transmitted anonymously.
texts.note: Buddhist devotional stotra within Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha; no attested author. Current_author was empty; left anonymous (no ascription found).

texts.title: Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchā [Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya ed., Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha I, no. 12, 1961; SBIP/DSBC input): anonymous, presented as a Buddhist sūtra with no author named in header or colophon. A canonical Mahāyāna text (member of the Ratnakūṭa/Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha).
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra; no human author/compiler named. Editor P.L. Vaidya.

texts.title: Śakrapraśna (Sanskrit fragment SF 241, ed. Waldschmidt)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (canonical sūtra fragment; ed. Ernst Waldschmidt)
texts.basis: SuttaCentral catalogs sf241 as 'Śakrapraśna — Ernst Waldschmidt' (San fragment, Other Fragments corpus). Edition: Waldschmidt, Bruchstücke buddhistischer Sūtras aus dem zentralasiatischen Sanskritkanon (KST 4, 1932). Anonymous canonical sūtra fragment from the Central Asian Sanskrit canon (Sanskrit parallel of the Pali Sakkapañha-sutta); Waldschmidt is editor/restorer, not author.
texts.note: Title was empty in workitem; identified as Śakrapraśna (SF 241). Anonymous Buddhavacana fragment; Waldschmidt = editor/transcriber (listed here as 'translator' role for the edition).

texts.title: Āryabhaṭīya (with commentary)
texts.current_author: Āryabhaṭa (mūla); commentary by a later astronomer (e.g. Bhāskara I, 629 CE, or Nīlakaṇṭha Somayāji)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: mūla by Āryabhaṭa, commentaries by Bhāskara I and Someśvara; opening 'ācāryāryabhaṭamukhāravinda... daśagītikā-sūtravyākhyānam ārabhyate'. Web (Springer/INSA/Exotic India) confirms Shukla 1976 ed. = Āryabhaṭīya with commentary of Bhāskara I (629 CE) and Someśvara. Bhāskara I = earliest commentator; Someśvara a later glossator.
texts.note: Three distinct authors. current_author guessed 'Bhāskara I OR Nīlakaṇṭha Somayāji' — wrong: this edition has Bhāskara I AND Someśvara, not Nīlakaṇṭha. Separated mūla / bhāṣya / ṭīkā roles.

texts.title: Mahāpratisarāvidyāvidhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header: anonymous ritual manual (vidyāvidhi), no authorial colophon; based on Gergely Hidas's 2010 edition (Acta Or. Hung. 63.4). Hidas is editor, not author.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist tantric spell-manual; modern editor Gergely Hidas not to be confused with author. Confirms current attribution.

texts.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā IV [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: anonymous Mahāyāna Prajñāpāramitā sūtra, no colophon or authorial claim; traditionally buddhavacana (dialogue of Subhūti/Śāriputra with Bhagavān). Kimura 1990 is editor.
texts.note: Canonical Perfection-of-Wisdom sutra; anonymous, traditionally ascribed to the Buddha's word. Confirms current attribution.

texts.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā V [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: anonymous Mahāyāna Prajñāpāramitā sūtra, no colophon/authorial claim; Kimura 1992 is editor. Same anonymous canonical genre as vol. IV.
texts.note: Canonical Perfection-of-Wisdom sutra; anonymous, traditionally buddhavacana. Confirms current attribution.

texts.title: Jātakastava
texts.basis: GRETIL header colophon: 'jātakastava ācāryajñānayaśasaḥ' (the Jātakastava of Master Jñānayaśas). Secondary lit (Yuan Ren 1998 'Manicudavadana' p.7370) cites 'Jatakastava of Jnanayasas'; PIATS-Munich-1985 and Puri 1996 discuss its Khotanese verse translation (by Vidyaśīla).
texts.note: Author secure via colophon; Khotanese metrical translation by Vidyaśīla (translator, separate from author). Confirms current attribution.

texts.title: Vasudhārānāmadhāraṇīstotra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: dhāraṇī with traditional colophon 'ārya śrīvasudhārānāmadhāraṇīparisūtra samāptā'; no human author, framed as Bhagavān's teaching to householder Sucandra (buddhavacana/pseudepigraphic). Samten & Bahulkar 2007 are editors.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist dhāraṇī-sūtra; traditionally ascribed to the Buddha (could also be tagged traditional/pseudepigraphic), no individual author. Confirms current attribution.

texts.title: Sāṃkhyasūtravivaraṇa
texts.current_author: anonymous (ascribed in some sources to a Kṛṣṇa)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Kāpila tradition; sometimes ascribed to a 'Kṛṣṇa')
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'iti sāṃkhyasūtravivaraṇākhyā kāpilasūtravṛttiḥ samāptā' and a final verse crediting only an unnamed 'munivara'; no personal name given. A vivaraṇa (commentary) on the Tattvasamāsa (the c.23-25 sūtra abstract attributed to Kapila). Web lookups echo the GRETIL description and a loose ascription to a 'Kṛṣṇa' but offer no firm scholarly attestation; secondary-lit files mention Tattvasamāsa only generically.
texts.note: Colophon names no author, only generic 'munivara' and the Kāpila tradition. Ascription to 'Kṛṣṇa' is weak/traditional and not confirmed; treated as anonymous.

texts.title: Tathāgatabimbakārāpaṇasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition by Adelheid Mette ('Zwei kleine Fragmente aus Gilgit', StII 7, 1981), input by Klaus Wille; manuscript GBM (Gilgit) fragment. Colophon: 'buddhapratimāpuṇyaphalaprakāśitam idaṃ sūtraṃ samāptam || tathāgatabiṃbakārāpaṇaṃ sūtraṃ samāptam' — names no author, only marks completion. As a Buddhist sūtra it is anonymous/Buddhavacana; Mette and Wille are editor/transcriber, not authors.
texts.note: Anonymous sūtra preserved in Gilgit fragments; colophon gives no personal author. Mette = editor, Wille = input only.

texts.title: Jñānasaṅkalinītantra
texts.basis: No source_url/edition in workitem. A short Tantra (c.107 ślokas) framed as a Śiva-Śakti dialogue on the cakras; Dasgupta's Philosophical Essays cites the 'Jñāna-saṅkalinī Tantra' as associating cakras with emotions but gives no human author. Tantras of this genre are presented as revealed (āgama), hence authorless; an occasional loose linkage to the Śaṅkara tantric corpus is unverified.
texts.note: Revealed tantra (Śiva-Śakti dialogue); no secure human author. Any ascription to Śaṅkara tradition is unconfirmed and treated as not established.

texts.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā VI-VIII [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition by Takayasu Kimura (Tokyo 2006), input by Klaus Wille (2007). Section colophons read 'āryapañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāyāṃ bhagavatyāṃ prajñāpāramitāyām abhisamayālaṃkārānusāreṇa saṃśodhitāyām' — a Mahāyāna Prajñāpāramitā sūtra with no named author. The text was redacted/'corrected in accordance with the Abhisamayālaṃkāra', but that is an editorial restructuring, not authorship.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra (25,000-line Perfection of Wisdom). Kimura = modern editor, Wille = input. Colophon notes redaction per the Abhisamayālaṃkāra but names no author.

texts.title: Lokeśvarastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL 108-Buddhist-stotras file (ed. J. S. Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha 1994; DSBC basis) lists 'BuSto_40 Lokeśvarastotra' with no author attribution in the header/list. NOTE: a separately transmitted *Lokeśvarastotra known via Tibetan translation has a colophon naming the author 'Tsaryadīpa' / *Caryādīpa (Dorji Wangchuk), but that is a distinct transmission, not the Pandey/DSBC stotra here.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Dorji Wangchuk_Cross-Cultural transmission of Buddhist texts.txt
texts.note: Anonymous in the Pandey Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha edition (our source). A different Lokeśvarastotra (Tibetan transmission) is ascribed to *Caryādīpa — kept separate; not applied here.

texts.title: Āgamaśāstra (Gauḍapādakārikā / Gauḍapādīyakārikā / Māṇḍūkyakārikā)
texts.primary_author: Gauḍapāda (traditional; single authorship disputed — esp. 4th/Alātaśānti prakaraṇa by a different hand)
texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes to Gauḍapāda; final colophon 'iti gauḍapādācāryakṛtā māṇḍūkyopaniṣadkārikāḥ saṃpūrṇāḥ'. Four prakaraṇas (Āgama, Vaitathya, Advaita, Alātaśānti). Modern scholarship disputes unified authorship: V. Bhattacharya argues the four are independent treatises by different authors (vocabulary mismatch with the Māṇḍūkya prose mantras); Richard King rejects the full four-author thesis but concludes the 4th (Alātaśānti, heavily Buddhist/Yogācāra) was composed by some other author for a different audience. Secondary lit (Ahir, Tuck, Joshi) confirms strong Mahāyāna influence and the 'both Advaitin and Buddhist' characterization.
texts.note: Traditional single author Gauḍapāda is contested. Bhattacharya: 4 independent treatises by different authors; King: 4th prakaraṇa (Alātaśānti) by a distinct Buddhist-leaning author. Flagged as conflated/disputed rather than securely one person.

texts.title: Daśakumāracarita
texts.basis: GRETIL header: colophons read 'śrīdaṇḍinaḥ kṛtau', text structured as Pūrvapīṭhikā + (core) + Uttarapīṭhikā. Secondary (Spink, Ajanta vol. II): the work survives incomplete; 'Purvapithika and Uttarapithika are clearly later additions' by 'anonymous pen-pushers'. Web (Wikipedia/Bronner): consensus the core 8-ucchvāsa Daśakumāracarita is Daṇḍin's; the two pīṭhikās are widely held to be later supplements by other hands.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Ajanta. History and Development. Vol, II_Walter M. Spink, 2006.txt + web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dashakumaracharita
texts.note: GRETIL e-text includes both pīṭhikās; only the central 8 ucchvāsas are securely Daṇḍin's, framing sections are anonymous later supplements — hence conflated. Colophons uniformly ascribe to Daṇḍin (a later attributive convention).

texts.title: Mahāpratisarāvidyādhāraṇī
texts.primary_author: anonymous (buddhavacana; one of the Pañcarakṣā dhāraṇīs)
texts.basis: Secondary (Nakamura, Indian Buddhism; Nariman, Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism): the Mahāpratisarā is a protective dhāraṇī, one of the five Pañcarakṣā spells, treated as buddhavacana with no named human author. Hidas's critical edition (cited in workitem) is a modern edition, not an attribution. No personal author in tradition.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt + secondary:cleaned_Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism_Nariman G K_1919.txt + tradition
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna/dhāraṇī text; traditionally buddhavacana, member of the Pañcarakṣā collection. No human author; remains anonymous.

texts.title: Rāvaṇavadha [Bhaṭṭikāvya]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Bhatti: Ravanavadha (Bhattikavya)', no other attribution. Secondary (skt-en-mono): work titled Rāvaṇavadha/Bhaṭṭikāvya; some traditions equate 'Bhatti' with Bhartṛhari ('Bhartṛ-kāvya') but scholarship holds Bhaṭṭi 'a distinct individual from either of the authors of the Śatakas or the Bhaṭṭikāvya'. Older popular view (Dutt; History of Civilisation) wrongly ascribed it to Bhartṛhari.
texts.note: Secure single author Bhaṭṭi; legendary equation with Bhartṛhari rejected by scholarship. Composition associated with the reign of Dharasena/Śrīdharasena of Valabhī.

texts.title: Larger Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā]
texts.basis: Workitem edition is Choong's study of a Gilgit-ms folio range (202r-205r) of the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā — a Mahāyāna sūtra. Secondary (Lit. about Philosophical Systems; Conze tr.): treated as an anonymous Prajñāpāramitā sūtra (buddhavacana), with later commentaries (Abhisamayālaṅkāra correlation) by others; the sūtra itself has no named human author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt + tradition
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra; this is a Gilgit-ms excerpt. Note: the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā was later recast to fit the Abhisamayālaṅkāra, but that redaction is distinct from this sūtra text. No author; anonymous.

texts.title: Vinayasūtra
texts.basis: Secondary (Nolot, Règles de Discipline: 'Gunaprabha: Vinaya-Sūtra and Auto-Commentary on the Same'; Davidson; Nakamura): the Vinayasūtra (Mūlasarvāstivāda) is by Guṇaprabha, a disciple of Vasubandhu, who also wrote the auto-commentary (svavyākhyāna/svopajñavṛtti). The Sāṅkṛtyāyana edition is the sūtra text. Sub-commentaries (e.g. by Jinaputra/Dharmamitra) are by others but not part of this item.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Regles de Discipline des Nonnes Bouddhistes_Edith Nolot, 1991.txt + secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Pure realms and hidden abodes.txt
texts.note: Secure: Guṇaprabha authored both the sūtra and its auto-commentary. This item is the sūtra (mūla) text only, so single author; not conflated.

texts.title: Āryabhaṭīya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (aryabhcu.htm): edition is 'Āryabhaṭīya of Āryabhaṭa, with the commentary of Bhāskara I and Someśvara' (Shukla, INSA 1976). The GRETIL file is the composite root-text-plus-commentary, so it bundles Āryabhaṭa's mūla kārikās with the bhāṣya/ṭīkā of two later astronomers. Secondary (Nance; Bauddhavidyāsudhākara) confirms Bhāskara's commentary on Āryabhaṭa I.
texts.note: Root Āryabhaṭīya securely by Āryabhaṭa I (499 CE). The cited GRETIL source file is the commentarial edition, conflating mūla with commentaries of Bhāskara I and Someśvara — hence roles separated and conflation_flag set.

texts.title: Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣadkārikā [Gauḍapādakārikā, Māṇḍūkyakārikā, Āgamaśāstra]
texts.primary_author: Gauḍapāda (traditional, prakaraṇas 1-3); Alātaśānti (prakaraṇa 4) possibly a later/different hand
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Gauḍapāda as author (ed. checked vs Kathavate 1890). Secondary (Sarao/Long 'Buddhism and Jainism'; 'Lit. about Phil. Systems') confirm traditional Gauḍapāda ascription but flag the four prakaraṇas and the heavily Buddhist Alātaśānti (ch.4) as problematic; Richard King ('Early Advaita Vedanta and Buddhism: the Mahayana Context of the Gaudapadiya-Karika') argues ch.4 is a later text by a different author for a different (Buddhist) audience.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Buddhism and Jainism_K. T. S. Sarao, Jeffery D. Long, eds., 2017.txt; web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaudapada
texts.note: Traditional single ascription to Gauḍapāda; modern scholarship (King) treats the Āgamaśāstra as likely composite, with Alātaśānti-prakaraṇa (book 4, 100 kārikās) a separate, later, Buddhist-flavoured stratum, possibly not by the author of prakaraṇas 1-3. Distinct from the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad itself (śruti).

texts.title: Kālottara
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śaiva Siddhānta scripture; traditionally spoken by Śiva)
texts.basis: Edition is Muktabodha digital e-text; IFP/EFEO critically edit the recensions. Secondary (Sanderson, 'History through textual criticism'; skt-en-mono / Goodall) treat the Kālottara as a Saiddhāntika revealed scripture (āgama) with no human author, surviving in multiple recensions of differing length (Dviśatika, Sārdhatriśatika, ... Bṛhatkālottara), names used interchangeably in Nepalese mss colophons.
texts.note: No human author: revealed Śaiva (Siddhānta) tantra/āgama, framed as Śiva's teaching. Transmitted in several recensions; this is a collective, anonymous redaction, not a single-author work.

texts.title: Daśakumāracarita: Ucchvāsas 3, 5 & 6
texts.basis: GRETIL colophons each read 'iti śrīdaṇḍinaḥ kṛtau daśakumāracarite...', attributing to Śrī Daṇḍin. Secondary (Spink, 'Ajanta... Arguments') confirms the famous prose core is by Daṇḍin; ucchvāsas 3, 5, 6 belong to that authentic core. NB: the surrounding Pūrvapīṭhikā and Uttarapīṭhikā are NOT by Daṇḍin but later anonymous additions — but those framing sections are not part of these three ucchvāsas.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Ajanta. History and Development. Vol, II, Arguments About Ajanta_Walter M. Spink, 2006.txt
texts.note: These three ucchvāsas are the genuine Daṇḍin core, hence secure (no conflation here). The work as a whole IS conflated (Pūrvapīṭhikā/Uttarapīṭhikā composed by other, anonymous hands per Spink), but those parts are excluded from this item.

texts.title: Nyāyāvatāra
texts.primary_author: Siddhasena Mahāmati (per Balcerowicz; traditionally ascribed to Siddhasena Divākara)
texts.basis: GRETIL header reads 'tarkapañcānanaśrī-siddhasenamahāmati-viracitaḥ' (composed by Siddhasena Mahāmati), with the Vivṛti commentary by Siddharṣi-gaṇin (ed. Balcerowicz 2008). Secondary (Balcerowicz, 'Two Siddhasenas and the authorship of the Nyāyāvatāra and the Sanmati-tarka-prakaraṇa', JIPh; cited in Dundas 2002, Potter Encyclopedia, Dan Martin bibliog.; Dhaky, 'Date and Authorship of Nyāyāvatāra') argue the Nyāyāvatāra was wrongly ascribed by tradition to Siddhasena Divākara (author of the Sanmatitarka) and is in fact by a distinct, later Siddhasena Mahāmati (c. 7th-8th c.).
texts.note: Two-Siddhasenas problem: traditional ascription to Siddhasena Divākara is disputed; the kārikās are now attributed to Siddhasena Mahāmati (≠ the Sanmatitarka author). The Vivṛti (Siddharṣi) and Ṭippana (Devabhadra) are separate commentarial layers, not part of this kārikā text.

texts.title: Kāmikāgama, Uttarabhāga
texts.basis: No source_url. Edition is the South Indian Arcaka Saṅgha (Madras) Grantha/Nagari print of this Śaivāgama. The Kāmika is one of the 28 mūla-āgamas of Śaiva Siddhānta, a revealed scripture (no human author); the Uttarabhāga (latter portion) is recognised as a distinct, later accreted stratum relative to the Pūrvabhāga, but still transmitted anonymously as scripture.
texts.note: Revealed Śaiva āgama, no named human author. Uttarabhāga is a later, separately accumulated layer than the Pūrvabhāga — a redactional stratification rather than two named authors, so not flagged as multi-author conflation.

texts.title: Kāvyādarśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Belvalkar 1924; ch.3 from Wiesbaden 2011 crit. ed.) names Daṇḍin; colophon 'iti kāvyādarśe ... prathamaḥ paricchedaḥ'. Secondary (Companion to World Philosophies; skt-en-mono; Dimitrov diss. noting the work also = Kāvyalakṣaṇa) uniformly attribute the Kāvyādarśa to Daṇḍin, traditionally the same Daṇḍin as the Daśakumāracarita author (7th c.).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Companion to World Philosophies_Eliot Deutsch, Ron Bontekoe, 1999.txt
texts.note: Single, secure author Daṇḍin. Alt. title Kāvyalakṣaṇa (esp. in Tibetan transmission, Dimitrov). The 'three Daṇḍins' question concerns identity/unification of works, not multi-author conflation of THIS text.

texts.title: Āgamaśāstra 3: Advaitaprakaraṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header: file contains (1) Māṇḍūkyopaniṣatkārikā by Gauḍapāda and (2) Āgamaśāstrabhāṣya, commentary ascribed to Śaṅkara. Kārikās traditionally by Gauḍapāda; the bhāṣya is ascribed to Śaṅkara but its authenticity is debated in scholarship.
texts.note: current_author lists only Gauḍapāda but etext bundles kārikā (Gauḍapāda) + bhāṣya (ascribed to Śaṅkara). Two distinct authors/roles; Śaṅkara ascription traditional/disputed.

texts.title: Saṃtānāntarasiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'ācāryadharmakīrtipraṇītaṃ santānāntarasiddhiprakaraṇaṃ samāptam' (root by Dharmakīrti). Negi 1997 ed. (Bibliotheca Indo-Tibetica) is the Saṃtānāntarasiddhi of Dharmakīrti AND the Saṃtānāntarasiddhi-ṭīkā of Vinītadeva (confirmed web: Exotic India / EAST), both reconstructed from Tibetan.
texts.note: Edition contains both root (Dharmakīrti) and ṭīkā (Vinītadeva); current_author omits the commentator.

texts.title: Sambandhaparīkṣā
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'ācāryadharmakīrtiviracitā sambandhaparīkṣā (prabhācandrakṛtavyākhyopetā)' and closing 'iti prabhācandrakṛtā sambandhaparīkṣāvyākhyā samāptā' — root by Dharmakīrti with commentary by Prabhācandra (Dwarikadas Shastri 1972 ed.). Note: Dharmakīrti also wrote a svavṛtti and Vinītadeva a ṭīkā (Nakamura, Indian Buddhism, lines 7760-7761), but THIS etext carries Prabhācandra's vyākhyā.
texts.note: Etext = root (Dharmakīrti) + Prabhācandra's commentary; current_author omits commentator. Distinct from Vinītadeva-ṭīkā and Dharmakīrti's own vṛtti.

texts.title: Sūtra fragment SF 279 (SuttaCentral 'Other Fragments'; Turfan Sanskrit canon)
texts.basis: SuttaCentral sf279 = a Sanskrit canonical sūtra fragment from the Central Asian (Turfan) Sanskrit canon, edited by Ernst Waldschmidt (Kleine Sanskrit-Texte 4, 1932, and later Turfan editions). Canonical sūtras have no individual author (traditionally Buddhavacana); Waldschmidt is the modern editor. Exact sūtra title uncertain (sits between SF 277 Agrajña and SF 280 Avasruta; possibly a Māratajjanīya-type fragment — unverified).
texts.note: Anonymous canonical fragment; no author to assign. Waldschmidt = editor only. current_author already empty.

texts.title: Pramāṇaviniścaya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (input Klaus Wille): Pramāṇaviniścaya of Dharmakīrti; this etext is the single-folio Sanskrit manuscript fragment ed. Matsuda & Steinkellner, WZKS 35 (1991). Authorship of the Pramāṇaviniścaya by Dharmakīrti is secure; fragment carries no separate commentary.
texts.note: Single-author root fragment (kārikā + svavṛtti are Dharmakīrti's own); no conflation. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Nyāyabindu
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition is Malvania (ed.), Durveka Miśra's Dharmottarapradīpa — a sub-commentary on Dharmottara's Nyāyabinduṭīkā, itself a commentary on Dharmakīrti's Nyāyabindu. Confirmed by Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, lines 7653-7655): Dharmottara wrote the Nyāyabinduṭīkā, Durveka (10th-11th c.) wrote the Dharmottarapradīpa sub-comm. Three-layer multi-author work.
texts.note: Root (Dharmakīrti) + ṭīkā (Dharmottara) + sub-comm. (Durvekamiśra). current_author lists only Dharmakīrti; conflates three authors.

texts.title: Śākyasiṃhastotra: yaśodharākṛtam
texts.primary_author: attributed to Yaśodharā (pseudepigraphic; actual author unknown)
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC bst-108 header (Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha) lists entry BuSto_86 as 'Śākyasiṃhastotra (yaśodharākṛtam)' — framed as composed by Yaśodharā, the Buddha's wife, not by a named historical scholar. No real author is recoverable.
texts.note: Stotra placed in the mouth of Yaśodharā; ascription is legendary/pseudepigraphic. True author anonymous. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Mahāmaṇivipulavimānaviśvasupratiṣṭhitaguhyaparamarahasyakalparājadhāraṇī
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Klaus Wille, ed. H. Matsumura 1983 from Gilgit MSS) states this is an anonymous dhāraṇī; manuscript fragmentary with beginning and end lost, no colophon or author attribution. Genre (kalparāja-dhāraṇī) is canonical/anonymous sūtra literature.
texts.note: Anonymous dhāraṇī from Gilgit MSS; no named author; matches current_author.

texts.title: Sambandhaparīkṣā
texts.basis: GRETIL header & colophons: 'ācāryadharmakīrtiviracitā sambandhaparīkṣā (prabhācandrakṛtavyākhyopetā)' and 'ityācāryadharmakīrtiviracitā sambandhaparīkṣā samāptā' + 'iti prabhācandrakṛtā sambandhaparīkṣāvyākhyā samāptā'. Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, p.305f) confirms Sambandhaparīkṣā as Dharmakīrti's, with his own vṛtti and a ṭīkā by Vinītadeva (this Bauddha Bharati ed. carries Prabhācandra's commentary).
texts.note: Root verses securely Dharmakīrti; edition bundles Prabhācandra's commentary. Root attribution matches current_author. Distinct commentator present but not a conflation of the root work's authorship.

texts.title: Vāsavadattā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Shukla 1966; input C. Ferstl) names Subandhu; internal verse 'sarasvatīdattavaraprasādaś cakre subandhuḥ...' and colophon 'iti mahākavi-Subandhu-viracitā Vāsavadattā nāma kathā samāptā'. Standard scholarly attribution; Subandhu is the recognized author of this prose kāvyā.
texts.note: Single-author prose romance; secure self-attribution in verse 13 and colophon. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Nyāyabinduprakaraṇa
texts.primary_author: Dharmakīrti (mūla); commentaries by Vinītadeva and Dharmottara in this edition
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Dharmakirti: Nyayabinduprakarana, with Vinitadeva's Nyayabindutikasisyahita and Dharmottara's Nyayabindutika'; commentary labels 'āryavinītadevakṛtiḥ' and 'ācāryadharmottarakṛtā nyāyabinduṭīkā'. Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, p.7650ff) confirms Nyāyabindu by Dharmakīrti with Vinītadeva's and Dharmottara's (c.730-800) commentaries.
texts.note: Root prakaraṇa securely Dharmakīrti; edition layers two distinct ṭīkās (Vinītadeva, Dharmottara). Root attribution matches current_author; commentators are separate persons but root authorship is not conflated.

texts.title: Pañcarakṣādevīstotrāṇi
texts.basis: Pañcarakṣā is an early anonymous collection of five protective dhāraṇīs/hymns to the five rakṣā goddesses (Mahāpratisarā, Mahāsāhasrapramardinī, Mahāmantrānusāriṇī, Mahāśītavatī, Mahāmāyūrī); Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, p.8020) describes it as a compiled collection, popular in Nepal. Tibetan records by c.800 CE. No named author; canonical/anonymous genre.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Anonymous compiled dhāraṇī/stotra collection of the five rakṣā goddesses; no historical author. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Āgamaśāstra 4: Alātaśāntiprakaraṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header: e-text = Māṇḍūkyakārikā (Gauḍapādakārikā/Āgamaśāstra) by Gauḍapāda + Āgamaśāstrabhāṣya ascribed to Śaṅkara; colophon 'śaṅkarabhagavataḥ kṛtau gauḍapādīyāgamaśāstravivaraṇe ʼlātaśāntyākhyaṃ caturthaṃ prakaraṇam'. Secondary (R. King 1995, citing V. Bhattacharya 1943): the GK is composite, four separate prakaraṇas possibly by different hands, GK IV (Alātaśānti) the most Buddhist/sophisticated and arguably independently authored. Karmarkar & Hacker question whether Śaṅkara wrote the bhāṣya.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:Early Advaita Vedanta and Buddhism (King 1995); secondary:Literature about the Philosophical Systems (Potter)
texts.note: Title conflates kārikā (Gauḍapāda) + bhāṣya (ascribed Śaṅkara, authorship disputed). Composite-text view: GK IV may be of separate authorship from prakaraṇas I-III. 'Gauḍapāda' itself is the traditional ascription of the kārikā.

texts.title: Kiraṇatantra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: mūla text extracted from Goodall's edition of Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha's Kiraṇavṛtti; no human author named. Secondary (Watson 2006; Flood 2006; Goodall 1998): Kiraṇatantra is a Śaiva Siddhānta scripture (āgama/tantra) traditionally revealed by Śiva, not authored by a human; the named commentator Rāmakaṇṭha is distinct from the scripture.
texts.note: Revealed scripture, no human author; traditionally Śiva's speech. The cited edition is Rāmakaṇṭha's commentary (Kiraṇavṛtti) but the e-text item is the mūla scripture only.

texts.title: Pramāṇavārttika
texts.basis: GRETIL header (DSBC input): 'Pramāṇavārtikam', root text only. Cited edition is Sāṅkṛtyāyana's PV with Manorathanandin's Vṛtti, but this e-text item is the mūla kārikās by Dharmakīrti. Secondary (Tillemans 2000; Nakamura 1980): PV verses by Dharmakīrti (7th c.); Manorathanandin's Vṛtti is a separate later commentary.
texts.note: Root verses by Dharmakīrti. Note Dharmakīrti also wrote a Svavṛtti (on ch. I) and Manorathanandin a later Vṛtti — not in this mūla e-text, so no conflation here.

texts.title: Pramāṇavārttikakārikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Miyasaka ed. of Pramāṇavārttika-kārikā (Sanskrit & Tibetan). Verses by Dharmakīrti, securely attributed (Tillemans 2000; Nakamura 1980).
texts.note: Kārikā-only edition; single author Dharmakīrti.

texts.title: Madhyamakāvatāra
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC e-text heading 'saṃskṛtabhāṣāyām madhyamakāvatārabhāṣyaṃ nāma' — this item is actually the Madhyamakāvatārabhāṣya (auto-commentary), which embeds the kārikās. Both root verses and auto-commentary are by Candrakīrti (La Vallée Poussin ed.; Tauscher; Apple 2019). Not a multi-author conflation since the bhāṣya is Candrakīrti's own.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:Jewels of the Middle Way (Apple 2019); secondary:Jnanagarbha (Eckel 1987)
texts.note: E-text is the bhāṣya (svabhāṣya), not bare kārikās; kārikā and auto-commentary both by Candrakīrti, so single author despite two roles.

texts.title: Tattvopaplavasiṃha
texts.basis: Secondary (Matilal 2002; Basham 1954; Burton 2001; Franco): Tattvopaplavasiṃha securely by Jayarāśi Bhaṭṭa, the lone surviving Cārvāka/skeptical text, c. 7th-8th c. Edition Sanghavi & Parikh 1940, revised after E. Franco 1987.
texts.source: secondary:Mind language and world (Matilal 2002); secondary:Wonder that was India (Basham 1954); secondary:Emptiness Appraised (Burton 2001)
texts.note: Single author; secure. A few date the author 7th vs 8th c. but authorship undisputed.

texts.title: Prasannapadā [Madhyamakaśāstravṛtti]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: root text Madhyamakaśāstra/Mūlamadhyamakakārikā by Nāgārjuna, commentary Prasannapadā (=Madhyamakaśāstravṛtti) by Candrakīrti; ed. P.L. Vaidya 1960. Secondary (Warrick 1983; Loizzo 2001; de la Vallée Poussin) confirm the edition prints Nāgārjuna's kārikās with Candrakīrti's commentary.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Wise One in the Prasannapada_M Warrick - 1983_OCR.txt
texts.note: current_author lists only Candrakīrti (the commentator); the edited text conflates Nāgārjuna's root verses with Candrakīrti's running commentary. Roles separated.

texts.title: Madhyamakaśāstrastuti
texts.basis: 14-verse eulogy of Nāgārjuna. Traditionally ascribed to Candrakīrti; the verses are transmitted between ch. 27 and the colophon of the Tibetan Prasannapadā/MMK (Apple 2019, citing de Jong 1962). Loizzo 2001 lists it among 'traditional attributions' to Candrakīrti; Nakamura 1980 'eulogical poem of 14 verses'. Edition: Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (Pandey 1994), stotra 41. De Jong, 'La Madhyamakaśāstrastuti de Candrakīrti' (Oriens Extremus 1979).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Jewels of the Middle Way_James B Apple, 2019.txt; secondary:cleaned_Candrakirti and Moon-flower of Nalanda_Loizzo, 2001.txt
texts.note: Attribution to Candrakīrti is traditional, not secure; transmitted appended to the MMK/Prasannapadā in Tibetan witnesses. current_author already flags 'traditionally attributed'.

texts.title: kālikāpurāṇam
texts.basis: Śākta (Upa)purāṇa, no named author; multi-layered compilation. Sanderson (Śaiva Age) notes Ballālasena's Dānasāgara (12th c.) omits it, suggesting it postdates him; Gray & Overbey 2016 note its current form unlikely to predate the 16th century (Stapelfeldt 2001). Venkatesvara Press ed. 1907/08.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_The Śaiva Age.txt; secondary:cleaned_Tantric traditions in transmission and translation_Gray & Overbey, 2016.txt
texts.note: Genuinely anonymous Purāṇa, traditionally framed as Vyāsa/sūta narration; product of redaction in NE-India Śākta milieu (ca. 11th-14th c. core). No individual author to assign.

texts.title: Madhyamakāvatārakārikā
texts.basis: Title specifies the kārikā (root verses) of the Madhyamakāvatāra, securely by Candrakīrti; ed. LVP (Bibliotheca Buddhica IX, 1907-12), which reconstructs the verses. Candrakīrti also wrote a separate auto-commentary (Madhyamakāvatārabhāṣya), but this work-item is the verse text alone. Secondary (Eckel 1987; Brunnhölzl 2009; Tauscher's MA/MABh edition; Nakamura 1980) consistently attribute MA and MABh to Candrakīrti.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Jnanagarbha on the Two Truths_Eckel.txt; secondary:cleaned_Luminous Heart_Brunnholzl, 2009.txt; edition:LVP BB IX
texts.note: kārikā = verse text only, single author Candrakīrti; the auto-commentary (bhāṣya) is a distinct work, so no conflation within this item. Attribution secure.

texts.title: Vinayasūtra [1: Pravrajyāvastu]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Guṇaprabha viracitaṃ Vinayasūtravṛttyabhidhānasvavyākhyānam' — both the sūtra and its auto-commentary (svavyākhyāna/vṛtti) by Guṇaprabha; ed. Bapat & Gokhale 1982. Secondary (Schopen, Dead Monks and Bad Debts; Brunnhölzl 2009 'Guṇaprabha 6th c.') confirm Guṇaprabha as author of the Vinayasūtra.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Schopen_Dead monks and bad debts.txt
texts.note: Single author for both root sūtra and auto-commentary (the edition includes the svavṛtti). Not a conflation despite two layers, since same author. Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya digest.

texts.title: Ajitasenavyākaraṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: no historical author named; colophon names only the scribe — 'likhitam idaṃ pustakaṃ dharmabhāṇakanarendradattena' (written by Dharmabhāṇaka Narendradatta). Ed. N. Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts I (1939). Nakamura 1980: Ajitasena-vyākaraṇa-nirdeśasūtra, story of conversion of King Ajitasena of Magadha; 'seems to have been composed in Kashmir'.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism_Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks_Schopen, 1997.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra (buddhavacana framing). Only attested name is the scribe Narendradatta in the Gilgit ms colophon — a copyist, not an author. No authorial attribution possible.

texts.title: Ajitasenavyākaraṇa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Buddhist sūtra, buddhavacana; scribe Narendradatta named in MS colophon)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (DSBC input, ed. N. Dutt, Gilgit MS vol. I) names no composer; closing colophon names only the scribe of the manuscript ('lisvitam idaṃ pustakaṃ dharmabhāṇaka-narendradattena') and a donor (deyadharma Bālasiṃha). Secondary lit treats it as an anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra (Schopen, Nakamura).
texts.note: Buddhavacana genre; named name (Narendradatta) is the copyist of the Gilgit MS, not the author. No single-author claim.

texts.title: Suvarṇaprabhāsasūtra (Suvarṇaprabhāsottamasūtrendrarāja)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. S. Bagchi, Mithila Institute 1967; DSBC input) claims no individual author; chapter colophons name only section titles. Standard scholarship (Nariman; Snellgrove) treats the 'Golden Light' sūtra as an anonymous, multiply-redacted Mahāyāna scripture attributed to the Buddha as speaker.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical sūtra; grew by accretion over recensions (8/19/21/31-ch), but no distinct named authors to separate — not a conflation of named persons.

texts.title: Agrajñasūtra (Waldschmidt SF 277, Central Asian Sanskrit canon fragment)
texts.basis: SuttaCentral identifies SF 277 as the Agrajñasūtra, a Central Asian Sanskrit canon (Turfan) sūtra fragment edited by Ernst Waldschmidt (Kleine Sanskrit-Texte 4, 1932). It is a canonical Sarvāstivāda/(Mūla)sarvāstivāda āgama text, spoken-by-Buddha genre, with no individual author; Waldschmidt is the modern editor.
texts.note: Title/author were blank in source; identified as Agrajñasūtra. Anonymous canonical fragment; Waldschmidt = editor only.

texts.title: Brahmayāmala (Picumata)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Śākta Śaiva Vidyāpītha tantra, revealed scripture; multi-stage redaction)
texts.basis: Sanderson, 'The Śaiva Age': the Picumata/Brahmayāmala is a Vidyāpītha Yāmala-tantra of the Bhairava cult, an anonymous revealed scripture (~12000 vv.) and a major source for the Laghuśaṃvara; its opening chapter narrates its own redactional history (Padmabhairava of Orissa and 14 disciples) — i.e. framed as revelation transmitted/redacted, not authored. Hatley's critical ed. (2018) treats it as composite/multi-layered but not as discrete named authors.
texts.note: Bhairava/Devī-spoken revealed tantra; composite layers exist but no named distinct authors to separate, so anonymous (not conflated).

texts.title: Śivadharma Nandiprokta (Śivadharmaśāstra, 'spoken by Nandin')
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Śaiva lay-religion śāstra; traditionally narrated by Nandikeśvara/Nandin)
texts.basis: Sanderson, 'The Śaiva Age': the Śivadharma corpus supplies the obligations of the uninitiated lay Śaiva devotee (and the king); it is an anonymous early Śaiva normative corpus. The work is framed as spoken by Nandikeśvara/Nandin (traditional narrator), not authored by a historical individual. No critical edition assigns it to a named author.
texts.note: Traditional ascription: dialogue narrated by Nandin/Nandikeśvara (legendary framing). Anonymous in fact; first text of the Śivadharma corpus.

texts.title: Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: transcription (Klaus Wille) of Edward Conze's ed. of the Gilgit MS, chs. 70-82 (6th-8th Abhisamayas), Roma 1974. The 18,000-line Prajñāpāramitā is an anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra (an expansion within the Large PP family), buddhavacana; no individual author claimed. Conze = editor, Wille = transcriber.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Prajñāpāramitā recension; part of the expansion/contraction series (8k/18k/25k/100k), but no named authors — not a conflation.

texts.title: Kalyāṇatriṃśatikāstotra
texts.basis: DSBC edition (Bauddha Stotra Saṃgraha, ed. Janardan Shastri Pandey 1994) lists only an editor, no author; it is a Vajrayoginī/Vajravārāhī tantric praise-hymn. Web searches found no named composer or traditional ascription. No secondary-literature hits on the title.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist tantric stotra (30-verse praise of Trikāyanivāsinī Vajradevī / Vajrayoginī); edition names only J.S. Pandey as editor of the stotra collection, not an author. No colophon author found.

texts.title: Fragments of Prajñāpāramitā texts
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Input by Klaus Wille' — Wille compiled fragments from 16 sources (Konow 1942, Bidyabinod 1927, Smith in Montell 1938, Bongard-Levin, Watanabe 1912, Karashima 2004 etc.). The underlying Prajñāpāramitā material is anonymous canonical Mahāyāna scripture; no single original author.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Prajñāpāramitā fragments; modern scholarly compilation by Klaus Wille drawing on many editors. Compiler role distinct from (anonymous) original authorship — not a single-author work.

texts.title: Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (canonical sūtra); Edward Conze (editor); Klaus Wille (digital input)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: text from the Gilgit manuscript, ed. Edward Conze, The Gilgit Manuscript of the Aṣṭādaśasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitā chs. 55-70 (Roma 1962, SOR 26) and chs. 70-82 (1974, SOR 46); digital input by Klaus Wille. No author attribution for the sūtra itself — anonymous Mahāyāna canonical scripture.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Prajñāpāramitā sūtra (18,000-line version) from Gilgit MS. Conze = modern critical editor (role noted as redactor of the edition), not author.

texts.title: Hayagrīvavidyā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. N. Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts I, 41-46): no colophon or author named; the text is a dhāraṇī/vidyā presented as 'uttered from the mouth of Avalokiteśvara' (āryāvalokiteśvaramukhodgīrṇam). Secondary lit (Epigraphic Evidence vol., von Hinüber 2014; N.N. Bhattacharyya; Ganhar) treats it as an anonymous Gilgit dhāraṇī. No human author.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist dhāraṇī/vidyā from Gilgit; framed as spoken by Avalokiteśvara (a deity-speaker frame, not a human author). Edited by N. Dutt.

texts.title: Mattavilāsaprahasana
texts.basis: GRETIL header quotes the text's own ascription 'śrīmahendravikramavarmapraṇītam' (composed by Mahendravikramavarman). Secondary lit (Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism, multiple passages) attributes the 7th-c. farce to Pallava king Mahendravarman I / Mahendravikramavarman (c. 650). Wikipedia/scholarly consensus concurs.
texts.note: Securely single-authored: Pallava monarch Mahendravarman I (alias Mahendravikramavarman), early-to-mid 7th c. Self-ascription in the prologue confirms it. Current_author correct.

texts.title: Nārāyaṇaparipṛcchā āryamahāmāyāvijayavāhinī nāma dhāraṇī
texts.basis: DSBC edition; the prior critical edition (A.C. Banerjee, Univ. of Calcutta 1941, with Tibetan trans. by Bstan pa'i nyin byed, from a 1361 CE palm-leaf MS) names editor/translator only, no author. Secondary lit (Boucher 2008; Garrett; McCombs 2014; Dhammika) treats it as an anonymous Mahāyāna dhāraṇī-sūtra cited in the Śikṣāsamuccaya. No human author.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Buddhist dhāraṇī-sūtra (a paripṛcchā framed as Nārāyaṇa's questions). A.C. Banerjee = editor; Bstan pa'i nyin byed = Tibetan translator — neither is the author.

texts.title: Sarvajñānottara
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śaiva āgama, traditionally spoken by Śiva)
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text (IFP T-334) gives no human author; Ganesan crit. ed. treats it as a Śaiva Siddhānta āgama. Watson 2006 lists it among revealed scriptures (Niśvāsa, Sārdhatriśatikālottara, Sarvajñānottara) 'spoken by Śiva himself' with no human author. A separate vidyāpāda/yogapāda commentary by Aghoraśiva exists but is not this text.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Self's Awareness of Itself Bhatta Ramakantha...Alex Watson, 2006.txt; gretil-header (Muktabodha)
texts.note: Revealed āgama, no human author; traditional ascription to Śiva. Not to be conflated with later commentaries (e.g. Aghoraśiva).

texts.title: Āyuḥparyantasūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (canonical Buddhist sūtra; reconstructed by Hisashi Matsumura)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: based on H. Matsumura, Āyuḥparyantasūtra (Göttingen 1989); text restored from Gilgit/Central Asian fragments (Raghu Vira & Lokesh Chandra facsimile); input Klaus Wille. Canonical sūtra (buddhavacana), no named human author.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical sūtra reconstructed from fragments; Matsumura is modern editor/reconstructor, not author.

texts.title: Viśvantarāvadāna
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Buddhist avadāna; ed. Kabita Das Gupta)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Kabita Das Gupta, Viśvantarāvadāna, eine buddhistische Legende, Berlin 1978; no original author named, only modern editor. The Viśvantara/Vessantara story is anonymous avadāna legend material; verse avadānamālā tradition generally anonymous (web: Britannica/Wikipedia on avadāna).
texts.note: Anonymous avadāna legend; Das Gupta is modern editor only.

texts.title: Sārdhatriśatikālottarāgama
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śaiva āgama, mūla); Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (vṛtti, not in this e-text)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: based on N.R. Bhatt's ed. WITH the commentary of Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha, but 'the commentary has not been entered' (input D. Goodall). Watson 2006 lists Sārdhatriśatikālottara among revealed scriptures spoken by Śiva (no human author) and lists Rāmakaṇṭha's Sārdhatriśatikālottaravṛtti among his surviving works.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Self's Awareness of Itself Bhatta Ramakantha...Alex Watson, 2006.txt
texts.note: E-text = anonymous mūla āgama only; Rāmakaṇṭha authored the vṛtti (a distinct work, excluded here). Listed authors separate the roles for completeness; conflation_flag false since the e-text contains only the mūla.

texts.title: Skanda-Purāṇa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (composite purāṇa; traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: based on the Adriaensen/Bakker/Isaacson critical edition (Groningen 1998-), modern editors only, no historical author. Purāṇas are anonymous composite works traditionally credited to Vyāsa; this early (c. 6th-7th c.) Skandapurāṇa recension has no individual author.
texts.note: Anonymous composite purāṇa; traditional ascription to Vyāsa. Editors are modern critical editors, not authors.

texts.title: Bṛhajjātaka (Bṛhat Jātaka)
texts.basis: GRETIL header & colophon (BJ_28.09) self-name the author 'varāhamihiro' the 'āvantika muni' (sage of Avanti). Ed. N. Chidambaram Iyer. Standard, undisputed attribution to Varāhamihira (6th c. CE astronomer/astrologer).
texts.note: Self-attribution in final colophon verse; secure single authorship. Commentaries (e.g. Bhaṭṭotpala) are separate works not in this e-text.

texts.title: Śiva-Upaniṣad
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally revealed by Śiva/Mahādeva to Ṛṣy Ātreya)
texts.basis: GRETIL header/text: no single author; presented as revelation from Mahādeva (Śiva) to Ṛṣy Ātreya; colophon 'ṛṣyātreyasagotreṇa muninā hitakāmyayā' implies a sage of Ātreya's lineage as transmitter/compiler, not original author. A minor Śaiva Upaniṣad of the Adyar 'unpublished Upaniṣads' collection.
texts.note: Anonymous Upaniṣad; traditional revelatory framing (Śiva → Ṛṣy Ātreya). No real authorial ascription.

texts.title: Bṛhatsaṃhitā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: digitalized text (Yano/Sugita) based on Tripathi ed. with Utpala's commentary readings integrated [marked U.]; text identifies Varāhamihira as composer. Secondary lit uniformly attributes Bṛhatsaṃhitā to Varāhamihira (6th c.) and the standard commentary (Vivṛti) to Bhaṭṭotpala (10th c.).
texts.note: Root text securely Varāhamihira's. Edition incorporates Utpala's commentarial readings but the work itself is single-author; Utpala = commentator only (not full conflation).

texts.title: Santānāntarasiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'ācāryadharmakīrtipraṇītaṃ santānāntarasiddhiprakaraṇaṃ samāptam' = root by Dharmakīrti. Edition title (Negi 1997) is 'Santānāntarasiddhi kārikā ṭīkā' — a Sanskrit reconstruction that includes the commentary. Secondary lit (Pind 2009 bibliography; Keyt 1980; Amar Singh 1984; Vostrikov) repeatedly: 'Santānāntarasiddhitīkā of Vinītadeva', edited by Stcherbatsky 1916 alongside Dharmakīrti's root. So this title bundles Dharmakīrti's verses + Vinītadeva's ṭīkā.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Dignaga’s Philosophy of Language. Dignaga on anyapoha. Pramanasamuccaya V_Ole Holten Pind, 2009.txt
texts.note: Conflation: kārikā/prakaraṇa = Dharmakīrti; ṭīkā = Vinītadeva. Negi's reconstructed edition presents both together; current_author named only Dharmakīrti.

texts.title: Śrīlokeśvarabhāṣitaparājikā
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (attributed in title to the teaching of Śrī Lokeśvara/Avalokiteśvara)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (teaching framed as spoken by Śrī Lokeśvara/Avalokiteśvara)
texts.basis: DSBC page at the cited URL returned Caṇḍamahāroṣaṇa-tantra content (URL/section mismatch) with no author, only digitization credits (Miroj Shakya). Secondary lit (Nakamura 1980, p.~9424): the Lokeśvara-Pārājikā is one of several Nepalese Pārājikā texts (Saddharma-, Tārā-, Mañjuśrī-, Vajrasattva-Pārājikā) — Buddhist adaptations of Brahmanical Dharmaśāstra (saṃskāra/prāyaścitta/vrata), anonymous, ascribed in title to the deity who 'speaks' them.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Nepalese Pārājikā text; title-ascription to Lokeśvara/Avalokiteśvara is a revelatory framing, not a human author. No editor/author in cited e-text page (which mismatched to Caṇḍamahāroṣaṇa).

texts.title: Ratnāvalīnāṭikā
texts.basis: GRETIL text Act I prologue: 'asmatsvāminā śrīharṣadevena ... ratnāvalī nāma nāṭikā kṛtā' — internally ascribed to Harṣadeva. Secondary lit (Basham, Wonder that was India, p.~ on Harṣa's three plays): the plays ascribed to King Harṣa 'may be the work of a ghost writer'; traditional accounts name Dhāvaka (and some scholars Bāṇa). Attribution to Harṣa is traditional/royal, with longstanding scholarly suspicion of authorship by a court poet.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Wonder that was India_A. L. Basham, 1954.txt
texts.note: Conventionally 'Harṣa's' but royal authorship is a traditional ascription; ghost-writer (Dhāvaka/Bāṇa) suspected. Not a true multi-part conflation, so conflation_flag=false; status downgraded to traditional.

texts.title: Śrīmahādevīvyākaraṇa [Śrīmahādevīvyākaraṇasūtra, Dvādaśadaṇḍakanāmāṣṭaśatavimalīkaraṇā]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Mahāyāna sūtra; word of the Buddha)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Dutt ed., Gilgit Mss vol. I): no author named, only editor N. Dutt; text opens 'evaṃ mayā śrutam', characteristic anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra (vyākaraṇa/prophecy of Mahādevī, with Avalokiteśvara). Secondary lit (Studholme 2002; Pagel; Sueki; Danish Mss catalogue) treats it as an anonymous Buddhist sūtra preserved in Gilgit, not an authored work.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Mahāyāna sūtra (buddhavacana); editor Dutt only. No human author.

texts.title: Priyadarśikā
texts.primary_author: Harṣa (Harṣavardhana), traditional; authorship disputed (Dhāvaka/Bāṇa ghostwriting tradition)

authors.name: Dhāvaka (alleged actual composer per Kāvyaprakāśa tradition)

texts.basis: GRETIL header: śrīHarṣadevaviracitā nāṭikā, ed. M.R. Kale; colophon 'samāpteyaṃ Priyadarśikā nāma nāṭikā' (no scribal name). Secondary (2500 Years of Buddhism; Nalinaksha Dutt): three plays Priyadarśikā/Ratnāvalī/Nāgānanda ascribed to Harṣa 'although there are scholars who doubt Harṣa's authorship'. Britannica/Wikipedia confirm traditional Harṣa attribution; classic Mammaṭa Kāvyaprakāśa tradition that Dhāvaka was paid to write Harṣa's plays.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_2500 Years Of Buddhism_P. V. Bapat, 1956.txt + web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Priyadar%C5%9Bik%C4%81
texts.note: Royal attribution traditionally accepted but disputed: a strand of tradition (Mammaṭa) credits the actual composition to a court poet Dhāvaka; some scholars also link Harṣa's plays to Bāṇa. Not a multi-author conflation of distinct textual parts, hence conflation_flag=false; disputed single authorship.

texts.title: Vivāhapaṭala
texts.basis: GRETIL header: stated author Varāhamihira, data entry Mizue Sugita, based on Lamsala ed. with Utpala's commentary (cited as 'U.'); closing colophon notes Varāhamihira 'composed' (cakre) the vivāhapaṭalam. The e-text annotations draw on Utpala's commentary but the base text is Varāhamihira.
texts.note: Base text securely Varāhamihira (6th c.); Bhaṭṭotpala/Utpala (10th c.) supplies the commentary consulted in the edition. Commentary is a distinct later hand but only referenced, not the conflated subject of the e-text, so conflation_flag=false.

texts.title: Yogayātrā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: author Varāhamihira, ed. Ramacandra Jha (KMSG 23, 1986), with Kern (1913) and Bhaṭṭotpala's commentary as reference. Secondary (Mylius, Geschichte der altindischen Literatur): 'Von Varāhamihira stammt ein weiteres Werk, das den Namen Yogayātrā [...]' confirming Varāhamihira's authorship.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Geschichte der altindischen Literatur_Klaus Mylius_2003 (1983).txt
texts.note: Securely Varāhamihira. This is the (shorter/vulgate) Yogayātrā; Bhaṭṭotpala's commentary referenced in edition but text is Varāhamihira's. No multi-author conflation in the e-text.

texts.title: Yogayātrā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: author Varāhamihira, ed. David Pingree, The Bṛhadyātrā (Yogayātrā), Govt. of Tamil Nadu 1972; intro by Pingree, foreword by R.N. Sampath; input Mizue Sugita. Secondary (Mylius) confirms Varāhamihira's Yogayātrā.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Geschichte der altindischen Literatur_Klaus Mylius_2003 (1983).txt
texts.note: Pingree's edition of the longer Bṛhadyātrā recension (= Yogayātrā) of Varāhamihira. Securely Varāhamihira; duplicate title to yogay2_u but a different recension/edition. Single-author text.

texts.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header/content: author Bāṇabhaṭṭa, input Sansknet; colophon 'iti śrīmahākavibāṇabhaṭṭakṛte harṣacarite rājadarśanaṃ nāma dvitīya ucchvāsaḥ'. Edition is Führer's Harṣacarita WITH Śaṅkara's commentary Saṅketa. Secondary (Buddhistic Studies, Law 1931; Sanderson, Hindu Tantric Chaplains) confirm 'Harṣacarita of Bāṇa, Saṅketa of Śaṅkara' as a base-text + commentary pairing.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Buddhistic Studies_Bimala Churn Law,ed., 1931.txt + secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_Hindu tantric chaplains.txt
texts.note: E-text Ucchvāsa 2 is Bāṇa's text (per colophon, mahākavi Bāṇabhaṭṭa). Śaṅkara is the commentator (Saṅketa) of the cited Führer edition, a distinct later author; listed for role-separation but the e-text content itself is Bāṇa only, so no conflation within the e-text.

texts.title: Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header: text in traditional Purāṇa frame (Sūta narrating, Śāṃśapāyana etc.); ed. Venkatesvara Steam Press, input Sansknet; no personal author. Secondary (skt-en-mono Purāṇa notes; Kane HD V): Purāṇas including Brahmāṇḍa are multi-layered compilations with no single author, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa; the 'Jaya' title and varying verse counts show composite, redacted transmission.
texts.note: Anonymous composite Mahāpurāṇa; traditional ascription to Vyāsa recorded separately (not a real author). It is multi-layered/redacted over centuries but not a conflation of identifiable distinct named authors, so authors=[] and conflation_flag=false.

texts.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 7
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'iti śrīmahākavibāṇabhaṭṭakṛtau harṣacarite chatralabdhirnāma saptama ucchvāsa' names Bāṇabhaṭṭa. Winternitz and secondary sources concur: Bāṇa, 7th c. court poet of Harṣa. E-text is mūla only; Śaṅkara's Saṅketa commentary (in Führer ed.) not present in this file.
texts.note: Single author Bāṇabhaṭṭa; ucchvāsa of an unfinished but single-author kāvya. Commentary by Śaṅkara cited in edition but not in this e-text.

texts.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 8
texts.basis: GRETIL Ucchvāsa files (Sansknet input) carry Bāṇabhaṭṭa colophons; Nirnaya-Sagar/Parab edition is 'The Harṣacharita of Bāṇabhaṭṭa'. Secondary literature (Winternitz; wisdomlib) uniformly attributes to Bāṇa, 7th c.
texts.note: Eighth (final extant) ucchvāsa; work breaks off unfinished but is wholly Bāṇa's, no second author of completion known.

texts.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 4
texts.basis: GRETIL colophons name 'śrīmahākavibāṇabhaṭṭakṛti'; Führer edition titled Bāṇabhaṭṭa's Harṣacarita. Secondary consensus (Winternitz, Britannica, wisdomlib) attributes to Bāṇa.
texts.note: Mūla ucchvāsa, single author. Śaṅkara's Saṅketa is a separate commentary not in this e-text.

texts.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 6
texts.basis: GRETIL colophons attribute to Bāṇabhaṭṭa; Führer edition Bāṇabhaṭṭa's Harṣacarita with Śaṅkara's Saṅketa. Secondary sources unanimous on Bāṇa, 7th c.
texts.note: Mūla ucchvāsa, single author.

texts.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 5
texts.basis: GRETIL colophons name Bāṇabhaṭṭa; Führer edition Bāṇabhaṭṭa's Harṣacarita. Secondary consensus on Bāṇa.
texts.note: Mūla ucchvāsa, single author.

texts.title: Harṣacarita
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Parab/Nirnaya-Sagar 7th ed. 1946, input Jens Thomas/Willem Bollée); colophons 'iti śrī-mahā-kavi-Bāṇa-bhaṭṭa-kṛte Harṣacarite'. No commentator in e-text. Winternitz, Britannica, wisdomlib: Bāṇa, 7th c. court poet of Harṣa; first two ucchvāsas autobiographical. Work unfinished but single-author.
texts.note: Complete extant text (8 ucchvāsas), all by Bāṇabhaṭṭa. Unfinished is not conflated; no separate completer or commentator present in this mūla e-text.

texts.title: Suprabhātastotra
texts.current_author: Harṣadeva / Śrīharṣadeva (King Harṣa of Kanauj), attributed
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives no internal author colophon (Pandey ed., BuSto_89). Secondary: Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, p.135) notes the Suprabhāta-stotra 'was also ascribed to him' [Śrīharṣa]; Lal Mani Joshi: 'Emperor Harsa composed morning prayer... called Suprabhata-stotra.' Web: recent study (Acta Asiatica Varsoviensia 35, 2022) 'Suprabhātastava by Śrīharṣadeva' notes attribution remains debated, linked to Śrīharṣadeva via 'haṛsa/harṣa' wordplay; 8 mss + Tibetan, 5 editions.
texts.source: web:https://aav.iksiopan.pl/index.php/en/current-issue/905-new-manuscript-evidence-for-suprabhatastava-by-srihar-adeva-the-drepung-texts-critically-examined; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Traditional ascription to King Śrīharṣa(deva) of Kanauj (c.590-647) but modern scholarship treats it as disputed/uncertain; internal evidence rests on harṣa wordplay, not an explicit author colophon. Status sharpened from bare 'attributed' to disputed.

texts.title: Daṇḍikathā
texts.basis: DSBC/Pandey Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (1994) source; no author named. Secondary: no hits in reference-literature for Daṇḍikathā (only spurious 'Dandi' substring matches). Web: Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha confirmed as a multi-author collection but no source names an author for the Daṇḍikathā specifically.
texts.note: No author identified in any of the three sources; remains genuinely anonymous. A minor Buddhist piece in the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha collection.

texts.title: Kiñcitatantra
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text prepared from a Nepalese (NGMCP) manuscript (mss 4-1867, reel b 142/7 per web); no printed critical edition, no author named. Secondary: no relevant hits in reference-literature. Web: confirmed only as a manuscript-based tantra in the Muktabodha digital library, no named author.
texts.note: Anonymous tantric text surviving in a single NGMCP manuscript; as is typical for tantras, no human author is named (revelatory framing). Remains anonymous.

texts.title: Tikanikayātrā
texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes to Varāhamihira (ed. V.R./V.K. Pandit, 'Tikanikayātra of Varāhamihira', JUB); but text itself has no author colophon. Web: the (Yoga)yātrā works on travel astrology are traditionally counted among Varāhamihira's; however attributions of several minor works to Varāhamihira are noted as 'of doubtful nature.'
texts.note: Attribution to Varāhamihira is the standard scholarly/traditional ascription (a short yātrā/travel-astrology text) but not secured by an internal colophon; minor works under his name are sometimes doubtful. Downgraded from implied 'secure' to traditional.

texts.title: Harṣacarita: Ucchvāsa 3
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon explicitly names the author: 'iti mahākaviśrībāṇabhaṭṭakṛte harṣacarite rājadarśanaṃ nāma dṛtīya[=tṛtīya] ucchvāsaḥ'. Secondary: uniformly attributed to Bāṇa/Bāṇabhaṭṭa, Harṣa's 7th-c. court poet (Winternitz, Thapar, Doniger, etc.).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_History of Indian Literature II_MAURICE WINTERNITZ, 1933.txt
texts.note: Colophon-secured. (The Harṣacarita as a whole is unfinished and has a separate maṅgala/introductory portion, but Ucchvāsa 3 is unambiguously Bāṇa's per its own colophon.)

texts.title: Nāgānandanāṭaka
texts.basis: GRETIL: internal verse Nā_1.3 'śrīharṣo nipuṇaḥ kaviḥ' names Śrī Harṣa as author; ed. Sansarachandra. Secondary: standard attribution to Śrīharṣa (Karmarkar ed. 'Nagananda of Sriharsa'; Tatz; Goyal). Web: a long tradition (Mammaṭa's Kāvyaprakāśa) holds Harṣa's plays were actually written for hire by his court poet Dhāvaka (others, e.g. Doniger, defend Harṣa's own authorship).
texts.note: Disputed (not conflated): the dispute is WHO the single author was (King Harṣa as nominal/actual author vs. ghost-writer Dhāvaka), not different parts by different people. Internal verse claims Harṣa; classical critics name Dhāvaka. Listed both candidates with confidences; no separate kārikā/bhāṣya division.

texts.title: Hetubindu
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Gokhale ed. 1997) names Dharmakīrti; text colophon reads 'hetubindur ācārya-dharmakīrti-kṛtaḥ samāptaḥ'. Web (SEP, EAST) confirms Hetubindu as one of Dharmakīrti's genuine works. Steinkellner's parallel edition (item 597) agrees.
texts.note: Colophon explicitly: composed by ācārya Dharmakīrti. Securely attributed, no commentary mixed in this kārikā/prose root text.

texts.title: Nyāyabindu Prakaraṇakārikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Dwarikadas Shastri ed., Bauddha Bharati 1994) attributes to Dharmakīrti; standard ed. Stcherbatsky Bibliotheca Buddhica VII. Web confirms Nyāyabindu among Dharmakīrti's seven pramāṇa treatises, regarded as a masterpiece and genuine.
texts.note: Root verses/aphoristic text by Dharmakīrti; this is the kārikā/mūla layer only (Dharmottara's ṭīkā not included here). Secure.

texts.title: Kādambarī
texts.current_author: Bāṇa (Bāṇabhaṭṭa); completed by his son Bhūṣaṇabhaṭṭa (Pulinda)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'śrībāṇabhaṭṭaviracitā kādambarī (pūrvabhāgaḥ)' — this e-text is the Pūrvabhāga, genuinely Bāṇa's portion. Web (Wikipedia, Cowell/Thomas tr.) confirms Kādambarī left unfinished at Bāṇa's death; son Bhūṣaṇabhaṭṭa (also called Pulinda) composed the Uttarabhāga, adding introductory verses honoring his father.
texts.note: Title 'Kādambarī' conflates two distinct authors: Bāṇa wrote Pūrvabhāga (this file), son Bhūṣaṇabhaṭṭa/Pulinda wrote the continuation (Uttarabhāga). current_author already captures both. This file = Pūrvabhāga (Bāṇa) only.

texts.title: Hetubindu
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Steinkellner 1967, reconstructed Sanskrit from Tibetan; input Motoi Ono) attributes to Dharmakīrti; colophon 'hetubindur ācārya-dharmakīrti-kṛtaḥ samāptaḥ'. Same work as item 594 (different edition). SEP/EAST confirm genuine.
texts.note: Steinkellner's reconstructed Sanskrit text of Hetubindu; securely Dharmakīrti. Duplicate work-title of bsa071_u via a different (reconstructed) edition.

texts.title: Vādanyāya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Gokhale ed. 1993, Bibliotheca Indo-Buddhica 126) attributes to Dharmakīrti; closing verse honors 'ācāryadharmakīrticaraṇānām'. Vādanyāya is a recognized genuine work of Dharmakīrti (SEP). Much's parallel edition (item 599) agrees.
texts.note: Root treatise by Dharmakīrti; Śāntarakṣita's vipañcitārthā commentary not included in this root-text file. Secure.

texts.title: Vādanyāya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (M.T. Much, Dharmakīrtis Vādanyāyaḥ Teil I, Wien 1991; input Motoi Ono) attributes to Dharmakīrti. No colophon in the file excerpt, but editorial framing and the parallel Gokhale edition (item 598) confirm. SEP lists Vādanyāya as genuine.
texts.note: Much's critical Sanskrit edition of Vādanyāya; securely Dharmakīrti. Duplicate work-title of bsa064_u via a different edition.

texts.title: Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Author: Brahmagupta (A.D. 628)', ed. S. Dvivedin 1902; chapter colophons read 'iti śrī-brāhmasphuṭasiddhānte ... adhyāyaḥ'. Mylius (Geschichte der altindischen Literatur) cites the Kuṭṭakādhyāya of the Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta as 'von Brahmagupta'. Wikipedia/Britannica/MacTutor: composed 628 CE at Bhillamāla. No separate commentary (e.g. Pṛthūdaka's) in this e-text.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Geschichte der altindischen Literatur_Klaus Mylius_2003 (1983).txt; web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brahmagupta
texts.note: Single-author astronomical/mathematical siddhānta, 628 CE. Pṛthūdaka Svāmin's commentary exists separately but is not part of this digitized root text.

texts.title: Haṃsasāra
texts.basis: Source is a Muktabodha (muktalib7) DL catalog e-text with no edition and no current_author given. No author surfaced in secondary literature (skt-en-mono hits are only the common compound haṃsa-sārasa 'geese and cranes', not the title) or in web search; haṃsa-themed works of this name circulate as anonymous tantric/yoga material with no securely attested author.
texts.note: Obscure title; no colophon author available. Left anonymous rather than inventing an ascription; uncertain whether this is a yoga/haṃsa-mantra tract or other compilation.

texts.title: Siddhilakṣmīdevyārcana
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text from a single NGMCP manuscript (MS 1373, reel V 199/13); no printed critical edition and no named author. The text is a ritual worship manual (devyarcana/pūjā) of the goddess Siddhilakṣmī, a Kālī form of the Jayadrathayāmala/Kālīkrama prevalent in Nepal (Sanderson, Śaiva Age, notes Siddhilakṣmī among Newar Mantramārgic Śākta deities). Such Newar paddhati/arcana texts are typically anonymous scribal/ritual compilations.
texts.note: Anonymous Newar Sarvāmnāya ritual text; current attribution (anonymous/traditional) confirmed. Goddess associated with Uttarāmnāya per Mukundarāja's Saṃvartārthaprakāśa, but the arcana text itself has no author.

texts.title: Sarvatathāgatādhiṣṭhānavyūhasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC e-text header names no author/translator; presented as anonymous Buddhavacana (Buddha's word). Edition = N. Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts vol. I, pp. 49-89. No colophon author.
texts.note: Mahāyāna sūtra; anonymous by convention. Same work as item SA_K10_bsu037_u (duplicate title, parallel e-text from the same Gilgit/Dutt edition).

texts.title: Anityatāsūtra [Āryānityatāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Isshi Yamada (IBK 20.2, 1972), prepared by Klaus Wille from RAS London Hodgson Ms.55 and Tokyo Univ. Ms.416. No colophon naming author/translator; opens 'evaṃ mayā śrutam', closes 'ity āryānityatāsūtraṃ samāptam' (standard formula). Presented as discourse of the Buddha.
texts.note: Short canonical sūtra on impermanence; anonymous Buddhavacana. Yamada/Wille are modern editor and digitizer, not authors.

texts.title: Sarvatathāgatādhiṣṭhānavyūhasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL e-text (DSBC/Nagarjuna Institute & University of the West input project), based on N. Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts vol. I, pp. 49-89. Header names no author/translator; no colophon attribution. Classified as Sūtra section text no. 37.
texts.note: Duplicate of SA_K10_n526u (same sūtra, same Dutt edition; this is the bsu037 DSBC e-text). Anonymous Mahāyāna Buddhavacana.

texts.title: Siddhilakṣmī-akṣaramālāmantra (siddhilakṣmīkyutākṣaramālāmantra)
texts.basis: Muktabodha header: NGMCP MS 4/304 transcription, data-entered under A. Dash, selected by M. Dyczkowski; colophon places the section in 'bhairava śrotasi vidyāpīṭhe śiracchede jayadrathayāmale mahātantre' (Jayadratha-yāmala), 36th chapter of the Siddhilakṣmīvidhāna — a revealed Śākta-Śaiva tantra with no human author. Sanderson (Śaiva Age) treats Siddhilakṣmī worship as a Newar Mantramārga/Śākta tradition.
texts.note: Revealed scripture embedded in Jayadrathayāmala; no human author. Traditionally framed as Bhairava's teaching. Dyczkowski/Dash are modern editors, not authors.

texts.title: Vasudhārādhāraṇī 1 [Vasudhārādhāraṇīsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Ngawang Samten & S.S. Bahulkar, Dhīḥ 44, 2007; input Klaus Wille) gives no author. Text frames itself as Buddha's teaching to the householder Sucandra; colophon 'ārya śrīvasudhārā-nāma-dhāraṇī-sūtra samāptā' — canonical sūtra with no named composer.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna dhāraṇī-sūtra; traditional ascription is to the Buddha (buddha-vacana). Samten/Bahulkar are modern editors.

texts.title: Dhāraṇīsaṃgraha
texts.basis: DSBC page (ed. Atul Bhosekar, gen. ed. Venkataramana Reddy, 2017) names a modern editor only; gives no original author. By nature an anthology/collection of pre-existing dhāraṇīs by many anonymous hands; no single compiler attested.
texts.note: Anonymous compilation of dhāraṇīs. Bhosekar/Reddy are modern editors of the 2017 print, not the original compiler.

texts.title: Kauśikaprajñāpāramitāsūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. P.L. Vaidya, Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha I, 1961; input DSBC/SBIP) gives no author; colophon 'kauśikanāma prajñāpāramitā samāptā'. 84000 (Toh 19) describes it as a short condensed prajñāpāramitā summarizing larger sūtras, with Kauśika/Śakra as interlocutor — anonymous canonical text, not authored. Title refers to the interlocutor Kauśika (Indra), not an author.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna PP sūtra, one of the 'six mother scriptures'. 'Kauśika' = Śakra/Indra, the interlocutor, not the author. Borrows verses from Vajracchedikā and Nāgārjuna's MMK homage.

texts.title: Pradeśasaṃgraha
texts.basis: GRETIL header titles the work 'Govind Kaul: Pradeśasaṃgraha' (ed. K. Züfle, MS Stein 128, MLU Halle-Wittenberg). Govind Kaul (b. 1846 Srinagar, d. June 1899) was a Kashmiri pandit, assistant to Aurel Stein (Rājataraṅgiṇī) and G. Grierson (Kashmiri dictionary, Hatim's Tales); Sanderson (Hindu Tantric Chaplains) names 'Pandit Govinda Kaula' as Grierson's collaborator. The Pradeśasaṃgraha is his original Sanskrit work on Kashmiri historical toponymy.
texts.note: Named author identified: Govind Kaul, 19th-c. Kashmiri pandit. current_author was empty; now secure attribution. Züfle is the modern editor (Erstedition of MS Stein 128).

texts.title: Vajravidāraṇī-dhāraṇī
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Buddhist scripture; traditionally proclaimed by Vajrapāṇi)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Yutaka Iwamoto, Kleinere Dhāraṇī-Texte, 1937; input Klaus Wille) gives no author. Opens 'evaṃ mayā śrutam'; mantra is 'prabhāṣate sma' by 'yakṣendro vajrapāṇiḥ' — framed as canonical proclamation by Vajrapāṇi, not a historical author.
texts.note: Anonymous dhāraṇī; traditional in-text speaker is Vajrapāṇi (yakṣendra). Iwamoto/Wille are modern editor/inputter.

texts.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
