texts.primary_author: anonymous (canonical Mahāyāna sūtra); ed. Nalinaksha Dutt
texts.basis: GRETIL header: editor Nalinaksha Dutt (Calcutta 1934), input Suzuki/Takahashi; no authorship colophon. Text is a canonical Prajñāpāramitā sūtra presented as Buddha-vacana ('evaṃ mayā śrutam'), traditionally anonymous. Secondary lit (Nariman, Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism) treats the large PP sūtras as anonymous canonical scripture.
texts.note: Editor (Dutt) listed as editorial 'translator' role, not original author. Text is anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra; no human composer named. current_author 'anonymous/traditional' confirmed.

texts.title: Uddhārakośa
texts.basis: Edition title (Raghu Vira & Lokesh Chandra 1938) ascribes to Dakṣiṇāmūrti. Web/Aufrecht CC: 'Dakṣiṇāmūrti' noted as a fabulous/divine name (epithet of Śiva), so the attribution is legendary rather than a securely historical author. The kośa is a compilation entirely of quotations from 47 named Tantric authorities, so the compiler post-dates those sources.
texts.note: Ascription to Dakṣiṇāmūrti is traditional/legendary (a divine name), not historically secure; work is a compilatory tantric syllabic-code dictionary drawing on 47 prior tantras.

texts.title: Advayatārakopaniṣad
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Yoga Upaniṣad of the minor Upaniṣad corpus)
texts.basis: One of the 108 (Yoga) Upaniṣads; no author named. Bendall Catalogue and 'Literature about the Philosophical Systems' list it among the anonymous minor/Yoga Upaniṣads; White (Yoga in Practice) places the South Indian Yoga Upaniṣad collection's written record c.1600-1750. Standard Adyar ed. carries the separate commentary of Upaniṣad-brahmayogin.
texts.note: Root text anonymous. Distinct commentary (ṭīkā) by Upaniṣad-brahmayogin in standard ed. — different author/role from the root Upaniṣad; not a conflation within the root text itself.

texts.title: Mekhalā-dhāraṇī
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Buddhist dhāraṇī sūtra); ed. Ch. Tripathi
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Ch. Tripathi, 'Gilgit Blätter der Mekhalā-dhāraṇī', StII 7 (1981); from Gilgit mss (GBM). Input Klaus Wille. No colophon/author; opens 'namaḥ sarvabuddhabodhisatvebhyaḥ evaṃ mayā śrutam' — presented as Buddha-vacana dhāraṇī.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical dhāraṇī. Tripathi is the modern editor (editorial 'translator' role), not author. current_author confirmed.

texts.title: Sarvadurgatipariśodhana Tantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (canonical Buddhist tantra); ed. Tadeusz Skorupski
texts.basis: GRETIL header: based on Skorupski's edition (Delhi 1983); input Klaus Wille. No human compiler in colophon; presented as teaching of Buddha Śākyamuni and Vajrapāṇi — revealed scripture, traditionally anonymous. Davidson (Indian Esoteric Buddhism) and Snellgrove treat it as an anonymous yoga-tantra.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed tantra; Skorupski is modern editor (editorial role), not author. current_author confirmed.

texts.title: Kāśikāvṛtti
texts.basis: sanskritlibrary.org header and edition title (Sharma/Deshpande/Padhye 1969-70) name both Vāmana and Jayāditya as joint authors. Wikipedia/standard scholarship: a single title (vṛtti on Pāṇini's Aṣṭādhyāyī, c. 7th c.) composed by TWO distinct authors — traditional division (per I-tsing) is Jayāditya the first five adhyāyas, Vāmana the last three. Editions do not merge them into one composer.
texts.note: Multi-author conflation under one title: Jayāditya = adhyāyas 1-5, Vāmana = adhyāyas 6-8 (traditional split per I-tsing). current_author lumps both as undifferentiated joint authors; record now separates roles by portion. Adhyāya assignment is traditional, the dual authorship secure.

texts.title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header names no author, only ed. N. Dutt 1934; the text is a Mahāyāna Prajñāpāramitā sūtra of collective transmission. Secondary lit (Lethcoe JAOS 1976; Conze) notes Dutt's text is the REVISED recension reorganized to match Haribhadra/Abhisamayālaṅkāra topics, vs an unrevised recension (Chinese).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Anonymous canonical sūtra. Dutt edition = 'revised' recension reorganized per Abhisamayālaṅkāra; editor is reviser/redactor, not author.

texts.title: Pramāṇavārttikavṛtti [Pramāṇavārttikasvavṛtti]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'DHARMAKIRTI: PRAMANAVARTTIKASVAVRTTI', ed. Gnoli 1960 'first chapter with the autocommentary'. Secondary (Nakamura, Indian Buddhism, p.302 n.11) confirms the svavṛtti is Dharmakīrti's own autocommentary on the kārikā.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Svavṛtti = autocommentary: Dharmakīrti authored BOTH root verses and the vṛtti, so two roles but one author (not a conflation).

texts.title: Śiṣyalekha
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'kṛtir ācāryacandragomipādasya' attributing the dharmakāvya to Candragomin. Secondary (Tatz 1978; Hahn 1990; Loizzo 2001) consistently credit Śiṣyalekha to Candragomin (one of nine works traditionally ascribed to him).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:Candragomin and the Bodhisattva Vow_Mark Joseph Tatz, 1978.txt
texts.note: Colophon names Candragomin; secure. (Broader scholarship debates whether the grammarian and Buddhist poet Candragomin are one person, but the Śiṣyalekha attribution itself is undisputed.)

texts.title: Tantravārttika 2.1.1–4
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'Kumarila: Tantravarttika ad 2.1.1-4', ed. Kataoka 2004 (critical ed. of Śābarabhāṣya & Tantravārttika ad 2.1.1-4). Secondary lit uniformly attributes the Tantravārttika to Kumārila Bhaṭṭa (Mīmāṃsā Vārttika on Śabara's Bhāṣya).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Secure attribution to Kumārila Bhaṭṭa. This file is the Tantravārttika portion only; Kataoka edits both Śābarabhāṣya (Śabara) and Tantravārttika (Kumārila) but the workitem is the vārttika.

texts.title: Dantyoṣṭhyavidhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header (data entry A. Griffiths) names no author; described as an anonymous Atharvavedic phonetic/parisiṣṭa treatise (the 'fourth Karṇa treatise', a Caraṇavyūha-class technical text), ed. Ramagopala Sastri 1921. No colophon author.
texts.note: Anonymous Atharvaveda ancillary (phonetic vidhi on dental/labial sounds). No author named in edition or tradition.

texts.title: Āgamaśāstra 2: Vaitathyaprakaraṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header states the file contains BOTH (1) Māṇḍūkyopaniṣatkārikā by Gauḍapāda AND (2) Āgamaśāstrabhāṣya 'commentary ASCRIBED to Śaṅkara' (text not proofread). Secondary/web (Wikipedia Gaudapada; Bhattacharya): Gauḍapāda authorship is traditional and the four prakaraṇas may be four independent treatises (Bhattacharya); the bhāṣya's ascription to Śaṅkara is itself disputed.
texts.note: Single GRETIL file conflates two authors: Gauḍapāda's kārikā + a bhāṣya only ASCRIBED to Śaṅkara. Both layers are uncertain: Gauḍapāda traditional (prakaraṇas possibly composite per Bhattacharya), Śaṅkara bhāṣya ascription disputed.

texts.basis: Workitem has empty title and empty current_author; only a generic SuttaCentral landing URL (https://suttacentral.net/) with no text identifier. Nothing to triangulate; no header, no colophon, no secondary-lit hit for 'sf68'.
texts.note: Empty/placeholder record (K10 fragment id 'sf68'); no title or text content available to determine authorship. Canonical SuttaCentral material is generally anonymous buddhavacana in any case.

texts.title: Bauddha pāribhāṣikāḥ śabdāḥ
texts.basis: Title means 'Buddhist technical terms'; it is a glossary/word-list of Buddhist paribhāṣika (technical) vocabulary, not an authored treatise. DSBC e-edition (dsbcproject.org) gives no editor/printed-edition metadata. No author named in any source; web searches return only generic Buddhist-glossary results and the general notion of pāribhāṣika ('technical') terms. No secondary-lit match in reference-literature/.
texts.note: Modern/anonymous editorial compilation of Buddhist technical terms; no single author. Current attribution 'anonymous/traditional (lexical compilation)' stands.

texts.title: Āryānityatāsūtra [Anityatāsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition Isshi Yamada, IBK 20.2 (1972), input Klaus Wille, from RAS London (Hodgson Ms. 55) and Tokyo Univ. (Kawaguchi-Takakusu Ms. 416). Header explicitly notes NO named human author: a canonical sūtra opening 'evaṃ mayā śrutaṃ', i.e. attributed to the Buddha; Chinese/Tibetan parallels noted but no separate composer. Yamada/Wille are editor and digitiser, not authors.
texts.note: Canonical sūtra = anonymous buddhavacana; traditionally ascribed to the Buddha. Yamada = modern editor; Wille = digitiser (roles, not authorship). Confirms current 'anonymous/traditional'.

texts.title: Cittatattvasaṅgraha
texts.basis: DSBC text content (dsbcproject.org/canon-text/content/329/1333): mangala verse invokes the Buddha (ādityabandhu); the colophon explicitly names the author — 'śrī laṅkāputrabhūtena paṇḍitācāryabhikṣuṇā āmerikādharmadūta priyanandena dhīmatā', i.e. composed by the learned monk Priyananda, 'son of Laṅkā (Sri Lanka)' and 'dharma-messenger to America', who also titles it 'vijñaptimātratāsiddhi-pañcāśikā' (Fifty verses on Vijñaptimātra). This is a MODERN (20th-c.) Yogācāra-style composition modelled on Vasubandhu's Viṃśatikā/Triṃśikā, not an ancient śāstra; secondary lit and web have no entry for an old 'Cittatattvasaṅgraha' Yogācāra treatise (only Śāntarakṣita's distinct Tattvasaṃgraha).
texts.note: Author recovered from colophon: Priyananda, a Sri Lankan monk and dharma-dūta to America (modern author; 'āmerikā' shows 20th-c. date). Single author; current_author was empty.

texts.title: Mañjuvajrastotra
texts.basis: Text header/colophon (sanskritdocuments.org): opening 'mañjugarbhakṛtaṃ namaḥ śrīmañjuvajrāya' and colophon 'svayambhūpurāṇoddhṛtaṃ mañjugarbhaviracitaṃ' — i.e. the eight-verse stotra is stated to be composed by Mañjugarbha and extracted from the Svayambhūpurāṇa. Edition: Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha, ed. J. S. Pandey (MLBD 1994). 'Mañjugarbha' is itself an epithet of Mañjuśrī, so the ascription is devotional/traditional rather than securely a historical poet; no secondary-lit hit (reference-literature/ only has 'Mañjuvajra' as a deity).
texts.note: Colophon ascribes composition to Mañjugarbha (also an epithet of Mañjuśrī); extracted from the Svayambhūpurāṇa. Traditional ascription; current was 'anonymous/traditional'.

texts.title: Maṅgalaṣoḍaśastuti
texts.basis: A 'sixteen verses of auspicious praise' Buddhist stotra in the Nepalese tradition; source given as Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha ed. J. S. Pandey (MLBD 1994). The supplied DSBC content URL (content/187/880) returned a different text (Sattvārādhanagāthā, ascribed to Nāgārjuna) — an id mismatch, so it cannot confirm this stotra. Web/secondary searches find no named author for the Maṅgalaṣoḍaśastuti; the Pandey collection groups many anonymous Nepalese stotras alongside a few ascribed ones.
texts.note: No author located; anonymous Nepalese Buddhist praise-hymn. Caution: provided content URL resolved to a different work (Sattvārādhanagāthā / Nāgārjuna), so not used for attribution. Current 'anonymous/traditional' retained.

texts.title: Kāllottara (Kālottara)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Sanderson, Saiva Age; Yoga Powers ed. Jacobsen; Goodall on 'non-eclectic recensions of the Kālottara'): the Kālottara is a Śaiva Siddhānta scripture revealed by Sadāśiva, surviving in many recensions (Sārdhatriśatikālottara, Devīkālottara, Bṛhatkālottara, etc.). No human author; root text is anonymous revealed āgama. Commentaries (e.g. Śivārcanācandrikā of Appayadīkṣita on the Sārdhatriśatikālottara) are separate works by named authors, not part of the root.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_The Śaiva Age.txt; secondary:cleaned_Yoga Powers_Knut A. Jacobsen, 2012_a.txt
texts.note: Revealed Śaiva āgama, multiple recensions; root anonymous. Edition matched is Devīkālottarāgama (Dwivedi). Named commentators (Appayadīkṣita etc.) belong to separate commentary layer, not root.

texts.title: Siddhayogeśvarīmata(tantra)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Yoga Powers ed. Jacobsen; Mayer, Phur-pa bcu-gnyis; Tanemura): the Siddhayogeśvarīmata is a foundational Trika/Vidyāpīṭha Śaiva yoginī scripture, anonymous revealed tantra. Critical edition of selected chapters by Judit Törzsök (D.Phil., Oxford 1999) — editor/translator, not author. No human author named.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Yoga Powers_Knut A. Jacobsen, 2012_a.txt; secondary:cleaned_Scripture of the Ancient Tantra Collection. The Phur-pa bcu-gnyis_Robert Mayer, 1996.txt
texts.note: Title was blank in workitem; it is the Siddhayogeśvarīmatatantra, anonymous Trika scripture. Törzsök is the modern editor/translator.

texts.title: Vāmana-Purāṇa [Saromāhātmya recension]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. A.S. Gupta, Kashiraj Trust 1967) names no author; text uses Lomaharṣaṇa as narrator-frame and conventional Vyāsa ascription. The Saromāhātmya is a māhātmya inserted after Adhy. 23 — a secondary/later interpolated layer within the Purāṇa, not a separately authored named work.
texts.note: Anonymous Purāṇic māhātmya; traditional Vyāsa ascription only. Inserted recension layer; no colophon naming an author/scribe.

texts.title: Vāmana-Purāṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. A.S. Gupta, Kashiraj Trust 1967) names no author; opening invocation honours Vyāsa (VamP_1.1 'vyāsaṃ'), standard Purāṇic convention. A composite/redacted text per genre, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Vyāsa; in reality an anonymous, multi-layered redacted Mahāpurāṇa. No historical author.

texts.title: Tantravārttika (on the Mīmāṃsāsūtra / Śābarabhāṣya)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Alper, Understanding Mantras: Kumārila Bhaṭṭa, 7th c., author of Ślokavārttika, Tantravārttika, Ṭupṭīkā 'which together provide a commentary on the ŚabaraBh'; Bronkhorst; de Simini): the Tantravārttika proper is securely Kumārila's. But as edited it is a layered work — Kumārila's vārttika comments on Śabara's Bhāṣya, which itself comments on Jaimini's (Pūrva-)Mīmāṃsāsūtra (Adhy. 1.2 – 3). Three distinct authors stacked.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Understanding Mantras_Harvey P. Alper, 1989.txt; secondary:cleaned_Buddhism in the Shadow of Brahmanism_Johannes Bronkhorst, 2011.txt
texts.note: Vārttika layer securely Kumārila Bhaṭṭa; but the editions stack Jaimini's sūtra + Śabara's bhāṣya + Kumārila's vārttika (the Tantravārttika covers MS 1.2–3.; Ślokavārttika covers 1.1, Ṭupṭīkā the rest). Conflation_flag set for the multi-author layered structure.

texts.title: Vajravidāraṇā-dhāraṇī
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Āryavajravidāraṇā Nāma Dhāraṇī, ed. Ngawang Samten & Janardan Pandey, Dhīḥ 40, 2005): anonymous Buddhist dhāraṇī scripture; opens 'evaṃ mayā śrutam', presented as buddhavacana spoken via Vajrapāṇi. No individual author; editors are modern.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna dhāraṇī (buddhavacana). Samten & Pandey are editors, not authors.

texts.title: Pratisarāstotra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha) names editor only, no author for the individual stotra; the visible collection does not attribute stotra no. 66 to any person. It is a devotional dhāraṇī-stotra of the Mahāpratisarā (one of the Pañcarakṣā, noted in Nakamura as a popular protective spell-text) — a liturgical composition with no named author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist protective stotra tied to the Mahāpratisarā/Pañcarakṣā cult; edition gives editor (Pandey), no author.

texts.title: Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa 3
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa as compiler)
texts.basis: GRETIL e-text (Veṅkaṭeśvara Press; SANSKNET input) gives no individual author. Secondary literature (Katz, Mysticism and Sacred Scripture) notes the Brahmāṇḍa, Vāyu and Viṣṇu Purāṇas ascribe to Vyāsa only the role of compiling — i.e. a conventional/traditional ascription, not real authorship. Book 3 contains the Lalitopākhyāna/Lalitāsahasranāma material (White, Harper & Brown), a composite anonymous accretion.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Mysticism and Sacred Scripture_Steven T. Katz, 2000.txt; tradition
texts.note: Anonymous composite Purāṇa text; Vyāsa is the standard legendary compiler-ascription for all Purāṇas, not a historical author.

texts.title: Kaṭhināvadāna
texts.basis: DSBC canon-text source (avadāna). Listed by Hunter (Hodgson) among Nepalese Buddhist avadāna texts with no author named. Degener's critical edition (Indica et Tibetica 1, 1990) treats it as an anonymous avadāna narrative; avadāna literature is characteristically anonymous Buddhist hagiographic narrative.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Hunter_Life of Brian Houghton Hodgson_British resident at the court of Nepal.txt; dsbc-header
texts.note: Anonymous avadāna (story of the kaṭhina robe-offering); no traditional personal ascription found.

texts.title: Kāvyālaṃkāra
texts.basis: GRETIL header titles it 'Kāvyālaṅkāra of Bhāmaha'; colophons read 'iti bhāmahālaṅkāre' and the final verses (6.65-66) name Bhāmaha as the author who explained five subjects in six chapters. Secondary literature (skt-en-mono) consistently cites Bhāmaha as author of the Kāvyālaṃkāra, the foundational alaṃkāraśāstra work. Edition by C. Sankara Rama Sastri; GRETIL input by D. Goodall.
texts.note: Secure single authorship by Bhāmaha; internal colophon self-naming. (Note: the related Kāvyālaṃkāra-sūtra-vṛtti is a different work by Vāmana — not this text.)

texts.title: Kāmaratnatantra
texts.basis: Muktabodha/Goswami edition (Shillong 1928) gives an editor, no author. Secondary references (Mayer; PIATS-06) cite the Kāmaratna only as a known tantric/sorcery treatise (alongside Siddhanāgārjunakakṣapuṭa etc.) without a named author; Bendall's catalogue lists it among anonymous collections of treatises on sorcery. A practical/magical tantra of uncertain, composite, anonymous authorship.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Mayer_Scriptural Revelation in India and Tibet.txt; secondary:cleaned_Bendall_Catalogue of Sanskrit, Pali, and Prakrit books in the British museum.txt
texts.note: Anonymous magical/tantric manual (kāmaratna 'sorcery' genre); no reliable personal ascription; sometimes loosely grouped with Nāgārjuna-attributed magical texts but not securely so.

texts.title: Vajrayoginīpraṇāmaikaviṃśikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha, stotra 101) names no author. Elizabeth English (Vajrayogini: Her Visualization, Rituals, and Forms) catalogues this 21-verse stotra as GSS42 of the Guhyasamayasādhanamālā and gives no author for it, whereas the immediately following GSS43 (Vajravilāsinīstotra) is explicitly attributed to Vibhūticandra — confirming GSS42 itself is anonymous.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_English_Vajrayogini_Her Visualization, Rituals, and Forms.txt
texts.note: Anonymous 21-verse Vajrayoginī praise-stotra (GSS42); contrast neighbouring GSS43 which is by Vibhūticandra. No author named in edition or study.

texts.title: Maṅgalāṣṭaka
texts.basis: DSBC (Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon) liturgical text. 'Maṅgalāṣṭaka' is a generic title ('eight auspicious verses') used for many independent short hymns; secondary lit (skt-en-mono) shows it appears both in a Kālidāsa-associated work-list and as a Maṅgalāgaurī Śaiva hymn, i.e. the title is shared by multiple unrelated compositions. The DSBC item is an anonymous Buddhist benedictory octet with no named author.
texts.note: Generic 8-verse benediction; no single named author. Title is reused across many distinct anonymous hymns (Buddhist liturgical here).

texts.title: Mahāvākya Upaniṣad
texts.basis: sanskritdocuments header: 'mahāvākyopaniṣat (atharvavedokta)', a minor Upaniṣad of the Atharvaveda within the 108 Muktikā canon; the root text is anonymous śruti-style literature. The Adyar edition cited in the workitem is '108 Upaniṣads with the commentary of Upaniṣad-brahmayogin' — so the bhāṣya/ṭīkā is by Upaniṣad-brahmayogin (18th c.), a distinct hand from the anonymous root Upaniṣad.
texts.note: Root Upaniṣad anonymous (Atharvaveda, Muktikā 108). Commentary distinctly by Upaniṣad-brahmayogin per Adyar ed.; root and commentary are different authors -> conflation.

texts.title: Liṅga-Purāṇa 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Venkatesvara 1906, rev. O. Hellwig): colophons read 'iti śrīlaiṅge mahāpurāṇe'; no explicit authorial colophon, only the conventional Puranic ascription to Vyāsa. Secondary lit (skt-en-mono) prints the title-page formula 'Kṛṣṇadvaipāyana-maharṣi-śrīvedavyāsa-praṇītam', i.e. the standard traditional attribution to Vyāsa. Scholarship treats Purāṇas as anonymous multi-layered compilations.
texts.note: Conventional ascription to Vyāsa; genuinely an anonymous, redacted/composite Purāṇic compilation. Traditional, not secure.

texts.title: Balimālikā

authors.name: Zhalu Lotsāva (Chos skyong bzang po) with the Indian monk Dīpaṃkara

texts.basis: Bendall (Cambridge Catalogue of Buddhist Skt MSS): 'Balimālikā is a short work on ritual in 52 numbered sections... invocations, mystic formulae', opening 'namāmi vajrasattvasya...' — an anonymous Buddhist tantric (Vajrasattva) bali-offering ritual text. Wayman (Buddhist Insight): preserved in Tibetan Tanjur as mantras. Tibetan Studies/PIATS: the Tibetan translation was made by Zhalu Lotsāva together with the Indian monk Dīpaṃkara. No Indian author named.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Bendall_Catalogue... | secondary:cleaned_Buddhist Insight Wayman | secondary:cleaned_Tibetan Studies PIATS5
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist tantric bali ritual (52 sections, Vajrasattva). Tibetan rendering by Zhalu Lotsāva + Dīpaṃkara (translators, not authors). NOT a grammatical/Śaiva work despite GRETIL folder path.

texts.title: Kālānalatantra
texts.basis: Muktabodha digital e-text from a manuscript transcript; no printed critical edition identified (per workitem edition note). A Śaiva/Śākta Tantra: as revelation (āgama) literature these are conventionally framed as Śiva's teaching and are authorless/anonymous. Web (Muktabodha library) confirms it as an uncritically-edited tantric MS text; no author traceable.
texts.note: Anonymous Tantra (āgama/revelation framing). No named human author; no critical edition. Tradition would frame it as spoken by Śiva.

texts.title: Paribhāṣāvṛtti
texts.basis: Item sits in the same T02 Buddhist/tantric ritual collection as the Balimālikā (GRETIL rellit path); it is a paribhāṣā ('conventions/metarules') vṛtti for tantric ritual, not the famous grammatical Paribhāṣāvṛtti. Secondary lit (Bronkhorst 2003; Wujastyk 1993; Potter EIP vol.5) names the GRAMMATICAL Paribhāṣāvṛttis — Vyāḍi's, Puruṣottamadeva's Laghu-, Sīradeva's Bṛhat- — which are DISTINCT works and must not be conflated with this ritual text. No author given in header or sources for this item.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Tradition and Argument...Bronkhorst 2003 | secondary:cleaned_Language and Reality Bronkhorst 2011
texts.note: Anonymous tantric paribhāṣā-vṛtti. Explicitly NOT the grammatical Paribhāṣāvṛtti of Vyāḍi/Puruṣottamadeva/Sīradeva (those are separate, named works). current_author was empty -> resolved to anonymous.

texts.title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Paramokṣanirāsakārikā
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text header: kārikā 'sadyojyotiḥśivācāryapraṇītā', commentary 'bhaṭṭarāmakaṇṭhakṛtavyākhyāsahitā', colophon credits Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (son of Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha). Secondary: 'Literature about the Philosophical Systems...' lists Paramoksanirasakarika with Ramakantha's commentary; Watson/Goodall/Anjaneya Sarma 2013 ed. confirms.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Root kārikās by Sadyojyoti (Śaiva Siddhānta); commentary (vṛtti) by Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha. Current_author lists only Sadyojyoti, omitting the distinct commentator.

texts.title: Āryabuddhabalādhānaprātihāryavikurvāṇanirdeśanāma Mahāyānasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts IV): no author named; text opens 'evaṃ mayā śrutam', framed as buddhavacana. No human composer.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Mahāyāna sūtra preserved in Gilgit mss; traditionally buddhavacana, not authored by an individual.

texts.title: Nityakarmapūjāvidhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Ngawang Samten & J. Pandey, Dhīḥ 33, 2002): no author named; colophon 'iti nityakarmapūjāvidhiḥ samāptaḥ'. Web search found no individual attribution.
texts.note: Anonymous daily-worship ritual manual transmitted within Vajrayāna lineage; no compiler named in edition.

texts.title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Mokṣakārikā
texts.basis: Edition title (Dvivedi 1988) gives Mokṣakārikā of Sadyojyoti 'with Rāmakaṇṭha's comm.'. Secondary: 'Literature about the Philosophical Systems...' explicitly lists Sadyojyoti's Moksakarika + Vrtti on it, and skt-en-mono.txt cites Ramakantha's 'Moksakarikavrtti'. Muktabodha direct e-text returned 404 but parallel Paramokṣanirāsa header confirms the same author/commentator pairing.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt | secondary:cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt
texts.note: Root kārikās by Sadyojyoti; vṛtti by Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (Moksakarikavrtti). Current_author omits the commentator; multi-author work.

texts.title: mukuṭāgama
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text from IFP/EFEO transcript T0247; no critical edition, no author. As a Śaiva āgama (Mukuṭa/Makuṭāgama) it is revealed scripture, traditionally Śiva's word. Secondary lit search returned no human attribution.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Śaiva āgama; traditionally ascribed to Śiva, no historical author. No published critical edition.

texts.title: Amoghapāśakalparāja Part VII
texts.basis: DSBC header: framed as 'āryāvalokiteśvaramukhodgīrṇa' (uttered by Avalokiteśvara), category Sūtrapiṭaka>Tantra>Kriyātantra; input/proofing by Miroj Shakya (2018) only — no author. Web: text known via Chinese tr. (Taishō 1092), no named human composer.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical Mahāyāna/kriyā tantra; divine speakers (Avalokiteśvara, Vajrapāṇi) framed as source, not a human author. 'Part VII' is a modern editorial division of the serial Taishō Univ. transcription.

texts.title: Sūryasiddhānta
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to a revelation by Sūrya, the Sun-god, to the asura Maya)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally revealed by Sūrya to Maya); Raṅganātha (commentary Gūḍhārthaprakāśaka)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: text frames itself as revealed by Sūrya to Maya; cites earlier teaching by Bhāskara; ed. Hall 1854 with Raṅganātha's Gūḍhārthaprakāśaka. Web: original karikas anonymous, composite (two planetary theories, revised over time, c. 600-1000 CE); 26+ commentaries, Raṅganātha's commentary dated AD 1603, the named one in this edition.
texts.note: Root verses anonymous (legendary divine revelation Sūrya->Maya); this edition bundles Raṅganātha's commentary Gūḍhārthaprakāśaka (AD 1603) — root vs commentary are distinct authors, hence conflated.

texts.title: Mṛtyuñjayakalpa
texts.basis: No edition/source content (muktabodha.org generic). Title is a kalpa (ritual prescription manual) for the Mṛtyuñjaya (Śiva) cult; kalpa/tantric ritual manuals of this type are characteristically anonymous. Web found only Maha-Mṛtyuñjaya mantra material (traditionally tied to Vasiṣṭha/Mārkaṇḍeya), not this kalpa text; no author located.
texts.note: Anonymous ritual kalpa. Traditional ascriptions for the related mantra (Vasiṣṭha; Mārkaṇḍeya) belong to the mantra, not this kalpa manual; no author for the kalpa itself.

texts.title: Āryābuddhabalādhānaprātihāryavikurvaṇanirdeśanāma mahāyānasūtra
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Literature about the Philosophical Systems file) identifies Buddhabalādhāna-prātihāryavikurvaṇa-nirdeśa as Toh. 186, a Mahāyāna sūtra edited/studied by Gregory Schopen ('The five leaves of the Buddhabalādhāna...'). Ed. P.L. Vaidya, Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha I (BST 17). As canonical sūtra it has no human author (Buddhavacana). NOTE: the workitem source_url bsu014_u.htm currently serves the Daśabhūmikasūtra file (GRETIL mislabel), but title/edition confirm the sūtra identity.
texts.source: secondary:reference-literature/cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; gretil-header
texts.note: Canonical Mahāyāna sūtra (Toh. 186), anonymous Buddhavacana; ed. Vaidya, studied by Schopen. source_url file is mislabeled (contains Daśabhūmika).

texts.title: Vajradevīstotra
texts.basis: Ed. J.S. Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (1994), stotra no. 94. GRETIL stotra collection (bst-108u) does not surface an author for BuSto_94 in the header/index. Secondary lit references Vajradevī as a Newar/Vajrayāna goddess (Sarasvatī form; worship in Nepal) but names no author of this stotra. No attribution found.
texts.note: Buddhist hymn to the goddess Vajradevī; no author named in the stotra-saṃgraha edition or secondary literature. Anonymous.

texts.title: Dattātreyabodha
texts.basis: sanskritdocuments.org Dattātreya corpus page does not list 'Dattātreyabodha' under a human author; the Dattātreya corpus (Avadhūta Gītā etc.) is uniformly ascribed to the legendary deified sage Dattātreya, with works said to be transcribed by disciples. No historical author identified. The work is a short Advaita/Yoga 'bodha' text in the Dattātreya/Avadhūta literature.
texts.note: Ascribed to the legendary/deified sage Dattātreya — a pseudepigraphic traditional attribution, not a historical author; actual composer anonymous.

texts.title: Buddhastotra
texts.primary_author: Ārya Nāgārjuna (per DSBC node); framed as Buddhavacana in colophon
texts.basis: DSBC node content/187/880 (the given source_url) does NOT contain a generic 'Buddhastotra' — it serves the Sattvārādhanagāthā, headed 'Author: Ārya Nāgārjuna', yet its colophon reads 'samyaksambuddhabhāṣitā ... samāptā' (spoken by the Buddha). Title mismatch with workitem ('Buddhastotra'); the stotra ascribed to Nāgārjuna is a traditional/late ascription. Generic 'Buddhastotra' as a title is otherwise anonymous hymn-to-Buddha literature.
texts.note: source_url resolves to Sattvārādhanagāthā (DSBC ascribes to Ārya Nāgārjuna; colophon frames as Buddha-spoken) — title mismatch with 'Buddhastotra'; ascription to Nāgārjuna is traditional/uncertain. Two competing framings (Nāgārjuna vs Buddhavacana) but a single nominal author, so not flagged as multi-author conflation.

texts.title: Vasudhārādhāraṇī 3 [Vasudhārādhāraṇīsūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: no single original author; presented as Buddha (Bhagavān) speaking to householder Sucandra; ed. Padmanabh S. Jaini 1968. Colophon names only a scribe/patron (saṃvat 1695; sāhaśrī indrajī suṃdara). Secondary lit treats Vasudhārā dhāraṇī as a transmitted canonical dhāraṇī, not an authored work.
texts.note: Manuscript colophon names scribe/patron (saṃvat 1695), not a composer. Traditionally Buddhavacana; no human author. Editor = Jaini (modern).

texts.title: Kaularahasya
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text header: data-entered under Mark S.G. Dyczkowski; no named human author. Chapter colophons read only 'iti śrīkaularahasya ... paṭalaḥ'; framed as Śiva–Devī/Pārvatī dialogue (revealed). Secondary (Encyclopedia of Tantra) cites Kaularahasya as an authoritative Kaula source without an author.
texts.note: Colophons name only the text, no composer. Dyczkowski = digitizer/editor, not author. Revealed-scripture genre.

texts.title: Kāvyālaṃkāra-sūtra-vṛtti
texts.current_author: Vāmana (NOT Bhāmaha; metadata author hint appears incorrect)
texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes both sūtra and vṛtti to Ācārya Vāmana ('śrīpaṇḍitavaravāmanaviracitakāvyālaṅkārasūtravṛttau') — the vṛtti is svopajña (auto-commentary). Distinct from Bhāmaha's separate Kāvyālaṅkāra. Confirms current_author note: it is Vāmana, not Bhāmaha. Ed. Bechana Jhā/Dwivedī 1976; Kāmadhenu ṭīkā by Gopendra Tripurahara Bhūpāla (separate later commentator, not part of this title's text).
texts.note: Vāmana wrote both kārikā-sūtras and his own vṛtti (svopajña), so not a multi-author conflation. Metadata hint correct: Vāmana, not Bhāmaha.

texts.title: Avalokiteśvarahayagrīvadhāraṇī
texts.basis: DSBC item; no edition given. Web confirms it is a canonical Buddhist dhāraṇī of Hayagrīva (wrathful form/attendant of Avalokiteśvara) transmitted in canonical collections; no individual human author named — dhāraṇīs are framed as Buddha-spoken.
texts.note: Genre = canonical dhāraṇī, no composer; traditionally Buddhavacana. No colophon author available (DSBC, no printed edition).

texts.title: Rūpastava
texts.basis: GRETIL Bauddha Stotra Saṃgraha (ed. Janārdan Śāstrī Pāṇḍeya 1994): Rūpastava listed as BuSto_69; no author attribution in the collection's apparatus and no colophon ascription found. No secondary-lit attribution located.
texts.note: No author named in the stotra collection; remains anonymous. Editor = Pāṇḍeya (modern, not composer).

texts.title: Vātulottarāgama (Vātulottara)
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib, IFP, shaivam.org): Vātulottara is an upāgama of the Vātulāgama, one of the 28 Siddhāntāgamas of the Śaiva canon; revealed via Sadāśiva→Śiva→Mahākāla→Devas→Ṛṣis→humans (pratisaṃhitā theory) — no human author. Also associated with Vīraśaiva use (Śivasiddhāntatantra/Vātulottaratantra). IFP manuscript item, no critical edition/named author.
texts.note: Revealed Śaiva āgama; no human composer. Traditional ascription = divine revelation (Sadāśiva), recorded separately not as authorship.

texts.title: Mahāmāyāsādhanopāyikā
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (several sādhanas attributed to various siddhas)
texts.primary_author: various siddhas (Kukkuripa; Ratnākaraśānti; et al.) — compiled in Sādhanamālā
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib/Tibetan Buddhist Encyclopedia): the Sādhanamālā contains more than one Mahāmāyā sādhana, one attributed to Kukkurīpāda (a mahāsiddha); Mahāmāyātantra sādhanas also preserved by Ratnākaraśānti, Ratnavajra et al. The 'Mahāmāyāsādhanopāyikā' under one title thus collects sādhanas by distinct authors compiled in B. Bhattacharyya's Sādhanamālā (GOS 26/41).
texts.note: Multiple distinct sādhanas under one title; one ascribed to Kukkuripa, another tradition to Ratnākaraśānti; gathered by Sādhanamālā compiler. Roles separated rather than left flatly 'anonymous'.

texts.title: Āryatriratnānusmṛtisūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (buddhavacana; traditionally word of the Buddha)
texts.basis: Web (Encyclopedia of Buddhism / Rigpa Wiki / 84000): the Sutra of Recollecting the Three Jewels (ārya-ratnatrayānusmṛti / triratnānusmṛti) is an anonymous canonical sūtra, traditionally buddhavacana, with no identified human author. A separate MODERN sādhana of the same name by Dzongsar Khyentse (2012) is unrelated.
texts.note: Anonymous canonical sūtra; traditional ascription to the Buddha (buddhavacana). Distinct from modern same-named sādhana.

texts.title: Pīṭhastava
texts.basis: GRETIL header (sa_108 buddhist-stotras, ed. Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha 1994): the Pīṭhastava (BuSto_62) appears in the 108-stotra collection with NO author named in header or colophon. Web searches found no scholarly attribution. (Note: a Śaiva/Kaula 'Pīṭhastava'—hymn to the 64 sacred seats, attributed to Śrīnātha—appears in reference-literature/cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt, but that is a DIFFERENT, non-Buddhist text.)
texts.note: Buddhist Pīṭhastava unattributed in its edition; no human author recoverable. Beware homonymous Śaiva Pīṭhastava (Śrīnātha) — not the same work.

texts.title: Kāvyādarśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header + colophons explicitly name ācārya Daṇḍin: 'ity ācāryadaṇḍinaḥ kṛtau kāvyādarśe ... prathamaḥ paricchedaḥ' etc. (ed. Raṅgācārya Raḍḍī Śāstrī, BORI 1938). Authorship of the Kāvyādarśa by Daṇḍin is the long-standing scholarly consensus.
texts.note: Secure single authorship; colophons name ācārya Daṇḍin. (This GRETIL file covers Paricchedas 1-2 only.)

texts.title: Pratītyasamutpādamahāyānasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Āryapratītyasamutpādo nāma mahāyānasūtram, ed. P.L. Vaidya, MSS Part 1, 1961): presented as an anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra, attributed to the Buddha rather than a human author; no scribal/authorial colophon, only the standard sūtra-closing formula.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra; traditional ascription to the Buddha (buddhavacana).

texts.title: Paratantra (Parātantra)
texts.basis: Web (Cambridge Digital Library MS-ADD-01477): the Parātantra is described as 'a scripture of Śākta affiliation' — i.e. a revealed Śākta Tantra/āgama, which by genre is anonymous (framed as Śiva-Śakti dialogue, no human author). No human attribution found in local reference-literature (matches there are to the philosophical term paratantra-svabhāva, not this titled work) or in web searches.
texts.note: Śākta tantric scripture (āgama); anonymous/revealed, no human author. 'Paratantra' matches in secondary lit refer to the Yogācāra concept, not this text.

texts.title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Tattvatrayanirṇaya
texts.basis: Secondary (Goudriaan ed., Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism): 'Tattvatrayanirnaya of Sadyojyoti with commentary (-vrtti) of Aghoraśivācārya. In AştPrak.' Vrajavallabha Dvivedi's Aṣṭaprakaraṇa edition prints Sadyojyoti's root text together with Aghoraśiva's commentary.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism. Studies in Honor of André Padoux_Teun Goudriaan, ed, 1992.txt
texts.note: Root prakaraṇa by Sadyojyoti (early Śaiva Siddhānta exegete, c. 7th-8th c.); the edition conflates it with Aghoraśiva's (12th c.) vṛtti, a distinct later author. Roles must be separated.

texts.title: Mahogratārāstuti
texts.basis: GRETIL/Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (Pandey 1994) gives no author. DSBC/sanskritdocuments text has only the closing formula 'iti śrīmahogratārāstutiḥ samāptā' with no author named in verses or colophon.
texts.note: No author in colophon or header; hymn to Mahogratārā. Genuinely anonymous; no traditional ascription recovered.

texts.title: Narakoddhārastotra
texts.basis: Tibetan Tangjur (2028) lists Narakoddhāraṇastava among the stava-corpus ascribed to Nāgārjuna (Mitrikeski; Kano/Meinert mss lists). McCagney (Nagarjuna and the Philosophy of Openness, citing Lindtner's Nagarjuniana) places Narakoddharastava among works 'most probably not genuine'.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Nagarjuna and the Philosophy of Openness_Nancy McCagney 1997.txt
texts.note: Traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna in the stotra/stava corpus but judged spurious by modern scholarship (Lindtner's non-genuine list); the Sanskrit Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha text carries no internal author. Pseudepigraphic Nāgārjuna ascription, not secure.

texts.title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Tattvasaṃgraha
texts.basis: Goudriaan ed. bibliography lists 'Tattvasangraha, by Sadyojyoti, commentary'. The edition (Dvivedi, Aṣṭaprakaraṇa) is described in the workitem as 'Tattvasaṃgraha of Sadyojyoti with Aghoraśiva's comm.'
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism. Studies in Honor of André Padoux_Teun Goudriaan, ed, 1992.txt
texts.note: Root text by Sadyojyoti; the printed Aṣṭaprakaraṇa conflates it with Aghoraśiva's later commentary. Separate the two roles.

texts.title: Sadyojyoti: Nareśvaraparīkṣā
texts.basis: Sanderson (Purity and Power bibliography): 'Sadyojyoti, Nareśvaraparīkṣā with commentary'. Edition (Madhusudan Kaul Shastri, KSTS 45, 1926) is 'The Nareśvaraparīkṣā of Sadyojyotiḥ with the commentary of Rāmakaṇṭha'. The commentator is Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (Nareśvaraparīkṣāprakāśa).
texts.note: Treatise by Sadyojyoti (c. 7th-8th c.); commentary (-prakāśa) by Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha II (c. 950-1000), a distinct later author bundled in the edition. Roles separated.

texts.title: Tārāstuti [Āryatārāstuti]
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (several Tārā hymns; cf. Sarvajñamitra's Sragdharāstotra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header for the Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha entry reads 'ācārya candradāsakṛtā' (composed by Ācārya Candradāsa). Sanderson (skt-en-mono) independently cites 'the Tarastuti of Candradāsa (Bauddhastotrasangraha pp. 86-7)'. This is NOT Sarvajñamitra's Sragdharāstotra, which is a separate distinct Tārā hymn.
texts.note: Secure ascription to Ācārya Candradāsa. The current_author note wrongly conflates this with Sarvajñamitra's Sragdharāstotra (a different work, by a different Kashmiri author); they are distinct texts. Author resolved, not anonymous.

texts.title: Brahmasiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Mandana Misra: Brahmasiddhi', ed. Kuppuswami Sastri 1937, with Tattvasamīkṣā variants. Secondary (Potter EIP vol.3): 'The only clearly Advaita work by Mandana is the Brahmasiddhi'; an extremely important Advaita source. E-text is the root text only; Śaṅkhapāṇi's commentary not included.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 3 - Potter 1981.txt
texts.note: Root text only in this e-text; no conflation. Commentary by Śaṅkhapāṇi exists but is not part of this file.

texts.title: Vibhramaviveka
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Maṇḍanamiśra: Vibhramaviveka', based on L. Schmithausen's critical edition (Wien 1965, 'Maṇḍanamiśra's Vibhramavivekaḥ'). Attribution to Maṇḍana standard in secondary literature (Schmithausen monograph in reference-literature).
texts.note: Short verse treatise on the theory of error; securely Maṇḍana's.

texts.title: Brahmasiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Mandana Misra: Brahmasiddhi', input Diwakar Acharya 1999-2003, ed. Kuppuswami Sastri 1937 with older Tattvasamīkṣā (ed. Acharya 2006) readings. Same work as SA_GSP33_brasi. Potter EIP vol.3 confirms Maṇḍana's authorship.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 3 - Potter 1981.txt
texts.note: Duplicate edition of the Brahmasiddhi root text; root text only, no commentary conflation.

texts.title: Santānāntarasiddhiṭīkā
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'ācāryavinītadevakṛtā santānāntarasiddhiṭīkā samāptā' — ṭīkā by Vinītadeva on an unnamed root text. The root Santānāntarasiddhi is Dharmakīrti's (Stcherbatsky: 'ridiculed by Dharmakīrti in his Santānāntarasiddhi'; Goodman, Bapat: Dharmakīrti's Santānāntarasiddhi + Vinītadeva's commentary). Edition Negi 1997 titled 'Santānāntarasiddhi-kārikā-ṭīkā'.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Buddhist proof for omniscience. ’Sarvajnasiddhi’ of Ratnakirti_Steven B. Goodman, 1989.txt; secondary:cleaned_Buddhist Logic. vol 2_Theodore Stcherbatsky, 1930, 1984.txt
texts.note: Two distinct authors: root kārikā by Dharmakīrti, ṭīkā by Vinītadeva. current_author omits Dharmakīrti.

texts.title: Liṅga-Purāṇa 2,1–55
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Liṅgapurāṇa Uttarabhāga, Venkatesvara Press 1906, SANSKNET input; no historical author. In-text narrative frame is Sūta relating Mārkaṇḍeya. Traditional Purāṇic ascription to Vyāsa; no Vyāsa attribution in this portion's text.
texts.note: Anonymous compiled Purāṇa; traditional ascription to Vyāsa noted separately; narrated by Sūta/Mārkaṇḍeya.

texts.title: Yuktidīpikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Yuktidīpikā, commentary on Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā, ed. Shiv Kumar & Bhargava; no author named. Secondary: Dunne 2004 lists it under author 'Unknown'; Sastri (Ālambanaparīkṣā) calls it 'the anonymous commentary, yuktidīpikā'. Author genuinely unknown.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Foundations of Dharmakirti's Philosophy_John D. Dunne, 2004_OCR.txt; secondary:cleaned_Alambana-pariksa of Dignaga…_N. Aiyaswami Sastri, 1980.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Sāṃkhya commentary; the verses commented on are Īśvarakṛṣṇa's but those are not part of this commentary text.

texts.title: Āryaśrīgrahamātṛkā nāma dhāraṇī
texts.basis: DSBC header (n2990): names only input/proofreader Miroj Shakya (2019), supplier Nagarjuna Institute, sponsor University of the West; no classical author, editor, or colophon attribution. Genre is a canonical dhāraṇī (frame: Buddha-spoken protective recitation), so it carries no individual human author.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist dhāraṇī; only modern digitizers named. Traditional ascription is Buddhavacana (frame device), not a historical author.

texts.title: Nītiśāstra
texts.basis: Secondary: Tāranātha (Chattopadhyaya 1990) records a Nīti-śāstra in the Tanjur attributed to Masurakṣa alias Masurakṣi/Maṣa, with colophon 'Masurakṣa-nīti' (Tib. masurakṣa'i lugs-kyi). Van der Kuijp (Marginalia to Sa-skya Paṇḍita) lists nīti by Cāṇaka and Vasurakṣa/Masurakṣa. Web (Encyclopedia of Buddhism): Nītiśāstra of Masūrākṣa, Sanskrit lost, survives in Tengyur; first verse says Masūrākṣa collected the verses from various artha-śāstras (i.e., compiler). The Nāgārjuna ascription belongs to a separate/parallel nīti tradition; no scholarly support links this Masūrākṣa recension to Nāgārjuna. DSBC book/274 page bibliography fields blank.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Taranatha's History of Buddhism in India_Debiprasad Chattopadhyaya, 1990.txt | web:https://encyclopediaofbuddhism.org/wiki/N%C4%ABti%C5%9B%C4%81stra_of_Mas%C5%ABr%C4%81k%E1%B9%A3a
texts.note: Compiler is Masūrākṣa (colophon Masurakṣa-nīti), gathering verses from earlier śāstras. The Nāgārjuna alternative is a conflation with the distinct Nāgārjuna-nīti tradition; demoted to low confidence.

texts.title: Sarvatathāgatatattvasaṅgraha [Sarvatathāgatatattvasaṃgraha]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (sarvttsu): based on Lokesh Chandra 1987; input Udip/Anula Shakya, proofread Milan Shakya; no original author named. Secondary: Karmay & Nagano (New Horizons in Bon Studies) — the Tattvasaṃgraha 'was probably compiled towards the' 7th c.; Davidson (Indian Esoteric Buddhism) cites the 726 CE Chinese translation. A foundational yoga-tantra with no individual author; Indian commentaries exist (Śākyamitra, Ānandagarbha) but are separate works, not part of this root text.
texts.note: Anonymous root yoga-tantra (compiled ~7th c., Chinese tr. 726 CE). Frame is Buddhavacana; commentators (Śākyamitra, Ānandagarbha) are distinct and not conflated into this root edition.

texts.title: Vasudhārādhāraṇī 2 [Sucandragṛhapatiparipṛcchā/Sucandrāvadāna]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (vadhdhgu): transliterated from Gilgit MS by Klaus Wille; identification as Sucandragṛhapatiparipṛcchā/Sucandrāvadāna by Paul Harrison; source facsimile ed. Raghu Vira & Lokesh Chandra. No author named; end of manuscript (and any colophon) missing. Narrative frame is the Buddha teaching the householder Sucandra — literary convention, not authorship.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna dhāraṇī-sūtra known via Gilgit MS; colophon lost ('End of manuscript missing'). Editors/identifiers are modern (Wille, Harrison).

texts.title: Siddhārthacaritrakāvya
texts.current_author: Buddhaghoṣa (Buddhaghoṣa Thera, modern Sri Lankan author)
texts.primary_author: Buddhaghoṣa (medieval South Indian poet; namesake, NOT the Pali commentator nor a modern author)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (bghsic): colophons read 'iti buddhaghoṣācāryaviracite padyacūḍāmaṇināmni mahākāvye' — i.e. the Padyacūḍāmaṇi, a 10-sarga Buddha-life mahākāvya. Web (academia.edu 2013/2019; UBC thesis): this Buddhaghoṣa is a mere namesake of the celebrated Pali commentator; author essentially unknown, poem tentatively 9th–10th c., likely South Indian. Secondary: Law (1923) discusses the Padyacūḍāmaṇi of Buddhaghoṣācārya within his Buddhaghosa study; Warder (Indian Buddhism) lists 'Buddhaghosa: Padyacudamani'. The current_author's claim ('Buddhaghoṣa Thera, modern Sri Lankan author') is a misidentification.
texts.source: gretil-header | web:https://www.academia.edu/27378019 | secondary:cleaned_Life and Work of Buddhaghosa_Bimala Charan Law,1923.txt
texts.note: Conflates THREE distinct Buddhaghoṣas: (1) medieval Sanskrit poet 'Buddhaghoṣācārya' who actually wrote this Padyacūḍāmaṇi (~9th–10th c., S. India); (2) the 5th-c. Pali commentator (a namesake, NOT the poet); (3) the supposed 'modern Sri Lankan author' in current_author (incorrect). Real author = the medieval Sanskrit poet, otherwise unidentified.

texts.title: Tārābhaṭṭārikānāmāṣṭottaraśatakastotra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (108-names stotra; traditionally Buddha-spoken, revealed via Avalokiteśvara)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (bst-108): based on J. S. Pandey, Bauddha Stotra Saṃgraha 1994; input by Sanskrit Buddhist Canon Input Project. No human author named; framed as spoken by Avalokiteśvara to Vajrapāṇi; colophon 'śrīāryatārābhaṭṭārikāyā nāmāṣṭottaraśataṃ buddhaparibhāṣitaṃ parisamāptam' — presented as Buddha-spoken.
texts.note: Anonymous Tārā nāma-stotra; colophon declares it Buddha-spoken (buddhaparibhāṣita), internal speaker Avalokiteśvara. No historical author.

texts.title: Tārāsragdharāstotra
texts.basis: DSBC header gives (Ārya)tārāsragdharāstotram, ed. Janardan Shastri Pandey, no author named on page. Secondary lit unanimous: Nakamura (Indian Buddhism) 'composed in polished Kāvya style by the Kashmiri poet Sarvajnamitra'; Nariman (Literary History) same; Willson (In Praise of Tara) 'the famous Sragdhara Praise of Sarvajñamitra', dating him a disciple/contemporary of Candrakirti-Candragomin circle.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_In Praise of Tara_Martin Willson 1992.txt
texts.note: Single-author Kāvya hymn (37 verses) to Tārā by the Kashmiri poet Sarvajñamitra; secure traditional+scholarly attribution. The medieval Sanskrit commentary (Vidyabhusana's ed.) is a separate later text not included here.

texts.title: Vaiśeṣikasūtraṭīkā
texts.basis: Edition is Jambūvijaya's Vaiśeṣikasūtra of Kaṇāda WITH the Commentary (Vṛtti) of Candrānanda, GOS 136, 1961 — the GRETIL e-text carries the sūtra-mūla of Kaṇāda together with Candrānanda's vṛtti. Ganeri (Philosophy in Classical India) cites 'Vaisesikasutra, by Kanada; critical ed. with Candrananda's Vrtti, Jambuvijaya'; Matilal (Logic, Language and Reality) follows 'the numbering of Candrananda'. Candrānanda's commentary is the earliest surviving Vaiśeṣikasūtra commentary (c. 7th-9th c.).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Philosophy in Classical India_Jonardon Ganeri, 2001.txt; secondary:cleaned_Logic, language, and reality_Matilal, 1990.txt
texts.note: Title labels only the ṭīkā (Candrānanda) but the work conflates two distinct authors: the root Vaiśeṣikasūtra of Kaṇāda and the vṛtti of Candrānanda. Candrānanda is the genuine commentator; Kaṇāda is the legendary/founding sūtrakāra.

texts.title: Candrajñānāgama (Candrajñāna)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śaiva āgama; traditionally proclaimed by Śiva)
texts.basis: Muktabodha/IFP Śaiva Siddhānta e-text, no human author. Web (wisdomlib): Candrajñānāgama is a Siddhānta(upā)gama, one of the 28 Śaivāgamas, a pratiṣṭhātantra on installation/worship of liṅgas and Śaiva deities; classed as revealed scripture 'proclaimed by the Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva' — no human composer.
texts.note: Revealed (apauruṣeya in tradition) Śaiva Siddhānta āgama; no human author. Traditional ascription = revelation of Śiva. Note: in some lists 'Candrajñāna' is also cited as a Kāpālika/non-Saiddhāntika śāstra (Encyclopedia of Tantra), but the IFP/Muktabodha text is the Saiddhāntika pratiṣṭhā-āgama.

texts.title: Grahamātṛkānāmadhāraṇī
texts.basis: DSBC header: Āryaśrīgrahamātṛkā nāma dhāraṇī, no author named; framed as buddhavacana with Buddha Śākyamuni as speaker; colophon only 'samāptam', no compositional/scribal attribution. Modern e-text input by Miroj Shakya (Nagarjuna Institute). Buddhist astral/dhāraṇī text on the planetary deities (graha).
texts.note: Canonical dhāraṇī treated as the Buddha's word; genuinely anonymous, no human author. Traditional ascription = revelation by Śākyamuni.

texts.title: Yogapīṭhakramodaya
texts.basis: No source_url and no printed edition located. Local lit search found only the common word 'yogapiṭha' (sacred seat), not this title. Title (Yogapīṭha-krama-udaya, 'arising of the krama/sequence of the yoga-seat') marks it as a Śākta/Śaiva tantric ritual text on maṇḍala/krama installation; no human author attested.
texts.note: No attestation of a named author found in any of the three source streams; treated as anonymous tantric krama/maṇḍala manual. Confidence medium only because the work itself could not be directly located/verified.

texts.title: Śālistambakamahāyānasūtraṭīkā [Āryaśālistambakamahāyānasūtraṭīkā]
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'ācāryanāgārjunena viracitā śālistambakavistarākhyā ṭīkā' (Śālistamba commentary 'Vistara' composed by ācārya Nāgārjuna); but the Sanskrit is reconstructed from the Tibetan (Sonam Rabten ed., CIHTS 2004), original Sanskrit lost. Scholarship (Apple, Jewels of the Middle Way; Tola & Dragonetti) lists the Āryaśālistambakamahāyānasūtraṭīkā among works ascribed to Nāgārjuna whose authorship beyond the MMK is not established; Nakamura notes two Śālistamba-sūtra commentaries, one being by Kamalaśīla — i.e. multiple commentators in the tradition.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Jewels of the Middle Way_James B Apple, 2019.txt; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism_Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Colophon names Nāgārjuna, but the Nāgārjuna ascription is scholarly-disputed (pseudo-Nāgārjuna of the Madhyamaka stotra/commentary corpus); a different Śālistamba commentary is by Kamalaśīla. Surviving only via Tibetan; Sanskrit retranslated. Single ascribed author, so no conflation flag.

texts.title: Ahorātravratacaityasevānuśaṃsāvadāna
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Handurukande ed., input K. Wille) names no author; text frames the narrative as taught by the Buddha and then by the sage Vasubandhu to King Indrapṛṣṭa, but Vasubandhu is an internal narrative speaker, not the composer. Colophon gives only a copy date (Saṃvat 32), no author/scribe of composition. Genre is anonymous Nepalese Buddhist avadāna (caitya-worship cycle).
texts.note: Anonymous avadāna. 'Vasubandhu' inside the text is a frame-narrator (sage instructing the king), NOT the author; do not attribute to the Yogācāra master Vasubandhu.

texts.title: Ātreyatilaka
texts.basis: source_url is only the DSBC project landing page (no text/header retrievable); edition is null. No exact match for the title in reference-literature/ (only incidental 'ātreya' hits). Web searches return no scholarly attribution for a work by this title. No author recoverable from any of the three sources.
texts.note: Obscure title, no edition and no retrievable e-text content; author genuinely unknown. Recorded uncertain (no author invented).

texts.title: Kamalākarasarvatathāgatastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL '108 Buddhist stotras' (BuSto_36) lists it by title only, no author given. 'Kamalākara' ('lake/pond of lotuses') is a poetic-title element recurring in Buddhist hymnody (cf. 'kamalākara iva tasmin nivasati lakṣmīḥ' in Schneider, Buddhist Hymns, hymns to Jambhala/Lakṣmī), i.e. an epithet/image, not a verified personal author. Web search finds no personal author; 'Kamalākara' in this corpus denotes the laudation/imagery, not a composer.
texts.note: Reject the speculative reading of 'Kamalākara' as the author's name; it belongs to the stotra's title/imagery. Treat as anonymous Buddhist stotra. (Distinct from the 17th-c. dharmaśāstra writer Kamalākara Bhaṭṭa.)

texts.title: Veṇīsaṃhāra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Grill 1871; also Kale ed. with Jagaddhara's commentary) attributes the six-act drama to Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa. Web/scholarship: the prastāvanā itself names the play and the author with his title Kavimṛgarāja ('lion among poets'); standard, undisputed authorship (poet c. 7th-8th c. CE).
texts.note: Secure. Author self-named in the prologue. Jagaddhara is a separate later commentator (not part of this root-text record).

texts.title: Mahādaṇḍadhāraṇīśītavatī
texts.basis: GRETIL (Hidas 2017 ed.) and Hidas's study: presented as Buddha-spoken apotropaic scripture (Bhagavān teaching Rāhula at Rājagṛha); no human author. Colophons give only title variants (ārya-mahādaṇḍadhāraṇī-śītavatī etc.), no composer; Skilling classes it Śrāvakayāna. A single 'original' is hard to trace (fluid recension).
texts.note: Anonymous canonical dhāraṇī/sūtra; Buddhavacana frame, no historical author.

texts.title: Bījagaṇita
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Apate ed., ASS 99): Bhāskara, A.D. 1150. Secondary (Contributions to the History of Indian Mathematics): Bījagaṇita is Bhāskarācārya's algebra; Sūryadāsa (16th c.) wrote the commentary Sūryaprakāśa on it. Standard, undisputed attribution of the root work to Bhāskara II.
texts.note: Secure: root Bījagaṇita by Bhāskara II (c. 1150). Commentaries (e.g. Sūryadāsa's Sūryaprakāśa, Kṛṣṇa Daivajña's Bījapallava) are separate works, not part of this record.

texts.title: Kiraṇatantra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: file is a composite of the anonymous revealed Kiraṇatantra root verses interspersed with Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha's Vṛtti; colophon 'śrīnārāyaṇakaṇṭhātmajabhaṭṭarāmakaṇṭhaviracitāyāṃ śrīmatkiraṇavṛtau'. Secondary (Watson 2006; Goodall 1998) confirm Rāmakaṇṭha authored the Kiraṇavṛtti on the anonymous Śaiva Siddhānta scripture.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Self's Awareness of Itself Bhatta Ramakantha's Arguments Against the Buddhist Doctrine of No-Self_Alex Watson, 2006.txt
texts.note: Current record marks only the anonymous scripture; the e-text in fact conflates the anonymous tantra (root) with Rāmakaṇṭha's named vṛtti. Root = revealed/anonymous; commentary = Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (c. 950-1000 CE), son of Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha.

texts.title: Śiśupālavadha
texts.basis: GRETIL header names 'Māgha: Śiśupālavadha'; e-text is the root mahākāvya ONLY (no Mallinātha commentary present, despite the edition citation), input by Sansknet. Authorship of the Śiśupālavadha by the poet Māgha is uncontested in scholarship.
texts.note: Edition citation mentions Mallinātha's Sarvaṅkaṣā commentary, but the GRETIL e-text contains only Māgha's verses; no commentator conflation in the actual text. Single secure author.

texts.title: Tārānamaskāraikaviṃśatistotra [Āryatārānamaskāraikaviṃśatistotra]
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (presented as words of the Buddha; Vairocana/Kriyātantra)
texts.basis: DSBC colophon: 'iti ... vairocanabhāṣitaṃ ... buddhabhāṣitaṃ parisamāptam' — presented as spoken by the Buddha (Vairocana). This is the famous 'Praise in Twenty-One Homages to Tārā'; web (Lotsawa House, etc.) classes it under 'Words of the Buddha', no human author claimed; transmitted via Kriyātantra/Tārā cycle.
texts.note: No human author; traditional ascription = buddhabhāṣita / Vairocana per colophon. Not anonymous-unknown but traditional scriptural attribution; matches current record.

texts.title: Mīmāṃsāślokavārttika
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'mīmāṃsāślokavārttikaṃ sucaritamiśrapraṇītayā kāśikākhyayā ṭīkayā sametam' — i.e. the e-text carries Sucaritamiśra's Kāśikā commentary, NOT Pārthasārathi Miśra's Nyāyaratnākara as the edition field claims. Secondary bibliography (Literature about the Philosophical Systems) confirms the Ślokavārttika is by Kumārila and lists distinct commentaries by Pārthasārathi (Nyāyaratnākara), Sucarita (Kāśikā), and Umbeka.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Root vārttika = Kumārila Bhaṭṭa (secure). E-text conflates it with Sucaritamiśra's Kāśikā ṭīkā (edition field wrongly says Pārthasārathi's Nyāyaratnākara). Two distinct authors present in the file.

texts.title: Jātakamālā [Ajātaśatrvavadāna]
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'Gopadatta: Jatakamala'; all 16 listed stories carry the 'Go' (Gopadatta) prefix — uniformly Gopadatta's work, not a mix of authors. Compilation of separately-edited stories (Hahn 1981 Ajātaśatru, Handurukande, Klaus, etc.) but one author. Secondary (Tatelman; de Jong; Hahn 'Haribhatta and Gopadatta: Two Authors in the Succession of Āryaśūra') confirms Gopadatta as a distinct jātakamālā author following Āryaśūra.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Tatelman_The Legend of the Buddha's Wife in the Bhadrakalpāvadāna.txt
texts.note: File compiles multiple Gopadatta stories edited by DIFFERENT modern editors (Hahn, Handurukande, Klaus) but all by the single ancient author Gopadatta — multi-editor, not multi-author. No conflation of ancient authorship.

texts.title: Līlāvatī
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'Bhāskara: Lilavati', digitalized by T. Hayashi; e-text is Bhāskara II's root Līlāvatī (the commentaries of Gaṇeśa/Buddhivilāsinī, Mahīdhara/Līlāvatīvivaraṇa, Śaṅkara/Kriyākramākarī are source editions, not embedded in the e-text). Secondary (Hist. of Civ. of Central Asia) confirms Bhāskara fl. 1150 authored the Līlāvatī.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_History of Civilization of Central Asia. V. Sixteenth to the mid-nineteenth century.txt
texts.note: Commentators (Gaṇeśa, Mahīdhara, Śaṅkara) are sources of the edition only; root Līlāvatī e-text is single-author Bhāskara II. Secure attribution.

texts.title: Kāmadhenutantra
texts.basis: No source_url. Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra; Keul ed. 2012; 'Explaining Mantras', citing Kāmadhenu Tantra 6.23ff on letters of the alphabet) treats it as a standalone Śākta/Tantric scripture with no named human author; framed as Śiva-Pārvatī revelation dialogue, quoted in Śāktānandataraṅgiṇī. Web (wisdomlib) confirms anonymous revealed tantra on mystical significance of the alphabet.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_explaining mantras and the efficacy of ritual.txt; web:https://www.wisdomlib.org/definition/kamadhenutantra
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Tantra; no human composer. Traditionally buddha/Śiva-vacana style (uttered by Śiva). Printed Allahabad ed. lacks ch. 22 and most of 23.

texts.title: Niśvāsakārikā
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text header gives no author. Secondary lit (White, Sinister Yogis; Sanderson, The Śaiva Age; Goudriaan ed. 1992; Sferra) describes the Niśvāsakārikā as a portion of the Niśvāsa(tattva)saṃhitā corpus surviving only in South Indian (IFP Pondicherry) transcripts — one of the earliest Śaiva tantras, anonymous revealed scripture, no critical ed. of the Kārikā.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_The Śaiva Age.txt; secondary:cleaned_Sinister Yogis_David Gordon White, 2009.txt
texts.note: Anonymous early Śaiva tantra; part of Niśvāsatattvasaṃhitā corpus. No author named; revealed (Śiva-uttered) scripture.

texts.title: Amoghapāśakalparāja Part VI
texts.basis: DSBC e-text header names only digitizers (Miroj Shakya; Nagarjuna Institute) and no author/translator. Secondary lit (Nakamura: Chinese version by 宝思惟 Ratnacinta 639 CE; Lessing & Wayman: the tantra 'pronounced by the Buddha') treats it as anonymous Mahāyāna/Kriyātantra buddhavacana, not authored by a named individual.
texts.source: gretil-header (dsbc); secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāyāna/Kriyā Buddhist scripture, presented as buddhavacana. Chinese transl. by Ratnacinta (639 CE) attests early circulation but Sanskrit text itself is anonymous. Title labeled Part VI; edition is Kimura Takayasu et al. transcription.

texts.title: Harṣacarita
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'śrīmahākavibāṇamaṭṭakṛtau harṣacarite ... prathama ucchvāsaḥ' — securely Bāṇa(bhaṭṭa). Secondary lit (skt-en-mono colophon 'iti śrībāṇabhaṭṭakṛtau harṣacarite'; Sanderson; Buddhistic Studies 1931; Law 1931) consistently attributes the kāvya to Bāṇabhaṭṭa, 7th c.
texts.note: Secure single-author kāvya by Bāṇabhaṭṭa (7th c., court of Harṣa). Print edition pairs it with Śaṅkara's 'Saṅketa' commentary (a distinct, later author), but the GRETIL e-text is root text only — no conflation in this e-text.

texts.title: Mañjuśrīnāmāṣṭottaraśatakastotra [Āryamañjuśrīnāmāṣṭottaraśatakastotra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Bauddha Stotra Saṃgraha, Pāṇḍeya ed. 1994) names no human author; colophon 'śrīāryamañjuśrīnāmāṣṭottaraśatakastotraṃ samāptam' is a praise-hymn to Mañjuśrī with no composer named. Distinct from the Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti (a separate tantra). No author found in secondary lit.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist stotra (108 names of Mañjuśrī). Not to be confused with the Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti. Traditionally devotional, no named human author.

texts.title: Siddhilakṣmī Ārāmika
texts.basis: No source_url, no edition. Secondary lit ag search returned no relevant hits (ārāmika hits are Buddhist vinaya, unrelated). Web confirms Siddhilakṣmī is a Newar Sarvāmnāya/Kālīkrama goddess (form of Kālī from the Jayadrathayāmala); the text is a ritual/paddhati-type work of the Newar Sarvāmnāya milieu with no identifiable named author.
texts.note: Anonymous Newar Sarvāmnāya (Śākta) text in the Siddhilakṣmī/Kālīkrama tradition. No named author located; some Sarvāmnāya works are linked to figures like Mukundarāja or royal compilers (e.g. Jitāmitramalla, Pratāp Siṅgh Śāh) but none specifically attached to this title.

texts.title: Vibhāṣāprabhāvṛtti
texts.current_author: the anonymous 'Dīpakāra' (the Abhidharmadīpa's author; project hint of 'Vimalamitra' is unverified—proposals also include Vasumitra/Īśvaravarman)
texts.primary_author: anonymous 'Dīpakāra' (author of the Abhidharmadīpa kārikā and its auto-commentary the Vibhāṣāprabhāvṛtti)
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC header (after Jaini 1959) marks author as 'Vimalamitra(?)' with a question mark for both root and commentary. Cox 1995 (Disputed Dharmas) treats the Abhidharmadīpa together with its auto-commentary Vibhāṣāprabhāvṛtti as one Sarvāstivāda/Vaibhāṣika polemical work (verse + prose by the same hand). Web/scholarship: the author calls himself only 'Dīpakāra' (= 'author of the Dīpa', not a real name); Jaini conjectured he may be Saṅghabhadra or a pupil (possibly Vimalamitra), opposed to Vasubandhu. Identity unresolved.
texts.note: Kārikā (Abhidharmadīpa) and bhāṣya/vṛtti (Vibhāṣāprabhāvṛtti) are by the SAME anonymous author ('Dīpakāra')—an auto-commentary, NOT a Maitreya/Vasubandhu-type split. 'Vimalamitra' is only Jaini's tentative guess; not the Dzogchen Vimalamitra. Genuinely anonymous; traditional ascription = none secure.

texts.title: Agnikāryapaddhati
texts.basis: No GRETIL header (Muktabodha/HK transcription; muktalib7 catalog link). Secondary: Sanderson ('Hindu Tantric Chaplains'; 'Meaning in Tantric Ritual') and 'Essais sur le rituel' cite an Agnikāryapaddhati only as Kashmirian Śaiva fire-ritual manual manuscripts (BnF Paris MS Sanscrit), with no named author. 'Agnikāryapaddhati' is a generic paddhati (ritual-manual) title used for several anonymous Śaiva fire-offering manuals.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_Hindu tantric chaplains.txt; secondary:cleaned_Essais sur le rituel.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Śaiva fire-sacrifice (agnikārya) paddhati; generic title—no individual author identified in scholarship. Confirms current 'anonymous/traditional'.

texts.title: Aniruddhasaṃhitā
texts.basis: Archive.org edition (Sreenivasa Iyengar 1956) names only the editor, no author. Secondary: Bühnemann (Maṇḍalas and Yantras) lists it as 'Sree Anirudha Samhita, one of Divyasaṃhitā in Pāñcarātra'. Web/scholarship (Cracow Indological Studies; wisdomlib): it is one of the Divyasaṃhitās, framed as Bhagavān's teaching in his Aniruddha aspect, narrated by the sage Bhṛgu—i.e., revealed scripture, no human author.
texts.source: web:https://archive.org/details/aniruddhasamhita; secondary:cleaned_Mandalas and Yantras in the Hindu Traditions_Gudrun Buhnemann 2003.txt; web:https://www.wisdomlib.org/definition/aniruddhasamhita
texts.note: Revealed Pāñcarātra Saṃhitā (a Divyasaṃhitā); divine 'speaker' Aniruddha/Bhagavān via Bhṛgu—not a human author. Confirms current. The name 'Aniruddha' is a Vyūha form of Viṣṇu, not the 15th-c. Sāṃkhya commentator Aniruddha.

texts.title: Pratiṣṭhālakṣaṇasārasamuccaya
texts.basis: Secondary: skt-en-mono (Goodall, Niśvāsa-circle scholarship) notes its prefatory verses overlap with the Bṛhatkālottara/Jñānapañcāśikā—i.e. a compilation drawing on Śaiva āgamas. Web/scholarship (Bühnemann 2003, 'Hindu Pantheon in Nepalese Line Drawings'; wisdomlib): the Pratiṣṭhālakṣaṇasārasamuccaya (= Lakṣaṇasārasamuccaya / Lakṣaṇasamuccaya / Lakṣaṇasaṅgraha) is an 11th–12th-c. Śaiva manual on liṅga construction/installation, compiled from various Āgamas (based on the Devyāmata), attributed to Vairocana.
texts.note: MEANINGFUL CORRECTION: current_author calls it 'anonymous' and a 'Buddhist iconography text'. It is actually a ŚAIVA pratiṣṭhā/liṅga-installation manual, 11th–12th c., traditionally attributed to VAIROCANA, compiled from Śaiva Āgamas (Devyāmata). Not Buddhist; not anonymous. Attribution is traditional and a compilation, hence Vairocana = compiler. (Distinct from the 'Lakṣaṇasamuccaya' that some dictionaries link to Hemādri.)

texts.title: Kūrma-Purāṇa 2
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa; framed as revealed by Viṣṇu as Kūrma)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet input; Gupta/Kashiraj Trust 1971 critical ed.): no human authorial colophon; framed as revealed scripture—teachings of Īśvara (Īśvaragītā), narrated by Vyāsa, with Manu on dharma. Like all Mahāpurāṇas it is anonymous, multi-layered, and traditionally compiled by the legendary Vyāsa; part 2 (uttarabhāga) contains the Īśvaragītā.
texts.note: Anonymous Purāṇa with the standard legendary ascription to Vyāsa; divine speaker is Viṣṇu/Śiva (Kūrma avatāra). Composite/multi-layered but no NAMED distinct human authors to separate, so not flagged as conflation. Confirms current.

texts.title: Sāṃkhyaparibhāṣā
texts.basis: GRETIL Sāṃkhya directory (dir listing returned 403); edition is the Sāṃkhyasaṃgraha (ed. V. Dvivedī, Chowkhamba/Vizianagram 1918), an anthology of minor Sāṃkhya tracts. Web/scholarship: searches (sanskritdocuments.org gives only 'from the Sāṃkhyasaṃgraha 1918') return no securely identified author for the Sāṃkhyaparibhāṣā; it is a short late Sāṃkhya 'definitions' compendium of obscure/unattributed authorship—not to be confused with the Vedānta-paribhāṣā tradition.
texts.note: Minor late Sāṃkhya compendium; no author securely identified in available scholarship. Kept anonymous/uncertain (status 'uncertain' since a named author may exist but is unverified). No traditional ascription located.

texts.title: Navaślokī
texts.basis: GRETIL/edition (Lindtner, Adversaria Buddhica) ascribes the Navaślokī to Kambala(pāda). Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980): 'Kambalapāda or Kambālāmbara wrote a small metrical treatise in nine verses on the Prajñāpāramitā', i.e. the Navaślokī on the Aṣṭasāhasrikā. Single author, no commentary conflated in this item.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Nine-verse (nava-ślokī) versified summary of Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā; author also known as Kambālāmbara/Siddhakambala, contemporary of Vinītadeva (8th c.).

texts.title: Bhagavadāśayānusaraṇabhāṣya
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Bhāskara: Bhagavadāśayānusaraṇabhāṣya (Commentary on the Bhagavad Gita)', ed. Subhadropadhyaya 1965; input Takahiro Kato. Secondary lit (Freschi & Maas, Adaptive Reuse) repeatedly treats Bhāskara as the bhedābhedavāda Vedānta commentator distinct from Śaṅkara. Bhagavadgītā is the root text (not authored by Bhāskara); this item is only Bhāskara's bhāṣya.
texts.note: Bhāskara's Gītā commentary; he is the c. 9th-c. Bhedābheda Vedāntin. Root Gītā verses are anonymous/traditional and not part of this attribution.

texts.title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Bhogakārikā
texts.basis: Edition title and secondary lit (Goudriaan ed., Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism, 1992: 'Bhogakārikā, by Sadyojyoti, commentary by A[ghoraśiva]') establish two distinct authors: the root Bhogakārikā verses by the early Śaivasiddhānta exegete Sadyojyoti(s) (c. 7th-8th c.), and the commentary by Aghoraśivācārya (12th c.). The edition prints both together in the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism. Studies in Honor of André Padoux_Teun Goudriaan, ed, 1992.txt
texts.note: Root kārikā = Sadyojyoti; commentary = Aghoraśiva, separated by ~4-5 centuries. Item conflates verse-author and commentator.

texts.title: Hāhārāvatantra
texts.basis: No author in any consulted source. Web (Śākta/Śaiva scholarship) identifies the Hāhārāva as a root tantra of the Uttarāmnāya Guhyakālī tradition — a revealed Śākta scripture with no named human author. No edition/source_url content available; not found in local reference-literature.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Tantra (Guhyakālī/Uttarāmnāya, Śākta-Śaiva); traditionally framed as divine revelation, no human author. No traditional personal ascription found.

texts.title: Jñānārṇavatantra
texts.basis: Edition: Apte (ed.), Ānandāśrama Sanskrit Series 69, 1912 — a Śrīvidyā/Śākta tantra cited in dharma digests (Encyclopedia of Tantra; skt-en-mono quotes its self-etymology). A revealed scripture with no named author. NOTE: distinct from the Jaina Jñānārṇava of Śubhacandra (a yoga treatise) — that is a different work and should not be conflated.
texts.note: Anonymous Śākta/Śrīvidyā Tantra (Apte 1912 ed.). Beware homonym: Jaina Jñānārṇava is by Śubhacandra — a separate, unrelated text.

texts.title: Mahāsattvamaṇicūḍamahārājabodhisattvāvadāna
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (later versified by Kṣemendra in the Avadānakalpalatā tradition)
texts.basis: Handurukande (ed./tr.), Maṇicūḍāvadāna, SBB 24, 1967 — an anonymous Mahāyāna prose avadāna whose style Handurukande likens to Divyāvadāna stories (Tatelman, Pūrṇāvadāna study). The Maṇicūḍa legend was separately versified by Kṣemendra in the Avadānakalpalatā (11th c.) and dramatized; those are distinct redactions, not the author of this prose avadāna.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Translation and Study of the Purnavadana_Joel Howard Tatelman, 1988.txt
texts.note: Anonymous prose avadāna; Kṣemendra is a later versifier/redactor of the same story (Avadānakalpalatā), not author of this text. Roles separated to avoid conflation.

texts.title: Prajñāpāramitāhṛdayasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya ed., DSBC input) gives no author; presented as a canonical sūtra with no individual attribution. This is the longer vistara recension. Buddhist canonical convention attributes such teaching to the Buddha, not a historical author.
texts.note: Heart Sutra (vistaramātṛkā/longer recension). Canonically framed as Buddha-vacana; no human author/translator named in this Sanskrit witness. Traditional ascription to the Buddha only.

texts.title: mahāguhyakālīvidhānam
texts.basis: No edition (edition=null), source is a manuscript digital-library catalog (Muktabodha/muktalib). A vidhāna (ritual manual) for the goddess Mahāguhyakālī; tantric revelatory genre. No author identified in secondary literature or web search.
texts.note: Anonymous Śākta/tantric vidhāna; revelatory genre framed as Śiva/goddess dialogue. No named author or editor located.

texts.title: Kūrma-Purāṇa 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet input; Gupta crit. ed.) gives no historical author. Text uses standard Purāṇic nested-narration frame: Sūta narrates to sages at Naimiṣa; ultimately ascribed to Vyāsa as compiler and to Viṣṇu (Kūrma avatar) as revealer. Composite, multi-layered Purāṇa.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Vyāsa as compiler (legendary); actual composition anonymous and accretional. Sūta is the internal narrator. Not a single historical author.

texts.title: Uḍḍāmareśvaratantra
texts.basis: Zadoo ed. (KSTS 70, 1947). Web (scholarly overviews): a 15-chapter compilation of magical rites (ṣaṭkarman), largely compilatory, no original metaphysics; Uḍḍāmareśvara is a Bhairava form to whom the tantra is named/ascribed. Revelatory Śaiva/tantric genre, no human author.
texts.note: Anonymous tantra of magical rites; nominal ascription to Śiva as Uḍḍāmareśvara/Bhairava (revelatory frame). Compilatory, no single composer.

texts.title: Tattvasamāsasūtravṛtti [Kramadīpikā]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Kramadīpikā vṛtti); root Tattvasamāsasūtra also anonymous (ascribed to Kapila)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Dhaval Patel data entry; Dvivedin ed. in Sāṃkhyasaṅgraha): commentary names no author; final verse only self-titles it Kramadīpikā ('sāṃkhyasūtrakrameṇaiṣā vyākhyātā kramadīpikā'). Secondary lit (Phil. Systems bibliography) lists the Tattvasamāsa with multiple separate anonymous/named commentaries (Kramadīpikā among them); root sūtra-text traditionally ascribed to Kapila but anonymous.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Two distinct anonymous layers conflated under one title: the root Tattvasamāsasūtra (≤25 sūtras, trad. ascribed to Kapila) and the Kramadīpikā vṛtti commenting on it — different (both anonymous) authors. Many other commentaries on the same root exist.

texts.title: Triṃśikāṭīkā
texts.basis: Edition note + web (BSOAS Sanskrit fragments ed.; Encyclopedia of Buddhism; CiNii Japanese editions): Vinītadeva (c. 690-750, Nālandā) is the secure author of the Triṃśikāṭīkā, a sub-commentary in the Triṃśikā/vijñaptimātratā tradition (on Vasubandhu's Triṃśikā, engaging Sthiramati's bhāṣya). Survives complete in Tibetan; Sanskrit only in fragments (Jaini/BSOAS).
texts.note: Secure single-author ṭīkā by Vinītadeva (sub-commentary). The underlying Triṃśikā kārikās are Vasubandhu's and Sthiramati wrote the bhāṣya, but THIS work (the ṭīkā) is solely Vinītadeva's — not itself conflated.

texts.title: Vīrāgama
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śaiva Āgama; traditionally revealed by Sadāśiva/Aghora face)
texts.basis: Vīrāgama is one of the 28 Siddhāntāgamas of the Śaivasiddhānta canon; per pratisaṃhitā/sṛṣṭikrama theory it is revealed scripture transmitted from Sadāśiva (Aghora face) down to ṛṣis and humans — no human author. Muktabodha catalogues it as an Āgama. No secondary source assigns a personal author.
texts.note: Revealed Śaiva Siddhāntāgama; traditional ascription to divine revelation (Sadāśiva), not a human; current attribution accurate.

texts.title: ugracaṇḍāpūjāpaddhati
texts.primary_author: anonymous (ritual paddhati for the worship of Ugracaṇḍā)
texts.basis: A paddhati (ritual manual) for the worship of the goddess Ugracaṇḍā — an 18-armed Mahiṣāsuramardinī Durgā form with eight ancillary Caṇḍās (Sanderson, The Śaiva Age, ll.2792-2800, cf. Ugracaṇḍāprakaraṇa). Paddhatis of this kind are anonymous practitioner compilations; no edition or scholarly source names an author.
texts.note: Anonymous Śākta-Śaiva ritual paddhati; no human author attributed; current attribution accurate.

texts.title: Brahmasūtra (Śaṅkarabhāṣya)
texts.current_author: Śaṅkara (Ādi Śaṅkarācārya); root sūtras attributed to Bādarāyaṇa
texts.basis: Classic multi-layer Vedānta text. Root sūtras = Bādarāyaṇa (Brahmasūtra/Vedāntasūtra, traditionally identified with Vyāsa); bhāṣya = Ādi Śaṅkara; the named edition (Anantakrishna Sastri 1938) layers further sub-commentaries: Bhāmatī of Vācaspati Miśra, Kalpataru of Amalānanda, Parimala of Appayya Dīkṣita. GRETIL URL (brsbh_pu.htm) returned 404; edition confirmed via Internet Archive (Ananta Krishna Shastri ed., authors listed Bādarāyaṇa, Śaṅkarācārya, Vācaspati Miśra, Amalānanda, Appaya Dīkṣita).
