texts.note: Conflates distinct authors by role: sūtra (Bādarāyaṇa) vs bhāṣya (Śaṅkara) vs three sub-commentaries (Vācaspati Miśra, Amalānanda, Appayya Dīkṣita). Current author named only sūtra+bhāṣya; the sub-commentators in the edition title are additional distinct authors.

texts.title: ūrdhvāmnāyapūjāvidhi
texts.basis: A pūjāvidhi (worship procedure) of the ūrdhvāmnāya (the 'upward/highest' Kaula transmission; cf. Kulārṇava Tantra traditionally the 5th section of the Ūrdhvāmnāya Tantra — Tantra in Practice, White ed.). Vidhi/paddhati ritual texts of this stream are anonymous compilations within revealed Kaula tradition; no author named. No source_url available.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Tantra in Practice_David Gordon White, ed, 2000_a.txt; tradition
texts.note: Anonymous Kaula ritual manual tied to the ūrdhvāmnāya transmission; no human author attributed; current attribution accurate.

texts.title: Śākyasiṃhastotra: navagrahakṛtam
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (framed as spoken by the Nine Planets)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (pseudo-divine framing: composed by the navagraha / Nine Planets)
texts.basis: Buddhist stotra (BuSto_82 in GRETIL's 108 Buddhist stotras, ed. Janardan Shastri Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha, MLBD 1994). Title 'navagrahakṛtam' frames the hymn as composed/spoken by the Nine Planets — a devotional pseudepigraphic device; no historical human author named in header or colophon.
texts.note: Devotional pseudepigraphy: ascribed to the navagraha (Nine Planets), a divine/celestial fiction rather than a real author; effectively anonymous. Current framing accurate.

texts.title: Śikṣāsamuccayakārikā
texts.basis: The Śikṣāsamuccaya = 27 kārikās plus a prose anthology (~95% sūtra quotations) with Śāntideva's own connecting commentary, all by Śāntideva (Clayton, Moral Theory in Śāntideva's Śikṣāsamuccaya, ll.255, 326, 1694-1745, 4993: 'twenty-seven kārikās (verses) by Śāntideva'; 'his twenty-seven verses'). The 'kārikā' item = the root verses, also Śāntideva. DSBC page (Vaidya 1960 ed.) labels it Śikṣāsamuccaya kārikā, no rival author. Note: Chinese canon misattributes translation to Dharmayaśas/Dharmakīrti as translator, not original author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_MORAL THEORY IN SANTIDEVA’S SIKSASAMUCCAYA_Barbra R. Clayton.txt; web:http://www.dsbcproject.org/canon-text/book/268
texts.note: Single author: both the 27 kārikās and the prose compilation/commentary are Śāntideva's; the anthologized sūtra quotations are quoted scripture, not co-authorship, so no conflation. Current attribution correct.

texts.title: Viśvasāratantra
texts.basis: Muktabodha HK e-text (ed. Jivananda Vidyasagara) has no named human author. Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra; Chinnamasta_Benard) treats Viśvasāratantra as a revealed Śākta/Tantric scripture cited for ritual prescriptions (initiation types, Chinnamastā sahasranāma) with no individual ascription; the Gūḍhāvatāra section is also linked to a 'Viśvasāroddhāra'.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt; secondary:cleaned_Chinnamasta The Aweful Buddhist & Hindu Tantric Goddess_Elisabeth Anne Benard 2010.txt
texts.note: Anonymous revealed tantra (śāstra spoken by Śiva/Devī); no human author. Traditional ascription = divine revelation only.

texts.title: Adhyardhaśatikā Prajñāpāramitā (Adhyardhaśatikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Tomabechi 2009 ed., input K. Wille) names only editor/inputter, no author; text closes 'ity adhyardhaśatikā bhagavatī prajñāpāramitā samāptā' with no human author. Iranica/scholarship: a tantric-style Prajñāpāramitā sūtra (the Rishukyō / Prajñāpāramitā-naya-śatapañcāśatikā), spoken by various Buddhas, by an unknown 7th-c. author; commentaries (e.g. Jñānamitra in Tibetan) are separate works not in this e-text (Nakamura).
texts.note: Canonical Mahāyāna/tantric sūtra, buddhavacana. E-text is root sūtra only; no commentary conflated.

texts.title: Adhyardhaśatikā Prajñāpāramitā (Adhyardhaśatikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya, Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha 1961; DSBC/Nagarjuna Institute input) names only editor, no author. Same canonical Prajñāpāramitā sūtra as adhsatpu, a different edition. Spoken by various Buddhas; no individual author.
texts.note: Duplicate root sūtra (Vaidya edition). Canonical, buddhavacana; no commentary conflated.

texts.title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti (per edition; title field blank in workitem)
texts.basis: Edition = Raghu Vira & Lokesh Chandra, Sanskrit Texts from the Imperial Palace at Peking (Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti / Kālacakra texts), 1966 = the ROOT Nāmasaṃgīti, not a commentary. Tradition: taught by Śākyamuni to Vajrapāṇi, said to be extracted from the samādhipaṭala of the Māyājālatantra (anonymous/buddhavacana); 7th-8th c. The well-known Vilāsavajra is the COMMENTATOR (Nāmamantrārthāvalokinī, Tribe 2016) on this text, NOT its author — a web summary conflating him as 'author' of the root text is incorrect.
texts.note: Root text only in this edition: anonymous/traditional (buddhavacana via Māyājāla). Distinct from Vilāsavajra's commentary (separate work, not edited here), so no in-text conflation. Title supplied from edition.

texts.title: Acintyaviśvasādākhyam (Acintyaviśvasādākhya)
texts.basis: No GRETIL/edition metadata (source_url null). Secondary lit (Padoux festschrift, Goudriaan 1992) classes the Acintyaviśvasādākhya among Śaiva texts that 'claim to be Upāgamas' but are most certainly based on ancient paddhatis, and notes it (like the present Pauṣkara) may have been 'born this way' i.e. compiled/redacted from older material. Revealed scripture, no named human author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism. Studies in Honor of André Padoux_Teun Goudriaan, ed, 1992.txt
texts.note: Śaiva Āgama/Upāgama, revealed; anonymous. Scholarship suspects later compilation/redaction from paddhatis, but no individual author is identifiable.

texts.title: Advayaśatikā Prajñāpāramitāsūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (M.B. Shakya ed., Buddhist Himalaya 1988; DSBC input): titled Ārya-advayaśatikā-prajñāpāramitā-sūtra, names only editor; 'no author listed... traditionally attributed to the Buddha.' Listed as a Prajñāpāramitā title (Advayaśatikāprajñāpāramitā) in Dan Martin's bibliography with no author. Short Mahāyāna sūtra, buddhavacana.
texts.note: Canonical Mahāyāna Prajñāpāramitā sūtra, anonymous/buddhavacana; no commentary conflated in e-text.

texts.title: Bhaja-Govindam
texts.primary_author: Śaṅkara (Dvādaśamañjarikā + 5 closing verses, traditional); 14 disciples (Caturdaśamañjarikā, one verse each)

authors.name: fourteen disciples of Śaṅkara (named only by tradition)

texts.basis: Web (Wikipedia/Grokipedia): tradition holds Śaṅkara composed the opening 'Dvādaśamañjarikā' (Moha-mudgara) and 5 final verses, while each of his 14 disciples contributed one verse to the 'Caturdaśamañjarikā'; no conclusive evidence for any of the attributions, and the mixed bhakti/dvaita/advaita content reflects multiple hands. Bendall catalogue lists 'Moha-mudgara'/'Dvadasa Manjari' under Śaṅkarācārya and notes 'Bhajagovinda ascribed to Saṅkara'.
texts.note: Single title conflates distinct authors: Śaṅkara (core 12 + 5) and 14 disciples (1 verse each = Caturdaśamañjarikā). Whole-work ascription to Śaṅkara alone is traditional/disputed; disciple verses are traditional-only.

texts.title: Kalaśārcanavidhi of Siddhalakṣmīdevī
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (Newar Sarvāmnāya milieu; some such manuals copied/composed under Malla royal patronage)
texts.basis: No source names an author. Secondary lit (Tantra in Practice, White ed.; YOSHIZAKI Newar Buddhism) confirms Siddhilakṣmī/Siddhalakṣmī as a high deity of the Northern (Paścimāmnāya/Kaula) transmission and Kalaśārcana(-pūjā) as a standard Newar ritual rite; the 'Kalaśārcanavidhi' is a deity-specific ritual manual (vidhi), a genre transmitted anonymously in the Newar Sarvāmnāya milieu. No personal author or colophon attribution located.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Tantra in Practice_David Gordon White, ed, 2000_a.txt | secondary:cleaned_YOSHIZAKI_Newar Buddhism.txt | tradition
texts.note: 'of Siddhalakṣmīdevī' designates the deity worshipped, not a human author. Anonymous ritual manual; current 'anonymous/traditional' assessment confirmed.

texts.title: Tridaśaḍāmarāpratyaṅgirāviṣayakanānātantra
texts.basis: Title itself ('nānātantra' = 'various tantras on the subject of Tridaśaḍāmarā-Pratyaṅgirā') marks this as a compilation/excerpt-bundle from multiple tantras, not a single authored work. Pratyaṅgirā is a well-attested fierce protective goddess/dhāraṇī tradition (Nakamura, Indian Buddhism: Mahā-pratyaṅgirā-dhāraṇī popular 5th-18th c.). Muktabodha digital library is the source; no author/colophon named.
texts.note: Title's 'nānātantra' indicates a multi-source compilation of Pratyaṅgirā material; anonymous. Not attributable to one person.

texts.title: Saptabuddhastotra
texts.basis: DSBC header (WebFetch): no author named; colophon only 'śrīsaptabuddhastotraṃ samāptam'; ed. J.S. Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha 1994. Nariman (Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism, 1919) lists Saptabuddhastotra among hymns surviving only in manuscripts, with no named author. A liturgical hymn to the seven Buddhas (saptatathāgata), transmitted anonymously.
texts.source: gretil-header(dsbc):https://www.dsbcproject.org/canon-text/content/113/882 | secondary:cleaned_Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism_Nariman G K_1919.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist stotra; colophon names no author, only the title's completion. Confirmed anonymous.

texts.title: Tattvamīmāṃsā
texts.current_author: uncertain (a Sāṃkhya Tattvamīmāṃsā; not the modern work of Ganapati Muni)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (root Tattvasamāsa-sūtra; traditionally ascribed to Kapila/Pañcaśikha)
texts.basis: The given GRETIL source_url (tttvsamu.htm) is in fact the *Tattvasamāsa* (a Sāṃkhya digest of c.23-25 sūtras), not a distinct 'Tattvamīmāṃsā'. GRETIL header (WebFetch): no author named for the root text; the sūtras were extracted from Sīmānanda-dīkṣita's Sāṃkhyatattvavivecana and four commentarial divisions are given (Bhāvāgaṇeśa, Sāṃkhyasūtravivaraṇa, etc.). The Tattvasamāsa root is anonymous; tradition ascribes the Sāṃkhya sūtra-corpus to Kapila (and the Tattvasamāsa specifically is sometimes linked to Kapila/Pañcaśikha). Ed. confirmed: Dvivedin, Sāṃkhyasaṅgraha, ChSS 50,1.
texts.source: gretil-header:https://gretil.sub.uni-goettingen.de/gretil/1_sanskr/6_sastra/3_phil/samkhya/tttvsamu.htm | secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Title/URL mismatch: the file is the Tattvasamāsa (anonymous Sāṃkhya root sūtras), traditionally ascribed to Kapila/Pañcaśikha. Distinct commentators (Bhāvāgaṇeśa, Sīmānanda, Nārendra) are NOT the root author. Resolves the 'uncertain' to: anonymous root text.

texts.title: Vikramāraccivaibhava
texts.primary_author: Śānti Bhikṣu Śāstrī (Paṇḍita Śāntibhikṣu Śāstrī, Sāhityācārya)
texts.basis: DSBC header + colophon (WebFetch): composed by 'paṇḍita-śrīśāntibhikṣuśāstriṇaḥ sāhityācāryasya', a disciple of the venerable Dharmānanda, North-India-based, doctorate-holder; romanized 2014 by Milan Shakya. Śānti Bhikṣu Śāstrī is a documented 20th-c. scholar-monk (Visva-Bharati, Santiniketan; restored Buddhist works from Chinese, e.g. Jñānaprasthāna, Abhidharmāmṛta, Bodhicittotpāda; authored Buddha-Vijaya-Kāvya) per Nakamura's bibliography and Biblia Impex. 'Vikramāracci' = Sinhalese surname (Wickramaratchi); this is a modern original Sanskrit panegyric/kāvya he composed.
texts.note: Author was empty; colophon names Śānti Bhikṣu Śāstrī (Sāhityācārya, pupil of Dharmānanda Kosambi). Modern (20th-c.) original Sanskrit composition. Secure single authorship.

texts.title: Yogajāgama
texts.basis: IFP/Muktabodha catalogue (digitalcollections.ifpindia.org item 374552): 'this is in fact a compilation of statements on a range of ritual topics from various scriptures' (Yogaja, Suprabheda, Ajita, Mataṅga āgamas plus paddhatis such as Somaśambhupaddhati, Siddhāntasārāvalī); no author/compiler named, listed as anonymous. Web search confirms it is a Śaivasiddhānta anthology (Āgamavacanasaṅgraha).
texts.note: Anonymous anthology/compilation of Śaiva āgama passages; not a single-author or singly-revealed scripture. Despite āgama-style title, it is a derivative saṅgraha.

texts.title: Buddhagadyaya [Buddhagajjaya]
texts.basis: GRETIL Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (ed. J.S. Pandey 1994): collection of Buddhist stotras; this item carries no named author. Secondary: Bauddhavidyasudhakarah (Bechert Fs.) describes the Buddhagadya as 'a Sanskrit stotra from 17-18th [c.]' edited by a Benedictine PTS scholar; Nakamura, Indian Buddhism lists Buddhagadya among late stotras with no named author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Bauddhavidyasudhakarah. In Honour of Heinz Bechert_ Michael Han, Jens-Uwe Hartmann, Konrad Klaus, 1997_ (A).txt
texts.note: Late (17th-18th c.) anonymous Sanskrit Buddha-stotra; no colophon author found in collection or secondary literature.

texts.title: Kulasāra
texts.basis: Sanderson, The Śaiva Age, cites 'Kulasāra f. 72r2' and f.72r as a Kaula/Śaiva revealed scripture (Śiva dialogue, 'priye' addressee), quoted by folio from manuscript; no human author named. Treated as an anonymous early Śaiva (kaula/tantric) scripture; Muktabodha digital library holds the MS.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Kaula/Śaiva tantra (Śiva-Devī dialogue form); cited by Sanderson by folio. No traditional human ascription.

texts.title: Āyurvedamahodadhau Annapānavidhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header (samapviu.htm) and repeated colophon: 'ity āyurvedamahodadhau suṣeṇakṛte' ('thus in the Āyurvedamahodadhi composed by Suṣeṇa'); 'suṣeṇakṛte' throughout. Edition by S. Venkatasubrahmanya Sastri (Madras 1950). Web/Aufrecht confirm Suṣeṇa as the medical author; Annapānavidhi is the food-and-drink section of his Āyurvedamahodadhi.
texts.note: Single-author medical text; colophon names Suṣeṇa as composer. Annapānavidhi = a chapter/section of the larger Āyurvedamahodadhi.

texts.title: Vajrasatvapārājikā [Vajrasattvapārājikā]
texts.primary_author: Anupamavajra (as author of the Ādikarmapradīpa, of which this is a section)
texts.basis: La Vallée Poussin, Bouddhisme: études et matériaux (the cited edition): the colophon reads 'ādikarmapradīpaḥ samāptaḥ | kṛtir ācārya-anupamavajrasya' — 'the Ādikarmapradīpa is finished; the work of ācārya Anupamavajra'; LVP: 'Le colophon nous apprend que notre livre a pour auteur l'ācārya Anupamavajra'. The Vajrasattvapārājikā is a constituent vidhi/section within Anupamavajra's Ādikarmapradīpa.
texts.note: Not truly anonymous: the containing work, the Ādikarmapradīpa, is colophon-attributed to ācārya Anupamavajra (Nepalese, MS copied at Śrīyaśodharavarmamahāvihāra). The Vajrasattvapārājikā is one of its sections.

texts.title: Vajramahākālastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (ed. J.S. Pandey 1994), item BuSto_95 in the 108-stotra list; only the title appears, with no named author. No author attribution found in secondary literature or web; the stotra to Vajramahākāla circulates anonymously within the tantric stotra corpus.
texts.note: Anonymous tantric praise-hymn to Vajramahākāla within Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha; no colophon author identified.

texts.title: Mugdhopadeśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Jalhaṇa (c. 1st half 12th c., Kashmir); final verse JMu_65 is a self-naming colophon 'so 'yaṃ saṃprati jalhaṇena kavinā mugdhopadeśaḥ kṛtaḥ'. Bendall catalogue lists 'Jalhaṇa. Mugdhopadeśa'.
texts.note: Single-author satirical kāvya; colophon explicitly self-names the poet Jalhaṇa.

texts.title: Viśālāmalavatī Pramāṇasamuccayaṭīkā
texts.basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism: 'Jinendrabuddhi (early 8th A.D.) wrote a commentary on it (Viśālāmalavatī nāma Pramāṇasamuccayaṭīkā)'. Steinkellner/Krasser/Lasic critical edition attributes the ṭīkā to Jinendrabuddhi. The work is a subcommentary on Dignāga's Pramāṇasamuccaya (kārikā + svavṛtti); the ṭīkā itself is by one author.
texts.note: ṭīkā on Dignāga's root text (kārikā+svavṛtti by Dignāga); the commentary is solely Jinendrabuddhi's, so the title is not multi-authored, only a layered commentary tradition.

texts.title: Māravijayastotra
texts.basis: Lokesh Chandra edited/published it in Cultural Horizons of India vol. 2 as editor, not author; no named composer in edition or secondary sources. DSBC content ID returned an unrelated text (Sekoddeśa), confirming no reliable author metadata. No traditional personal ascription found.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist stotra; Lokesh Chandra is modern editor only. 'traditional' qualifier dropped since no traditional ascription is attested.

texts.title: Lokeśvarapārājikā
texts.basis: Newar Buddhist text recorded in J.K. Locke, Karunamaya (1980); title/framing ascribes the teaching to Lokeśvara (Avalokiteśvara), a deity, not a historical author. DSBC content ID returned an unrelated text (Caṇḍamahāroṣaṇatantra). No historical author identifiable; divine ascription only.
texts.note: Divine/legendary ascription to Lokeśvara; no human author. Pseudepigraphic rather than plain anonymous because the title itself claims the deity as speaker/author.

texts.title: Śākyasiṃhastotra: surapatikṛtam
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (framed as spoken by Indra, lord of the gods)
texts.basis: GRETIL (Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha 1994, no. BuSto_84) gives subtitle 'surapatikṛtam' = made by the lord of gods (Indra); no human author named in header or colophon. Nakamura references Sri-Sakyasimhastotra as a Buddhastotra (p.120) without a human author. Divine ascription only.
texts.note: Subtitle surapatikṛtam ascribes composition to Indra (a god); pseudepigraphic divine attribution, no historical author.

texts.title: Ahorātravratakathā 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. R. Handurukande, Three Sanskrit Texts on Caitya Worship, 2000; input Klaus Wille): no traditional colophon names an author/compiler. Narrative frames Buddha and Vasubandhu as internal characters, not as authors. Handurukande edits/synopsizes as a scholar only.
texts.note: Anonymous vrata-kathā; Buddha/Vasubandhu appear as in-text characters, not historical authors. Handurukande is modern editor.

texts.title: Catuḥstavasamāsārtha [Catuḥstavasamārtha]
texts.basis: GRETIL header + colophon 'catuḥstavasamāsārthaḥ paṇḍitāmṛtākarasyeti' attribute the work to paṇḍita Amṛtākara. Tucci, Minor Buddhist Texts I (1956), describes it as Amṛtākara's summary-commentary (samāsārtha) on the Catuḥstava, the four hymns traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna; the underlying hymns are Nāgārjuna's, the digest/commentary is Amṛtākara's.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Minor Buddhist Texts. Part I _Giuseppe Tucci, 1956.txt
texts.note: Composite: Amṛtākara is author of the samāsārtha (digest of the four hymns); the base Catuḥstava is traditionally Nāgārjuna's. Colophon names paṇḍita Amṛtākara.

texts.title: Durgārcanasmṛti
texts.basis: Muktabodha digital library record names Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa Goswāmī. No edition given and no source_url to a specific text page; web and local literature searches returned nothing on this title's authorship. As a Durgā-worship smṛti/paddhati it is a ritual compilation, so the named figure is best treated as compiler rather than original author.
texts.note: Unverified beyond the catalog ascription; role likely compiler of a Durgā-pūjā smṛti. Low confidence.

texts.title: Bodhyāpattideśanāvṛtti
texts.basis: DSBC source. Lindtner, Nagarjuniana (1987), p. ~refs: the Bodhyāpattideśanāvṛtti (Peking 5506) is 'ascribed to Nāgārjuna' but 'the authorship of this very interesting commentary remains questionable'; he places it in the 'circle' of early Madhyamaka, related to Mātṛceṭa's Sugatapañcatriṃśatstotra. Web (Wikipedia/IEP) corroborates general unreliability of many Nāgārjuna ascriptions.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Nagarjuniana. Studies in the Writings and Philosophy of Nagarjuna_Chr Lindtner, 1987.txt
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Nāgārjuna; explicitly doubted by Lindtner. It is a vṛtti on the Triskandhaka/confession material. Disputed, not secure.

texts.title: Rakṣākāla(kara)stava
texts.basis: GRETIL Buddhist-stotras collection lists it as BuSto_67 Rakṣākāla(kara)stava but the displayed material gives no author or colophon. Pandey (ed.) Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (1994) prints it without a secure author. No secondary or web source supplies an author.
texts.note: No author named in header or colophon; remains anonymous (a protective stotra). Current 'anonymous/traditional' retained.

texts.title: Pratyakṣalakṣaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header: this Pratyakṣalakṣaṇa is the perception section of the Tarkarahasya (colophon 'iti tarkarahasye ... dvitīyaḥ'), ed. Yaita 1988. The Tarkarahasya is attributed to Vidyākaraśānti (cf. Bühnemann, 'Tarkarahasya and Vādarahasya'; Tucci's Minor Buddhist Texts edits Vidyākaraśānti's Tarkasopāna in the same volume). Web results confirm Tarkarahasya is the Buddhist-logic work whose pratyakṣa chapter this is.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Sanskrit Texts from Giuseppe Tucci’s Collection. Part I_Francesco Sferra, ed, 2008.txt; web:academia.edu/11836650
texts.note: Standalone extract = chapter 2 (Pratyakṣalakṣaṇa) of the Tarkarahasya; author Vidyākaraśānti by tradition. Current author field was blank.

texts.title: Śatagāthā
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'ācāryavararucikṛtā śatagāthā samāptā' (verses by Vararuci); 'bhāratīyopādhyāyavinayacandreṇa' (Indian upādhyāya Vinayacandra as translator with Lotsāwa Chos kyi shes rab) and 'bhikṣuṇā dharmaprajñena' as annotator. Hahn (1990) and his 'The Śatagāthā attributed to Vararuci' (Tibetan and Buddhist Studies) treat the Vararuci ascription as traditional; web (Exotic India, Academia A115) confirms Vinayacandra as translator.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Hundert Strophen von der Lebensklugheit. Nagarjunas Prajnasataka_Michael Hahn, 1990.txt; web:academia.edu/5590945
texts.note: Title conflates roles: Vararuci (root verses, traditionally), Vinayacandra (translator/redactor into Tibetan), Dharmaprajña (annotator). Hahn calls it 'attributed to Vararuci', hence traditional not secure.

texts.title: Hetubinduṭīkā
texts.primary_author: Arcaṭa (Bhaṭṭa Arcaṭa), ṭīkā on Dharmakīrti's Hetubindu; with Āloka subcommentary by Durveka Miśra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'śrīmadarcaṭaviracitā hetubinduṭīkā' — composed by Arcaṭa, a commentary (ṭīkā) on Dharmakīrti's root Hetubindu, edited together with the sub-commentary Āloka by Durveka Miśra (Sanghavi & Jinavijayaji, GOS 113, 1949). Nakamura 1980 and Kajiyama 1966 confirm: Arcaṭa wrote the ṭīkā on Dharmakīrti's HB, Durveka Miśra wrote the Āloka on HBṬ.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Introduction to Buddhist Philosophy. An Annotated Translation of the Tarkabhasa of Moksakaragupta_Kajiyama, Yuichi, 1966.txt
texts.note: The ṭīkā proper is by Arcaṭa (8th c.), but it is a layered work: root Hetubindu by Dharmakīrti and printed/transmitted edition includes the Āloka subcommentary by Durveka Miśra (ca. 970-1030). Distinct authorial roles; current_author captures only Arcaṭa.

texts.title: Hetubinduṭīkā
texts.primary_author: Arcaṭa (Bhaṭṭa Arcaṭa), ṭīkā on Dharmakīrti's Hetubindu; with Āloka subcommentary by Durveka Miśra
texts.basis: Same edition/source as arhebt2u (the two GRETIL files are versions of the same Sanghavi & Jinavijayaji 1949 edition). Header attributes ṭīkā to Arcaṭa; root Hetubindu is Dharmakīrti's; printed edition carries Durveka Miśra's Āloka. Confirmed by Nakamura 1980 and Kajiyama 1966.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Introduction to Buddhist Philosophy. An Annotated Translation of the Tarkabhasa of Moksakaragupta_Kajiyama, Yuichi, 1966.txt
texts.note: Duplicate item of the same Hetubinduṭīkā (same source_url). Layered authorship: Dharmakīrti (root HB) / Arcaṭa (ṭīkā) / Durveka Miśra (Āloka subcommentary).

texts.title: Śikṣāsamuccaya
texts.primary_author: Śāntideva (compiler; Śikṣāsamuccaya-kārikā + prose), an anthology of quoted sūtras
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Śāntideva: Śikṣāsamuccaya'; ed. Bendall, Bibl. Buddhica 1, 1897-1902. Content is largely a compilation/anthology of sūtra quotations strung on Śāntideva's own root verses (Śikṣāsamuccaya-kārikā) plus connective prose. Secondary lit (Bendall & Rouse; Wallace 1997 bibliography citing Śāntideva's Śikṣāsamuccaya-kārikās) confirms Śāntideva as author/compiler.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Guide to the Bodhisattva way of life_Santideva_Vesna A. Wallace & B. Alan Wallace, tr , 1997.txt
texts.note: Single author (Śāntideva) but his role is compiler/anthologist: the bulk is verbatim sūtra extracts (many distinct, unnamed scriptures) framed by his kārikās and prose. Not a multi-author conflation in the attribution sense — the quoted sūtras are sources, not co-authors — so conflation_flag=false. Attribution to Śāntideva is secure.

texts.title: Tattvasaṃgraha
texts.primary_author: Śāntarakṣita (kārikā/root verses); commentary Pañjikā by Kamalaśīla (not in this e-text)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Śāntarakṣita: Tattvasamgraha'; grammatical analysis by Cahill & Stern. This e-text contains the ROOT VERSES ONLY (ends at v. 498 in excerpt), not Kamalaśīla's Pañjikā. The cited edition (Krishnamacharya, GOS 30-31, 1926) prints both root and Pañjikā, but this digital file is the kārikās of Śāntarakṣita alone. Attribution to Śāntarakṣita (8th c.) is secure and undisputed.
texts.note: Root Tattvasaṃgraha verses by Śāntarakṣita; the famous Pañjikā commentary is by his pupil Kamalaśīla. This particular e-text is the root only (so single author for the digital file), but the work/edition is conventionally a verse+Pañjikā pair. Kamalaśīla listed for completeness; conflation_flag=false because this file is root-only.

texts.title: Saptavidhānuttarastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL stotra anthology (sa_108-buddhist-stotras, item BuSto_75) ed. J. S. Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha 1994 — gives editor only, no author. No author surfaces in header/colophon excerpt or web search. The title reflects the liturgical 'saptavidhānuttarapūjā' (sevenfold supreme worship: vandanā, pūjana, pāpadeśanā, anumodanā, adhyeṣaṇā/dhyeṣaṇā, bodhicittotpāda, pariṇāmanā) — a devotional/liturgical formula (cf. LVP citing Dharmasaṃgraha XIV), not a signed individual composition.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist devotional stotra tied to the sevenfold-worship liturgy; no original author named, only modern editor Pandey. Already marked anonymous/traditional. No author invented.

texts.title: Avadhānastotra [Vandanāstava]
texts.basis: DSBC page (book/119) names no author, translator, or scribe — only modern editor J. S. Pandey, Bauddha Stotra Saṃgraha 1994. Web search (sanskritdocuments.org lists 'avadhānastotram athavā vandanāstavam') confirms it circulates without an authorial attribution. No colophon naming an author.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist vandanā-type stotra. Not to be confused with the Aṣṭamahāsthānacaityavandanastava traditionally ascribed to King Harṣa Śīlāditya (a distinct text, per Lamotte) — that ascription is NOT applied here to avoid mis-conflation. Already anonymous/traditional; unchanged.

texts.title: Devīkālottara-Āgama
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śaiva āgama); commentary by Nirañjanasiddha
texts.basis: GRETIL header: mūla text only, no original author named; revealed Śaiva āgama (Kālottara recension). Edition (Dwivedi 2000) supplies a Sanskrit commentary by Nirañjanasiddha — a distinct, later author than the root scripture.
texts.note: Root verses anonymous revealed āgama; commentary (not in GRETIL mūla file) by Nirañjanasiddha. Roots vs commentary are different hands but the GRETIL text is mūla-only, so no in-text conflation.

texts.title: Rudrayāmalatantra (Uttarakāṇḍa)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (N.N. Bhattacharyya, History of the Tantric Religion; Goudriaan ed., Ritual and Speculation): treated as an anonymous revealed Yāmala Tantra; many later texts merely 'profess to be extracted from the Rudrayāmala', indicating it functions as a tradition/category. The Uttarakāṇḍa is a later anonymous compilation; ed. Jīvānanda Vidyāsāgara only published it.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion An Historical, Ritualistic, and Philosophical Study_N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt
texts.note: No named author; pseudo-quotations from 'Rudrayāmala' are common, the work itself is an anonymous, layered revealed Tantra. Editor Jīvānanda Vidyāsāgara is publisher, not author.

texts.title: Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa, Part 2
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Nakamura, Indian Buddhism 1980): self-styled Mahāvaipulya-mahāyāna-sūtra of Avataṃsaka affiliation but Mantrayāna in content; composite/stratified (first three chapters added later; ch.53 Rājavyākaraṇa composed mid-8th c. under Gopāla). No individual author; Ganapati Sastri is editor only.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Stratified anonymous compilation accreting over centuries; chronologically composite but no distinct NAMED authors, so anonymous rather than conflated. T. Ganapati Sastri = editor.

texts.title: Aṣṭāṅgahṛdayasūtra (Aṣṭāṅgahṛdaya, Sūtrasthāna)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Maas, Indian Medicine and Ayurveda): Aṣṭāṅgahṛdayasaṃhitā attributed to Vāgbhaṭa, distinct from the Aṣṭāṅgasaṅgraha 'also attributed to an author named Vāgbhaṭa'. Web (Wikipedia/wisdomlib): two-Vāgbhaṭas debate concerns whether AH and AS share one author, not internal authorship of AH itself. Edition adds commentaries Sarvāṅgasundarā (Aruṇadatta) and Āyurvedarasāyana (Hemādri).
texts.note: AH securely by Vāgbhaṭa (the GS40 file is the mūla Sūtrasthāna). The 'two Vāgbhaṭas' question is about the relation of AH to the Aṣṭāṅgasaṅgraha, not a conflation within this title. Commentators are distinct later hands but not in the mūla text.

texts.title: Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa (DSBC, ed. T. Ganapati Śāstrī)
texts.basis: DSBC book 78 page: title 'Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpam', M. M. T. Ganapati Shastri listed as EDITOR (not author). Same work as the MMK parts above: anonymous, stratified Mahāyāna/Mantrayāna compilation (Nakamura).
texts.note: Work-item had empty title/author; identified as the Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa (TSS 70/76/84, Ganapati Śāstrī ed.). Anonymous compilation; Ganapati Śāstrī = editor only, not author.

texts.title: Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa, Part 3
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Nakamura, Indian Buddhism 1980): anonymous, stratified Mahāyāna–Mantrayāna compilation; chapters of differing dates (e.g. ch.53 mid-8th c.). T. Ganapati Sastri (TSS 84) is editor only.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Same work as parts 2 and the DSBC edition; anonymous composite text, no named author. Editor = T. Ganapati Sastri.

texts.title: Abhisamayālaṃkārāloka [Abhisamayālaṅkārāloka]
texts.primary_author: Haribhadra (vyākhyā); on Maitreya's Abhisamayālaṃkāra kārikā + the Aṣṭasāhasrikā sūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL/Wogihara ed. titles it 'The Work of Haribhadra'; secondary (Snellgrove, Ichimura, Takasaki) state it is Haribhadra's commentary (vyākhyā/ālokā) on the Abhisamayālaṃkāra of Maitreya(nātha)/Asaṅga and on the Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā. The composite work embeds and expounds Maitreya's kārikās plus the root sūtra.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Snellgrove_Indo-Tibetan Buddhism.txt; secondary:cleaned_NAGARJUNA'S PHILOSOPHY OF SUNYATA AND HIS DIALECTIC_SHOHEI ICHIMURA, 1972.txt
texts.note: Commentary genuinely structured around another author's verses: the kārikā = Abhisamayālaṃkāra (Maitreya, transmission assoc. Asaṅga), the running commentary/ālokā = Haribhadra (8th c.). Haribhadra authorship of the ālokā itself is secure.

texts.title: parākramapūjā
texts.basis: Muktabodha/NGMCP source: an unnamed Śaiva-Śākta ritual (pūjā) section excerpted from a Nepalese MS (NGMCP 5-4656 / A 175/11) beginning with Aśeṣakulavallarī; identified and edited by Mark S.G. Dyczkowski. No traditional author; revealed ritual text. No secondary lit hits.
texts.note: Modern editor (Dyczkowski) only; no original author named. Title is a ritual-section heading, not an authored work. Traditional ascription, if any, would be scriptural/revelatory.

texts.title: Tāratantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śākta tantra; framed as Śiva-Devī dialogue)
texts.basis: Ed. A. K. Maitra, Tantrik Texts series (1913), Tāratantram No. I (Internet Archive). A Śākta tantra cast as Śiva-Devī dialogue, i.e. traditionally regarded as revelation with no human author. Maitra is editor, not author. Dialogue-frame revelation is the standard self-attribution of such tantras.
texts.note: Editor A. K. Maitra (Akshaya Kumar Maitra) is modern; original author anonymous. Traditional ascription = revelation (Śiva to Devī). Not the Buddhist Tārā-mūla-kalpa, a distinct Śākta Tāratantra.

texts.title: Bodhicaryāvatāra [Bodhisattvacaryāvatāra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Vaidya ed. 1960) attributes to Śāntideva; secondary (Clayton, Nakamura, Snellgrove, Eckel) uniformly attribute to Śāntideva (8th c.), with Prajñākaramati's Pañjikā as the standard commentary. GRETIL notes lines supplemented from an alternative recension.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_MORAL THEORY IN SANTIDEVA'S SIKSASAMUCCAYA_Barbra R. Clayton.txt
texts.note: Authorship secure. A shorter Dunhuang recension exists (canonical vs Dunhuang versions differ), but both ascribed to Śāntideva; commentary (Pañjikā) by Prajñākaramati is a separate work, not conflated here.

texts.title: Bṛhatkathāślokasaṃgraha
texts.basis: GRETIL header (input Bigger, ed. Lacôte 1908) attributes to Buddhasvāmin/Budhasvāmin. It is a verse recension/redaction of the lost Bṛhatkathā tradition (Guṇāḍhya); but the surviving ślokasaṃgraha is Budhasvāmin's own composition. Secondary (Przyluski) notes the Bṛhatkathā survives only in late recensions.
texts.note: Budhasvāmin is author of this saṃgraha. Underlying narrative tradition is Guṇāḍhya's lost Bṛhatkathā (different source-author), but the extant Sanskrit text is Budhasvāmin's; no in-text conflation.

texts.title: Śāstravārtāsamuccaya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Dixit ed. 1969) and closing colophon (vv.699-701) where Haribhadra names himself as composer of the 700-verse work. Secondary (Tomlinson 2019, Padmarajiah, Buddhist Studies in Honour of Horner) consistently attribute to the Jain Ācārya Haribhadrasūri. Commentaries (Dikpradā; Syādvādakalpalatā by Yaśovijaya) are separate.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Buddhahood and Philosophy of Mind ... Tomlinson, 2019.txt
texts.note: Jain Haribhadrasūri (8th c.), distinct from the Buddhist Haribhadra of item 1. Self-named in colophon. Sub-commentaries by others not conflated into this root text.

texts.title: Bodhicaryāvatāra [Bodhisattvacaryāvatāra]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Mahoney input) gives Śāntideva as author of root text; the named edition (Vaidya 1960) is explicitly 'Bodhicaryāvatāra of Śāntideva with the Commentary Pañjikā of Prajñākaramati', so the title bundles root verses (Śāntideva) and the only surviving Sanskrit Indian commentary, the Pañjikā, by Prajñākaramati (10th c.). Secondary lit (Eckel; PIATS-06; Nakamura; Clayton) confirms Śāntideva as root author and Prajñākaramati as pañjikā author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Jnanagarbha on the Two Truths_Eckel 1987; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism_Nakamura 1980; secondary:cleaned_MORAL THEORY IN SANTIDEVA'S SIKSASAMUCCAYA_Clayton
texts.note: Root Bodhicaryāvatāra securely by Śāntideva (8th c.); edition adds Prajñākaramati's Pañjikā (10th c.). current_author lists only Śāntideva, omitting the commentator bundled in the edition title.

texts.title: Guhyasamājatantra
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (revealed; traditionally ascribed to the Buddha/Vajradhara; sometimes associated with Asaṅga or with the mahāsiddha tradition)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed tantra; traditionally ascribed to the Buddha/Vajradhara)
texts.basis: No GRETIL header (source_url null). Edition is Bhattacharyya 1931 (Tathāgataguhyaka). Secondary lit (Capriles; PIATS-06) treats it as a revealed Yoga/Father tantra; the Ārya exegetical lineage (Nāgārjuna, etc.) is interpretive commentary, not authorship. Traditional ascription is to the Buddha/Vajradhara; no historical human author identifiable.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Capriles_Budd philosophical schools and rdzogs chen; secondary:cleaned_PIATS-06-Fagernes-1992-v2; tradition
texts.note: Revealed scripture; traditionally ascribed to the Buddha/Vajradhara. Ārya-tradition figures (Nāgārjuna, Candrakīrti, etc.) are later interpreters, not the author. Remains anonymous historically.

texts.title: Nyāyapraveśapravṛtti
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Haribhadrasūrikṛtā Nyāyapraveśapravṛttiḥ', colophon 'kṛtiriyaṃ haribhadrasūreḥ' — commentary by Haribhadrasūri. The root Nyāyapraveśa(ka) was formerly misascribed to Dignāga but is now attributed to his pupil Śaṅkarasvāmin (Nakamura; Steinkellner; Tucci; Ganeri; Joshi 'formerly ascribed to Dinnāga, is now [Śaṅkarasvāmin]'; Kapstein notes it cannot be Dignāga's Nyāyamukha).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism_Nakamura 1980; secondary:cleaned_Steinkellner_Buddhist Epistemological Tradition; secondary:cleaned_Tucci_Doctrines of Maitreya; secondary:cleaned_Studies in the Buddhistic Culture of India_Joshi
texts.note: Pravṛtti (commentary) by Haribhadrasūri (the current_author); root Nyāyapraveśa by Śaṅkarasvāmin (formerly misattributed to Dignāga). Title conflates commentator and root author.

texts.title: Mṛgendrāgama [Mṛgendratantra]: 4. Yogapāda
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Goodall input): 'Mūla text only', based on M.K. Shastri KSTS 50 ed.; no human author, framed as revealed knowledge ('harādindrakramāyātaṃ jñānaṃ'). Secondary lit (Essais sur le rituel; Yoga Powers_Jacobsen; Goudriaan ed.) treats it as a Śaiva āgama with vṛtti by Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha. This file is root text only, so Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha's commentary is not in the digitized content.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Yoga Powers_Knut A. Jacobsen 2012; secondary:cleaned_Essais sur le rituel
texts.note: Revealed Śaiva Siddhānta scripture; anonymous. Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha (vṛtti, ca. 10th c.) is associated by tradition/edition but absent from this mūla-only GRETIL file.

texts.title: Mṛgendrāgama [Mṛgendratantra]: 3. Caryāpāda
texts.basis: GRETIL TEI transformation header: text presented as revealed scripture, no human author credited; edition N.R. Bhatt (IFI 1962) / M.K. Shastri (KSTS 50). Commentator is Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha (vṛtti). Secondary lit (Jacobsen; Goudriaan ed.; Essais sur le rituel) confirms āgama status and Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha as commentator. GRETIL presentation is the scripture text.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Yoga Powers_Knut A. Jacobsen 2012; secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism_Goudriaan ed
texts.note: Revealed Śaiva Siddhānta scripture; anonymous. Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha wrote the vṛtti (associated by edition/tradition); the root Caryāpāda itself has no human author.

texts.title: Bāhyārthasiddhikārikā
texts.basis: DSBC source (no specific GRETIL header). Secondary lit is firm: Nakamura — 'Subhagupta (Dge-sruṅs ca. 650-750)... wrote the Bāhyārthasiddhikārikā'; Ruegg, Matilal, Sinha, Seton all cite Śubhagupta's Bāhyārthasiddhi(kārikā). Work survives mainly in Tibetan with Sanskrit fragments (Sastri/Hattori). Single author, secure.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism_Nakamura 1980; secondary:cleaned_ruegg_bka' gnad 8; secondary:cleaned_Logic, language, and reality_Matilal 1990
texts.note: Securely by Śubhagupta (Bhadanta Śubhagupta, ca. 650-750, a Vaibhāṣika/Sautrāntika realist refuted by Śāntarakṣita-Kamalaśīla). Kārikā = root verses; single author.

texts.title: Hitopadeśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header titles it 'Narayana: Hitopadesa' (input Brzezinski, rev. Stiehl); the frame narrator within the text is Viṣṇuśarman (the Pañcatantra storyteller). Olivelle (Pañcatantra, 1999) states the Hitopadeśa is 'the radical recasting of the Pañcatantra carried out by an author named Nārāyaṇa in Bengal,' partly based on the Southern Pañcatantra, with four books and changed frame stories.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Pancatantra. The Book of India's Folk Wisdom_Patrick Olivelle, 1999.txt
texts.note: Nārāyaṇa is the recaster/compiler, not an original author: the bulk derives from the Pañcatantra tradition (its anonymous material/Viṣṇuśarman frame). Title thus conflates Nārāyaṇa's redaction with inherited Pañcatantra authorship; Nārāyaṇa otherwise nearly unknown, patron Dhavalacandra.

texts.title: Tridaśaḍāmaratantre Siddhikālīstotra
texts.basis: Muktabodha digital e-text (cat. M00203), entered under M.S.G. Dyczkowski from Nepalese MSS; no critical edition, no named human author. A stotra to Siddhikālī embedded in the Tridaśaḍāmara(tantra), a Ḍāmara-class tantra. Web (wisdomlib Damaratantra) confirms Ḍāmara tantras are framed as Śiva's revealed speech with no human author; modern 'Damara Tantra' editions are by editors (e.g. Ram Kumar Rai), not original authors.
texts.note: Tantric stotra/dialogue framed as revelation of Śiva (Mahādeva). No human author; traditional ascription is to Śiva. Stays anonymous/traditional as currently recorded.

texts.title: Pramāṇavārttikaṭīkā
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'Sakyabuddhi: Pramanavarttikatika', based on Inami/Matsuda/Tani 1992 ed. of the Kathmandu National Archives MS (input Klaus Wille). Steinkellner Festschrift (Pramāṇakīrtiḥ) calls Śākyabuddhi 'the commentator (7th-8th cent.)' on Dharmakīrti; Tillemans (2000) lists 'Pramāṇavārttikaṭīkā of Śākyabuddhi [= Śākyamati]' alongside Devendrabuddhi and Manorathanandin.
texts.note: Sub-commentary (ṭīkā) on Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttika, comments on Devendrabuddhi's Pañjikā. Author Śākyabuddhi (a.k.a. Śākyamati), 7th-8th c.; attribution secure. Root verses are Dharmakīrti's (not the author of this ṭīkā).

texts.title: Nairātmyaparipṛcchāmahāyānasūtra
texts.basis: DSBC page (dsbcproject.org/canon-text/book/51) gives title 'Nairātmyaparipṛcchā nāma mahāyānasūtram' in Vaidya's Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha Part 1 (BST 17, 1961); no author named. As a Mahāyāna sūtra it is canonically anonymous, framed as the word of the Buddha.
texts.note: Mahāyāna sūtra (a 'paripṛcchā' / question-dialogue). No human author; traditional ascription is Buddhavacana. Editor P. L. Vaidya is modern editor only, not author.

texts.title: Niruttaravāda
texts.basis: Muktabodha digital-library e-text; no edition, no current_author given. The Niruttara doctrine (niruttaravāda) belongs to the Niruttara/Kālī class of tantras; the Niruttaratantra is traditionally attributed to Mahādeva (Śiva) and is revealed scripture (shivashakti.com; wisdomlib). No human author identified.
texts.note: current_author was blank. A tantric 'niruttara' doctrine text framed as Śiva's revelation; no human author. Traditional ascription to Mahādeva/Śiva. Set to anonymous (was empty).

texts.title: Mṛgendrāgama
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śaiva āgama; commentaries by Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha & Rāmakaṇṭha)
texts.basis: Edition (KSTS 50, Kaul Shāstrī 1930; IFP, Bhatt 1962) is the Mṛgendra mūla āgama WITH commentary (vṛtti): Vidyā/Yogapāda comm. by Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha, Kriyā/Caryāpāda comm. (-vṛtti) by Rāmakaṇṭha. Sanderson (Purity and Power) lists 'Mrgendratantra with commentary (-vrtti) by Nā[rāyaṇakaṇṭha]' and the Bhatt ed. with Rāmakaṇṭha's vṛtti. Goudriaan/Padoux vol. cites Mṛgendra vidyāpāda doctrine as Śaiva Siddhānta scripture.
texts.note: Śaiva Siddhānta āgama: the mūla is anonymous/revealed (counted as part of the Kāmikāgama upāgama tradition, word of Śiva). The standard editions conflate it with commentaries by TWO different authors — Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha (Vidyā/Yoga) and Rāmakaṇṭha (Kriyā/Caryā). Roles separated; conflation flagged.

texts.title: Śākyasiṃhastotra: viṣṇukṛtam
texts.basis: DSBC header gives the title with the qualifier 'viṣṇukṛtam' = 'composed by Viṣṇu', a devotional framing device (the god Viṣṇu praising the Buddha/Śākyasiṃha), not a real human authorship claim; no editor-asserted author. Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, p.120) lists Śrī-Śākyasiṃhastotra among Buddhastotras with no human author named. No critical attribution found in secondary literature.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Buddhastotra in praise of the Buddha (Śākyasiṃha). 'viṣṇukṛtam' is a divine-speaker frame (Viṣṇu as eulogist), hence pseudepigraphic; the real composer is anonymous. Tradition-internal ascription to Viṣṇu noted separately.

texts.title: Mālatīmādhava
texts.basis: Secondary literature is unanimous: Nakamura, Romila Thapar (Penguin History of Early India, p.311), Bibbhuti Baruah, Kinsley all name the Mālatīmādhava as the drama of Bhavabhūti (7th-8th c. CE). The stated Bhandarkar edition (BSS 15, 1876) carries the commentary of Jagaddhara, a separate commentator.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Penguin History of Early India_Romila Thapar, 2001.txt | gretil-header
texts.note: Root prakaraṇa drama securely by Bhavabhūti; the commentary in this edition is by Jagaddhara (distinct person, role separated but not a conflation of the work's authorship).

texts.title: Bauddhatantrakośa I
texts.primary_author: anonymous (modern compilation; input by Milan Shakya)
texts.basis: DSBC header: 'Bauddha tantra kośa (I)', input by Milan Shakya (2013), proof-read by Miroj Shakya, supplied by Nagarjuna Institute / University of the West. It is a lexicon/kośa of Buddhist tantric terminology compiling quotations from many classical tantras; no original author or pre-modern compiler named. No secondary-literature attribution found.
texts.note: Modern digital compilation (a glossary of tantric terms drawing on many sources). No single classical author; Milan Shakya is the modern editor/inputter, not the author. Anonymous compilation.

texts.title: Adhyātmasāraśataka [Adhyātmasāraśatakasaṭīka]
texts.primary_author: uncertain (śataka verses + ṭīkā, both unattributed in available sources)
texts.basis: No edition stated and no author in workitem. DSBC homepage search returned no record; web searches for 'Adhyātmasāraśataka' / 'Adhyatmasarasataka' returned no reliable authorship (a Jain 'Adhyatmasarasataka' is listed in reference literature under DU532 Pattisappu Venkatesvara, a modern Advaita writer, but that is a separate Vedanta/Jain context and cannot be safely identified with this T02 Buddhist-corpus item). The title flags a two-layer text (śataka + saṭīkā = with commentary) but no person can be assigned to either layer.
texts.source: web:dsbcproject.org (no record) | secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Title indicates verses (śataka) plus a ṭīkā (saṭīka), potentially two authorial layers, but no attribution could be triangulated; deliberately not inventing one. Possible homonym with a Jain/Advaita Adhyātmasāraśataka — not confirmed for this item.

texts.title: Madhyapīṭhapūjāvidhi
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text transcribed from a Nepalese manuscript (no printed critical edition); web evidence (Muktabodha / INDOLOGY) describes it as a ritual manual (pūjāvidhi/paddhati) for worship at the central seat (madhyapīṭha), part of Nepal National Archives paddhati manuscripts. No author named; such vidhi/paddhati texts are characteristically anonymous.
texts.note: Anonymous ritual prescription (pūjā-vidhi) preserved only in manuscript. No scribal or authorial name recovered; genuinely anonymous.

texts.title: Niśvāsakārikā
texts.basis: Scholarship (Goodall, Sanderson, Isaacson; EFEO/Hamburg Niśvāsatattvasaṃhitā project) treats the Niśvāsa corpus as the earliest surviving Śaiva Tantra (mūla layer 5th-6th c. CE), an anonymous revealed scripture. The Niśvāsakārikā is a related text surviving independently in three South Indian (Pondicherry/IFP) transcripts, referred to as 'Kārikā' at Guhyasūtra 18.15; no human author — revelation framed as Śiva's teaching.
texts.source: web:publications.efeo.fr / tantric-studies.uni-hamburg.de | secondary:cleaned_Samdarshi_Sacred Feminine Imagery in Tantric Buddhism.txt
texts.note: Anonymous āgamic/revealed Śaiva scripture (Niśvāsa corpus). Multiple recensional layers but no named authors; attributed by tradition to Śiva, hence treated as anonymous with revelatory framing.

texts.title: Buddhagaṇḍīstava
texts.current_author: attributed (traditionally) to Aśvaghoṣa or Āryadeva; uncertain
texts.basis: This is the Gaṇḍī-stava / Gaṇḍī(-stotra), a hymn to summon by the monastery gong-bell (gaṇḍī), traditionally numbered among Aśvaghoṣa's stotras; web (Tibetan Buddhist Encyclopedia, BodhiSvara) describes the Gaṇḍistotra as a eulogy of the monastery bell by Aśvaghoṣa, while edition/tradition also names Āryadeva. Many works ascribed to Aśvaghoṣa are recognized as later/incorrect ascriptions. Secondary lit (reference-literature) had no direct entry under this exact title.
texts.note: Single hymn; not multi-author. Traditional/legendary ascription to Aśvaghoṣa, with alternative Āryadeva; genuinely uncertain. Edited in Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha 1994.

texts.title: Vādanyāyaṭīkā
texts.basis: DSBC e-text header explicitly opens 'ācāryadharmakīrteḥ vādanyāyaḥ ... ācāryaśāntarakṣitapraṇītayā vipañcitārthābhidhayā ṭīkayā saṃvalitaḥ' — root Vādanyāya by Dharmakīrti, this ṭīkā (the Vipañcitārthā) by Śāntarakṣita. Edition cited (Dwarikadas Shastri 1972) is precisely Śāntarakṣita's Vipañcitārthā. Web (Encyclopedia of Buddhism / EAST) confirms the Vipañcitārthā ṭīkā is by Śāntarakṣita, whereas Vinītadeva wrote a SEPARATE, distinct Vādanyāyaṭīkā. Secondary lit (Nakamura 1980; Frauwallner/Potter bibliography) lists Śāntarakṣita's Vipañcitārtha on the Vādanyāya.
texts.source: gretil-header | web:https://encyclopediaofbuddhism.org/wiki/V%C4%81dany%C4%81ya | secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: current_author 'Vinītadeva' is WRONG for THIS text: the e-text/edition is Śāntarakṣita's Vipañcitārthā ṭīkā. Two distinct Vādanyāya commentaries exist (Śāntarakṣita's Vipañcitārthā vs Vinītadeva's ṭīkā); title conflates root verses (Dharmakīrti) + commentary (Śāntarakṣita).

texts.title: Tattvasiddhi
texts.basis: Web (Wikipedia 'Tattvasiddhi'; Śāntarakṣita art.) and secondary lit confirm a tantric Tattvasiddhi attributed to Śāntarakṣita: the ascription is old (Bhavyakīrti, early 10th c.), accepted by Lindtner (1997) but seriously doubted by Steinkellner (1999). Secondary lit (Studies in the Buddhistic Culture of India, Josh) calls it 'a Tantrika text attributed to Śāntarakṣita' but notes it is 'neither possible' to confirm; Pramāṇakīrtiḥ (Steinkellner Fs) discusses a Tattvasiddhi within the (pseudo-)Nāgārjuna/Guhyasamāja orbit — multiple distinct works share the title.
texts.source: web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tattvasiddhi | secondary:cleaned_Studies in the Buddhistic Culture of India_Lal Mani Josh.txt | secondary:cleaned_Pramāṇakīrtiḥ-Steinkellner 70th_v2.txt
texts.note: Beware homonyms: distinct from Harivarman's *Tattvasiddhi (Satyasiddhiśāstra) and from Yoginī Cintā's Vyaktabhāvānugata-tattvasiddhi. This tantric Tattvasiddhi's ascription to Śāntarakṣita is debated (Steinkellner contra Lindtner).

texts.title: Cidānandanātha: Svacchandapaddhati
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text colophon reads 'śrīmaccidānandanātha viracitāyāṃ svacchandapaddhatyāṃ' — internal colophon names Cidānandanātha as composer; edited by Sītalāprasād Upādhyāya. Header and colophon agree.
texts.note: Single-author Śaiva ritual paddhati; colophon-confirmed. Editor Sītalāprasād Upādhyāya is modern, not the author.

texts.title: Paścāttāpa
texts.basis: DSBC page (book/874) lists Paścāttāpa as a kāvya in the śāstrapiṭaka with Russell Webb as (modern) editor and the UK Association for Buddhist Studies as publisher (Buddhist Studies Review, 2003); no ancient author or colophon name is stated. Web searches found no named classical author; it is a short Buddhist repentance (paścāttāpa = 'remorse') poem.
texts.note: No author given on e-text; Russell Webb is the 2003 editor, not the author. Recorded as anonymous (was empty current_author).

texts.title: mahālakṣmīdīpadānavidhiḥ
texts.basis: Title is a ritual prescription (vidhi) for the lamp-offering (dīpadāna) to Mahālakṣmī — a paddhati/vidhi-genre ritual manual, characteristically transmitted without a named author and often excerpted from purāṇic/nibandha material. No author named in source (Muktabodha digital library); web found no attributed author.
texts.note: Anonymous ritual vidhi; no traditional personal ascription found. Consistent with current 'anonymous/traditional'.

texts.title: Jñānasāra
texts.current_author: uncertain (multiple works share this title; e.g. Yaśovijaya for the Jain Jñānasāra)
texts.primary_author: Title conflates several distinct works: Yaśovijaya (Jain Jñānasāra); a post-Bhāvaviveka Madhyamaka author (Buddhist Jñānasārasamuccaya); and an anonymous/Śaiva Jñānasāra (Muktabodha)
texts.basis: Secondary lit: 'Literature about the Philosophical Systems' lists Yaśovijaya's Jñānasāra (Dvātriṃśikā/Aṣṭakaprakaraṇa) with Vivaraṇa; Nakamura (Indian Buddhism) and Buddhism&Jainism (Sarao/Long) describe the distinct Buddhist Jñānasārasamuccaya by a post-Bhāvaviveka Mādhyamika; source_url points to the Śaiva Muktabodha library (a separate Śaiva Jñānasāra). Distinct works/authors share the bare title 'Jñānasāra'.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism..._Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Buddhism and Jainism_Sarao,Long 2017.txt
texts.note: Bare title 'Jñānasāra' is ambiguous; Jain (Yaśovijaya, 17th c.), Buddhist (Jñānasārasamuccaya), and Śaiva works coexist. Muktabodha (source_url) = Śaiva recension. Disambiguation needed per edition.

texts.title: Saddharmapārājikā
texts.basis: DSBC canonical text (source_corpus=dsbc). 'Pārājikā' is a Vinaya/prātimokṣa category (the four/eight gravest expulsion offenses), per wisdomlib & Pratimokṣa entries; such canonical Vinaya material carries no individual human author and is transmitted as buddhavacana/saṅgha redaction. WebFetch of the given node returned tantric content (node mapping unstable), but title + current_author confirm anonymous canonical status.
texts.note: Pārājikā = Vinaya category, not a named author's work; transmitted anonymously. No personal attribution; matches current 'anonymous/traditional'.

texts.title: Ṣaṭsahasrakālottarāgama (Ṣaṭsāhasra Kālottara)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Buddhist Forum 1991, Skorupski) cites 'the Kālottara' as a Śaiva scriptural source prescribing ritual kīlas, treated as revealed scripture (āgama), not an authored work. The 6000-verse (ṣaṭsāhasra) recension is one length-recension of the Kālottara within the Kālajñāna/Kālottara stream of the Siddhānta. No human author; ascribed to Śiva's revelation.
texts.note: Revealed Śaiva āgama; the '6000' is a verse-count recension label, not authorship. Anonymous; matches current attribution.

texts.title: Ṣaṭtriṃśatsaṃvarastuti
texts.basis: GRETIL header (sa_108-buddhist-stotras): the text appears as BuSto_77 'Ṣaṭtriṃśatsaṃvarastuti' in the list of 108 Buddhist stotras (ed. J.S. Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha, 1994); no author/composer is named in the header or available colophon for this specific stotra. Editor = Janardan Shastri Pandey (modern editor, not author).
texts.note: No author named in GRETIL/Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha for this stotra; J.S. Pandey is editor only. Anonymous, matches current.

texts.title: Makuṭāgama (Vīraśaiva recension)
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to revelation by the Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva)
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib): Makuṭāgama is one of the 28 Siddhāntāgamas, presented as revealed scripture imparted by Sadāśiva down a transmission chain (Śiva > Mahādeva > Devas > Ṛṣis > humans), proclaimed via the Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva; no human author. Edition note: e-text from C. Swaminatha Gurukkal's grantha ed. (Devakottai), corrected by M.S.G. Dyczkowski (editorial/redactional roles, not authorship).
texts.note: Revealed āgama (Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva); anonymous. Gurukkal/Dyczkowski are editors of the e-text only. Matches current attribution.

texts.title: Bauddha manīṣā - Bauddheṣu Pramāṇamīmāṃsā
texts.primary_author: uncertain (modern Sanskrit treatise on Buddhist epistemology; named modern author not confirmed)
texts.basis: DSBC text (source_corpus=dsbc). Title is a modern Sanskrit scholarly composition ('Bauddha manīṣā' = a series/scholarly-essay label; 'Bauddheṣu Pramāṇamīmāṃsā' = 'investigation of pramāṇa among the Buddhists'), i.e. a 20th-c. expository work on Buddhist logic/epistemology, not a classical canonical text. Web searches (incl. dsbcproject.org) did not surface a confirmed individual author; secondary lit (reference-literature) has no hit. Not anonymous in principle (modern work has an author) but the specific name is unverified, so recorded as uncertain rather than invented.
texts.note: Modern Sanskrit work on Buddhist pramāṇa; author name not verifiable from available sources. Left authors=[] pending confirmation; status=uncertain (a modern author exists but is unconfirmed). current_author was empty.

texts.title: Bhāvanākrama
texts.basis: GRETIL header opens 'ācāryakamalaśīlapraṇīto Bhāvanākramaḥ' and colophon 'ācāryakamalaśīlena'; secondary lit (Tucci, Minor Buddhist Texts; Wangmo; Adams) uniformly assigns the three Bhāvanākramas to Kamalaśīla; the Tibetan revision is by Prajñāvarman/Jñānasena (translators, not authors).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Mipham Gyatso Rinpoche's 'Makeover' of Hwashang Moheyan (Tucci ed.)
texts.note: Single author Kamalaśīla; colophon also names Tibetan reviewer/translators Prajñāvarman and Jñānasena (transmission roles, not co-authors).

texts.title: Ḍākinījālasaṃvararahasya
texts.basis: Davidson (Indian Esoteric Buddhism) cites 'Ananga Yogin, at the beginning of his Dākinījālasamvararahasya' and its bibliography lists 'Dākinījālasamvararahasya. Asc. Ananga Yogin' (ed. Samdhong Rinpoche & V. Dwivedi 1990, RBTS 8, Sarnath); exoticindia lists the Sarnath edition as 'by Anangayogi'. A tantric exegetical work, not a root tantra.
texts.note: Was blank; resolved to Anaṅga Yogin (ascribed). This is a tantric commentary/rahasya, distinct from the root Sarvabuddhasamāyoga-ḍākinījālasaṃvara-tantra.

texts.title: Svayaṃbhūstava
texts.basis: GRETIL stotra collection (Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha, ed. Pandey 1994, text no. 90) gives only the title with no author; the collection comprises Nepalese devotional hymns, most anonymous. No colophon author traced in secondary lit. (Distinct from the Jain Svayambhūstotra of Samantabhadra.)
texts.note: Buddhist Svayaṃbhū (Nepal) hymn; no author named in edition. NB: not the Jain Svayambhūstotra by Samantabhadra.

texts.title: Nāmāṣṭaśataka
texts.basis: Within Pandey's Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (1994) as text in the GRETIL 108-stotras collection; '108-names' litany stotras in this corpus carry no named author (cf. parallel nāmāṣṭottaraśata stotras in the same collection, all anonymous). Likely the Prajñāpāramitā-nāmāṣṭaśataka (108 names of the Perfection of Wisdom), traditionally canonical/anonymous.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Gha rung pa...Lamp That Illuminates (cites Prajñāpāramitānāmāṣṭaśataka as scripture, no author)
texts.note: Name-litany (aṣṭaśata) stotra; no author in edition; traditionally anonymous canonical text.

texts.title: Prajñāpāramitāvajracchedikāṭīkā
texts.basis: Title pairs the Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā sūtra (canonical Buddhavacana, anonymous; DSBC colophon 'āryavajracchedikā bhagavatī prajñāpāramitā samāptā') with Kamalaśīla's ṭīkā. Schoening (Śālistamba) confirms 'D 3817, the Prajñāpāramitā-vajracchedikā-ṭīkā of Kamalaśīla'. The fetched DSBC content (76/633) actually carries only the sūtra text; the ṭīkā is the secured Kamalaśīla layer.
texts.note: Two distinct layers: root sūtra (anonymous Buddhavacana) vs ṭīkā by Kamalaśīla. Current attribution credits only Kamalaśīla; flagged as conflation of sūtra + commentary.

texts.title: Madhyamakāloka
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'Ācāryakamalaśīlaviracito Madhyamakālokaḥ'; secondary lit (Keira, Madhyamika and Epistemology; Nagashima; Kuijp) consistently attributes the Madhyamakāloka to Kamalaśīla. Work largely survives in Tibetan (P/D); Sanskrit e-text from DSBC.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems (Keira); cleaned_Kuijp_Dating two ldeu chronicles
texts.note: Single author Kamalaśīla; secure.

texts.title: Buddhabhaṭṭārakastotra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (colophon ascribes composition to Brahmā, legendary)
texts.basis: DSBC colophon: 'buddhabhaṭṭārakasya brahmāviracitaṃ stotraṃ samāptam' — the hymn is said to be composed by Brahmā (the deity), a legendary/devotional ascription, not a real human author. DSBC edition prepared by staff (Miroj Shakya proofing), Nagarjuna Institute source.
texts.note: Colophon names Brahmā as composer — pseudepigraphic divine ascription; genuine human author unknown. Differs from bare 'anonymous/traditional' by recording the specific legendary ascription.

texts.title: Samyaksambuddhabhāṣita Pratimālakṣaṇa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (ascribed to the Buddha as buddhavacana; iconometric tradition also linked to Atreya/Mañjuśrī)
texts.basis: GRETIL header/opening: 'evaṃ mayā śrutam... buddho bhagavān' and the very title 'Samyaksambuddhabhāṣita' present it as a discourse spoken by the Buddha to Śāriputra (buddhavacana), no historical author named. Edited by Ngawang Samten & Janardan Pandey (Dhīḥ 35, 2003). Secondary (Anālayo 2017; Revire 2016) treat it as an anonymous Buddhist iconometry manual in sūtra frame; web notes a parallel attribution of the iconometric tradition to Atreya, and the related Citrakarmaśāstra to Mañjuśrī.
texts.note: Pseudepigraphic buddhavacana (Buddha to Śāriputra); no real author. Editors named but not authors. Atreya/Mañjuśrī appear only for the broader iconometry genre, not this text specifically.

texts.title: Kulānandādi
texts.basis: Edition: Bagchi 1934, 'Kaulajñānanirṇaya and Some Minor Texts of the School of Matsyendranātha' — Kulānanda is one of the minor texts of that school. Eliade (Yoga) and Dan Martin's bibliography confirm a corpus 'of the school of Matsyendranātha.' Web (Bagchi/Internet Archive) confirms texts are ascribed to the school/Matsyendranātha tradition; manuscripts 11th-12th c. Attribution is to the Kaula/Matsyendranātha school rather than a securely documented single author.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Matsyendranātha as eponymous founder of the Kaula school; authorship is school-level, not securely personal. No conflation evident in this short treatise.

texts.title: Tattvasamāsa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally — and per scholarship 'wrongly' — ascribed to Kapila)
texts.basis: GRETIL header names no author ('an abstract of Sāṃkhya philosophy in (23-)25 sūtras'); editor Vindhyeśvarī Prasāda Dvivedin (Sāṃkhyasaṃgraha). Potter, Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies vol. 4 (1987): the Sāṃkhya sūtra-collection is 'attributed to Kapila (wrongly)'; Tattvasamāsasūtra possibly old but a late sūtra collection, intelligible only via its (very late) commentaries (e.g. Kramadīpikā). Foundational sage Kapila is a legendary ascription, not the actual author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 4 - Potter 1987.txt
texts.note: Anonymous; Kapila ascription explicitly called 'wrongly' attributed by Potter — hence pseudepigraphic to the legendary founder. The kārikā-text is distinct from its later commentaries (Kramadīpikā etc.), which have their own authors.

texts.title: Aparimitāyuḥsūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Mahāyāna sūtra ascribed to the Buddha as buddhavacana)
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives no author/colophon; SBIP/Nagarjuna Institute input, ed. M. Walleser 1916. Text is a Mahāyāna dhāraṇī-sūtra (Aparimitāyur-jñāna-nāma-mahāyāna-sūtra) framed as the Buddha addressing Mañjuśrī; named figures are the Buddha and bodhisattvas within the narrative, not authors. Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, 1980) and Nariman list it as an anonymous Mahāyāna sūtra.
texts.note: Anonymous buddhavacana sūtra; no individual author. Buddha/Mañjuśrī are internal speakers, not historical authors; recorded as anonymous per instructions (no genuine author known).

texts.title: Laghuyamasmṛti
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Dharmaśāstra metrical smṛti traditionally ascribed to the sage/god Yama)
texts.basis: GRETIL smṛti directory (HTTP 403 on listing); edition Vināyak Gaṇeś Āpṭe, Smṛtīnāṃ Samuccayaḥ, Ānandāśrama Sanskrit Series 48, 1929. As with the smṛti genre, the 'Laghu-' (lesser) Yamasmṛti is a versified Dharmaśāstra ascribed to the eponymous sage/deity Yama; no historical author. No specific secondary discussion of distinct multi-author layers found.
texts.note: Pseudepigraphic ascription to Yama (legendary lawgiver); a 'Laghu' recension distinct from the larger Yamasmṛti, both anonymous in real authorship. Recorded as pseudepigraphic rather than bare anonymous to capture the Yama ascription.

texts.title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti
texts.primary_author: anonymous (root tantra; traditionally Buddhavacana, Māyājālatantra cycle)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Pandey (Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha 1994); colophon names Pandey as saṃgrahakartā sampādakaśca and Ratnakāji Vajrācārya as source paṇḍita. Web: root NMS is an anonymous tantra, traditionally preached by Śākyamuni to Vajrapāṇi, of the advaya/Māyājāla class; the named figure Vilāsavajra (8th c.) is the COMMENTATOR (Nāmamantrārthāvalokinī), not the root author.
texts.note: Root text anonymous/traditional Buddhavacana; do not conflate with Vilāsavajra's commentary. Colophon = compiler/editor only (Pandey), not authorship.

texts.title: Sakalāgamasārasaṅgraha
texts.primary_author: uncertain; traditionally ascribed to Nigamajñānadeśika (Śaiva Siddhānta, 16th c.) — unconfirmed for this title
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text, no printed critical edition. Local secondary (skt-en-mono): the Sakalāgamasārasaṅgraha is cited as a later Saiddhāntika digest (e.g. 1:412-422 quoting Kiraṇa), confirming it is a Śaiva Siddhānta āgama compendium. Web could not confirm a specific compiler; Nigamajñānadeśika (16th c.) is a known Śaiva Siddhānta digest-author but no source ties him firmly to THIS title.
texts.note: Śaiva Siddhānta āgama-digest; compiler not securely identified. Possible Nigamajñānadeśika but low confidence; treat as uncertain rather than firmly anonymous.

texts.title: Āryamañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti (Mukherji 1963 ed.)
texts.basis: Edition record: I. P. Mukherji (ed.), Āryamañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti, Sanskrit and Tibetan texts, Visva-Bharati 1963 (PDF cert expired, not fetchable). Same root text as the other NMS items: anonymous tantric work, traditionally Buddhavacana. Mukherji is editor/translator only.
texts.note: current_author was empty; root NMS is anonymous/traditional. Mukherji = editor of Skt+Tib parallel texts, not author.

texts.title: Ratnaguṇasaṃcayagāthā
texts.primary_author: anonymous (verse-summary of the Prajñāpāramitā); recension/chapter-division by Haribhadra
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: includes a praśasti of Ācārya Haribhadra claiming to have purified/critically prepared the text, plus a separate lekhakapraśasti (scribe). Web: the Ratnaguṇasaṃcayagāthā is among the earliest Prajñāpāramitā strata, an anonymous verse summary; in the 8th c. Haribhadra produced the recension that rearranged/divided the verses into chapters (he also wrote the Subodhinī commentary on it).
texts.note: Anonymous early verse-summary BUT the transmitted (Vaidya/GRETIL) text is the Haribhadra recension with his praśasti — distinct hands: original anonymous composer vs Haribhadra redactor (vs scribe). Flagged as conflated.
texts.conflation_note: root verses (anon) vs Haribhadra's chapter-recension/praśasti vs scribal colophon

texts.title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti
texts.primary_author: anonymous (root tantra; traditionally Buddhavacana, Māyājālatantra cycle)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Pandey (Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha 1994, checked vs Raghu Vira, Śata-piṭaka 18); colophon names Pandey as saṃgrahakartā sampādakaśca. Identical root text to manjn1pu. Root NMS anonymous/traditional Buddhavacana; Vilāsavajra is commentator not author.
texts.note: Duplicate GRETIL encoding of the same Pandey edition as manjn1pu. Root text anonymous; editor/compiler = Pandey only.

texts.title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti (Minaev 1887 ed.)
texts.basis: Edition record only: I. P. Minaev, NMS Sanskrit text in 'Buddizm', vol. I.2, St. Petersburg 1887 (no source_url). Same root text as other NMS items: anonymous tantric work, traditionally Buddhavacana of the Māyājāla cycle. Minaev = editor.
texts.note: current_author was empty; root NMS anonymous/traditional. Minaev = 19th-c. editor, not author.

texts.title: Aparimitāyunāmamahāyānasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Walleser ed. 1916) names only the editor; the text is a Mahāyāna sūtra/dhāraṇī with no human author. Web (84000, Encyclopaedia Iranica): canonical sūtra concerning merit of recalling Aparimitāyurjñāna, edited by Walleser/Konow, no historical author; traditionally Buddha-word. Over a hundred Skt mss.
texts.note: Buddhavacana by convention; Walleser is editor/translator only. No personal author; traditional ascription = words of the Buddha.

texts.title: Kāvyālaṃkārasārasaṃgraha
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Udbhata: Kavyalamkarasarasamgraha, Karikas (extracted from the commented version)' — i.e. the kārikās alone, drawn from the version with Indurāja's commentary. Web (wisdomlib, Bronner): Bhaṭṭa Udbhaṭa, c. 8th c., sabhāpati at the Kashmiri court of Jayāpīḍa (779-813); work of 6 vargas / ~79 kārikās defining 41 alaṃkāras following Bhāmaha.
texts.note: Distinct from Udbhaṭasiddhasvāmin (the Buddhist hymnist of the Viśeṣastava). This GRETIL e-text = kārikās only, extracted from Pratīhāra Indurāja's commented edition; commentary not included here.

texts.title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti
texts.basis: Edition is Davidson 1981 (critical edition + translation), so Davidson is editor/translator only. Web (Wikipedia, Encyclopedia of Buddhism): a tantric work of 167 verses plus a prose chapter, traditionally taught by Buddha Śākyamuni; no historical author. Secondary lit notes numerous later commentaries (e.g. Nāmasaṃgītiṭippanī by Raviśrījñāna; a Nāmasaṃgītiṭīkā attributed to Nāgārjuna) — these are separate works, not the root.
texts.note: Workitem title/current_author blank; title is Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti. Root tantra is anonymous (buddhavacana); Davidson = editor/translator. Commentaries by Raviśrījñāna etc. are distinct texts.

texts.title: Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi
texts.primary_author: Ḍombīheruka (mūla, disputed); Dhyānacandra alias Madhyamakarūci (Mahāsukhaprakāśikā ṭīkā, per DSBC source)
texts.basis: Cited edition = Guhyādi-aṣṭasiddhi-saṅgraha (Samdhong Rinpoche & V. Dwivedi 1987), where the Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi is one of the eight siddhi texts attributed to Ḍombīheruka (attribution traditional/debated). BUT the DSBC source_url content is actually the commentary: colophon 'iti tattvajñānasaṃsiddhau madhyamakarūcibhikṣuśrīdhyānacandra viracitāyāṁ mahāsukhaprakāśikāyāṁ' — i.e. the Mahāsukhaprakāśikā ṭīkā by the monk Dhyānacandra (Madhyamakarūci). Secondary lit (Literature about Philosophical Systems) also records a HOMONYMOUS Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi by Śūnyasamādhipāda (ed. with Vīryaśrīmitra's Marmakalikāpañjikā, Pandey 2000) — a different work.
texts.source: gretil-header(dsbc-colophon) | secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt | web:https://www.academia.edu/37966200
texts.note: Two layers conflated under one title: root (mūla) ascribed to Ḍombīheruka in the aṣṭasiddhi edition vs the ṭīkā Mahāsukhaprakāśikā by Dhyānacandra at the DSBC URL. Beware also a separate Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi by Śūnyasamādhipāda.

texts.title: Āgamakalpalatā
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text header attributes the Āgamakalpalatā (Tantric digest) to Yadunātha Cakravartī. Secondary lit (Alper, Understanding Mantras) attests Yadunātha Cakravartin as author of the Mantraratnākara, a Tantric nibandha author of the same milieu, supporting the attribution.
texts.note: Tantric digest (nibandha) ascribed to Yadunātha Cakravartī; consistent with his Mantraratnākara. Confidence medium — limited independent confirmation specifically for Āgamakalpalatā.

texts.title: Āṅgirasasmṛti
texts.primary_author: pseudepigraphic — ascribed to the sage Aṅgiras (actually an anonymous dharmaśāstra compilation)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Krishna Aiyangar ed., Adyar 1953, input Hellwig): text opens with frame narrative — sages approach the sage Aṅgiras ('munimāṅgirasaṃ'), who replies 'vacmi tānakhilān dharmān' — a traditional ascription, not a historical author. Web (wisdomlib): a smṛti law-book on impurity and prāyaścitta, ascribed to the inspired lawgiver Aṅgiras; quoted by Vijñāneśvara, Mādhava, Hemādri etc., indicating an anonymous compiled text under the sage's name.
texts.note: Legendary ascription to the Vedic sage Aṅgiras; genuinely an anonymous/compiled smṛti. No historical author recoverable.

texts.title: Kāvyālaṃkārasārasaṃgraha
texts.primary_author: Udbhaṭa (kārikā/root text); Pratīhārendurāja a.k.a. Indurāja (Laghuvṛtti commentary)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: root work by Udbhaṭa (Kashmir court c.779-813), with Laghuvṛtti commentary by Pratīhārendurāja, who attributes his learning to Bhaṭṭa Mukula (attribution verse: 'pratīhārendurājena kāvyālaṃkārasaṃgrahaḥ'). Edition title itself names both. Secondary lit (Encyclopedia / alaṃkāra surveys) confirms the Udbhaṭa+Indurāja pairing.
texts.note: Root text secure to Udbhaṭa; the e-text bundles Indurāja's (Pratīhārendurāja) Laghuvṛtti commentary, a distinct later author. current_author lists only the root author and omits the commentator.

texts.title: Cakrasaṃvaratantra Vol. 2
texts.primary_author: Anonymous (scriptural tantra; traditionally revealed via the siddha Lūipa)
texts.basis: Web (Wikipedia / Gray 2012-2007): a foundational Yoginītantra with no single historical author, composed in India mid-to-late 8th c. (no later than early 9th c.); traditionally attributed/revealed to the siddha Lūipa (and Saraha). Secondary lit (Gray, Cakrasamvara article) treats it as a scripture, not an authored treatise; David B. Gray is the modern editor, not author.
texts.note: Genuinely anonymous scripture; traditional ascription to Lūipa noted but not historical. Gray is editor/translator of the cited edition.

texts.title: Kaulāvalīnirṇaya
texts.basis: Web (shivashakti.com / Avalon's Tantric Texts vol. XIV intro): a Kaula nibandha (digest) compiled by Jñānānanda Paramahaṃsa, 'about whom nothing else is known'; name not connected to any other book. Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra) cites it repeatedly as a Kaula handbook quoting many tantras (compilation, not original revelation).
texts.note: Attribution rests on the colophon/tradition only; author otherwise unknown. Role is best described as compiler of a nibandha rather than original author.

texts.title: Viṣvaksenasaṃhitā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: a Pāñcarātra saṃhitā (11 chapters on temple/ritual), framed as revelation with Viṣvaksena as speaker and Nārada as interlocutor; ed. L.N. Bhatta (Tirupati 1972). No human author named; Sansknet input source.
texts.note: Scriptural Āgama; 'authorship' is the divine/legendary speaker Viṣvaksena. Genuinely anonymous as to human composer.

texts.title: Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi with Mahāsukhaprakāśikā
texts.basis: DSBC colophons: 'tattvajñānasaṃsiddhau madhyamakarūcibhikṣuśrīdhyānacandraviracitāyāṃ mahāsukhaprakāśikāyāṃ' — the Mahāsukhaprakāśikā (vivṛti) is by Dhyānacandra, a Buddhist (Mādhyamika) monk; the work concerns Vajravārāhī. The root Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi is a separate Buddhist tantric text whose author is not given in the colophon (web/bibliographies list it without a secure author). Edition's 'Jñānacandra' is a misreading for Dhyānacandra.
texts.note: Two distinct works fused: anonymous/uncertain root Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi + Dhyānacandra's Mahāsukhaprakāśikā commentary. Edition filename 'Jñānacandra' corrected to Dhyānacandra (the commentator), who is NOT the root author.

texts.title: Cinmayadīkṣāvidhāna
texts.basis: Edition note: Muktabodha e-text transcribed from manuscript, no printed critical edition and no author stated. Title indicates a dīkṣā-vidhāna (initiation ritual manual) in the Śaiva/Śākta tantric domain. Web/secondary searches return no named author or attribution for this title.
texts.note: Ritual manual (vidhāna) of unknown authorship; no colophon attribution surfaced. Remains anonymous.

texts.title: Mahādānavidhiḥ
texts.basis: No printed critical edition; unpublished ritual paddhati MS digitized by Muktabodha. No author/editor named in catalog. ag over reference-literature/ returned no hits for the title; web returned nothing specific. Genre (dāna-vidhi paddhati) is typically anonymous compilation.
texts.note: Ritual paddhati (gift/donation procedure); no human author claimed, anonymous compilation. Muktabodha HK e-text only.

texts.title: Svacchandatantra
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (revelation; dialogue of Bhairava and Devī)
texts.basis: Davidson (Indian Esoteric Buddhism) and Sanderson (Essais sur le rituel) treat the Svacchandatantra as an anonymous revealed Bhairava scripture (Bhairava-Devī dialogue); the Uddyota commentary is by the Kashmirian exegete Kṣemarāja (pupil of Abhinavagupta). KSTS edition by Madhusudan Kaul Shastri prints tantra + Kṣemarāja's Uddyota.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Essais sur le rituel.txt; secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism.txt
texts.note: Root tantra anonymous revelation; the named human contribution is Kṣemarāja's Uddyota commentary (distinct role, but the work item is the tantra itself, so root stays anonymous).

texts.title: Tattvasaṃgrahapañcikā [Tattvasaṃgrahapañjikā]
texts.basis: Multiple sources (Nakamura, Eckel, Tripathi, Tarthang Tulku, Tillemans, Steinkellner Festschrift) consistently state the Tattvasaṃgraha (root kārikās) is by Śāntarakṣita and the Pañjikā commentary is by his pupil Kamalaśīla (both 8th c.). Krishnamacharya GOS edition prints both together.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism_Hajime Nakamura.txt; secondary:cleaned_Jnanagarbha on the Two Truths_Eckel.txt
texts.note: Title conflates two authors: mūla verses by Śāntarakṣita, pañjikā commentary by Kamalaśīla. current_author lists only Kamalaśīla (correct for the pañjikā), but the bundled mūla is Śāntarakṣita's.

texts.title: Pheṭakapratiṣṭhācaṇḍikāgnividhi
texts.basis: Unpublished Newar Caṇḍikā fire/installation ritual paddhati MS digitized by Muktabodha; no author/editor named. ag found only incidental occurrences of 'pheṭaka' (the shield attribute) in Nepalese MS catalogs (Lienhard) and the Durgatipariśodhanatantra (Skorupski), not the title; no authorship. Local Newar pratiṣṭhā-vidhi paddhatis are anonymous compilations.
texts.note: Newar installation/fire ritual paddhati (caṇḍikāgnividhi); no human author, anonymous local compilation. 'pheṭaka' = the shield iconographic attribute.
