texts.title: Dvādaśakāranayastotra
texts.basis: Edited in Pandey's Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (1994) under Nāgārjuna's name; DSBC transmits it as Nāgārjuna. Web/scholarly consensus: a large body of stotras circulates under 'Nāgārjuna' and many are later/doubtful pseudepigrapha; this short hymn on the twelve aspects (dvādaśa-kāra) of dependent origination is among the traditionally Nāgārjuna-ascribed stotras, authenticity not securely established.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Nāgārjuna; like most stotras under his name, attribution is doubtful (possibly pseudepigraphic) but conventionally retained. Confidence low.

texts.title: Kaṅkālamālinītantra
texts.basis: Listed in Encyclopedia of Tantra among revealed tantras. Web (shivashakti.com, wisdomlib): a short Kalikula-category tantra of five paṭalas whose chapter colophons ascribe it to the Dakṣiṇāmnāya (southern current); no human author named — a revealed scripture (Śiva/Bhairava–Devī dialogue).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt; web:https://shivashakti.com/kankala
texts.note: Anonymous revealed tantra; colophons ascribe it to Dakṣiṇāmnāya, no individual author. Relatively late (post-classical) Śākta-Kaula compilation.

texts.title: Avalokiteśvarastotra
texts.basis: Edition (Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha) ascribes the hymn to Carpaṭipāda. Secondary lit confirms Carpaṭi as one of the 84 mahāsiddhas (Moonbeams of Mahāmudrā / Tāranātha) and explicitly names 'Carpati's Avalokiteśvarastotra' (Dorji Wangchuk, Cross-Cultural transmission of Buddhist texts).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Dorji Wangchuk_Cross-Cultural transmission of Buddhist texts; secondary:cleaned_Moonbeams of Mahamudra
texts.note: Traditional ascription to the siddha Carpaṭi/Carpaṭipāda; a single-author stotra, no commentary-conflation.

texts.title: Bṛhadyamasmṛti
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to the sage Yama)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: no human author named; an alternative 182-verse version of the Yamasmṛti, colophons read 'śrīyāmye dharmaśāstre' and the speaker is bhagavān Yamaḥ. Secondary lit (Yajneshwara Shastri) treats the Yamasmṛti as a dharmaśāstra dated 6th-7th c. CE with no historical author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Traverses on Less Trodden Path of Indian Philosophy and Religion
texts.note: Smṛti attributed to the divine sage Yama (legendary). No real author; traditional ascription noted, status anonymous.

texts.title: Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpikā [Sāṃkhyārthatattvapradīpikā]
texts.basis: GRETIL header and the text itself name the author: 'bhaṭṭakeśavasambhūtasadānandātmajaḥ sudhīḥ' (Keśava, son of Sadānanda). Colophon title Sāṃkhyārthatattvapradīpikā; closing namaskāra to Kapila is to the system's founder, not the author.
texts.note: Self-identified author Bhaṭṭa Keśava in the text; secure single-author work (a Sāṃkhya manual in the Vijñānabhikṣu tradition).

texts.title: Tripurārahasya, Māhātmyakhaṇḍa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Haritāyana Saṃhitā; traditionally revealed Dattātreya → Paraśurāma → Haritāyana)
texts.basis: Web (Wikipedia; aghori.it PDF) and skt-en-mono: the Tripurārahasya = Haritāyana Saṃhitā, framed as taught by Dattātreya (Viṣṇu avatāra) to Paraśurāma to Haritāyana, who is the legendary author/compiler. No historical author; medieval Śākta tantric text (11th-17th c.). Māhātmyakhaṇḍa is one of three khaṇḍas (~2,163 ślokas).
texts.note: Traditional/pseudepigraphic frame: revelation chain Dattātreya>Paraśurāma>Haritāyana; Haritāyana the legendary compiler. Effectively anonymous medieval Śākta work.

texts.title: Kṣurikā Upaniṣad
texts.basis: Edition (A. Mahādeva Śāstrī, The Yoga Upaniṣads, Adyar 1920) prints the anonymous Kṣurikā Upaniṣad together WITH the commentary of Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (Brahmayogin / Śrī Upaniṣad-Brahmendra, 18th c.) — a distinct author. Secondary lit lists Kṣurikā among the minor (Yoga) Upaniṣads with Nārāyaṇa's Dīpikā as another commentary; the mūla text is an anonymous late Vedic/yoga Upaniṣad.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy; secondary:cleaned_Origin and Development of Early Indian Contemplative Practices
texts.note: Mūla Upaniṣad anonymous; the named Adyar edition bundles the bhāṣya of Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (distinct commentator) — root vs commentary by different hands, hence conflation_flag.

texts.title: Abhisamayālaṅkāravṛttiḥ sphuṭārthā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'ācaryaharibhadrakṛtā' — the Sphuṭārthā vṛtti is by Haribhadra (his shorter commentary, vs his longer Āloka). The work expounds the Abhisamayālaṅkāra root kārikās ascribed to Ārya Maitreya (opening homage 'āryamaitreyaḥ...'). Secondary lit (Obermiller 1933 'Small Commentary of Haribhadra'; Makransky, Buddhahood Embodied) confirms.
texts.note: Haribhadra is securely the author of the vṛtti; but the edited text embeds the root Abhisamayālaṅkāra kārikās of Maitreya — distinct authors (kārikā vs vṛtti), hence conflation_flag.

texts.title: Arthaviniścayasūtra + Nibandhana
texts.current_author: sūtra anonymous; commentary (Nibandhana) by Bhikṣu Vīryaśrīdatta of Nālandā
texts.basis: Samtani's edition title (in workitem) credits the Nibandhana to Bhikṣu Vīryaśrīdatta of Śrī-Nālandāvihāra. Secondary lit confirms: Skilling (Vyākhyāyukti study) states the Nibandhana on the Arthaviniścayasūtra was 'composed by Vīryaśrīdatta at Nālandā during the reign of Dharmapāla'; Lal Mani Joshi and Cox/Collett also note the Nibandhana commentary; Samtani's 'Sectless ācāryas' studies Vīryaśrīdatta's commentary. The sūtra itself is an anonymous canonical Buddhist text.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Skilling_Vasubandhu Vyākhyāyukti.txt; secondary:cleaned_Studies in the Buddhistic Culture of India_Lal Mani Josh.txt
texts.note: Two distinct authorial layers: anonymous root sūtra vs. Nibandhana by Vīryaśrīdatta of Nālandā (fl. reign of Dharmapāla, c. late 8th–early 9th c.). Title conflates both.

texts.title: Adhyātmasāraśataka + ṭīkā
texts.basis: Workitem id ('Prabhākaragupta-Śiromaṇi ... Adhyātmasāraśatakaṭīkā') and Potter's Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies (Vol. 1 bibliography) list 'Adhyātmasāraśataka (Prabhākaragupta)' as the root work; the title 'Adhyātmasāraśatakaṭīkā' is a commentary (ṭīkā), here ascribed to Śiromaṇi. Published in Dhīḥ (Rare Buddhist Texts Project, CIHTS Sarnath, 1998). Karṇāṭaka Vīraśaiva author Siddharāma Śivayogin also wrote a 'Commentary on Prabhakaragupta's Adhyatmasarasataka' per the Indian-philosophy bibliography, so multiple commentarial traditions exist.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 1 - Potter.txt; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Workitem had blank title/author. Root verses (śataka) by Prabhākaragupta; the published ṭīkā ascribed to Śiromaṇi. Distinct from the 9th-c. Buddhist logician Prabhākaragupta; identity uncertain hence medium confidence.

texts.title: ūrdhvāmnāya damanārohaṇapaddhati
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text of an unpublished Newar/ūrdhvāmnāya ritual paddhati manuscript; no critical edition and no named author. Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra; White, Tantra in Practice) attests the ūrdhvāmnāya/ūrdhvāmnāya-saṃhitā tantric stream but names no author for this damanārohaṇa paddhati. Ritual paddhatis of this kind are characteristically anonymous compilations.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt; tradition
texts.note: Anonymous ūrdhvāmnāya ritual manual (damanārohaṇa / damanaka-flower offering). No personal attribution; traditional Newar/ūrdhvāmnāya transmission only.

texts.title: Avalokiteśvarāṣṭakastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL e-text (BuSto 12, from Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha): header and colophon name no author. Colophon reads only 'śrī āryāvalokiteśvarabhaṭṭārakasya ślokāṣṭakaṃ samāptam' — an eight-verse praise of Avalokiteśvara with no composer named. Web search likewise yields no scholarly attribution.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist aṣṭaka-stotra to Avalokiteśvara; colophon gives only the deity, no author.

texts.title: Hāratīstotra
texts.basis: DSBC e-text colophon: 'iti śrī-bhavaratna-vinirgataṃ bālarakṣākaraṃ hāratīstotram' — the child-protecting Hāratīstotra issued from/extracted from the Bhavaratna (a source work), with no personal author named. DSBC credits only proofreaders (Miroj Shakya). No scholarly attribution found.
texts.note: Anonymous; colophon ascribes provenance to 'Śrī Bhavaratna' (likely a source text/anthology, possibly a person), but no clear individual author. Tentative anonymous.

texts.title: Saubhāgyataraṅgiṇī
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text titled Saubhāgyataraṅgiṇī, a commentary on the Saundaryalaharī (attributed to Śaṅkara), traditionally ascribed to Mukunda. Secondary lit lists the standard set of Saundaryalaharī commentaries (Lakṣmīdhara, Saubhāgyavardhanī, Aruṇāmodinī, etc.) but does not independently confirm the Saubhāgyataraṅgiṇī/Mukunda pairing; web search inconclusive. Attribution rests on the e-text/tradition.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Traverses on Less Trodden Path...Yajneshwara Shastri.txt (Saundaryalaharī commentary tradition); tradition (Muktabodha)
texts.note: Mukunda is the traditional author of this Saundaryalaharī commentary; not independently corroborated in scholarly lit, so traditional/medium. The base text Saundaryalaharī is separately ascribed (traditionally) to Śaṅkara — not part of this commentary's authorship.

texts.title: Devīrahasya (Devīrahasyatantra)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (compiled by an unnamed Kashmiri redactor; traditionally ascribed to the Rudrayāmala)
texts.basis: Editor M.S. Kaul's intro (per shivashakti.com) states internal references show the compilation was made by a Kashmiri writer, long after Muslim/European presence in India; text self-ascribes to the Rudrayāmala (a frame/pseudepigraphic claim, considered questionable). Encyclopedia of Tantra cites it only as a source work, no author named.
texts.source: web:https://shivashakti.com/rahasya; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt
texts.note: Scripture framed as Śiva–Pārvatī dialogue; claims to derive from Rudrayāmala. Late Kashmiri compilation. No individual author; traditional ascription to Rudrayāmala noted separately.

texts.title: Śaktisaṃgamatantra, Sundarīkhaṇḍa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (late Tantric compilation; ed. Benoytosh Bhattacharyya 1947)
texts.basis: Śaktisaṃgamatantra is an anonymous late Tantric scripture; Sundarīkhaṇḍa (vol. III) ed. Benoytosh Bhattacharyya, GOS 104, Baroda 1947. Encyclopedia of Tantra cites the Śaktisaṃgama as a source text without naming an author. No personal authorship attested.
texts.note: One of four khaṇḍas of the Śaktisaṃgamatantra. Bhattacharyya is editor, not author. Anonymous scriptural compilation.

texts.title: Svacchandatantra [Svacchandabhairavatantra]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Bhairava/Mantramārga scripture); Kṣemarāja (Svacchandoddyota commentary, not in this mūla e-text)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (svact_iu.htm) states this is MULA TEXT ONLY, transcribed by EFEO Pondicherry Saiva group (Goodall, Yang, Rout, Sathyanarayanan, Sarma, 2004); NO mention of Kṣemarāja's commentary in this file. Secondary lit (Sanderson 'Essais sur le rituel'; Davidson) confirms the scripture is anonymous revealed Bhairava-tantra and Kṣemarāja wrote the Svacchandoddyota commentary (separate text). The KSTS edition in the edition field includes Kṣemarāja's udyota, but the GRETIL transcript is the root text alone.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Essais sur le rituel.txt; secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism.txt
texts.note: Root tantra anonymous (revealed scripture). Kṣemarāja (11th c.) authored the Svacchandoddyota commentary — listed for completeness since the named KSTS edition pairs them, but the e-text is mūla only; not a conflation within this transcript.

texts.title: Sāṃkhyatattvavivecana
texts.primary_author: Ṣimānanda Dīkṣita (= 'Kṣemānanda' in some bibliographies; single author)
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon names the author unambiguously: 'iti kānyakubjaśrīṣimānandadīkṣitaviracitaṃ sāṃkhyatattvavivecanaṃ samāptam'; opening verse: 'raghunandanasutenedam... kānyakubjadvijāgryeṇa ṣimānandena tanyate' (son of Raghunandana, brahmin of Kanyakubja/Kannauj). Secondary lit (Literature about Philosophical Systems; Potter EIP) lists the same work as 'Kṣemānanda's Sāṃkhyatattvavivecana' on the Tattvasamāsa — variant reading of one and the same author, not two persons.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Single author; a commentary (vivecana) on the Tattvasamāsa. Name read as Ṣimānanda Dīkṣita in the colophon vs 'Kṣemānanda' in bibliographies; 'Kṣemendra' is a further mis-reading. No conflation of multiple authors — only an orthographic/transmission variant of one name.

texts.title: Siddhalakṣmīpūjāpaddhati
texts.basis: Muktabodha manuscript-based e-text, no printed edition, no named author. Secondary lit (Sanderson, Śaivism and the Tantric Traditions; Hindu Tantric Chaplains; Tantra in Practice) discusses the goddess Siddhalakṣmī/Siddhilakṣmī (Kālīkula, Northern Transmission, Nepal) but identifies no author for any pūjāpaddhati. Genre = anonymous ritual manual.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_Śaivism and the tantric traditions.txt; secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_Hindu tantric chaplains.txt
texts.note: Anonymous worship manual (paddhati) of the goddess Siddhalakṣmī (Siddhilakṣmī in Nepal). No author or compiler named in tradition or scholarship.

texts.title: Mahālakṣmīpūjāvidhiḥ
texts.primary_author: anonymous (ritual manual for the worship of Mahālakṣmī)
texts.basis: Muktabodha on-line Digital Library e-text from an unpublished pūjā paddhati manuscript; no printed critical edition and no named author. No attribution found in secondary literature (no hits for Mahālakṣmīpūjā in reference-literature). Genre = anonymous ritual procedure (vidhi).
texts.note: Anonymous pūjā-vidhi for Mahālakṣmī. Such ritual manuals are typically uncredited liturgical compilations; no author identifiable.

texts.title: Aśeṣakulavallarī
texts.basis: shivashakti.com (Mike Magee site) hosts the e-text/translation; site is run and translated by Mike Magee (© 1975-2022), text ascribed to Gaṇananāthabhaṭṭa. No secondary-lit or scholarly web corroboration found for the title or author; obscure tantric/MB work.
texts.note: Single ascribed author Gaṇananāthabhaṭṭa retained; modern English translation by Mike Magee noted separately as translator role. Attribution not independently verifiable hence 'traditional'.

texts.title: Raghunandanaprasāda Śukla: Dīkṣāprakāśa
texts.basis: Title itself names the author (Raghunandanaprasāda Śukla: Dīkṣāprakāśa); hosted/translated on shivashakti.com by Mike Magee. NOT the famous 16th-c. Bengali smārta Raghunandana (Wikipedia confirms that is a different person, author of Smṛti digests/Dāyabhāga). No secondary-lit confirmation found.
texts.note: Author retained as given. Distinct from the celebrated Raghunandana Bhaṭṭācārya of Bengal. Mike Magee = translator. Single author, no conflation.

texts.title: Śrīguhyasamājatantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (root tantra); traditionally ascribed to the Buddha; exegetical Ārya tradition ascribed to Nāgārjuna (disputed)
texts.basis: Snellgrove (Indo-Tibetan Buddhism, p.6231) discusses the contested authorship of the Guhyasamāja Tantra and Bhattacharyya's retreat from rash early views. Web/Wikipedia & Grokipedia: root tantra anonymous, composed in India by early 8th c. CE (Amoghavajra ref. mid-8th c.); traditionally taught by the Buddha in esoteric form; closing verse/Tibetan colophon ascribe to Nāgārjuna, but his tantric authorship is debated and chronologically impossible for a c.150-250 CE figure.
texts.note: Root tantra genuinely anonymous; Buddha-ascription is doctrinal framing, Nāgārjuna-ascription pseudepigraphic/legendary (linked to the 'second/tantric Nāgārjuna' problem). Kept anonymous; conflation_flag false (single root-tantra layer, not a multi-author edition).

texts.title: Kāvyālaṅkārasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'pāṇḍitavaraśrīvāmanaviracitāni kāvyālaṅkārasūtrāṇi' with svavṛtti by Vāmana himself; the edition annotations/ṭippaṇī by Nārāyaṇarāma Ācārya Kāvyatīrtha (modern editor). Standard scholarship: the Kāvyālaṅkārasūtra and its vṛtti are both by Vāmana (c. 8th c.), a single-author work.
texts.note: Sūtras AND self-commentary (svavṛtti) both by Vāmana — one author, not a conflation. Modern editor Nārāyaṇarāma noted as redactor of the printed edition only.

texts.title: Gaṇapatihṛdaya-Dhāraṇī
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives only edition (Iwamoto, Kleinere Dhāraṇī Texte, 1937) and digital input by Klaus Wille; colophon 'āryamahāgaṇapatihṛdayaṃ samāptam' names no author. Dhāraṇīs are anonymous canonical Buddhist genre, traditionally Buddha-word.
texts.note: No personal author; colophon only marks completion. Buddhavacana framing. Kept anonymous.

texts.title: Mahāsubhāṣitasaṅgraha
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (modern critical compilation ed. by L. Sternbach and others)
texts.primary_author: Ludwik Sternbach et al. (modern compiler/editor); verses anonymous from many authors
texts.basis: Edition info + secondary lit (Hahn, Prajñādaṇḍa; Dimitrov) reference Sternbach's Mahā-subhāṣita-saṃgraha (8 vols, VVRI Hoshiarpur 1974-2007) as a modern critical anthology of Sanskrit gnomic verses drawn from innumerable sources/authors. GRETIL URL 404 at fetch time, but edition metadata is unambiguous.
texts.note: Modern anthology: thousands of subhāṣitas by countless (mostly anonymous/various) authors, compiled by Ludwik Sternbach. Not a single-author original work; verses are an aggregate rather than a conflation of a fixed set of named authors, so conflation_flag=false but compiler role recorded.

texts.title: Parameśvarīmatatantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śaiva/Śākta-Kubjikā scripture, ascribed to Śiva/Bhairava)
texts.basis: Muktabodha MS-based e-text gives no human author. Web/secondary: Parameśvarīmata(tantra) is listed among the independent Kubjikā tantras (Goudriaan-Schoterman corpus); like other āgamas it is a revealed scripture without a named historical author. Distinct from the Śaiva-Siddhānta Pārameśvaratantra.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Kubjikā-tradition tantra; traditional ascription to Śiva/Bhairava only. Not to be confused with Pārameśvaratantra (Śaiva Siddhānta).

texts.title: Mālinīvijayottaratantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Trika scripture); ed. Somadeva Vasudeva / Madhusudan Kaul Shastri
texts.basis: GRETIL header: input/critical edition by Somadeva Vasudeva (Oxford 2000; Pondicherry c.2004) for adhikāras 1-4,7,11-17 and M.K. Shastri (KSTS 37, Bombay 1922) for the rest; text self-identifies as śrīmālinīvijayottaratantram with no human author. One of the five core Trika tantras (revealed by Śiva).
texts.note: Editors (Vasudeva, Kaul Shastri) are not authors; recorded with role 'translator/editor'. Mūla is anonymous āgama.

texts.title: Mālinīvijayottaratantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Trika scripture); ed. Madhusūdan Kaul Śāstrī (KSTS 37, 1922)
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text of the KSTS 37 (Kaul Śāstrī, Bombay/Srinagar 1922) edition. Same anonymous revealed Trika scripture as SA_GR13_malinivu; no named human author. Editor Kaul Śāstrī recorded as editor only.
texts.note: Duplicate title (Muktabodha encoding) of the Mālinīvijayottara; anonymous āgama. Kaul Śāstrī = editor, not author.

texts.title: Nigamasāranirṇaya
texts.basis: Muktabodha catalogue ascribes the work to Rāmāramaṇadeva; no critical edition (edition: null). No corroborating scholarly statement found in local reference-literature or web searches. Title (a 'nirṇaya' / determination of the essence of the nigamas) is a nibandha-type digest, so a named human author is plausible but the ascription remains unconfirmed.
texts.note: Carries over the catalogue/traditional ascription to Rāmāramaṇadeva; confidence low, unverified by secondary literature.

texts.title: Kriyākālaguṇottara
texts.basis: Slouber (Early Tantric Medicine, OUP 2017) critically edits chs. 1-7, 30-31, 35 from Nepalese MSS; describes the text as a c.10th-c. Gāruḍa Tantra drawing on older Gāruḍa and Bhūta tantras, i.e. a composite, anonymously redacted revealed scripture with no named author. Muktabodha e-text from NGMCP 3-392.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Gāruḍa-tantra of composite/layered origin (multiple anonymous redactors); Slouber is editor/translator, not author. Not flagged conflated as the distinct contributors are not separately named persons.

texts.title: Parātriṃśikā
texts.primary_author: anonymous (verses = final portion of the Rudrayāmala, revealed Trika scripture)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (input Somadeva Vasudeva 1998): mūla extracted from the Parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa edition (Mukunda Rāma Śāstrī, KSTS 18, 1918). Secondary/web: the Parātriṃśikā (a.k.a. Parātrīśikā) verses are the last portion of the Rudrayāmala — anonymous revealed scripture; the celebrated Vivaraṇa COMMENTARY on them is by Abhinavagupta. This item is the root verses, hence anonymous; Abhinavagupta listed for the commentary role to keep the distinction.
texts.note: Root verses are anonymous scripture (part of Rudrayāmala); do NOT attribute the mūla to Abhinavagupta — he authored only the Vivaraṇa. Conflation risk noted but item = verses only, so flag false.

texts.title: māheśvaratantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śaiva tantra; framed as Śiva–Devī/Kārttikeya dialogue)
texts.basis: Edition (Khemraj Shrikrishnadass / Venkateshvara Press, Bombay) presents it as a revealed āgama/tantra with no human author; secondary lit (Mirror of Beryl) references 'Maheśvara tantras' only as a scriptural class, no named composer. Distinct from any single author.
texts.source: tradition; secondary:cleaned_Mirror of Beryl final.txt; web:shivashakti.com/bibby
texts.note: Revealed tantra; no human author. Traditional framing only (Śiva as revealer). Not the Buddhist Tibetan 'Maheśvara tantra' class.

texts.title: Bhūtaśuddhitantra
texts.basis: Encyclopedia of Tantra (Santideva) treats Bhūtaśuddhi as a ritual concept (purification of the five elements) and lists 'Bhutasuddhi-tantra' among revealed Tantra titles with no named author; no human authorship anywhere.
texts.source: tradition; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt
texts.note: Revealed tantra; anonymous. Title also denotes the bhūtaśuddhi ritual; no individual composer attested.

texts.title: Vijñānabhairava
texts.basis: GRETIL header (encoded by M. Faliero, 1998) gives only the bare root text as a Śrī Devī–Bhairava dialogue, no author/commentary; secondary lit (Padoux, Essais sur le rituel) confirms it is a revealed Trika tantra. This GRETIL file is the MULA text only, without the Kṣemarāja/Śivopādhyāya commentary.
texts.note: Root tantra only in this e-text; anonymous revealed work. Commentary (conflated authorship) is in the separate MB edition record, not here.

texts.title: Yogaśataka
texts.current_author: anonymous; traditionally attributed to Nāgārjuna (Nāgārjunīya school)
texts.primary_author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (also to Vararuci); actually anonymous compiler of the Nāgārjunīya tradition
texts.basis: Walser (Nāgārjuna in Context): work ascribed to 'a Nāgārjuna' but does not claim same author as the Mūlamadhyamaka; datable 7th–9th c. Wallace: some Indian MSS ascribe it to Vararuci instead of Nāgārjuna; Tengyur ascribes to Nāgārjuna. Filliozat (IFP ed.): compiler in a Nāgārjunīya milieu, not the philosopher.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Walser_Nāgārjuna in context.txt; secondary:cleaned_Wallace_Medicine and Astrology...txt; web:ifpindia.org/bookstore/pifi62
texts.note: Disputed Nāgārjuna vs Vararuci; pseudepigraphic use of 'Nāgārjuna' name. Single (unknown) compiler, not multi-author — no conflation.

texts.title: Mahogratārāṣṭakastotra
texts.basis: DSBC e-text (input/proofed by Miroj Shakya) gives no author; it is a devotional aṣṭaka-stotra to Mahogratārā. Secondary lit attests the deity Ugratārā/Mahogratārā and a Mahogratārākalpa class but names no composer for this stotra.
texts.source: dsbc; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt; secondary:cleaned_From Bodhgaya to Lhasa to Beijing...McKeown.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist stotra; Miroj Shakya is the e-text inputter (not author). No individual ascription found.

texts.title: Vijñānabhairava (Vijñānabhairavatantra)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (root tantra); Kṣemarāja (uddyota, vv. 1–23); Śivopādhyāya (vivṛti, completion)
texts.basis: Edition title itself (Mukunda Rāma Śāstrī, KSTS 8, 1918) = 'with commentary partly by Kṣemarāja and partly by Śivopādhyāya'. Padoux (Essais sur le rituel): Kṣemarāja's uddyota survives only on verses 1–23, completed by Śivopādhyāya's commentary. Root tantra anonymous revealed work.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Essais sur le rituel.txt; secondary:cleaned_Indian Epistemology and Metaphysics_Tuske 2017.txt; web (KSTS edition title)
texts.note: MULTI-AUTHOR: anonymous root tantra + Kṣemarāja uddyota (vv.1–23) + Śivopādhyāya completing the vivṛti. Distinct from the bare GRETIL root-only e-text (SA_GSP30_vijnbhau).

texts.title: Jñānasiddhi
texts.basis: Secondary lit unanimously names Indrabhūti: Snellgrove (Indo-Tibetan Buddhism) 'Indrabhūti's Jñānasiddhi'; Davidson, Guenther, Josh; ed. Bhattacharyya 'Two Vajrayana Works' (Gaekwad's OS 44). Davidson contrasts a Sahajasiddhi 'ascribed to Indrabhūti' vs Dombi's Śrīsahajasiddhi, so attributions are tracked carefully. One outlier (Thinley) confuses it with Lakṣmīṅkarā; mainstream rejects this.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Snellgrove_Indo-Tibetan Buddhism.txt; secondary:cleaned_Studies in the Buddhistic Culture of India_Lal Mani Josh.txt
texts.note: Traditional ascription to the siddha-king Indrabhūti of Uḍḍiyāna; complicated by the well-known multiplicity of 'Indrabhūti' figures in the lineage tradition, hence 'traditional' rather than 'secure'.

texts.title: Śrīguhyasamājamaṇḍalavidhi
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (such maṇḍalavidhis ascribed to authors of the Ārya school, e.g. Dīpaṃkarabhadra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Dīpaṃkarabhadra, Guhyasamājamaṇḍalavidhi'; text opens naming Dīpaṃkarabhadra as author. Secondary: Encyclopaedia of Tantra vol.4 'Dipamkarabhadra has written a work well-known in the Guhyasamaja tradition (the Guhyasamajamandala-vidhi)'; Wayman (Yoga of the Guhyasamājatantra) credits Dīpaṅkarabhadra with the key Generation-Stage ritual work; Wedemeyer notes A-myes treats it as authoritative.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopaedia of Tantra. Vol 4_Sadhu Santidev 1999.txt
texts.note: Edition is specifically Dīpaṃkarabhadra's Guhyasamājamaṇḍalavidhi; current_author hedged it as anonymous/traditional, but both e-text header and secondary lit secure the named author.

texts.title: Mṛgendrāgama [Mṛgendratantra]: 1. Vidyāpāda
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śaiva āgama; commentary by Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: text names no human author, presented as revealed Śaiva scripture (śruti); commentary by Bhaṭṭa-Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha (ed. N.R. Bhatt, IFI Pondicherry 1962; Kashmir ed. Madhusudan Kaul Shastri 1930). This item is the Vidyāpāda mūla only.
texts.note: Mūla āgama is anonymous/revealed; the named commentator Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha is a separate role (his commentary accompanies the edition but is not the mūla). No conflation within the mūla itself.

texts.title: Kuṭṭanīmata
texts.basis: GRETIL header / Dezső & Goodall ed. 2012: 'Dāmodaraguptaviracitaṃ Kuṭṭanīmatam', an 8th-c. verse novel by Dāmodaragupta (minister of Jayāpīḍa of Kashmir per Rājataraṅgiṇī tradition).
texts.note: Single-author kāvya; authorship secure and uncontested.

texts.title: Pramāṇavārttikavṛtti Commentary [Pramāṇavārttikavṛttiṭīkā]
texts.primary_author: Karṇakagomin (ṭīkā); commenting on Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttika (kārikā) + svopajñavṛtti
texts.basis: Edition (Sāṅkṛtyāyana 1943) bundles three textual layers: Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttika svārthānumāna kārikās + Dharmakīrti's own svopajña(sva)vṛtti + Karṇakagomin's ṭīkā on the vṛtti. Secondary: Wayman (Millennium of Buddhist Logic) 'Karṇakagomin's Commentary on the Pramāṇavārttikavṛtti of Dharmakīrti'; Kajiyama, Horin (8th c.) confirm Karṇakagomin's Pramāṇavārttikasvavṛttiṭīkā is a sub-commentary on the chapter on inference-for-oneself.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Millennium of Buddhist Logic. Vol1_Alex Wayman.txt; secondary:cleaned_Horin...Buddhist Logic_Talmo Aoyama (ed) (2004)_(A).txt
texts.note: Title names Karṇakagomin (correct for the ṭīkā layer), but the work as edited conflates Dharmakīrti's root kārikās and his own svavṛtti with Karṇakagomin's sub-commentary — three distinct textual roles, two distinct authors.

texts.title: Śrīsahajasiddhi
texts.basis: Davidson (Hevajra's Abhisamaya): the canonical Śrīsahajasiddhi (To. 2223), ed./tr. Malati Shendge IIJ 10 (1967), is Ḍombiheruka's; distinct from the Sahajasiddhi 'ascribed to Indrabhūti'. Stearns (Taking the Result as the Path): Śrīsahajasiddhi by Virūpa's disciple Ḍombi Heruka. Davidson (Tibetan Renaissance), Kapstein, Dorji Wangchuk concur. Note a separate Tibetan redaction (gDams-ngag-mdzod) by Grags-pa rgyal-mtshan is 'substantially different' from this canonical Sanskrit text.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Hevajra's Abhisamaya and tshogs bshad.txt; secondary:cleaned_Taking the Result as the Path...Cyrus Stearns (2006).txt
texts.note: Traditional ascription to the siddha Ḍombī Heruka; matches current_author. Distinguish from the Sahajasiddhi attributed to Indrabhūti and from the later Sa-skya redaction of a same-named work.

texts.title: Kāllottara (Kālottara)
texts.basis: GRETIL: no source_url for this MB/Muktabodha e-text. Secondary (Sanderson, History through textual criticism): the Kālottara is an anonymous Saiddhāntika revealed Tantra (āgama), current in the 9th c., cited by Padmavajra's Guhyasiddhi and grouped with the Niśvāsa as the source-scriptures of the Siddhānta. It survives in several recensions of differing length (Sārdhatriśatika- 350, Trayodaśaśatika- 1300, Bṛhatkālottara) — i.e. a recensional tradition, not a single named author. Title spelled Kāllottara/Kālottara.
texts.note: Anonymous Śaiva Siddhānta scripture; multiple recensions (Sārdhatriśatika/Trayodaśaśatika/Bṛhat-Kālottara). No human author; traditionally Śiva's revelation.

texts.title: Timirodghāṭana
texts.basis: GRETIL header (source_url): 'Input by Somadeva Vasudeva' — editor/transcriber only, not author; no original author named; no human-author colophon. The text is an early Śaiva scripture quoted by Abhinavagupta and Kṣemarāja, hence pre-11th c., transmitted in a single Kathmandu palm-leaf MS (NGMPP A35/3). No mention in local secondary literature (no hits).
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Śaiva Tantra; S. Vasudeva is the modern transcriber/editor of the unedited Kathmandu MS, not the author.

texts.title: Nirvāṇatantra
texts.basis: source_url is a Muktabodha digital-library landing page (no header). Secondary: N. N. Bhattacharyya, History of the Tantric Religion, cites the Nirvāṇatantra repeatedly as a Śākta/Kaula āgama (on ācāras, cakras, vīra) with no named author; it is a relatively late anonymous revealed Tantra (framed as Śiva–Śakti dialogue). Distinct from the Mahānirvāṇatantra.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion An Historical, Ritualistic, and Philosophical Study_N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Śākta/Kaula revealed Tantra; do not confuse with Mahānirvāṇatantra. No human author.

texts.title: Kāvyālaṅkārasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (source_url): 'pāṇḍitavaraśrīvāmanaviracitasavṛtti-kāvyālaṅkārasūtrāṇi' — the sūtras WITH Vāmana's own vṛtti, both by Vāmana; this e-text contains only Vāmana's sūtra+vṛtti, NOT Gopendra Tripurahara Bhūpāla's Kāvyālaṅkārakāmadhenu commentary (so no conflation). Web (wisdomlib/Wikipedia): attribution secure, Vāmana fl. later 8th c. (refs Bhavabhūti; cited by Rājaśekhara who names a Vāmanīya school). Local (Dimitrov) lists Vāmana as the standard author.
texts.note: Securely Vāmana; sūtra + auto-vṛtti both his. Gopendra's Kāmadhenu ṭīkā is NOT in this e-text, so no multi-author conflation here.

texts.title: Ādyanātha: Anuttaraprakāśapañcāśikā
texts.current_author: attributed to Ādyanātha (Ādinātha, the primordial Śiva-teacher; traditional/legendary attribution)
texts.primary_author: ascribed to Ādyanātha (Ādinātha, legendary primordial Śiva-teacher); true author unknown
texts.basis: source_url is a Muktabodha catalog page (no header). The title itself ascribes the work to Ādyanātha = Ādinātha, the legendary primordial Śiva-teacher of the Nātha/Kaula lineage — a divinized founder name, not a datable historical author. Web/local searches return no historical author; 'anuttaraprakāśa' (the supreme light of consciousness) is standard Kashmiri Śaiva/Trika vocabulary (cf. Padoux, Vāc, on anuttaraprakāśa). Best read as a pseudepigraphic ascription to the lineage founder.
texts.note: Ascribed to Ādyanātha/Ādinātha (legendary primordial guru) — pseudepigraphic; real author unknown. Trika/Kaula short hymn (pañcāśikā = 50 verses).

texts.title: Brahma-Purāṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (source_url): 'Input by Peter Schreiner and Renate Söhnen-Thieme (Tübingen Purana Project)' — editors only; the text uses the traditional narrator frame ('lomaharṣaṇa uvāca'). As with all Mahāpurāṇas, scholarship treats it as an anonymous, composite, multi-layered compilation accreted over centuries; traditional ascription to Vyāsa is legendary, not historical. No single author.
texts.note: Composite anonymous Mahāpurāṇa; Vyāsa ascription is traditional/legendary. Schreiner & Söhnen-Thieme are modern editors, not authors.

texts.title: Harṣacarita: Pariśiṣṭa
texts.primary_author: various poets/anthologists (Bāṇa-praśastayaḥ, appended verses); NOT Bāṇa

authors.name: various authors of praise-verses (incl. Candradeva; Ruyyaka in Vyaktiviveka-vyākhyāna; etc.)

texts.basis: GRETIL header: this Pariśiṣṭa is a collection of 'Bāṇa-praśastayaḥ' (laudatory verses ABOUT Bāṇa), explicitly ascribing individual verses to multiple figures (Śrīcandradeva, Ruyyaka's Vyaktiviveka-vyākhyāna, etc.); it is not composed by Bāṇa. Web (wisdomlib/Banabhatta studies): Bāṇa wrote only Harṣacarita+Kādambarī; Ruyyaka's Harṣacarita-vārttika is a separate lost work. So the supplement conflates many verse-authors collected by the modern editor.
texts.note: Pariśiṣṭa = appended praise-verses on Bāṇa by diverse authors (Candradeva, Ruyyaka, etc.), compiled in the edition; not authored by Bāṇa himself. Multi-author conflation under the Harṣacarita title.

texts.title: Tārārahasyavṛttikā
texts.basis: MS source (Raghunath Temple, Jammu, Devanagari Tantra 4965) titles it Tārārahasyavṛtti of Śaṅkara. Secondary: LVP (Bouddhisme, études et matériaux p.5230) cites a Tārārahasyavṛtti drawing on the Ekavīratantra; Mallinson (Khecarī-Vidyā) lists the Tārārahasyavṛtti among Tārā tantric sources; Encyclopedia of Tantra lists 'Tārārahasya-vṛtti'. Distinct from the Tārārahasya of Brahmānanda(giri). Web found no contradicting attribution.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_LVP_Bouddhisme_études et matériaux.txt; secondary:cleaned_Khecari-Vidya (Hatha Yoga) _James Mallinson, 2003.txt
texts.note: Vṛtti (commentary/gloss) on a Tārā tantra; ascribed to Śaṅkara Āgamācārya of Bengal. Do not confuse with Brahmānanda's Tārārahasya.

texts.title: Guhyasamājamaṇḍalavidhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Dīpaṃkarabhadra as author (ed. Klein-Schwind/Isaacson, Hamburg). Secondary: Encyclopaedia of Tantra and Yarnall (Emptiness that is Form) repeatedly reference Dīpaṃkarabhadra's Guhyasamājamaṇḍalavidhi = 'The Four Hundred and Fifty [Verses]', with Śāntipā's ṭīkā (Toh.1871) as a SEPARATE commentary on it.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Emptiness that is Form...Thomas Freeman Yarnall.txt
texts.note: Root mūla work (~450 verses) by Dīpaṃkarabhadra; Śāntipā's ṭīkā is a distinct work, not part of this file.

texts.title: Ucchiṣṭacāṇḍālinīkalpa
texts.basis: No edition/author in workitem; muktabodha digital library MS. Secondary: N.N. Bhattacharyya (History of the Tantric Religion) and White (Tantra in Practice) treat Ucchiṣṭacāṇḍālinī (a form of Mātaṅgī) as a tantric goddess; kalpa ritual texts of this class are transmitted without named human authors (revealed/āgamic).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion..._N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt; secondary:cleaned_Tantra in Practice_David Gordon White, ed, 2000_a.txt
texts.note: Anonymous tantric kalpa of goddess Ucchiṣṭacāṇḍālinī/Mātaṅgī; no traditional named author located.

texts.title: Mahāvyutpatti
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Tibetan imperial translation committee, c. 814 CE)

authors.name: imperially appointed translation committee (incl. Jinamitra, et al.)

texts.basis: Secondary: Karmay & Nagano (New Horizons in Bon Studies) date the Mahāvyutpatti compilation to 814; Shakabpa names Jinamitra as one of the compilers; Pagel (Bodhisattvapiṭaka) and D'Amato describe it as a committee compilation/lexicon for translators. No single author; a corporate/imperial work.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Karmay and Nagano_New Horizons in Bon Studies.txt; secondary:cleaned_Shakabpa_One Hundred Thousand Moons_v1.txt
texts.note: Sanskrit-Tibetan lexicon compiled by a royally appointed committee of translators (~814 CE); no individual author. Committee members (Jinamitra etc.) function as compilers/translators.

texts.title: Kalyāṇakāraka
texts.basis: Edition (Shastri 1940) titles it Kalyāṇakārakam of Ugrādityācārya. Secondary: Dan Martin's bibliography identifies Ugrāditya as a 9th-century Jain physician and medical commentator. Consistent across sources.
texts.note: Jain Ayurvedic treatise by Ugrāditya, 9th-c. Jain physician; attribution secure.

texts.title: Siddhilakṣmīkoṭyāhutidinakṛtya
texts.basis: Source is a Newar manuscript-catalogue digital library (muktalib7), not an authored edition. Title is a ritual prescription (dinakṛtya = daily rite) for the koṭyāhuti (ten-million oblation) to Siddhilakṣmī, a Newar Sarvāmnāya goddess; such paddhati/karmakāṇḍa digests are compiled anonymously in the Newar ritual milieu. No author surfaced in secondary literature or web.
texts.note: Anonymous Newar ritual digest (paddhati genre); no named author. Current attribution (anonymous/traditional, Newar Sarvāmnāya) retained.

texts.title: Kāṇhapādasya dohākoṣa
texts.primary_author: Kāṇha / Kṛṣṇacaryāpāda (dohā root verses); anonymous (surviving Sanskrit commentary)
texts.basis: Edition (Shahidullah 1928) ascribes the Apabhraṃśa dohā couplets to Kāṇha/Kṛṣṇacaryāpāda (mahāsiddha, ca. 8th–9th c.). Davidson (Indian Esoteric Buddhism, bibliography s.v. 'Kānhapādasya dohākoṣa') and Bagchi 1935 note the dohās survive WITH an anonymous Sanskrit commentary; web (Kṛṣṇacaryāpādasya Dohākoṣaṭīke study) notes named commentaries also exist (Amṛtavajra's Dohākoṣaṭīkā, 12th c.; Mekhalāṭīkā). Root verses and commentary are by different hands.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
texts.note: Root dohās by Kāṇha; surviving Sanskrit commentary anonymous (named ṭīkās: Amṛtavajra, Mekhalā). Conflates author of verses + commentator.

texts.title: Caryāsaṃgraha
texts.primary_author: multiple mahāsiddhas (caryāgīti anthology); no single author

authors.name: various mahāsiddhas (Lūyipa, Kukkurīpā, Kāṇha, Saraha, et al.)

texts.basis: Title means 'collection of caryā(gīti)'; the caryāgīti corpus is an anthology of tantric performance-songs by numerous Buddhist siddhas (Lūyipa, Kukkurīpā, Kāṇha/Kanhapa, Saraha, etc.), per Kvaerne and Bagchi & Śāstrī (Caryāgīti-Koṣa or Buddhist Siddhas, 1956). Edition is the Guhyādi-Aṣṭasiddhi-saṅgraha (RBTS 1, ed. Samdhong Rinpoche & Dwivedi 1987), itself a compilation of siddha works. No single author; a compilation.
texts.note: Anthology/compilation of caryā songs by multiple named mahāsiddhas; not single-authored. Empty current_author corrected to multi-author compilation.

texts.title: Śivamahimnastava
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Brown 1965 ed.): no original author in main header, Brown is editor/translator. Appended colophon verses (Smst_Add_7) ascribe the stotra to the gandharva-king Kusumadaśana/Puṣpadanta — a legendary celestial musician, i.e. a traditional/devotional attribution embedded in the text, not a historically verifiable author.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Puṣpadanta via internal colophon (Gandharva legend); historical author unknown. Retains current traditional attribution.

texts.title: Mañjuvajramukhyākhyāna
texts.basis: Tanaka 2017/2019 (Mañjuvajra-mukhyākhyāna: A ritual manual belonging to the Jñānapāda school of the Guhyasamāja-tantra) describes it as a late ritual manual (ca. 1185 CE) of the Jñānapāda tradition centered on Mañjuvajra; Tanaka is the modern editor, not the author. No ancient author is named for the manual itself.
texts.note: Late (~1185 CE) anonymous Guhyasamāja/Jñānapāda ritual manual; Tanaka is modern editor. Empty current_author set to anonymous.

texts.title: Vijñānabhairava, with commentary Kaumadī (Kaumudī)
texts.current_author: root text anonymous (revealed tantra); commentary Kaumudī by Bhaṭṭa Ānanda (Ānanda Bhaṭṭa)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Vijñānabhairava tantra); Ānanda Bhaṭṭa (Kaumudī commentary)
texts.basis: Root Vijñānabhairava is a revealed Trika/Kashmir-Śaiva tantra, traditionally a portion of the Rudrayāmala — anonymous scripture (confirmed in skt-en-mono.txt: 'the Vijñānabhairava which is, on its own authority, considered to be part of the Rudrayāmala-tantra'). The Kaumudī commentary is by Ānanda Bhaṭṭa, per the KSTS ed. (Mukunda Rāma Śāstrī 1918); NCat (cited in 'Literature about the Philosophical Systems...') records Ānanda Bhaṭṭa Upādhyāya, son of Jātaveda Bhaṭṭopādhyāya. (Note: other VBh commentaries are by Kṣemarāja and Śivopādhyāya — distinct works.)
texts.note: Anonymous revealed tantra (Rudrayāmala) + Kaumudī commentary by Ānanda Bhaṭṭa; two distinct hands. Matches current attribution.

texts.title: Viṣṇusmṛti
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally revealed by Viṣṇu; single Vaiṣṇava/Kāṭhaka author per Olivelle)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. V. Krishnamacharya, Adyar 1964) frames it as the Viṣṇu-Smṛti, words placed in the mouth of Viṣṇu; no human author named. Olivelle (Law Code of Viṣṇu) and Wikipedia: composed by a single unnamed author of the Kāṭhaka branch with Vaiṣṇava/Pāñcarātra leanings, c. Kashmir 7th c.; metrical frame possibly later but largely one author, not a true composite.
texts.note: Pseudepigraphic-revelatory framing (spoken by Viṣṇu) but text genuinely anonymous; one human author per Olivelle, not multi-author. Traditional ascription to Viṣṇu noted.

texts.title: Śaktisūtra
texts.basis: No source_url/edition. Secondary lit (skt-en-mono): aphorisms quoted by Jayaratha; Gnoli identified their source as a 'Śaktisūtra' he says was written by [a named author] but gave no evidence, and the work has not been traced — scholar concludes Gnoli was perhaps mistaken. The MB-collection Śākta sūtra text is transmitted anonymously.
texts.note: Authorship unverifiable; Gnoli's putative attribution doubted in literature. Treat as anonymous Śākta sūtra-text.

texts.title: Vārāhītantra
texts.basis: No critical edition/author (edition note: only a thesis crit. ed. of chs.1-15). Secondary lit (Bhattacharyya, History of the Tantric Religion): cited as a Śākta Āgama/Tantra defining Āgama topics and listing special Āgamas — a revealed scripture, no human author. Encyclopedia of Tantra likewise treats it as anonymous tantra.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion ... N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Śākta tantra (dialogue framing); no individual author. Likely composite/accreted scripture but no distinct named authors to separate.

texts.title: Sarahapādasya dohākoṣa
texts.basis: source_url (DSBC book/258) actually resolves to a different text (Bodhicaryāvatāra) — header mismatch, unusable. Edition (Bagchi 1938; Shahidullah 1928) and secondary lit (Snellgrove, Davidson) plus web (Wikipedia/Rigpa) uniformly attribute the Dohākoṣa to Saraha, the c.8th-c. mahāsiddha; the Dohākoṣa = the 'People Dohā' of his three-cycle (People/King/Queen) corpus.
texts.note: Traditional (mahāsiddha) attribution, secure within tradition. Dohākoṣa = People Dohā; transmitted dohākoṣas often carry later commentary (e.g. Advayavajra), but this item is the root verses by Saraha. DSBC URL points to wrong book.

texts.title: Ānandatantra
texts.basis: No source_url/edition. Secondary lit (skt-en-mono, citing Sanderson): the Ānandatantra quoted by Abhinavagupta belongs to the Vāma-srotas part of the canon ('venerable Ānandatantra' quoted scripturally). A revealed tantra cited as authority, no human author named.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Śākta/Vāma-srotas tantra; cited by Abhinavagupta/Jayaratha. No individual author.

texts.title: Kākacaṇḍeśvarīmata
texts.basis: No source_url/edition and no hit in local reference-literature. Title is a -mata ('teaching of') revealed scripture in the Kākacaṇḍeśvarī Śākta/Kaula tradition; such mata-texts are transmitted as anonymous revelation (dialogue framing), with no attested human author.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Śākta tantra (-mata genre); no individual author traced. Low confidence only on completeness of search, not on anonymity.

texts.title: Mantrakoṣa
texts.basis: No source_url; no edition. Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra, Santideva) lists a 'Mantrakoṣa' but attributes it to Jagannātha Cakravartī — i.e. 'Mantrakoṣa' is a generic title borne by several distinct works. Web search found no independent confirmation of Āśāditya Tripāṭhī for this specific text. Attribution rests only on the supplied current_author.
texts.note: Title 'Mantrakoṣa' is shared by multiple distinct authors (e.g. a Mantrakoṣa ascribed to Jagannātha Cakravartī); current ascription to Āśāditya Tripāṭhī unverified. Single-author record retained but low confidence.

texts.title: Prajñopāyaviniścayasiddhi
texts.basis: DSBC e-text colophon/verses name 'śrīmatānaṅgavajreṇa' (Anaṅgavajra). Davidson (Indian Esoteric Buddhism, bibl.: 'Asc. Anangavajra'), Snellgrove (Indo-Tibetan Buddhism: 'Prajñopāyaviniścayasiddhi of Anangavajra', pupil of Padmavajra/Mahāpadmavajra), and Nakamura (Indian Buddhism) all attribute it to Anaṅgavajra. Edition Bhattacharyya 1929 (GOS 44).
texts.note: Anaṅgavajra, Siddha lineage from Uḍḍiyāna; teacher Mahāpadmavajra. Edited with Jñānasiddhi of Indrabhūti (a separate work/author — not conflated here).

texts.title: Kālāgniyajñavidhi
texts.basis: source_url is only a generic Muktabodha/muktalib catalog landing page, no specific header. No match for the title in reference-literature (only unrelated 'Kālāgni' deity/Upaniṣad hits). Web search returned no author. It is a ritual prescription (yajñavidhi) of the anonymous āgamic/paddhati type.
texts.note: Anonymous ritual manual; no traditional ascription recoverable. Matches current 'anonymous/traditional'.

texts.title: Bhuvaneśvarīmahāstotra
texts.basis: Edition title (Bahura, Rajasthan Puratan Granthamala 54) and archive.org item title both ascribe the stotra to Pṛthvīdharācārya / Prithvi Dhar Acharya. Archive page gives no further biographical/critical detail; no independent scholarly attribution found in reference-literature (Pṛthvīdhara there is the Mṛcchakaṭika commentator — possibly unrelated namesake). Ascription rests on the named edition.
texts.note: Stotra ascribed to Pṛthvīdharācārya by its edition; traditional/editorial attribution, not independently corroborated. Do not conflate with Pṛthvīdhara the dramatist-commentator on the Mṛcchakaṭika.

texts.title: Mahāmāyātantra (Toh 425)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (root tantra, buddhavacana); Ratnākaraśānti (Guṇavatīṭīkā); Jinavara & Gö Lhetsé (Tibetan translation)
texts.basis: 84000 (Toh 425, tr. Damron/Dharmachakra): root tantra presented as buddhavacana with no named human author (begins without 'thus I have heard'); Sanskrit original lost, reconstructed via Tibetan Kangyur + Sanskrit MSS of Ratnākaraśānti's Guṇavatīṭīkā commentary. Tibetan translation colophon credits Indian preceptor Jinavara and Tibetan translator Gö Lhetsé. Kṛṣṇavajra/Ratnākaraśānti offer differing views on the speaker.
texts.note: Distinct roles: anonymous/buddhavacana root tantra vs Ratnākaraśānti's Guṇavatīṭīkā commentary vs the Jinavara/Gö Lhetsé Tibetan translation. current_author was empty; record now separates these contributors.

texts.title: Vajrasattvaniṣpādanasūtra [Vajrasattvasādhana]
texts.current_author: Candrakīrti (the tantric author of the Guhyasamāja Ārya school)
texts.basis: DSBC e-text; no firm printed edition. Reference-literature: Wedemeyer (diss.) lists 'the (dubious) Vajrasattvasādhana' among works ascribed to (the Guhyasamāja-Ārya) Candrakīrti; the Pramāṇakīrtiḥ (Steinkellner Festschrift) explicitly speaks of 'the pseudo-Candrakīrti's Vajrasattvasādhana and Pradīpoddyotana'. Attribution to Candrakīrti is rejected/doubted by scholars.
texts.note: Ascribed to the tantric Candrakīrti but treated as pseudepigraphic ('pseudo-Candrakīrti', 'dubious') by Wedemeyer and in the Pramāṇakīrtiḥ volume. Status downgraded from secure attribution to pseudepigraphic.

texts.title: Pramāṇavārttikālaṃkāra
texts.basis: EAST/Wikipedia/Sadhukhan: the Pramāṇavārttikālaṅkāra (= Pramāṇavārtikabhāṣya) is Prajñākaragupta's commentary embedding and glossing Dharmakīrti's root Pramāṇavārttika kārikās; two verses are Prajñākaragupta's own additions. Sāṅkṛtyāyana ed. 1953 (TSWS 1).
texts.note: Title names only the commentator; root verses are Dharmakīrti's, so the text conflates two distinct authors (mūla vs bhāṣya). Prajñākaragupta early 10th c. (Bhattacharya); Dharmakīrti 7th c.

texts.title: Ratnāvalī (commentary on the Hevajratantra) — Kamalanātha
texts.primary_author: Kamalanātha (Ratnāvalī, commentary); on the anonymous Hevajratantra (mūla)
texts.basis: Conlon 2023 (PhD diss., Hamburg) edits the Ratnāvalī, a Sanskrit commentary on the Hevajratantra ascribed to Kamalanātha (editio princeps). The Hevajratantra root is an anonymous Buddhist yoginītantra; encyclopedias note Kamalanātha's Ratnāvalī used to constitute the root verses, untranslated into Tibetan.
texts.note: Record was blank; commentary by Kamalanātha vs anonymous root tantra = two roles. Some traditions associate the Ratnāvalī attribution with Mañjuśrī/Kamalanātha.

texts.title: Piṅgalāmata
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (a Nepalese manuscript names Śrīkaṇṭhanātha as promulgator)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Śaiva/Bhairava tantra; traditionally promulgated by Śrīkaṇṭhanātha/Śiva)
texts.basis: Web (pparihar/indica) and local refs (Goyal; Baruah): the Piṅgalāmata is an old Bhairava/yāmala tantra revealed by Śiva and 'come down through tradition'; a Nepal MS names Bhagavat Śrīkaṇṭhanātha as promulgator. As an āgama it has no human author.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Śiva/Śrīkaṇṭhanātha is revelatory (promulgator), not human authorship; retained anonymous.

texts.title: Netratantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Netratantra mūla); commentary Uddyota by Kṣemarāja
texts.basis: Sanderson and Śākta Traditions: Netratantra is an anonymous Śaiva āgama, Kashmir c. 700-850 CE; the edition (KSTS 46 & 61, M.K. Shastri) prints it with Kṣemarāja's Uddyota commentary (c. 1000-1050). Local: Sanderson 'Purity and Power' cites Netratantra with -uddyota by Kṣemarāja.
texts.note: Root tantra anonymous; commentator Kṣemarāja is distinct and named for completeness. Edition is a separate matter; root remains anonymous.

texts.title: Ratnāvalī (commentary on the Hevajratantra) — Kamalanātha
texts.primary_author: Kamalanātha (Ratnāvalī, commentary); on the anonymous Hevajratantra (mūla)
texts.basis: Duplicate of the Conlon 2023 Ratnāvalī item: edition (editio princeps, 2023) of the Ratnāvalī, a Sanskrit commentary on the Hevajratantra by Kamalanātha. Hevajratantra itself is an anonymous Buddhist tantra.
texts.note: Record blank; same work/authors as the K12 entry. Commentary (Kamalanātha) vs anonymous root tantra = two roles.

texts.title: Viṣṇusmṛti
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (revealed by Viṣṇu in the frame)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Vaiṣṇava-Dharmaśāstra mūla); commentary Keśavavaijayantī by Nandapaṇḍita
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition (Krishnamacharya 1964, Adyar 93) with commentary Keśavavaijayantī of Nandapaṇḍita; no original author named. Olivelle (Law Code of Viṣṇu): the Vaiṣṇava-Dharmaśāstra is anonymous, composed by a Kāṭhaka-Pañcarātra Vaiṣṇava in Kashmir c. 7th c.; frame attributes revelation to Viṣṇu.
texts.note: Mūla anonymous (legendary frame: revealed by Viṣṇu to the Earth-goddess). Nandapaṇḍita is the commentator (Keśavavaijayantī), a distinct named role.

texts.title: Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa [Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (composite/multi-author Buddhist scripture; framed as Buddhavacana via Mañjuśrī)

authors.name: anonymous (multiple redactors across layers, incl. ~750 CE 'Prophecy of Kings'/Rājavyākaraṇa stratum)

texts.basis: GRETIL header: anonymous, no author; ed. T. Ganapati Sastri; framed as taught by Buddha via Mañjuśrī. Davidson (Indian Esoteric Buddhism) repeatedly speaks of 'the authors of the Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa' (plural) and dates the Rājavyākaraṇa/Prophecy-of-Kings chapter to ~750 CE, i.e. a multi-stratum text by several anonymous hands.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
texts.note: Composite anonymous tantra/kalpa accreted over centuries; distinct chapters (esp. ~750 CE Prophecy of Kings) by different anonymous authors/redactors. No single author; traditional ascription is Buddhavacana via Mañjuśrī.

texts.title: Sitātapatrā
texts.primary_author: anonymous (dhāraṇī; framed as Buddhavacana, issuing from the Buddha's uṣṇīṣa)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Ngawang Samten & J. Pandey ed., Dhīḥ 33, 2002): no author named; colophon 'āryasarvatathāgatoṣṇīṣasitātapatrānāmaparājitā pratyaṅgirā mahāvidyārājñī samāptā', no scribe/author signature. Web (Samye/Rigpa/Toh 590): self-framed as mantra-dhāraṇī issuing from Śākyamuni's uṣṇīṣa, no human author. Nakamura lists the Sitātapatradhāraṇī as anonymous liturgical work.
texts.note: Anonymous uṣṇīṣa protective dhāraṇī/vidyā; traditionally Buddhavacana. No human attribution.

texts.title: Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa (Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (composite/multi-author Buddhist scripture; framed as Buddhavacana via Mañjuśrī)

authors.name: anonymous (multiple redactors across layers, incl. ~750 CE 'Prophecy of Kings'/Rājavyākaraṇa stratum)

texts.basis: Same work as SA_K12_mamuka (duplicate DSBC/GRETIL e-text of the Ganapati Sastri ed., cf. Vaidya BST 18). GRETIL header: anonymous, no author. Davidson: 'the authors of the Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa' (plural), distinct strata; Prophecy-of-Kings chapter ~750 CE.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
texts.note: Duplicate of the MMK; multi-stratum composite by several anonymous authors/redactors. conflation_flag=true.

texts.title: Sattvārādhanagāthā
texts.current_author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (āryanāgārjunakṛtā), almost certainly pseudepigraphic
texts.basis: DSBC e-text opens 'ārya nāgārjunakṛtā' yet closes 'samyaksambuddhabhāṣitā ... samāptā' (= spoken by the Buddha) — internally inconsistent attribution. The name Nāgārjuna attracts much pseudepigrapha (web/maitripa, JIABS). Hartmann studied the related Sattvārādhanastava (cited in 'Literature about the Philosophical Systems' bibliography). No critical edition securing the Madhyamaka Nāgārjuna.
texts.source: dsbc:header | secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt | web:https://maitripa.org/library/subject-guides/madhyamaka/source-treatises-of-madhyamaka/nagarjuna/
texts.note: Colophon ascribes to Nāgārjuna but also calls it Buddha-spoken; almost certainly not the 2nd-c. Madhyamaka Nāgārjuna. Pseudepigraphic; traditional ascription retained with low confidence.

texts.title: Unmattabhairava
texts.basis: No edition/source_url given. Sanderson (History through textual criticism) cites the Unmattabhairava as one of the canonical group of Bhairava/Dakṣiṇa-tantras (listed with Svacchanda-, Caṇḍa-, Krodha-, Ugra-bhairava etc. in Picumata, Śrīkaṇṭhīya, and Kṣemarāja ad Svacchanda 1.4c-7) — a revealed scripture, not a human-authored work.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Bhairavatantra of the Mantrapīṭha; traditionally divine revelation (Śiva/Bhairava), no human author. Cited as a canonical tantra title by Sanderson.

texts.title: Skanda-Purāṇa: Revākhaṇḍa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Oṅkārānanda Giri 1994; input/proofing J. Neuss, FU Berlin): titled Narmadāpurāṇa/Revākhaṇḍa; colophons 'iti śrīskandapurāṇe revākhaṇḍe ... adhyāyaḥ'. Neuss flags it as the genuine Revākhaṇḍa, distinguishing it from a 232-ch. text wrongly attached to SkP that is actually Vāyupurāṇa material. Secondary lit (skt-en-mono / Tagare-style notes) speaks of 'the author of the Revākhaṇḍa' as an anonymous Purāṇic redactor (e.g., the Pippalāda/Yājñavalkya tale).
texts.note: Anonymous Purāṇic māhātmya of the Narmadā (Revā); standard Purāṇic ascription to Vyāsa is traditional/legendary, not historical. Identity vs. the misattributed Vāyupurāṇa text clarified by Neuss.

texts.title: Gaṇeśastotra
texts.basis: A short Buddhist hymn to Gaṇeśa anthologized in Pandey's Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (1994). Stotras in this collection are by various, often unnamed, hands; no colophon names an author. Web confirms the genre is a devotional Buddhist Gaṇapati hymn with no firm authorial ascription. Secondary lit (Bendall catalogue) lists Gaṇeśa-stotras generically without a named composer.
texts.note: Edition compiler J.S. Pandey is editor of the anthology, not the hymn's author. No traditional named ascription; treat as anonymous devotional stotra.

texts.title: Pañcakrama
texts.primary_author: Ārya Nāgārjuna (tantric) — chs. 1,3,4,5; Śākyamitra — ch. 2 (Sarvaśuddhiviśuddhikrama / Anuttarasaṃdhi)

authors.name: Ārya Nāgārjuna (tantric, Guhyasamāja Ārya tradition; distinct from the Madhyamaka Nāgārjuna)

texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes whole to Nāgārjuna (śrīnāgārjunapādānām), BUT the section colophons differ: ch.2 reads 'kṛtiriyaṃ śākyamitrapādānām', chs.1,3,4,5 'nāgārjunapādānām'. Wedemeyer (diss.) confirms ch.2 (Sarvaśuddhiviśuddhi-krama, aparanāma Anuttarasaṃdhi) is unmistakably by Śākyamitra, an independent text assimilated into Nāgārjuna's work, cited separately as Anuttarasaṃdhi in the Caryāmelāpakapradīpa; debated by Bu-ston/Tsong kha pa/A-myes-zhabs. Snellgrove/Davidson treat the tantric Nāgārjuna as distinct from the Madhyamaka founder.
texts.note: ONE TITLE CONFLATES TWO AUTHORS: ch.2 by Śākyamitra, rest by (tantric) Nāgārjuna. Also: tantric Nāgārjuna ≠ Madhyamaka Nāgārjuna (pseudepigraphic identification). Tripathi 2001 / Mimaki-Tomabechi 1994 critical eds.

texts.title: Piṇḍīkrama

authors.name: Ārya Nāgārjuna (tantric Guhyasamāja master; traditionally but disputedly identified with the Madhyamaka Nāgārjuna)

texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'ācāryanāgārjunapraṇītaḥ'; colophon 'kṛtirācāryaśrīnāgārjunapādānām'. Also called Piṇḍīkṛta-sādhana / Piṇḍīkramasādhana. Secondary lit (Wayman, Willson & Brauen, Cantwell-Mayer, Almogi) uniformly cites it as a sādhana of Akṣobhya by Nāgārjuna of the Ārya tradition. Single-author work (unlike the Pañcakrama).
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Yoga of the Guhyasamajatantra_Wayman + cleaned_Willson and Brauen + cleaned_Cantwell and Mayer
texts.note: Single tantric author. The 'Nāgārjuna' here is the Guhyasamāja/Ārya tantric master, traditionally—but not securely—equated with the Madhyamaka Nāgārjuna; status best called traditional.

texts.title: Yogacūḍāmaṇyupaniṣat (Yogacūḍāmaṇi Upaniṣad)
texts.basis: A minor Yoga Upaniṣad; revelatory śruti-style text with no named human author. Web (Wikipedia/Rosen) dates it ca. 14th–15th c. CE as a late Yoga Upaniṣad. Local lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra; bibliographic list) groups it among the Yoga Upaniṣads without an author.
texts.note: Upaniṣad. Commentary printed in the Adyar edition is by Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (commentator, NOT author of the Upaniṣad)—not part of this root-text item. Traditionally affiliated to the Sāmaveda; no personal ascription.

texts.title: Nityākaulatantra (Nityākaula)
texts.basis: A Śākta/Kaula tantric scripture (āgama), revealed text with no human author by genre convention. The cited e-text is a diplomatic transcription of a fragmentary NAK palm-leaf MS; later critically edited by C. Kiss / A. Sanderson—those are modern editors, not authors. No colophon names a composer.
texts.note: Revealed scripture; Kiss & Sanderson are editors not authors. Distinct from the Śākta 'Nityātantra' quoted in compendia (Prāṇatoṣiṇī etc.); avoid conflating with that.

texts.title: Namaskāraikaviṃśatistotra [Namastāre-ekaviṃśatistotraguṇasahita / Tārā-ekaviṃśatistotra]
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (revealed; ascribed to the Buddha/Tārā cycle)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (canonically ascribed to the Buddha — buddhavacana)
texts.basis: The Praise in Twenty-one Homages to Tārā. Its own colophon (quoted by Wayman) reads 'samyaksaṃbuddhabhāṣitaṃ ... namaskāraikaviṃśatistotraṃ'—i.e. claimed as spoken by the Perfectly Awakened One (buddhavacana), hence pseudepigraphic rather than a personal composition. Wayman analyses it as a Śaiva-Buddhist syncretic praise; Willson notes it is treated as Kriyā-tantra. The five Indian commentaries are by Sūryagupta (mid-9th c.) and are separate works, not this root stotra.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Buddhist Insight_Wayman + cleaned_In Praise of Tara_Willson + edition-metadata (de Blonay 1895)
texts.note: Root stotra: anonymous, canonically buddhavacana (status pseudepigraphic). Do NOT attribute to Sūryagupta—he wrote the commentary cycle, not the praise. Tibetan tr. by Nyän (11th c.), rev. Drakpa Gyaltsen—translators, not authors.

texts.title: Kāvyamīmāṃsā
texts.basis: GRETIL colophons repeatedly read 'iti rājaśekharakṛtau kāvyamīmāṃsāyāṃ kavirahasye prathame'dhikaraṇe...'; secondary lit (skt-en-mono) treats Rājaśekhara as the author, noting his use of Rudraṭa's Kāvyālaṅkāra. Single author; the surviving text is only Adhikaraṇa 1 (Kavirahasya) of a planned larger work, but no separate-author commentary is conflated here.
texts.note: Colophon name rājaśekharakṛta; early-10th-c. court poet/theorist. No attribution dispute; only Adhikaraṇa 1 survives.

texts.title: Lokeśvaraśataka
texts.basis: Warder (Indian Buddhism) lists 'Vajradatta: Lokeśvaraśataka, ed. Karpelès JA 1919'; Dan Martin bibliography and Rajendralala Mitra (Skt Lit of Nepal p.112) both give author as Vajradatta; Holt dates the stotra to 9th c. AD. Single-author hymn (śataka of 100 verses to Avalokiteśvara).
texts.note: Vajradatta, 9th c., poet at court of Devapāla (Pāla); identified with Vajradeva in some Tibskrit lists. Tradition: stotra said to have cured leprosy.

texts.title: Dhyānaratnāvali
texts.basis: IFP manuscript catalogue (item 397153) records colophon 'iti trilocana śivācāryaviracitā dhyānaratnāvalī samāptā' and lists Trilocanaśivācārya as Author of Text. Trilocanaśiva is a known 12th-c. South Indian Śaivasiddhānta author (Prāyaścittasamuccaya, ed. Sathyanarayanan/Goodall; the Siddhāntasārāvali is a separate Trilocana work). A devotional/visualisation work (dhyāna of Sadāśiva) of the Śaivasiddhānta paddhati milieu.
texts.note: Colophon attributes to Trilocanaśivācārya; previously listed anonymous/traditional. Confidence medium because attribution rests on MS colophon, not a critical edition; distinct from his Siddhāntasārāvali and Prāyaścittasamuccaya.

texts.title: Tantrābhidhāna
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (title also used for a modern reference lexicon)
texts.basis: Tantric bīja-lexicon (with Bījanighaṇṭu and Mudrānighaṇṭu); cited as an anonymous nighaṇṭu/reference for bīja-mantra names (Almogi bibliography lists it without author; 'explaining mantras' uses it as a standard glossary of bījas). Printed Tantrik Texts Vol. I (1937) was edited/compiled under Arthur Avalon, ed. Pañcānana Bhaṭṭācārya. No single classical author is named.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_explaining mantras and the efficacy of ritual.txt
texts.note: Anonymous tantric lexicon/nighaṇṭu; editor (not author) = Pañcānana Bhaṭṭācārya / Avalon. No personal classical authorship; do not confuse with the modern Tantrābhidhāna reference dictionary.

texts.title: Vimalāvatītantra
texts.current_author: attributed to Vimalaśiva (per project hint; uncertain)
texts.basis: No secondary or web evidence found documenting authorship of a Vimalāvatītantra; only the Muktabodha MS transcription. The project hint 'Vimalaśiva' is unsupported: the Vimalaśiva attested in Sanderson (The Śaiva Age) is a 12th-c. Kalacuri Śaiva pontiff/donor (Jubbulpore inscription), not recorded as a tantra author. As a tantra it belongs to the anonymous revealed-scripture genre.
texts.note: Genre = āgamic/revealed tantra → anonymous. Traditional ascription to Vimalaśiva is uncertain and unverified; the inscriptional Vimalaśiva is a guru/donor, not an author. No critical edition.

texts.title: Garuḍa-Purāṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL text gives only a divine transmission frame (Viṣṇu→Garuḍa→Kaśyapa; Vyāsa as traditional compiler: 'garuḍoktaṃ kaśyapāya purā vyāsācchrutaṃ mayā'); no human author/editor credited (Sansknet input, Venkatesvara vulgate). A composite, multi-layered Mahāpurāṇa accreted over centuries (esp. the Pretakalpa/uttara sections), not the work of one person.
texts.note: Anonymous composite Purāṇa; traditional ascription to Vyāsa (low confidence, conventional). Frame: narrated by Viṣṇu to Garuḍa. Not a single-author text but a stratified compilation rather than a conflation of named authors.

texts.title: Maṇḍalabrāhmaṇa Upaniṣad
texts.primary_author: anonymous (root text); Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (commentary in this edition)
texts.basis: Web (Wikipedia/scholarship): a minor Yoga Upaniṣad of the Muktikā canon, of unknown author/date (Flood early 1st mill.; others post-10th c. due to Haṭha elements); cast as a dialogue of Yajñavalkya and the Sun-Puruṣa Nārāyaṇa. The cited Adyar edition pairs the anonymous root text with the bhāṣya of Śrī Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin, a distinct historical commentator.
texts.note: Root text genuinely anonymous (traditional dialogue speaker Sūrya/Nārāyaṇa to Yājñavalkya); but the edition conflates it with the named commentary of Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin — separate role flagged.

texts.title: Vajrasattvaniṣpādanasūtra (Vajrasattvasādhana)

authors.name: Candrakīrti (tantric author of the Ārya/Guhyasamāja school)

texts.basis: Publisher/OEAW page (via WebSearch) and edition title: the Vajrasattvaniṣpādanasūtra is ascribed to Candrakīrti and belongs to the Ārya School of Guhyasamāja exegesis (the circle of Nāgārjuna, Āryadeva, Nāgabodhi). This is the tantric Candrakīrti, traditionally — but problematically — identified with the Madhyamaka Candrakīrti. Critical ed. by Luo Hong & Tomabechi (2009).
texts.note: Tantric Candrakīrti (Guhyasamāja Ārya school), to be distinguished from the Prāsaṅgika-Madhyamaka Candrakīrti; the two are conflated in tradition. Title was empty in workitem — supplied.

texts.title: Samājasādhanavyavastholi
texts.basis: DSBC header (WebFetch): colophon 'kṛtir ācārya nāgabuddhipādānām iti' — work of master Nāgabuddhi; ed. Kimiaki Tanaka, input Miroj Shakya 2018. Wedemeyer diss. confirms Nāgabodhi as a major Ārya Guhyasamāja exegete whose works are reference points for the tradition.
texts.note: Colophon secures Nāgabuddhi (=Nāgabodhi); Guhyasamāja Ārya-school exegete. Attribution traditional rather than independently 'secure'.

texts.title: Vīṇāśikhatantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Śaiva Vāma tantra; revealed by Śiva to Devī)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (WebFetch): no author named; framed as dialogue of Śiva (Īśvara) and Devī; colophon 'vīṇāśikhaṃ vāmatantraṃ sampūrṇam' gives no author. Ed. T. Goudriaan 1985; e-text by Somadeva Vasudeva. Secondary lit (Samuel, Origins of Yoga and Tantra) treats it as a scriptural Vāma/Tumburu tantra, not a single-author work.
texts.note: Genuinely anonymous Śaiva scripture of the Left Current; traditional 'speaker' is Śiva. No human author.

texts.title: Jayākhyasaṃhitā
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Encyclopaedia of Tantra vol.4) treats the Jayākhyasaṃhitā as an authoritative Pāñcarātra ritual scripture, not a single-author composition; ed. Embar Krishnamacharya, GOS 54, 1931. As a saṃhitā it is anonymous revealed scripture.
texts.note: Anonymous Pāñcarātra (Vaiṣṇava tantric) saṃhitā; traditionally divine revelation. Editor Krishnamacharya is editorial role only, not author.

texts.title: Śivadharma (Śivadharmaśāstra)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Sanderson, Hindu tantric chaplains) and web scholarship (academia.edu 'Rudras on Earth'; A Śaiva Utopia): the Śivadharmaśāstra is a foundational anonymous lay-Śaiva text (c. 6th-7th c.), part of the eight-work Śivadharma corpus; no named author. Ed. Yogi Naraharinath / Muktabodha e-text.
texts.note: Anonymous early-medieval lay-Śaiva śāstra of the Śivadharma corpus; transmitted as dialogue, no human author. New critical eds in progress (Shivadharma Project).

texts.title: Guhyasamājatantrapradīpoddyotanaṭīkā-ṣaṭkoṭivyākhyā
texts.basis: Edition (Bahulkar, Dhīḥ 48, 2009) titles it Pradīpoddyotanaṭīkā-ṣaṭkoṭivyākhyā, a ṭīkā on the Guhyasamājatantra ascribed to Candrakīrti. Secondary lit (Nakamura; Wedemeyer diss.; Wayman; Yarnall Toh.1785) is unanimous that the Pradīpoddyotana is by the *tantric* Candrakīrti, a figure distinct from the 7th-c. Madhyamaka Candrakīrti though sharing the name; Tibetan tradition identifies them, Western scholarship dates this author later (ca. late 1st millennium). Web confirms 'Candrakīrti II / tantric Candrakīrti'.
texts.note: current_author empty; assigned to the tantric Candrakīrti. NB the name conflates two distinct historical figures in tradition (tantric vs Madhyamaka Candrakīrti); the ṣaṭkoṭi-vyākhyā = exposition of the six hermeneutic positions. Author single, so conflation_flag=false, but identity is the classic doubled-name problem.

texts.title: Candrajñānāgama (Vīraśaiva recension)
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to revelation by the Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Śaiva/Vīraśaiva āgama; traditionally revealed by Śiva)
texts.basis: Edition (Dwivedi/Ghose 1995) presents it as a Śaiva āgama (Kriyāpāda & Caryāpāda). As with the āgama genre, no human author: traditionally revelation. Encyclopedia of Tantra lists Candrajñāna among revealed Śaiva śāstras (with Bhairavāṣṭaka, Hṛdbhedatantra, Kālavāda). No human author named in literature.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt; tradition
texts.note: Anonymous āgama; traditional ascription = revelation by Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva. No human author to recover; matches current attribution.

texts.title: Spandakārikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Faliero 1998): author Vasugupta, colophon 'vasuguptavac' linking to Śiva. Secondary (Encyclopedia of Tantra): the Spandasūtras are 'attributed to Vasugupta by Kṣemarāja' but 'appear to have been composed by Kallaṭa, a pupil of Vasugupta'; Kallaṭa also wrote the Vṛtti. Authorship explicitly called controversial. Standard printed text = kārikā + Kallaṭa's vṛtti (KSTS).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt
texts.note: Genuine authorship dispute Vasugupta vs his pupil Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa (the latter per Kṣemarāja's transmission account); not a multi-author conflation but a single disputed authorship. This GRETIL e-text = verses only (no Kallaṭa vṛtti).

texts.title: Avayavinirākaraṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL URL 404 (header not retrievable). Secondary lit strongly confirms: Nakamura (Indian Buddhism) lists 'Pandita-Aśoka (9th c.): Avayavinirakarana'; Tatz (Candragomin) distinguishes the logician Aśoka (Avayavinirākaraṇa, ed. H.P. Shastri in Six Buddhist Nyāya Tracts 1910) from the *tantric* Aśoka, dating the logician ca. 1000 A.D.; Keira and Sferra (Aśokanibandhau) corroborate. Edition matches Shastri 1910, Bibliotheca Indica 185.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey...Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Candragomin and the Bodhisattva Vow_Mark Joseph Tatz, 1978.txt
texts.note: Secure: Paṇḍita Aśoka the Buddhist logician (ca. 1000 CE), explicitly distinguished from the homonymous tantric Aśoka. Companion tract = Sāmānyadūṣaṇa(dikprasāritā).

texts.title: Śṛṅgāratilaka
texts.basis: GRETIL header: end-of-section colophons read 'śrīrudrabhaṭṭaviracite śṛṅgāratilakābhidhāne' — internally ascribed to Rudrabhaṭṭa. Web (wisdomlib; Kalidasa Wikipedia): Rudraṭa 'is also known as Rudrabhaṭṭa', a long-standing identification dispute, and this Śṛṅgāratilaka has at times been *misattributed to Kālidāsa* (note a separate short erotic poem also called Śṛṅgāratilaka circulates under Kālidāsa's name — a different work).
texts.note: Author named in colophon as Rudrabhaṭṭa; current attribution stands. Identity disputed (Rudrabhaṭṭa vs Rudraṭa the Kāvyālaṅkāra author); also spuriously linked to Kālidāsa. Single author, so conflation_flag=false.

texts.title: Śivasūtra (this e-text = Śivasūtravārtika of Varadarāja)
texts.basis: Edition is explicitly 'Śivasūtravārtika of Varadarāja' (M.K. Shastri, KSTS 43, 1925; input Hellwig). GRETIL header presents 'Śivasūtra with Vārtika' = Vasugupta's root sūtras embedded in a verse vārtika. Web confirms two distinct vārtikas: Bhāskara's (orig., 11th c.) and Varadarāja's = Kṛṣṇadāsa's, the latter described as a verse rehash of Kṣemarāja's Vimarśinī. Root Śivasūtra traditionally revealed to Vasugupta (9th c.).
texts.note: CONFLATION: current_author 'Vasugupta (revealer)' captures only the root sūtras. This specific edition is the Śivasūtravārtika authored by Varadarāja (= Kṛṣṇadāsa); the sūtras (Vasugupta) and the vārtika (Varadarāja) are by different persons. Distinguish from Bhāskara's homonymous Śivasūtravārtika.

texts.title: Amṛtabindūpaniṣad
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Yoga Upaniṣad of the Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda; commentary by Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin)
texts.basis: Amṛtabindu is one of the minor/Yoga Upaniṣads (Muktika no. 20, Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda); secondary lit (Eliade, Yoga) lists it among anonymous late Yoga Upaniṣads composed in the Vedānta/Yoga milieu, no individual author. The cited edition's named persons are the commentator Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin and editor Mahadeva Sastri, not the author.
texts.note: Genuinely anonymous śruti-class text; traditionally ascribed to no human author. Commentary distinct from root.

texts.title: Daśavidhahetunirūpaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header: opening verses read 'śrī śrīnivāsamakhikṛte śrīvaikhānasagṛhyasūtratātparyacintāmaṇau daśavidhahetunirūpaṇam' — i.e. this is a section of his larger commentary the Tātparyacintāmaṇi; dedicatory verses name 'śrīnivāsāryadīkṣitam' titled vedāntadeśika. Editions by Pārthasārathi Bhaṭṭācārya.
texts.note: Colophon/opening self-attribution; part of the Vaikhānasagṛhyasūtra-tātparyacintāmaṇi. Current attribution confirmed.

texts.title: Samantabhadra nāma sādhana ṭīkā
texts.basis: WebSearch (Tanaka 2017 ed., BEFEO/Persée; UC Berkeley diss): the root Samantabhadra-nāma-sādhana is by Buddhajñānapāda, founder of the Jñānapāda school of the Guhyasamāja; the manuscript edited by Tanaka is a fragment of a commentary (ṭīkā) on it whose commentator is unidentified. DSBC page names only the digitizers (Miroj Shakya), no author/commentator. Secondary lit (Wedemeyer, Kongtrul) lists the Samantabhadra-sādhana among the fourteen works of the Jñānapāda/Mañjuśrī tradition.
texts.note: Title conflates root sādhana (Buddhajñānapāda) and an anonymous commentary (ṭīkā) on it; commentator genuinely unidentified.

texts.title: Hevajratantra with Yogaratnamālā Pañjikā of Kṛṣṇācārya
texts.primary_author: Kṛṣṇācārya / Kāṇhapāda (Yogaratnamālā pañjikā); root Hevajratantra is anonymous revealed tantra

authors.name: Kṛṣṇācārya (Kāṇha / Kṛṣṇa[pā]; called Kṛṣṇa Samayavajra in some Tibetan sources)

texts.basis: Secondary lit: Tanemura, Yarnall, Snellgrove, Davidson, Sobisch all attribute the Yogaratnamālā pañjikā on the Hevajratantra to Kṛṣṇa/Kāṇha(pāda)/Kṛṣṇācārya (Yarnall: 9th c.); Sobisch (2008) notes Tibetan tradition variously names him 'Kṛṣṇa Samayavajra' vs 'Paṇḍita Kṛṣṇa' (minor identity confusion). The Hevajratantra itself is an anonymous revealed yoginī-tantra; the volume pairs root tantra + commentary.
texts.note: Empty title/author in source. Edition = root Hevajratantra (anonymous) + Yogaratnamālā by Kṛṣṇācārya/Kāṇha. Commentator name variant: Kṛṣṇa Samayavajra in some Tibetan sources.

texts.title: Dīkṣāpaddhati
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text from an unpublished manuscript with no published critical edition and no author cited. 'Dīkṣāpaddhati' is a generic title for Śaiva initiation ritual manuals (paddhati) composed/compiled by various ritualists; this particular MS gives no individual attribution.
texts.note: Generic paddhati title, multiple homonymous works by different ritualists; this MS unattributed. No single author determinable.

texts.title: Kālīkalpalatā
texts.basis: Krama/Kālīkula (Śrīkula) tantric digest. Vimarśānandanātha is a -nātha initiatory guru-name consistent with the tradition; the ascription is the one carried by the Muktabodha e-text but is not corroborated in accessible scholarship (web searches surface the related Krama Kalpalatā but do not independently confirm Vimarśānandanātha's authorship of a Kālīkalpalatā). Most Kālīkula texts survive only in MS and are little-studied.
texts.note: Traditional/colophon-style ascription to Vimarśānandanātha; not independently confirmed in secondary literature. Treated as traditional, not secure.

texts.title: Svabhāvavāda
texts.primary_author: Various / anonymous (modern collection compiled by Ramakrishna Bhattacharya)
texts.basis: Edition statement and web (academia.edu/researchgate) confirm this is a modern scholarly COLLECTION ('Verses Relating to Svabhāvavāda: A Collection', JIP) of svabhāvavāda verses gathered from many disparate Sanskrit/Prakrit sources by R. Bhattacharya; the verses themselves derive from numerous distinct (mostly anonymous/lost Svabhāvavāda-Lokāyata) authors. Not a single-author work.
texts.note: Not a unitary text: a compilation. Individual verses are by many different (largely anonymous) sources; modern compiler/editor is Ramakrishna Bhattacharya. Conflates multiple distinct verse-authors under one title.

texts.title: Sarvopakāriṇī
texts.basis: Sarvopakāriṇī is a ṭīkā (commentary) on the Sāṃkhya Tattvasamāsa; secondary lit ('Literature about the Philosophical Systems', Potter's Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies vol. 4) lists it as an anonymous commentary on the Tattvasamāsa. wisdomlib entry gives '[commentary] Sarvopakāriṇī' with NO named author. The root Tattvasamāsa is itself traditionally (pseudo-)ascribed to Kapila but is a separate work; the Sarvopakāriṇī commentary author is unnamed.
