texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 4 - Potter 1987.txt; web:https://www.wisdomlib.org/definition/tattvasamasa
texts.note: Anonymous Sāṃkhya commentary (ṭīkā) on the Tattvasamāsa. Distinct from the root sūtras (traditionally ascribed to Kapila). No commentator named in sources.

texts.title: Garuḍa-Purāṇa 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Śrīgaruḍamahāpurāṇam, Venkatesvara Steam Press ed., SANSKNET input) gives no human author, only divine dialogue (Kṛṣṇa/Garuḍa); this file = Uttarakhaṇḍa, Dvitīyāṃśa, Dharmakāṇḍa = the Pretakalpa (funeral rites, afterlife). Secondary (skt-en-mono colophon: 'This Garuda Purana ... Vyasa formerly narrated') and scholarship treat Purāṇas as anonymous composite works traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa.
texts.note: Pretakalpa portion (Uttarakhaṇḍa/Dharmakāṇḍa). Anonymous, multi-layered Purāṇic compilation; traditional ascription to Vyāsa is legendary, not historical.

texts.title: Mahāgaṇapatistotra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (transliterated from webdunia.com, no critical edition) ascribes the stotra to Rāghavacaitanya. Web (sanskritdocuments.org / stotranidhi) confirms the colophon styles the author 'Śrīmatparamahaṃsa-parivrājakācāryavarya Śrīrāghavacaitanya' — a self-named devotional ascription. No critical scholarship verifies the historical identity; attribution rests on the colophon/tradition.
texts.note: Single-author devotional stotra; colophon names Rāghavacaitanya, an ascetic (Paramahaṃsa). Attribution traditional, not independently verified.

texts.title: Garuḍa-Purāṇa 3
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Śrīgaruḍamahāpurāṇam, Venkatesvara Steam Press ed., SANSKNET input) names no human author; this file covers Uttarakhaṇḍa Tṛtīyāṃśa (Brahmakāṇḍa) and Dvitīyāṃśa (Dharmakāṇḍa), divine dialogue Kṛṣṇa/Garuḍa. Same anonymous composite-Purāṇa status with legendary Vyāsa ascription as garup2.
texts.note: Brahmakāṇḍa + Dharmakāṇḍa portions of Uttarakhaṇḍa. Anonymous Purāṇic compilation; traditional Vyāsa ascription only.

texts.title: Vāṅmaṇḍalanamaskāraślokāḥ
texts.basis: Item in J. S. Pandey (ed.), Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (MLBD 1994), GRETIL 'Buddhist stotras' corpus. GRETIL stotra-collection file (fetched) does not list this short namaskāra-ślokāḥ under any named author; the anthology is a collection of hymns of mixed/various authorship, and web/secondary searches yield no author for this specific piece. Treated as an anonymous devotional namaskāra (homage to the sphere/maṇḍala of speech).
texts.note: Short anonymous Buddhist namaskāra verses in Pandey's anthology; no author named in header, colophon, or secondary literature. Editor of the collection (not author): Janardan Shastri Pandey.

texts.title: Prakāśa-Saṃhitā [Pāñcarātra-Saṃhitā]
texts.basis: Pāñcarātra Saṃhitās are revealed/āgamic scripture with no named human author. Secondary lit (Dasgupta, History of Indian Philosophy vol. 3, via wisdomlib) describes the Prakāśa-saṃhitā's structure (two parts: Paramatattvanirṇaya 15 ch., Paratattvaprakāśa 12 ch.) but names no author. Archive.org collection page does not list this saṃhitā or any editor.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Pāñcarātra Saṃhitā; traditionally framed as divine revelation (Vāsudeva), no human author; current 'anonymous/traditional' is correct.

texts.title: Kāmākhyātantra
texts.basis: Śākta Tantra of the Kāmākhyā/Kāmarūpa (Assam) tradition. N. N. Bhattacharyya, History of the Tantric Religion, cites the Kāmākhyātantra for its enumeration of the characteristics of the Vīra but assigns no author; it is treated as anonymous revealed Tantra (Śiva-Śakti dialogue). No named author in printed eds. (Chaturvedi 2003; Tripāṭhī, Chaukhamba 2006).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion An Historical, Ritualistic, and Philosophical Study_N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Śākta Tantra in dialogue form; no human author claimed. Modern Hindi/Skt eds. are editions, not authorship.

texts.title: Śivadharmottara
texts.basis: One of the two earliest texts of the Śivadharma corpus (with the Śivadharmaśāstra), framed as Skanda's teaching to laity. Sanderson (Hindu Tantric Chaplains) and Mallinson-Singleton (Roots of Yoga) treat it as an anonymous Śaiva lay-religion text; De Simini's Nepal-corpus work names no author. Probably composed c. 6th-8th c.; earliest corpus MS dated 1036 CE.
texts.note: Anonymous; no individual author. Sometimes called Śivadharmottarapurāṇa. Traditional speaker Skanda; not pseudepigraphic in the named-sage sense.

texts.title: Jñānadīkṣā
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text of a Śaiva Jñānadīkṣā ('initiation into knowledge') from manuscript; no published critical edition and no author cited. 'Jñānadīkṣā' is a ritual/topic title (a dīkṣā type) rather than an authored treatise; treated as anonymous āgamic/paddhati material. Reference-lit hits (Alper, Understanding Mantras) use 'jñānadīkṣā' as a technical term, not a titled authored work.
texts.source: gretil-header (Muktabodha catalog); secondary:cleaned_Understanding Mantras_Harvey P. Alper, 1989.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Śaiva initiation text from MS; no author in the e-text header; title denotes a ritual category.

texts.title: Śāṇḍilya Upaniṣad
texts.primary_author: anonymous (mūla Upaniṣad); Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (commentary in the Adyar ed.)
texts.basis: The root Śāṇḍilya Upaniṣad is an anonymous minor (Yoga) Upaniṣad, ascribed by tradition to/named after sage Śāṇḍilya, no real author; part of the 108-Upaniṣad South-Indian recension. The cited edition (A. Mahadeva Sastri, Adyar, Yoga Upaniṣads) prints it WITH the commentary of Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin, an 18th-c. South Indian commentator (Rāmacandrendra Sarasvatī) who glossed all 108 Upaniṣads. Root text and commentary are by different hands and eras.
texts.note: Conflation: anonymous mūla Upaniṣad (traditional eponym Śāṇḍilya) + 18th-c. ṭīkā of Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin bundled in the Adyar edition. Current 'anonymous/traditional' misses the named commentator.

texts.title: Prodgītāgama (Prodgīta)
texts.basis: Prodgīta/Prodgītāgama is an Āgama mūlāgama, classically listed among the 28 Śaiva Siddhāntāgamas (one of the 18 Rudrabhedāgamas), with upāgamas such as Kavacāgama and Pāśabandhāgama; transmitted as divine revelation (Śiva/Śūlin) per pratisaṃhitā theory. No human author. (Workitem frames it as Pāñcarātra/revealed; either tradition treats it as anonymous revealed scripture.)
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Āgama; no edition/source_url available. Classed as Śaiva Siddhāntāgama in scholarship; current 'anonymous/traditional (revealed scripture)' is correct.

texts.title: Samanvayadiś
texts.primary_author: Kuḍaka (Samanvayadiś); Devaśarman (Samanvayapradīpa + autocommentary, bundled in same edition)
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: Samanvayadiś colophon reads 'kṛtī rājānapaṇḍitacchuḍḍakasya' (= Kuḍaka). The same Slaje edition (WZKS 36, 1992) bundles Devaśarman's separate Samanvayapradīpa with its autocommentary Samanvayapradīpasaṅketa, colophon 'paṇḍitadevaśarmaṇā', pupil of Gaṅgādhara. Two distinct authors in one e-text.
texts.note: Samanvayadiś is securely Kuḍaka's; but the work-item/edition conflates it with Devaśarman's Samanvayapradīpa+autocommentary (distinct author). Flagged as conflation.

texts.title: Guhyāvalī
texts.basis: DSBC source page is a generic portal (no per-text header retrieved). No author found in local reference-literature (8201 files) or web; web returns only Guhyasamāja/Guhyakālī noise, no named author for a 'Guhyāvalī'. A garland-type (āvalī) Buddhist tantric compilation, transmitted anonymously.
texts.note: No author identifiable; stays anonymous. Title 'āvalī' (garland) suggests a compiled/anthological tantric text.

texts.title: kuladīpikā
texts.basis: Muktabodha portal (generic page, no per-text header retrieved). 'Tryambaka' is the legendary eponymous founder of the Trika/Tryambaka āmnāya in Kashmir Śaiva tradition; an ascription to 'Tryambakabhaṭṭāraka' is a traditional/lineage attribution rather than a historically secured authorship. 'Kuladīpikā' is a generic title used for several unrelated works (cf. Bendall catalogue: Harischandra-kuladīpikā; Potter: Sampradāyakuladīpikā). No corroboration of this specific Tryambaka ascription found.
texts.source: tradition; secondary:cleaned_Bendall_Catalogue / cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies Vol.1 - Potter
texts.note: Ascription to Tryambakabhaṭṭāraka is traditional/legendary (lineage founder name), not securely historical; confidence low. Generic title shared by several distinct works.

texts.title: Śaivakalāviveka
texts.basis: Web (IFP Pondicherry / Ganesan, 'Two Śaiva Teachers of the Sixteenth Century: Nigamajñāna I and his disciple Nigamajñāna II'; ed. Sitarama Somayaji & Krishnadikshita, Bengaluru 1966): the Śaivakalāviveka is a Sanskrit work of the Śaivasiddhānta teacher Nigamajñānamuni (16th c. Tamilnadu), transmitted with a commentary by Gaṇapatibhaṭṭa. No support for 'anonymous'.
texts.note: Changed from anonymous/uncertain: root text by Nigamajñānamuni (16th c. Śaivasiddhānta), with separate commentary by Gaṇapatibhaṭṭa -> root vs commentary by different hands (conflation flag set).

texts.title: Kāmyakarmakamalā (by Prāṇamañjarī)
texts.current_author: Prāṇamañjarī (as given in the metadata; possibly a name/colophon attribution)
texts.basis: Muktabodha portal (generic page; no per-text header retrieved). No independent attestation of 'Prāṇamañjarī' or 'Kāmyakarmakamalā' in local reference-literature or web (only generic kāmyakarma / kamalā-dhyāna hits). Attribution rests solely on the title/colophon embedded in the work-item; the name is unusual (possibly a maṅgala/colophon name or scribe), unverifiable externally.
texts.note: Author name 'Prāṇamañjarī' taken from title/colophon metadata; no external corroboration -> uncertain, confidence low. A kāmya ritual manual (paddhati-type).

texts.title: Ratnamālāstotra
texts.basis: GRETIL 108-Buddhist-stotras page lists it (BuSto_68) without an author header/colophon; edition is Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha (1994), a collection of mostly anonymous Buddhist devotional hymns from Nepalese MSS. Web: a 'Ratnamālā' is sometimes linked to Nāgārjuna, but that is a distinct work; no secure author for this stotra. No local-lit attribution found.
texts.note: No author named in the e-text or edition for this stotra; stays anonymous. Do NOT conflate with Nāgārjuna's 'Ratnamālā' (different work).

texts.title: Gaṇḍīstava
texts.current_author: traditionally attributed to Aśvaghoṣa (also to Āryadeva); uncertain
texts.primary_author: Āryadeva (per DSBC colophon); also traditionally ascribed to Aśvaghoṣa
texts.basis: DSBC/sanskritdocuments e-text colophon reads 'iti kṛtir iyam ācārya-āryadevapādānāṃ' and metadata 'AryadevapAdakRitam' (=Āryadeva). Web/secondary: hymn (praise of the monastery gong) is also listed among works attributed to Aśvaghoṣa; Tibetan canon has a Gaṇḍī-stotra-gāthā under Aśvaghoṣa. Genuine attribution dispute, not one work by multiple authors.
texts.note: Single-author work with rival attributions. The actual DSBC edition this item points to attributes it to Āryadeva; Aśvaghoṣa is the older traditional ascription. Disputed, not conflated.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 8
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: root prakaraṇa (Tattvāloka, 8th) 'mahāmahopādhyāya-śālikanāthamiśra-praṇītā'; the e-text also carries the Nyāyasiddhi commentary by Jayapuri Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa. Secondary lit confirms 'Nyayasiddhi on Salikanatha Misra's Prakaranapancika' (cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems...).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Root by Śālikanātha (Prabhākara Mīmāṃsā); the edition/e-text conflates it with the later Nyāyasiddhi commentary of Jayapuri Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa.

texts.title: Gauragovindārcanasmaraṇapaddhati
texts.basis: GRETIL header names 'śrīpāda dhyāna-candra gosvāmī' as author; a Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava sādhana/smaraṇa manual. No colophon dispute; no commentary conflation in the e-text.
texts.note: Single author confirmed by GRETIL header. Dhyānacandra Gosvāmī, Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava (associate of Gopāla Bhaṭṭa tradition).

texts.title: Sādhanamālā, Vol. 1
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (compilation of many authors); critically edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya
texts.primary_author: Multi-author anthology (c. 47 named masters, e.g. Abhayākaragupta, Advayavajra); ed. Benoytosh Bhattacharyya
texts.basis: Web/secondary: Sādhanamālā is a heterogeneous compendium of ~300 sādhanas by ~47 named masters (Abhayākaragupta of Vikramaśīla, Advayavajra, etc.), many sādhanas anonymous; latest composed ~12th c.; compiler unknown (sometimes loosely linked to Abhayākaragupta). Critically edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya (GOS 26, 1925). Secondary refs in reference-literature corpus (Nakamura; Lewis) cite Bhattacharyya's Sādhanamālā/Iconography.
texts.note: Anthology conflating many distinct authors plus anonymous sādhanas; individual colophons name the sādhana authors. Bhattacharyya is the modern editor, not the author.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 6
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: root 6th prakaraṇa (Pramāṇapārāyaṇa) by 'mahāmahopādhyāya-śālikanāthamiśra'; e-text carries the Nyāyasiddhi commentary by Jayapuri Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa. Secondary confirms Nyāyasiddhi on Śālikanātha's Prakaraṇapañcikā.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Root by Śālikanātha; e-text conflates the later Nyāyasiddhi commentary of Jayapuri Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 3
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 3rd prakaraṇa (Nayavīthī/Nītipatha) by 'śālikanāthamiśra' (Prabhākara doctrine, 'lokahitārthāya'); e-text carries Nyāyasiddhi commentary ('nyāyasiddhyākhyayā vyākhyayā') by Jayapuri Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa. Secondary confirms the Nyāyasiddhi commentary relationship.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Root by Śālikanātha; e-text conflates the later Nyāyasiddhi commentary of Jayapuri Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 12
texts.basis: GRETIL header opening 'śrīmatprabhākaragurutantradhurandhareṇa mahāmahopādhyāya-śālikanāthamiśreṇa praṇītā prakaraṇapañcikā' and colophon 'iti mahāmahopādhyāyaśrīmacchālikanāthamiśrapraṇītāyāṃ prakaraṇapañcikāyāṃ viṣayakaraṇīyaṃ nāma dvādaśaṃ prakaraṇaṃ samāptam'. The edition prints Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa's Nyāyasiddhi as a separate commentary layer, but the e-text item is the root Prakaraṇapañcikā.
texts.note: Colophon names Śālikanātha Miśra, follower of Prabhākara. Nyāyasiddhi by Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa is a separate accompanying commentary, not part of this root-text item.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 5
texts.basis: Same Prakaraṇapañcikā work; GRETIL headers and colophons across chapters consistently attribute the root work to mahāmahopādhyāya Śālikanātha Miśra, the leading Prābhākara Mīmāṃsaka. Nyāyasiddhi commentary by Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa printed separately in the edition.
texts.note: Root work by Śālikanātha Miśra; commentary layer (Nyāyasiddhi) is by a different author but is not the indexed item.

texts.title: Nyāyakalikā
texts.basis: No source_url (not on GRETIL as fetched). Secondary 'Literature about the Philosophical Systems' (Karl Potter bibliography) lists Nyāyakalikā under Jayanta Bhaṭṭa's works alongside the Nyāyamañjarī. Web (critical edition by Watanabe/McAllister; Wikipedia) notes Jayanta explicitly claims authorship in the concluding verse; attribution uncontroversial.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jayanta_Bhatta
texts.note: Short Nyāya primer ('a bud of the Nyāya tree'); Jayanta claims authorship in the final verse. No dispute found.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 14
texts.basis: Same Prakaraṇapañcikā; GRETIL header/colophon pattern attributes root work to Śālikanātha Miśra (Prābhākara). Edition includes Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa's Nyāyasiddhi as a separate commentary.
texts.note: Root work by Śālikanātha Miśra; Nyāyasiddhi commentary by Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa is separate.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 10
texts.basis: Same Prakaraṇapañcikā; GRETIL header/colophon attributes root work to Śālikanātha Miśra. Nyāyasiddhi commentary by Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa printed separately in the edition.
texts.note: Root work by Śālikanātha Miśra; commentary by a different author is not the indexed item.

texts.title: Cittaviśuddhiprakaraṇa
texts.current_author: Āryadeva (tantric Āryadeva; traditionally identified with the Madhyamaka Āryadeva)
texts.primary_author: Āryadeva (tantric/Vajrayāna master, distinct from the Madhyamaka Āryadeva)
texts.basis: GRETIL header titles it 'Aryadeva: Cittavisuddhiprakarana' and the colophon reads 'kṛtiriyamāryadevapādānām' (work of the venerable Āryadeva). However, secondary literature (Karen C. Lang, 'Āryadeva on the Bodhisattva's Cultivation of Merit and Knowledge', 1983, pp. ~1728-1732) states the Cittaviśuddhiprakaraṇa 'is the work of a second Āryadeva, a Vajrayāna master, who bears the name of the earlier Madhyamaka philosopher'. Listed under a separate tantric Āryadeva in Potter's bibliography too.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Aryadeva on the Bodhisattva's Cultivation of Merit and Knowledge (India)(Catuhsataka)_Karen Christina Lang, 1983.txt
texts.note: Name 'Āryadeva' in colophon, but scholarship (Lang) identifies the author as a later tantric/Vajrayāna Āryadeva, NOT the Catuḥśataka/Madhyamaka Āryadeva. Traditional identification with the Madhyamaka author is rejected by modern scholars. Single author (not a multi-author conflation), but a homonym/misidentification issue, hence disputed and changed (sharpened away from the traditional Madhyamaka identification).

texts.title: Caryāmelāvaṇapradīpa
texts.current_author: Āryadeva (tantric Āryadeva of the Guhyasamāja Noble tradition)
texts.primary_author: Āryadeva (tantric Āryadeva of the Guhyasamāja Noble/Ārya tradition)
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'kṛtirīyaṃ mahācārya-āryadevapādānām' attributes it to the great teacher Āryadeva. Wedemeyer (2007, ed.) and web confirm this is the TANTRIC Āryadeva of the Guhyasamāja Noble (Ārya/Nāgārjuna) tradition; Tibetan tradition conflated him with the earlier Madhyamaka Āryadeva as a legitimation strategy, but the work is by the tantric Āryadeva.
texts.note: Colophon names 'mahācārya Āryadeva'. Identity disputed: tantric Āryadeva vs. Madhyamaka Āryadeva (tradition conflates the two for authority); the text itself = tantric Āryadeva. Current_author already correct.

texts.title: Spandakārikā
texts.current_author: Vasugupta or his pupil Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa (disputed)
texts.primary_author: Vasugupta or Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa (disputed); Kṣemarāja (Spandanirṇaya commentary)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Vasugupta [or Kallata Bhatta?]: Spandakrika' with commentary (Spandanirṇaya) by Kṣemarāja. Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra, Sanderson, Hazra): kārikās 'discovered' by Vasugupta but 'appears to have been composed by Kallata, a pupil of Vasugupta' per others. This GRETIL e-text bundles the kārikās WITH Kṣemarāja's commentary = two distinct authors.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt | secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_Śaivism and the tantric traditions.txt
texts.note: Root kārikās disputed Vasugupta vs Kallaṭa; the included commentary Spandanirṇaya is by Kṣemarāja (made for his pupil Śūra). Current_author omits the commentator Kṣemarāja, hence conflation flagged.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 4
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon (prakp01u checked): 'mahāmahopādhyāya-śālikanāthamiśreṇa praṇītā prakaraṇapañcikā', a Prabhākara Mīmāṃsā work; Nyāyasiddhi commentary by Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa. Secondary lit (Lit. about Phil. Systems) confirms Śālikanātha = Prabhākara follower and lists 'Nyayasiddhi on Salikanatha Misra's Prakaranapancika' by Jaya(narayana). Edition for this item names the Nyāyasiddhi.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Root mūla by Śālikanātha Miśra (secure); the edition/e-text includes the Nyāyasiddhi commentary by a distinct author Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa, hence conflation flagged. Current_author lists only the mūla author.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 9
texts.basis: Same work/edition as other Prakaraṇa parts: Śālikanātha Miśra (Prabhākara Mīmāṃsā) is the mūla author per GRETIL colophon; the Nyāyasiddhi commentary printed with it is by Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa (edition statement + secondary lit confirm distinct authors).
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Mūla by Śālikanātha Miśra; Nyāyasiddhi commentary by Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa. Conflation flagged; current_author lists only mūla author.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header directly checked: opening 'śrīmatprabhākaragurutantradhurandhareṇa mahāmahopādhyāya-śālikanāthamiśreṇa praṇītā prakaraṇapañcikā'; colophon 'śālikanāthamiśrapraṇītāyāṃ prakaraṇapañcikāyāṃ śāstramukhaṃ nāma prathamaṃ prakaraṇaṃ samāptam'. Nyāyasiddhi vyākhyā by Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa. Secondary lit confirms.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: First prakaraṇa = 'Śāstramukha'. Mūla by Śālikanātha Miśra (secure, a Prabhākara/disciple of Prabhākara's school); included Nyāyasiddhi commentary by Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa. Conflation flagged.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 2
texts.basis: Same work/edition as prakp01u (header verified there): Śālikanātha Miśra is the mūla author per GRETIL colophon; the printed Nyāyasiddhi commentary is by Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa per edition statement and secondary lit.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Mūla by Śālikanātha Miśra; Nyāyasiddhi commentary by Jayapurī Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa. Conflation flagged; current_author lists only mūla author.

texts.title: Spandakārikā
texts.primary_author: Vasugupta or Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa (kārikā, disputed); Rāmakaṇṭha / Rājānaka Rāma (vivṛti)
texts.basis: Secondary lit: Sanderson (Śaivism and the tantric traditions) states the Spandakārikā was 'discovered' by Vasugupta and 'composed by Vasugupta according to some, or by his pupil [Kallaṭa]'; this KSTS edition pairs the kārikās with the Vivṛti of Rāmakaṇṭha (Rājānaka Rāma), a distinct later commentator (9th-10th c.), so the title bundles root verses + commentary by different authors.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_Śaivism and the tantric traditions.txt; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Kārikā authorship disputed Vasugupta vs Kallaṭa (per Sanderson, Kṣemarāja). Edition adds Rāmakaṇṭha's Vivṛti -> multi-author title; flagged conflated.

texts.title: Āryamāyājālamahātantroddhṛtamaṇḍalagāthāṭippaṇī
texts.primary_author: Nāgārjuna (tantric; ṭippaṇī), pseudepigraphic; gāthās extracted from the Māyājālamahātantra (scriptural)
texts.basis: DSBC page colophon attributes the work to 'ācāryaśrīnāgārjunapādānām' (Nāgārjuna). The title shows layering: maṇḍalagāthās 'uddhṛta' (extracted) from the Māyājālamahātantra (anonymous tantric scripture) plus a ṭippaṇī gloss ascribed to Nāgārjuna. The ascription is to the tantric Nāgārjuna, conventionally identified with but distinct from the Madhyamaka founder -> pseudepigraphic/traditional.
texts.note: Colophon: ācārya Nāgārjunapāda for the ṭippaṇī; underlying gāthās drawn from Māyājāla tantra (scriptural, anonymous). Vajrayāna Nāgārjuna != Madhyamaka Nāgārjuna.

texts.title: Umāsaṃhitā
texts.primary_author: anonymous (5th saṃhitā of the Śivapurāṇa; traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (skt-en-mono Śivamahāpurāṇa translation) confirms the Umāsaṃhitā is the fifth of the seven saṃhitās of the Śivapurāṇa: 'The fifth compendium is called Umāsaṃhitā'. As a Purāṇic saṃhitā it is anonymous compilatory literature, traditionally framed as spoken by Vyāsa/sūta; no individual historical author.
texts.note: Fifth saṃhitā of the Śivamahāpurāṇa. Anonymous; conventional Purāṇic ascription to Vyāsa. No conflation of distinct historical authors.

texts.title: Nyāyamañjarī
texts.basis: Web (Wikipedia/scholarly): the Nyāyamañjarī was composed in Kashmir in the 9th c. CE by Bhaṭṭa Jayanta; standard, undisputed single-author attribution. Edition (Sūrya Nārāyaṇa Śukla, Kashi Sanskrit Series 106) names Jayanta Bhaṭṭa. GRETIL etext is the kārikā/text only (Cakradhara's Granthibhaṅga ṭīkā is a separate work, not bundled here).
texts.note: Securely Jayanta Bhaṭṭa (9th c. Kashmir). Confirms current_author.

texts.title: Svalpākṣarā Prajñāpāramitā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edited by P.L. Vaidya (Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha, BST 17); colophon only 'svalpākṣarā prajñāpāramitā samāptā' with no author. Secondary lit lists it among the short Prajñāpāramitā sūtras (Mipham/Phuntsho; Conze ed.). As a Prajñāpāramitā sūtra it is anonymous Mahāyāna scripture (traditionally buddhavacana).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Mipham’s Dialectics and the Debates on Emptiness...txt
texts.note: Anonymous Prajñāpāramitā sūtra; no named author. Vaidya is the modern editor, not author. Traditional ascription: word of the Buddha.

texts.title: Anuttaraprakāśapañcāśikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header names author Ādyanātha (ed. Mukundarāma Śāstrī, KSTS 13). Secondary lit: Potter's Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies Vol.1 indexes 'Anuttaraprakāśapañcāśikā (Ādyanātha)'; skt-en-mono cites 'the commentary (bhāṣā) uttered by Ādyanātha'. Ādyanātha ('primordial lord') is itself a quasi-mythic/initiatory Śaiva guru name, so attribution is traditional rather than firmly historical, but consistently transmitted.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 1 - Potter.txt
texts.note: Ādyanātha confirmed by edition + Potter index; name has a legendary/initiatory ring -> classed traditional. Single author, no conflation.

texts.title: Śiva-Purāṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Venkatesvara Press ed., input J. Takashima) names no individual author; Purāṇas are by tradition ascribed to Vyāsa. Secondary lit (Klostermaier, Purāṇa scholarship) treats Mahāpurāṇas as anonymous composite/sectarian compilations grown over centuries.
texts.note: Composite Śaiva Mahāpurāṇa; legendary ascription to Vyāsa, no historical author. Matches current attribution.

texts.title: Kaṭhopaniṣadbhāṣya
texts.basis: Edition = Works of Śaṅkarācārya vol. 1 (MLBD), Ten Principal Upaniṣads with Śāṅkarabhāṣya. Local lit (Literature about the Philosophical Systems) lists 'Bhasya on Kathopanisad' among Śaṅkara's genuine Upaniṣad bhāṣyas. Web: Andrijanić 2020 (IJHS) confirms via Hacker's criteria and computational General Imposters analysis no grounds to reject Śaṅkara's authorship.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt | web:link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11407-020-09279-z
texts.note: One of the genuine bhāṣyas of Ādi Śaṅkara on a principal Upaniṣad; authorship affirmed by both philological and stylometric methods. The Upaniṣad text itself (Kaṭha, Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda) is separate and anonymous; this work is the commentary only.

texts.title: Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sāṃkhyasaṃgraha, Chowkhamba ed. Dvivedin) names author Kavirājayati / Kavirājagiri. Colophon describes the work as proclaimed by Śrī-Vaikuṇṭhaśiṣya, a wandering paramahaṃsa (an epithet/identity of the same ascetic author), and names the text Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpa.
texts.note: Author is an ascetic (yati/giri) self-identified in colophon as disciple of Vaikuṇṭha; single-author Sāṃkhya treatise. Matches current attribution.

texts.title: Nyāyabinduṭīkāṭippaṇī
texts.basis: DSBC header: opening 'ācāryadharmottarakṛtāyā nyāyabinduṭīkāyāḥ ṭippaṇī' — a ṭippaṇī (gloss) on Dharmottara's Nyāyabinduṭīkā, no author named, no colophon. Stcherbatsky's Bibliotheca Buddhica VII ed. treats it as anonymous. Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, p.302) lists an anonymous Tippana/ṭippaṇī tradition on Dharmottara's Nyāyabinduṭīkā.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Anonymous sub-gloss on Dharmottara's Nyāyabinduṭīkā (itself on Dharmakīrti's Nyāyabindu). Note layered structure: Nyāyabindu = Dharmakīrti; ṭīkā = Dharmottara; this ṭippaṇī = anonymous. No grounds to identify with Mallavādin's tippani or Durveka Miśra's Dharmottarapradīpa, which are distinct named works. Stays anonymous.

texts.title: Yogatattvopaniṣat (Yogatattva Upaniṣad)
texts.basis: Edition = Adyar Library Yoga Upaniṣads with the commentary of Upaniṣad-brahma-yogin (ed. G. Srinivasa Murti, tr. T. R. Srinivasa Ayyangar). The Upaniṣad itself is an anonymous late minor/Yoga Upaniṣad (Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda); secondary lit (Wynne, White) treats it as a late text with no named author. The named individual pertains only to the accompanying commentary.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:Origin of Buddhist Meditation_Alexander Wynne, 2007.txt | secondary:Sinister Yogis_David Gordon White, 2009.txt
texts.note: Root Upaniṣad = anonymous (late Yoga Upaniṣad). The 18th-c. commentary in this edition is by Upaniṣad-brahmayogin (Rāmacandrendra Sarasvatī); listed in authors as ṭīkā role though the cataloged work is the Upaniṣad itself. Not a conflation of root authors.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 5
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives no specific source edition; vulgate text. Tradition (BhP 1.x self-statement) ascribes it to Vyāsadeva. Web (Wikipedia; Bryant; wisdomlib 'Date and Authorship'): scholarly consensus is the BhP is a composite 'epic of growth' whose authorship of individual parts cannot be fixed; a minority medieval/colonial tradition attributed it to Bopadeva (13th–14th c.), now rejected — Bopadeva was a commentator, not the author.
texts.note: Anonymous composite Vaiṣṇava Mahāpurāṇa; legendary ascription to Vyāsa; rejected Bopadeva theory noted. Book 5 only; no distinct author for this skandha. Matches current attribution.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 3
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only the data-entry contributor, no author. Tradition ascribes the whole BhP to Vyāsa. Web (wisdomlib/Wikipedia) and Survey of Hinduism (Klostermaier) report the text self-ascribes to Vyāsa; the Vopadeva/Bopadeva authorship theory (13th c., via Hemādri) is rejected by Farquhar et al. since Madhva and earlier writers cite the BhP. Scholarly view: anonymous, composite, c.9th-10th c.
texts.note: bhp_03u GRETIL e-text is the bare vulgate mūla text (unaccented), NOT carrying Śrīdhara's commentary inline, so no commentator conflation. Traditional ascription to Vyāsa is pseudepigraphic; Vopadeva theory rejected.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 6
texts.basis: Same work as the other BhP books. GRETIL header: data-entry only, no author. Tradition: Vyāsa. Scholarship (Farquhar, Klostermaier) rejects the Vopadeva attribution; treats the BhP as an anonymous composite Purāṇa.
texts.note: bhp_06u is the vulgate mūla text only; no inline Śrīdhara commentary, no conflation. Pseudepigraphic ascription to Vyāsa.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 10
texts.basis: Book 10 (Kṛṣṇa carita) of the BhP. GRETIL header: data-entry only. Tradition: Vyāsa. Scholarship treats the BhP as anonymous/composite; Vopadeva theory rejected (Farquhar). Book 10's growth/redaction layers reinforce non-single authorship.
texts.note: bhp_10u vulgate mūla only, no inline commentary, no conflation. Pseudepigraphic ascription to Vyāsa; composite text.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 4
texts.basis: Same work as other BhP books. GRETIL header: data-entry only, no author. Tradition: Vyāsa. Scholarship (Farquhar, Klostermaier) treats the BhP as anonymous composite; Vopadeva attribution rejected.
texts.note: bhp_04u vulgate mūla only; no inline Śrīdhara commentary, no conflation. Pseudepigraphic ascription to Vyāsa.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 8
texts.basis: Same work as other BhP books. GRETIL header: data-entry only. Tradition: Vyāsa. Scholarship treats the BhP as anonymous composite; Vopadeva theory rejected (Farquhar).
texts.note: bhp_08u vulgate mūla only, no inline commentary, no conflation. Pseudepigraphic ascription to Vyāsa.

texts.title: Matsyendranātha: Kaulajñānanirṇaya
texts.basis: Bagchi 1934 ed. ascribes it to Matsyendra; it is the principal extant text attributed to him, from Nepalese MSS dateable 11th-12th c. Web: modern scholarship (Bagchi, S. Dasgupta, D.G. White, R. Davidson) treats Matsyendra as a semi-legendary Yoginī Kaula founder (9th-10th c., E. India) and the identification/single-author attribution as a strong working hypothesis, not settled fact. Secondary lit (Snellgrove, Szántó: 'Macchendra/Mīnanātha') treats him as a Nāth siddha/deity figure rather than confirming personal authorship.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to a semi-legendary siddha; authorship of a single historical Matsyendra is disputed/uncertain. Edited (not authored) by P.C. Bagchi — editorial role distinct from authorship. No multi-author conflation within the text itself.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 9
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives only the e-text input/encoding, no editor or author; content opens with the Śuka/Parīkṣit frame. Tradition ascribes the whole Bhāgavata to Vyāsa; modern scholarship treats it as an anonymous South-Indian work (c. 9th-10th c. CE). This is Skandha 9, one book of a single multi-skandha work.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Vyāsa is legendary; book/skandha of a single anonymous purāṇa, not an independent author.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 12
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives only e-text encoding, no editor/author. Skandha 12 of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa; scholarship regards the purāṇa as anonymous (c. 9th-10th c.). Skandha 12 itself contains a phalaśruti naming the work as Vyāsa's, i.e. internal traditional self-ascription.
texts.note: Skandha 12 carries the internal colophon-style ascription of the whole work to Vyāsa; legendary, single anonymous work.

texts.title: Sātvatatantra
texts.basis: GRETIL page for satvtanu currently 404; edition (Dvivedī 1982) and wisdomlib confirm the Sātvatasaṃhitā/Sātvatatantra is an anonymous Pāñcarātra revealed scripture (framed as divine dialogue, taught via Nārada; one of the 'three jewels' ratnatraya with Pauṣkara and Jayākhya), with a separate later (19th c.) commentary (bhāṣya) by Alaśiṅga Bhaṭṭa. Mahābhārata-era references in reference-literature/cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt use 'Sātvata Tantra' as a religious system, not this text.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Hinduism and Buddhism Vol 2_Charles Eliot 1921.txt + web:wisdomlib.org/definition/satvatatantra + edition (Dvivedī 1982)
texts.note: Root text anonymous Āgamic revelation; the bundled bhāṣya is by Alaśiṅga Bhaṭṭa — two distinct hands, so conflation flagged. Root probably early (some date ~5th-7th c.); commentary 19th c.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 11
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives no editor/author. Skandha 11 (contains the Uddhava-gītā) of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally Vyāsa; scholarship treats the purāṇa as anonymous (c. 9th-10th c.).
texts.note: Legendary ascription to Vyāsa; book of a single anonymous purāṇa.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 2
texts.basis: Fetched GRETIL header: only e-text encoding/input notes, no named editor or author; content begins 'śrīśuka uvāca' within the Parīkṣit frame. Skandha 2 of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally Vyāsa; scholarship anonymous (c. 9th-10th c.).
texts.note: Legendary ascription to Vyāsa; book of a single anonymous purāṇa.

texts.title: Pauṣkarasaṃhitā
texts.basis: Source is muktabodha digital library (generic landing page; no per-text header). Edition: Sampathkumara Ramanuja Muni (ed.) 1934. Web (wisdomlib) confirms the Pauṣkarasaṃhitā is an anonymous Pāñcarātra Āgama (~6500 verses on temple architecture/iconography/consecration), one of the 'three jewels' (ratnatraya) with the Sātvata and Jayākhya saṃhitās; framed as revelation, no human author. Reference-literature (Eliot 1921) cites it among the early authoritative Pāñcarātra/Śāttvata-Pauṣkara-Parama scriptures.
texts.note: Anonymous Āgamic revelation; Sampathkumara Ramanuja Muni is editor (1934), not author. No commentary author named in this work-item.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 7
texts.basis: GRETIL header names no author/editor; text is the vulgate mūla only (no Śrīdhara commentary embedded). Tradition ascribes the whole Bhāgavatapurāṇa to Vyāsa; scholarship treats it as an anonymous composite work (c. 9th-10th c.).
texts.note: Book 7 of an anonymous composite Purāṇa; pan-traditional ascription to Vyāsa is legendary, not historical. Edition cites Śrīdhara's comm. but the GRETIL e-text is mūla text only.

texts.title: Kādambarīkathāsāra
texts.basis: GRETIL header and the colophon of the first section name the author as 'śrī...ācāryaśrījayantasūnor abhinanda' — Abhinanda, son of the teacher Jayanta (the Gauḍa poet Abhinanda, c. 9th c.). This kāvya is a verse summary (kathāsāra) of Bāṇa's Kādambarī.
texts.note: Colophon: Abhinanda son of Jayanta. The Gauḍa Abhinanda (distinct from the Kashmirian Abhinanda); secure single authorship.

texts.title: Troṭalātantra (Troṭala/Troṭalātantra)
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text (NGMCP MS 5-4852) is a Garuḍa/Tvaritā tantra in īśvara–Garuḍa dialogue form; no author or scribe colophon. Presents itself as divine revelation; catalog (Dyczkowski) gives only modern data-entry credit.
texts.note: Anonymous unpublished tantra; goddess Tvaritā (Troṭalā). No human author; traditional ascription is to Śiva as revealer.

texts.title: Mohacūḍottara (Mohacūḍottaratantra)
texts.basis: Web/scholarly: Mohacūḍottara is a pre-12th-c. Śaiva Siddhānta upabheda tantra (revealed scripture), known from early Nepalese MSS and quoted in early commentaries. No human author; standard āgamic dialogue form.
texts.note: Anonymous Śaiva Siddhānta tantra; revelatory authority (Śiva). No printed critical edition listed.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 13, Paricchedas 1 & 2
texts.primary_author: Śālikanātha Miśra (mūla); Jayapurī Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa (Nyāyasiddhi comm., not in this excerpt)
texts.basis: GRETIL header and section colophons attribute the Prakaraṇapañcikā to 'mahāmahopādhyāyaśrīmacchālikanāthamiśra', the leading Prābhākara Mīmāṃsaka (c. 9th-10th c.). The cited edition pairs it with the Nyāyasiddhi vyākhyā of Jayapurī Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa, but the e-text excerpt is the Śālikanātha mūla.
texts.note: Mūla securely Śālikanātha. Edition's commentary (Nyāyasiddhi) is by a separate author, Jayapurī Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa; not a true conflation since this e-text is mūla only — listed for role clarity.

texts.title: Nāṭyaśāstra, Vol. 2
texts.primary_author: attributed to Bharata Muni (anonymous composite root text); Abhinavagupta (Abhinavabhāratī comm.)
texts.basis: The Baroda GOS edition (Vol. II) presents the Nāṭyaśāstra mūla WITH the Abhinavabhāratī commentary of Abhinavagupta (c. 950-1020). The root text is traditionally ascribed to Bharata but is scholarly regarded as anonymous/composite, possibly multi-authored over centuries; 'Bharata' may be a title not a person. The commentary is securely Abhinavagupta's. This single title thus conflates the composite root text and a distinct commentator.
texts.note: Two distinct layers: anonymous/composite Bharata root + Abhinavagupta's Abhinavabhāratī. Edition (ed. M. R. Kavi, GOS, Baroda 1934) combines them; separated for roles.

texts.title: Kulasāra
texts.basis: source_url is only the muktalib7 digital-library catalog root, not the text itself. Local secondary literature (skt-en-mono: 'the Kulasara and Kuloddisa Tantras'; Encyclopedia of Tantra lists 'Kulasara' among Kaula scriptures) treats it as an anonymous revealed Kaula/Kuloddīśa-class Tantra with no named human author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Tantric scripture of the Kaula/Kuloddīśa class; no human author named. Current 'anonymous/traditional' is accurate.

texts.title: Bhallaṭaśataka
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives author Bhallaṭa (input Somadeva Vasudeva). Secondary (skt-en-mono) identifies Bhallaṭa as a poet contemporary of King Śaṅkaravarman of Kashmir (9th c.) and author of a śataka. A separate dhvani-allegory passage in skt-en-mono calls the collection 'an anthology drawn from many authors,' but that is an interpretive gloss on the poem's meaning, not a real conflation; standard scholarship treats Bhallaṭa as the single author.
texts.note: Securely Bhallaṭa, 9th-c. Kashmiri poet under Śaṅkaravarman. 'Anthology of many authors' remark is an allegorical reading, not multi-authorship.

texts.title: Nādabindu Upaniṣad
texts.basis: sanskritdocuments e-text gives no author ('Vedic Tradition'), classed as a Rigvedic Yoga Upaniṣad (38/108). Edition cited (Adyar, ed. Mahadeva Sastri) carries the commentary of Upaniṣad-brahma-yogin, a much later (c.18th-c.) commentator distinct from the anonymous mūla. Bronkhorst notes verses shared with Muktikā/other Upaniṣads (compilatory transmission).
texts.source: web:https://sanskritdocuments.org/doc_upanishhat/nadabindu.html; secondary:cleaned_Two Traditions of Meditation in Ancient India_Johannes Bronkhorst, 1993.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Yoga Upaniṣad (Rigveda affiliation). Edition's commentary is by Upaniṣad-brahma-yogin (distinct, later). Mūla itself anonymous; not flagged as conflation since the e-text is the mūla only.

texts.title: Stavacintāmaṇi
texts.basis: GRETIL header: hymns by Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa with commentary by Kṣemarāja; colophon BhStc_120 'Bhaṭṭanārāyaṇo vyadhāt' confirms verse authorship. Edition (KSTS XI, ed. Mukunda Rama Shastri 1918) is 'with the commentary of Kṣemarāja.' Secondary (Sanderson; Silburn's French ed.; skt-en-mono 'Stavacintāmaṇi with vivṛti by Kṣemarāja') confirms the two-author structure: stotra by Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa, vivṛti by Kṣemarāja.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_Śaivism and the tantric traditions.txt; secondary:cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt
texts.note: Title/edition conflate two distinct authors: hymns = Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa; vivṛti commentary = Kṣemarāja. current_author lists only the verse-author.

texts.title: Prakaraṇapañcikā: Prakaraṇa 11, Paricchedas 1 & 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header and colophon 'mahāmahopādhyāyaśrīmacchālikanāthamiśrapraṇītāyāṃ savṛttau vākyārthamātṛkāyāṃ' confirm Śālikanātha Miśra as author of both the text and its own vṛtti. Verified the e-text contains only Śālikanātha's mūla + self-vṛtti; the Nyāyasiddhi of Jayapurī Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa is named in the edition title but is NOT present in this GRETIL file. Local lit (Philosophical Systems index) lists Śālikanātha as Prābhākara Mīmāṃsaka.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Single author Śālikanātha Miśra (incl. self-vṛtti). Edition bundles Jayapurī Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa's Nyāyasiddhi commentary, but that commentary is absent from this e-text, so no conflation flagged for the text content.

texts.title: Rāmacaritamahākāvya 1–3
texts.basis: GRETIL header & colophon 'ity abhinandakavikṛtau rāmacarite mahākāvye' attribute the work to Abhinanda. Secondary (skt-en-mono: 'Abhinanda... undoubtedly the author of the Ramacarita; his father Satananda... under 9th-c. Pāla patronage'; Tubb via Dan Martin's Bibliography) distinguishes TWO contemporaneous Gauda Abhinandas: the Rāmacarita author = son of Śatānanda; the Kādambarīsāra author = son of Jayanta (Kashmiri). The Rāmacarita is by the SON OF ŚATĀNANDA, not the son of Jayanta.
texts.note: current_author conflates two distinct Abhinandas ('son of Jayanta/Śatānanda'). The Rāmacarita author is specifically Abhinanda son of Śatānanda (9th-c. Pāla court); the son-of-Jayanta Abhinanda wrote the Kādambarīsāra. Corrected to son of Śatānanda.

texts.title: (Saroruhavajra's Hevajra-lineage works, ed. Gerloff 2017)
texts.primary_author: Saroruhavajra (alias Padmavajra; root works) with later Hevajra-lineage authors
texts.basis: Gerloff diss. (source PDF) edits the surviving Sanskrit/Tibetan works of Saroruhavajra's Hevajra-lineage: not one author but Saroruhavajra's own root works (Hevajra commentary on difficult points = Padminī/Hevajrapañjikā, and a Hevajra-sādhana) plus commentarial/lineage works by later masters. Davidson and Kongtrul confirm Saroruhavajra=Padmavajra (~9th c.), originator-figure of one Hevajra system, author of many Tengyur works.
texts.note: current_author empty; this is a scholarly edition spanning a lineage. Core authorship = Saroruhavajra (=Padmavajra/Saroruha); additional surviving works by later lineage authors edited alongside. Gerloff is modern editor, not author.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 10, 29–33
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally Vyāsa; narrated by Śuka) — root text; Śrīdhara Svāmin (Bhāvārthadīpikā commentary)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: this e-text is BhP 10.29-33 WITH Śrīdhara's commentary (Bhāvārthadīpikā); closing colophon 'seyaṃ śrī...śrīdharanirmitā śrībhāgavatabhāvārthadīpikā' names Śrīdhara as commentator. Root Purāṇa is traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, narrated by Śuka ('śrīśuka uvāca'); historically anonymous/composite.
texts.note: Current attribution (anonymous/Vyāsa) covers only the mūla. The e-text CONFLATES the anonymous/traditional Purāṇa text with Śrīdhara Svāmin's distinct commentary (Bhāvārthadīpikā) per colophon. Rāsapañcādhyāyī chapters.

texts.title: Pauṣkara-Saṃhitā
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Pāñcarātra Āgama; framed as Bhagavān to Pauṣkara)
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editor Prabhakar Pandurang Apte (Tirupati 2006); no authorship claim. Text is a revealed Pāñcarātra Saṃhitā (dialogue Bhagavān–Pauṣkara). Secondary lit (Bhattacharyya, Eliot, Goudriaan, Buhnemann) treats it as one of the three oldest Pāñcarātra Saṃhitās (Sātvata, Jayākhya, Pauṣkara), pre-800 CE; no individual human author.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:History of the Tantric Religion (Bhattacharyya) | secondary:Hinduism and Buddhism (Eliot)
texts.note: No human author; revealed Āgama. Traditional ascription to divine revelation only. Confirms current 'anonymous/traditional'.

texts.title: Manubhāṣya
texts.basis: Edition: Manusmṛti with the Manubhāṣya of Medhātithi (ed. G. Jhā). Secondary lit (skt-en-mono / Olivelle; Silk diss.) treats Medhātithi as author of the earliest surviving full commentary (Manubhāṣya) on Manusmṛti; Bühler relied on it. Root Manusmṛti traditionally ascribed to Manu/Svayambhū (1.58), historically anonymous/composite Dharmaśāstra.
texts.note: Commentary securely by Medhātithi (~9th c.). Title bundles Manubhāṣya with the underlying Manusmṛti root text (different/anonymous authorship) — root mūla and bhāṣya are by distinct hands, hence conflation_flag.

texts.title: Mahāmāyātantra
texts.basis: Edition: Mahāmāyātantram WITH the Guṇavatī commentary of Ratnākaraśānti (ed. Samdhong Rinpoche & V. Dwivedi, 1992). Web (Tibetan Buddhist Encyclopedia; researchgate on Ratnākaraśānti's Māyā/Mahāmāyā): the tantra is a revealed yoginī/mother-tantra (no human author); Ratnākaraśānti (fl. 11th c.) authored the Guṇavatī ṭīkā grounding it in Yogācāra.
texts.source: gretil-edition | web:https://tibetanbuddhistencyclopedia.com (Mahamaya-tantra) | web:researchgate Ratnakarasanti Maya and Mahamaya
texts.note: Root tantra is anonymous revealed scripture (matches current). Edition/e-text CONFLATES it with Ratnākaraśānti's distinct Guṇavatī commentary; roles must be separated.

texts.title: Kulacūḍāmaṇi Nigama
texts.basis: Edition: Kulachudamani Tantra (ed. G. C. Vedantatirtha, Tantrik Texts 4, 1915). No source_url. Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra; Gendun Chopel) lists it among Kaula/Śākta Tantras with no individual author; a revealed Nigama (Devī–Śiva dialogue). No personal authorship attested.
texts.source: secondary:Encyclopedia of Tantra (Santideva) | secondary:Gendun Chopel (Lopez) | tradition
texts.note: Revealed Kaula Nigama; no human author. Confirms current 'anonymous/traditional'. (Nigama = Devī instructs Śiva, inverse of Āgama.)

texts.title: Saṃnyāsa Upaniṣads
texts.basis: Olivelle 1992 (Saṃnyāsa Upaniṣads, OUP) treats them as anonymous Hindu renunciation scriptures of varied date; the Adyar edition (ed. Mahadeva Sastri 1925) pairs the anonymous śruti texts with the 18th-c. commentary of Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (confirmed in Bronkhorst 1993, White 2009 reference lists).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Two Traditions of Meditation in Ancient India_Johannes Bronkhorst, 1993.txt; secondary:cleaned_Early Buddhists and Urban Centers...txt (Olivelle 1992)
texts.note: Anonymous corpus of renunciatory Upaniṣads; the edition conflates the anonymous root texts with Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin's commentary (Advaita ascetic, fl. c. 1700-1750).

texts.title: Vāmakeśvarīmata (Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed tantra); commentaries by Jayaratha (Vivaraṇa) and Bhāskararāya (Setubandha)
texts.basis: Encyclopedia of Tantra and Essais sur le rituel record the Vāmakeśvarīmata/Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava as a Śrīvidyā revealed tantra (no human author), transmitted with Jayaratha's Vivaraṇa (KSTS LXVI ed. Madhusudan Kaul) and Bhāskararāya's Setubandha commentary.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt; secondary:cleaned_Essais sur le rituel.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Śrīvidyā tantra (āgama, treated as revelation). Editions conflate the root with named commentators (Jayaratha; Bhāskararāya's Setubandha).

texts.title: Āścaryayogamālā
texts.basis: Edition (Bal Dev Prasad, 1914; on Internet Archive as 'Ascharya Yogamala Tantra Of Nagarjuna') ascribes it to Nāgārjuna. Web/secondary scholarship notes many alchemical/tantric works are pseudepigraphically ascribed to 'Nāgārjuna,' a name covering several distinct figures distinct from the 2nd-c. Madhyamaka philosopher; manuscript attributions to him need verification.
texts.note: Pseudepigraphic; 'Nāgārjuna' here is the legendary alchemist/Siddha, not the Madhyamaka master. Single (legendary) attributed author, so no conflation.

texts.title: Mantroddhāra
texts.basis: DSBC text (printed edition not stated). 'Mantroddhāra' is a generic Buddhist tantric mantra-extraction procedure/text type (cf. Tomabechi, 'The extraction of mantra (mantroddhāra) in the Sarvabuddhasamāyogatantra,' in Pramāṇakīrtiḥ Steinkellner Festschrift); no named author is attached to this short extraction text.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist tantric mantroddhāra text; title denotes a mantra-extraction genre rather than a named composition.

texts.title: Stavacintāmaṇi
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Isaacson e-text): root stava by Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa, commentary (vivṛti) by Kṣemarāja, ed. Mukunda Rama Shastri KSTS X 1918; colophon v.120 'stavacintāmaṇiṃ...bhaṭṭanārāyaṇo vyadhāt'. Secondary: Silburn, 'La bhakti: le Stavacintāmaṇi de Bhaṭṭanārāyaṇa'; Marjanovic transl. confirms Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa + Kṣemarāja commentary. The GRETIL e-text includes both root and commentary.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1748-0922.2012.01612_12.x
texts.note: Root devotional stava by Bhaṭṭanārāyaṇa (Kashmir Śaiva); Kṣemarāja (pupil of Abhinavagupta, 11th c.) wrote the vivṛti included in the same e-text. Distinct from the playwright Bhaṭṭanārāyaṇa of the Veṇīsaṃhāra.

texts.title: Bhāmatī
texts.basis: The Bhāmatī is securely Vācaspati Miśra's sub-commentary (ṭīkā) on Śaṅkara's Brahmasūtra-bhāṣya (Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism; Oxford Reference; Wikipedia Vācaspati Miśra). The edition cited bundles Bhāmatī with Amalānanda's Kalpataru and Appayyadīkṣita's Parimala, but those are separate works; the Bhāmatī itself has one author.
texts.note: Bhāmatī = Vācaspati Miśra (9th-10th c.), ṭīkā on Śaṅkara's Brahmasūtrabhāṣya; founds the Bhāmatī-prasthāna. Author of the Bhāmatī proper is single and secure; conflation is only at the edition-bundle level (Kalpataru/Parimala = other authors), not within this text item.

texts.title: Nyāyabhūṣaṇa
texts.basis: Edition header: Nyāyabhūṣaṇa is Bhāsarvajña's own auto-commentary (bhūṣaṇa) on his Nyāyasāra. Potter EIP Vol.2 treats it throughout as Bhāsarvajña's (the 'Bhūṣaṇakāra'), a single radical Naiyāyika author (c. 9th-10th c.); his Nyāya leans Pāśupata/Sāṃkhya. No multi-author structure: same person wrote both sūtra (Nyāyasāra) and commentary.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 2 - Potter 1977.txt
texts.note: Auto-commentary on Nyāyasāra; same author as root. Securely Bhāsarvajña.

texts.title: Amṛtanādopaniṣad
texts.basis: A minor Yoga Upaniṣad of the Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda; transmitted as śruti and therefore authorless by genre/tradition. Standard ed. in The Yoga Upaniṣads (Mahadeva Sastri, Adyar 1920) carries a later commentary by Upaniṣad-brahmayogin, but the Upaniṣad text itself has no historical author. No personal ascription found in secondary literature.
texts.note: Anonymous śruti (Yoga Upaniṣad). Commentary by Upaniṣad-brahmayogin is a separate, later layer not part of this text item.

texts.title: Somānanda II: Parātriśikātātparyadīpikā and Śāktavijñāna
texts.basis: Title bundles TWO distinct works under one heading. (a) Parātriśikā(=Parātriṃśikā)-tātparyadīpikā/-vivaraṇa: Somānanda's prose commentary on the Parātriṃśikā, which is LOST and known only through quotations in Abhinavagupta's own Parātriṃśikā commentary (web: wisdomlib/Wikipedia 'Somananda' — distinct from Abhinavagupta's homonymous work). (b) Śāktavijñāna: attributed to Somānanda but, per scholarship, 'there is no explicit proof' of the authorship (wisdomlib). The 'Somānanda II' label flags doubt whether this is the same Somānanda(nātha) as the Śivadṛṣṭi author. GRETIL header (Śāktavijñāna, ed. Zadoo 1947) names only 'Somānanda' as author and Zadoo as editor; no colophon disambiguation.
texts.note: Two separate works conflated: a lost Parātriṃśikā commentary (Somānanda, attested only via Abhinavagupta) + the Śāktavijñāna (ascribed to Somānanda, not securely). Attribution to 'Somānanda II' is uncertain/disputed.

texts.title: Śivadṛṣṭi
texts.basis: Root treatise (7 chs.), the first philosophical work of monistic Kashmir Śaivism, securely by Somānandanātha (skt-en-mono and Padoux-Festschrift cite the Śivadṛṣṭi as Somānanda's foundational Pratyabhijñā text). The edition used (Madhusudan Kaul Shastri, KSTS LIV, 1934) is explicitly 'with the Vṛtti by Utpaladeva' — Somānanda's disciple — a DISTINCT author of the running commentary (skt-en-mono bibliography: 'Śivadṛṣṭi with the vṛtti by Utpaladeva'). So the published text combines mūla by Somānanda + vṛtti by Utpaladeva.
texts.note: Mūla/kārikā by Somānanda(nātha); vṛtti by his pupil Utpaladeva. Two distinct authors in one edition.

texts.title: Pārānandasūtra
texts.basis: Tantric/Śākta sūtra-style work named after and traditionally ascribed to Pārānanda (Parānanda Muni); ed. Yegnanarayana Sastri, rev. Srinivasan, GOS 56, Baroda 1931 (archive.org confirms the GOS edition). No web/secondary source contradicts the eponymous ascription or splits it among multiple authors; the attribution is by tradition rather than firmly documented historically.
texts.note: Eponymous traditional ascription to Pārānanda Muni; no independent confirmation, no conflation.

texts.title: Kulālaśāstra
texts.basis: Obscure Kaula/Śaiva-Śākta scriptural text. No author named in the work item (current_author empty), no source_url content, and targeted web search (Kaula/Śaiva texts) returned no authorship for a 'Kulālaśāstra'; secondary-literature filename hits were incidental (general Kaula/Tantra studies), none ascribing this title to a person. As a Tantric śāstra it is transmitted as revealed/anonymous scripture.
texts.note: No author determinable; anonymous revealed Tantric śāstra. No conflation.

texts.title: Guhyāvalīvivṛti
texts.basis: Edition id (Dhīḥ journal of Rare Buddhist Texts, CIHTS Sarnath 2008) encodes the title as Guhyāvalīvivṛti, i.e. a vivṛti (commentary) on the Guhyāvalī. Root Guhyāvalī ascribed to the siddha Dauḍīpāda; the vivṛti to Ghanadeva. No GRETIL header (source_url null); web confirms Guhyāvalī/Vajrāvalī-class Guhyasamāja tantric exegetical literature but no contradicting attribution found.
texts.note: Two distinct roles in one title: mūla verses (Guhyāvalī) by Dauḍīpāda, the vivṛti commentary by Ghanadeva. Siddha ascription is traditional; both names from the edition's own framing.

texts.title: Prāṇābharaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header and colophon: 'Paṇḍitarājaśrī Jagannāthaviracitaṃ prāṇābharaṇaṃ tatkṛtayaiva ṭippaṇyā sametam' — verses and the accompanying ṭippaṇī both by Jagannātha himself (auto-commentary). Ed. Kāvyamālā vol. 1, Durgaprasad & Parab.
texts.note: Single author; auto-commentary (ṭippaṇī) by the poet himself, so no conflation. Colophon explicit.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītābhāṣya
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'with the commentary ascribed to Samkara'. The Bhagavadgītā mūla verses are part of the Mahābhārata (traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa), distinct from the bhāṣya. Scholarship treats Śaṅkara's Gītābhāṣya as genuine/undisputed (unlike many works ascribed to him).
texts.note: Title conflates mūla (Gītā, traditionally Vyāsa) and bhāṣya (Śaṅkara). The Gītābhāṣya is one of Śaṅkara's securely authentic works per scholarly consensus; the verses themselves are not his.

texts.title: Agni-Purāṇa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (composite; traditionally narrated by Agni to Vasiṣṭha)
texts.basis: GRETIL e-text (ed. Rajendralala Mitra, Bibliotheca Indica). Secondary lit (Wayman, Buddhist Insight 1984) speaks of 'the author of this chapter of the Agnipurana' — i.e. an encyclopedic, multi-chapter compilation with no single author. Traditional frame: Agni narrates to Vasiṣṭha.
texts.note: Composite encyclopedic Purāṇa, no historical author; traditional ascription is the divine narration frame (Agni→Vasiṣṭha). Multiple anonymous redactors over time but not distinct named authors, so conflation_flag false.

texts.title: Cakrasaṃvarastuti
texts.basis: GRETIL 108-Buddhist-stotras collection (= Janardan Shastri Pandey ed., Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha 1994): no author attribution or colophon for this stotra (BuSto_23). Gerloff (Saroruhavajra/Hevajra study) lists a 'Śrīcakrasamvarastuti' tentative edition from unidentified folios — no named author. Web searches yield no attribution.
texts.note: Genuinely anonymous Cakrasaṃvara hymn; no colophon names an author.

texts.title: Catuḥṣaṣṭisaṃvarastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL 108-Buddhist-stotras (Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha 1994): no author attribution or colophon for this text (BuSto_25). Listed in sanskritdocuments.org stotra index without an author; no scholarly attribution found on web.
texts.note: Genuinely anonymous Saṃvara (64-deity) hymn; no colophon authorship.

texts.title: Śāktavijñāna
texts.basis: GRETIL header names author Somananda, ed. J.D. Zadoo (KSTS 74, 1947); text presents the teaching as taught by Śambhu (verse 3) but no internal colophon naming a different author. Secondary (skt-en-mono) lists 'Saktavijnana by Somanandanatha' alongside his Śivadṛṣṭi; Somānanda is the founder of the Pratyabhijñā/Trika lineage (9th c.).
texts.note: Ascribed to Somānanda (Somānandanātha), author of the Śivadṛṣṭi; traditional single-author Kashmir Śaiva attribution, no commentary layer conflated here.

texts.title: Saundaryalaharī
texts.primary_author: traditionally ascribed to Śaṅkarācārya (disputed; likely not by him)
texts.basis: Edition: W. Norman Brown, HOS 43, 1958. Secondary literature uniform: Agehananda Bharati (Tantric Tradition 1965): 'traditionally but incorrectly ascribed' to Śaṃkarācārya; skt-en-mono: 'traditionally ascribed to Śaṅkarācārya'; Padoux (Vāc) and encyclopedias list it under Śaṅkara but as ascription. The work is often analyzed as two parts (Ānandalaharī vv.1-41 + Saundaryalaharī proper vv.42-100), but tradition treats it as one hymn; no distinct second author is identifiable, so flagged disputed rather than conflated.
texts.note: Pan-Indian Śākta hymn; persistently ascribed to Ādi Śaṅkara but rejected by scholarship (Bharati). Two-section structure (Ānandalaharī / Saundaryalaharī) noted but no separately named author, hence disputed not conflated.

texts.title: Vajrayoginyāḥ piṇḍārthastutiḥ
texts.basis: DSBC source; no edition given. Web search did not yield a secure author for this specific piṇḍārthastuti; a related but distinct Vajrayoginyāḥ stutipraṇidhānam (DSBC) is ascribed to Siddhācārya Virūpa, but that is a different title and cannot be transferred. No colophon naming an author located.
texts.note: Short Vajrayāna stuti; no secure authorial ascription found. Do not conflate with the Virūpa-ascribed stutipraṇidhānam. Remains anonymous/traditional.

texts.title: Mahāmantrānusāriṇī
texts.primary_author: anonymous (canonical Buddhist sūtra, attributed to the Buddha by tradition)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Peter Skilling, Mahāsūtras I, PTS 1994, pp.608-622; input Klaus Wille) credits no individual author; the text is a buddhavacana mahāsūtra/rakṣā text with the Buddha (bhagavān) as speaker; colophons C.8-C.9 give only title variants and benedictions, no composer.
texts.note: Canonical protective sūtra (mahāsūtra); traditionally buddhavacana, no historical author. Editor Skilling is editor, not author.

texts.title: Caryāgītikoṣa
texts.current_author: multiple mahāsiddhas (Kāṇha, Lui-pā, Śabara, Bhusuku and others)
texts.primary_author: multiple Buddhist Siddhācāryas/mahāsiddhas (Kāṇha/Kṛṣṇacaryā, Luipā, Śabara, Bhusuku, Saraha, et al.)
texts.basis: Edition: Bagchi & Shastri, Caryāgīti-koṣa of Buddhist Siddhas, Visva-Bharati 1956. Schaeffer (Saraha dissertation): the Caryagitikosa is 'a collection of fifty songs attributed to many of the same famous names of later tantric Buddhism' (the Siddhacaryas). Encyclopedia of Tantra: 'well-known Siddhacaryas, to whom are attributed the' songs. DSBC page itself gives no per-song colophon (only the Upodghāta visible).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Schaeffer_Saraha dissertation.txt; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt; web:http://www.dsbcproject.org/canon-text/content/833/1416
texts.note: Anthology of ~50 caryā songs each ascribed to a different mahāsiddha (Apabhraṃśa originals with Skt. chāyā); genuine multi-author conflation. Current_author already lists the siddhas correctly.

texts.title: Upadeśasāhasrī
texts.primary_author: Śaṅkara (Ādi Śaṅkara) — padya (verse) and gadya (prose) parts
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Sengaku Mayeda 1973; input Andrijanić) attributes to Śaṅkara; text in two parts — Padya (verse, prakaraṇas) and Gadya (GADYABANDHA prose). Mayeda (1965) 'The authenticity of the Upadeśasāhasrī ascribed to Śaṅkara' established genuine Śaṅkara authorship; Bronkhorst ed. (Mimamsa and Vedanta 2007) discusses arguments that Śaṅkara is the author and the question of whether all parts share one author, concluding standard attribution to Śaṅkara of this prakaraṇa work.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Mimamsa and Vedanta. Interaction and Continuity_Johannes Bronkhorst, ed., 2007.txt
texts.note: Verse (padya/Padyabandha) and prose (gadya/Gadyabandha) sections are both Śaṅkara's; one author, two genres — not a multi-author conflation. Authenticity defended by Mayeda; the most securely genuine of Śaṅkara's prakaraṇa works.

texts.title: Matsyendranātha: Jñānakārikā
texts.basis: Edition: Bagchi 1934 groups the Jñānakārikā among 'Some Minor Texts of the School of Matsyendranātha' (with Akulavīra-tantra, Kulānanda-tantra), i.e. school-attributed rather than autograph. Secondary lit (Eliade; Saddhatissa Festschrift list of works attributed to Matsyendranātha; Szantó cites Jñānakārikā 3.15 as a Kaula work) treats it as a Yoginī-kaula/Nātha-school text, not a securely authored composition.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_YOGA, Immortality & Freedom_Mircea Eliade; secondary:cleaned_Buddhist Studies in Honour of Hammalava Saddhatissa
texts.note: Traditional ascription to the legendary siddha Matsyendranātha; in practice a text of his school/lineage, not a verified individual author.

texts.title: Kriyāsāratattvaprakāśinīvyākhyā
texts.current_author: uncertain (a commentary on the Kriyāsāra of Nīlakaṇṭhaśivācārya)
texts.basis: Title names the commentary (Tattvaprakāśinīvyākhyā) on the Vīraśaiva Kriyāsāra. Secondary lit (Alper, Understanding Mantras; Potter Encyclopedia / Literature about the Philosophical Systems) confirms the Kriyāsāra is by Nīlakaṇṭha(śivācārya) and that a separate commentary 'Sarvasvabhūṣaṇa' exists on it; the author of this particular vyākhyā is not named in the consulted sources.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Understanding Mantras_Harvey P. Alper; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy
texts.note: Two distinct roles conflated under one title: root Kriyāsāra = Nīlakaṇṭhaśivācārya; commentary (vyākhyā) author unknown. Commentary author remains anonymous/uncertain.

texts.title: Mīmāṃsāślokavārttikakāśikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives base Mīmāṃsāślokavārttika + commentary Kāśikā of Sucaritamiśra, ed. K. Sāmbaśiva Śāstrī (TSS 90/99/150). Secondary lit uniformly attributes the Ślokavārttika to Kumārila Bhaṭṭa and the Kāśikā to Sucaritamiśra (kāśikākāra) — e.g. Kajiyama, Tripathi, Bronkhorst ed. (Mimamsa and Vedanta). NB the GRETIL auto-summary's 'Jaimini' is an error; Jaimini wrote the Mīmāṃsāsūtra, not the Ślokavārttika.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Introduction to Buddhist Philosophy...Tarkabhasa_Kajiyama; secondary:cleaned_Problem of Knowledge in Yogacara Buddhism_Tripathi
texts.note: current_author lists only the commentator. Title conflates root verses (Kumārila) and commentary (Sucaritamiśra); both attributions secure.

texts.title: Yogaśikhopaniṣat (Yogaśikhā Upaniṣad)
texts.basis: A minor (late) Yoga Upaniṣad with no individual author; classed among the Yoga Upaniṣads (Mallinson & Singleton, Roots of Yoga; Qvarnström notes it is a 'late Yogaśikhā Upaniṣad'). Edition (Adyar 1920/1938) supplies the commentary of Upaniṣad-brahmayogin (Potter EIP dates the commentator c.1350+). Base text itself remains anonymous/traditional Vedic-corpus ascription.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Roots of Yoga_Mallinson & Singleton; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies Vol.1_Potter
texts.note: Base Upaniṣad anonymous; the edition's commentary is by Upaniṣad-brahmayogin (recorded as a distinct role). Not a conflation of the work itself.

texts.title: kālikārahasya
texts.basis: A Śākta 'rahasya' (Kālī ritual/esoteric) text. No author found in local reference-literature (no hits) and no scholarly attribution located via web search; such Kālī-rahasya/nibandha materials circulate as anonymous tantric compilations without a named author.
texts.note: Genuinely unknown author; anonymous tantric Śākta text. No traditional individual ascription identified.

texts.title: Spandakārikā, with Vivṛti by Rāmakaṇṭha
texts.current_author: Spandakārikā by Kallaṭa Bhaṭṭa (or Vasugupta); Vivṛti commentary by Rāmakaṇṭha
texts.primary_author: Spandakārikā: Vasugupta or Kallaṭa Bhaṭṭa (disputed); Vivṛti: Rāmakaṇṭha (Rājānaka Rāma)
texts.basis: Secondary lit: Sanderson (Śaivism and the Tantric Traditions) states the Spandakārikā was 'composed by Vasugupta according to some, or by his pupil Kallaṭa according to others' — a genuine authorship dispute. Kṣemarāja attributes the verses to Vasugupta (Encyclopedia of Tantra); others (Abraham/Roy, Hazra) credit Kallaṭa. The Vivṛti is by Rājānaka Rāma = Rāmakaṇṭha (Potter/Literature lists 'Rajanaka Rama's Vivrti' and 'Ramakantha's Spandakarikavivrti').
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_Śaivism and the tantric traditions; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy
texts.note: Two-author conflation plus a dispute: root kārikās attributed to Vasugupta OR Kallaṭa Bhaṭṭa (unresolved); commentary (Vivṛti/Vivaraṇa) securely by Rāmakaṇṭha (Rājānaka Rāma, 10th c.).

texts.title: Tantrasadbhāva (selected chapters)
texts.basis: Edition is Junglan Bang's study of selected chapters from 11th-c. Nepalese Śaiva MSS; no human author. Secondary lit (Goudriaan ed., Mayer 1996, Tanemura, Jacobsen 'Yoga Powers') treats it as a revealed Trika/Bhairava scripture of the Vidyāpīṭha, cited by Kṣemarāja (Alper 1989); Dyczkowski's etext from MSS. Scriptural revelation, no named composer.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism (Goudriaan 1992); secondary:cleaned_Yoga Powers (Jacobsen 2012); web
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Śaiva Tantra (Trika Vidyāpīṭha); cited by Kṣemarāja; current_author already empty.

texts.title: Dharmottarapradīpa
texts.primary_author: Durveka Miśra (Dharmottarapradīpa); subcommentary on Dharmottara's Nyāyabinduṭīkā on Dharmakīrti's Nyāyabindu
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Krasser/Malvania ed.): three-tiered hierarchy — Dharmakīrti's Nyāyabindu (root), Dharmottara's Nyāyabinduṭīkā (commentary), Durvekamiśra's Dharmottarapradīpa (subcommentary, c. 1100). The edited title-text is Durveka's pradīpa but it embeds the lower layers by distinct authors.
texts.note: Subcommentary securely by Durveka Miśra (c.1100); embeds Dharmottara's ṭīkā and Dharmakīrti's mūla — distinct authors per layer.

texts.title: Triśikhibrāhmaṇopaniṣat (Triśikhibrāhmaṇa Upaniṣad)
texts.basis: Edition (Adyar, A. Mahadeva Sastri 1920) prints the text with the commentary of Śrī Upaniṣad-Brahma-Yogin. Secondary lit (White, Yoga in Practice; Eliade) lists Triśikhibrāhmaṇa among the late minor Yoga Upaniṣads — anonymous Vedic-style scripture, no individual author. The 18th-c. commentary is by the named Brahmayogin.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_White_Yoga in Practice; secondary:cleaned_YOGA Immortality & Freedom (Eliade); edition
texts.note: Upaniṣad itself anonymous (late Yoga Upaniṣad); commentary by Upaniṣad-Brahma-Yogin — distinct named commentator.

texts.title: Rasahṛdayatantra
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: root Rasahṛdayatantra by Govindabhagavatpāda, commentary Mugdhāvabodhinī by Caturbhujamiśra (son of Hariharamiśra). Secondary (N.N. Bhattacharyya, History of the Tantric Religion) calls it 'Bhikṣu Govinda's Rasahṛdaya'; Maas dates the Rasahṛdayatantra to 10th-11th c. The traditional ascription to the alchemist Govinda(bhagavatpāda) is sometimes (popularly) conflated with Śaṅkara's guru Govinda — Maas flags the identification as uncertain.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion (Bhattacharyya); secondary:cleaned_Maas_Rasayana
texts.note: Mūla by Govindabhagavatpāda (Govinda Bhikṣu, alchemist; identity vs Śaṅkara's guru uncertain); commentary Mugdhāvabodhinī by Caturbhujamiśra — distinct authors.

texts.title: Jñānasiddhyāgama
texts.basis: Sanderson (The Śaiva Age) refers to 'the scripture Jñānasiddhyāgama' alongside the Siddhāntapaddhati of Jñānaśiva, treating it as a revealed Śaiva āgama (associated with the post-12th-c. Tripurā/Naṭarāja Śākta-Śaiva milieu). Not to be confused with the Buddhist Jñānasiddhi of Indrabhūti. No named human author.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Śaiva āgama (Sanderson); distinct from Buddhist Jñānasiddhi of Indrabhūti. No author.

texts.title: Gaṇakārikā 1 (with Ratnaṭīkā)
texts.current_author: kārikās attributed to Haradattācārya, with the Ratnaṭīkā of Bhāsarvajña
texts.basis: GRETIL header credits Bhāsarvajña (edition titled 'Gaṇakārikā of Bhāsarvajña', GOS 15, Dalal 1920); commentary cites 'bhāṣyakṛt' but the ṭīkā author is unnamed in colophon. Potter (EIP Vol.2) and Tattvasaṅgraha (Jha) credit Bhāsarvajña (10th c.) with authorship of the Gaṇakārikās themselves; traditional/web (wisdomlib, Dasgupta) ascribe the kārikās to Haradatta(ācārya) with Bhāsarvajña's Ratnaṭīkā commentary. Pāśupata handbook either way.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies Vol.2 (Potter 1977); secondary:cleaned_Sanskrit and Indian Studies (Ingalls Fs); web
texts.note: Disputed: kārikās traditionally ascribed to Haradattācārya but credited to Bhāsarvajña by Potter/edition; Ratnaṭīkā commentary securely by Bhāsarvajña (10th c.). Roles separated.

texts.title: Ghaṭakarparakāvya
texts.current_author: Ghaṭakarpara (traditionally ascribed to Kālidāsa); NOTE: the project hint 'Abhinavagupta' refers to the commentator, not the poet
texts.primary_author: Anonymous poet, traditionally ascribed to Kālidāsa (kāvya); Abhinavagupta (vivṛti/commentary)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: kāvya poet not named in MS; commentary 'Ghaṭakarparavivṛti' colophon 'śrīmadabhinavaguptapādaracitavivṛtyupetaṃ' = by Abhinavagupta; ed. B. Parlier 1975. Web (Wikipedia/scholarship): Ghaṭakarpara is among works wrongly ascribed to Kālidāsa, attribution disputed/pseudepigraphic.
texts.note: Two distinct persons conflated by edition title: the (pseudo-/traditionally-Kālidāsa) poet of the kāvya vs Abhinavagupta the commentator. Kāvya attribution to Kālidāsa is spurious/traditional; sometimes the poet 'Ghaṭakarpara' is named eponymously from the last verse.

texts.title: Dīkṣottara
texts.basis: Web/scholarship (Goodall, Sanderson context): Dīkṣottara is a Siddhāntatantra transmitted in the North alongside Sarvajñānottara and Kālottara, part of the anonymous Śaiva 'Āgamaśāstra' corpus (Niśvāsa/Kālottara orbit). No human author; revealed scripture. No GRETIL header (Muktabodha library only).
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Śaiva tantra; presented as Śiva's teaching. Traditionally part of the Kālottara/Niśvāsa scriptural corpus; no individual ascription.

texts.title: Vakroktijīvita
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'śrīmadrājānakakuntakaviracitaṃ vakroktijīvitam' (kārikās + author's own auto-commentary/vṛtti, both by Kuntaka); ed. Krishnamoorthy 1977. Secondary lit (skt-en-mono) cites Kuntaka's Vakroktijīvita as his work.
texts.note: Verses and auto-vṛtti both by Kuntaka, so NOT a multi-author conflation despite kārikā/vṛtti structure. Attribution secure.

texts.title: Tattvajñānasaṁsiddhi-mahāsukharakāśikā-ṭīkā
texts.primary_author: Bhikṣu Śrī Dhyānacandra (ṭīkā Mahāsukhaprakāśikā); Śūnyasamādhivajra (root Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi)
texts.basis: DSBC header/colophon: ṭīkā 'Mahāsukhaprakāśikā' by Madhyamakaruci Bhikṣuśrī Dhyānacandra ('...madhyamakaruci bhikṣuśrīdhyānacandra viracitāyāṁ mahāsukhaprakāśikāyāṁ ṭīkāyāṁ'). Secondary (Orofino 1994, Sekoddeśa TJS): root Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi by Śūnyasamādhivajrapāda, with the Mahāsukhaprakāśikā ṭīkā by Bhikṣuśrīdhyānacandra.
texts.note: Two distinct authors: root verses by Śūnyasamādhivajra(pāda) vs the ṭīkā Mahāsukhaprakāśikā by Dhyānacandra. (Note: a separate Marmakalikāpañjikā on this root is by Vīryaśrīmitra per Pandey's Sarnath 2000 ed. — different commentary, not this one.)

texts.title: Vajra(mahā)bhairavatantra
texts.primary_author: Anonymous (revealed tantra; traditionally revealed to Lalitavajra)
texts.basis: Edition: Wenta, The Vajramahābhairavatantra (D.Phil. Oxford 2020; Wisdom 2023). Secondary (Kapstein ed. 2009; Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism): Yamāntaka-Vajrabhairava-tantra is a revealed scripture, traditionally revealed in Uḍḍiyāna to the 10th-c. master Lalitavajra (*Mañjuśrīkīrti?); no human author. Buddhavacana framing.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Buddhism between Tibet and China_Matthew Kapstein, ed. 2009.txt + web:https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:3e18e7fb-22de-407d-a2c8-3b3e43c7b7aa
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Buddhist tantra. Lalitavajra is the traditional revealer/discoverer, not the author. Wenta is the modern editor, not the author.

texts.title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header: text traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa (Vedavyāsa/Bādarāyaṇa). Composite Purāṇic compilation; modern scholarship treats it as anonymous multi-layered work, conventionally dated and not by a single historical author.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Vyāsa recorded but not historical; composite/anonymous Purāṇa. Single composite work, so no multi-author conflation flag despite redactional layering.

texts.title: Sāṃkhyatattvakaumudī
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Potter EIP vol.2; Bibliography of Indian Philosophy) repeatedly lists the Tattvakaumudī as Vācaspatimiśra's commentary (ṭīkā) on the Sāṃkhyakārikā of Īśvarakṛṣṇa; standard KSS 123 ed. Attribution of the kaumudī itself is uncontested.
texts.source: secondary:Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy; secondary:Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies Vol.2 - Potter
texts.note: Kaumudī securely by Vācaspatimiśra (~10th c.); it is a commentary on a separate root text, the Sāṃkhyakārikā of Īśvarakṛṣṇa. Roles listed for completeness but no genuine conflation within this commentary.

texts.title: Saundaryalaharī
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Śaṃkara (attrib.)', no internal colophon confirming it. Secondary lit: Bharati (Tantric Tradition) calls it 'traditionally but incorrectly ascribed' to Śaṅkarācārya; Monier-Williams and others say 'traditionally ascribed'. W.N. Brown's HOS 43 ed. disputes Śaṅkara authorship.
texts.note: Tantric Śākta hymn; traditional ascription to Ādi Śaṅkara is widely rejected by scholars. Tradition sometimes splits it: first 41 verses (Ānandalaharī) said to be revealed/by a separate hand vs. the rest — but no secure named author. Kept as disputed/effectively anonymous.

texts.title: Mahānayaprakāśa (anonymous)
texts.current_author: anonymous (one of several Krama works of this title; sometimes ascribed to Arṇasiṃha)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Krama work; sometimes ascribed to Arṇasiṃha)
texts.basis: Secondary lit: skt-en-mono notes 'Mahānayaprakāśa (Trivandrum edition)' as a Krama tract; Encyclopedia of Tantra cites it on Krama decline. Web (Springer/MDPI Śākta studies): there are THREE distinct Kashmiri works titled Mahānayaprakāśa — by Śitikaṇṭha (Old Kashmiri), by Arṇasiṃha, and an anonymous Sanskrit one. TSS 130 (Sāmbaśiva Śāstrī 1937) is the anonymous Sanskrit text.
texts.note: Title shared by several distinct authors (Śitikaṇṭha, Arṇasiṃha, and anonymous); THIS item (TSS 130) is the anonymous one. A library-catalogue ascription of TSS 130 to Abhinavagupta is a confusion across the homonymous titles. Conflation is across works sharing the title, not within this single work, so conflation_flag kept false; no secure author.

texts.title: Satyaśāsanaparīkṣā
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Potter EIP; Lit. about Philosophical Systems) lists Satyaśāsanaparīkṣā under Vidyānanda/Vidyānandin (Jaina/Digambara). Web (Borgland 2020 ed./tr. Harrassowitz; Springer JIP): consistently attributed to the Digambara philosopher Vidyānandin (~10th c.); text survives incompletely (some announced chapters, e.g. on Anekānta, not extant).
texts.source: secondary:Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies Vol.1 - Potter; web:https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10781-011-9147-7
texts.note: Single author, secure. Transmitted incomplete (planned chapters lost) but no authorship dispute or multi-author structure.

texts.title: Śivastotrāvalī
texts.basis: GRETIL header: author Utpaladeva, based on Bailly ed. (text = verses only, no commentary).
