Secondary lit (skt-en-mono, Dan Martin bibliography, Potter EIP): Śivastotrāvalī by Utpaladeva with a commentary by Kṣemarāja; the famous devotional hymn collection of the Pratyabhijñā master Utpaladeva (~925-975).
texts.note: Verses securely by Utpaladeva. A separate commentary by Kṣemarāja exists but is NOT part of this GRETIL e-text (verses only), so no conflation in this item.

texts.title: Śrīkaṇṭha: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Ratnatrayaparīkṣā
texts.basis: Secondary lit: Goudriaan (Ritual and Speculation) and Padoux (Vāc) both give 'Ratnatraya of Bhaṭṭa Śrīkaṇṭha, with commentary'; Potter EIP / Lit. about Philosophical Systems list Ratnatrayaparīkṣā (Śaiva Siddhānta) by Śrīkaṇṭha with commentaries by Rāmakaṇṭha and Aghoraśiva.
texts.source: secondary:Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism (Goudriaan ed.); secondary:Vac_Padoux 1990
texts.note: This item is the Ratnatraya(parīkṣā) kārikā by Śrīkaṇṭha alone. The Aṣṭaprakaraṇa is a multi-author anthology (other tracts — Tattvaprakāśa, Bhogakārikā, Mokṣakārikā etc. — are by Sadyojyoti, and a Tattvasaṅgraha by Sadyojyoti), and commentaries on the Ratnatraya are by Rāmakaṇṭha/Aghoraśiva; but the Ratnatraya text itself is single-author Śrīkaṇṭha, so conflation_flag is for the collection, not this work.

texts.title: Utpaladeva: Īśvarasiddhi
texts.basis: Edition (Kaul, Siddhitrayī, KSTS 34) ascribes it to Rājānaka Utpala; Sanderson notes Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha quotes the Īśvarasiddhi of Utpaladeva (teacher of Lakṣmaṇagupta). One of the three Siddhi treatises (Siddhitrayī) by Utpaladeva.
texts.note: Part of Utpaladeva's Siddhitrayī (Ajaḍapramātṛ-, Īśvara-, Sambandha-siddhi); securely his.

texts.title: Subodhamañjarī
texts.basis: Secondary (skt-en-mono): two BHU manuscripts, 'Svabodhamañjarī' and 'Subodhamañjarī'; per colophons the author is Vāmanadatta (= Vāmananātha / Śrī-vāmana / Hrasvanātha, a Kashmiri Śaiva author cited by Śivopādhyāya and Abhinavagupta). No source_url/edition; web search found nothing specific.
texts.note: Colophon ascribes to Vāmanadatta; identity overlaps with Dvayasampatti/Bodhavilāsa author cluster (Vāmananātha/Hrasvanātha). current_author was blank.

texts.title: Mokṣopāya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Slaje et al. critical ed.) titles it 'Anonymus Casmiriensis: Mokṣopāya'; author unknown. Internal frame has Vālmīki teaching Bharadvāja, which is literary, not historical authorship.
texts.note: Anonymous Kashmirian work; later vulgate redaction = Yogavāsiṣṭha pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki. Traditional ascription to Vālmīki noted; not historical.

texts.title: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvṛtti
texts.basis: GRETIL header (input Somadeva Vasudeva; Kaul 1921 rev. Torella 1994): Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā with the author's own vṛtti. Colophon: 'udayākarasūnunā īśvarapratyabhijñeyam utpalenopapāditā'; 'samāpteyam īśvarapratyabhijñāvṛttiḥ'.
texts.note: Auto-commentary: both kārikā and vṛtti by Utpaladeva (son of Udayākara). Same author for both layers — not a conflation.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā
texts.primary_author: Bhagavadgītā: anonymous (trad. Vyāsa); Sarvatobhadra commentary: Rājānaka Rāmakaṇṭha
texts.basis: Edition (Kaul, KSTS 64) = Bhagavadgītā with the commentary Sarvatobhadra of Rājānaka Rāmakaṇṭha. Root Gītā is anonymous/traditionally part of Mahābhārata ascribed to Vyāsa; Sarvatobhadra is a Kashmiri commentary by Rāmakaṇṭha (c. 9th c., one of the oldest Gītā commentaries). Web confirms authorship of Sarvatobhadra.
texts.note: Title conflates the anonymous/Vyāsa-ascribed Gītā with Rāmakaṇṭha's Sarvatobhadra ṭīkā — distinct authors/roles separated.

texts.title: Ajaḍapramātṛsiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header (input Somadeva Vasudeva; Kaul 1921, KSTS 34): author 'śrīmān-mahāmāheśvarācārya-utpaladevapāda'. Part of Utpaladeva's Siddhitrayī. (Edition includes editor Madhusudan Kaul's framing verses, but the text is Utpaladeva's.)
texts.note: Securely Utpaladeva; first of the three Siddhi treatises. Colophon-era note (Śaka 1841) is the editor's, not original authorship.

texts.title: Mokṣopāya
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Anonymus Casmiriensis', no colophon naming author; Slaje critical edition treats it as the work of an anonymous Kashmiri author (10th c., Pradyumna hill, Śrīnagar). Web (Wikipedia/Slaje): real author unknown; later pseudepigraphically framed as a Vālmīki–Vasiṣṭha dialogue, which produced the recast Yogavāsiṣṭha.
texts.note: Genuinely anonymous root text. Traditional/pseudepigraphic ascription to Vālmīki (sage Vasiṣṭha as speaker) belongs to the later Yogavāsiṣṭha recension, not this Mokṣopāya edition.

texts.title: Sambandhasiddhi
texts.basis: Edition (Kaul Shastri, KSTS 34, Srinagar 1921: 'The Siddhitrayī ... of Rājānaka Utpala Deva') ascribes the Sambandhasiddhi — one of the three Siddhi tracts (Ajaḍapramātṛsiddhi, Īśvarasiddhi, Sambandhasiddhi) — to Utpaladeva. Secondary lit (Pratyabhijñā / Kashmir Śaiva sources) consistently attributes the Siddhitrayī to Utpaladeva; he also wrote his own vṛtti.
texts.source: tradition; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Securely Utpaladeva (c. 925-975). The Siddhitrayī carried Utpaladeva's autocommentary (vṛtti); this work-item targets the kārikā text itself.

texts.title: Śivastotrāvalī
texts.basis: Hymns (stotra collection) composed by Utpaladeva; the edition (KSTS 15, Srinagar 1917) prints them with the Vivṛti commentary of Rājānaka Kṣemarāja (Abhinavagupta's disciple, Utpaladeva's great-granddisciple). Web confirms Kṣemarāja authored the Śivastotrāvalīvivṛti and describes assembling the verses left in disorder.
texts.note: Two distinct authors: root hymns by Utpaladeva vs. Vivṛti by Kṣemarāja. Current_author lists only Utpaladeva; the commentary author was omitted.

texts.title: Mṛgendrapaddhatiṭīkā
texts.current_author: Naṭeśaguru (alias Vaktraśambhu); on the Mṛgendrapaddhati attributed to Aghoraśivācārya
texts.primary_author: Naṭeśaguru alias Vaktraśambhu (ṭīkā); on Aghoraśiva's Mṛgendrapaddhati
texts.basis: Sanderson, 'The Śaiva Age': cites 'Vaktraśambhu, Mṛgendrapaddhativyākhyā' and 'Mṛgendrapaddhativyākhyā of Vaktraśambhu' (IFP MS Transcript 1021); base Mṛgendrapaddhati by Aghoraśiva (Kriyākramadyotikā completed 1157). Web (IFP project): commentator Naṭeśaguru = Vaktraśambhu, son of Śaṃkara, confirmed by the ṭīkā's closing verse/colophon.
texts.note: The ṭīkā itself is securely Naṭeśaguru/Vaktraśambhu; it comments on a distinct author's work (Aghoraśiva's Mṛgendrapaddhati). Two distinct persons bound in one title -> conflation_flag true. Current_author already captured both.

texts.title: Siddhitraya: Part 3: Saṃvitsiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'paramācāryaśrīmadyāmunamunisamanugṛhīte siddhitraye - saṃvitsiddhiḥ', 'siddhāñjananāmakavyākhyopetā' — root Saṃvitsiddhi by Yāmuna, accompanied by a Siddhāñjana commentary whose author the header does not name. Web/Wikipedia confirm Siddhitraya is Yāmuna's; the Siddhāñjana glosses appear to be the modern editorial commentary (Ubhaya Vedanta Granthamala ed., Ramanujachari/Narasimha Ayyangar), not a famous classical commentator.
texts.note: Root verses securely Yāmuna (c. 10th c. Śrīvaiṣṇava). The GRETIL etext bundles a 'Siddhāñjana' commentary by a different/unnamed (likely modern editorial) hand -> conflation. Commentator identity uncertain; flagged low confidence.

texts.title: Lalitā Sahasranāma
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (framed as revealed by the Vāgdevatās; dialogue of Hayagrīva and Agastya)
texts.basis: The Lalitāsahasranāma is an anonymous nāmastotra forming part of the Lalitopākhyāna in the (uttarabhāga of the) Brahmāṇḍapurāṇa, traditionally framed as revealed by the Vāgdevatās in a Hayagrīva–Agastya dialogue. The cited edition (Pansikar, NSP 1935) prints it with the Saubhāgyabhāskara commentary of Bhāskararāya Makhin (c. 1685-1775). Web (IFP catalog, wisdomlib) confirm Bhāskararāya's authorship of the bhāṣya.
texts.note: Anonymous/traditional stotra root vs. named commentator Bhāskararāya. The work-item title is the stotra but the edition (Saubhāgyabhāskara) conflates it with Bhāskararāya's commentary -> conflation_flag true.

texts.title: Timirodghāṭa(tantra)
texts.basis: No source_url. Secondary lit (ag over reference-literature/) returned no hits for the title. Web searches (Śaiva and Buddhist/Vajrayāna framings) found nothing on a 'Timirodghāṭa' tantra. As a tantra/scripture it is a revealed, authorless category text with no named human author.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed tantra ('Dispelling of Darkness'); no human author named in any source. Current 'anonymous/traditional' confirmed.

texts.title: Sarvamatasaṃgraha
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Ganapati Sastri, TSS 62, 1918; input O. Hellwig) names NO author and the text carries no author colophon. This doxography (Cārvāka...Vedānta) is distinct from Mādhava's Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha; it is sometimes loosely associated with the Advaita/Śaṅkara orbit but with no secure ascription. Secondary lit gave no firm author.
texts.note: Anonymous Vedānta-leaning doxographical compendium; not to be conflated with the Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha. Attribution genuinely uncertain; current label confirmed.

texts.title: Vivekacūḍāmaṇi
texts.current_author: Traditionally attributed to Śaṃkara (Ādi Śaṅkara); authorship disputed by modern scholarship
texts.basis: GRETIL header self-attributes ('Samkara (attrib.)') and colophon reads 'iti śaṅkarācāryaviracitaṃ vivekacuḍāmaṇi'. Secondary lit (Potter EIP vol.3) lists it among prakaraṇa works frequently ascribed to but often rejected for Śaṅkara. Web: scholarly debate (Hacker pro, Ingalls contra, Grimes 'likely'); attribution traditional and contested.
texts.note: Colophon names Śaṅkarācārya; modern scholarship divided (Ingalls/Hacker/Grimes). Single (disputed) author, not multi-author. Current 'disputed' label confirmed.

texts.title: Ratnatrayaparīkṣā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Dvivedi, Aṣṭaprakaraṇam 1988; input D. Goodall) and the text's own colophon name the author: 'ratnatrayaparīkṣeyaṃ kṛtā śrīkaṇṭhasūriṇā ... śrīrāmakaṇṭhasadvṛttiṃ'. Secondary lit (Watson 2006, 'Self's Awareness of Itself') places Śrīkaṇṭha as a 10th-11th c. Kashmiri Śaiva Siddhānta exegete in the lineage of Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha/Rāmakaṇṭha.
texts.note: Authorial colophon names Śrīkaṇṭhasūri; work follows Rāmakaṇṭha's vṛtti (influence, not co-authorship). Secure single author. Current attribution confirmed.

texts.title: Svabodhodayamañjarī
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Torella 2000 ed.; input D. Cuneo) and colophon v.45/44 name the author: 'mīmāṃsāvanasiṃhasya harṣadattasya sūnunā / kṛtā vāmanadattena svabodhodayamañjarī'. Secondary lit (Sanderson, 'History through textual criticism') identifies the Śaiva Vāmanadatta, son of Harṣadatta, author of Bodhavilāsa and Svabodhodayamañjarī (distinct from his namesake).
texts.note: Colophon names Vāmanadatta son of Harṣadatta; Sanderson distinguishes him from another Vāmanadatta. Secure single author. Current attribution confirmed.

texts.title: Aṣṭāṅganighaṇṭu
texts.basis: GRETIL header titles it 'Vāhaṭa: Aṣṭāṅganighaṇṭu' (ed. P.V. Sharma 1973) but makes no explicit authorial claim and shows no colophon. Web/secondary: it is an Āyurvedic nighaṇṭu (synonyms of ~257+ drugs) drawn from the Aṣṭāṅga corpus, traditionally ascribed to a Vāgbhaṭa ('Vāhaṭa' = Prakritic form of Vāgbhaṭa). The 'two Vāgbhaṭas' (elder/Vṛddha vs younger) problem leaves the precise person unresolved.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Vāhaṭa/Vāgbhaṭa; which of the homonymous Vāgbhaṭas is unsettled. Not multi-author. Current attribution confirmed as traditional, not secure.

texts.title: Pāśupatabrahma Upaniṣad
texts.basis: Edition title (Śaiva Upaniṣads with commentary of Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin) names the commentator; secondary lit (Potter, Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies vol.1; Eliade) lists Pāśupatabrahma among the minor/late Yoga-Śaiva Upaniṣads with no named author and notes Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin commented on it. Web confirms anonymous late Upaniṣad.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 1 - Potter.txt; tradition
texts.note: Root Upaniṣad anonymous (late, of the Atharvaveda corpus); the edition's commentary is by Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (Rāmacandrendra Sarasvatī), 18th c. — distinct role conflated under one title.

texts.title: Rasādhyāya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Kashi Sanskrit Series 79, ed. Ramakrishna Sarma) does not name the author or commentator; presents the work as composed by an unnamed disciple in the tradition of Śrī Kaṅkāla. Web (Exotic India ed. of Rasādhyāyaḥ 'of the disciple of Śrī Kaṅkālaya'): author is an unnamed disciple of Kaṅkālaya Yogi per opening/closing stanzas; his name is not given. Edition prints the mūla with a ṭīkā (separate, unnamed hand).
texts.note: Rasaśāstra/alchemy text; author self-identifies only as a pupil of Kaṅkālaya (Kaṅkālanātha), name unstated -> anonymous, traditional teacher-ascription noted. ṭīkā is a distinct (anonymous) layer printed with the text.

texts.title: Laghuyogavāsiṣṭha, with commentary Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā
texts.current_author: abridgement traditionally ascribed to Abhinanda (of Kashmir); commentary Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā by Ātmasukha (Mummaḍideva)
texts.primary_author: Abhinanda (abridgement); Ātmasukha (Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā, prakaraṇas 1–3); Mummaḍi Devarāya (Saṃsārataraṇī, prakaraṇas 4–6)
texts.basis: Web (mlbd/Exotic India on the Nirṇaya Sāgara/Pansikar edition): the abridgement (Laghuyogavāsiṣṭha) is by Abhinanda; the Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā commentary by Ātmasukha covers the first three prakaraṇas, while the last three prakaraṇas carry Mummaḍi Devarāya's Saṃsārataraṇī commentary — both printed together. Secondary-lit hits for 'Abhinanda'/'Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā' in reference-literature were unrelated (Jain/Buddhist), so attribution rests on edition + web.
texts.note: Current_author wrongly equates Ātmasukha with Mummaḍideva; in fact they are two different commentators — Ātmasukha (Vāsiṣṭhacandrikā, prak. 1–3) and Mummaḍi Devarāya (Saṃsārataraṇī, prak. 4–6). Plus Abhinanda as abridger. Three distinct hands.

texts.title: Nārada-Purāṇa 2 [Nāradīya-Purāṇa 2]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet input, no edition stated) names no author; chapter colophons read 'iti śrībṛhannāradīyapurāṇottarabhāge ... adhyāyaḥ' identifying the work as the Bṛhannāradīya/Nāradīya Mahāpurāṇa, Uttarabhāga, with no human author. Purāṇic tradition ascribes the corpus to Vyāsa.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāpurāṇa (Uttarabhāga); colophon style 'śrībṛhannāradīyapurāṇa'. Traditional ascription to Vyāsa only — no individual author identifiable.

texts.title: hayaśīrṣapañcarātra
texts.basis: Edition (Hayaśīrṣa Pañcarātra, Ādikāṇḍa, Varendra Research Society 1976) presents a revealed Pāñcarātra Saṃhitā/Āgama; web (architexturez study of chs.1–14; wisdomlib) describes it as a Pāñcarātra Āgama of ~9th c., probably Eastern India (Bengal/Orissa), named for Hayaśīrṣa the revealer-form of Viṣṇu, with no human author. No relevant attribution in reference-literature (only passing mentions).
texts.note: Revealed Vaiṣṇava Āgama (Pāñcarātra), framed as Viṣṇu's revelation in his horse-headed (Hayaśīrṣa) form -> anonymous by genre; no individual author.

texts.title: Nārada-Purāṇa 1 [Nāradīya-Purāṇa 1]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet input, no edition stated) names no author; colophons read 'iti śrībṛhannāradīyapurāṇe pūrvabhāge ...' identifying it as the Bṛhannāradīya/Nāradīya Mahāpurāṇa, Pūrvabhāga, with no human author. Purāṇic tradition ascribes the corpus to Vyāsa.
texts.note: Anonymous Mahāpurāṇa (Pūrvabhāga); colophon 'śrībṛhannāradīyapurāṇe pūrvabhāge'. Traditional Vyāsa ascription only.

texts.title: Hetutattvopadeśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Tucci ed., input Wille) gives author Jitāri; colophon 'kṛtir iyaṃ paṇḍitarājajitāripādānām'. Secondary lit (Tucci, Minor Buddhist Texts I; Orofino/Sekoddeśa; Hugon) lists it as 'Hetutattvopadeśa of Jitari'.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Minor Buddhist Texts. Part I _Giuseppe Tucci, 1956.txt
texts.note: Colophon names Paṇḍitarāja Jitāripāda (Jitāri, c. 940-1000, teacher of Atiśa). Secure single-author work.

texts.title: Sāmānyadūṣaṇadikprasāritā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Haraprasad Shastri, Six Buddhist Nyaya Tracts) gives 'Paṇḍitāśokaviracitā'; colophon 'kṛtir iyaṃ paṇḍitāśokasya'. Secondary lit (Tripathi 1972; skt-en-mono) cites 'Sāmānya-dūṣaṇadik-prasāritā by Pandita Asoka'.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Problem of Knowledge in Yogacara Buddhism_Chhote Lal Tripathi, 1972.txt
texts.note: Buddhist nyāya tract refuting universals (sāmānya). Author Paṇḍitāśoka secure; dating debated (verse shared with Jñānaśrīmitra's Apohaprakaraṇa, per skt-en-mono).

texts.title: Paraśambhumahimnastava (Paraśambhumahimnaḥstava)
texts.current_author: attributed to one styled 'Krodhabhaṭṭāraka' (alias; the epithet is traditionally associated with the sage Durvāsas in the Krama tradition)
texts.primary_author: ascribed to Durvāsas (under the alias Krodhabhaṭṭāraka)
texts.basis: No critical edition; Muktabodha/sanskritdocuments e-text ascribes the stava to Durvāsas (parashambhUmahimnastavadUrvAsa). Krama tradition (studied by A. Sanderson, 'The Śaiva Exegesis of Kashmir') treats the legendary sage Durvāsas, styled Krodhabhaṭṭāraka, as revealer/author of Krama stotras. No verifiable historical author found in local reference-literature.
texts.note: Devotional Krama stava. Attribution to Durvāsas/Krodhabhaṭṭāraka is legendary (the sage is a mythic lineage-founder); actual human author unknown. Pseudepigraphic.

texts.title: Hetutattvopadeśa
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC header (Chattopadhyaya 1939 reconstruction) attributes to 'paṇḍitapravarajitāripāda'; colophon 'iti paṇḍitapravarajitāripādakṛto hetutattvopadeśaḥ sampūrṇaḥ'. Same work as jithettu. Tibetan translators (Kumārakalaśa, Śākyaprabha) noted in header but are translators, not authors.
texts.note: Duplicate edition of the Jitāri text (Chattopadhyaya Skt-reconstruction from Tibetan). Author Jitāri secure; Tibetan translators are a separate role, not authorial.

texts.title: Jātinirākṛti
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (attributed in some editions to Dharmakīrti or his school)
texts.basis: DSBC colophon: 'jātinirākṛtiriyaṃ jitāripādānām' (= of Jitāri). Tucci (Minor Buddhist Texts I) states he discovered and edited the Jātinirākṛti as a work of Jitāri. Bühnemann, 'Jitari: Kleine Texte' edits it among Jitāri's works; Aris (Sources for the History of Bhutan) lists Jātinirā[kṛti] in the Jitāri manuscript. No support for Dharmakīrti attribution.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Minor Buddhist Texts. Part I _Giuseppe Tucci, 1956.txt; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: CHANGED: current_author claims anonymous/Dharmakīrti, but colophon and three independent scholarly sources (Tucci, Bühnemann, Aris) securely attribute to Jitāri. 'jitāripādānām' = honorific plural for Jitāri, not a school.

texts.title: Mokṣasopāna
texts.basis: Muktabodha Digital Library e-text (with commentary from an NGMCP manuscript) gives author as Kāṇḍadvayātītayogī. No printed critical edition; no entry in local reference-literature for the title, author-epithet, or 'Kāṇḍadvaya'. Name is an honorific/pen-name ('the yogī who has transcended the two divisions/sections'), not a historically identified individual.
texts.note: Yoga/mokṣa text. Author known only by the cryptic epithet Kāṇḍadvayātītayogī (likely a self-designation); real identity and date undetermined. Treated as uncertain, not anonymous, since the epithet is the text's own claim.

texts.title: Rasādhyāya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (KSS 79, ed. Ramakrishna Sarma, 1930; input Oliver Hellwig) names no original author; titled 'Rasādhyāya (with ṭīkā)' but commentator unnamed. This is the rasaśāstra (mineral-alchemy) work in the Ayurveda series, NOT Bharata's Nāṭyaśāstra ch.6 Rasādhyāya. Local lit only turns up the homonymous Nāṭyaśāstra Rasādhyāya, confirming the two must be kept distinct.
texts.note: Anonymous alchemical Rasādhyāya plus an anonymous ṭīkā printed together (two unnamed hands). Do not confuse with the Nāṭyaśāstra Rasādhyāya (Bharata, with Abhinavagupta's Abhinavabhāratī).

texts.title: Tarkarahasya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (TSWS 20, ed. Paramananda Shastri, Patna 1979; provided by H. Krasser) names no author and shows no authorial colophon; text is incomplete. EAST/IKGA database catalogs this very edition's Tarkarahasya as 'by Anonymous', terminus post quem Jñānaśrīmitra (~11th c.). A Jitāri attribution is sometimes floated but is not accepted. NOT to be confused with Guṇaratna's Jain Tarkarahasyadīpikā on Haribhadra's Ṣaḍdarśanasamuccaya (seen in local lit).
texts.note: Anonymous late Buddhist pramāṇa work; occasional/unconfirmed ascription to Jitāri rejected by EAST. Distinct from Guṇaratna's Tarkarahasyadīpikā.

texts.title: Rāmakaṇṭha: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Nādakārikā
texts.basis: Goudriaan (Ritual and Speculation, 1992): 'Nādakārikā, by Ramakantha, commentary by A[ghoraśiva]'. Watson 2006 lists the Nādakārikā among Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (II)'s genuine works (within the Sārdhatriśatikālottaravṛtti). The Aṣṭaprakaraṇam edition (Dvivedi 1988) prints the kārikā with Aghoraśiva's commentary. Note: the Saiddhāntika Nādakārikā tradition; some scholars debate Rāmakaṇṭha I vs II, but it is securely a Rāmakaṇṭha work.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism. Studies in Honor of André Padoux_Teun Goudriaan, ed, 1992.txt
texts.note: Edition conflates root kārikā (Rāmakaṇṭha) with Aghoraśiva's commentary; current_author records only the kārikā author.

texts.title: Svabodhasiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header (transformation of Hanneder's edition): Svabodhasiddhi attributed to Bhūti(rāja) in title and throughout; ed. Jürgen Hanneder, 'Bhūtis Svabodhasiddhi', ZDMG 167 (2017), 153-166. Bhūtirāja is a known Kashmirian Śaiva figure.
texts.note: Single-author Kashmirian Śaiva work, secure per Hanneder 2017.

texts.title: Sāmbapañcāśikā
texts.basis: Sun-hymn (sūrya-stotra) traditionally ascribed to the legendary Sāmba, son of Kṛṣṇa (cured of leprosy by Sūrya) — a legendary, not historical, attribution. Sanderson (The Śaiva Age) cites 'Sāmbapañcāśikā with the commentary of Kṣemarāja, ed. Durgāprasād and...' (Kāvyamālā 13). The real datable author is the commentator Kṣemarāja (Kashmir, 11th c.).
texts.note: Pseudepigraphic hymn ascribed to legendary Sāmba; printed edition conflates the stotra with Kṣemarāja's commentary (the one historically identifiable hand).

texts.title: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha: Vyomavyāpistava
texts.current_author: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (II) (commentator); the Vyomavyāpin/Vyomavyāpistava hymn itself is a Śaiva mantra-stotra
texts.basis: Watson 2006 (Self's Awareness of Itself) lists the Vyomavyāpistava among Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (II)'s own works, noting 'The last of these neither refers to, nor is referred to' by his other works. The Vyomavyāpin is the well-known 81-unit Śaiva mantra (Sanderson, Padoux); Rāmakaṇṭha composed the stava (hymn) celebrating it — he is its author, not merely a commentator on a pre-existing stava.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Self's Awareness of Itself Bhatta Ramakantha's Arguments Against the Buddhist Doctrine of No-Self_Alex Watson, 2006.txt
texts.note: Vyomavyāpistava is Rāmakaṇṭha's own composition (a stava on the Vyomavyāpin mantra), not a commentary; single author. Current_author's 'commentator' framing corrected to author.

texts.title: Dviśatikālottara
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text from MS, no human author named. Secondary lit (Goudriaan ed. 1992; From Material to Deity 2005; Vāc/Padoux 1990) treats the Kālottara as a Śaiva āgama whose recensions (Sārdhatriśatikālottara, Dviśatikālottara, etc.) are revealed scripture; only later commentaries (e.g. Rāmakaṇṭha on the Sārdhatriśati) are authored. Web (Sanderson/Goodall, academia.edu Śatikā-Kālajñāna ed.): Dviśatikālottara is a 200-verse non-eclectic recension of the Kālottara, anonymous āgama predating Brahmaśambhu (937/8).
texts.note: Revealed Śaiva Siddhānta scripture; one of several Kālottara recensions by verse-count (200 = dviśati). No human author; commentaries are by others (Rāmakaṇṭha, etc.) and not part of this root text.

texts.title: Gītārthasaṅgraha
texts.basis: GRETIL header (input S. Ishitobi) names Yāmuna as composer; no printed source stated, no scribal colophon. Secondary lit (Literature on Phil. Systems lists Gītārthasaṅgraha/Stotraratna among Yāmuna's Viśiṣṭādvaita works; Narasimhachary, Contribution of Yāmunācārya to Viśiṣṭādvaita 1998) confirms. Standard ed. printed with Vedānta Deśika's Gītārthasaṅgraharakṣā (a separate commentary, not part of this work).
texts.note: Yāmunācārya (c. 10th c., teacher/grandfather-figure of the Śrīvaiṣṇava lineage before Rāmānuja). The verse-summary of the Gītā; not to be conflated with Vedānta Deśika's later -rakṣā commentary.

texts.title: Īśvarasiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet Project), no printed source named. Īśvarasiddhi is part 2 of Yāmuna's Siddhitraya (the trio Ātmasiddhi/Īśvarasiddhi/Saṃvitsiddhi). Secondary lit: Potter, Presuppositions, cites 'Siddhitraya by Yāmunācārya'; Bronkhorst (Mīmāṃsā & Vedānta 2007) and Lindtner (Ālokamālā 1985, quoting 'Yāmunācārya's Siddhitraya') confirm the attribution.
texts.note: Second of the three siddhi-treatises; survives incomplete/fragmentary like the rest of the Siddhitraya. Secure to Yāmunācārya.

texts.title: Laghustuti, with commentary by Rāghavānanda
texts.current_author: Laghubhaṭṭāraka (also a name used in the Krama/Śākta tradition); commentary by Rāghavānanda
texts.basis: Edition TSS 60 (T. Ganapati Sastri, Trivandrum 1917) = Laghustuti with the Vṛtti of Rāghavānanda — confirmed by web (Trivandrum Sanskrit Series / archive.org listing of TSS-060, 'Laghustuti with Vritti of Raghavananda'). The root Krama/Śākta hymn (to Kālī, 12 Kālīs context) carries the traditional ascription Laghubhaṭṭāraka; the commentary is a separate, securely-named author Rāghavānanda. Secondary lit (Bendall Catalogue) lists 'Laghustuti' within Śākta stotra collections (Pañcastavī-type) but does not fix the hymn's author.
texts.note: Single title conflates two distinct authors/roles: root stuti (traditionally Laghubhaṭṭāraka, attribution medium-confidence/uncertain in scholarship) vs. the vṛtti by Rāghavānanda. Roles separated.

texts.title: Siddhitraya: Part 1: Ātmasiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet Project), no printed source named. Ātmasiddhi = part 1 of the Siddhitraya. Secondary lit: Bronkhorst (Mīmāṃsā & Vedānta 2007, n.6): 'The Ātmasiddhi is traditionally considered part of Yāmuna's Siddhitraya'; Potter, Presuppositions, 'Siddhitraya by Yāmunācārya'; Narasimhachary 1998. Standard ed. R. Ramamisra Sastri / M. Narasimhachary.
texts.note: First of the three siddhis. Traditionally/securely Yāmunācārya; text survives only in fragmentary form. 'Traditionally considered part of...' (Bronkhorst) but attribution to Yāmuna itself is uncontested.

texts.title: Ahirbudhnyasaṃhitā, Volume 1
texts.basis: Edition by M. D. Ramanujacharya under supervision of F. O. Schrader (Adyar 1916) — these are modern editors, not the author. Secondary lit (Olson, Book of the Goddess 2002; Joshi, Studies in Buddhistic Culture — 'written in Kashmir c. 4th c. AD'; Essais sur le rituel; Encyclopedia of Tantra) treats the Ahirbudhnyasaṃhitā as an early Pāñcarātrāgama / revealed Vaiṣṇava scripture, framed as dialogue (Ahirbudhnya/Nārada), with no human author. One Dhammapada verse is even embedded (Nakamura).
texts.source: web:tradition (Pāñcarātra āgama) + secondary:cleaned_Book of the Goddess Past and Present_Carl Olson, 2002
texts.note: Revealed Pāñcarātra scripture; no human author. Editors Ramanujacharya (ed.) & Schrader (supervisor) listed as compiler/redactor roles only, not attributed authorship. Traditional dialogic frame Ahirbudhnya->Nārada.

texts.title: Laghutantraṭīkā
texts.basis: Cicuzza's critical edition title and scholarship (Sferra/Tucci collection, Nakamura) consistently ascribe this ṭīkā on the first chapter of the Cakrasaṃvaratantra to Vajrapāṇi (called 'Bodhisattva Vajrapāṇi', an honorific for the historical c.11th-c. Buddhist author, a Kālacakra-school figure). It is a commentary (ṭīkā) on the Laghuśaṃvara/Cakrasaṃvara root tantra, which is itself a separate revealed scripture.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanskrit Texts from Giuseppe Tucci’s Collection. Part I_Francesco Sferra, ed, 2008.txt; web:https://www.academia.edu/45511596/
texts.note: Author is the commentator; the root Cakrasaṃvaratantra it glosses is a distinct anonymous/revealed scripture and is not the work being attributed here, so no conflation. 'Bodhisattva Vajrapāṇi' is the honorific used in the tradition/edition title.

texts.title: Kālasaṃkarṣiṇīmatatantraṭippaṇī
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text from manuscript material of the Krama/Kālasaṃkarṣiṇī tradition; no printed critical edition and no named author. It is a ṭippaṇī (annotation/gloss) on the Kālasaṃkarṣiṇīmata, a Krama Śaiva scripture; secondary literature (Sanderson) treats Kālasaṃkarṣiṇī material as revealed Krama scripture without naming the glossator.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_Śaivism and the tantric traditions.txt; tradition
texts.note: Glossator unidentified; the underlying Kālasaṃkarṣiṇīmata is a traditionally revealed (anonymous) Krama tantra. No author can be assigned without inventing one.

texts.title: Bodhicaryāvatārapañjikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'śāntidevaviracitaḥ bodhicaryāvatāraḥ ... prajñākaramativiracitayā pañjikākhyavyākhyayā saṃvalitaḥ'; colophon 'iti prajñākaramativiracitāyāṃ bodhicaryāvatārapañjikāyāṃ'. The e-text bundles Śāntideva's root verses (Bodhicaryāvatāra) together with Prajñākaramati's pañjikā commentary — two distinct authors.
texts.note: Pañjikā by Prajñākaramati (c. 10th-11th c.); root Bodhicaryāvatāra by Śāntideva (c. 8th c.). The single title/file conflates root verses + commentary by different authors, hence conflation_flag true; primary commentary attribution (Prajñākaramati) is unchanged.

texts.title: Kramastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'mayā 'bhinavaguptena mārgaśīrṣe stutaḥ śivaḥ' and 'iti śrī abhinavaguptapādācāryakṛtaṃ kramastotraṃ sampūrṇam', naming Abhinavagupta as composer in an internal/closing verse.
texts.note: Self-named author in colophon and final verse; the famous Kashmiri Krama/Pratyabhijñā master Abhinavagupta. Note: an older Krama Kramastotra/Kramastava tradition exists, but this text explicitly names Abhinavagupta.

texts.title: Kauśikapaddhati
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'Keśava: Kauśikapaddhati' citing the Limaye/Dandekar/Kashikar/Bhide/Bahulkar critical edition; secondary 'From Material to Deity' (Einoo & Takashima) refers to it as 'Keśava's Kauśikapaddhati' and dates its composition to the 11th c. (ed. Introduction pp. xxxv-xxxviii).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_From Material to Deity. Indian Rituals of Consecrations_Einoo & Takashima, ed., 2005.txt
texts.note: Keśava (c. 11th c.), author of this paddhati on the Kauśikasūtra of the Atharvaveda. Attribution secure and undisputed.

texts.title: Kalāvilāsa
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon (end of canto 10): 'iti mahākaviśrīkṣemendraviracite kalāvilāse ... daśamaḥ sargaḥ', explicitly crediting the great poet Kṣemendra; matches Kāvyamālā edition (Durgaprasada & Parab).
texts.note: Kṣemendra, 11th-c. Kashmiri satirist/poet; attribution secure via internal colophons. This didactic-satirical kāvya is part of his well-documented corpus.

texts.title: vāruṇapaddhativyākhyāna
texts.basis: Web: the base Vāruṇapaddhati (also called Ātmārthapūjāpaddhati), a Śaivasiddhānta ritual paddhati, was composed by Varuṇaśivācārya from the Śaivasiddhāntāgamas; this item is the vyākhyāna (commentary) on it, whose author is not named in the Muktabodha catalog. No secondary-literature hits for the commentary's author.
texts.note: Base root-paddhati = Varuṇaśivācārya; the vyākhyāna/commentary (this title) is itself anonymous. Distinct from the root work — title conflates root author and unnamed commentator.

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 10. Gadāparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Kṣemendra; closing colophon: 'iti kṣemendraviracitāyāṃ bhāratamañjaryāṃ gadāparva' explicitly attributes the work to Kṣemendra. Single-author kāvya abridgement of the Mahābhārata.
texts.note: Colophon 'kṣemendraviracitāyāṃ bhāratamañjaryāṃ'; secure 11th-c. Kashmiri author. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 13. Śāntiparvan
texts.basis: Same Bhāratamañjarī as ksbhm10u; GRETIL header and colophons (e.g. 'iti śrīkṣemendraviracitāyāṃ bhāratamañjaryāṃ ...parva') attribute the entire work to Kṣemendra. Single author.
texts.note: Parvan-section of Kṣemendra's single-author Bhāratamañjarī; secure. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka]
texts.primary_author: Ānandavardhana (vṛtti); kārikās disputed (possibly an earlier Dhvanikāra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon ('iti śrī-rājānakānandavardhanācārya-viracite dhvany-āloke ...uddyotaḥ') attributes the whole work to Ānandavardhana. But secondary literature (Sanskrit and Indian Studies, Ingalls Festschrift; J. Brough 1919 'The Dhvanikāra and Ānandavardhana'; Sivaprasad Bhattacharya; P.V. Kane) documents the classic Dhvanikāra debate: many hold the kārikās are by an earlier separate author (the Dhvanikāra, sometimes 'Sahṛdaya') with Ānandavardhana as vṛtti/granthakāra; Krishnamoorthy and Mahimbhaṭṭa attribute both to Ānandavardhana. Abhinavagupta calls Ānandavardhana the Vṛttikāra.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanskrit and Indian Studies. Essays in Honour of Daniel H.H. Ingalls...; web:wisdomlib.org
texts.note: Genuine multi-author dispute: kārikās possibly by an anonymous Dhvanikāra, vṛtti by Ānandavardhana. The Locana (separate commentary by Abhinavagupta) is NOT in this e-text. Refines current single-author attribution.

texts.title: Āgamaprāmāṇya
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text opens 'śrīyāmunācāryasvāminā praṇītam' (composed by Śrī Yāmunācāryasvāmin), naming him paramaguru of Śrībhāṣyācārya (Rāmānuja). Secondary lit (Bartley, Intro to Indian Philosophy; INDOLOGY) confirms Yāmuna as author defending Pāñcarātra authority; a Tippaṇī commentary exists on it. Dates disputed (c. 918-1038 vs c. 1050-1125) but authorship secure.
texts.source: gretil-header (muktabodha e-text); secondary:cleaned_Introduction to Indian Philosophy_Christopher Bartley
texts.note: Single author Yāmuna, Śrīvaiṣṇava ācārya. Only the floruit dating is contested, not the attribution. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 18. Svargaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header and colophon 'iti śrīkṣemendraviracitāyāṃ bhāratamañjaryāṃ svargaparva' explicitly attribute the work to Śrīkṣemendra. Single author.
texts.note: Colophon 'śrīkṣemendraviracitāyāṃ bhāratamañjaryāṃ svargaparva'; secure. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Mukundamālā
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'iti śrīkulaśekharanṛpativiracitā mukundamālā samāptā' and title-page 'śrīkulaśekharanṛpativiracitā mukundamālā' attribute it to King Kulaśekhara; identification with the Vaiṣṇava Āḻvār Kulaśekhara is the received tradition.
texts.note: Colophon names King Kulaśekhara; traditionally identified with the Tamil Āḻvār. No conflicting attribution in the edition.

texts.title: Daśarūpaka
texts.basis: GRETIL header and closing verse (DhDaś_4.80) name Dhanañjaya, son of Viṣṇu, as author. Note: this e-text gives ONLY the root Daśarūpaka WITHOUT Dhanika's commentary 'Avaloka' (Avaloka excluded from this digitization, though present in Hall's 1865 edition). So no commentary-author conflation in the actual text supplied.
texts.note: Root kārikā work by Dhanañjaya. Edition title mentions Avaloka by Dhanika, but this e-text omits the commentary; thus single author. (Dhanika is generally held to be Dhanañjaya's brother/associate.)

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 12. Strīparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header and colophon 'iti kṣemendraviracitāyāṃ bhāratamañjaryāṃ strīparva' attribute it to Kṣemendra; Kṣemendra (11th c. Kashmir) is the secure author of the Bhāratamañjarī verse-epitome of the Mahābhārata.
texts.note: Colophon explicitly names Kṣemendra. Sansknet/GRETIL only as modern digitizers.

texts.title: Ṣaṭsāhasrikā Hevajratantrapiṇḍārthaṭīkā, Ch. 1 (ed. Sferra 2009)
texts.basis: Edition is Sferra's chapter-1 edition of the Hevajratantrapiṇḍārthaṭīkā (a Kālacakra-school commentary epitomizing the Hevajratantra), attributed in colophon/tradition to the bodhisattva Dāśabhūmīśvara Vajragarbha. Secondary lit (Newman, Thurman/Tsong kha pa, Kongtrul's Treasury, Kalacakra: Textual and Ritual Perspectives) consistently names Vajragarbha. Editors note doubts about the authorship of the LATER chapters, but ch.1 (this item) is within the secure core.
texts.note: current_author empty; assigned Vajragarbha (traditional ascription, well attested in Tibetan tradition). Sferra is modern editor. NB: source_url 44704162 is Sferra's related 'Elucidation of True Reality' Kālacakra-commentary piece; the 2009 'Laud of the Chosen Deity' is the ch.1 edition cited. Authorship of later chapters of the whole ṭīkā is doubted, but not relevant to ch.1; not a conflation within this item.

texts.title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka], Uddyota 4
texts.basis: GRETIL marks two layers: kārikās (DhvK) and vṛtti (DhvA); colophon names rājānaka Ānandavardhanācārya. Secondary/web: long-standing scholarly debate (P.V. Kane, Krishnamoorthy) on whether kārikās and vṛtti share one author. Following Abhinavagupta's tradition, the kārikās were composed earlier by a separate Dhvanikāra (called 'Sahṛdaya'), with Ānandavardhana writing the vṛtti and illustrations; Mahimabhaṭṭa and others ascribe both to Ānandavardhana.
texts.note: Conflation: kārikā-layer (Dhvanikāra/'Sahṛdaya', per Abhinavagupta) vs vṛtti (Ānandavardhana). Disputed; some scholars give both to Ānandavardhana. Uddyota 4 colophon names Ānandavardhana as vṛtti author.

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 9. Śalyaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header and colophon 'iti kṣemendraviracitāyāṃ bhāratamañjaryāṃ śalyaparva' attribute it to Kṣemendra; secure attribution to the 11th-c. Kashmiri poet Kṣemendra.
texts.note: Colophon names Kṣemendra. Sansknet/GRETIL only modern digitizers.

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 19. Harivaṃśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Kṣemendra; secondary lit (Bendall catalogue, Digitalisierte Werke, Sūryakānta) confirms the Bhāratamañjarī is Kṣemendra's single-author verse epitome of the Mahābhārata. The 'Harivaṃśa' here is an editorial section/parvan of that one work, not a separate author.
texts.note: Single-author kāvya abridgment; section division follows the MBh parvans but authorship is uniform (11th c. Kashmiri Kṣemendra).

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 6. Bhīṣmaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Kṣemendra; the Bhāratamañjarī is his single-author MBh epitome, confirmed by reference catalogues.
texts.note: Editorial parvan division; one author throughout.

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 17. Mahāprasthānikaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Kṣemendra; single-author MBh epitome confirmed by secondary literature.
texts.note: Editorial parvan division; one author throughout.

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 8. Karṇaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Kṣemendra; single-author MBh epitome confirmed by secondary literature.
texts.note: Editorial parvan division; one author throughout.

texts.title: Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā
texts.current_author: Kṣemendra (with the final avadāna added by his son Somendra)
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon ascribes the whole to 'kṣemendraviracita'. But secondary lit (Kaul, Buddhism in Kashmir, pp.~1256-1308; Lin, Adapting the Buddha's Biographies pp.~603-607; Ganhar, Buddhism in Kashmir and Ladakh) states Kṣemendra composed 107 avadānas (pallavas) and his son Somendra added an introduction plus the 108th/final avadāna (to reach the auspicious 108) and the dated concluding colophon. Two distinct 11th-c. authors.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Kaul_Buddhism in Kashmir.txt; secondary:cleaned_Lin_Adapting the Buddha's Biographies...txt
texts.note: Conflation: father Kṣemendra wrote pallavas 1-107; son Somendra wrote the prefatory matter and the final (108th) avadāna and added the colophon. Current_author already noted Somendra's final avadāna; refined to separate roles and add Somendra's introduction.

texts.title: Darpadalana
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Kṣemendra; the text self-names the author (kṣemendraḥ, v.1.3, 'darpadoṣacikitsakaḥ'). Bendall catalogue and Lin (Kṣemendra Studies) confirm it as Kṣemendra's ethical satire.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Bendall_Catalogue...; secondary:cleaned_Lin_Adapting the Buddha's Biographies...txt
texts.note: Self-attribution in the text itself; single author.

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 7. Droṇaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'iti kṣemendraviracitāyāṃ bhāratamañjaryāṃ droṇaparva'. Secondary lit (Mylius, Sanderson) treats the Bhāratamañjarī as Kṣemendra's verse epitome of the Mahābhārata. No multi-author structure for this kāvya; it is single-author.
texts.note: Single-author kāvya epitome; colophon explicitly names Kṣemendra as composer (viracita). 11th-c. Kashmir.

texts.title: Sumāgadhāvadāna [Sumāgadhāvadānasūtra]
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'iti kṣemendraviracitāyāṃ bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatāyāṃ sumāgadhāvadānaṃ trinavatitamaḥ pallavaḥ' (93rd pallava). Secondary lit (Lessing & Wayman 1968: of 108 chapters, the first 107 are by Kṣemendra and only the last by his son Somendra; Granoff: Somendra added one story). Pallava 93 falls within Kṣemendra's portion.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Introduction to the Buddhist Tantric Systems_Lessing & Wayman, 1968.txt
texts.note: Part of Avadānakalpalatā (collection conflates Kṣemendra 1-107 + Somendra ch.108 + Somendra's intro verses), but THIS pallava no.93 is securely Kṣemendra's own. Whole collection is multi-author; this individual avadāna is not.

texts.title: Vimalaprabhāṭīkā on Śrīlaghukālacakratantrarāja (ed. Upādhyāya 1986, Vol.1)
texts.basis: Edition title itself separates roles: 'Vimalaprabhāṭīkā of Kalkin Śrīpuṇḍarīka on Śrīlaghukālacakratantrarāja by Śrīmañjuśrīyaśas'. Web (Tibetan Buddhist Encyclopedia, wisdomlib): the abridged Laghukālacakratantra is ascribed to king Mañjuśrīyaśas; the Vimalaprabhā commentary to his successor Puṇḍarīka (c. 9th-11th c.). Both are legendary Kalkin/Śambhala figures (traditional ascriptions).
texts.note: current_author empty; volume conflates root tantra (Mañjuśrīyaśas) and Vimalaprabhā commentary (Puṇḍarīka). Both ascriptions are traditional/legendary (Kalkin kings of Śambhala).

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 11. Sauptikaparvan
texts.basis: Same work as item 1 (Bhāratamañjarī, different parvan). Colophons throughout name 'kṣemendraviracita'. Single-author Mahābhārata verse epitome; no commentary or multi-author structure.
texts.note: Single-author kāvya; secure attribution to Kṣemendra (11th-c. Kashmir).

texts.title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 2
texts.primary_author: Udayana (Udayanācārya) [kārikās disputed: possibly earlier Nyāya author]
texts.basis: GRETIL text = kārikās only; colophon 'iti nyāyakusumāñjalau dvitīyastabakaḥ'. Traditional/universal attribution: Udayana (c.984 CE), magnum opus on proving God (Potter, Enc. Indian Phil. vol.2). DISPUTE: Ruzsa, 'Structure and Authorship of the Kusumâñjali' (JIPh 2022, 50:803-819) argues the work is two texts — a śloka-kārikā core by an EARLIER Nyāya author + Udayana's prose commentary; some early commentators do not take Udayana as author of the verses, and a legend says Udayana 'received' the Kusumāñjali.
texts.note: GRETIL file is the kārikā (verse) layer — the very part Ruzsa argues is by an earlier author, not Udayana. Traditional attribution still Udayana; verses' authorship disputed. Prose auto-commentary (Udayana) not in this file; four named commentaries (Bodhanī etc.) in print edition also absent from GRETIL text.

texts.title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 1
texts.primary_author: Udayana (Udayanācārya) [kārikās disputed: possibly earlier Nyāya author]
texts.basis: GRETIL text = kārikās only; colophon 'iti śrīnyāyakusumāñjalau prathamaḥ stabakaḥ'. Traditional attribution: Udayana (c.984 CE; Potter 1977). DISPUTE: Ruzsa (JIPh 2022) — abstract argues the Nyāya-kusumāñjali is two texts, an earlier ~65-śloka core (NKś) by an earlier Nyāya author plus Udayana's prose commentary; external evidence = mss of verses alone, commentaries on verses only, a Bhāḍūri-Brahmin legend that Udayana 'received' the Kusumāñjali.
texts.note: GRETIL file = kārikā layer (the disputed verses). Traditional/secure for most scholarship = Udayana; Ruzsa 2022 disputes verse authorship and posits verse-vs-prose conflation. Print edition's four commentaries (Bodhanī/Prakāśa/Prakāśikā/Makaranda by separate authors) not present in this text file.

texts.title: Kubjikāmatatantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Kaula scripture; tradition: Śiva/Kubjikā revelation)
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives only editors (Goudriaan & Schoterman 1988) and digitizer (Somadeva Vasudeva); no personal author. As a revealed Śaiva/Śākta Kaula tantra (Paścimāmnāya/Kulālikāmnāya), it is anonymous scripture traditionally presented as Śiva's revelation. Goudriaan ed. (Padoux Festschrift discusses it as a mūlatantra of the school) treats it as an anonymous tantra.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism. Studies in Honor of André Padoux_Teun Goudriaan, ed, 1992.txt
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Kaula tantra; editors Goudriaan & Schoterman are modern editors, not authors. Traditional ascription is divine revelation, not a historical person.

texts.title: Garbhāvakrāntyavadāna
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'iti kṣemendraviracitāyāṃ bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatāyāṃ garbhāvakrāntinirdeśo nāma daśamaḥ pallavaḥ' — pallava 10 of the Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā composed by Kṣemendra. Ed. Michael Hahn 1997. Web (Wikipedia/scholarship): of the 108 pallavas only the 108th (Jīmūtavāhanāvadāna) was added by Kṣemendra's son Somendra; pallavas 1-107, incl. no. 10, are Kṣemendra's own.
texts.note: Pallava 10, securely Kṣemendra; only pallava 108 is by son Somendra, so no conflation for this chapter. Somendra added short framing verses to each pallava but the avadāna proper is Kṣemendra's.

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 14. Āśvamedhikaparvan
texts.basis: Bhāratamañjarī is Kṣemendra's verse epitome of the Mahābhārata (Kāvyamālā 65 ed.); attribution to the Kashmirian polymath Kṣemendra (11th c.) is undisputed. This is parvan 14 (Āśvamedhika) of that single-author work.
texts.note: Secure single-author work; parvan division is internal structure, not separate authorship.

texts.title: Mukundamālā
texts.primary_author: Kulaśekhara (traditionally the Āḻvār / Cera king; attribution disputed)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'śrīkulaśekharanṛpativiracitā mukundamālā' (composed by King Śrī Kulaśekhara). Web (S. Anandakichenin; scholarly blog/academia.edu): authorship contested — the Mukundamālā's intense Kṛṣṇa-bhakti and epithets differ markedly from the Rāma-centred Perumāḷ Tirumoḻi of Kulaśekhara Āḻvār, leading scholars (e.g. Velukkudi) to suggest it may be by a later namesake/descendant rather than the Āḻvār himself.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Kulaśekhara Āḻvār is disputed; possibly a later Kulaśekhara. Single author either way (no conflation), but status downgraded from secure to disputed.

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 15. Āśramavāsikaparvan
texts.basis: Bhāratamañjarī is Kṣemendra's verse abridgement of the Mahābhārata (Kāvyamālā 65); undisputed attribution to Kṣemendra (11th c. Kashmir). This is parvan 15 (Āśramavāsika) of that single-author poem.
texts.note: Secure single-author work; parvan is internal structure only.

texts.title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka], Uddyota 3
texts.primary_author: Ānandavardhana (vṛtti); kārikās possibly by an earlier 'dhvanikāra' (disputed)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'śrī-rājānaka-ānandavardhana-ācārya-viracito dhvany-ālokaḥ' (single attribution). But secondary lit documents the classic 'old and confounding authorship problem': J.L. Masson (Sanskrit and Indian Studies, Ingalls Festschrift 1980) reviews the long debate since Bühler — either kārikās and vṛtti are both by Ānandavardhana, OR the kārikās are by an older unknown writer (dhvanikāra) and only the vṛtti by Ānandavardhana; Masson argues the kārikās are by an earlier author. Abhinavagupta's Locana is itself ambiguous on whether kārikākāra and vṛttikāra are one person.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Sanskrit and Indian Studies. Essays in Honour of Daniel H.H. Ingalls_Bimal Krishna Matilal, M. Nagatomi, J. M. Masson, E. C. Dimock Jr.,1980_a.txt
texts.note: Genuine kārikā-vs-vṛtti authorship controversy (parallel to MSA). Vṛtti securely Ānandavardhana; kārikās possibly by an earlier anonymous 'dhvanikāra'. Flagged as conflated/disputed. Note: this work = the integral Dhvanyāloka including its kārikās; Uddyota 3 is a chapter division.

texts.title: Pradīpoddyotanaṭīkā (commentary on the Guhyasamājatantra)
texts.basis: Edition title (Chakravarti 1984) = Guhyasamājatantrapradīpodyotanaṭīkāṣaṭkoṭīvyākhyā, a commentary on the Guhyasamājatantra. Nakamura (Indian Buddhism, p.334): 'The Pradīpoddyotana (Tohoku 1785) ascribed to Candrakīrti is the only commentary whose Sanskrit text exists.' Wedemeyer diss.: this tantric 'Candrakīrti' lived in the 9th c. and is NOT the same as the 6th-c. Madhyamaka Candrakīrti.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Wedemeyer diss.txt
texts.note: Attributed to Candrakīrti but the tantric Candrakīrti (author of this Guhyasamāja commentary, ~9th c., Ārya tradition) is distinct from the Madhyamaka Candrakīrti; identity disputed. Commentary on the Guhyasamājatantra root tantra (root tantra anonymous, not by Candrakīrti).

texts.title: Bhāratamañjarī: 16. Mausalaparvan
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet input) credits the Bhāratamañjarī (verse epitome of the Mahābhārata) to Kṣemendra; no competing attribution. This is one parvan-section of his single-authored work.
texts.note: Secure single authorship; 11th-c. Kashmiri polymath Kṣemendra. No multi-author structure (a verse epitome, not a root+commentary).

texts.title: Vṛttaratnākara (with Sulhaṇa's Sukavihṛdayānandinī commentary)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Kedarabhatta: Vrttaratnakara, with Sulhana's Sukavihrdayanandini (comm.)'; colophons in content read 'iti sulhaṇaviracitāyāḥ sukavihṛdayānandinyāḥ'. Hahn (metrics intro): Vṛttaratnākara by Kedārabhaṭṭa, c. 11/12th c. Secondary lit also notes other commentaries (Vṛttaratnākarapañcikā), confirming root vs commentary are by different hands.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Hahn_A brief introduction into the Indian metrical system...txt; secondary:cleaned_Untersuchungen zur Buddhistischen Literatur...1997.txt
texts.note: This GRETIL file conflates two authors: the mūla metrics treatise by Kedārabhaṭṭa and the Sukavihṛdayānandinī commentary by Sulhaṇa. current_author lists only Kedārabhaṭṭa.

texts.title: Paramārthasāra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Bansat-Boudon/Codet/Törzsök, from KSTS 7, 1916): root Paramārthasāra by Abhinavagupta, commentary (Paramārthasārasaṃgrahavivṛti) by Yogarāja. The GRETIL e-text aparmyou includes Yogarāja's commentary alongside the kārikās.
texts.note: The mūla verses are Abhinavagupta's (itself an adaptation of the Ādhāra/Śeṣa Paramārthasāra); the included vivṛti is by Yogarāja. Root attribution to Abhinavagupta secure; file conflates root + commentary author. current_author omits Yogarāja.

texts.title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka], Uddyota 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes the whole Dhvanyāloka to Ānandavardhana ('śrī-rājānaka-ānandavardhanācārya-viracito dhvanyālokaḥ'). BUT a long-standing scholarly debate (since Bühler/Jacobi; Ingalls Festschrift essay 'authorship problem of the Dhvanyāloka', Krishnamoorthy, Ruegg) concerns whether the Dhvani-kārikās are by an older, unknown 'Dhvanikāra' and only the Vrtti by Ānandavardhana, or whether kārikā+vṛtti are by one author. Ruegg lists it as a 'thorny question' parallel to the Vasubandhu problem.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Sanskrit and Indian Studies. Essays in Honour of Daniel H.H. Ingalls...1980_a.txt; secondary:cleaned_Buddhist Philosophy of the Middle...Ruegg, 2010_a.txt
texts.note: Classic kārikā-vs-vṛtti authorship dispute: kārikās possibly by a separate older Dhvanikāra, vṛtti securely Ānandavardhana's. Uddyota 1 of the root work (kārikā+vṛtti); Abhinavagupta's Locana NOT included. Flagged as conflation per the disputed two-author structure.

texts.title: Śivajñānasiddhi
texts.current_author: uncertain (a Śivajñānasiddhi is associated with Arulnandi Śivācārya; Tamil tradition)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy) repeatedly: 'Arunanti Sivacariyar's Sivajnanasiddhiyar' (Cupakkam/Parapakkam parts), a core Tamil Śaiva Siddhānta work with six commentaries (Devasenapathi). The title corresponds to the Tamil Civañāṉacittiyār of Aruḷnandi Śivācārya (13th c., disciple of Meykaṇṭār). Muktabodha digital library hosts a Sanskrit version; no Sanskrit-specific competing author found.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:https://muktabodha.org/digital-library/
texts.note: Traditionally ascribed to Aruḷnandi (Aruṇanti) Śivācārya; original is Tamil (Civañāṉacittiyār, Cupakkam + Parapakkam), this being a Sanskrit rendering/version. Distinct from Bhojadeva's homonymous works; attribution traditional, not text-critically secured for the Sanskrit recension.

texts.title: Antarvyāptisamarthana
texts.basis: GRETIL page 404 at fetch time, but edition (Six Buddhist Nyāya Tracts, ed. H. Shastri 1910) ascribes it to Ratnākaraśānti. EAST/IKGA database and wisdomlib confirm Ratnākaraśānti (Śāntipa, c. 1000 CE, Vikramaśīla) as author of this pramāṇa/logic tract defending the antarvyāpti doctrine. Nakamura associates the antarvyāpti theory with the Ratnakīrti/Ratnākaraśānti milieu.
texts.note: Secure single-author logic tract. Note antarvyāpti is also championed by Ratnakīrti, but this specific Samarthana is Ratnākaraśānti's.

texts.title: Bodhapañcadaśikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (encoded M. Faliero 1998) names Abhinavagupta. Closing colophon verse is self-referential: 'ime 'bhinavaguptena ślokāḥ pañcadaśoditāḥ' (these fifteen verses uttered by Abhinavagupta to instruct disciples), an internal author signature.
texts.note: Internal colophon names Abhinavagupta; short fifteen-verse Pratyabhijñā/Trika digest.

texts.title: Vṛttaratnākara
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Kedārabhaṭṭa: Vṛttaratnākara, with Sulhaṇa's Sukavihṛdayānandinī'. The e-text bundles the root metrics treatise (Kedārabhaṭṭa) AND Sulhaṇa's commentary; Sulhaṇa's intro verse names him as son of Veladitya/Sukavi and his colophon: 'iti sulhaṇaviracitāyāṃ sukavihṛdayānandinyabhidhānāyāṃ vṛttaratnākaracchaṃdovṛttau'. Two distinct authors in one file.
texts.note: Root verses by Kedārabhaṭṭa; the included commentary Sukavihṛdayānandinī is by Sulhaṇa (a distinct, later author) — title conflates the two roles.

texts.title: Vyomavatī
texts.basis: GRETIL URL (vyomavpu.htm) 404 at fetch; secondary lit (Potter, Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies vol. 2, 1977) confirms: 'the Vyomavatī of Vyomaśiva', one of three commentaries on Praśastapāda's Padārthadharmasaṃgraha (= Praśastapādabhāṣya), alongside Śrīdhara's Nyāyakandalī and Udayana's Kiraṇāvalī.
texts.source: secondary:Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 2 - Potter 1977
texts.note: Vyomavatī itself is wholly Vyomaśiva's commentary; Praśastapāda listed only as author of the commented base text (not conflated within this work).

texts.title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 3
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet input) titles it 'nyāyakusumāñjalau tṛtīyastabakaḥ' by Udayana; colophon 'iti nyāyakusumāñcalau tṛtīyaḥ stabakaḥ'. Wikipedia/wisdomlib confirm Udayana (c. 984 CE) as author. The e-text content is the mūla stabaka only; the printed edition bundles four commentaries but they are not in this file.
texts.note: Third stabaka of Udayana's theistic Nyāya work; e-text is root only, so no commentary conflation here despite the multi-commentary edition.

texts.title: Tantrasāra
texts.basis: Edition: Abhinavagupta, Tantrasāra, ed. M. R. Shāstrī, KSTS 17 (1918). Securely Abhinavagupta's own prose digest (sāra) of his Tantrāloka; standard scholarship attributes it unambiguously to him. No source_url to fetch (corpus 'other').
texts.note: Self-abridgement of the Tantrāloka; do not confuse with Mādhva or other Tantrasāra works.

texts.title: Mālinīślokavārttika: Kāṇḍa 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header: author Abhinavagupta, ed. Hanneder (I.1-399) / Madhusudan Kaul Shastri (KSTS XXXI 1921). Sanderson (Śaiva Age) and Hanneder 1998 treat it as Abhinavagupta's own vārttika. The work is Abhinavagupta's verse exposition (vārttika) of the root Mālinīvijayottaratantra; that root scripture is a separate text, not part of this item.
texts.note: Authorial vārttika by Abhinavagupta; root Mālinīvijayottaratantra is a distinct (anonymous/scriptural) work and is not bundled here, so no conflation.

texts.title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 5
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'śrīmadudayanaviracita', pañcamastabakaḥ. Potter (EIPh Vol.2) repeatedly names Nyāyakusumāñjali as Udayana's classic Nyāya work on proof of God. GRETIL e-text is the root kārikā/prose of Udayana only, no commentary bundled.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 2 - Potter 1977.txt
texts.note: Root text of Udayana alone (5th stavaka); commentaries (e.g. Varadarāja, Vardhamāna, Haridāsa) not present in this e-text, so no conflation.

texts.title: Paryantapañcāśikā
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'paryantapañcāśikā nāma' of 'śrīmadābhinavaguptanāthasya'. Listed under Abhinavagupta's authentic works in Potter EIPh Vol.1 (Kashmir Śaiva) and ed. V. Raghavan 1951.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 1 - Potter.txt
texts.note: Colophon names Abhinavagupta; single-author stotra/philosophical poem of ~50 verses.

texts.title: Hevajratantra with Muktāvalī Pañjikā of Ratnākaraśānti
texts.basis: Edition (Tripathi & Negi 2001, Bibliotheca Indo-Tibetica 48) is 'Hevajratantram with Muktāvalī Pañjikā of Mahāpaṇḍitācārya Ratnākaraśānti' — i.e. the anonymous root Hevajratantra (traditionally Buddha-vacana, no human author) together with the Muktāvalī (Hevajrapañjikā) commentary by Ratnākaraśānti. Davidson (Indian Esoteric Buddhism) cites the Muktāvalī as ascribed to Ratnākaraśānti and references this same edition; Snellgrove on the composed Hevajra Tantra. Two distinct strata by different authors.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt; tradition
texts.note: Root tantra anonymous/Buddha-vacana; commentary (Muktāvalī Pañjikā) by Ratnākaraśānti (~early 11th c.). Title/author fields were empty; this title conflates root scripture + commentator.

texts.title: Nyāyakusumāñjali: Stavaka 4
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Udayana, Nyāyakusumāñjali, 4th stavaka (Sansknet input). Potter EIPh Vol.2 confirms Udayana's authorship of the Nyāyakusumāñjali; 4th book discusses pramāṇa/Īśvara's eternal knowledge. E-text is root text only.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 2 - Potter 1977.txt
texts.note: Root text of Udayana alone (4th stavaka); no commentary bundled, so no conflation.

texts.title: Anuttarāṣṭikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes the eight-verse stotra to Abhinavagupta (ed. in KSTS). Listed among the minor stotras traditionally ascribed to Abhinavagupta; authorship of these short non-dual stotras is conventionally accepted but not as firmly documented as his major works.
texts.note: Minor 8-verse stotra; traditional ascription to Abhinavagupta accepted, but for short stotras the attribution is conventional rather than independently secured.

texts.title: Tantrasāra
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Abhinavagupta as author (ed. Mukunda Rāma Śāstrī, KSTS 17, 1918); Sanderson lists Tantrasāra among Abhinavagupta's works. Single-author prose digest of the Tantrāloka in 18 āhnikas; no commentary embedded in this e-text.
texts.note: Secure. Abhinavagupta's own prose abridgement of his Tantrāloka.

texts.title: Nibandhāvalī
texts.basis: SARIT/Thakur ed. (TSWS V, 1959) = Jñānaśrīmitranibandhāvalī, the codex unicus collecting ~12 treatises ALL authored by the single Vikramaśīla scholar Jñānaśrīmitra (11th c., pramāṇa school after Dignāga/Dharmakīrti). 'Nibandhāvalī' = collection of his own nibandhas, not multiple authors.
texts.note: Collection of multiple WORKS but a single author; not an author-conflation. SARIT XML returned 502 at fetch time; confirmed via Thakur edition descriptions.

texts.title: Parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Somadeva Vasudeva input; ed. Gnoli Roma 1985 / Singh 1994) names Abhinavagupta. Sanderson lists Parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa among Abhinavagupta's works. This e-text is the vivaraṇa (commentary) only; the root Parātriṃśikā (a few Tantric verses, traditionally part of the Rudrayāmala) is a separate base text.
texts.note: Abhinavagupta is author of the vivaraṇa. The brief root verses (Parātriṃśikā) are anonymous/āgamic but are not the unit catalogued here.

texts.title: Tantrāloka
texts.basis: GRETIL header: root text by Abhinavagupta WITH the commentary (Viveka) of Rājānaka Jayaratha; KSTS ed. by Mukunda Rāma & Madhusūdan Kaul Śāstrī. The e-text interleaves Abhinavagupta's mūla and Jayaratha's much later (12th-13th c.) ṭīkā — two distinct authors in one file. Sanderson treats them as separate hands.
texts.note: Root by Abhinavagupta (c. 1000 CE); commentary -Viveka- by Rājānaka Jayaratha (~12th-13th c.). Current_author records only Abhinavagupta; the bundled Jayaratha commentary makes this an author-conflation.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa; embedded in the Mahābhārata)
texts.basis: Item title is Bhagavadgītā but the edition is the Kashmirian-recension Gītā WITH Abhinavagupta's Gītārthasaṃgraha (KSTS 4, Lakshman Raina 1933; Muktabodha HK). The Gītā mūla is anonymous/traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa (embedded in MBh); the Gītārthasaṃgraha is Abhinavagupta's Śaiva commentary (c.1000). Secondary lit (Padoux Festschrift; Lit. on Phil. Systems §582) and web confirm Abhinavagupta's authorship of the commentary.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism. Studies in Honor of André Padoux_Teun Goudriaan, ed, 1992.txt
texts.note: Two distinct hands bundled: anonymous/Vyāsa Gītā + Abhinavagupta's Gītārthasaṃgraha. Current_author records only the Gītā mūla; commentator omitted.

texts.title: Bhāvacūḍāmaṇi
texts.basis: Sanderson (History through Textual Criticism): 'Bhaṭṭa Vidyākaṇṭha (Bhāvacūḍāmaṇi on Maya[saṃgraha])' — the Bhāvacūḍāmaṇi is Vidyākaṇṭha's commentary on the Saiddhāntika Pratiṣṭhātantra MAYASAṂGRAHA, NOT on the Mṛgendrāgama as the work-item edition string claims. The Mṛgendra vṛtti is by his son Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha. Author of the Bhāvacūḍāmaṇi is securely Vidyākaṇṭha.
texts.note: Author secure = Vidyākaṇṭha. CAUTION: edition metadata wrongly links it to the Mṛgendrāgama/Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha; the Bhāvacūḍāmaṇi is actually on the Mayasaṃgraha. Father Vidyākaṇṭha vs son Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha are distinct.

texts.title: Tattvaratnāvaloka [Tattvaratnāloka]
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC colophon: 'kṛtiriyaṃ paṇḍitavāgīśvarakīrtipādānām' (this work is of Paṇḍita Vāgīśvarakīrti). Davidson, Tibetan Renaissance: Vāgīśvarakīrti composed the Tattvaratnāvaloka (based on Guhyasamāja/Hevajra), ed. Janardan Pandey 1997. Makransky/Yoshizaki confirm Vāgīśvarakīrti as a late-Indian (Vikramaśīla, Newar-connected) master.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Tibetan Renaissance. Tantric Buddhism in the Rebirth of Tibetan Culture_Ronald M. Davidson, 2005.txt
texts.note: Single-author tantric work; colophon names Vāgīśvarakīrti. No conflation.

texts.title: Parātrīśikālaghuvṛtti
texts.current_author: Abhinavagupta (the catalogue 'unknown' author hint is incorrect)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (skt-en-mono; Encyclopedia of Tantra): the Parātrīśikā/Parātriṃśikā root verses form the concluding portion of the Rudrayāmala-tantra (revealed/anonymous scripture); the Laghuvṛtti is Abhinavagupta's short commentary on them. KSTS 18 (ed. Mukunda Rama Shastri) prints the scripture + Abhinava's Laghuvṛtti together.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt
texts.note: Title conflates the anonymous Parātrīśikā scripture (Rudrayāmala) with Abhinavagupta's Laghuvṛtti commentary. Vṛtti securely Abhinavagupta; catalogue 'unknown' hint refers only to the scriptural verses.

texts.title: Tantrāloka, chapters 15-38 (āhnikas)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra; Pramāṇakīrtiḥ; Essais sur le rituel): Tantrāloka is the magnum opus of Abhinavagupta; the concluding portion gives his pedigree. The KSTS edition bundles Jayaratha's Viveka/Tantrālokaviveka (13th c.) as commentary, but the titled work (the āhnikas) is Abhinavagupta's.
texts.note: Mūla (āhnikas 15-38) securely by Abhinavagupta. Jayaratha's Viveka is a separate later commentator bundled in the edition; listed for role-clarity but the item is Abhinava's text, so not flagged as conflation.

texts.title: parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra): 'The Parātriṃśikā-vivṛti (or vivaraṇa) is a commentary on the Parātriṃśikā, the text of which constitutes the concluding portion of the Rudrayāmala-tantra.' The Vivaraṇa is Abhinavagupta's long commentary (its colophon supplies his pedigree); the root verses are anonymous Trika scripture. KSTS 18 prints both together.
texts.note: Title conflates the anonymous Parātriṃśikā scripture with Abhinavagupta's Vivaraṇa. Commentary securely Abhinavagupta; root verses anonymous/revealed.

texts.title: Abhinavagupta: Bodhapañcadaśikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (enc. M. Faliero) + colophon: 'ime 'bhinavaguptena ślokāḥ pañcadaśoditāḥ' (these fifteen verses were uttered by Abhinavagupta). Encyclopedia of Tantra lists Bodhapañcadaśikā among Abhinava's works (intended for less-intelligent disciples).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt
texts.note: Short single-author Śaiva primer; self-naming colophon. No conflation.

texts.title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka], Uddyota 2
texts.primary_author: Ānandavardhana (vṛtti); kārikās ascribed to the 'Dhvanikāra' (possibly Ānandavardhana himself or an earlier author)
texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes the whole Dhvanyāloka to śrī-rājānakānandavardhanācārya. Classic scholarly dispute (Jacobi, S.K. De, Kane, S. Mukherjee) holds the dhvani-kārikās are by a separate earlier 'Dhvanikāra' with only the prose Vṛtti by Ānandavardhana; later scholars argue for single authorship. skt-en-mono repeatedly cites the 'dhvanikāra' as distinct from the vṛtti-kāra. (Note: 'Uddyota' is the division term; the Locana commentary on these uddyotas is by Abhinavagupta, not in this e-text item.)
texts.note: Kārikā vs vṛtti authorship historically disputed (Dhvanikāra hypothesis). E-text item = Dhvanyāloka mūla+vṛtti, Uddyota 2; vṛtti securely Ānandavardhana, kārikās' separate authorship unresolved. Locana (Abhinavagupta) not included here.

texts.title: Mālinīvijayavārttika
texts.basis: Secondary literature (Padoux, Vāc; Sanderson; Tournier/Eltschinger volume; KSTS ed. of Madhusūdan Kaul Shāstrī) uniformly cites the Mālinīvijayavārttika as a work of Abhinavagupta (a vārttika/verse exegesis on the Mālinīvijayottaratantra). No separate commentary conflated in this item.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Vac. The Concept of the Word in Selected Hindu Tantras_Andre Padoux, 1990.txt; secondary:cleaned_Tournier_Eltschinger_Sernesi_Archaeologies of the Written.txt
texts.note: Single-author Kashmir Śaiva vārttika by Abhinavagupta; edition by Madhusūdan Kaul Shāstrī, KSTS 31, 1921.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītā
texts.primary_author: anonymous/traditional (mūla ascribed to Vyāsa); Vīraśaiva bhāṣya-kāra unidentified
texts.basis: The Bhagavadgītā mūla is the traditional, composite Mahābhārata text ascribed to Vyāsa (anonymous in scholarly terms). This item adds a Vīraśaiva (Lingāyat) bhāṣya whose author is NOT named in the workitem and not identifiable from secondary lit: the best-known Vīraśaiva exegete Śrīpati Paṇḍitācārya wrote the Śrīkarabhāṣya on the Brahmasūtra, not a Gītā bhāṣya, so cannot be assumed here.
texts.note: Two distinct strata: anonymous/Vyāsa-ascribed mūla + a separate, unidentified Vīraśaiva bhāṣya. Bhāṣya author left anonymous rather than guessing Śrīpati.

texts.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 6, with commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
texts.current_author: attributed to Vālmīki (text); commentary Tātparyaprakāśa by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
texts.primary_author: anonymous (mūla = Kashmirian Mokṣopāya, pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki); Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī (Tātparyaprakāśa)
texts.basis: Slaje/Hanneder (Mokṣopāya Project) show the mūla is the anonymous Kashmirian Mokṣopāya (10th c.), later Vedānticized and circulated as Yogavāsiṣṭha under a traditional/pseudepigraphic ascription to Vālmīki. The Tātparyaprakāśa commentary is securely by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī (confirmed in Pansīkar Nirnaya Sagar ed. and Bronkhorst, Jacobsen, Bendall catalogue).
texts.source: web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mokshopaya; secondary:cleaned_Mimamsa and Vedanta. Interaction and Continuity_Johannes Bronkhorst, ed., 2007.txt; secondary:cleaned_Bendall_Catalogue...British museum.txt
texts.note: Two distinct authors: anonymous Mokṣopāya mūla (Vālmīki ascription pseudepigraphic) + ṭīkā by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī. Part 6 of the saṃpūrṇa.

texts.title: Tantrāloka, chapters 1-14 (āhnikas)
texts.basis: Universally attributed to Abhinavagupta (c. 1000 CE; Davidson, Sanderson, Padoux). The KSTS edition cited in the workitem prints it together with the Tantrālokaviveka commentary of Rājānaka Jayaratha (Freschi/Maas; Steinkellner Festschrift), so the published item bundles two distinct authors though the mūla itself is securely single-author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism.txt; secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse.txt; secondary:cleaned_Pramāṇakīrtiḥ-Steinkellner 70th_v2.txt
texts.note: Mūla securely Abhinavagupta; conflation_flag true because edition/item includes Jayaratha's viveka commentary (a distinct author).

texts.title: Navasāhasāṅkacarita
texts.basis: GRETIL header and the per-canto colophons name the author explicitly: 'iti śrīmṛgāṅkaguptasūnoḥ parimalāparanāmnaḥ padmaguptasya kṛtau navasāhasāṅkacarite mahākāvye'. Secondary lit (Davis, Images Miracles and Authority) confirms it as the 10th-c. Paramāra-court mahākāvya of Padmagupta.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Images, Miracles, and Authority in Asian Religious Traditions_Richard H. Davis, ed., 1998.txt
texts.note: Colophon: Padmagupta alias Parimala, son of Mṛgāṅkagupta. Single author; matches current.

texts.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 1, with the commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
texts.current_author: attributed to Vālmīki (anonymous/composite); commentary by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
texts.primary_author: anonymous (mūla = Kashmirian Mokṣopāya, pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki); Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī (Tātparyaprakāśa)
texts.basis: Same structure as Part 6: anonymous Mokṣopāya mūla (Slaje/Hanneder Mokṣopāya Project; 10th-c. Kashmir, later Vedānticized), traditional/pseudepigraphic Vālmīki ascription, plus the securely attributed Vāsiṣṭhamahārāmāyaṇatātparyaprakāśa commentary of Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī (Pansīkar Nirnaya Sagar ed.; Bronkhorst).
texts.source: web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mokshopaya; secondary:cleaned_Mimamsa and Vedanta. Interaction and Continuity_Johannes Bronkhorst, ed., 2007.txt
texts.note: Vairāgyaprakaraṇa = Part 1. Two distinct authors: anonymous mūla (Vālmīki ascription pseudepigraphic) + ṭīkā by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī.

texts.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 5, with commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
texts.current_author: attributed to Vālmīki (text); commentary Tātparyaprakāśa by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
texts.primary_author: Vālmīki (traditional, pseudepigraphic — actually anonymous, orig. Mokṣopāya); commentary Tātparyaprakāśa by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
texts.basis: Edition ascribes the mūla to Vālmīki and the Tātparyaprakāśa commentary to Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī. Scholarship (Slaje/Hanneder, Wikipedia summary) shows the text is not by the legendary Vālmīki: it derives from the anonymous Kashmiri Mokṣopāya (10th c.), later reworked into the Trika-influenced Yogavāsiṣṭha (11th–14th c.); real author unknown. Commentary is a distinct, much later author.
texts.note: Mūla pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki; genuinely anonymous (Mokṣopāya layer). Commentary Tātparyaprakāśa securely by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī — distinct author/role, hence conflation of text + commentary.

texts.title: Abhinavagupta: Paramārthasāra
texts.primary_author: Abhinavagupta (reworked kārikās, after Ādiśeṣa's older Paramārthasāra); Yogarāja (vivṛti)
texts.basis: Edition (KSTS 7) = Abhinavagupta's Paramārthasāra with Yogarāja's vivṛti. Secondary lit (skt-en-mono; sanskrit-trikashaivism.com) states Abhinavagupta's 105-verse Paramārthasāra is a Trika reworking of an older Vaiṣṇava Paramārthasāra (Ādhāra-kārikā) by Ādiśeṣa/Śeṣa; Yogarāja (disciple-line of Kṣemarāja/Abhinavagupta) composed the vivṛti commentary.
texts.note: Verses = Abhinavagupta's reworking of Ādiśeṣa's earlier Paramārthasāra; commentary (vivṛti) = Yogarāja. Three distinct contributors across roles → conflation; current_author lists only Abhinavagupta.

texts.title: Stotraratna
texts.basis: GRETIL header names no author (input S. Ishitobi only); but the Stotraratna is universally credited to Yāmuna/Yāmunācārya (Bartley, Intro to Indian Philosophy; Potter EIP I). Edition is the stotra alone, without Vedānta Deśika's commentary, so the e-text is single-author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Introduction to Indian Philosophy_Christopher Bartley, 2011.txt
texts.note: Devotional hymn securely by Yāmuna (c. 11th c.), grandson of Nāthamuni. No commentary in this e-text → no conflation.

texts.title: Saṃvitsiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header credits Yāmuna; edition is Mesquita's critical ed. (1988). Secondary lit (Bartley; Potter EIP I; Mimamsa and Vedanta, Bronkhorst ed.) lists Saṃvitsiddhi as part of Yāmuna's Siddhitrayī (Ātmasiddhi, Īśvarasiddhi, Saṃvitsiddhi). Note: survives only in fragments, reconstructed by Mesquita.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Mimamsa and Vedanta. Interaction and Continuity_Johannes Bronkhorst, ed., 2007.txt; secondary:cleaned_Introduction to Indian Philosophy_Christopher Bartley, 2011.txt
texts.note: Third tract of Yāmuna's Siddhitrayī; securely Yāmuna. Text is fragmentary (Mesquita reconstruction) but authorship undisputed; single author.

texts.title: Nyāyavārttikatātparyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 1.2
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'ity audayanatātparyapariśuddhau ... prathamādhyāyaḥ' — names Udayana. Secondary lit (Ganeri, Philosophy in Classical India; Burton; Potter EIP II) all assign the Pariśuddhi to Udayana. The work is a sub-commentary on Vācaspati Miśra's Tātparyaṭīkā, but this e-text contains only Udayana's text.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Philosophy in Classical India. The Proper Work of Reason_Jonardon Ganeri, 2001.txt
texts.note: Udayana's sub-comm. on Vācaspati's Tātparyaṭīkā (chain: Nyāyasūtra → Vātsyāyana Bhāṣya → Uddyotakara Vārttika → Vācaspati Tātparyaṭīkā → Udayana Pariśuddhi). E-text is Udayana's text only → single author, not conflated.

texts.title: Parātriśikāvivṛti (Parātrīśikāvivaraṇa)
texts.primary_author: Abhinavagupta (vivaraṇa); root Parātriśikā = anonymous Tantric āgama
texts.basis: Edition (KSTS 18) = Abhinavagupta's Parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa. Secondary lit (Gnoli's ed./tr.; Lit. about Phil. Systems; Encyclopedia of Tantra) treats the vivaraṇa (long commentary) as securely Abhinavagupta's, commenting on the Parātriśikā/Parātrīśikā — an anonymous scriptural fragment (excerpt of the Rudrayāmala/Trika āgama), not authored by a named historical person.
texts.source: gretil-header N/A (muktabodha url); secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:tradition
texts.note: Commentary (vivaraṇa) securely Abhinavagupta; root verses = anonymous Tantric scripture (Parātriśikā). Distinct author roles → conflation; current_author records only Abhinavagupta the commentator.

texts.title: Śiva-Purāṇa 7 [Vāyavīya-Saṃhitā]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Venkatesvara ed., input J. Takashima) names no author; no colophon naming a composer. Purāṇic genre, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa as redactor/revealer. Composite stratified text.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Vyāsa only; no historical author. Composite anonymous Purāṇic saṃhitā.

texts.title: Saṃvitsiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Mesquita 1988 crit. ed.) titles it 'Yamuna: Samvitsiddhi'. Secondary lit (Bartley 2011; Bronkhorst ed. 2007) places Saṃvitsiddhi as one of the three Siddhis of Yāmuna's Siddhitraya (with Ātmasiddhi, Īśvarasiddhi).
texts.note: Single Viśiṣṭādvaita author; text survives in fragments, critically reconstructed by Mesquita. Part of the Siddhitraya.

texts.title: Paramārthasāra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Faliero) and v.3 attribute the root verses to Abhinavagupta; this e-text is the mūla only (Yogarāja's Vivṛti NOT included here). Secondary lit confirms Abhinavagupta authorship.
texts.note: Distinct from the OLDER Paramārthasāra by Ādiśeṣa (a Vaiṣṇava Ādhāra-kārikā work) which Abhinavagupta recast/adapted; title is shared by two authors but this e-text is Abhinavagupta's recension. Yogarāja's Vivṛti is a separate commentary not in this file.

texts.title: Bhairavastava
texts.basis: GRETIL e-text (Faliero 1998) internal colophon: 'vasurasapauṣe kṛṣṇadaśamyām abhinavaguptaḥ stavam imam akarot' and closing 'stavam idam abhinavākhyam'. Self-attributed.
texts.note: Colophon explicitly names Abhinavagupta as composer; minor stotra (nine verses).

texts.title: Abhinavagupta: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvivṛtivimarśinī, Volume 1
texts.basis: Edition (Kaul Shastri, KSTS 60, 1938). The IPVV is Abhinavagupta's 'long commentary' (vivṛti-vimarśinī) on Utpaladeva's Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā and Utpala's own (largely lost) Vivṛti. Title=Abhinavagupta's work.
texts.note: Layered text: root kārikās by Utpaladeva, the sub-commentary (IPVV) by Abhinavagupta. The work itself (IPVV) is securely Abhinavagupta's; Utpaladeva listed as kārikā author for the underlying base. Not a conflation of a single title's authorship.

texts.title: Mālinīślokavārttika: Kāṇḍa 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Kaul Shastri KSTS XXXI 1921; Hanneder 1998 for I.1-399) and colophon 'kāśmīriko 'bhinavaguptaḥ ... vārttikam etad racayati sma'. Vārttika on the Mālinīvijayottaratantra.
texts.note: Colophon names Abhinavagupta; the Mālinīślokavārttika (=Mālinīvijayavārttika) is his vārttika on the Mālinīvijayottaratantra. Single author.

texts.title: Nyāyavārttikatātparyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 1.1
texts.basis: GRETIL header states author = Udayana, ed. Dvivedin (Bibliotheca Indica); text is itself a sub-commentary on Vācaspati Miśra's Tātparyaṭīkā but the Pariśuddhi is securely Udayana's own work.
