(Header gloss calling Udayana a 'Kashmir Śaiva logician' is erroneous — he is the Naiyāyika of c. 10th–11th c.)
texts.note: Secure Nyāya attribution. The work is the topmost layer of the NS commentary tower (Vātsyāyana Bhāṣya > Uddyotakara Vārttika > Vācaspati Tātparyaṭīkā > Udayana Pariśuddhi) but the Pariśuddhi proper is single-authored by Udayana.

texts.title: Abhinavagupta: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvivṛtivimarśinī, Volume 3
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism; skt-en-mono) and web (Springer/JIP, Sanderson/Torella) confirm the title is Abhinavagupta's vimarśinī ON Utpaladeva's (now largely lost) Vivṛti, which itself glosses Utpaladeva's own Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā. The composition (vimarśinī) is Abhinavagupta's; the underlying vivṛti and the root kārikās embedded/commented are Utpaladeva's.
texts.note: Current record lists only Abhinavagupta. The title-complex conflates Utpaladeva (kārikā + the 'Aṣṭasāhasrī' vivṛti, mostly lost) with Abhinavagupta (vivṛtivimarśinī). Distinct from Abhinavagupta's separate Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī (on the kārikā alone).

texts.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 4, with the commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
texts.current_author: attributed to Vālmīki (anonymous/composite); commentary by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Hanneder, 'The Yogavāsiṣṭha and its Kashmirian recension, the Mokṣopāya'; Slaje; NCat references to Bhāskarakaṇṭha's Mokṣopāyaṭīkā) shows the root work is the anonymous/composite Mokṣopāya, only pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki; the Tātparyaprakāśa commentary is by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Root text: pseudo-Vālmīki, anonymous/composite (orig. Kashmirian Mokṣopāya). Commentary: Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī. Two distinct authorial layers — conflation flagged.

texts.title: Bījanighaṇṭu
texts.primary_author: anonymous (framed as teaching of Śiva, transmitted via Bhairava)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. from Ram Kumar Rai, Dictionaries of Tantrashastra; data entry O. Hellwig) states the text names no human author; framed as 'śrīśiva uvāca' (Śiva speaking), a tantric mantra-lexicon of bīja-syllables transmitted through Bhairava/ḍāmara tantras — divine ascription, not a historical author.
texts.note: No historical author. Traditional/divine ascription to Śiva (via Bhairava) noted but not a real authorship claim; remains anonymous.

texts.title: Mṛtyuvañcanopadeśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (critical ed. Johannes Schneider 2010) and the chapter colophons 'iti śrīpaṇḍitavāgīśvarakīrtiviracite mṛtyuvañcanopadeśe ... paricchedaḥ' name Vāgīśvarakīrti (śrīpaṇḍitācāryavāgīśvarakīrti) as composer.
texts.note: Secure: author named in repeated colophons (viracita). Vāgīśvarakīrti = Vikramaśīla scholar, c. 11th c.

texts.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 2, with the commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
texts.current_author: attributed to Vālmīki (anonymous/composite); commentary by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
texts.basis: Same as Part 4: secondary lit (Hanneder, Slaje) shows root = anonymous/composite Mokṣopāya, pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki; commentary Tātparyaprakāśa by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī. This part = Mumukṣuvyavahāraprakaraṇa (Prakaraṇa 2).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Root text pseudo-Vālmīki (anonymous/composite); commentary Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī. Mumukṣuvyavahāra is the 2nd prakaraṇa. Two authorial layers — conflation flagged.

texts.title: Parātrīśikāvivṛti (Parātrīśikā-laghuvṛtti)
texts.primary_author: Abhinavagupta (laghuvṛtti/vivṛti); Parātrīśikā root verses anonymous (Trika tantra)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra) states the Parātrīśikā/Parātriṃśikā text 'constitutes the concluding portion' of a Trika scripture (Rudrayāmala tradition), and the Parātrīśikā-vivṛti/laghuvṛtti is Abhinavagupta's COMMENTARY on it. Zādoo ed. 1947 titled 'Parātriśikā Lāghuvritti by Abhinavagupta'. The scriptural root verses are not Abhinavagupta's composition.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt; secondary:cleaned_Understanding Mantras_Harvey P. Alper, 1989.txt
texts.note: Title conflates: anonymous scriptural Parātrīśikā root verses (concluding portion of a Trika/Rudrayāmala tantra) vs Abhinavagupta's laghuvṛtti commentary. Only the vṛtti is his.

texts.title: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'śrīmadācāryotpaladevaśiṣya ... śrīmadācāryābhinavaguptaviracitāyāṃ śrīpratyabhijñāvimarśinyāṃ' — the Vimarśinī is Abhinavagupta's commentary on Utpaladeva's Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā. Secondary lit confirms Bhāskarī is in turn a commentary by Bhāskara on the Vimarśinī.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Kārikā by Utpaladeva; vimarśinī (the work itself) by Abhinavagupta, disciple-lineage of Utpaladeva via Lakṣmaṇagupta. Edition adds Bhāskara's Bhāskarī sub-commentary.

texts.title: Abhinavagupta: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī
texts.basis: Same work as SA_GSP30_abhiprvu (KSTS ed.). The Vimarśinī is Abhinavagupta's commentary on Utpaladeva's Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā; colophon names Abhinavagupta as author of the commentary on the (Utpaladeva) sūtra/kārikā.
texts.source: gretil-header (parallel abhiprvu); secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Kārikā by Utpaladeva; commentary (vimarśinī = the named work) by Abhinavagupta. Muktabodha/KSTS no. 22 & 33.

texts.title: Abhinavagupta: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvivṛtivimarśinī, Volume 2
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Goudriaan ed. 1992 abbrev. list; Pandey/skt-en-mono) confirms IPVV is Abhinavagupta's 'great commentary' (bṛhatī vimarśinī) on Utpaladeva's own Vivṛti (Ṭīkā) on his Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā. The underlying kārikā and vivṛti are Utpaladeva's; the vivṛtivimarśinī is Abhinavagupta's.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism ... Padoux 1992.txt; secondary:cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt
texts.note: Three-layer text: Utpaladeva's kārikā, Utpaladeva's (now largely lost) vivṛti, and Abhinavagupta's vivṛtivimarśinī (the named work, KSTS 62). The work itself is Abhinavagupta's.

texts.title: Mṛtyuvañcanopadeśatārāsādhana
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Schneider crit. ed. 2010) names Vāgīśvarakīrti as author; colophon 'mṛtyuvañcanopadeśatārāsādhanaṃ samāptam' — single unified work, no multiple authorial voices. Secondary lit confirms Vāgīśvarakīrti as an 11th-c. Buddhist (Newar/Vikramaśīla) author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_YOSHIZAKI_Newar Buddhism.txt; secondary:cleaned_Emptiness that is Form ... Yarnall.txt
texts.note: Single-author Buddhist death-cheating Tārā sādhana. Attribution to Vāgīśvarakīrti secure per critical edition.

texts.title: Siddhitraya: Part 2: Īśvarasiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: Īśvarasiddhi is part of Yāmuna's Siddhitraya, attributed to Yāmuna (Ālavandar). Secondary lit (Bronkhorst ed. 2007; skt-en-mono) confirms Yāmuna's authorship of the Siddhitraya (Ātmasiddhi, Īśvarasiddhi, Saṃvitsiddhi), noted to survive in fragments. The GRETIL colophon also names a Siddhāñjana vyākhyāna by Śrī Kṛṣṇamācārya (Aṇṇaṅgācārya) — a separate later commentary layer in this edition.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Mimamsa and Vedanta ... Bronkhorst 2007.txt; secondary:cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt
texts.note: Root Īśvarasiddhi by Yāmuna (secure); the three siddhis survive only as fragments. Edition/file also carries Kṛṣṇamācārya's Siddhāñjana commentary — distinct author, not conflated into the root attribution.

texts.title: Ṣaṭtriṃśattattvasandoha
texts.current_author: Amṛtānanda(nātha) (attributed; sometimes given as Rājānaka Ananda)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Potter, Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies Vol.1, items 675.1 and the entry 'Vivaraṇa on Amṛtānanda Nātha's Ṣaṭtriṃśattattvasandoha') treats the mūla verses as by Amṛtānandanātha and the Vivaraṇa as a separate commentary; the KSTS edition is the Ṣaṭtriṃśattattvasandoha WITH the vivaraṇa of Rājānaka Ānanda. The current_author wrongly conflates the two and treats Rājānaka Ānanda as an alternative name for the verse-author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 1 - Potter.txt; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Mūla (Trika/Kashmir Śaiva 36-tattva compendium) by Amṛtānandanātha; vivaraṇa by Rājānaka Ānanda — two distinct persons. Edition (KSTS, ed. Mukunda Rāma Shāstrī) prints both.

texts.title: Sāratamā [āryāṣṭasāhasrikāyāḥ prajñāpāramitāyāḥ sāratamākhyā pañjikā]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Ratnākaraśāntiviracitā', a pañjikā on the Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā. Confirmed by Jaini's editio princeps (TSWS 18, 1979) and by Sferra (Tucci Collection Part I) and Drewes (Oral Texts in Indian Mahāyāna), both naming Ratnākaraśānti as author of the Sāratamā.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Sanskrit Texts from Giuseppe Tucci's Collection. Part I_Francesco Sferra.txt; secondary:cleaned_Drewes_Oral_Texts_in_Indian_Mahayana.txt
texts.note: Single-author pañjikā commenting on the Aṣṭasāhasrikā (root sūtra is anonymous Mahāyāna scripture, not co-author of this work). Variant titles Sāratarā/Sārottamā noted by Drewes.

texts.title: Chandoratnākara
texts.basis: Hahn 1982 edition (Nepal Research Centre). Secondary lit (Sferra/Tucci, Epperson 'Kālidāsa in Tibet') names Ratnākaraśānti as author; edition cited as 'Chandoratnākaraḥ: Svopajñavṛttyā Samanvitaḥ' — verses AND the author's own vṛtti are both by Ratnākaraśānti, so a single author throughout (not a conflation).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanskrit Texts from Giuseppe Tucci's Collection. Part I_Francesco Sferra.txt; secondary:cleaned_Epperson_Kālidāsa in Tibet_Messenger Poetry in Translation.txt
texts.note: Metrics treatise with svopajña (auto-)commentary; verses + vṛtti both Ratnākaraśānti's.

texts.title: Vṛttamālāstuti
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives the colophon verbatim: 'kṛtir iyaṃ mahāpaṇḍitasthaviraJñānaśrīmitrasya'. Confirmed by Hahn 2008 edition and by Epperson ('Kālidāsa in Tibet'). A separate commentary (Vṛttamālāvivṛti) exists by Śākyarakṣita, but that is a different work not contained in this e-text.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Epperson_Kālidāsa in Tibet_Messenger Poetry in Translation.txt
texts.note: Colophon names Jñānaśrīmitra. The e-text is the stuti (verses) only; Śākyarakṣita's Vṛttamālāvivṛti commentary is separate and not conflated here.

texts.title: Hetubinduṭīkāloka
texts.primary_author: Durveka Miśra (Āloka); Bhaṭṭa Arcaṭa (Hetubinduṭīkā); Dharmakīrti (Hetubindu mūla)
texts.basis: GRETIL header colophon: 'paṇḍitadurvekamiśrakṛto hetubinduṭīkālokaḥ' — the Āloka sub-commentary is by Durveka Miśra. But the edition (Sanghavi & Jinavijaya, GOS CXIII, 1949) and the e-text present a three-tier nested work: Dharmakīrti's Hetubindu (mūla), Bhaṭṭa Arcaṭa's Hetubinduṭīkā, and Durveka Miśra's Āloka sub-commentary. Confirmed by Nakamura (Indian Buddhism) and Kajiyama: Durveka the Brāhmaṇa commentator (late 10th–early 11th c.), Arcaṭa's ṭīkā on Dharmakīrti's Hetubindu.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt; secondary:cleaned_Introduction to Buddhist Philosophy...Tarkabhasa of Moksakaragupta_Kajiyama, 1966.txt
texts.note: Title is the Āloka by Durveka Miśra, but the e-text/edition embeds three authors' strata: Dharmakīrti's Hetubindu (mūla), Arcaṭa's ṭīkā, Durveka's Āloka sub-commentary. current_author captures only Durveka.

texts.title: Vṛttamālāstuti
texts.basis: Same e-text as SA_T12_n2957u (jvrtmspu.htm). GRETIL header colophon: 'kṛtir iyaṃ mahāpaṇḍitasthaviraJñānaśrīmitrasya'. Confirmed by Hahn 2008 and Epperson.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Epperson_Kālidāsa in Tibet_Messenger Poetry in Translation.txt
texts.note: Duplicate work-item of the Vṛttamālāstuti (same file). Single author Jñānaśrīmitra; Śākyarakṣita's separate vivṛti not in this e-text.

texts.title: Daśākuśalakarmapathadeśanā
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC colophon: 'praṇītā daśākuśalakarmapathadeśanā ... mahācārya-dīpaṅkaraśrījñānapādena'; further 'bhikṣu-jayaśīlena cānūditaḥ sampāditaśca' (recited/edited by monk Jayaśīla, an Indian preceptor).
texts.note: Composed by Atiśa; colophon also names monk Jayaśīla as transmitter/editor (anūditaḥ sampāditaśca), a redactional role, not a co-author.

texts.title: Śaraṇagamanadeśanā
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC colophon names author 'gurubodhisattva-dīpaṅkara-śrījñāna' (Atiśa). One of Atiśa's well-attested short doctrinal teachings (deśanā) in the DSBC Atiśa corpus.
texts.note: Secure single-author work of Atiśa; no commentary layer.

texts.title: Garbhasaṅgraha
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon (Negi ed., CIHTS 1992): 'garbhasaṅgraho nāma mahācārya-dīpaṅkaraśrījñānaviracitaḥ samāptaḥ' — attributes to Atiśa, NOT Nāgārjuna. Apple 2019 (Illuminator of the Awakened Mind, no. 239) lists Garbhasaṃgraha among Atiśa's works.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Atisa Dipamkara  Illuminator of the Awakened Mind_James B  Apple, 2019.txt
texts.note: current_author records a traditional Nāgārjuna ascription, but the edition's colophon and modern scholarship (Negi 1992; Apple 2019) assign it to Atiśa/Dīpaṅkaraśrījñāna. Attribution is therefore disputed; best display author = Atiśa.

texts.title: Kubjikātantra
texts.basis: Dyczkowski, Canon of the Śaivāgama (1988): treats the Kubjikā corpus as revealed scripture of the Paścimāmnāya (Western Kaula), no human author; pre-12th c. The Kubjikāmatatantra (Goudriaan & Schoterman 1988) is a redacted scripture, framed as Śiva/Kubjikā revelation.
texts.note: Anonymous āgamic/tantric revelation; traditionally ascribed to Śiva. No individual author; sources do not single out a redactor by name.

texts.title: Bodhisattvādikarmikamārgāvatāradeśanā
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC colophon: '...mārgāvatāradeśanā ācāryadīpaṅkaraśrījñānaviracitā samāptā'; also 'anūditā' by the paṇḍita and Jayaśīla ('lokacakṣuṣā ... jayaśīlena').
texts.note: Authored by Atiśa; colophon adds Jayaśīla as transmitter/editor (anūditā), a redactional role only.

texts.title: Suprabhedāgama
texts.basis: Saiddhāntika Śaiva Āgama; Sanderson, The Śaiva Age, cites the Suprabheda among scriptural āgamas (alongside Kāraṇa, Acintya, Pauṣkara, Vātulaśuddhākhya) treated as revealed canon. Muktabodha e-text (Devakottai series) presents it as scripture, not an authored work.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed āgama; revelation ascribed to Śiva. No human author; composite/redacted over time but no named layers in sources.

texts.title: Vimalaprabhāṭīkā on Śrī-Laghukālacakratantra (Vol. 2)
texts.basis: Edition title: Vimalaprabhāṭīkā of Kalkin Śrīpuṇḍarīka on the Śrī-Laghukālacakratantrarāja of Śrī-Mañjuśrīyaśas. Web (Wikipedia/Encyclopedia of Buddhism): root Laghutantra ascribed to Mañjuśrī-Yaśas, king of Shambhala; Vimalaprabhā commentary ascribed to Puṇḍarīka, also a Shambhala king/Kalki. Both are traditional Shambhala-lineage ascriptions, not historically verifiable persons.
texts.note: Two distinct (legendary) authors conflated under one volume: mūla tantra by Mañjuśrīyaśas, ṭīkā (Vimalaprabhā) by Kalki Śrīpuṇḍarīka. Ascriptions are Shambhala-lineage/pseudepigraphic; actual composition Indian, c. 11th c.

texts.title: Sūkṣmāgama
texts.basis: Edition (Sambandhaśivācārya, Ganesan et al., IFP/EFEO Collection Indologie 114) is a critical edition of a revealed Śaiva Siddhānta scripture. Āgamas are traditionally regarded as the word of Śiva, transmitted not authored; no human author. Editors function as redactors/editors, not authors.
texts.note: Revealed Śaivasiddhānta āgama; traditionally Śiva's teaching, no human author. Sambandhaśivācārya/Ganesan are modern editors, not authors. Remains anonymous.

texts.title: Tejobindu Upaniṣad
texts.basis: One of the Yoga Upaniṣads (White, Yoga in Practice; Potter EIP Vol.1): anonymous late/minor Upaniṣad, no human author. The Adyar edition (Mahadeva Sastri 1920) carries the bhāṣya/dīpikā of Śrī Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin, an 18th-c. South Indian commentator who glossed the 108 Upaniṣads.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_White_Yoga in Practice.txt | secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 1 - Potter.txt
texts.note: Mūla Upaniṣad anonymous; commentary by Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (distinct, datable person). Edition conflates anonymous root with named commentator, but root text stays anonymous.

texts.title: Ṣaṭsāhasrikā Hevajraṭīkā
texts.basis: Edition (Shendge 2004) = critical edition of the Ṣaṭsāhasrikā Hevajraṭīkā, a commentary on the Hevajratantra. Web: manuscript folio reads 'hevajra-tika-Vajragarbhasya' with Vajragarbha named as author (also styled Daśabhūmīśvara Vajragarbha; he is the bodhisattva-interlocutor of the Hevajratantra, so the ascription is a traditional/quasi-legendary authorial persona). Shendge doubts Vajragarbha's authorship of the later chapters.
texts.note: Commentary (ṭīkā) on the Hevajratantra, not the tantra itself. Ascribed to Vajragarbha (the tantra's own interlocutor) — traditional ascription; editor flags doubt over later-chapter authorship.

texts.title: Laghutantraṭīkā
texts.basis: Edition (Cicuzza 2001, Serie Orientale Roma 86): The Laghutantraṭīkā by Vajrapāṇi, critical edition. Web: a commentary on the first chapter of the Cakrasaṃvaratantra, blending Cakrasaṃvara and Kālacakra doctrines, datable to c. late 10th/early 11th c.; ascribed to (Bodhisattva) Vajrapāṇi.
texts.note: Commentary on Cakrasaṃvaratantra ch.1 by Vajrapāṇi (a human author using the bodhisattva name/persona). Not a root tantra. Single author.

texts.title: Vimalaprabhāṭīkā on Śrī-Laghukālacakratantra (Vol. 3)
texts.basis: Edition title (Dwivedi & Bahulkar, Vol. III): Vimalaprabhāṭīkā of Kalkin Śrīpuṇḍarīka on the Śrīlaghukālacakratantrarāja by Śrīmañjuśrīyaśas. Same work-pair as Vol. 2: root Laghutantra ascribed to Mañjuśrī-Yaśas; Vimalaprabhā commentary ascribed to Puṇḍarīka — both Shambhala-lineage (legendary) ascriptions.
texts.note: Conflates two (legendary) authors: mūla tantra by Mañjuśrīyaśas, ṭīkā (Vimalaprabhā) by Kalki Śrīpuṇḍarīka. Pseudepigraphic Shambhala ascriptions; vol. 3 of same edition as the vol. 2 item.

texts.title: Sekoddeśaṭīkā (Paramārthasaṃgraha)
texts.primary_author: Nāḍapāda / Nāropa (ṭīkā); root Sekoddeśa = anonymous Kālacakra tantra (traditionally Buddha-spoken)
texts.basis: Carelli 1941 edition titles it 'Sekoddeśaṭīkā of Nāḍapāda (Nāropā), being a commentary of the Sekoddeśa section of the Kālacakratantra'. Secondary lit (Williams/Tribe/Wynne; Newman, Brief History of Kālacakra) confirms Nāropa wrote a large commentary (= Paramārthasaṃgraha-nāma-sekoddeśaṭīkā) on the Sekoddeśa, which is itself a root-tantra section. The Sekoddeśa root (Kālacakra/Paramādibuddha ch. 5) is traditionally Buddha-spoken, not authored by Nāropa.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Newman_Brief History Kalachakra.txt; secondary:cleaned_Buddhist Thought_Williams Tribe Wynne 2000.txt; edition (Carelli 1941)
texts.note: Two layers: root Sekoddeśa (anonymous tantric root, traditionally Bhagavān/Sucandra) commented on by Nāropa in the ṭīkā (Paramārthasaṃgraha). current_author empty; set Nāropa as ṭīkā author and flag conflation with anonymous root.

texts.title: Spandasandoha
texts.basis: Bhattacharyya, History of the Tantric Religion: 'Spandanirṇaya and Spandasandoha by Kṣemarāja'. Potter EIP vol.1 and the Literature-about-Philosophical-Systems index both list 'Kṣemarāja's Spandasandoha'. Abhinavagupta-school self-reference in skt-en-mono ('I have extensively demonstrated in Spandasandoha') consistent with Kṣemarāja's authorship. KSTS no.16 edition (1917) is of Kṣemarāja's text. Spandasandoha is Kṣemarāja's commentary on the first kārikā of the Spandakārikā.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion_Bhattacharyya 2006.txt; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies Vol.1 - Potter.txt
texts.note: Secure: Kṣemarāja (pupil of Abhinavagupta), early 11th c. Pratyabhijñā/Spanda lineage. Single author.

texts.title: Kṣaṇabhaṅgasiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header reports the colophon: 'kṛtir iyaṃ mahāpaṇḍita-ratnakīrti-pādānām' (this composition is by the great scholar Ratnakīrti). Text is one of the Buddhist Nyāya tracts edited in Thakur's Ratnakīrtinibandhāvalī and in Haraprasad Shastri's Six Buddhist Nyāya Tracts.
texts.note: Colophon-attested to Ratnakīrti (11th c., Vikramaśīla, pupil of Jñānaśrīmitra). Single author, no commentary layer in this file.

texts.title: Apohasiddhi
texts.basis: GRETIL header reports closing colophon 'kṛtir iyaṃ mahāpaṇḍita-ratnakīrti-pādānām' (composed by the great scholar Ratnakīrti); scribal colophon names copyist Trailokyadata (not the author). Part of Ratnakīrtinibandhāvalī (Thakur).
texts.note: Colophon-attested to Ratnakīrti (11th c.). Scribe Trailokyadata is copyist, not author. Single author.

texts.title: Mṛtyuvañcanasitatārāsādhana
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (within the circle of Vāgīśvarakīrti's Mṛtyuvañcana cycle)
texts.primary_author: Vāgīśvarakīrti (source teaching); sādhana itself anonymous/redactional within his Mṛtyuvañcana cycle
texts.basis: GRETIL header: opening verse 'vāgīśvarakṛtādeśān mṛtyuvañcanasaṃgrahāt' (from the teachings composed by Vāgīśvarakīrti, drawn from the Mṛtyuvañcana compendium), so the sādhana explicitly draws on / is ascribed to Vāgīśvarakīrti's cycle. Schneider 2010 (Vāgīśvarakīrtis Mṛtyuvañcanopadeśa) is the edition framing it within Vāgīśvarakīrti's death-cheating literature.
texts.note: Text self-references Vāgīśvarakīrti as the source authority; sādhana (SM 103) is a derivative ritual text within his Mṛtyuvañcana cycle. Promotes from 'anonymous/traditional' to traditional ascription to Vāgīśvarakīrti (medium confidence — sādhana may be redactional rather than autograph).

texts.title: Sekoddeśa [Sekoddeśaṭīkā]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (root Sekoddeśa, Kālacakra root tantra, traditionally Buddha-spoken); Nāropa only for the ṭīkā
texts.basis: GRETIL file narsekou actually contains the ROOT Sekoddeśa verses only (ends 'iti śrīsekoddeśaḥ samāptaḥ'; opens 'oṃ namaḥ kālacakrāya', 'sucandra āha', 'bhagavān āha'), edited from Gnoli/Orofino. Newman (Brief History of Kālacakra) n.11: 'The Sekoddeśa is a section extracted from the fifth chapter of the Paramādibuddha' (the root mūlatantra of Kālacakra). So the root is anonymous/Buddha-spoken, NOT authored by Nāropa; Nāropa authored only the ṭīkā (Paramārthasaṃgraha).
texts.note: Title conflates the anonymous root Sekoddeśa with Nāropa's ṭīkā. current_author 'Nāropa' is wrong for the root verses present in this file (which are the Kālacakra root section). Flag conflation; root = anonymous, ṭīkā = Nāropa.

texts.title: Yaśodharācarita
texts.basis: Edition note and DSBC identify this as a modern (20th-c.) Sanskrit kāvya by the named Sri Lankan monk-scholar; web confirms Davuldena Gnanissara Thera (1915-2017), a Sinhala scholar-monk proficient in Pali/Sanskrit. Distinct from the medieval Yaśodharacaritas of Vādirāja/Puṣpadanta (Winternitz).
texts.note: Modern named author; not the medieval Yaśodhara legend versions (Vādirāja Sūri, Puṣpadanta's Jasaharacariu) mentioned in Winternitz.

texts.title: Caryāsaṅgrahapradīpa
texts.primary_author: Dīpaṅkaraśrījñāna (Atiśa) per colophon; attributed to (tantric) Āryadeva in Tibetan tradition
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'caryāsaṅgrahapradīpo mahāpaṇḍitācārya-dīpaṅkara-śrījñānakṛtaḥ samāptaḥ' (= Atiśa), matching current_author and Tucci's edition. However secondary lit (Ontology of Consciousness, Wautischer ed.) repeatedly attributes the Carya-samgraha-pradipa (Tib. Chod-du), a Guhyasamāja completion-stage work, to Āryadeva. Two distinct attributions for the title.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Ontology of Consciousness. Percipient Action_Helmut Wautischer, ed., 2008.txt
texts.note: Sanskrit text per colophon is Atiśa's; but the title is widely cited as Āryadeva's (tantric Āryadeva of the Guhyasamāja Ārya school). Same title, competing authorial ascriptions; not a single secure attribution.

texts.title: Bodhavilāsa
texts.primary_author: Attributed to Kṣemarāja in transmitted colophon; scholarship reassigns to Vāmanadatta (= Dvayasampatti)
texts.basis: GRETIL/Pensa colophon: 'iti śrīkṣemarājakṛtaṃ bodhavilāsākhyaṃ yogaśāstram' (= Kṣemarāja). But secondary lit (skt-en-mono, summarizing Rastogi/Sanderson) identifies the Bodhavilāsa with the Dvayasampatti, whose Kashmiri/BHU mss colophon names the author as the son of Harṣadatta, 'a lion in the jungle of Mīmāṃsā' = Vāmanadatta (cf. his Svabodhodayamañjarī colophon). Treated as a Kaulayoga work commenting on the Vijñānabhairava.
texts.note: Title transmitted under Kṣemarāja's name (Pensa's ed.) but scholarly identification with the Dvayasampatti points to Vāmanadatta; attribution to Kṣemarāja is doubtful.

texts.title: Kṣaṇabhaṅgasiddhi vyatirekātmikā
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'kṛtiriyaṃ mahāpaṇḍitaratnakīrttipādānām' (= Ratnakīrti). Secondary lit (Kajiyama, Tarkabhāṣā intro) describes the Kṣaṇabhaṅgasiddhi as Ratnakīrti's digest of Jñānaśrīmitra's Kṣaṇabhaṅgādhyāya; Potter, Nakamura, Mookerjee all list it under Ratnakīrti. The 'vyatirekātmikā' is the recension proving momentariness via vyatireka (vs the anvayātmikā).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Introduction to Buddhist Philosophy...Tarkabhasa of Moksakaragupta_Kajiyama 1966.txt
texts.note: Author secure (Ratnakīrti, 11th c.); the work is a digest based on Jñānaśrīmitra's Kṣaṇabhaṅgādhyāya, but authorship of this redaction is Ratnakīrti's own.

texts.title: Bhairavānukaraṇastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL/Gnoli colophon: 'iti śrīmahāmāheśvarācāryaśrīkṣemarājaviracitaṃ bhairavānukaraṇastotraṃ samāptam' (= Kṣemarāja). Edition is Gnoli 1958, who published it as 'by Kṣemarāja'. No competing attribution found.
texts.note: Stotra securely ascribed to the Kashmir Śaiva Kṣemarāja (pupil of Abhinavagupta), per colophon and editor.

texts.title: Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya
texts.basis: GRETIL/Chatterji colophon: 'śrīmato rājānakakṣemarājācāryasya' (= Kṣemarāja, devotee of Abhinavagupta). Secondary lit (Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism, Goudriaan ed.) describes it as 'Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya of Rājānaka Kṣemarāja with auto[commentary]'. The sūtras and the prose vṛtti are both by Kṣemarāja - distinct roles, single author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism...Padoux_Goudriaan 1992.txt
texts.note: Single author Kṣemarāja for both sūtras and auto-commentary; a summary of Pratyabhijñā doctrine drawing on Abhinavagupta, but not a multi-author conflation.

texts.title: Sūtrārthasamuccayopadeśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'viracitaḥ' by Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna, then 'anūdya sampādya ca nirṇītaḥ' by the mahāsaṃśodhaka Jayaśīla (an editorial reviser, not co-author). Single composer.
texts.note: Colophon names Atiśa as composer; Jayaśīla as scholarly reviser/finalizer (saṃśodhaka), a redactional role only.

texts.title: Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya (text version)
texts.basis: Secondary (Goudriaan ed. vol., Ritual and Speculation): 'Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya of Rājānaka Kṣemarāja with auto-[commentary]' — Kṣemarāja composed both the sūtras and his own vṛtti; this 'text version' is the sūtra text. Editions (KSTS III) name Kṣemarāja throughout.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism. Studies in Honor of André Padoux_Teun Goudriaan, ed, 1992.txt
texts.note: Single author; the work is a summary of Pratyabhijñā doctrine by Kṣemarāja with his own auto-vṛtti (not transmitted in this text-only version).

texts.title: Spandanirṇaya
texts.primary_author: Kṣemarāja (Spandanirṇaya commentary); Vasugupta or Kallaṭa (Spandakārikā root verses, disputed)
texts.basis: GRETIL file is the Spandakārikā with the Spandanirṇaya commentary combined: root kārikā by Vasugupta 'according to some, or by his pupil Kallaṭa according to others' (Sanderson); the Nirṇaya/commentary is by Kṣemarāja (colophon: 'nirṇītaṃ kṣemarājena' at request of pupil Śūra). current_author lists only the commentator.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_Śaivism and the tantric traditions.txt; gretil-header
texts.note: Two distinct authorial layers: root Spandakārikā (Vasugupta vs Kallaṭa, disputed) + Spandanirṇaya commentary (Kṣemarāja). Title/current_author conflate the commentator with the unnamed kārikā author.

texts.title: Śivasūtravimarśinī (text version) [commentary on the Śivasūtra revealed to Vasugupta]
texts.current_author: Śivasūtra revealed to/by Vasugupta; Vimarśinī commentary by Kṣemarāja
texts.primary_author: Kṣemarāja (Vimarśinī commentary); Śivasūtra traditionally revealed (ascribed to Śiva, 'discovered' by Vasugupta)
texts.basis: Sanderson: the Śivasūtra (9th c.) was 'discovered/revealed' by Vasugupta — traditionally divine revelation (Āgama ascribed to Śiva), not authored by Vasugupta as a human composer; the Vimarśinī commentary is by Kṣemarāja (KSTS I). Two distinct layers: revealed root + scholastic commentary.
texts.note: Śivasūtra is traditionally revealed (counted as Āgama, ascribed to Śiva via Vasugupta) — not a securely human-authored root; Vimarśinī commentary securely by Kṣemarāja. Root listed as anonymous with traditional ascription noted.

texts.title: Bodhipathapradīpa
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'dīpaṅkaraśriyā bodhipathaḥ proktaḥ samāsataḥ' — composed by Atiśa; ed. Losang Norbu Shastri (Sarnath 1984). Web (Wikipedia/scholarship): widely considered Atiśa's magnum opus, authorship well established. Sanskrit largely lost, preserved in Tibetan.
texts.note: Single author Atiśa; secure. Sanskrit original lost, reconstructed/preserved via Tibetan (Eimer ed.).

texts.title: Satyadvayāvatāranāma
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'ācāryadīpaṅkaraśrījñānaviracitaḥ satyadvayāvataraḥ samāptaḥ', then reviewed/translated by paṇḍita-anuvādaka Vīryasiṃha. Web (Apple/Springer scholarship): one of Atiśa's best-known works, composed by him. Authorship secure.
texts.note: Atiśa is composer (viracita); Vīryasiṃha is translator/reviser (anūdya saṃśodhya nirṇītaḥ), not co-author. Sanskrit largely lost.

texts.title: parāprāveśikā
texts.basis: Web (sanskrit-trikashaivism / scholarly): Parāprāveśikā composed by Kṣemarāja, disciple of Abhinavagupta (c. 1000-1050 CE); the standard KSTS edition (Mukunda Rama Shastri 1918) titles it as the Parāprāveśikā of Kṣemarāja. No alternative attribution or dispute found. Secondary lit had no contrary note.
texts.note: Single-author Trika/Pratyabhijñā prose primer; securely Kṣemarāja, matches current_author.

texts.title: Stūpalakṣaṇakārikāvivecana
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon (input Klaus Wille, ed. G. Roth 1997): 'Ārya-Lokottaravādino yat kṛtir Ācārya-bhadravyūhasya' — work of Ācārya Bhadravyūha of the Lokottaravādin school. Secondary (Deeg, Legend and Cult, p.454) cites Roth's 'Edition of the Stūpa-lakṣaṇa-kārikā-vivecanam'. No separate commentator named: the kārikā + vivecanaṃ form a single ascribed work, not a verse/commentary split between persons.
texts.note: current_author empty -> Bhadravyūha per colophon. Roth = modern editor, not author. Title pairs verses (kārikā) and analysis (vivecana) but both ascribed to one author.

texts.title: Aṣṭāvakragītā
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to the sage Aṣṭāvakra)
texts.basis: GRETIL header names no author (Source field blank; data entry John Richards). Web (Wikipedia/scholarly): text ascribed to sage Aṣṭāvakra but almost certainly not by him — Aṣṭāvakra and Janaka are the legendary guru/disciple interlocutors of the dialogue; real author is an anonymous Advaita writer; date disputed (5th c. BCE to 14th c. CE, Brockington favors post-Śaṅkara).
texts.note: Legendary/traditional ascription to Aṣṭāvakra is to the dialogue character, not a historical author; genuinely anonymous. Matches current_author intent.

texts.title: Garbhasaṃgraha
texts.basis: Secondary lit: Apple (Atiśa Dīpaṃkara, 2019), Vetturini (Kadam School, citing Drakpa Gyeltsen's list), Potter (Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies vol.1, item 18), and the Atiśa Cultural Renaissance conference proceedings all list a Garbhasaṃgraha among Atiśa's works (Tibetan Tanjur, tr. Tshul khrims 'byung gnas). NOTE homonym: Davidson (Tibetan Renaissance) records a different tantric *Garbhasaṃgraha by Kāṇha/Kṛṣṇācārya — a distinct work, not the same text, so no conflation within this title.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Atisa Dipamkara  Illuminator of the Awakened Mind_James B  Apple, 2019.txt
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Atiśa upheld; flag homonymous Kāṇha Garbhasaṃgraha as a separate work to avoid misidentification. Survives only in Tibetan translation.

texts.title: Vimalaprabhāṭīkā on the Laghukālacakratantra (Upadhyaya ed. 1986)
texts.primary_author: Kalki Śrī Puṇḍarīka (Vimalaprabhā ṭīkā); Mañjuśrīyaśas (root Laghukālacakratantra)
texts.basis: Edition title itself (Upadhyaya 1986) and web (Tibetan Buddhist Encyclopedia, Tsadra/Buddha-Nature, 84000): the volume joins TWO distinct authors — the root Śrī Laghukālacakratantrarāja abridged by the Kalkin king Mañjuśrīyaśas, and the Vimalaprabhā commentary (Stainless Light) by his successor Kalki Śrī Puṇḍarīka (emanation of Avalokiteśvara), composed first half of 11th c.
texts.note: Multi-author title: root tantra (Mañjuśrīyaśas) + ṭīkā (Puṇḍarīka) by different persons in the Kalkin lineage. current_author empty -> separated roles.

texts.title: Cittotpādasaṃvaravidhikrama
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC colophon: 'bodhicittotpāda-bodhisattvasaṃvaravidhir mahācārya-dīpaṅkaraśrījñāna-kṛtaḥ samāptaḥ' — composed by mahācārya Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna (Atiśa); later reviewed/edited by Bhikṣu Kuśalabodhi and Bhikṣu Jayaśīla.
texts.note: Colophon names Atiśa as author plus two reviewers (redactors); matches current_author. Reviewers recorded as secondary roles, not a conflation of distinct authors.

texts.title: Gurukriyākrama
texts.basis: GRETIL source URL 404 (not retrievable). Lessing & Wayman (Intro to Buddhist Tantric Systems, p.~) cite the Gurukriyākrama (Toh. 3977) as Atiśa's work on the seven-membered preliminary rite (saptāṅgavidhi). Vetturini, Kadam School vol2, lists bla ma'i bya ba'i rim pa [gurukriyākrama] as 'jo bos mdzad pa' = composed by the Jowo (Atiśa).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Introduction to the Buddhist Tantric Systems_Lessing & Wayman, 1968.txt; secondary:cleaned_Vetturini_Kadam School of TBism_vol2.txt
texts.note: Toh. 3977; short ritual work, single author Atiśa. Confirmed by Tibetan colophon ascription 'jo bos mdzad pa'.

texts.title: Bhojadeva: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Tattvaprakāśa
texts.current_author: Bhojadeva (King Bhoja of Dhārā, Paramāra dynasty)
texts.basis: No source_url. Secondary lit unanimous: Goudriaan (Ritual & Speculation) lists 'Tattvaprakāśaḥ by Mahārājādhirāja Bhoja with Tātparyadīpikā and Vṛtti'; Gavin Flood and Karl Potter bibliography (Literature about the Philosophical Systems) give Bhojadeva's Tattvaprakāśa, a Śaiva Siddhānta root work, with commentaries Tātparyadīpikā by Śrīkumāra and Vṛtti by Aghoraśivācārya.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism..._Goudriaan, 1992.txt; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; secondary:cleaned_gavin-flood.txt
texts.note: Root mūla (kārikā) text by Bhoja. The edition's commentaries (Tātparyadīpikā=Śrīkumāra, Vṛtti=Aghoraśiva) are separate works; the title here denotes the root, so no conflation for this item. Root authorship secure.

texts.title: Vajrayoginyāḥ stutipraṇidhānam
texts.basis: DSBC source (generic URL). Corresponds to the Trikāyavajrayoginīstutipraṇidhāna (GSS27) of the Guhyasamayasādhanamālā, catalogued in Elizabeth English, Vajrayoginī: Her Visualizations, Rituals & Forms. The GSS is an anonymous multi-author 12th-c. compilation of ~46 Vajrayoginī sādhanas/stotras; no secure single author is attached to GSS27.
texts.note: = Trikāyavajrayoginīstutipraṇidhāna / -stotra (GSS27). Part of anonymous GSS collection; remains anonymous. No traditional single ascription confirmed.

texts.title: Marmakālikā
texts.basis: GRETIL URL 404. Potter bibliography (Literature about the Philosophical Systems, DU758A) states: 'Marmakalikapanjika on Sunyasamadhipada's Tattvajnanasamsiddhi', edited with Vīryaśrīmitra's Marmakalikāpañjikā by Janardana Shastri Pandey (Sarnath 2000) — matching the edition cited. Web confirms commentary Marmakālikāpañjikā authored by Vīryaśrīmitra on the Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi root.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:WebSearch Tattvajnanasamsiddhi/Viryasrimitra
texts.note: Title 'Marmakālikā' = the Marmakalikāpañjikā commentary by Vīryaśrīmitra; conflates root (mūla) Tattvajñānasaṃsiddhi of Śūnyasamādhipāda with the pañjikā of Vīryaśrīmitra. Was 'anonymous'; commentator now identified.

texts.title: Vimalaratnalekha
texts.basis: DSBC generic URL. Warder (Indian Buddhism) names the Vimalaratnalekha as Atiśa's epistle to Emperor Nayapāla. Eimer & Germano (Many Canons 2) and Ehrhard identify Dri ma med pa rin po che'i 'phrin yig = Vimalaratnalekha, Atiśa's letter to King Neyapāla. Potter bibliography (item 22D) lists it among Atiśa's works.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism_Warder, 2000.txt; secondary:cleaned_Many Canons of Tibetan Buddhism 2_Eimer/Germano, 2002.txt; secondary:cleaned_Ehrhard_16 thig le and glegs bam.txt
texts.note: Verse-epistle (lekha) of Atiśa addressed to King Nayapāla/Neyapāla. Single author, secure.

texts.title: Bhramaharanāma Hevajrasādhana
texts.basis: Edition = Isaacson 2002 critical edition (Studies in Ratnākaraśānti's Tantric Works III), explicitly Ratnākaraśānti's. Seton (Defining Wisdom, Saratama) lists 'Khrul pa spong ba... (Bhramaharanāma-Hevajrasādhana) by Rin-chen-'byung-gnas-zhi-ba (Ratnākaraśānti); trans. unknown (Atiśa?). Sobisch (Hevajra and Lam 'bras) also attributes it to Ratnākaraśānti.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Defining Wisdom. Ratnakarasanti's Saratama_Seton, 2015.txt; secondary:cleaned_Hevajra and Lam 'bras Literature_Sobisch, 2008.txt; edition:Isaacson 2002
texts.note: Hevajra sādhana ('removing error/the bee'); single author Ratnākaraśānti. Tibetan translator uncertain (possibly Atiśa) but Sanskrit authorship secure.

texts.title: Subhāṣitaratnakoṣa
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Vidyākara, Subhāṣitaratnakoṣa', ed. Kosambi & Gokhale (HOS 42, 1957), input H. Isaacson. The Subhāṣitaratnakoṣa is an anthology compiled by the Buddhist scholar Vidyākara (Jagaddala, c. 11th-12th c.); each verse internally attributed to its individual poet, but the work itself is Vidyākara's compilation.
texts.note: Anthology: thousands of source poets cited per verse, but authorship of the WORK = compiler Vidyākara. Not a conflation in the multi-author-role sense; it is a koṣa (treasury) by definition citing many poets. vidsripu = unicode input version.

texts.title: Subhāṣitaratnakoṣa
texts.basis: GRETIL header: same work as vidsripu; this (vidsrgpu) is the GRETIL-revised/corrected hybrid version (Isaacson text with Brzezinski/Gaudiya readings reconciled, missing verses restored, reference system revised). Author of compilation = Vidyākara.
texts.note: Duplicate work-record, corrected/revised e-text version of the Subhāṣitaratnakoṣa. Same compiler. Per-verse poets cited internally.

texts.title: Advayavajrasaṃgraha
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib, Springer/JIPh, Maitripada-Wikipedia): the Advayavajrasaṃgraha (ed. H. P. Shāstri / B. Bhattacharyya, GOS 40, Baroda 1927) is a COLLECTION of ~21 short works ascribed to Advayavajra, an alternate name of the 11th-c. mahāsiddha Maitrīpa; corresponds to the Tibetan Amanasikāra cycle. Secondary lit (Sferra, Tucci's Collection; Moonbeams of Mahāmudrā; Davidson; Kongtrul) confirms Advayavajra = Maitrīpa and treats the collection as his amanasikāra corpus.
texts.note: Title field was empty; supplied 'Advayavajrasaṃgraha'. A single-author NAMED corpus (Advayavajra's collected short works), not a conflation of distinct authors; however attribution of some individual pieces within the 21 is uncertain (medium confidence). Advayavajra ≡ Maitrīpa ≡ Maitreyanātha (siddha).

texts.title: Laghustava
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (one of the five hymns of the Pañcastavī)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Pañcastavī tradition; trad. ascr. Kālidāsa / Śaṅkara / a Kashmiri sage)
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib, Stotranidhi, Exotic India, INDOLOGY): Laghustava is the first of the five hymns of the Pañcastavī (Laghustava, Carcāstava, Ghaṭastava, Ambāstava, Sakalajananīstava), a Kashmiri Śākta/Kuṇḍalinī hymn to the Goddess; ed. with comm. by Pt. Harabhaṭṭa Śāstri (KSTS 90). Authorship of the Pañcastavī is unknown; variously/legendarily ascribed to Kālidāsa, to Śaṅkarācārya (impressed by it in Kashmir), or to an anonymous Kashmiri pandit. No secure author.
texts.note: Distinct from the homonymous Kālidāsa-corpus 'Laghustava' (a separate stotra of doubtful attribution noted in skt-en-mono among spurious Kālidāsa works). Traditional ascriptions vary; no author secured -> anonymous. Part of Pañcastavī pentad.

texts.title: Ṣaṭsāhasrikā Hevajrapiṇḍārthaṭīkā (Hevajratantrapiṇḍārthaṭīkā), ch. 10 (Tattvapaṭala)
texts.basis: Web (Sferra academia.edu; wisdomlib; Tibetan Buddhist Encyclopedia) + edition title: the Ṣaṭsāhasrikā Hevajrapiṇḍārthaṭīkā is a commentary (ṭīkā) on the Hevajratantra ascribed to (Daśabhūmīśvara) Vajragarbha, written from the Kālacakra viewpoint; Sferra 2009 edits/translates ch. 10 (the Tattvapaṭala commentary). Secondary (Kongtrul, Treasury v8p3): the commentary is 'generally attributed to Vajragarbha, whose identity is uncertain, and may in fact have' another origin — i.e. attribution traditional, author's historical identity unsettled.
texts.note: Title field was empty; supplied. Commentary (ṭīkā) on Hevajratantra; root tantra is separate (anonymous tantric scripture). Author Vajragarbha is the traditional commentator but his identity is flagged as uncertain in Kongtrul. The base tantra and its commentator are distinct, but this work-record is the COMMENTARY only -> single attributed role, no conflation.

texts.title: Revākhaṇḍa of the Vāyupurāṇa
texts.basis: not processed by fleet
texts.note: dropped from fleet batch; carried current author

texts.title: Tattvaratnāvalī
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Snellgrove, Indo-Tibetan Buddhism, citing Advayavajrasaṃgraha p.21) and web (Springer JIPh critical edition by Mathes) consistently credit the Tattvaratnāvalī to Advayavajra; ed. H.P. Shastri in Advayavajrasaṃgraha (GOS 40, 1927). GRETIL page n2918u returned 404.
texts.note: Single author. Advayavajra is the tantric mahāsiddha Maitrīpa (Tib. Maitrīpa), NOT the Yogācāra Maitreya(nātha) of the Five Treatises; the current_author string risks conflating these homonyms — they are one and the same person here, so no conflation flag, but the 'Maitreyanātha' label is potentially misleading.

texts.title: Phetkāriṇītantra
texts.basis: Muktabodha transcription of a 19th-c. printed ed. (Tantrasaṃgraha, ed. G. Kaviraj, 1970, pp.161-306). As a Tantra it is anonymous/revealed scripture; web (wisdomlib) notes only that it is quoted in later digests (Tantrasāra, Śāktānandataraṅgiṇī, Āgamatattvavilāsa). No named human author.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Tantra; quoted by later digests so pre-dates them. No reliable individual authorship; traditional ascription only as divine/āgamic revelation.

texts.title: Bhaṭṭāraka Sundara: Īśvarapratyabhijñākaumudī
texts.basis: Title and catalog (Muktabodha) name Bhaṭṭāraka Sundara as author of the Kaumudī, which by its title (-kaumudī, 'moonlight') is a commentary on Utpaladeva's Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā. Web could not independently corroborate Bhaṭṭāraka Sundara beyond MS-catalog level; one caution: 'Īśvarapratyabhijñākaumudī' is also used as an alternate catalog title for Abhinavagupta's Vimarśinī (Srinagar ORL no.838), so identity needs care.
texts.note: Root kārikās by Utpaladeva; commentary (kaumudī) ascribed to Bhaṭṭāraka Sundara — two distinct authors (verses vs commentary), hence conflation flag. Sundara attribution is traditional/catalog-level, not firmly corroborated.

texts.title: Tarkasopāna
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Tucci, Minor Buddhist Texts I, 1956) gives closing colophon 'kṛtir iyaṃ paṇḍitavidyākaraśāntipādānām' (this work is by master Vidyākaraśānti). Secondary lit (Nakamura, Indian Buddhism) lists 'Vidyākaraśānti: Tarkasopāna' and notes he depends on Dharmakīrti's logic.
texts.note: Colophon-confirmed single author; a Buddhist Nyāya/logic primer (sopāna).

texts.title: Nibandhāvalī
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. A. Thakur, Ratnakīrti-nibandhāvaliḥ, TSWS 3, Patna 1957/1975) presents a single-author anthology of Ratnakīrti's own essays (e.g. Sarvajñasiddhi, Īśvarasādhanadūṣaṇa); colophon verse uses Mañjuśrī-name epithets of the author. Secondary lit (Nakamura, Kajiyama) treats these as Ratnakīrti's works.
texts.note: Nibandhāvalī = 'collection of essays', all by the one author Ratnakīrti — an anthology of his works, not multiple authors, so no conflation.

texts.title: Gītagovinda
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. B.S. Miller 1977) cites 'śrījayadeva-kaveḥ' throughout and a colophon verse naming Jayadeva, son of Rāmādevī, associated with Bhojadeva's court. Universally attributed to the 12th-c. poet Jayadeva.
texts.note: Colophon-attested single author Jayadeva (12th c.); no authorship dispute.

texts.title: Kṣaṇabhaṅgasiddhi Vyatirekātmikā
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'kṛtiriyaṃ mahāpaṇḍitaratnakīrttipādānām' (work of Mahāpaṇḍita Ratnakīrti). Secondary lit (Kajiyama 1966; Wayman, Millennium of Buddhist Logic; Mookerjee 1935; Thakur ed. Ratnakīrtinibandhāvalī) uniformly attributes it to Ratnakīrti, a digest of Jñānaśrīmitra's Kṣaṇabhaṅgādhyāya.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Millennium of Buddhist Logic. Vol1_Alex Wayman.txt
texts.note: 11th-c. Ratnakīrti wrote two recensions of the same proof: Vyatirekātmikā (negative/exclusion correlation, this text) and Anvayātmikā (positive correlation); single author for both.

texts.title: Sumāgadhāvadāna
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'iti kṣemendraviracitāyāṃ bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatāyāṃ sumāgadhāvadānaṃ' — this is chapter no. 93 of Kṣemendra's Bodhisattva-Avadānakalpalatā (P. L. Vaidya ed.), authored/versified by Kṣemendra. The Sumāgadhā narrative as a story exists independently in older anonymous avadāna literature, but this particular kāvya version is Kṣemendra's.
texts.note: current_author 'anonymous/traditional' refers to the underlying legend; this edition is specifically Kṣemendra's chapter 93 of the Avadānakalpalatā (11th c., Kashmir).

texts.title: Gītagovinda-kāvya
texts.basis: GRETIL header + colophons attribute throughout to Jayadeva: 'śrījayadevaka', son of Rāmādevī, lineage of Bhojadeva (GG 12.21-22). Universally accepted as the work of the 12th-c. poet Jayadeva.
texts.note: Authorship undisputed; only the poet's region/patron and a few interpolated aṣṭapadis are debated, not the core attribution.

texts.title: Guhyavajravilāsinīsādhana
texts.basis: Sādhanamālā (Bhattacharyya ed.) is a compilation of 312 sādhanas by various authors; many bear traditional ascriptions. Dan Martin's bibliography records the Guhyavajravilāsinīsādhana ('Yoginīsarvasva') as a work of Siddhācārya Śābara (Śabaripāda); cf. English, Vajrayoginī, pp. 360-362.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Bibliography_tib_skt_philology_Dan Martin, 2006.txt; secondary:cleaned_Buddhist Goddesses of India_Mirenda Shaw, 2006.txt
texts.note: Traditionally ascribed to the tantric siddha Śābara/Śabaripāda; not strictly anonymous though transmitted within the multi-author Sādhanamālā collection (collection itself is a compilation).

texts.title: Janmamaraṇavicāra
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon attributes to 'śrīmadbhaṭṭavāmadeva', a mahāmāheśvarācārya. Secondary lit (skt-en-mono / Sanderson) refers to 'Vāmadeva, the author of the Janmamaraṇavicāra' and cites it (p. 21, p. 25), placing him in a Kashmirian Śaiva milieu.
texts.note: Kashmirian Śaiva author Bhaṭṭa Vāmadeva; attribution secure. Edition prepared under M. R. Shastri (KSTS 1918).

texts.title: Śivasūtravārttika
texts.basis: The title 'Śivasūtravārttika' designates TWO DISTINCT works by two different authors: (1) Bhāskara's Śivasūtravārttika (KSTS 4&5, 1916 — the edition cited here), an independent verse vārttika; (2) Varadarāja's Śivasūtravārttika (KSTS 43, ed. Madhusudan Kaul Shastri, 1925), a versification based on Kṣemarāja's Śivasūtravimarśinī. Web/secondary note Varadarāja = Kṛṣṇadāsa (same person). Sanderson and skt-en-mono cite 'Bhāskara, Śivasūtravārttika'. The current_author's 'ambiguity' arises from conflating these two homonymous works.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt; secondary:cleaned_sanderson_Purity and power among brahmans.txt; web:https://www.wisdomlib.org/definition/varadaraja
texts.note: This edition (KSTS 4&5) is specifically Bhāskara's. Varadarāja/Kṛṣṇadāsa is the author of a separate same-titled vārttika (KSTS 43), not an alternative name for Bhāskara — hence the title conflates two distinct authors.

texts.title: Yoginīhṛdaya
texts.primary_author: anonymous (root tantra); Dīpikā by Amṛtānanda; Setubandha by Bhāskararāya
texts.basis: Web (Padoux, Heart of the Yogini; wisdomlib): no information on origin of the Yoginīhṛdaya or its parent Vāmakeśvarīmata/Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava; presented as revealed Śrīvidyā tantra (dialogue Bhairava-Goddess), root text anonymous (~10th-12th c.). The cited edition bundles the anonymous mūla with two named commentaries (Amṛtānanda's Dīpikā, Bhāskararāya's Setubandha).
texts.note: Root tantra genuinely anonymous (traditionally framed as Bhairava's revelation, part of/sequel to Vāmakeśvarīmata). Edition conflates mūla + Amṛtānanda's Dīpikā + Bhāskararāya's Setubandha — distinct authors/roles.

texts.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha, Part 3, with the commentary Tātparyaprakāśa
texts.current_author: attributed to Vālmīki (anonymous/composite); commentary by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
texts.primary_author: anonymous/composite (mūla, pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki); Tātparyaprakāśa by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib, A History of Indian Philosophy Dasgupta): Yogavāsiṣṭha attributed to Vālmīki but real author unknown; composite text of many upākhyānas. Tātparyaprakāśa by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī (pupil of Gaṅgādharendra). Bendall catalogue (secondary, reference-literature) confirms text 'attributed to Valmiki' with Ānandabodhendra's Tātparyaprakāśa commentary.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Bendall_Catalogue of Sanskrit, Pali, and Prakrit books in the British museum.txt; web:https://www.wisdomlib.org/definition/yoga-vasishtha
texts.note: Mūla pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki (Rāmāyaṇa author); actually anonymous composite (Kashmir, ~10th c.). Commentary Tātparyaprakāśa securely by Ānandabodhendra Sarasvatī. Two distinct authorial layers.

texts.title: Śaivāgamādimāhātmya (Śivāgamādimāhātmyasaṃgraha)
texts.basis: Web (academia.edu working edition; OCHS lecture on Pauṣkarāgama): Śivāgamādimāhātmyasaṃgraha is a compilation of Purāṇa/Śaivāgama passages establishing the validity of the Śaivāgamas, with the author's own explanatory comments, by Jñānaprakāśa, a resident of Śālivāṭi (Tirunelveli), Jaffna, Sri Lanka, fl. late 16th-early 17th c. Same Jñānaprakāśa commented on the Pauṣkarāgama.
texts.note: Securely authored/compiled by Jñānaprakāśa of Śālivāṭi (Jaffna). Strictly a saṃgraha (anthology of cited scripture) with his own commentary, so role is compiler+commentator.

texts.title: Paraśurāmakalpasūtra
texts.current_author: traditionally ascribed to Paraśurāma (compiled by Sumedha); historically anonymous
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to Paraśurāma; compilation attributed to Sumedha); commentary Saubhāgyodaya by Rāmeśvara
texts.basis: Web (Wikipedia Parashurama Kalpasutra; Kamakoti Mandali; Weber 2010 critical edition): Śākta Śrīvidyā/Kaula sūtra-text traditionally ascribed to Paraśurāma, compiled by his disciple Sumedha; real author unknown. Cited edition (A. Mahadeva Sastri, GOS 22) includes Rāmeśvara's commentary Saubhāgyodaya (presented 1832).
texts.note: Mūla pseudepigraphically ascribed to Paraśurāma / compiled by Sumedha (both legendary); historically anonymous. Edition conflates anonymous sūtra-mūla with Rāmeśvara's Saubhāgyodaya commentary — distinct authors.

texts.title: Caryāgītikoṣa (Caryāgītikoṣavṛtti)
texts.primary_author: Songs (caryās) by various Buddhist siddhas (Saraha, Kṛṣṇacārya/Kāṇha, Lui, etc.); commentary (vṛtti) by Munidatta

authors.name: various mahāsiddhas (Saraha, Kāṇha/Kṛṣṇacārya, Lui-pā, Kambala, Virūpa, et al.)

texts.basis: Web (Wikipedia Charyapada; MDPI Religions 2023): anthology of ~47/50 caryā songs composed by 22+ Buddhist siddhacāryas (8th-12th c.), each pada's author named in the manuscript; Sanskrit commentary Caryāgītikoṣavṛtti by Munidatta (12th-13th c.). Secondary (reference-literature): Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (Bagchi & Śāstri ed. cited); Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism, extensive discussion of Munidatta's commentary on the Caryāgīti and the siddha-authors (Virūpa et al.).
texts.note: Gold-standard conflation: a single title bundles songs by many distinct mahāsiddhas (each caryā ascribed individually in the MS) PLUS a separate Sanskrit commentary (vṛtti) by Munidatta. Roots vs commentary by different authors; root songs themselves multi-author. Item had blank current_author.

texts.title: Dvāviṃśatyavadānakathā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Okada 1993 ed., input Klaus Wille): no author named, a medieval Buddhist text on donation-piety (Spendenfrömmigkeit), no traditional colophon author. Web (Okada ed. based on 22 Nepalese MSS; academia.edu work on Nepalese avadānamālā scribes): anonymous Nepalese avadāna collection; authorship a matter of compiler/scribe activity, no individual author.
texts.note: Genuinely anonymous late/medieval Nepalese avadāna compilation (22 stories on dāna). No author or traditional ascription; modern ed. by Mamiko Okada. Compiler unnamed.

texts.title: Kathāsaritsāgara
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'mahākaviśrīsomadevabhaṭṭaviracitaḥ kathāsaritsāgaraḥ' (composed by Somadeva Bhaṭṭa). Secondary lit uniformly attributes this 11th-c. Kashmirian kathā collection to Somadeva, court poet of Anantadeva of Kashmir.
texts.note: Single secure author; the Durgāprasād-Parab 1915 edition title itself names Somadevabhaṭṭa. Based ultimately on the (lost) Bṛhatkathā tradition but Somadeva is the composer of this recension.

texts.title: Carcāstava
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (one of the five hymns of the Pañcastavī)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Kashmirian Śākta hymn; one of the five stavas of the Pañcastavī)
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib/Kashmir hymn lit): the Pañcastavī is a pentad of hymns to Mahātripurasundarī of uncertain/anonymous Kashmirian Śākta-Krama authorship, traditionally only loosely associated (e.g. with Śaṅkara's Kashmir visit, or with Kashmirian pandits); no secure single author. Carcāstava is one of its five constituent hymns. Edition supplies Harabhaṭṭa Śāstrī's commentary.
texts.note: Root hymn anonymous; do not invent an author. Harabhaṭṭa Śāstrī is editor/commentator, not the original poet. Traditional ascription of the Pañcastavī is unsettled (variously to Dharmācārya, a Kashmirian pandit, etc.).

texts.title: Yogavāsiṣṭha
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vālmīki; core text = Mokṣopāya, of unknown Kashmiri authorship)
texts.primary_author: anonymous Kashmirian author (Mokṣopāya root, pseudo-Vālmīki); Bhāskarakaṇṭha (Mokṣopāya-Ṭīkā)
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: this GRETIL file is actually Bhāskarakaṇṭha's Mokṣopāya-Ṭīkā — 'Bhāskarakaṇṭho racayati ... vyākhyām' and colophon 'iti śrībhāskarakaṇṭhaviracitāyāṃ śrīmokṣopāyaṭīkāyām' — i.e. commentary by Bhāskarakaṇṭha embedding the root text. Secondary lit (Hanneder, Slaje, McCutcheon): the root is the Kashmirian Mokṣopāya, composed in Kashmir, of unknown authorship, only later/pseudepigraphically ascribed to Vālmīki and recast as the Yogavāsiṣṭha.
texts.note: Three roles conflated under one title: (1) anonymous Kashmirian author of the root Mokṣopāya; (2) pseudepigraphic ascription to Vālmīki (the vulgate Yogavāsiṣṭha); (3) Bhāskarakaṇṭha's ṭīkā, which is what this Slaje-based GRETIL text actually transmits. Changed vs current_author because the e-text is specifically the Bhāskarakaṇṭha commentary, not the bare root.

texts.title: Kaulopaniṣad
texts.basis: Edition (Tantrik Texts vol. 11, ed. Sitarama Shastri, 1922) = 'Kaula and other Upanishads, with commentary by Bhāskararāya'. Secondary lit (Digitalisierte Werke catalogue; Potter Encyclopedia, which lists 'Commentary on Kaula Upaniṣad' among Bhāskararāya's works) confirms the Kaulopaniṣad is an anonymous minor Śākta Upaniṣad to which the 18th-c. Śrīvidyā scholar Bhāskararāya wrote the commentary.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 1 - Potter.txt
texts.note: Root Upaniṣad anonymous (current_author correct for the text proper); conflation flagged because the edition transmits it together with Bhāskararāya's distinct authored commentary. No source_url available.

texts.title: Kathāsaritsāgara
texts.basis: Same GRETIL e-text as SA_GK21_sokss: header 'mahākaviśrīsomadevabhaṭṭaviracitaḥ kathāsaritsāgaraḥ'. Secure single author, Somadeva Bhaṭṭa of 11th-c. Kashmir.
texts.note: Duplicate work-item of the same Kathāsaritsāgara file (Durgāprasād-Parab 1915, Mallinson et al. input). Author secure.

texts.title: Kāvyaprakāśa
texts.primary_author: Mammaṭa (most of the work); Allaṭa/Alaka (completion of the final portion); Jhalakikar (Bālabodhinī comm.)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Mammaṭa's Kāvyaprakāśa with Jhalakikar's Bālabodhinī; bold = kārikās vs surrounding vṛtti. Secondary lit + web (Cambridge JRAS 'The Dual Authorship of the Kāvyaprakāśa'; skt-en-mono: 'completed by Allaka or Allaṭa, eleventh-century Kaś-[miri]'; wisdomlib): Mammaṭa composed the work up to (traditionally) the Parikara, and the remainder — about the last third of the 10th ullāsa on alaṃkāras, from c. v.118b — was completed by Allaṭa/Alaka. Authorship dispute persists since Māṇikyacandra (1159).
texts.note: Like the gold-standard example: one title conflates distinct authors — root composition split Mammaṭa + Allaṭa/Alaka (the latter finishing the alaṃkāra section of ullāsa 10), plus the modern Bālabodhinī commentary by Jhalakikar carried in this edition. current_author lists only Mammaṭa.

texts.title: Kāvyaprakāśa
texts.primary_author: Mammaṭa (kārikā + most of the vṛtti); Allaṭa/Alaka (completion of the last portion)
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Mammaṭa, ed. Ganganatha Jha. Secondary (skt-en-mono.txt): Kāvyaprakāśa 'completed by Allaka or Allaṭa (eleventh-century Kāśmīri)'. Web (JRAS 'Dual Authorship of the Kāvya-prakāśa'; wisdomlib): traditional verse via Rājānaka Ānanda — Mammaṭa wrote as far as 'parikara', the rest (roughly the last ~tenth, from 10th ullāsa v.118b onward) by Alaka; problem unsolved since Māṇikyacandra (1159).
texts.note: Classic dual-authorship case: Mammaṭa left the work unfinished; final portion of the Alaṅkāra (10th ullāsa) completed by Allaṭa/Alaka. Whole file = full text incl. vṛtti, so the completed portion is present.

texts.title: Kāvyaprakāśa
texts.primary_author: Mammaṭa (kārikā); last kārikās of 10th ullāsa by Allaṭa/Alaka
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Kāvyaprakāśa (Kārikāvalī)', Mammaṭa, ed. Jha — this file is the verse-only kārikā extraction. Same dual-authorship as the full text: the closing kārikās of the 10th ullāsa belong to the completion by Allaṭa/Alaka (secondary skt-en-mono.txt; JRAS Dual Authorship).
texts.note: Kārikā-only version of the same work; even the verses include the final block completed by Allaṭa, so conflation applies.

texts.title: Kādambarīsvīkaraṇasūtramañjarī
texts.primary_author: Anonymous (pseudepigraphically ascribed to the legendary king-sage Purūravas)
texts.basis: GRETIL header & colophon: 'iti śrīmahārājarṣivaryeṇa purūravasā viracitaṃ ... kādambarasvīkaraṇasūtraṃ savyākhyānaṃ samāptam' — ascribed to the mythical king Purūravas. Such ascription to a Purāṇic figure is conventional pseudepigraphy in kāmaśāstra erotica; real author unknown. Ed. input Oliver Hellwig 2001/02.
texts.note: Colophon ascribes work to legendary Purūravas; treat as pseudepigraphic, not a real historical attribution. Colophon also notes the text comes savyākhyāna (with its own gloss), but no separate commentator named.

texts.title: Mokṣopāya (with Bhāskarakaṇṭha's Ṭīkā)
texts.primary_author: Mokṣopāya: anonymous (trad. ascribed to Vālmīki); Bhāskarakaṇṭha (ṭīkā)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Bhāskarakaṇṭha's Mokṣopāya-Ṭīkā, ed. Walter Slaje (Graz/Aachen 1993-2002). Base text Mokṣopāya traditionally ascribed to Vālmīki but real author unknown (Kashmir, 10th c.; Hanneder/Slaje); later reworked into the Yogavāsiṣṭha. Commentary by the 18th-c. Kashmiri Bhāskarakaṇṭha.
texts.note: Two distinct layers: anonymous/pseudo-Vālmīki Mokṣopāya base + Bhāskarakaṇṭha's ṭīkā. The file is Bhāskarakaṇṭha's commentary on prakaraṇas 1-4 (Slaje ed.).

texts.title: Śrībhāṣya: 1,1.3
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Ramanuja: Sribhasya 1,1.3', input Sadanori Ishitobi; ed. R. D. Karmarkar (Catuḥsūtrī, Poona 1959). Śrībhāṣya is the standard, securely attributed Viśiṣṭādvaita commentary by Rāmānuja on the Brahmasūtra. This file is the bhāṣya on sūtra 1.1.3 only; the underlying Brahmasūtra (Bādarāyaṇa) is the lemma, not a co-authored part.
texts.note: Securely Rāmānuja's. Excerpt of one sūtra-section of his commentary; no conflation.

texts.title: Pratimālakṣaṇavivaraṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL file (DSBC transform) is actually the BASE text 'Samyaksaṃbuddhabhāṣitaṃ Pratimālakṣaṇam' (ed. Ngawang Samten & Janardan Pandey, Dhīḥ 35, 2003), colophon 'iti samyaksambuddhabhāṣitaṃ pratimālakṣaṇaṃ samāptam' — presented as Buddha-spoken, no named author; Mūlasarvāstivāda iconometry, pre-Atiśa (~10th c.). Secondary (YOSHIZAKI, PIATS-01): a separate Vivaraṇa commentary exists (Skt 'Sambuddha-bhāṣita-pratimā-lakṣaṇa-vivaraṇī'), Tib. tr. by Grags-pa-rgyal-mtshan & Dharmadhara (before 1322) — but it is NOT in this file.
texts.note: Workitem title says 'Pratimālakṣaṇavivaraṇa' (the commentary), but the GRETIL/DSBC text is the base Pratimālakṣaṇa only — anonymous, attributed to the Buddha. The actual Vivaraṇa (a distinct commentary) is not present here, so no conflation within this file.

texts.title: Śāstradīpikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Pārthasārathi Miśra as author (commentary on Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra). Secondary lit (Jadunath Sinha, Indian Realism; Bilimoria, Śabdapramāṇa) consistently attributes the Śāstradīpikā to Pārthasārathi Miśra (c. 9th c. Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsaka), also author of Nyāyaratnākara and Tantraratna. Numerous sub-commentaries (Yuktisnehaprapūraṇī, Prakāśa, Mayūkhamālikā) treat it as his.
texts.note: Single author; the work itself is a base commentary that later attracted multiple distinct sub-commentators (Rāmakṛṣṇa, Somanātha, Appayya Dīkṣita) — those are separate works, not parts of this title.

texts.title: Janmamaraṇavicāra
texts.basis: No GRETIL URL. Secondary lit (skt-en-mono): Bhaṭṭa Vāmadeva is the author of the Janmamaraṇavicāra; he refers to himself as the disciple of Yogīśvarācārya (identified by ed. Mukunda Rāma Śāstrī with Yogarāja, pupil of Kṣemarāja and commentator on the Paramārthasāra), and is said to have been a contemporary of king Anantadeva of Kashmir. A monist/idealist Śaiva.
texts.note: Self-named Kashmiri Śaiva author; KSTS edition by Mukunda Rāma Śāstrī 1918. Distinguished from the Siddhaguru/Siddhanātha of Śaivasiddhānta — not that author.

texts.title: Mānamanohara
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'iti śrīvādivāgīśvarācāryaviracitaḥ mānamanoharaḥ samāptaḥ' — composed by Vādivāgīśvarācārya (son of Vāgīśvarācārya); a Vaiśeṣika/Nyāya work on the seven categories defending each against Mīmāṃsā and Advaita. Ed. Swami Yogindrananda 1973. (Potter's Encyclopedia mentions a 'Manamanohara' near a Maithila grammarian Divākara Upādhyāya — a separate/loosely associated reference, not the basis of attribution here.)
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 2 - Potter 1977.txt
texts.note: Colophon firmly names Vādivāgīśvara. A possibly distinct 'Manamanohara' is cited under Divākara in Potter; not adopted, since colophon and standard edition are unambiguous.

texts.title: Tarkabhāṣā
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'śrīmanmahājagaddalavihārīya mahāpaṇḍita bhikṣu mokṣākaragupta viracitāyāṃ tarkabhāṣāyāṃ' — composed by the monk Mokṣākaragupta of Jagaddala vihāra. Kajiyama (1966, annotated translation) confirms authorship and distinguishes this Buddhist Tarkabhāṣā from two homonymous works (Naiyāyika Keśavamiśra's and a Jaina one); a late Buddhist epistemological summary (c. 11th–12th c.).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Introduction to Buddhist Philosophy... Tarkabhasa of Moksakaragupta_Kajiyama, Yuichi, 1966.txt
texts.note: Title shared by three different authors (Mokṣākaragupta=Buddhist, Keśavamiśra=Nyāya, Yaśovijaya/others=Jaina); this item is securely the Buddhist Mokṣākaragupta version. Not a conflation within one text.

texts.title: Kālahastīśavilāsa (Kālahastīśvaravilāsa)
texts.current_author: uncertain (attributed to a South Indian, Vijayanagara/Nāyaka-era court poet)
texts.primary_author: anonymous / uncertain (South Indian court poet, Vijayanagara–Nāyaka era)
texts.basis: No source_url. No hits in reference-literature/ for the title under any spelling. Web searches (title + author/Nāyaka/Vijayanagara) returned no scholarly statement identifying the author; results concerned the Kālahasti dynasty/place, not the work. No edition data. Attribution to a South Indian court poet remains a working guess, unconfirmed.
texts.note: Genuinely undetermined author; title names the deity of Kālahasti (Śiva). Retain 'uncertain'; do not assign a name. authors=[] as no individual is securely identifiable.

texts.title: Vedārthasaṃgraha
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Rāmānuja as author (input Sadanori Ishitobi). Secondary lit treats the Vedārthasaṃgraha as a genuine work of Rāmānuja alongside Śrībhāṣya, Vedāntadīpa, Vedāntasāra; van Buitenen's critical ed./tr. (Poona 1956) and Raghavachar's ed./tr. are standard. Later sub-commentaries (Tātparyadīpikā by Sudarśanasūri, Candrikātilaka) confirm it as Rāmānuja's base text.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Securely Rāmānuja (Viśiṣṭādvaita, 11th–12th c.). The text attracted distinct later sub-commentators (Sudarśanasūri etc.) — separate works, no internal conflation.

texts.title: Vādarahasya [Udayananirākaraṇa]
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'The authorship of the text is uncertain'; title taken from colophon 'udayananirākaraṇe vādarahasye'. Pandey (1984) ascribed it to Ratnakīrti, but Tillemans (1992) states 'its authorship by Ratnakīrti is doubtful' and that the title should be Vādarahasya; Dan Martin's bibliography flags 'wrong (or at least quite doubtful) identification of both author and title' in Pandey's ed. Ratnakīrti = 11th-c. disciple of Jñānaśrīmitra at Vikramaśīla.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Etudes bouddhiques offertes a Jacques May...Tillemans, ed.,1992.txt; secondary:cleaned_Bibliography_tib_skt_philology_Dan Martin, 2006.txt
texts.note: Attribution to Ratnakīrti is doubtful per Tillemans & Dan Martin; colophon names work as Vādarahasya (Udayananirākaraṇa), not the author.

texts.title: Vātūlanāthasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Vātūlanāthasūtra with the Vṛtti of Anantaśaktipāda'; colophon 'samāpto 'yaṃ śrīmad-vātūlanātha-sūtra-vṛttiḥ / kṛtir śrīmad-anantaśaktipādānām' attributes the commentary to Anantaśaktipāda. The Kashmir Krama-Śaiva sūtras are traditionally attributed to the Siddha Vātūlanātha; the Vṛtti is a distinct work by Anantaśaktipāda. The GRETIL e-text bundles both sūtra and vṛtti.
texts.note: Conflation: root sūtras traditionally ascribed to Siddha Vātūlanātha; Vṛtti composed by Anantaśaktipāda (per colophon). E-text contains both.

texts.title: Bhagavadgītābhāṣya
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Ramanuja's Bhagavadgitabhasya', input by Sadanori Ishitobi (2002). The Gītābhāṣya is the well-attested commentary of Rāmānuja (Viśiṣṭādvaita, 11th-12th c.); the work comments on the (pre-existing) Bhagavadgītā but the bhāṣya itself is securely Rāmānuja's.
texts.note: Secure single-author commentary; note the base text (Gītā) is itself an embedded epic text, but the bhāṣya is Rāmānuja's.

texts.title: Vātūlanāthasūtra
texts.basis: Same work as SA_GSP30 (archive.org copy of Madhusudan Kaul Shastri's KSTS 39 ed.). Edition title: 'The Vātūlanātha-Sūtras with the Vṛtti of Anantaśaktipāda'. Root sūtras traditionally ascribed to Siddha Vātūlanātha (Kashmir Krama Śaivism); Vṛtti by Anantaśaktipāda (colophon: kṛtir śrīmad-anantaśaktipādānām).
texts.note: Conflation: sūtra (Vātūlanātha, traditional) + vṛtti (Anantaśaktipāda). Edition bundles both, two distinct authors.

texts.title: Sekoddeśapañjikā
texts.basis: DSBC page titles the text 'Sekoddeśapañjikā' (input Miroj Shakya, Nagarjuna Institute); no author named in content. Orofino/PIATS-1992 explicitly speaks of 'the anonymous Sekoddeśapañjikā', listing it as a SEPARATE commentary on the Sekoddeśa distinct from Nāropā's Sekoddeśaṭīkā (= Paramārthasaṃgraha), Vijayapāda's Sekoddeśaṭippaṇī, and Kālacakrapāda's Sekoddeśaṭīkā. NB: the workitem's 'edition' field cites Carelli's ed. of Naḍapāda/Nāropā's Sekoddeśaṭīkā, which is a DIFFERENT work; the DSBC text bears the title Pañjikā.
texts.note: Sekoddeśapañjikā is anonymous (Orofino/PIATS). Distinct from Nāropā's Sekoddeśaṭīkā cited in the workitem's edition field — likely a mislabeled edition reference. No traditional ascription.

texts.title: Ahirbudhnyasaṃhitā, Volume 2
texts.basis: The Ahirbudhnyasaṃhitā is a Pāñcarātra Āgama/saṃhitā, an anonymous revealed scripture (no human author); framed as dialogue (Ahirbudhnya/Rudra to Nārada). Edited by M.D. Ramanujacharya, rev. F.O. Schrader (Adyar 1916); Schrader's 'Introduction to the Pāñcarātra and the Ahirbudhnya Saṃhitā' (1916) treats it as the most important Pāñcarātra saṃhitā, an anonymous composite text. Date debated (Schrader). Vol. 2 is part of the same anonymous saṃhitā.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion...N.N.Bhattacharyya 2006.txt; secondary:cleaned_gavin-flood.txt; tradition
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Pāñcarātra scripture; no human author. Edited by Ramanujacharya/Schrader — editors, not authors.

texts.title: Mitākṣarā, a commentary on the Yājñavalkya-dharmaśāstra
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Mitākṣarā commentary by Vijñāneśvara (son of Padmanābha-bhaṭṭa, court of Vikramāditya VI, d.1127); root Yājñavalkyasmṛti ascribed to legendary sage Yājñavalkya/Yogīśvara. Edition prints root + commentary together.
texts.note: current_author lists only commentator Vijñāneśvara; the edition couples it with the (legendary, pseudepigraphic) root smṛti of Yājñavalkya. Mitākṣarā = secure attribution to Vijñāneśvara (~late 11th/early 12th c.); root smṛti traditionally ascribed to sage Yājñavalkya.

texts.title: Kālacakrapūjāvidhi
texts.basis: Edition (RBTRD, CUTS Sarnath 2014) and item id label it 'Anonymous'. Kālacakrapūjāvidhi is a Kālacakra worship/ritual manual; such pūjāvidhis are typically anonymous compilations. No author named in edition metadata or located in secondary literature (only a bare title entry in Dan Martin bibliography).
texts.note: No named author; ritual compilation. Title also appears unattributed in Martin's bibliography. Leave anonymous.

texts.title: Abhayapaddhati (commentary on the Buddhakapālamahātantra)
texts.basis: Edition (Chog Dorje 2009): Abhayapaddhati of Abhayākaragupta, commentary on Buddhakapālamahātantra. Davidson 'Indian Esoteric Buddhism' lists Śrī-Buddhakapālamahātantrarājaṭīkā Abhayapaddhatiḥ ascribed to Abhayākaragupta (To. 1654). The root Buddhakapālatantra itself is an anonymous/revealed tantra (separate text).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
texts.note: Secure attribution of the ṭīkā to Abhayākaragupta (fl. c.1100). Root Buddhakapālamahātantra is anonymous scripture, not edited here as a separate item.

texts.title: Mitākṣarā
texts.basis: Same GRETIL file as SA_GSD36_mitaksara. Header: commentary Mitākṣarā by Vijñāneśvara; root Yājñavalkyasmṛti ascribed to legendary Yājñavalkya. Edition prints root + commentary together.
texts.note: Duplicate work-item of the Mitākṣarā. Commentary securely Vijñāneśvara; root smṛti pseudepigraphically ascribed to sage Yājñavalkya. Conflation of root + commentary in one edition.

texts.title: Kālacakrāvatāra
texts.basis: Edition: Kālacakrāvatāra of Abhayākaragupta, transcription from sole ms. (Asiatic Society, Calcutta). Newman 'Epoch of the Kālacakra': 'Kālacakrāvatāra of Abhayakaragupta. Asiatic Society of Bengal MS no. G. 4732.' Confirms author.
texts.note: Secure attribution to Abhayākaragupta (fl. c.1100), confirmed by Newman; single surviving ms. in Calcutta.

texts.title: Sekanirdeśapañjikā
texts.basis: Isaacson & Sferra 2014 edition title itself: 'The Sekanirdeśa of Maitreyanātha (Advayavajra) with the Sekanirdeśapañjikā of Rāmapāla'. Secondary lit (Mathes 2015; Szántó; Steinkellner Fs.): Sekanirdeśa root by Maitrīpa/Advayavajra (986-1063); pañjikā by his disciple Rāmapāla. Edition publishes root + pañjikā together.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Fine Blend of Mahamudra and Madhyamaka..._Klaus-Dieter Mathes, 2015.txt
texts.note: Work-item names the pañjikā (Rāmapāla), but the edition conflates the root Sekanirdeśa of Maitreyanātha (= Advayavajra = Maitrīpa) with Rāmapāla's pañjikā. Rāmapāla = one of Maitrīpa's four chief disciples.

texts.title: Sādhanamālā, Vol. 2
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (compilation of many authors); critically edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya
texts.primary_author: Anonymous compilation (many sādhana authors); ed. Benoytosh Bhattacharyya
texts.basis: Edition itself states it is a compilation of sādhanas by many different authors (individual sādhanas carry their own colophon ascriptions); critically edited by Benoytosh Bhattacharyya (GOS 41, 1928). Secondary lit (Nakamura; Lewis & Tuladhar; Bhattacharyya's Indian Buddhist Iconography 1958) consistently treats the Sādhanamālā as an anthology rather than a single-author work.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhist Iconography…Based on Sadhanamala_Benoytosh Bhattacharyya, 1958.txt
texts.note: Anthology of ~300+ sādhanas by diverse, mostly named Vajrayāna authors (e.g. Asaṅga, Nāgārjuna, Indrabhūti, etc. in individual colophons); no single author. Conflation flag true because the title aggregates many distinct authors. Bhattacharyya is the modern editor, not an author.

texts.title: Cakrapāṇinātha: Bhāvopahārastotra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (input Somadeva Vasudeva, ed. Mukunda Ramashastri, KSTS 14, 1918): root Bhāvopahārastotra by Cakrapāṇinātha, Vivaraṇa commentary by Ramyadeva Bhaṭṭa. Sanderson (The Śaiva Age, p.448) confirms Cakrapāṇinātha as the stotra's author, earlier than commentator Ramyadeva; a 1158/9 ms (as Bhāvopahārapūjā) puts him relatively early among Krama/Kaula Śaiva authors.
texts.note: GRETIL file bundles root stotra (Cakrapāṇinātha) WITH the Vivaraṇa of Ramyadeva Bhaṭṭa — two distinct authors. current_author lists only Cakrapāṇinātha; commentator role added (vivaraṇa = vṛtti-type).

texts.title: Cakrapāṇinātha: Bhāvopahārastotra
texts.basis: Same GRETIL file/edition as cakbh_pu (cakbhavu.htm, ed. Mukunda Ramashastri, KSTS 14, 1918): Bhāvopahārastotra by Cakrapāṇinātha + Vivaraṇa by Ramyadeva Bhaṭṭa. Sanderson confirms attribution and relative early date.
texts.note: Duplicate work-id of cakbh_pu (same title/URL/edition); file conflates root stotra by Cakrapāṇinātha and the Vivaraṇa commentary by Ramyadeva Bhaṭṭa.

texts.title: Śabdavyāpāravicāra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. M. R. Telang, Nirnaya Sagar 1916; input Daniele Cuneo) gives author as Rājānaka Mammaṭācārya; colophon: 'iti nikhilavipaściccakravartiśrīrājānakamammaṭācāryaviracitaḥ śabdavyāpāravicāraḥ samāptaḥ'. Single-author treatise; the Abhidhāvṛttimātṛkā of Mukula Bhaṭṭa is a separate, co-printed work, not part of this text.
texts.note: Colophon explicitly names Rājānaka Mammaṭa as composer. Attribution to Mammaṭa (the Kāvyaprakāśa author) is traditional but the colophon here is secure for this text. No conflation within the Śabdavyāpāravicāra itself.

texts.title: Abhayapaddhati
texts.basis: Edition (Chog Dorje, RBTS 25, 2009): Abhayapaddhati = commentary (paddhati/ṭīkā) on the Buddhakapālamahātantra by Abhayākaragupta. Davidson (Indian Esoteric Buddhism, pp.8788, 13357, 14113) lists 'Śrī-Buddhakapālamahātantrarājaṭīkā Abhayapaddhatiḥ' ascribed to Abhayākaragupta (To. 1654). Nakamura dates Abhayākaragupta late 11th–early 12th c.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
texts.note: Abhayapaddhati is itself a ṭīkā/paddhati on the root Buddhakapālamahātantra (root tantra is anonymous/revealed, not by Abhayākaragupta). Author of the commentary = Abhayākaragupta, secure.

texts.title: Pañcakramaṭippaṇī Yogimanoharā
texts.primary_author: Muniśrībhadra (ṭippaṇī); on root Pañcakrama asc. Nāgārjuna
texts.basis: Edition (Jiang & Tomabechi 1996) titles it 'The Pañcakramaṭippaṇī of Muniśrībhadra'. Hatchell (Naked Seeing diss.) abbreviates 'PKTYM: Muniśrībhadra's Pañcakramaṭippaṇī Yogīmanoharā'. Steinkellner-Festschrift (Pramāṇakīrtiḥ, pp.14503ff): only extant work attributed to Muniśrībhadra; a ṭippaṇī combining explanation of stages 1,3–5 of the Pañcakrama with the Guhyasamāja pseudo-Nāgārjuna's Piṇḍīkramasādhana; translated by Bu ston (1320s–1335). Kongtrul Treasury also lists it under Muniśrībhadra.
texts.note: Workitem title/current_author empty; filled from edition: title = Pañcakramaṭippaṇī Yogimanoharā, author of the ṭippaṇī = Muniśrībhadra. The commented root Pañcakrama is ascribed to (pseudo-)Nāgārjuna of the Guhyasamāja Ārya school — distinct from the commentator but root not conflated into this commentary file.

texts.title: Kriyāsaṁgrahapañjikā
texts.basis: DSBC e-text (ed. R. Tanemura 2004) and multiple secondary works attribute the Kriyāsaṃgrahapañjikā squarely to Kuladatta: O'Neal 2011 'Kriyāsamgrahapañjikā of Kuladatta, a Buddhist Tantric ritual compendium'; Schopen 1997 cites 'Kuladatta's Kriyāsamgraha'; Banerjee 1977 notes it is also called 'Kuladattapañjikā'; Skorupski 2002 edition. Single author of this ritual compendium/pañjikā; no separate kārikā by another hand.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Written Stupa, Painted Sutra…_Halle Elizabeth O'Neal,2011.txt
texts.note: Title is itself a pañjikā (commentarial compendium) but is authored as a unified work by Kuladatta; alt. name Kuladattapañjikā confirms single-author identification. ~12th c. Newar Buddhist ritual text.

texts.title: Karpūrādistotra (Karpūrādi-stotra)
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the Mahānirvāṇa/Śākta tantric tradition)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (root stotra; traditionally ascribed to Mahākāla); Vimalānanda Svāmī (Svarūpavyākhyā commentary)
texts.basis: Avalon/Woodroffe preface (Tantrik Texts IX): this Kaula stotra 'is attributed to Mahākāla Himself' — i.e. a divine/traditional ascription, not a historical human author.
