Kālī-rahasyam prefaces it as by 'the ṛṣi Mahākāla'; scholars note internal phrases (we are 'dull of mind', 'bonded animal') imply an actual human (late-medieval, ~14th-17th c.) composer who is unnamed. Eckel/Herling 2008 quotes the Karpūrādi Stotra as a Kālī hymn. The edition pairs the root stotra with the separate Svarūpavyākhyā commentary by Vimalānanda Svāmī (pupil of Rāmānanda Svāmī).
texts.note: Root hymn anonymous, traditionally/pseudepigraphically ascribed to the god Mahākāla (Śiva); historical author unknown. Edition conflates two distinct hands: anonymous stotra + Vimalānanda Svāmī's Svarūpavyākhyā commentary. Translator Arthur Avalon (Woodroffe).

texts.title: Viśvaprakāśa
texts.basis: Bendall (British Museum catalogue) repeatedly refers to 'the Viśvaprakāśa of Maheśvara' and treats it as the author's own work (with the Śabdabhedaprakāśa described as a sequel to 'the author's Viśvaprakāśa'). Gold 2007 (Sakya Paṇḍita) groups it among standard lexicographic kośas alongside the Amarakośa. Web: a homonymic kośa composed 1111/1112 CE by Maheśvara, son of Brāhmendra/Brāhma and grandson of Kṛṣṇa. Single-author lexicon.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Bendall_Catalogue of the Sanskrit manuscripts in the British museum.txt
texts.note: Anantar­tha/homonym lexicon, dated by its own colophon to Śaka 1033 (= 1111/12 CE). Author Maheśvara (epithet Kavi/Kavīndra); current attribution stands. Distinct from the deity Maheśvara.

texts.title: Kālacakrāvatāra
texts.basis: DSBC e-text header/colophon (input Bibek Shakya 2018, Nagarjuna Institute / Univ. of the West) reads 'paṇḍita abhayākaraguptapādānām Kālacakrāvatāraḥ', attributing the work to Abhayākaragupta. Abhayākaragupta is the well-attested late-11th/early-12th c. Vikramaśīla/Nālandā mahāpaṇḍita (Nakamura 1980, PIATS 1992, Kapstein 2009 in secondary lit). No separate commentary by another author in this e-text.
texts.note: current_author was empty; resolved to Abhayākaragupta per DSBC colophon. Sanskrit reconstructed from the sole Calcutta Asiatic Society ms. A stray web claim attributing a 'Kālacakrāvatāra' to Dharmakīrti is unreliable/confused and not adopted.

texts.basis: Edition record names the work 'Kālacakrāvatāra of Abhayākaragupta', ed./transcribed by Vijayarāja Vajrācārya (CUTS, Sarnath); cf. R. Mishra 2016 edition; sole surviving ms. from the Asiatic Society, Calcutta. Same text and author as SA_T02_n2965u (DSBC colophon 'abhayākaraguptapādānām'). Abhayākaragupta is the historically attested Vikramaśīla mahāpaṇḍita (late 11th–early 12th c.).
texts.note: Duplicate work record (title field blank in item; = Kālacakrāvatāra). current_author empty; resolved to Abhayākaragupta. Modern editors Vijayarāja Vajrācārya / R. Mishra (2016) are editors, not authors.

texts.title: Śrīvatsa: Cidgaganacandrikā
texts.basis: Trivikrama Tirtha edition (Tantrik Texts XX) and scholarship (wisdomlib Cidgaganacandrikā study) identify the author as Śrīvatsa, with 'Kālidāsa' as an honorific title (not the classical poet) earned for poetic brilliance per his own statement. The 312-verse Krama-Śākta poem in praise of Kālī is itself framed as an eulogy/commentary on Siddhanātha's Kramastuti. Single author.
texts.note: Author Śrīvatsa; 'Kālidāsa' is an honorific epithet, not the classical kavi — care taken not to conflate. Krama Kashmir-Śaiva/Śākta work; current attribution stands. Intro by Arthur Avalon/Woodroffe is editorial, not authorial.

texts.title: Niṣpannayogāvali
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Author: Abhayakaragupta', ed. Meisezahl (Oriens 1976) for chap.1; Kano (Munimatālaṃkāra survey) lists the Niṣpannayogāvali among the ~21 securely attributed works of Abhayākaragupta (ca. 11th-12th c., active under Rāmapāla). Single-author iconographic maṇḍala compendium.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Kazuo Kano_A Survey of Passages from Rare Buddhist Works Found in the Munimatālaṃkāra.txt
texts.note: Securely Abhayākaragupta; no commentary/multi-author structure. Colophon ends 'iti mañjuvajramaṇḍalam'.

texts.title: Toḍalatantra
texts.basis: GRETIL header names only editor Gopinath Kaviraj (Tantrasaṃgraha vol.2) and digitizer Oliver Hellwig; no human author, no colophon naming a composer. As a Śākta āgama/tantra it is traditionally revelatory (Śiva-Śakti dialogue), not individually authored.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed tantra; traditional ascription is to divine revelation, no historical author. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Munimatālaṁkāra
texts.basis: Kano (dedicated survey paper) treats the Munimatālaṃkāra as one of Abhayākaragupta's own datable works (composed in the 30th year of Rāmapāla, ca. AD 1113); Abhayākaragupta cites it by title in his Āmnāyamañjarī and Marmakaumudī. Single author who embeds borrowed passages without attribution.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Kazuo Kano_A Survey of Passages from Rare Buddhist Works Found in the Munimatālaṃkāra.txt
texts.note: Securely Abhayākaragupta, dated colophon ca. 1113 CE (30th yr Rāmapāla). No printed complete ed.; partial ed. Li Xuezhu & Kano.

texts.title: Āryāsaptaśatī
texts.primary_author: Govardhana (Govardhanācārya); commentary Vyaṅgyārthadīpanā by Ananta Paṇḍita
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Author Govardhana (c.12th c.), āryā-meter collection. Govardhana was court poet of Sena king Lakṣmaṇasena (12th c.). The Kāvyamālā 1 edition cited bundles the commentary Vyaṅgyārthadīpanā of Ananta Paṇḍita (son of Bālopaṇḍita) — a distinct later commentator — alongside the root verses.
texts.note: Root saptaśatī securely by Govardhana; the named edition conflates a separate commentary (Vyaṅgyārthadīpanā) by Ananta Paṇḍita. Roles separated.

texts.title: Kriyāsaṃgrahapañjikā
texts.basis: Edition string is Tanemura's critical ed./tr. of Kuladatta's Kriyāsaṃgrahapañjikā (Groningen 2004). Title was blank in workitem; reference literature (Schopen, O'Neal) and web (Tanemura, Brill, Buswell/Brill Encyclopedia) consistently name Kuladatta as author of this Buddhist tantric ritual compendium (a.k.a. Kuladattapañjikā), ca. 11th-13th c., foundational for Newar Buddhism.
texts.note: current_author was empty/title blank; filled: Kuladatta. A pañjikā (ritual compendium); despite the name it functions as a single-authored compilation, no separate root attributed to another person.

texts.title: Somaśambhupaddhativyākhyā
texts.current_author: uncertain (commentary on the Somaśambhupaddhati of Somaśambhu)
texts.basis: Title is a vyākhyā on the Somaśambhupaddhati (Karmakāṇḍakramāvalī), whose mūla is by Somaśambhu (11th c.). Web (Sanderson/Cracow Indological Studies, Goodall via wisdomlib) identifies Trilocanaśiva — disciple of Aghoraśiva and Jñānaśambhu, 12th c. — as the author famous for his commentary on the Somaśambhupaddhati. Two distinct authors: root paddhati vs. vyākhyā.
texts.note: Was 'uncertain'. Mūla Somaśambhu; commentary attributed to Trilocanaśiva (12th c.). Vyākhyākāra identity medium-confidence; root/commentary roles separated.

texts.title: Matatrayaikyaprakāśikā
texts.basis: Muktabodha catalog (matatrayaikyaprakAzikA-E0009) lists the work; web confirms it is a Śaiva treatise arguing the unity of three doctrines (Śaiva/Vaiṣṇava/Vedānta), ascribed to Aiyyaṇācārya. No conflicting attribution found; single author, no separate commentary in this title.
texts.note: Single-author treatise on the unity of the three 'matas'; attribution to Aiyyaṇa(ācārya) traditional, not independently corroborated in scholarly secondary lit located.

texts.title: Varuṇapaddhativyākhyā
texts.current_author: commentary by Nigamajñānadeva (Nigamajñāna II); base text Varuṇapaddhati ascribed to a Varuṇaśiva/Varuṇa
texts.primary_author: Nigamajñāna II / Vedajñāna II (vyākhyā); base Varuṇapaddhati ascribed to Varuṇaśiva/Varuṇa
texts.basis: Edition (T. Ganesan 2009, IFP/EFEO, 'Two Śaiva Teachers of the Sixteenth Century: Nigamajñāna I and his disciple Nigamajñāna II') attributes the vyākhyā to Nigamajñāna II; skt-en-mono confirms Vedajñāna II = Nigamajñānadeśika, a 16th-c. Śaiva author of Sanskrit commentaries. Base Varuṇapaddhati is a separate (paddhati) text ascribed to Varuṇaśiva/Varuṇa.
texts.note: Title conflates base paddhati (Varuṇaśiva/Varuṇa) and its 16th-c. vyākhyā (Nigamajñāna II = Vedajñāna II). Commentary attribution high; base-text author uncertain/low.

texts.title: Śrīvajravārāhīsādhana
texts.current_author: anonymous/traditional (numerous Vajravārāhī sādhanas by various siddhas, e.g. Umāpatideva)
texts.basis: DSBC colophon: 'kṛtir iyaṃ paṇḍitamahopādhyāya-śrī-Umāpatideva-pādānām' = composed by Umāpatideva. Confirmed by E. English (Vajrayoginī, Wisdom 2002) and Bangdel (Tantra in Nepal), who identify this as the 12th-c. Vajravārāhī Sādhana of Umāpatideva. DSBC e-text input/proofed by Miroj Shakya 2014.
texts.note: Colophon names Umāpatideva; this specific sādhana is securely his (not anonymous). Current_author over-hedged as anonymous/various — this text is identified.

texts.title: Vyaktivivekavyākhyā
texts.primary_author: Rājānaka Ruyyaka (vyākhyā); base Vyaktiviveka by Rājānaka Mahimabhaṭṭa
texts.basis: GRETIL header: critical e-text (Hugo David 2018) based on Gaṇapati Śāstrī ed. (TSS 5, 1909) of the Vyaktiviveka of Mahimabhaṭṭa with commentary of Ruyyaka. Matilal & Evans (1986) and skt-en-mono confirm: root Vyaktiviveka by Mahimabhaṭṭa (late 11th c.), this vyākhyā by Rājānaka Ruyyaka, 'learned commentator of Mahimabhaṭṭa'.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Buddhist Logic and Epistemology...Matilal and Evans 1986.txt; secondary:cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt
texts.note: Title is the vyākhyā = Ruyyaka (correct), but it presupposes the root Vyaktiviveka by Mahimabhaṭṭa; flagged as conflating root author + commentator.

texts.title: Kalpadrumāvadānamālā
texts.basis: Nariman (Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism, 1919): the Kalpadrumāvadānamālā is an anonymous Buddhist avadāna 'garland' (mālā) opening from the last story of the Avadānaśataka; no named author — a compiled/collective Mahāyāna avadāna anthology.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism_Nariman G K_1919.txt
texts.note: Anonymous avadānamālā compilation; no traditional single author. Compiler unknown.

texts.title: Kāmikāgama: Pūrvabhāga
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Bhattacharyya, History of the Tantric Religion; Goudriaan, Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism) treats the Kāmikā(gama) as the foremost of the 28 Śaiva Siddhānta Āgamas — a revealed scripture with no human author; ed. C. Swaminatha Sivacarya 1975.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion_N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt; secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism_Goudriaan 1992.txt
texts.note: Revealed Śaiva Āgama (Pūrvabhāga of the Kāmikāgama); authorless by tradition (śivoktam). No human author to assign.

texts.title: Īśānaśivagurudeva: Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati, Volume 1
texts.basis: Edition (T. Ganapati Sastri, TSS) and secondary lit (wisdomlib; Sanderson 'Śaiva Age') treat the whole paddhati as a single-author Śaiva/temple compendium by Īśānaśiva, ca. 11th–12th c. This is Vol. 1 (Sāmānyapāda/Mantrapāda), one part of that single work.
texts.note: One author across all four volumes (Sāmānya/Mantra/Kriyā/Yoga pādas); no commentary-layer conflation. Author also styled Īśānaśivagurudevamiśra.

texts.title: Īśānaśivagurudeva: Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati, Volume 3
texts.basis: Same single-author work as Vols 1/2/4; this is Vol. III (Kriyāpāda), ed. T. Ganapati Sastri, TSS. Secondary lit (wisdomlib, Sanderson) consistently ascribes the paddhati to Īśānaśiva.
texts.note: Volume of one multi-part work; single author, no conflation.

texts.title: Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati, Volume 4
texts.basis: Vol. 4 of the paddhati, ed. T. Ganapati Sastri (TSS). Edition title 'of Īśānaśivagurudeva'. Single-author Śaiva manual; secondary lit agrees.
texts.note: Same author as Vols 1–3; current_author already gives the fuller name Īśānaśivagurudevamiśra.

texts.title: Īśānaśivagurudeva: Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati, Volume 2
texts.basis: Vol. II, ed. T. Ganapati Sastri (TSS No. LXXII). One part of the single-author paddhati by Īśānaśiva; secondary lit concurs.
texts.note: Volume of one multi-part work; single author, no conflation.

texts.title: Śabdabhedaprakāśa

authors.name: Maheśvara (the lexicographer, son of Brāhma; author of the Viśvaprakāśa)

texts.basis: Edition (Yashovijaya Jain Granthamala 1940) presents Maheśvara's root Śabdabhedaprakāśa WITH the ṭīkā of the Jain monk Jñānavimalagaṇi. Maheśvara is the lexicographer (son of Brāhma, author of the Viśvaprakāśa, c. 1111/12th c.); the Śabdabhedaprakāśa (= Śabdabhedanāmamālā) is his homonym/orthographic-variant glossary appended to the Viśvaprakāśa (skt-en-mono lexical sources; wisdomlib 'shabdabheda').
texts.note: Root by Maheśvara is secure; the named edition bundles a distinct author's commentary (ṭīkā) by Jñānavimalagaṇi — two distinct authors/roles, hence conflation_flag. Mūla attribution unchanged.

texts.title: Alaṅkārasarvasva
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Dvivedi ed., KSS 206) titles it 'Alaṃkārasarvasva of Śrī Rājānaka Ruyyaka & Maṅkha, with the Vimarśinī of Jayaratha'. T. Ganapati Sastri's TSS 40 ed. is titled 'Alankārasūtra (Ruyyaka), vṛtti on Alaṅkārasarvasva (Maṅkhuka), with vyākhyā of Samudrabandha' — splitting sūtra=Ruyyaka vs vṛtti=Maṅkhuka. Secondary lit (wisdomlib 'ruyyaka'; skt-en-mono cites the work both as 'of Mankhaka' and 'of Rajānaka Ruyyaka') records the standing dispute: some hold Ruyyaka wrote only the sūtras and Maṅkha (his pupil) the gloss; others credit Ruyyaka with both.
texts.note: Classic multi-author conflation: kārikā/sūtra by Ruyyaka; vṛtti by Maṅkha/Maṅkhuka (disputed — possibly Ruyyaka himself). Jayaratha's Vimarśinī and Samudrabandha's vyākhyā are later commentary layers. current_author 'Rājānaka Ruyyaka' alone omits the disputed vṛtti authorship.

texts.title: Yogaviṣaya
texts.basis: Muktabodha DL entry (no firm authorial claim). Web (Shiva Shakti 'Yogaviśaya of Mīnanāth', Wikipedia/Mallinson) and secondary lit: short Nātha haṭhayoga text traditionally ascribed to the legendary Matsyendra/Mīna under the pen-name Mīnanātha; scholars (Mallinson, White) hold the actual author unknown and the work likely late (perhaps 18th–19th c. recension).
texts.note: Ascription to Matsyendra/Mīna is legendary/pseudepigraphic, not secure. Genuine author unknown; status downgraded from a flat attribution to pseudepigraphic.

texts.title: Alaṅkārasarvasva
texts.primary_author: Rājānaka Ruyyaka (sūtra/kārikā; vṛtti disputed, possibly Maṅkha); Jayaratha (Vimarśinī commentary)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Ruyyaka: Alamkarasarvasva with Jayaratha's commentary Vimarsini' — this file embeds Jayaratha's Vimarśinī alongside Ruyyaka's text. Edition title (Dvivedi 1971) reads 'Ruyyaka & Maṅkha'. Web (wisdomlib Ruyyaka/Mankhaka): scholarly view that Ruyyaka wrote only the sūtras and the gloss/vṛtti may be by his pupil Maṅkha (disputed).
texts.note: Multi-author: Ruyyaka (sūtras), possible Maṅkha (vṛtti, disputed in literature), and Jayaratha (Vimarśinī ṭīkā included in this e-text). current_author named only Ruyyaka.

texts.title: Alaṅkārasarvasva
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Ruyyaka: Alamkarasarvasva (Sutras only!) with Vidyacakravartin's commentary Sanjivani'; edition Janaki 1965. The e-text gives Ruyyaka's sūtras embedded within Vidyācakravartin's Sañjīvanī commentary.
texts.note: Two distinct authors: Ruyyaka (base sūtras) and Vidyācakravartin (Sañjīvanī ṭīkā). current_author named only Ruyyaka.

texts.title: Rasārṇava: Paṭalas 1-12
texts.basis: GRETIL header names no author (Oliver Hellwig data entry; ed. P. C. Ray, Bibliotheca Indica 1908-10); text is a Bhairava–Devī dialogue frame, not authorial. Secondary (Dash PDF): 'the author's name of the Rasārṇava-tantra ... is not mentioned in the text.' No firm basis for Bhairavānanda.
texts.note: Confirmed anonymous Rasaśāstra/Tantra; Bhairavānanda ascription unsupported. Revealed-text dialogue frame (Bhairava/Devī).

texts.title: Tarkabhāṣāṭīkā
texts.current_author: Śrīmokṣākaragupta (text) / commentary by a later ṭīkākāra
texts.basis: GRETIL/DSBC header and colophon: 'mahāpaṇḍita bhikṣu-mokṣākaragupta-viracitāyāṃ tarkabhāṣāyāṃ' — the e-text is the base Tarkabhāṣā by Mokṣākaragupta (Buddhist logician, ~11th–12th c.), NOT a separate ṭīkā. Secondary (Nakamura, Ganeri, van der Kuijp) consistently attribute Tarkabhāṣā to Mokṣākaragupta.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey...Nakamura, 1980.txt | secondary:cleaned_Philosophy in Classical India...Ganeri, 2001.txt
texts.note: Title says 'Tarkabhāṣāṭīkā' but the actual e-text is the base Tarkabhāṣā by Mokṣākaragupta; no separate ṭīkākāra present in this source. (Distinct from Keśava Miśra's homonymous Tarkabhāṣā.) Removed the unsupported 'later ṭīkākāra'.

texts.title: Kulārṇavatantra
texts.basis: Edition (Tārānātha Vidyāratha ed., intro Avalon/Woodroffe, 1917) names only modern editor; the Tantra itself is a revealed Śiva–Śakti dialogue with no human author. Web (wisdomlib, Oxford Reference) and secondary (Tantra in Practice, White): Kaula scripture of unknown authorship, dated c. 1000–1400 CE.
texts.source: tradition | web:https://www.wisdomlib.org/definition/kularnavatantra | secondary:cleaned_Tantra in Practice_David Gordon White, ed, 2000_a.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Kaula Tantra. Tārānātha Vidyāratha = modern editor only (translator/editor role), not author. Revealed-text framing.

texts.title: Śuka-saptati
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet input) names no author; opens 'śukasaptatiḥ' with a vinodinī Skt-Hindi vyākhyā but gives no composer. Secondary lit (Mylius Geschichte; Bendall catalogue; Schmidt eds.) treats the work as an anonymous frame-story collection transmitted in textus simplicior and textus ornatior recensions, both ed. Richard Schmidt; no individual author is assigned. The ornatior is a later literary reworking.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Geschichte der altindischen Literatur_Klaus Mylius_2003 (1983).txt; secondary:cleaned_Bendall_Catalogue...British museum.txt
texts.note: Anonymous frame-tale anthology; two recensions (textus simplicior, textus ornatior) both ed. Schmidt 1893/1899. No traditional named author; ornatior is a distinct later redaction. Current 'anonymous/traditional' confirmed.

texts.title: Prāyaścittasamuccaya
texts.current_author: Trilocanaśiva (a similar, slightly earlier work of the same name is by Hṛdayaśiva)
texts.basis: Sathyanarayanan/Goodall 2015 (Collection Indologie 127) edit Trilocanaśiva's 12th-c. South Indian Prāyaścittasamuccaya, securely attributed; the volume also transcribes a SEPARATE, earlier Northern (Malwa) homonymous work by Hṛdayaśiva. These are two distinct works sharing a title, NOT one title conflating authors. Local lit confirms Trilocanaśiva as a Śaiva-siddhānta author (Siddhāntasamuccaya cited alongside Aghoraśiva).
texts.note: Edited text = Trilocanaśiva (12th c., South India). Hṛdayaśiva's same-named work is an independent earlier text, not part of this composition; hence no intra-title conflation. Attribution secure per IFP/EFEO edition.

texts.title: Mātṛkācakraviveka
texts.basis: Local lit (skt-en-mono / Maharthamanjari French ed. bibliography) lists 'Mātṛkācakraviveka by Svatantrānandanātha, with Sanskrit bhāṣya by Śivānanda and Hindi translation by Kṛṣṇānanda Budhauliya'. The KSTS edition cited transmits the root verses by Svatantrānandanātha. A separate explanatory bhāṣya by Śivānanda accompanies it in printed editions but is a later commentary, not the root work.
texts.note: Root Mātṛkācakraviveka securely by Svatantrānandanātha (Śākta-Trika). A distinct Sanskrit bhāṣya by Śivānanda is transmitted with it; root attribution unchanged. Not flagged as conflation since the item targets the root text and roles are cleanly separable.

texts.title: Rājataraṅginī
texts.basis: GRETIL e-text (Slaje ed., Halle 2014) explicitly marks two authorial layers: Jonarāja's genuine Rājataraṅginī (continuation of Kalhaṇa, to Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn's reign) and interpolated/continuation passages tagged '[Ps-JRT]' (Pseudo-Jonarāja), beginning at JRt_B.47. Slaje's edition is built precisely on distinguishing these. The single GRETIL title thus conflates the genuine author and an anonymous later continuator.
texts.note: This is Jonarāja's RT (second of the four Kashmiri chronicles), NOT Kalhaṇa's. Genuine Jonarāja verses + anonymous Pseudo-Jonarāja additions ([Ps-JRT]) marked in the Slaje edition. Two-layer text -> conflation_flag true. Current author already notes both.

texts.title: Ādikarmapradīpa [Ādikarmapradīpa Bodhicaryāvatāraṭīkā]
texts.basis: La Vallée Poussin's 1898 'Bouddhisme, études et matériaux' is a composite volume: the Ādikarmapradīpa (a tantric ādikarman manual ascribed to Anupamavajra) is printed together with a Bodhicaryāvatāraṭīkā material relating to Śāntideva's Bodhicaryāvatāra. Internet Archive catalogues the volume under 'Anupamavajra, Śāntideva'. The Ādikarmapradīpa attribution to Anupamavajra is an editorial/colophon ascription (not firmly secure); the bundled BCA-ṭīkā belongs to the Śāntideva commentarial tradition, a different author-layer.
texts.note: Composite title: Ādikarmapradīpa (ascr. Anupamavajra, attribution not firmly secured) + Bodhicaryāvatāraṭīkā tied to Śāntideva's BCA. Two distinct text-layers/authors under one bundled title -> conflation_flag true; status disputed for the Anupamavajra ascription. Differs from current single-author framing.

texts.title: Caurapañcāśikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Tadpatrikar ed.) names Bilhaṇa but the text carries no internal authorial colophon. Secondary lit (skt-en-mono: 'Caurapañcāśikā, ascribed to Bilhaṇa') and web (Wikipedia; Miller, Phantasies of a Love-Thief 1971; Britannica) show the work survives in multiple divergent recensions (Northern/Kashmiri = Caurīsuratapañcāśikā ed. Solf 1886; Western-Southern with happy ending) and that some manuscripts name the poet as 'Caura' or 'Sundara' rather than Bilhaṇa. The Bilhaṇa attribution is traditional, not text-internally secured.
texts.note: Single-author lyric but attribution only traditional/legendary: ascribed to Bilhaṇa (11th c.), variant ascriptions to 'Caura'/'Sundara'; multiple recensions with no autograph colophon. Status downgraded secure->traditional; no multi-author conflation (recensional variation, not distinct authors in one text).

texts.title: Śāradātilaka
texts.basis: Edition (Tantrik Texts) names Lakṣmaṇadeśikendra; Rāghavabhaṭṭa wrote only the Padārthādarśa commentary, not the root text. Sanderson (Śaiva Age) and Alper (Understanding Mantras) name the Śāradātilaka of Lakṣmaṇadeśika. The commentary is a separate work, so the root tantra is single-authored.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_The Śaiva Age.txt; secondary:cleaned_Understanding Mantras_Harvey P. Alper, 1989.txt
texts.note: Root Śāradātilakatantra by Lakṣmaṇadeśika; Rāghavabhaṭṭa's Padārthādarśa is a distinct commentary (not conflated into this record).

texts.title: Sahṛdayalīlā
texts.basis: GRETIL header and repeated colophons read 'iti śrīrājānakaruyyakaviracitāyāṃ sahṛdayalīlāyāṃ' (composed by Rājānaka Ruyyaka), after each of the four sections; final 'samāpteyaṃ sahṛdayalīlā'.
texts.note: Internal colophon names Rājānaka Ruyyaka as composer; attribution secure.

texts.title: Ardhanārīśvarastotra
texts.basis: Slaje (ZDMG 165, 2015) edited the hymn as Kalhaṇa's, connecting it to the Rājataraṅgiṇī tradition. GRETIL header reflects this scholarly attribution, but no independent self-naming colophon is present; the current_author already flags it as 'attributed'.
texts.note: Ascription to the Rājataraṅgiṇī author Kalhaṇa rests on Slaje's scholarship; no contemporary colophon, so traditional/attributed rather than fully secure.

texts.title: Jñānaśambhu: Śivapūjāstava
texts.basis: GRETIL header: edition titled 'Śivapūjāstavaḥ ... Jñānaśambhuśivācāryapraṇītaḥ'; closing verse (JsSpst_47) names 'jñānaśambhus' as composer ('siddhāntajñas jñānaśambhus śivoktyā cakre...'). Saiddhāntika author.
texts.note: Self-naming final verse confirms Jñānaśambhu (Jñānaśiva); attribution secure.

texts.title: Āmaraughaśāsana
texts.basis: GRETIL/KSTS edition presents it as 'of Gorakṣanātha'. Jacobsen (Yoga Powers, 2012) treats it as an early Nātha haṭhayoga text likely predating the Haṭhapradīpikā; the corpus of works ascribed to the legendary Nātha founder Gorakṣanātha is conventionally attributed rather than securely his. current_author already marks it 'attributed'.
texts.note: Ascribed to the legendary founder Gorakṣanātha; Nātha pseudo/traditional authorship, not securely a single historical author — kept as traditional.

texts.title: Siddhāntārthasamuccaya
texts.basis: Secondary literature (skt-en-mono / Goodall-circle apparatus) cites 'Trilocana's Siddhāntārthasamuccaya'; Sanderson (Śaiva Age) identifies Trilocanaśiva as a 12th-c. South-Indian Saiddhāntika preceptor. The current 'anonymous/traditional' tag is too weak.
texts.note: Resolved from anonymous/traditional to Trilocanaśiva (12th c., South-Indian Śaiva Siddhānta), per scholarly citations.

texts.title: Mahotsavavidhikrama (Mahotsavavidhi)
texts.primary_author: Aghoraśiva (genuine prose core); + later compilers/interpolators (āgama-saṅgraha, Kacchapeśvara verses)
texts.basis: Davis 2010 translates it as Aghoraśiva's Mahotsavavidhi (6th bhāga of Kriyākramadyotikā). But Goodall (Kiraṇavṛtti ed., skt-en-mono.txt p.xiii-xiv n.24) shows the published Mahotsavavidhi volume 'contains much else that is manifestly not by the hand of Aghorasiva and nothing that can be proved to be his': only the first two prose sections' colophons name him (pp.52,61); thereafter appear Kacchapeśvara's commentary maṅgala verses, a late agamavacanasangraha citing authorities Aghoraśiva never cites (Kāmika, Vīra, Kāraṇa, Suprabheda etc.), and copied tantra-colophons. Bendall catalogue: 'forming part 6 of the Kriyākramadyotikā'.
texts.note: Title nominally Aghoraśiva's but the transmitted/printed text is a muddle conflating his genuine ātmārthapūjā prose with later interpolated compilations and Kacchapeśvara's verses; only first two prose sections securely his.

texts.title: Aghoraśivācārya: Mṛgendravṛttidīpikā
texts.basis: Muktabodha header: 'Author: aghoraśivācārya'; text self-describes as 'bhaṭṭanārāyaṇakaṇṭhaviracita mṛgendravṛttyāḥ vyākhyā' — i.e. a Dīpikā (sub-commentary) on Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha's Vṛtti on the Mṛgendrāgama. N.R.Bhatt ed. (IFI 23, 1962) titles it Dīpikā of Aghoraśivācārya. Secondary lit (Sinister Yogis; Lit. on Phil. Systems) confirms Aghoraśiva's Dīpikā on Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha's vṛtti.
texts.source: gretil-header (muktalib7 M00255); secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems...txt; secondary:cleaned_Sinister Yogis...txt
texts.note: The item is specifically the Dīpikā, securely Aghoraśiva's. The work is layered: root Mṛgendrāgama (anonymous scripture) -> Vṛtti by Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha -> Dīpikā by Aghoraśiva. Other authors noted for completeness; the Dīpikā layer is single-authored.

texts.title: Sarvajñānottaravṛtti
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text (no source_url given) from IFP/OCHS mss; described as Aghoraśivācārya's vṛtti on the Sarvajñānottara-āgama. No published critical edition located; attribution rests on manuscript/catalogue tradition. Aghoraśiva is well attested as a 12th-c. Śaiva Siddhānta commentator (fl. 1157 CE per his Kriyākramadyotikā colophon).
texts.note: Vṛtti layer ascribed to Aghoraśiva on the anonymous scriptural Sarvajñānottara-āgama; attribution traditional (no critical edition to confirm), not securely verified. Root āgama is anonymous/revealed.

texts.title: Kriyākramadyotikā
texts.primary_author: Aghoraśiva (Aghoraśivācārya), with later accretions in transmission

authors.name: anonymous (later interpolators / compilers of suspect bhāgas)

texts.basis: The Kriyākramadyotikā is the core ritual paddhati securely by Aghoraśiva (1157 CE; his Gotrasantati colophon names guru Sadāśiva of Kāñcī and dates the lineage — Goodall, Kiraṇavṛtti ed. p.xiii). However Goodall (skt-en-mono.txt p.xiii-xiv n.24) notes 'various other texts belonging to parārthapūjā have been published as parts of Aghorasiva's Kriyakramadyotikā... all are suspect' (e.g. Prāyaścittavidhi opening with Aṃśumattantra material; Mahotsavavidhi muddle). Bendall lists Mahotsava/Prāyaścitta vidhis as bhāgas 6-7. Commentaries by Nirmalamaṇi (Prabhā) and Kacchapeśvara exist.
texts.note: Core work securely Aghoraśiva's, but the printed Kriyākramadyotikā as transmitted conflates his genuine ātmārthapūjā sections with suspect/interpolated parārthapūjā bhāgas (Mahotsava-, Prāyaścitta-vidhi) not provably his — hence conflation_flag true while primary attribution stays secure.

texts.title: Ṣaḍaṅgayoga (with Guṇabharaṇī commentary)
texts.basis: Sferra 2000 (Serie Orientale Roma LXXXV) edition title: 'The Ṣaḍaṅgayoga by Anupamarakṣita, with Raviśrījñāna's Guṇabharaṇīnāmaṣaḍaṅgayogaṭippaṇī.' Secondary: PIATS-06 (Fagernes 1992) — Sādhuputra was a disciple of Anupamarakṣita; the Guṇabharaṇī is 'by Raviśrījñāna'. Davidson (Pure realms) — Raviśrījñāna author of the Kālacakra-based Amṛtakaṇikā commentary on the Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti. Two distinct Kālacakra authors.
texts.source: tradition (Sferra 2000 edition); secondary:cleaned_PIATS-06-Fagernes-1992-v2.txt; secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Pure realms and hidden abodes.txt; web:wisdomlib
texts.note: current_author empty. One title bundles two distinct authors: root Ṣaḍaṅgayoga (six-limbed Kālacakra yoga) by Anupamarakṣita; commentary Guṇabharaṇī(-ṭippaṇī) by the later Raviśrījñāna. Separate roles recorded.

texts.title: Khasarpaṇasādhana
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Sādhanamālā compilation); traditionally ascribed in Tibetan sources to Atiśa Dīpaṅkaraśrījñāna
texts.basis: Ed. in Bhattacharyya, Sādhanamālā vol.I (GOS 26, 1925), a compiled anthology (~c.1165) of sādhanas of mixed/often anonymous authorship; the shorter Khasarpaṇa (Avalokiteśvara) sādhanas in the SM generally lack an author colophon. A 'venerable Khasarpaṇasādhana' is listed among Atiśa Dīpaṅkaraśrījñāna's works in the Tibetan biography (Apple, Jewels of the Middle Way 2019) — a traditional ascription, not necessarily the SM text. WebSearch confirms Khasarpaṇa is one of ~37 Avalokiteśvara sādhanas in the SM.
texts.source: gretil (Sādhanamālā, Bhattacharyya ed.); secondary:cleaned_Jewels of the Middle Way..._Apple_2019.txt; web:wisdomlib.org/definition/khasarpana
texts.note: Anonymous within the Sādhanamālā compilation; no author colophon for this sādhana. Traditional Tibetan ascription of a Khasarpaṇasādhana to Atiśa Dīpaṅkaraśrījñāna noted separately, not confirmed for this exact text. Compiler/redactor of SM collection itself unidentified.

texts.title: jñānaratnāvalī
texts.basis: Sanderson, The Śaiva Age, explicitly names 'the Jñānaratnāvalī of Jñānaśiva' as a major later Śaivasiddhānta paddhati alongside Aghoraśiva's Kriyākramadyotikā; the Saiddhāntika guru-lineage verses (Somaśambhu > ... > Jñānaśambhu / Jñānaśiva) confirm Jñānaśiva and Jñānaśambhu are one and the same person. Muktabodha e-text edition attributes it to Jñānaśiva.
texts.note: Jñānaśiva = Jñānaśambhu (single Saiddhāntika author, ritual paddhati); name doublet not two persons.

texts.title: Pavanadūta
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Chakravarti ed. 1926) gives 'Pavanadutam of Dhoyi'; closing colophon in the e-text reads 'śrīdhoyīkavirājaviracitaṃ pavanadūtākhyaṃ kāvyaṃ samāptam' — the kāvya called Pavanadūta composed by the illustrious Dhoyī. 12th-c. Sena-court poet.
texts.note: Colophon self-attribution to Dhoyī (Dhoyīkavirāja); single-author messenger kāvya.

texts.title: Manthānabhairava Tantra
texts.basis: Manthānabhairavatantra is a large anonymous Kubjikā-school (Paścimāmnāya) tantra of the Kālīkula/Kubjikā corpus, transmitted as divine revelation (Bhairava–Goddess dialogue), not ascribed to a human author. Dyczkowski (2009) edited & translated the Kumārikākhaṇḍa but is editor/translator, not author. Secondary lit (Goudriaan ed.; Encyclopedia of Tantra) treats the Kubjikā tantras as anonymous scriptures.
texts.source: secondary:Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism (Goudriaan ed.).txt
texts.note: Traditional ascription to revelation (Bhairava); human composer unknown. Dyczkowski = ed./tr. only, not author.

texts.title: Karaṇāgama
texts.basis: Kāraṇāgama is one of the 28 Śaivasiddhānta Mūlāgamas, an anonymous revealed scripture (treated as taught by Śiva). Secondary lit references it as a scripture (N.N. Bhattacharyya, History of the Tantric Religion p.62; Davis cites its etymological definitions; Bendall catalogues a Pūrva-/Uttara-kāraṇa edition; Lefèvre cites a 1921 Madras ed.). No human author given.
texts.source: secondary:History of the Tantric Religion (N.N. Bhattacharyya).txt; Bendall Catalogue.txt
texts.note: Revealed Āgama with Pūrvakāraṇa + Uttarakāraṇa divisions; ascribed to Śiva, no human author.

texts.title: Gorakṣaguṭikā
texts.primary_author: ascribed to Gorakṣanātha (legendary; actual author unknown)
texts.basis: A minor Nātha haṭha/alchemical work ascribed to Gorakṣanātha. Web/scholarly consensus: Gorakṣanātha (Gorakhnāth) is a semi-legendary ascetic to whom a large floating corpus of later Nātha works is attributed; his historical existence and authorship are doubtful (cf. Gorakhbāṇī believed composed ~17th c. yet ascribed to a much earlier semi-legendary guru). Gorakṣaguṭikā is not in the standard Butalia list of works ascribed to him, underscoring the loose ascription.
texts.note: Pseudepigraphic: ascribed to legendary founder-figure Gorakṣanātha, not a securely identified historical author; date later than the namesake.

texts.title: Mokṣopāya-Ṭīkā (2. Mumukṣuvyavahāra-Prakaraṇa) — commentary on the Mokṣopāya
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Slaje, Bhāskarakaṇṭhas Mokṣopāya-Ṭīkā, Graz 1993; input by W. Slaje) presents the Mokṣopāya WITH Bhāskarakaṇṭha's ṭīkā; the commentary's own verse 'Bhāskarakaṇṭho racayati ... vyākhyām' names the commentator. The root Mokṣopāya (Kashmirian recension underlying the Yogavāsiṣṭha) is itself an anonymous work (Mokṣopāya Project / Hanneder & Slaje). Bhāskarakaṇṭha = 18th-c. Kashmiri scholar (also commented on Abhinavagupta's Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī, per Goudriaan ed.).
texts.note: Two distinct hands: anonymous Mokṣopāya mūla (verse/scripture) + Bhāskarakaṇṭha's prose ṭīkā (Mumukṣuvyavahāra-prakaraṇa). Current_author blank; resolved both roles.

texts.title: Matsyendranātha: Akulavīratantra
texts.primary_author: attributed to Matsyendranātha (Mīnanātha); textually anonymous revealed scripture
texts.basis: Bagchi ed. (Kaulajñānanirṇaya and Some Minor Texts of the School of Matsyendranātha) groups it with Matsyendra's school. Saddhatissa vol. lists Tantric works 'attributed to Matsyendranātha'; Urban and White report it is 'said to have been revealed to Matsyendranātha'. Mallinson, Sanderson, White note many such works are posthumous/legendary ascriptions (revealed Kaula scripture, not a personal composition).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Power of Tantra...HUGH B. URBAN, 2010.txt; secondary:cleaned_Buddhist Studies in Honour of Hammalava Saddhatissa...txt; web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matsyendra
texts.note: Revealed Kaula tantra of the Matsyendra/Yoginīkaula school; ascription to Matsyendranātha is traditional/legendary, likely posthumous. MS dates range 9th-13th c. Single traditional ascription, no multi-author structure.

texts.title: Lakṣmītantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Pāñcarātra Āgama; framed as divine revelation)
texts.basis: Krishnamacharya ed. (Adyar) and Gupta trans. (Brill) treat it as an anonymous Pāñcarātra scripture. Kinsley and Olson call it a popular Pāñcarātra text with no named author. Sanderson (cited in skt-en-mono) dates it (with Ahirbudhnyasaṃhitā) to South India 12th-13th c. on Taittirīya-recension mantra evidence; older view 9th-11th c.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt; secondary:cleaned_Tantric Visions of the Divine Feminine...DAVID KINSLEY, 1998.txt; web:https://www.wisdomlib.org/definition/lakshmitantra
texts.note: Anonymous revealed scripture (narrative frame: Śrī/Nārada/Indra). No personal author claimed; tradition treats it as āgama. Date disputed (9th-13th c.).

texts.title: Ekākṣarakośa
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Hellwig, from R. K. Rai ed.) names author Puruṣottamadeva. Secondary lit (Joshi, Mylius, Smith, Saddhatissa vol.) consistently identifies Puruṣottamadeva, the Buddhist lexicographer of the Amarakośa/Trikāṇḍaśeṣa tradition, c. 12th century (often the same as the author of the Bhāṣāvṛtti).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Studies in the Buddhistic Culture of India_Lal Mani Josh.txt; secondary:cleaned_Smith_Among Tibetan Texts.txt
texts.note: Single-author lexicon (kośa) of one-syllable words by Puruṣottamadeva, 12th c. Bengal. Secure and unanimous; matches current_author.

texts.title: Kālacakrabhagavatsādhanavidhi
texts.basis: Title was empty; edition note states the slug encodes author/title/journal/year, giving author Dharmākaraśānti, title Kālacakrabhagavatsādhanavidhi, published in Dhīḥ: Journal of Rare Buddhist Texts (Sarnath/CIHTS), 1997. Web confirms Dharmākaraśānti as the author of the Kālacakrabhagavatsādhanavidhiḥ edited in Dhīḥ. He is a known Buddhist tantric (Kālacakra) author.
texts.note: Resolved from empty fields: author Dharmākaraśānti, a single-author Kālacakra sādhana-vidhi. Confidence medium pending direct edition verification; no conflation.

texts.title: Lakṣmītantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Pāñcarātra Āgama; framed as divine revelation)
texts.basis: Same work as SA_MB_laksmiitantramHK (Krishnamacharya Adyar ed.). Treated as anonymous Pāñcarātra revealed scripture in all secondary literature (Kinsley, Olson, Gupta trans.); dated South India 12th-13th c. by Sanderson.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt; secondary:cleaned_Book of the Goddess...Carl Olson, 2002.txt; web:https://www.wisdomlib.org/definition/lakshmitantra
texts.note: Duplicate record of the Lakṣmītantra (different e-text/library copy). Anonymous āgama, no named author.

texts.title: Karatoyāmāhātmya
texts.primary_author: anonymous; colophon ascribes composition to Paraśurāma (legendary)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Arlo Griffiths, from P. C. Sen 1929) presents it as part of the (lost) Uttara-Pauṇḍra-khaṇḍa. Colophon: 'ity uttarapauṇḍrakhaṇḍe sūtaśaunakasaṃvāde paraśurāmaviracitaṃ karatoyāmāhātmyaṃ samāptam' — ascribing it to the mythic sage Paraśurāma. A māhātmya (regional pilgrimage glorification of the Karatoyā river) of the Purāṇic type; actual author unknown.
texts.note: Colophon pseudepigraphically credits Paraśurāma; genuine author anonymous. Purāṇic māhātmya embedded in lost Uttara-Pauṇḍra-khaṇḍa. Noted traditional ascription separately from anonymous real author.

texts.title: Mokṣopāya-Ṭīkā (4. Sthiti-Prakaraṇa fragments)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Mokṣopāya (=Yogavāsiṣṭha) root traditionally ascribed to Vālmīki, taught by Vasiṣṭha to Rāma; the Ṭīkā (the actual edited text, Slaje 2002) is by Bhāskarakaṇṭha, grandson of Vaiḍūryakaṇṭha, son of Avatārakaṇṭha (18th-c. Kashmir). Potter Encyclopedia & Dan Martin bibliography confirm Bhāskarakaṇṭha as commentator (also Bhāskarī on Abhinavagupta, Cittānubodhaśāstra).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 1 - Potter.txt; secondary:cleaned_Bibliography_tib_skt_philology_Dan Martin, 2006.txt
texts.note: Edition is the commentary (ṭīkā) only; root verses by a different (legendary) author. Slaje vol. covers 4th (Sthiti) Prakaraṇa fragments.

texts.title: Ṣaḍaṅgayoga with Guṇabharaṇī-ṭippaṇī
texts.basis: Edition title (Sferra 2000) explicitly: 'The Ṣaḍaṅgayoga by Anupamarakṣita with Raviśrījñāna's Guṇabharaṇīnāmaṣaḍaṅgayogaṭippaṇī'. Secondary lit (PIATS-06 Fagernes 1992; Davidson) confirms Raviśrījñāna as author of the Guṇabharaṇī and of the Kālacakra-based Amṛtakaṇikā; Anupamarakṣita is the root-text author on the six-limbed yoga.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_PIATS-06-Fagernes-1992-v2.txt; secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Pure realms and hidden abodes.txt; edition-title
texts.note: Two distinct authors: root by Anupamarakṣita, commentary (ṭippaṇī) Guṇabharaṇī by Raviśrījñāna. Kālacakra/ṣaḍaṅgayoga tradition.

texts.title: Āryamañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti with Amṛtakaṇikā-ṭippaṇī and Amṛtakaṇikoddyota-nibandha
texts.basis: Edition (Banarsi Lal 1994): Āryamañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti (root tantric text, traditionally revealed/anonymous) + Amṛtakaṇikā-ṭippaṇī by Bhikṣu Raviśrījñāna + Amṛtakaṇikoddyota-nibandha by Vibhūticandra. Secondary: Davidson & PIATS-10 (Heller/Orofino) confirm Raviśrījñāna authored the Amṛtakaṇikā commentary on the Nāmasaṃgīti; Hatchell, Nakamura confirm Mahāpaṇḍita Vibhūticandra (active early 13th c., Nepal/Tibet).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Davidson_Pure realms and hidden abodes.txt; secondary:cleaned_PIATS-10_Heller and Orofino.txt; secondary:cleaned_Hatchell_Naked Seeing_diss.txt; edition-title
texts.note: Nibandha (sub-commentary) by Vibhūticandra builds on Raviśrījñāna's Amṛtakaṇikā ṭippaṇī, both on the root Nāmasaṃgīti. Three distinct textual layers/authors.

texts.title: Makuṭāgama
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Śaivāgama; traditionally ascribed to Śiva)
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib): Makuṭāgama is one of the 28 Siddhāntāgamas of the Śaivasiddhānta, traditionally proclaimed by the Tatpuruṣa face of Śiva; no human author. IFP/Muktabodha e-text from Grantha palm-leaf mss; no critical edition with named author. No secondary-lit hit naming a human author.
texts.note: Revealed scripture (āgama); traditional ascription to Śiva, not a historical person. Current 'anonymous/traditional' is correct.

texts.title: Tattvaratnāvalī
texts.basis: Web confirms a Śaiva Tattvaratnāvalī by Vimalaśiva surviving in a fragmentary Nāgarī palm-leaf ms (NAK 1-1697 7/6, NGMPP B 26/16); collection MB (Muktabodha, Śaiva). DISTINCT from the well-known Buddhist Tattvaratnāvalī by Advayavajra/Maitrīpa (Snellgrove, Steinkellner Festschrift, Nakamura) — NOT to be conflated. Local lit only discusses the Buddhist homonym.
texts.note: Śaiva work by Vimalaśiva; carefully distinguished from Advayavajra/Maitrīpa's Buddhist Tattvaratnāvalī (same title, different author). Single author.

texts.title: Vajravilāsinīstotra
texts.basis: DSBC e-text header and colophon explicitly attribute it: 'mahāpaṇḍitavibhūticandrapādakṛtam' and colophon 'kṛtir iyaṃ mahāpaṇḍitavibhūticandrapādānām' — the work is by Mahāpaṇḍita Vibhūticandra. Vibhūticandra is a well-attested early-13th-c. author (Hatchell, Davidson, Nakamura).
texts.note: Colophon names Vibhūticandra; current 'anonymous/traditional' should be updated to secure attribution to Vibhūticandra.

texts.title: Tattvacintāmaṇi
texts.basis: GRETIL header: author Gaṅgeśa, Śabdakhaṇḍa, Bibliotheca Indica ed. Tarkavāgīśa. Secondary lit (Matilal, Ganeri, Bilimoria) uniformly attributes the Tattvacintāmaṇi to Gaṅgeśa (Gaṅgeśopādhyāya), c. AD 1300, founder of Navya-Nyāya. This e-text is the mūla only (no commentary).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Mind, language, and world. Collected essays of Bimal Krishna Matilal. 1_Matilal, Bimal Krishna, Ganeri, Jonardon, 2002.txt
texts.note: Secure single-author mūla. The famous commentaries (Mathurānātha's Rahasya, Rucidatta, Rāmakṛṣṇa, Jayadeva Pakṣadhara's Āloka) are by other authors but are NOT included in this e-text, so no conflation here.

texts.title: Haṃsa Upaniṣad
texts.basis: One of the minor Yoga Upaniṣads; no historical author, transmitted as śruti (traditionally affiliated with the Śukla-Yajurveda). The edition used (Adyar Library, ed. Mahadeva Sastri) is 'The Yoga Upaniṣads WITH THE COMMENTARY of Śrī Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin' — so the printed source bundles an anonymous root text with a distinct named commentator.
texts.note: Root text genuinely anonymous; conflation flag set because the cited edition pairs the anonymous Upaniṣad with the commentary of Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (18th c.), a separate author/role.

texts.title: Devīgītā [Devībhāgavata-Purāṇa]
texts.basis: Devīgītā = Devībhāgavata-Purāṇa 7.31-40, an embedded gītā within a Śākta purāṇa. Purāṇas are conventionally ascribed to Vyāsa but are anonymous, multi-layered compilations; the Devībhāgavata is a late (c. 11th-14th c.) Śākta text. No individual author.
texts.note: Traditional/pseudepigraphic ascription to Vyāsa as purāṇic redactor; actual authorship anonymous. Not a multi-author conflation within this excerpt.

texts.title: Subodhālaṅkāra
texts.basis: DSBC e-text colophon: 'iti saṃgharakkhita mahāsāmiviracite subodhālaṃkāre' — composed by Saṅgharakkhita. Web (SOAS/Jaini scholarship): Pali poetics work by the Sinhalese monk Saṅgharakkhita, late 12th c., modelled on Daṇḍin's Kāvyādarśa; comprises kārikā + (auto-)vṛtti, with later separate Poraṇa-ṭīkā and Abhinava-ṭīkā.
texts.note: Base text (kārikā + vṛtti) by Saṅgharakkhita per colophon. The Poraṇa-ṭīkā and Abhinava-ṭīkā are later separate commentaries but are NOT in this DSBC e-text, so no conflation. (Note: this Pali Saṅgharakkhita is unrelated to the modern Westerner of the same name appearing in some reference files.)

texts.title: Kathāsaritsāgara
texts.basis: GRETIL header + colophon (cf. White: 'mahākavi Śrī Somadeva...viracitaḥ'): Somadevabhaṭṭa, Kashmiri court poet, composed it c. 1063-81 CE for queen Sūryavatī. Secondary lit (Thapar, Sutherland) consistently names Somadeva. The work is Somadeva's Sanskrit recasting of Guṇāḍhya's lost Paiśācī Bṛhatkathā.
texts.source: gretil-header(colophon); secondary:cleaned_Kiss of the Yogini. Tantric Sex in its South Asian Contexts_David Gordon White, 2003.txt
texts.note: Somadeva is the genuine author/poet of this work; Guṇāḍhya listed as ultimate source-author of the underlying lost Bṛhatkathā (medium confidence, not a true co-author of this text), hence conflation_flag left false.

texts.title: Rasendracūḍāmaṇi
texts.primary_author: Somadeva (the alchemist; NOT the Kathāsaritsāgara poet)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Rasendracūḍāmaṇi of Somadeva, ed. Siddhinandan Misra, Chaukhamba 1999, input O. Hellwig. Web (wisdomlib): a rasaśāstra/alchemy treatise by Somadeva, dated c. 12th-13th c. (some sources 10th-12th). This Somadeva is a distinct person from the 11th-c. Kashmiri poet Somadeva of the Kathāsaritsāgara.
texts.note: Author name unchanged, but DISAMBIGUATION: the alchemist Somadeva is NOT the same individual as Somadeva Bhaṭṭa (Kathāsaritsāgara). Two homonymous authors; this is the rasaśāstra Somadeva. Not a within-title conflation.

texts.title: Gorakṣaśataka
texts.primary_author: traditionally Gorakṣanātha (retroactive Nātha ascription; compilation, orig. = Vivekamārtanda)
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Briggs 1938): colophon 'iti gorakṣaśatakaṃ sampūrṇam' ascribes to Gorakṣa, but an anonymous note flags many verses drawn from Haṭhayogapradīpikā and an incomplete compilation. Mallinson (Khecarīvidyā, 2003): Nātha association 'almost certainly a retroactive attribution'; text earlier known as Vivekamārtanda, one MS colophon reads 'śrīgorakṣadevaviracito vivekamārttaṇḍaḥ'.
texts.note: Ascription to Gorakṣanātha is retroactive/traditional, not secure; this 'Briggs' recension is a compilation overlapping HYP. Earlier title Vivekamārtanda (also ascribed to Viśvarūpadeva in Trivandrum ed.).

texts.title: Gorakṣayogaśāstram
texts.primary_author: traditionally Gorakṣanātha (title is an alias of the Gorakṣaśataka/Vivekamārtanda text family)
texts.basis: Bouy 1994 (cited in Mallinson 2003) lists 'Gorakṣayogaśāstra' among the alternate names of the single text otherwise called Gorakṣaśataka / Gorakṣapaddhati / Vivekamārtanda etc.; same retroactive Nātha attribution to Gorakṣanātha. Muktabodha source URL returned 404, so header/colophon not directly verified.
texts.note: Title is one of the names of the Gorakṣaśataka/Vivekamārtanda corpus; attribution to Gorakṣanātha is traditional/disputed, not secure. Source e-text URL 404 (could not confirm colophon).

texts.title: Kriyākramadyotikāvyākhyā
texts.primary_author: Kacchapeśvaraśiva (vyākhyā); on the Kriyākramadyotikā of Aghoraśiva (mūla)
texts.basis: Sanderson, The Śaiva Age: cites 'Kacchapeśvaraśivācārya, Kriyākramadyotikāvyākhyā' (IFP MS Transcript 109) — a commentary on the Kriyākramadyotikā of Aghoraśiva (completed 1157 CE). Goudriaan ed. vol. confirms Kriyākramadyotikā (=Aghoraśivācāryapaddhati) of Aghoraśiva, with separate later commentary tradition (Nirmalamani's Prabhā). Muktabodha source URL returned 404.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_The Śaiva Age.txt; secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism..._Goudriaan, ed, 1992.txt
texts.note: Title bundles two distinct authors: mūla Kriyākramadyotikā by Aghoraśiva (1157 CE), the vyākhyā by Kacchapeśvaraśiva. current_author named only the commentator; flagged conflated. Source e-text URL 404.

texts.title: Candrāloka
texts.primary_author: Jayadeva (alias Pīyūṣavarṣa), son of Mahādeva and Sumitrā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Beyaert/Filliozat): colophon names 'sukavijayadevena racite', author Jayadeva alias Pīyūṣavarṣa, with parents Mahādeva and Sumitrā. Monier-Williams-era reference (skt-en-mono): 'The author of the Candraloka, a work on poetics, is known as Jayadeva Pīyūṣavarṣa' (ed. Khiste, Kashi Sanskrit Series 75).
texts.note: Secure: this Jayadeva (author of Prasannarāghava) distinct from the Gītagovinda Jayadeva; sources warn derivative works are wrongly ascribed to the latter.

texts.title: Kulapradīpa
texts.primary_author: Śivānandācārya (alias Sevānandācārya), son of the Gauḍa author Jagannivāsa
texts.basis: White, Yoga in Practice: 'the Kulapradīpa of Śivānandācārya (alias Sevānandācārya, son of the Gauḍa author Jagannivāsa)', listed among Kaula sources. No source_url/edition supplied; attribution consistent with current_author.
texts.note: Attribution to Śivānandācārya confirmed by White; note possible distinction from the Śivānanda who wrote Ṛjuvimarśinī on Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava (different Śivānanda). No e-text URL to verify colophon.

texts.title: Mātṛkābhedatantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Śaiva/Śākta tantra; framed as Śiva-Pārvatī dialogue)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: only Oliver Hellwig named as digitizer/input (Berlin 2001/02); no author/colophon. Text is a Śiva-to-Caṇḍikā dialogue, no individual composer named. Printed ed. by Chintamani Bhattacharya (Calcutta Sanskrit Series). Encyclopedia of Tantra cites it by patala only, no author.
texts.note: Genuinely anonymous tantra; revelatory framing (Śiva to the Goddess), no historical author; current attribution 'anonymous/traditional' confirmed.

texts.title: Mokṣopāya-Ṭīkā (Utpatti-prakaraṇa fragments) [Mokṣopāya / Yogavāsiṣṭha tradition]
texts.primary_author: Bhāskarakaṇṭha (ṭīkā); mūla Mokṣopāya anonymous (trad. ascribed Vālmīki)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: this file is Slaje's ed. of Bhāskarakaṇṭha's Mokṣopāya-Ṭīkā (3rd/Utpatti-prakaraṇa fragments); commentator self-names 'Bhāskarakaṇṭho racayati...vyākhyām'. Secondary (Lit. about Phil. Systems; Slaje/Hanneder Mokṣopāya Project) treats Bhāskarakaṇṭha as 18th-c. Kashmirian commentator. Web (McGill ARC; Hanneder 'Vasiṣṭha the Void'): the Mokṣopāya itself is an anonymous 10th-c. Kashmirian work; Vālmīki is the legendary frame-narrator, not the real author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:https://arcjournal.library.mcgill.ca/article/download/331/350/2913
texts.note: Two distinct roles: ṭīkā by Bhāskarakaṇṭha (18th c. Kashmir); root Mokṣopāya is anonymous (10th c., trad. ascribed to Vālmīki). The e-text content here is the commentary fragments. current_author was empty.

texts.title: Anuvyākhyāna
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'śrīmadānandatīrthabhagavatpādācāryaviracite śrīmadbrahmasūtrānuvyākhyāne' — composed by Madhva Ānandatīrtha, a Dvaita verse commentary on the Brahmasūtra. Secondary (Freschi/Maas Adaptive Reuse; Lit. about Phil. Systems) consistently attributes the Anuvyākhyāna to Madhva; standard Dvaita work. Single author, no conflation (the Nyāyasudhā of Jayatīrtha is a separate commentary ON it, not part of this file).
texts.note: Secure authorship; Madhva's own verse exposition of the Brahmasūtra. Sub-comm. Nyāyasudhā (Jayatīrtha) is distinct and not included here.

texts.title: Rasaratnasamuccaya
texts.current_author: Vāgbhaṭa (the alchemical Vāgbhaṭa; not the Ayurvedic author of the Aṣṭāṅgahṛdaya)
texts.basis: GRETIL header + colophon: 'sūnunā siṃhaguptasya rasaratnasamuccayaḥ' — by the son of Siṃhagupta; identified as a Vāgbhaṭa distinct from the Aṣṭāṅgahṛdaya author. Secondary (Walter alchemy dissertation: 'bhata as the author of the Rasaratnasamuccaya'; Dan Martin bib: Vāgbhaṭa 13th c. on mercurial/mineral medicines) confirms this alchemical Vāgbhaṭa, conventionally dated c. 13th–14th c.
texts.note: Traditional/conventional ascription to the alchemical Vāgbhaṭa (son of Siṃhagupta), deliberately distinguished from the Āyurvedic Vāgbhaṭa. This file = mūla only, no commentary.

texts.title: Rasaratnasamuccaya (with commentary)
texts.current_author: Vāgbhaṭa (mūla); commentary by a later commentator (e.g. Āśubodha/Vinodalāla or others)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: mūla = Vāgbhaṭa's Rasaratnasamuccaya; commentary Rasaratnasamuccayabodhinī by Āśubodhavidyābhūṣaṇa and Nityabodhavidyāratna (the Senagupta brothers, Calcutta); plus a separate Rasaratnasamuccayaṭīkā whose commentator is not named in the file. Calcutta 1927 ed., input O. Hellwig. Secondary as for the mūla.
texts.note: Multi-author: root (Vāgbhaṭa) + Bodhinī comm. by two named modern commentators (Senagupta brothers) + an additional unattributed ṭīkā. Refines current_author's tentative 'Āśubodha/Vinodalāla'.

texts.title: Rasaratnākara
texts.basis: GRETIL header + colophon: Rasaratnākara attributed to Nityanāthasiddha (alias Nemanātha); a compilatory alchemical/rasāyana work 'gathered from various sources'. Secondary (White, Kiss of the Yogini: Rasaratnākara of Nityanatha Siddha, a Hindu alchemist; LVP memorial vol.: Nityanātha Siddha author of tantric/vaidyaka works, possibly 12th c.) treats him as a legendary Siddha-tradition author/compiler; the large Rasaratnākara is widely held to be a composite compilation under his name.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Kiss of the Yogini_David Gordon White, 2003.txt; secondary:cleaned_Louis de la Vallee Poussin memorial volume.txt
texts.note: Traditional Siddha ascription to Nityanātha/Nemanātha; functions as compiler of a heterogeneous corpus (kept single-author display per tradition; compilatory nature noted).

texts.title: Brahma-Saṃhitā: Adhyāya 5 (with Jīva Gosvāmin's Digdarśanī-ṭīkā)
texts.primary_author: Mūla anonymous (trad. ascribed Brahmā); Digdarśanī-ṭīkā by Jīva Gosvāmin
texts.basis: GRETIL header + colophon: 'iti śrī-brahma-saṃhitāyāṃ ... śrīla-śrīpāda-śrī-jīva-gosvāmi-kṛtā dig-darśanī nāmnī ṭīkā samāptā' — commentary Digdarśanī by Jīva Gosvāmin on Adhyāya 5 of the Brahma-saṃhitā; root traditionally ascribed to Brahmā (so framed in Gauḍīya tradition, recovered by Caitanya). Secondary (Encyclopedia of Tantra; Wayman): Brahma-saṃhitā an authoritative Bengal-Vaiṣṇava work with Jīva's comm.; root is pseudepigraphic/anonymous.
texts.note: Conflates anonymous/pseudepigraphic mūla (legendarily Brahmā) with the named ṭīkā of Jīva Gosvāmin (16th c.). current_author noted only the root ascription.

texts.title: Mahābhāratatātparyanirṇaya
texts.basis: GRETIL header opens 'Śrīmad Ānandatīrthabhagavatpādācārya-viracitaḥ Śrī Mahābhāratatātparyanirṇayaḥ' and every chapter colophon repeats 'śrīmadānandatīrthabhagavatpādācāryaviracite'. Madhva (Ānandatīrtha, 13th c.) is the undisputed Dvaita Vedānta author; std ed. Bannanje Govindacarya. No dispute or multi-author structure found.
texts.note: Colophon names Ānandatīrtha (= Madhva); secure single-author work.

texts.title: Kṛṣṇāmṛtamahārṇava
texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes to Mādhva Ānandatīrtha; ed. Govindacarya in Sarvamūlagranthāḥ–Prasthānatrayī vol.5 (Udupi 1974). E-text input T.K. Gugler, who wrote a monograph on it (Münster 2009) as a work of Madhva. Compilation of devotional verses by Madhva; no dispute.
texts.note: Ānandatīrtha = Pūrṇaprajña = Madhva, one person; secure.

texts.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'śrī-candra-śekhara-mahā-kavi-candra-sūnu-śrī-viśvanātha-kavirāja-kṛtaṃ' and 'ity ālaṅkārika-cakravarti...mahāpātra-śrī-viśvanātha-kavirāja-kṛte sāhitya-darpaṇe'. Viśvanātha Kavirāja (Orissa, 14th c.) is the secure author (Kane ed., Wikipedia). 'Compilation' critique concerns originality, not multiple authorship.
texts.note: Colophon self-names Viśvanātha s/o Candraśekhara; secure single author.

texts.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa (archive bad OCR)
texts.basis: Same work/edition as visvsdpu (poor-OCR variant of the GRETIL Sāhityadarpaṇa). Author Viśvanātha Kavirāja per colophon and standard scholarship (Kane). No author change introduced by the OCR variant.
texts.note: Duplicate/OCR-variant of Sāhityadarpaṇa; same secure author.

texts.title: Rasendracintāmaṇi
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Siddhinandana Miśra, Caukhambha 2000) names Ḍhuṇḍhukanātha as author of this rasaśāstra/alchemy text. BUT secondary lit (N.N. Bhattacharyya, History of the Tantric Religion, p.~1103) states the Rasendracintāmaṇi is a general rasa text 'the authorship of which is disputed'. Web confirms Ḍhuṇḍhukanātha is the conventional ascription.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion An Historical, Ritualistic, and Philosophical Study_N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt
texts.note: Ḍhuṇḍhukanātha is the standard ascription but Bhattacharyya flags authorship as disputed; downgraded to disputed (one alleged author, no conflation).

texts.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 5
texts.basis: Pariccheda 5 of the Sāhityadarpaṇa (same Satyavrata Siṃha ed., 1992). Author Viśvanātha Kavirāja per colophon and standard scholarship (Kane). A single chapter of the unified work, not a separate author.
texts.note: Chapter 5 extract of Sāhityadarpaṇa; same secure single author.

texts.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 6
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Viśvanāthakavirāja as author; colophon: 'śrī-viśvanātha-kavirāja-kṛtaṃ prabandham'. Secondary lit (Mylius, Basham, Matilal, Coomaraswamy) uniformly attributes the Sāhityadarpaṇa to Viśvanātha Kavirāja (14th c.). Both kārikā and vṛtti are by Viśvanātha (auto-commentary); GRETIL file gives kārikā-mātra only.
texts.note: Single-author poetics treatise; kārikā + vṛtti both by Viśvanātha (no conflation). Son of Candraśekhara/Mahākavi Candra.

texts.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 3
texts.basis: GRETIL header and colophon name Viśvanātha Kavirāja as sole author. Secondary literature (Mylius, Basham, Matilal cites SD III.2 to Viśvanātha) confirms single authorship.
texts.note: Single-author auto-commentary (kārikā + vṛtti by Viśvanātha); no conflation.

texts.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 7
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: composed by Viśvanātha Kavirāja. Secondary lit uniformly attributes the Sāhityadarpaṇa to him (14th c.).
texts.note: Single-author poetics treatise; no multi-author structure.

texts.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon name Viśvanātha Kavirāja; secondary literature concurs.
texts.note: Single-author auto-commentary; no conflation.

texts.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 10
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon attribute to Viśvanātha Kavirāja; secondary lit confirms sole authorship of the SD.
texts.note: Single-author poetics treatise (final pariccheda on alaṃkāras); no conflation.

texts.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Viśvanāthakavirāja; colophon 'śrī-viśvanātha-kavirāja-kṛtaṃ prabandham'. Secondary literature (Mylius, Basham, etc.) attributes the entire Sāhityadarpaṇa to Viśvanātha Kavirāja (14th c.), with kārikā and vṛtti both his.
texts.note: Single-author auto-commentary; no conflation despite kārikā+vṛtti structure (both by Viśvanātha).

texts.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 9
texts.basis: GRETIL header and colophon name śrī-viśvanātha-kavirāja as composer of the entire Sāhityadarpaṇa (kārikā + auto-vṛtti are by the same author). Secondary lit (skt-en-mono) consistently credits Viśvanātha Kavirāja; he is a well-known late-14th-c. Sanskrit poetician.
texts.note: Pariccheda 9 is one chapter of a unified single-author treatise (text + author's own gloss). No multi-author structure.

texts.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa: Pariccheda 8
texts.basis: Same edition/header as Pariccheda 9; colophon names śrī-viśvanātha-kavirāja. Single author of both kārikā and integral vṛtti.
texts.note: Chapter 8 of the same unified single-author treatise; current attribution correct.

texts.title: Rājanighaṇṭu [Nighaṇṭurāja, Abhidhānacūḍāmaṇi]
texts.basis: GRETIL header titles it 'Narahari Pandita [alias Nrsimha]: Rajanighantu'. Secondary lit (Dash; Hahn) cites 'Narahari's Rājanighaṇṭu' as the materia-medica/lexicon work. Kashmiri Brahmin lexicographer, c. 13th-14th c.
texts.note: Single author (a nighaṇṭu/lexicon). 'traditional' since attribution rests on the author's own colophon verses; no dispute found.

texts.title: Pañcasāyaka
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'kaviśekharetibirudavatā jyotirīśvareṇa viracitaḥ pañcasāyakaḥ' and 'iti kaviśekharaśrījyotīśvarācāryaviracite pañcasāyake'. Sanderson (Śaiva Age) cites Jyotirīśvara as author of related Pañcasāyakamañjarī.
texts.note: Kāmaśāstra work by the Maithila poet Jyotirīśvara (c. 14th c.). Colophon-secure single authorship.

texts.title: Śivayogiśivācārya: Siddhāntaśikhāmaṇi
texts.basis: Root verse-text traditionally ascribed to Śivayogi Śivācārya, identified in secondary lit (Lit. about the Philosophical Systems) as 'Reṇuka's Siddhāntaśikhāmaṇi' with 'Tattvapradīpikā on Reṇuka's Siddhāntaśikhāmaṇi' by Maritoṇṭadārya. Web (Vīraśaiva studies): a Vīraśaiva doctrinal work, traditionally pre-Bāsava but more likely 13th-14th c.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:https://www.wisdomlib.org/definition/siddhantashikhamani
texts.note: Root text = current author (Śivayogi Śivācārya, alias Reṇukācārya). The cited edition BUNDLES Maritoṇṭadārya's Tattvapradīpikā commentary (distinct, later author), listed as ṭīkā, but the work proper is single-author so conflation_flag stays false. 'traditional' because the legendary Reṇuka ascription and pre-Bāsava dating are contested by scholars.

texts.title: Vijayottaratantra
texts.basis: Muktabodha Śaiva-tantra e-text; no human author named. As a scriptural/āgamic tantra it is traditionally regarded as divine revelation (Śiva). Distinct from the Mālinīvijayottaratantra (the only related title in secondary lit, exegeted by Abhinavagupta). No personal-author attribution found in local lit or web.
texts.note: Genuinely anonymous tantra; current 'anonymous/traditional' confirmed. Traditional ascription is to divine revelation, not a historical author. NOT the same text as Mālinīvijayottaratantra.

texts.title: Ānandakanda
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. Radhakrishna Sastri 1952) names no human author; text framed as Śrībhairava–Śrībhairavī dialogue, no colophon author. Web/scholarly (academia/researchgate articles on Ānandakanda in Indian alchemy) attribute it traditionally to Maṇṭhāna Bhairava, ~13th c. rasaśāstra work.
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Maṇṭhāna Bhairava; edition itself gives no author, work cast as Bhairava/Bhairavī revelation. Single (legendary) author, no multi-author structure.

texts.title: Phaladīpikā
texts.basis: GRETIL htm returned 404 (file not retrievable), but edition (V. Subrahmanya Sastri) and uniform scholarship (wisdomlib, academia.edu translations) attribute the 28-chapter jyotiṣa work (865 verses) securely to Mantreśvara (Tamil Nadu, c. 15th c.).
texts.note: Author Mantreśvara secure; root text only (commentaries by others exist but not this item).

texts.title: Uḍḍāmareśvaratantra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. J.D. Zadoo 1947) names no human author; text is Śiva (Īśvara/Bhairava)–Pārvatī revelatory dialogue. No scholarly attribution to a named author found; standard anonymous tantric scripture.
texts.note: Anonymous Tantra; nominal divine speaker Śiva. Traditional ascription is to Śiva, not a historical author.

texts.title: Pratāparudrīya [Pratāparudrayaśobhūṣaṇa]
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet/BSS 65, Trivedi 1909) confirms base alaṃkāra text by Vidyānātha and the Ratnāpaṇa commentary by Kumārasvāmin; closing colophon names Kumārasvāmi Somapīthin (son of Mallinātha's son, brother of Peddayārya) as commentator.
texts.note: This GRETIL item bundles root text (Vidyānātha) with Kumārasvāmin's Ratnāpaṇa ṭīkā — two distinct authors; current_author lists only Vidyānātha.

texts.title: Śitikaṇṭha: Mahānayaprakāśa
texts.basis: Sanderson (Śaiva Age) lists 'Mahānayaprakāśa of Śitikantha (Old Kashmiri) with a Sanskrit commentary'; web/scholarship: three distinct Krama texts share the title Mahānayaprakāśa (one anonymous, one by Arṇasiṃha, one by Śitikaṇṭha). Śitikaṇṭha's = Old Kashmiri/Apabhraṃśa verses with his own Sanskrit commentary (auto-commentary), single author.
texts.note: Title shared by 3 different Krama works (Arṇasiṃha; anonymous; Śitikaṇṭha) — this item is specifically Śitikaṇṭha's, verses + own Skt commentary, so no internal conflation. Rājānaka Śitikaṇṭha.

texts.title: Kaṭhināvadāna
texts.basis: DSBC list/text page gives no author or editor. Avadāna genre is anonymous compiled narrative literature; Hodgson's Nepalese avadāna list (secondary: Hunter, Life of Hodgson) records 'Kathinavadana' as item 16 among collected avadānas. No named author in any source.
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist avadāna (Mūlasarvāstivāda-related narrative tradition); no traditional individual ascription beyond the genre.

texts.title: Vedāntadeśika: Pāñcarātrarakṣā
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Freschi & Maas, Adaptive Reuse): the Pāñcarātrarakṣā is Veṅkaṭanātha's apologetic defense of the Pāñcarātra; edition Duraiswami Aiyangar & Venugopalacharya 1942 (the Adyar edition named in the workitem). Single, securely attributed work.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse_Aspects of Creativity in South Asian Cultural History.txt
texts.note: Veṅkaṭanātha = Vedānta Deśika (Nigamāntamahādeśika), 13th-14th c. Viśiṣṭādvaita ācārya. Single author, no commentary conflation in this e-text.

texts.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 1.1
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Venkatanatha [Vedantadesika] as author; closing benedictory verse 'śrīmān veṅkaṭanāthāryaḥ kavitārkikakesarī / vedāntācāryavaryo me saṃnidhattāṃ sadā hṛdi'. Secondary lit (Freschi & Maas; Potter-style bibliography) confirms Nyāyapariśuddhi by Vedānta Deśika.
texts.note: GRETIL e-text contains only Deśika's root text; the Nyāyasāra commentary (by Śrīnivāsācārya, a distinct later author) is NOT included here, so no conflation.

texts.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 5.2
texts.basis: Part of the same Nyāyapariśuddhi (Chowkhamba 1918 ed.) attributed to Veṅkaṭanātha Vedāntācārya; GRETIL header and secondary lit agree.
texts.note: Adhyāya division of single-author work; root text only, Nyāyasāra commentary not included.

texts.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Bhūmikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (fetched): author Venkatanatha [Vedantadesika], homages to Rāmānuja and Nigamāntamahādeśika, closing verse names Veṅkaṭanātha. Secondary lit confirms.
texts.note: Introductory section (bhūmikā) of the Nyāyapariśuddhi; same single author.

texts.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 5.1
texts.basis: Adhyāya of the Nyāyapariśuddhi (Chowkhamba 1918) by Veṅkaṭanātha Vedāntācārya; GRETIL header and secondary lit agree.
texts.note: Adhyāya division of single-author work; root text only.

texts.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 4.2
texts.basis: Adhyāya of the Nyāyapariśuddhi (Chowkhamba 1918) by Veṅkaṭanātha Vedāntācārya; GRETIL header and secondary lit agree.
texts.note: Adhyāya division of single-author work; root text only, no commentary conflation.

texts.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 4.1
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon names śrīmadveṅkaṭanāthasya vedāntācāryasya; secondary lit (Literature about the Philosophical Systems, lines 4515-4716) lists Nyāyapariśuddhi as a work of Vedānta Deśika with later commentaries (Nyāyasāra, Prakāśikā) by others; web confirms.
texts.note: Chapter-portion (adhyāya 4.1, smṛtyadhyāya) of one single-author Viśiṣṭādvaita pramāṇa-treatise. Commentaries Nyāyasāra/Prakāśikā are separate works, not part of this file; no conflation.

texts.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 3.1
texts.basis: Same work as 4.1; GRETIL header/colophon attributes to Veṅkaṭanātha Vedāntācārya; secondary lit corroborates Vedānta Deśika authorship.
texts.note: Adhyāya 3.1 portion of the single-author Nyāyapariśuddhi; no conflation.

texts.title: Nyāyapariśuddhi: Adhyāya 1.2
texts.basis: Same work as 3.1/4.1; GRETIL header/colophon attributes to Veṅkaṭanātha Vedāntācārya; secondary lit corroborates.
texts.note: Adhyāya 1.2 portion of the single-author Nyāyapariśuddhi; no conflation.

texts.title: Rasārṇavasudhākara
texts.basis: GRETIL header 'śrī-siṃha-bhūpāla-viracito rasārṇava-sudhākaraḥ', editor T. Venkatacharya, sole author; web (wisdomlib, academia) confirms 14th-c. Recarla king Sarvajña Siṅgabhūpāla II son of Anapota; local History of the Andhras confirms Recherla Singama Nayaka lineage.
texts.note: Single-author alaṃkāra/nāṭya-rasa treatise; cites Bharata/Rudraṭa as sources only, not co-authors. Note: this id and SA_GK16_simhrs_u point to the same GRETIL file (simhrs_u.htm) — duplicate work entries, not a conflation of authors.

texts.title: Rasārṇavasudhākara
texts.basis: Identical GRETIL file (simhrs_u.htm) to SA_GK16_simhrsku; header attributes sole authorship to Siṃhabhūpāla; web confirms.
texts.note: Duplicate entry of the same Rasārṇavasudhākara; single author Siṃhabhūpāla; no conflation.

texts.title: Śivastuti
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon 'iti śrīlaṅkeśvaraviracitā śivastutiḥ samāptā' accepts the traditional Laṅkeśvara ascription with no critical comment; Kāvyamālā vol.1 (Durgāprasād & Parab); web (researchgate, JIS Śaiva Literature survey) treats Rāvaṇa-attributed Śaiva hymns as legendary/pseudonymous, real author unknown.
texts.note: Short 10-stanza (Pṛthvī metre) Śiva hymn ascribed to Laṅkeśvara=Rāvaṇa, the legendary rākṣasa king; attribution is traditional/legendary, not historical — real author anonymous. Listed Rāvaṇa as low-confidence traditional ascription only.

texts.title: Kāmakalāvilāsa
texts.basis: Secondary lit (N.N. Bhattacharyya, History of the Tantric Religion: 'Punyānanda's Kāmakalāvilāsa... Its commentary, known as Cidvalli was composed by [Naṭanānanda]'; Dan Martin bibliography: 'Cidvalli Sanskrit Commentary by Śrī Naṭanānanda Nātha'; Avalon ed. title 'with the commentary of Naṭanānandanātha'). Root verses = Puṇyānanda; the standard transmitted text bundles the Cidvallī commentary by a different author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion An Historical, Ritualistic, and Philosophical Study_N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt; secondary:cleaned_Bibliography_tib_skt_philology_Dan Martin, 2006.txt
texts.note: Root kārikās by Puṇyānanda(nātha) (Śākta Śrīvidyā). Cidvallī commentary by Naṭanānandanātha — distinct author; flagged as conflation since title/edition carries both.

texts.title: Puṇyānanda: Kāmakalāvilāsa
texts.basis: Same work as SA_MB_kamakalavilasaHK (duplicate item). Root verses Puṇyānanda; Cidvallī commentary Naṭanānandanātha (Bhattacharyya; Dan Martin biblio; Avalon ed.).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion An Historical, Ritualistic, and Philosophical Study_N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt
texts.note: Duplicate of the other Kāmakalāvilāsa entry. Verses Puṇyānanda; commentary Naṭanānandanātha — distinct authors.

texts.title: Sāhityadarpaṇa
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'śrīviśvanātha-kavirājakṛtau sāhityadarpaṇo' and header line 'śrīviśvanāthakavirājaviracitaṃ sāhityadarpaṇaṃ nāma'. Secondary lit uniformly: Viśvanātha (Kavirāja) of Orissa, c. early 14th c. (Basham; skt-en-mono). Single author of kārikā+vṛtti.
texts.note: Standard alaṅkāra/poetics work; author Viśvanātha Kavirāja, secure self-colophon. The kārikās and accompanying vṛtti are by the same author.

texts.title: Ahorātravratakathā 2
texts.basis: GRETIL id ahovk2_u = Ahorātravratakathā (prose version), ed. R. Handurukande, 'Three Sanskrit Texts on Caitya Worship in Relation to the Ahorātravrata', Tokyo 2000. Text ends 'iti ahorātravratakathā samāptā' with no author named — an anonymous Buddhist (Newar) vrata-kathā on the day-and-night caitya-worship vow. (Note: the DSBC source_url currently resolves to an unrelated Abhidharmakośa chapter — mismatched link; identification rests on GRETIL id and Handurukande edition.)
texts.note: Anonymous Buddhist vrata-kathā; no traditional named author. DSBC link mismatched (returns Abhidharmakośa).

texts.title: Lalleśvarī: Lalleśvarīvākyāni
texts.primary_author: Lalleśvarī (Lal Dĕd / Lalla) (traditional ascription); orally transmitted, redacted later

authors.name: anonymous oral transmitters / Dharma-dāsa Darwesh (reciter to Grierson)

texts.basis: Secondary/web: vākhs ascribed to the 14th-c. Kashmiri Śaiva mystic Lalleśvarī (Lal Dĕd) but never written by her; transmitted orally for centuries. First written collection commissioned by Grierson 1914, transcribed from oral recitation by Dharma-dāsa Darwesh (kula-paramparā). Attribution rests on diction/prosody, not manuscripts; corpus accretive with later/spurious verses.
texts.source: web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lalleshwari; secondary:cleaned_Kragh_Determining the Corpus of South Asian Female-Authored Buddhist Texts...txt
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Lalleśvarī; orally transmitted/accreted, redacted only in 20th c. (Grierson via Dharma-dāsa). Authorship of individual vākhs not securable.

texts.title: Maheśvarānanda: Mahārthamañjarī
texts.primary_author: Maheśvarānanda (Mahārāṣṭrī Prakrit verses + Sanskrit autocommentary Parimala)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Essais sur le rituel: 'Mahārthamañjarī (Mahārāṣṭri Prakrit) with Sanskrit autocommentary (-parimala) by Maheśvarānanda/Goraksa'; Wangchuk; Tuske ed.). Verses (Prakrit gāthās) and the Parimala commentary are BOTH by Maheśvarānanda — an autocommentary, so single author across both layers.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Essais sur le rituel.txt; secondary:cleaned_Indian Epistemology and Metaphysics-Bloomsbury Academic_Joerg Tuske, ed , 2017_a.txt
texts.note: Single author Maheśvarānanda (also called Gorakṣa). Parimala is his own autocommentary — not a conflation despite two textual layers.

texts.title: Śataratnasaṃgraha
texts.basis: Archive/edition title page: 'Śataratnasaṃgraha of Śrī Umāpati Śivācārya' (ed. Thirugnanasambandhan, gen. ed. T.M.P. Mahadevan, Madras 1973). Secondary lit (N.N. Bhattacharyya, Hist. of Tantric Religion): 'Umāpati-Śivācārya was the author... to reveal the essence of all Āgamas'; the work is a Śaiva Siddhānta compilation of 100 verses (ratna) drawn from the Āgamas. Note: a separate verse-commentary, the Śataratnollekhinī (cited in Tatia 1951; Pañcānana Śāstrī ed., Tantrik Texts XXII), accompanies the text in some editions but is not part of this item.
texts.source: web:https://archive.org/details/acc.no.25435sataratnasangraha1973; secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion ... N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt
texts.note: Śaiva Siddhānta āgamic anthology by Umāpati Śivācārya (Tamil/Dharmapuram lineage, ~14th c.). Distinct from Umāpatidhara/Umāpatideva. Accompanying commentary Śataratnollekhinī is a separate work (sometimes ascribed to Umāpati himself), not in this item.

texts.title: Nyāyasudhā: Adhyāya 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Jayatīrtha's Nyāyasudhā, 'a subcommentary on Mādhva's Anuvyākhyāna, a commentary on Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra'; the GRETIL 'integrated version' interleaves three textual layers (Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtras + Madhva's Anuvyākhyāna + Jayatīrtha's Nyāyasudhā). Secondary lit (Lit. about Philosophical Systems): lists 'Sannyāyaratnāvalī or Nyāyasudhā on Madhva's Anuvyākhyāna' under Jayatīrtha, with numerous sub-commentaries (Vāgvajra, Gurvarthadīpikā, Parimala, etc.).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Nyāyasudhā itself securely by Jayatīrtha (Ṭīkācārya, 14th c.). conflation_flag=true because the e-text integrates three authorial layers (Bādarāyaṇa sūtra / Madhva bhāṣya-anuvyākhyāna / Jayatīrtha sub-commentary). Homonym caution: a distinct Nyāyasudhā by Someśvara Bhaṭṭa on Kumārila's Tantravārttika is unrelated.

texts.title: Nyāyasudhā: Adhyāya 4, Pāda 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Jayatīrtha's Nyāyasudhā, subcommentary on Madhva's Anuvyākhyāna (on Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra); integrated e-text layers Brahmasūtra + Anuvyākhyāna + Nyāyasudhā. Secondary lit attributes Nyāyasudhā (= Sannyāyaratnāvalī) to Jayatīrtha.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Same work as adhyāya 1, Pāda 4.1 portion. Jayatīrtha secure; conflation_flag=true for the three interleaved authorial layers in the e-text.

texts.title: Nyāyasudhā: Adhyāya 3
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Jayatīrtha's Nyāyasudhā, subcommentary on Madhva's Anuvyākhyāna (on Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra); integrated e-text layers Brahmasūtra + Anuvyākhyāna + Nyāyasudhā. Secondary lit attributes Nyāyasudhā to Jayatīrtha.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Same work as adhyāya 1 (adhyāya 3 portion). Jayatīrtha secure; conflation_flag=true for the three interleaved authorial layers.

texts.title: Nyāyasudhā: Adhyāya 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Jayatīrtha's Nyāyasudhā, subcommentary on Madhva's Anuvyākhyāna (on Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra); integrated e-text layers Brahmasūtra + Anuvyākhyāna + Nyāyasudhā. Secondary lit attributes Nyāyasudhā to Jayatīrtha.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Same work as adhyāya 1 (adhyāya 2 portion). Jayatīrtha secure; conflation_flag=true for the three interleaved authorial layers.

texts.title: Smaradīpikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes to Mīnanātha by title only; no internal colophon names an author (Hellwig digitization from Kāmakuñjalatā, Chowkhamba 1967). Web/wisdomlib: lists 'Smaradīpikā by Mīnanātha (?)' with a scholarly question-mark, and a DISTINCT homonymous Smaradīpikā by Rudra (with a commentary by Vātsya Mahādeva). Mīnanātha is a legendary Nātha-siddha name (associated with Matsyendranātha, teacher of Gorakṣa).
texts.note: Ascription to Mīnanātha is traditional and uncertain (scholarly '?'); a Nātha pseudo-attribution to a legendary adept. Must NOT be confused with the separate Smaradīpikā of Rudra (comm. Vātsya Mahādeva). This item is the Mīnanātha kāmaśāstra text from Kāmakuñjalatā.

texts.title: Guṇakāraṇḍavyūhasūtra
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Lokesh Chandra ed., DSBC input) names no author/colophon, only editor/digitizers. Web: verse Guṇakāraṇḍavyūha-mahāyānasūtrarāja composed anonymously in Nepal, prob. first half 15th c., reworking the prose Kāraṇḍavyūha and incorporating Bodhicaryāvatāra verses; no named author (speculative Vanaratna link rejected for lack of evidence). Secondary lit lists it among canonical sūtras without author.
texts.note: Anonymous Nepalese verse recension of the Kāraṇḍavyūha; traditionally treated as Buddha-vacana sūtra, no historical author.

texts.title: Nīlakaṇṭha: Kriyāsāra, volume 2
texts.basis: No source_url. Edition (Narayana Swamy Shastry, Mysore Oriental Series 1957) titled 'Kriyāsāra of Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya'.
