Secondary lit (Literature about Philosophical Systems) lists 'Sarvasvabhuṣana on Nīlakaṇṭha's Kriyāsāra', confirming Nīlakaṇṭha as author with a known commentary on it. Web confirms Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya as Vīraśaiva author of the Kriyāsāra (Brahmasūtra-oriented work in the Vīraśaiva/Śivādvaita frame).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:https://brill.com/view/journals/saih/1/1/article-p13_2.xml?language=en
texts.note: Vīraśaiva author Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya; vol. 2 = Upadeśas 5-14. Not the astronomer/Mahābhārata commentator Nīlakaṇṭha.

texts.title: Jīvanmuktiviveka
texts.basis: GRETIL vedanta URL 404 at fetch time. Secondary lit (Literature about Philosophical Systems; Poussin's Abhidharmakośa notes; Bendall catalogue) consistently cites 'Vidyāraṇya's Jīvanmuktiviveka' with multiple sub-commentaries (Pañcaratnadīpikā, Pūrṇānandendukaumudī etc.). Web: work of Mādhava who took the sannyāsa name Vidyāraṇya; composed c.1380; attribution to Vidyāraṇya secure via the Mādhava=Vidyāraṇya identification.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vidyaranya
texts.note: Vidyāraṇya is the renunciate name of Mādhava; the JMV (unlike the SDS) is securely his, written after he became a sannyāsin.

texts.title: Sarva-darśana-saṃgraha
texts.current_author: Mādhava (commonly identified with Vidyāraṇya); attribution discussed
texts.basis: GRETIL vedanta URL 404 at fetch time; edition titled 'Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha of Sāyaṇa-Mādhava'. Secondary lit explicitly hedges: 'Sarvadarsanasamgraha (traditionally attributed to Madhava)'; Doniger 'of Madhava (not to be confused...)'; skt-en-mono refers to 'Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha of Sāyaṇamādhava'. Web: active debate — Mādhava may not be the author; candidates include Sāyaṇa-Mādhava, Bhāratītīrtha, Cinnambhaṭṭa/Channibhaṭṭa (Thakur: plan by Mādhava, written by Channibhaṭṭa with Sāyaṇa's help).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarva-Darsana-Sangraha
texts.note: Traditional ascription to Mādhava/Vidyāraṇya; modern scholarship genuinely disputes it (Channibhaṭṭa / Sāyaṇa-Mādhava / Bhāratītīrtha hypotheses). Status sharpened from 'discussed' to disputed.

texts.title: Kriyāsāra (volume 1)
texts.basis: source_url is archive.org scan titled 'Kriyāsāra of Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya'. Secondary lit (Literature about Philosophical Systems) lists 'Sarvasvabhuṣana on Nīlakaṇṭha's Kriyāsāra', confirming authorship. Web confirms Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya as the Vīraśaiva author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:https://books.google.com/books/about/The_Kriy%C4%81s%C4%81ra_of_Sri_N%C4%ABlaka%E1%B9%87%E1%B9%ADa.html
texts.note: Same work as vol. 2 item; vol. 1 of the Kriyāsāra. Vīraśaiva author Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya.

texts.title: Jagaddhara Bhaṭṭa: Stutikusumāñjali
texts.basis: No source_url. Edition title itself names two authors: 'Stutikusumāñjali of Jagaddhara Bhaṭṭa WITH the Commentary of Rājānaka Ratnakaṇṭha' (Kāvyamālā 23, 1891). Secondary lit: Bendall catalogue notes 'rare commentary of Jagaddhara/Ratnakaṇṭha'; skt-en-mono describes how Ratnakaṇṭha 'commenced his commentary on the Stutikusumāñjali'. Web (Hanneder, 'Poetry as Prayer'): Stutikusumāñjali is the Śaiva hymn-cycle (~39 chapters) of the 14th-c. Kashmiri Jagaddhara Bhaṭṭa; the Laghupañcāśikā commentary is by Rājānaka Ratnakaṇṭha (17th c.).
texts.note: Edition conflates root stotra (Jagaddhara Bhaṭṭa, Kashmir, 14th c.) with the much later commentary (Rājānaka Ratnakaṇṭha, 17th c.). Current_author records only the poet; commentator separated as ṭīkā.

texts.title: Haṭhayogapradīpikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Svātmārāma as author; colophons read 'iti haṭhapradīpikāyāṃ ...upadeśaḥ'. Secondary lit (Mallinson; Potter EIP vol.1) treats it as a 15th-c. compilation by Svātmārāma drawing on earlier hatha texts.
texts.note: Single named compiler; the work compiles material from earlier sources (Gorakṣaśataka/Vivekamārtaṇḍa etc.) but is securely transmitted under Svātmārāma. No multi-author commentary structure in this edition.

texts.title: Sāṃkhyasūtra
texts.current_author: traditionally ascribed to Kapila (actually an anonymous late author)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (late, c. 14th c.); traditionally ascribed to Kapila
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives 'Kapila (trad. author): Samkhyasutra'. Potter EIP vol.4 states the sūtras (=Sāṃkhyapravacana) are 'attributed to Kapila (wrongly)' and are a late work, presupposed by Aniruddha's 15th-c. vṛtti and Vijñānabhikṣu's bhāṣya; nothing reliable known of its actual author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 4 - Potter 1987.txt
texts.note: Pseudepigraphic: legendary ascription to the Sāṃkhya founder Kapila; actual author anonymous and late (c. 14th c.). Current_author already records this. Commentaries (Aniruddha, Vijñānabhikṣu) are separate works, not part of this sūtra text.

texts.title: Bhaktiśataka
texts.basis: Secondary lit confirms: Dan Martin bib notes Rāmacandra Kavibhāratī, a Bengali who moved to Śrī Laṅkā under Parākramabāhu and converted to Buddhism, as 'A Great Buddhist Poet of Bengal' and author of the Bhaktiśataka; Nakamura lists the Bhaktiśataka among Sanskrit Buddha-stotras preserved in Ceylon.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Bibliography_tib_skt_philology_Dan Martin, 2006.txt; secondary:cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey..._Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
texts.note: Secure single author. WARNING: the recorded source_url (DSBC content/820/2949) actually resolves to a Dhāraṇī Saṃgraha page, not the Bhaktiśataka — header could not confirm; attribution rests on secondary literature.

texts.title: Camatkāracandrikā
texts.basis: GRETIL TEI colophons repeatedly name the author: 'śrīviśveśvarakavicandrapraṇītāyāṃ ... camatkāracandrikāyāṃ'. An alaṅkāra work composed for/under Siṅgabhūpāla.
texts.note: Single named author confirmed by internal colophons; no commentary-layer conflation.

texts.title: Śrīkarabhāṣya
texts.basis: No source_url (other corpus). Web/scholarly (wisdomlib essay on the Śrīkara Bhāṣya; ed. C. Hayavadana Rao 1936) confirms Śrīpati Paṇḍitācārya authored this Vīraśaiva bhāṣya on Bādarāyaṇa's Brahma/Vedānta-sūtras; he names himself 'Śrīpati Paṇḍita Bhagavatpādācārya' / Nirābhāra Vīraśaiva yati.
texts.note: Secure single-author bhāṣya. It is a commentary ON the Brahmasūtras (Bādarāyaṇa = mūla, a separate work), but the bhāṣya itself is wholly Śrīpati's — no multi-author conflation within this title.

texts.title: Gorakṣaśataka
texts.primary_author: attributed to Gorakṣanātha (legendary); actual author anonymous
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Goraksanatha (=Gorakhnath): Goraksasataka'. Mallinson ('The Original Gorakṣaśataka') and White (Yoga in Practice) show the title conflates TWO distinct texts: (a) the 'original Gorakṣaśataka' (c.1400, 3 surviving Tamil-Nadu MSS) and (b) the much more widely transmitted Vivekamārtaṇḍa, which circulated under the name Gorakṣaśataka through confusion, eclipsing the original.
texts.note: Pseudepigraphic ascription to the semi-legendary Nāth guru Gorakṣanātha (Gorakhnāth) — not a securely datable historical author. Conflation_flag=true: 'Gorakṣaśataka' designates at least two different anonymous works (original Gorakṣaśataka vs. Vivekamārtaṇḍa); the named author is legendary, real composer(s) anonymous.

texts.title: Brahmavidyā Upaniṣad
texts.primary_author: anonymous (root text); Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (commentary in the Adyar ed.)
texts.basis: Secondary (White, Yoga in Practice): a late minor Yoga Upaniṣad, no named author, transmitted in short northern (14 vv.) and longer southern recensions. Goudriaan (Ritual & Speculation) notes more than one text of this name circulated. The Adyar/Theosophical edition cited carries the commentary (Dīpikā) of Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (Brahmayogin), a distinct 18th-c. commentator on the 108 Upaniṣads.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_White_Yoga in Practice.txt; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Root text genuinely anonymous (no traditional named ṛṣi); the edition's value-add commentary is by Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin — separate author/commentator role, hence conflation_flag.

texts.title: Sarvollāsatantra
texts.basis: Encyclopedia of Tantra (Santideva): 'Sarvananda, author of the Tantric work, Sarvollasa'; his biography Sarvānanda-taraṅgiṇī written by his son Śivanātha. Web (wisdomlib, sacred-texts, Khandelwal ed. on archive.org): Sarvānandanātha of Meharpītha, medieval Bengali Tantric saint; the Sarvollāsatantra is a large Śākta compendium/digest (64 chapters) compiling many tantras — earliest dateable evidence of the ten Mahāvidyās (per Sanderson).
texts.note: Attribution secure to Sarvānandanātha but his role is essentially compiler of a digest of prior tantras; traditional/devotional framing. Current_author confirmed.

texts.title: Yogakuṇḍalyupaniṣat (Yogakuṇḍalinī / Yogakuṇḍalī Upaniṣad)
texts.primary_author: anonymous (root text); Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (commentary in the Adyar ed.)
texts.basis: Secondary (White, Yoga in Practice): a 'late Yoga Upaniṣad' whose first chapter incorporates most of the Gorakṣaśataka — i.e. anonymous compilation drawing on Haṭha sources; no named author. Bronkhorst (Two Traditions of Meditation) treats it as an anonymous late text. Mahādeva Śāstrī's Adyar edition cited supplies the commentary of Śrī Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin (distinct named commentator).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_White_Yoga in Practice.txt; secondary:cleaned_Two Traditions of Meditation in Ancient India_Johannes Bronkhorst, 1993.txt
texts.note: Root anonymous; chap. 1 largely borrowed from Gorakṣaśataka. Edition's commentary by Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin = separate commentator role, hence conflation_flag.

texts.title: Vasantatilakā with Rahasyadīpikā
texts.primary_author: Kṛṣṇācārya / Caryāvratī Śrīkṛṣṇācārya (Kāṇha) (mūla Vasantatilakā); Vanaratna (Rahasyadīpikā commentary)
texts.basis: Edition title itself (Rare Buddhist Text Series 7, Samdhong Rinpoche & V. Dwivedi 1990) and web (CiNii, Aditya Prakashan, Google Books): root verse text Vasantatilakā by Caryāvratī Śrīkṛṣṇācārya (the Buddhist tantric Kṛṣṇa/Kāṇha), with the Rahasyadīpikā commentary by Vanaratna (the 15th-c. Bengali paṇḍita, last great Indian scholar to teach in Tibet). Secondary: Kongtrul's Treasury (v8) lists Vasantatilaka (dPyid kyi thig le) under Kṛṣṇa/Lūipa; Martin/PIATS confirm Vanaratna as 13th–15th-c. teacher. NB Vasantatilakā is a Cakrasaṃvara/Hevajra-circle work — not to be confused with the metre or the Lalitavistara verses.
texts.source: gretil-header (edition title); web:https://ci.nii.ac.jp/ncid/BA12684417; secondary:cleaned_Kongtrul_Treasury_v8p3_The Elements of Tantric Practice.txt
texts.note: current_author was empty; this title conflates a mūla (Kṛṣṇācārya/Kāṇha) and its commentary (Vanaratna, fl. 15th c.) — two distinct authors centuries apart.

texts.title: Kramadīpikā
texts.current_author: Keśava Kāśmīrī Bhaṭṭa (text attributed to him; 'Rasikamohana Caṭṭopādhyāya' is the modern editor, not the author)
texts.basis: Web (krks.name, Wikipedia 'Keshav Kashmiri', wisdomlib): the Kramadīpikā is a ritual treatise (8 chapters, Vaiṣṇava/Nimbārka-sampradāya) self-attributed in its closing verse ('Keśavena kṛtā Kramadīpikā'). Modern editions add commentaries (e.g. Govinda Bhaṭṭa's Vivaraṇa); editors (Govinda Bhaṭṭa / Rasikamohana Caṭṭopādhyāya / S. Malaviya) are not the author. Secondary lit confirms Keśava Kāśmīrī as a prolific Nimbārka commentator.
texts.note: Author secure via colophon self-naming; editor Rasikamohana Caṭṭopādhyāya correctly excluded as non-author. Distinct from the Tantric/Mantra Kramadīpikās of other traditions.

texts.title: Śivasaṃhitā
texts.primary_author: anonymous (framed as Śiva's revelation to Pārvatī)
texts.basis: Web (Wikipedia 'Shiva Samhita'; Mallinson, critical ed.) and secondary (White, Yoga in Practice): author and date unknown; a compiled Haṭha/Tantra yoga manual cast as Śiva's instruction to Pārvatī. Mallinson dates it pre-1500 (c.1300–1500 CE), composed in/around Varanasi; some earlier scholarship suggested 17th c. The Vasu 1914 edition (cited) attributes no human author.
texts.note: Genuinely anonymous; traditional ascription is to Śiva (divine revelation), not a historical author. No conflation — single compiled work.

texts.title: Ratnamālāvadāna
texts.basis: GRETIL header: ed. Kanga Takahata (1954), transcribed by Klaus Wille; no author named, framed as Upagupta-Aśoka narrative; tale colophons mark completion only, not authorship. Web (wisdomlib/Avadāna lit.): a Mahāyāna garland of 38 edifying tales, anonymous compilation in the avadānamālā genre.
texts.note: Anonymous compiled avadāna anthology; no human author/compiler named in colophons. Within-text narrator is Upagupta to Aśoka (frame device, not authorship).

texts.title: Kokilasandeśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header explicitly: 'Uddaṇḍa Śāstrī's Kokila Sandeśa', based on edition/translation by Shankar Rajaraman & Venetia Kotamraju (Rasāla 2012); orig. ed. N.P. Unni 1997. Uddaṇḍa is the well-known 15th-c. Kerala (sandeśakāvya) poet.
texts.note: Single secure author; matches current attribution.

texts.title: Haṭhayogapradīpikā
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Kohn, Chinese Healing Exercises): 'compiled in the fifteenth century on the basis of the notes and instructions of earlier masters by Svatmarama Swami'; White, Yoga in Practice: 'fifteenth-century Indian author Svātmarāma'. Multiple bibliographies (Literature about the Philosophical Systems) list HYP under Svātmārāma.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Chinese Healing Exercises. The Tradition of Daoyin_Livia Kohn, 2008.txt; secondary:cleaned_White_Yoga in Practice.txt
texts.note: Svātmārāma is named compiler-author (15th c.), drawing on earlier Nātha-siddha verses. Standard commentary Jyotsnā by Brahmānanda is a separate work, not part of this base-text item; no conflation within the mūla.

texts.title: Vedāntasāra
texts.basis: Edition is G.A. Jacob's Vedāntasāra of Sadānanda. Web (Wikipedia): 15th-c. Advaita manual by Sadānanda Yogīndra Sarasvatī; draws on Pañcadaśī (Vidyāraṇya d.1386), Upadeśasāhasrī, Naiṣkarmyasiddhi. (GRETIL https/http URL returned 404 at fetch time.)
texts.note: Secure single author. NB name-conflation caution: distinct from the Sadānanda who wrote Advaitabrahmasiddhi (different person), but that does not affect this title; no internal multi-author structure.

texts.title: Darśanopaniṣat (Darśana Upaniṣad)
texts.basis: Web (Wikipedia): one of the minor Yoga Upaniṣads, attached to Sāmaveda, anonymous ancient author. Edition (Yoga Upaniṣads, Adyar 1920) supplies the commentary of Śrī Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin = Rāmacandrendra Sarasvatī, an 18th-c. Advaitin who commented on all 108 Muktikā Upaniṣads.
texts.note: Root Upaniṣad is anonymous; the cited edition conflates it with a named 18th-c. commentary (Upaniṣad-Brahmayogin / Rāmacandrendra Sarasvatī). Separated mūla vs ṭīkā roles -> conflation_flag true. Mūla itself remains anonymous.

texts.title: Bṛhannīlatantra
texts.basis: Muktabodha/Muktalib e-text: structured as Bhairava-Bhairavī (Devī) saṃvāda; section colophons 'śrībṛhannīlatantre bhairavabhairavīsaṃvāde'; no human author. Ed. Madhusudan Kaul Shastri (KSTS, Srinagar 1941); transcribed under M.S.G. Dyczkowski. Tantric revelation framework, divine speakers only.
texts.note: Anonymous revealed tantra; traditional ascription to Bhairava (śiva-vākya), no human composer. Madhusudan Kaul Shastri is editor, not author.

texts.title: Svayambhū Purāṇa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Newar Buddhist compilation); traditionally narrated by the Buddha via Upagupta
texts.basis: GRETIL header (DSBC input, based on Shakya & Bajracharya 2001 ed./transl.) names no original author. Web (Wikipedia/Bajracharya.org): a Newar Buddhist scripture with complex layered compositional history, copied and rewritten over centuries; framed as the Buddha's narration retold by Upagupta to Aśoka. No single historical author.
texts.note: Traditional (legendary) ascription: revealed by the Buddha, transmitted via Sanavasa->Upagupta. Actual text an anonymous evolving compilation; multiple recensions of differing length exist.

texts.title: Rasamañjarī
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon verse (Rmañj_1.3) names the author: 'śrīvaidyanāthatanayaḥ ... śrīśālinātha ... ārambhate ... rasamañjarīyam' — Śālinātha, son of Vaidyanātha. Web (wisdomlib) confirms Śālinātha, son of Vaidyanātha, as author of this rasaśāstra text.
texts.note: Single-author rasaśāstra (alchemy/medicine) work. Distinct from the homonymous kāvya Rasamañjarī of Bhānudatta — this is the Ayurvedic one by Śālinātha s. Vaidyanātha.

texts.title: Paraśurāma-Kalpasūtra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Śrīvidyā ritual digest); pseudepigraphically ascribed to Paraśurāma (Bhārgava)
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon (Parks_10.85) ascribes the root text to the mythic sage 'śrī-paraśurāma-bhārgava', mahā-kulācārya. This is a legendary/pseudepigraphic ascription, not a historical author. Secondary (Encyclopedia of Tantra, Santideva 1999) refers to 'Ramesvara, commentator of the Parasuramakalpasutra' — the famous Saubhāgyodaya commentary by Rāmeśvara is a SEPARATE work not contained in this GRETIL root-text edition.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt
texts.note: Root text is anonymous, pseudepigraphically attributed to sage Paraśurāma. This GRETIL/Gaekwad edition is the sūtra alone (no commentary); Rāmeśvara's Saubhāgyodaya ṭīkā exists separately and would be a distinct author if included. No conflation in THIS item.

texts.title: Vārāhopaniṣat (Varāha Upaniṣad)
texts.basis: Edition is the Adyar Yoga Upaniṣads with the commentary of Upaniṣad-Brahma-Yogin (commentator named, but commentary is a separate layer). Web (Wikipedia): author, authenticity and source in question; a late minor/Yoga Upaniṣad, absent from early canonical lists (Dārā Shukoh, Colebrooke, Nārāyaṇa). Framed as Viṣṇu's teaching to Ṛbhu — narrative framing, not authorship.
texts.note: Root Upaniṣad is anonymous. The cited Adyar edition adds the dīpikā of Upaniṣad-Brahma-Yogin (Brahmayogin) — a separate later commentator; but this workitem is the Upaniṣad text itself, so no conflation flagged for the base text.

texts.title: Rāmodanta
texts.basis: GRETIL header (input C. Vielle) states explicitly 'an anonymous work of unknown date' — a short poetic Rāma epitome traditionally used by Sanskrit beginners in South India/Kerala. Kaṭapayādi in final verse encodes 156 verses. No poet named in text; no reliable historical attribution.
texts.note: Anonymous pedagogical kāvya. (Sometimes loosely linked to a 'Vararuci', but GRETIL editor and scholarship treat it as anonymous of unknown date.)

texts.title: Śāradātilaka Tantra (with Padārthādarśa of Rāghavabhaṭṭa)
texts.basis: Edition title (Woodroffe/Avalon, Tantrik Texts 16-17, 1933) explicitly pairs the Śāradātilaka of Lakṣmaṇadeśikendra with the Padārthādarśa commentary of Rāghavabhaṭṭa. Web (Hindupedia/wisdomlib): root by Lakṣmaṇadeśika(endra), c. 11th c.; Padārthādarśa by Rāghavabhaṭṭa, son of Pṛthvīdhara of Janasthāna (Nāsik), c. 1493-94 CE, the most important commentary. Secondary (Encyclopedia of Tantra; skt-en-mono) repeatedly cite 'Rāghavabhaṭṭa's comm. on the Śāradātilaka' as distinct from the mūla.
texts.note: Two distinct authors ~400 yrs apart: mūla-tantra by Lakṣmaṇadeśika (11th c.), Padārthādarśa ṭīkā by Rāghavabhaṭṭa (1493/94 CE). Workitem string foregrounds Rāghavabhaṭṭa (the commentator); roles separated. Note skt-en-mono flags Rāghavabhaṭṭa as 'fabricating an ancient authorship' for the root — i.e. the tantra's own self-ascription is itself contested.

texts.title: Subhāṣitāvalī
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Vallabhadeva as compiler of the anthology (Peterson 1886 ed.). Secondary (skt-en-mono): 'verse anthology compiled by Vallabhadeva in c. fifteenth-century Kashmir'; brings together verses by numerous authors. Web (wisdomlib/Haksar, JRAS 'On the Date'): Vallabhadeva is the COMPILER, not author; ~3527 verses ascribed to 362 poets plus anonymous; his date disputed (Aufrecht 16th c. vs trad. 15th c.) and some of his own ascriptions of verses are known to be incorrect.
texts.note: Vallabhadeva is the COMPILER of an anthology, not its author; role corrected to compiler. Anthology by nature contains 362+ named poets but that is not single-title conflation, so conflation_flag=false. Compiler's date debated; some verse-ascriptions within are erroneous.

texts.title: Rasasaṃketakalikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (data entry O. Hellwig, Chaukhamba ed. 1984) names Cāmuṇḍa, c.1490 CE; in-text closing verse 'cāmuṇḍo yoginīpure / rasasaṃketakalikāṃ kṛtavān' explicitly self-attributes authorship. No competing attribution found.
texts.note: Author confirmed by colophon verse naming Cāmuṇḍa at Yoginīpura. Matches current_author.

texts.title: Kramadīpikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Kramadīpikā (with commentary by Govindabhaṭṭācārya)', author Keśavakāśmīri, commentator Govindabhaṭṭācārya (Malaviya ed. 1989). Web (wisdomlib, swaminarasingha, Wikipedia 'Keshav Kashmiri'): Kramadīpikā is a Vaiṣṇava ritual/mantra work (Nimbārka-sampradāya, also called Gopālamantra) by Keśava Kāśmīrī, quoted in Gauḍīya Haribhaktivilāsa. NOTE: Potter (Encyclopedia Indian Philosophies vol.4) hits on a Kramadīpikā are a DIFFERENT Sāṃkhya Tattvasamāsa commentary (ascr. Pañcaśikha) and are not relevant here.
texts.note: E-text is the edition that bundles the root Kramadīpikā of Keśava Kāśmīrī WITH Govindabhaṭṭācārya's commentary; current_author lists only Keśava, so commentator added and conflation flagged. Distinct from the unrelated Sāṃkhya Kramadīpikā.

texts.title: Jñānāmṛtasāra-Saṃhitā [Nārada-Pañcarātra]
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Pāñcarātra Saṃhitā; traditionally framed as narrated by Vyāsa to Śuka)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: dialogue text (Śuka questions, Vyāsa narrates; Nārada, Brahmā, Śiva, Sanatkumāra speak); no historical author named, only chapter colophons 'iti śrī-nārada-pañca-rātre jñānāmṛta-sāre...'. As a Pāñcarātra Saṃhitā it is an anonymous compiled/revealed text; no individual author identified.
texts.note: Anonymous Vaiṣṇava Pāñcarātra Saṃhitā. Traditional/legendary framing: revealed dialogue narrated by Vyāsa to Śuka; no real author. Matches current 'anonymous/traditional'.

texts.title: Tarkatāṇḍava: Pariccheda 3
texts.basis: GRETIL header (Sansknet input): Tarkatāṇḍava by Vyāsatīrtha 'śrīrāghavendratīrthakṛtanyāyadīpākhyāvyākhyāsamalaṅkṛtam' — i.e. the e-text carries Rāghavendratīrtha's Nyāyadīpa commentary. Web (Springer JIPh; harshalarajesh Raghavendra works; wisdomlib): Tarkatāṇḍava is a Dvaita Navya-Nyāya work of Vyāsatīrtha; Rāghavendratīrtha (a later Mādhva) wrote the Nyāyadīpa, the only printed commentary (Mysore ORI ed.).
texts.note: E-text bundles mūla (Vyāsatīrtha) + Nyāyadīpa commentary (Rāghavendratīrtha, distinct later author); current_author omits the commentator, so added and conflation flagged.

texts.title: Tarkatāṇḍava: Pariccheda 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header explicitly: 'śrīrāghavendratīrthakṛtanyāyadīpākhyāvyākhyāsamalaṅkṛtam' (adorned with the Nyāyadīpa commentary composed by Rāghavendratīrtha); mukhopodghāta lists three works under Vyāsatīrtha's name (Candrikā, Nyāyāmṛta, Tarkatāṇḍava). Same edition/structure as Pariccheda 3; mūla by Vyāsatīrtha, commentary by Rāghavendratīrtha.
texts.note: Same conflation as Pariccheda 3: mūla Vyāsatīrtha + Nyāyadīpa commentary Rāghavendratīrtha; commentator added, conflation flagged.

texts.title: Tarkatāṇḍava: Pariccheda 2
texts.basis: GRETIL header explicitly: 'śrīvyāsatīrtharacitam ... śrīrāghavendratīrthakṛtanyāyadīpākhyavyākhyāsamalaṅkṛtam' — the e-text presents the root Tarkatāṇḍava together with Rāghavendratīrtha's Nyāyadīpa commentary. Secondary lit (Potter EIP vol.2; Bibliography of Philosophical Systems) confirms Tarkatāṇḍava by Vyāsatīrtha with later commentaries.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Root Mādhva-Nyāya polemic by Vyāsatīrtha (c.1460-1539); this file bundles Rāghavendratīrtha's (17th c.) Nyāyadīpa ṭīkā. current_author records only the root author — commentary author distinct.

texts.title: Mantrakaumudī
texts.basis: Edition (Ramanath Jha, Mithila Institute, Darbhanga; archive.org) ascribes the Tantra(Mantra)kaumudī to MM Devanātha Ṭhakkura Tarkapañcānana, a Maithila paṇḍit (15th-17th c.). wisdomlib notes a homonymous Tantrakaumudī by Sadānandanātha — a different work, not part of this text.
texts.note: Single author secure for this Mithila edition; title also given as Tantrakaumudī. Distinct same-title work by Sadānandanātha exists but is not conflated here.

texts.title: Padyāvalī
texts.primary_author: Rūpa Gosvāmī (compiler); verses by many poets (Jayadeva, Bilvamaṅgala, et al.)

authors.name: Jayadeva, Bilvamaṅgala, Śaraṇa, and numerous others (incl. anonymous 'kasyacit')

texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'iti śrī-mad-rūpa-gosvāmi-samāhṛtā padyāvalī samāptā' — samāhṛtā = compiled/collected, not composed. The work itself names contributing poets (Jayadeva, Bilvamaṅgala etc.). Secondary lit (skt-en-mono: 'Padyavali of Rupa Gosvamin... is a collection of devotional verses'; EIP).
texts.note: Anthology (saṃgraha) of Vaiṣṇava verses curated by Rūpa Gosvāmī (16th c.); the individual padyas are by dozens of distinct poets. Rūpa's role is compiler/editor, not sole author.

texts.title: Ujjvalanīlamaṇi
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Rūpa Gosvāmin as author; this file is the root treatise only. Secondary lit (skt-en-mono; EIP; Companion to World Philosophies) consistently attributes the Ujjvalanīlamaṇi (rasa-śāstra on madhura-rasa) to Rūpa Gosvāmin. Known commentaries (Jīva Gosvāmin's Locanarocanī; Viśvanātha Cakravartin's Ānandacandrikā) are NOT in this e-text.
texts.note: Single secure author. Named commentaries by Jīva and Viśvanātha exist in tradition but are absent from this root-only file; no conflation in the e-text.

texts.title: Haṃsadūta
texts.basis: GRETIL header attributes the Haṃsadūta to Rūpa Gosvāmī (no internal colophon in the file). Edition note cites Brzezinski's ed./trans. (Uddhava-sandeśa & Haṃsadūta of Rūpa Gosvāmī, 1999); Gauḍīya dūtakāvya tradition. Distinct from Vedānta Deśika's Haṃsasandeśa (different Śrīvaiṣṇava work).
texts.note: Gauḍīya messenger-poem by Rūpa Gosvāmī (16th c.). Title shared with other dūtakāvyas but this is the Rūpa text; single secure author.

texts.title: Cinmantramālinī
texts.current_author: Prakāśānanda (likely the 16th-c. author; identity uncertain for this title)
texts.basis: No source_edition; Muktabodha digital-library landing page only (no specific record retrieved). Web/secondary searches (wisdomlib, Muktabodha catalog) returned no confirmation of authorship for this title. current_author itself flags identity as uncertain.
texts.note: Obscure tantric mantra-text. Ascription to a Prakāśānanda is unverified and the author's identity (which of several Prakāśānandas) is undetermined; retained as low-confidence traditional ascription, not secure.

texts.title: Haribhaktirasāmṛtasindhu
texts.basis: GRETIL header credits Rūpa Gosvamin as author; internal colophons 'iti śrī-śrī-bhakti-rasāmṛta-sindhau'. Secondary literature (Klostermaier; Haberman 2003 ed./tr.; Potter's bibliography listing later commentaries on it) uniformly treats it as Rūpa's work. This GRETIL etext is the mūla only (the cited Haridāsa Dāsa ed. carries commentaries, not present here).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; secondary:cleaned_Kvinnliga gestalter_Chakra, 1, 2004.txt
texts.note: 16th-c. Gauḍīya foundational rasa-śāstra/bhakti work; author secure, no attribution dispute.

texts.title: Haribhaktivilāsa 1–11
texts.current_author: Gopāla Bhaṭṭa Gosvāmī (largely composed/redacted by Sanātana Gosvāmī)
texts.primary_author: Gopāla Bhaṭṭa Gosvāmin (named compiler); Sanātana Gosvāmin (traditional/actual composer-redactor)
texts.basis: GRETIL header and every MS colophon name Gopāla Bhaṭṭa ('iti śrī-gopāla-bhaṭṭa-vilikhite śrī-bhagavad-bhakti-vilāse'). Local secondary (Encyclopedia of Tantra, vol entry) explicitly: 'identity of the author is controversial... really a work of Sanātana Gosvamin who associated it with the name of Gopala Bhatta. There is an opinion that it was their joint work... authorship is doubtful.' Web (Brill critical ed. 2023 titled 'Haribhaktivilāsa of Sanātana Gosvāmin'; wisdomlib): Jīva Gosvāmin and Caitanyacaritāmṛta ascribe to Sanātana, who also authored the accompanying Digdarśinī ṭīkā.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt; web:https://brill.com/edcollchap-oa/book/9789004537651/BP000001.xml
texts.note: Multi-role: Gopāla Bhaṭṭa = named compiler in colophons; Sanātana = traditional actual composer/redactor and author of the Digdarśinī ṭīkā. Disputed (Bhaktiratnākara, Bhaktisiddhānta Sarasvatī, Purīdāsa give differing accounts); often called joint work.

texts.title: Śāktānandataraṅgiṇī
texts.basis: Edition (Tripāṭhī 1987) names Brahmānanda Giri. Local secondary (Encyclopaedia of Tantra vol.4): '[Śākt]ānandataraṅgiṇī by Brahmananda Giri (16 century)'; the work is cited as 'ST' authority in the Tantra encyclopedia. Muktabodha source.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopaedia of Tantra. Vol 4_Sadhu Santidev 1999.txt; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt; tradition
texts.note: 16th-c. Śākta nibandha by Brahmānanda Giri (Brahmānandagiri); attribution undisputed.

texts.title: Tārārahasya
texts.basis: Edition (Sarayūprasād Śāstrī 1960) names Brahmānanda Giri. Local secondary: 'Tararahasya of Brahmananda' (Encyclopedia of Tantra; Bhattacharyya, Indian Buddhist Iconography both cite 'the Tararahasya of Brahmananda'). Same author as the Śāktānandataraṅgiṇī.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt; secondary:cleaned_Bhattacharyya_Indian Buddhist Iconography.txt; tradition
texts.note: Tārā-worship manual; securely by Brahmānanda Giri (16th c.), cited as 'TR' in tantric nibandha literature.

texts.title: Vāruṇapaddhati
texts.basis: Not found in local reference-literature (no hits). Web (Muktabodha digital library notes / Śaiva Siddhānta survey): Vāruṇapaddhati (a.k.a. Ātmārthapūjāpaddhati) composed by Varuṇaśivācārya, culling from the Śaivasiddhāntāgamas; one MS carries a commentary attributed to the 16th-c. South-Indian teacher Nigamajñānadeva.
texts.note: Śaivasiddhānta ritual paddhati. Root paddhati by Varuṇaśivācārya; an accompanying ṭīkā ascribed to Nigamajñānadeva — distinct author/role, hence conflation flag.

texts.title: Muktācarita
texts.basis: GRETIL header credits Raghunathadasa Gosvami ('Raghunathadasa Gosvami: Muktacarita'). Local secondary (Tantra in Practice, White ed.) places Raghunātha Dāsa among the Vṛndāvana Gosvāmins alongside Jīva and Gopāla Bhaṭṭa; the small Muktā-carita allegory is traditionally his. No competing attribution found.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Tantra in Practice_David Gordon White, ed, 2000_a.txt; tradition
texts.note: Short Gauḍīya allegorical kāvya (the 'pearl' narrative); securely by Raghunāthadāsa Gosvāmin (16th–17th c.). GRETIL etext is mūla only.

texts.title: Caitanyacandrāmṛta
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Prabodhananda Sarasvati Gosvami: Caitanyacandramrta'. Web (Vaniquotes, Gosai, scholarly) confirm Prabodhānanda authored it; a separate, unrelated debate concerns whether the man Prabodhānanda was identical to the Advaitin Prakāśānanda Sarasvatī — that touches his biography, not the text's authorship.
texts.note: Compilation of 12 śatakas of verses by a single author. Note: identity-debate (= Prakāśānanda Sarasvatī?) is biographical, not an authorship dispute over the text; attribution to Prabodhānanda is secure.

texts.title: Śaivasiddhāntaparibhāṣā
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Literature about the Philosophical Systems...) lists the Śaivaparibhāṣā among Śivāgrayogin's Śaiva Siddhānta works alongside his Śivāgrabhāṣya on Sivajñānabodha and other commentaries; Jayendra Soni studies Śaivasiddhānta 'with reference to Śivāgrayogin's commentaries'. No competing attribution found.
texts.source: secondary:reference-literature/cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: 16th-c. Śaiva Siddhānta digest, single author Śivāgrayogin.

texts.title: Laghubhāgavatāmṛta
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Rupa Gosvami: Laghubhagavatamrta'. Well-known Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava work of Rūpa Gosvāmin (16th c.). The GRETIL e-text is the root text only (no commentary), so no multi-author structure here.
texts.note: Root text only in this e-text; later commentaries (e.g. Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa) are not included, so no conflation.

texts.title: Tattvacintāmaṇi (Śrītattvacintāmaṇi), chapters 1-5
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra) repeatedly names Pūrṇānanda as author of the Śrī-tattva-cintāmaṇi and lists it among his Tantric works (Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa, Śyāmārahasya, Tattvānanda-taraṅgiṇī, etc.). This Tantric Śrītattvacintāmaṇi is DISTINCT from Gaṅgeśa's Navya-Nyāya Tattvacintāmaṇi — no conflation between them.
texts.note: Beware homonymy: NOT Gaṅgeśa's Nyāya Tattvacintāmaṇi. The edition's commentary (Cintāmaṇi Bhaṭṭācārya) is a separate modern hand but not part of the root e-text scope.

texts.title: Āśaucadīpikā
texts.basis: Web (Stanford SearchWorks; IFP/Śaivasiddhānta studies by T. Ganesan) confirm Nigamajñāna II, disciple-nephew of Nigamajñāna I, was a 16th-c. Śaiva Siddhānta teacher at Cidambaram who composed numerous Sanskrit/Tamil works; 'Vedajñāna' is the Sanskritized form of the dīkṣā name Nigamajñāna. Āśaucadīpikā (on ritual impurity) is among his attributed Śaiva works; IFP/Muktabodha e-text from Pondicherry MSS.
texts.note: Vedajñāna II = Nigamajñāna II (same person). No critical edition; attribution rests on Śaivasiddhānta tradition and IFP MS scholarship; confidence medium pending direct colophon.

texts.title: Gopālacampū: Pūrvacampū, 1-11
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'Jiva Gosvamin: Gopalacampu', input by Jan Brzezinski; colophons read 'iti śrī-śrī-gopāla-campūm anu ... pūraṇam'. Standard Gauḍīya campū-kāvya of Jīva Gosvāmin (16th c.). E-text is the campū text itself, no commentary included.
texts.note: Pūrvacampū pūraṇas 1-11, single author Jīva Gosvāmin; no commentary conflation in this e-text.

texts.title: Gopālacampū: Pūrvacampū, 23-33
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Jīva Gosvāmin; text self-identifies as Śrī Śrī Gopāla Campū. Gopālacampū is a well-known Gauḍīya campū-kāvya by Jīva Gosvāmin (16th c.); no competing attribution. Local lit and web confirm Jīva as sole author.
texts.note: Pūrvacampū, cantos 23-33; single-author campū-kāvya, no commentary bundled in this e-text.

texts.title: Siddhāntabindu
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'siddhāntabindunāmā granthaḥ' composed by Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, disciple of Viśveśvara Sarasvatī (ed. Divanji, Baroda 1933, input Takahiro Kato). Secondary lit (Potter EIP; Radhakrishnan Source Book) describes Siddhāntabindu as Madhusūdana's commentary on Śaṅkara's Daśaślokī. Named sub-commentaries exist (Brahmānanda Sarasvatī's Nyāyaratnāvalī; Nārāyaṇa Tīrtha's Laghuvyākhyā; Puruṣottama Sarasvatī's) but are NOT included in this e-text.
texts.note: Bindu is itself a ṭīkā on Śaṅkara's Daśaślokī, but the GRETIL file is the Madhusūdana bindu-text alone; not conflated in this edition. Distinct sub-commentators (Brahmānanda, Nārāyaṇa Tīrtha) belong to separate works.

texts.title: Gopālacampū: Pūrvacampū, 12-22
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Jīva Gosvāmin; ed. Purīdāsa 1947, input Jan Brzezinski. Same campū-kāvya as the 23-33 segment; uniformly attributed to Jīva Gosvāmin.
texts.note: Pūrvacampū, cantos 12-22; single-author, no bundled commentary.

texts.title: Śivārcanacandrikā
texts.basis: Edition (Śāradāvilāsa Press, Kumbakonam 1922) names Appayya Dīkṣita; no source_url. Web (academic studies of Nīlakaṇṭha Dīkṣita's Saubhāgyacandrātapa; Bronner JAOS) confirm Śivārcanacandrikā as Appayya's Śaiva ritual manual, cited by his descendant Nīlakaṇṭha Dīkṣita.
texts.note: Śaiva pūjā/ritual paddhati by the 16th c. polymath Appayya Dīkṣita; secure, no dispute.

texts.title: Nāṭakacandrikā
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'iti dhvani-prasthāpana-paramācārya-śrīmad-rūpa-gosvāmi-prabhupāda-praṇītā śrī-nāṭaka-candrikā samāptā' — explicitly composed by Rūpa Gosvāmin. Standard ed. Purīdāsa 1949.
texts.note: Dramaturgical treatise by the Gauḍīya theorist Rūpa Gosvāmin; colophon attribution unambiguous.

texts.title: Vairāgyaśataka
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'śrīmadappayadīkṣitaviracitaṃ vairāgyaśatakam' at both opening and close (Kāvyamālā vol. 1, 1886; input Dhaval Patel). Web confirms Appayya Dīkṣita composed his own Vairāgyaśataka among his ~25-40 stotras, a DISTINCT work from Bhartṛhari's famous homonymous Vairāgyaśataka.
texts.note: Title is shared with Bhartṛhari's celebrated Vairāgyaśataka, but this is Appayya Dīkṣita's own distinct devotional/vairāgya stotra; attribution to Appayya is explicit in the edition's colophon. No conflation.

texts.title: Yoginītantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Śiva–Pārvatī dialogue; no human author named)
texts.basis: No source_url. Web: Śākta Yoginītantra is an anonymous Assam/Kāmarūpa tantra (Śiva–Pārvatī dialogue, 28 chapters in 2 parts, worship of Kālī/Kāmākhyā), composed c. 16th–17th c. CE; no author named. Local lit hits for 'yogini-tantra' mostly concern the Buddhist yoginītantra genre, not this text.
texts.note: Anonymous Śākta scripture of Kāmarūpa (Assam); traditionally framed as Śiva's teaching, no historical author. Dated ~16th/17th c. Distinct from Buddhist yoginītantra class.

texts.title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 5. Bhaktisandarbha
texts.primary_author: Jīva Gosvāmī (author/expander), based on preliminary notes of Gopāla Bhaṭṭa Gosvāmī
texts.basis: GRETIL header: author Jīva Gosvāmī, title Bhakti-sandarbha (part 5 of Ṣaṭsandarbha). Secondary (Freschi & Maas, Adaptive Reuse): 'Jīva mentions that his Sandarbhas are based on Gopāla Bhaṭṭa'. Web (sandarbhas.jiva.org; Wikipedia Sat Sandarbhas): Gopāla Bhaṭṭa did preliminary/incomplete work; Jīva expanded, systematized, edited and published the six Sandarbhas by order of Rūpa and Sanātana.
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse_Aspects of Creativity in South Asian Cultural History.txt | web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sat_Sandarbhas
texts.note: Securely Jīva's work but rests on Gopāla Bhaṭṭa Gosvāmī's antecedent notes (Jīva states this himself); two roles -> conflation_flag true (Gopāla Bhaṭṭa source/compiler, Jīva author-redactor).

texts.title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 6. Prītisandarbha
texts.primary_author: Jīva Gosvāmī (author/expander), based on preliminary notes of Gopāla Bhaṭṭa Gosvāmī
texts.basis: GRETIL header: author Jīva Gosvāmī, title Prīti-sandarbha (part 6 of Ṣaṭsandarbha). Same multi-role basis as Bhaktisandarbha: Jīva's own statement that the Sandarbhas derive from Gopāla Bhaṭṭa's preliminary work, which Jīva expanded/edited/published (Freschi & Maas; sandarbhas.jiva.org; Wikipedia).
texts.source: gretil-header | secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse_Aspects of Creativity in South Asian Cultural History.txt | web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sat_Sandarbhas
texts.note: Final/sixth Sandarbha. Jīva is author-redactor; built on Gopāla Bhaṭṭa Gosvāmī's antecedent notes. Two roles -> conflation_flag true.

texts.title: Rādhākṛṣṇārcanadīpikā
texts.basis: GRETIL header/content: colophon 'śrī-vṛndāvana-vāsinaḥ kasyacij jīvasya' (a certain Jīva residing in Vrindavan); Jīva refers to this work as Kṛṣṇārcana-dīpikā in his own Brahmasaṃhitā commentary (cross-reference confirms self-authorship). Edition pairs it with Jīva's Brahmasaṃhitā-ṭīkā.
texts.note: Independent ritual manual self-attributed by Jīva in the colophon; not part of the Sandarbha/Gopāla Bhaṭṭa complex, so no conflation. The accompanying Brahmasaṃhitā-ṭīkā is also Jīva's.

texts.title: Śaktisaṃgamatantra, Kālīkhaṇḍa
texts.primary_author: anonymous (Śākta compendium; Kālīkhaṇḍa is khaṇḍa 1 of 4)
texts.basis: source_url = archive.org scan of B. Bhattacharyya's 1932 GOS edition; no human author. Web (wisdomlib; indictoday): Śaktisaṃgamatantra is a voluminous Śākta compendium in four khaṇḍas — Kālī, Tārā, Sundarī, Chinnamastā (cf. N.N. Bhattacharyya, History of Tantric Religion: 'four parts: Kālī, Tārā, Sundarī, Chinnamastā... composed...'); claimed 60,000 ślokas, anonymous, framed as revealed tantra. Kālīkhaṇḍa = first khaṇḍa (20 chapters).
texts.source: web:https://www.wisdomlib.org/definition/shaktisangamatantra | secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion_N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt
texts.note: Anonymous revealed Śākta tantra; Kālīkhaṇḍa is one (the first) of four khaṇḍas of a large composite compendium (likely late, c. 16th–18th c.). No named author. Composite of khaṇḍas but all anonymous, so no author-conflation flagged.

texts.title: Vallālacarita
texts.basis: Edition (HP Sastri, Bibliotheca Indica 1901–04) and GRETIL e-lib name Ānandabhaṭṭa as author. Secondary (cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt): "Anandabhaṭṭa's Vallālacarita records that, when king Vallālasena inquired with his teacher Bhaṭṭa Simhagiri..." — Ānandabhaṭṭa cited as the author. GRETIL PDF is image-only (text not extractable). Traditionally composed 1510 CE at the request of the minister Buddhimanta Khan; a late (16th-c.) chronicle of king Vallālasena, of debated historical reliability but undisputed single authorship.
texts.note: Single author Ānanda Bhaṭṭa (c. 1510 CE). Note: value as a historical source on the 12th-c. Sena king Vallālasena is debated, but the attribution to Ānandabhaṭṭa is secure; not pseudepigraphic.

texts.title: Śaktisaṃgamatantra: Tārākhaṇḍa
texts.basis: Śaktisaṃgamatantra is a voluminous anonymous Tantric compendium in four books (Kālī, Tārā, Sundarī, Chinnamastā); the Tārākhaṇḍa is its second book. N.N. Bhattacharyya (History of the Tantric Religion) dates it 16th-17th c.; Ganganatha Jha's bibliography and Bhattacharyya describe it as a compendium with no named author. Ed. Benoytosh Bhattacharyya, GOS 91, 1941.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion An Historical, Ritualistic, and Philosophical Study_N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt
texts.note: Anonymous Tantric digest, 16th-17th c.; Tārākhaṇḍa = book 2 of 4. No author named; remains anonymous/traditional.

texts.title: Bhāvaprakāśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header names author Bhāvamiśra (ed. 1947, input Oliver Hellwig). Meisezahl and other secondary literature cite 'Bhāvaprakāśa von Bhāvamiśra (16. Jahrh.)'. NB: distinct from the homonymous Bhāvaprakāśana of Śāradātanaya (a dramaturgical work) noted in Freschi/Maas.
texts.note: Standard Ayurvedic nighaṇṭu/compendium securely by Bhāvamiśra, 16th c. Not to be confused with Śāradātanaya's Bhāvaprakāśana.

texts.title: Ṣaṭcakranirṇaya (Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa)
texts.basis: Multiple sources confirm the Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa is the sixth chapter (Prakāśa) of the Śrītattvacintāmaṇi composed by Paramahaṃsa Pūrṇānanda, a 16th-c. Bengali sādhaka. Avalon/Woodroffe's Serpent Power edits it under Pūrṇānanda. Web (wisdomlib, MLBD/Sankhyatirtha ed.) confirms.
texts.note: Resolves the conditional in current_author: securely by Pūrṇānanda; the Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa = ch. 6 of his Śrītattvacintāmaṇi (16th c.).

texts.title: Rasaprakāśasudhākara
texts.basis: GRETIL header names author Yaśodhara (ed. Jīvram Kālidās Śāstri, Gondal 1940; input Oliver Hellwig); the ch.2 colophon-like verse self-identifies the work. Dan Martin's bibliography lists 'Rasaprakāśasudhākara' (Yaśodhara) on mercury and metals. An alchemical (rasaśāstra) treatise, ca. 13th c.
texts.note: Securely Yaśodhara (Bhaṭṭa); rasaśāstra work on mercury/metals.

texts.title: Kulavṛtti
texts.basis: The Kulavṛtti is Pūrṇānanda(giri)'s vṛtti (commentary) on the anonymous Kulacūḍāmaṇitantra; printed in Kulacūḍāmaṇi Tantra, Tantrik Texts vol. 4 (ed. Girīśacandra Vedāntatīrtha, 1915). The root tantra is anonymous, dated by Finn (per Samuel, Origins of Yoga and Tantra) to the 9th-11th c.; the commentary is a later, separately-authored layer. Thus the title bundles a named commentary atop an anonymous mūla.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Origins of Yoga and Tantra. Indic Religions to the Thirteenth Century_Geoffrey Samuel, 2008 (2010).txt
texts.note: Root vs commentary distinction: Kulacūḍāmaṇitantra (anonymous, 9th-11th c.) carries the Kulavṛtti commentary by Pūrṇānanda(giri). current_author names only the commentator; flagged conflation for the two layers.

texts.title: Dīkṣādarśa
texts.basis: Sanderson (The Śaiva Age) lists the Dīkṣādarśa among the works of Vedajñānaguru II (a south-Indian Śaivasiddhānta author/Cidambaram milieu) alongside the Ātmārthapūjāpaddhati and Śaivāgamaparibhāṣāmañjari, composed in the second half of the 16th century.
texts.note: Śaivasiddhānta dīkṣā manual; securely by Vedajñānaguru II, latter half 16th c. (Sanderson).

texts.title: Vīramitrodaya: Samayaprakāśa
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon: 'śrīmanmitramiśrakṛte śrīvīramitrodayābhidhanibandhe samayaprakāśe' names Mitramiśra as author; he was at the court of Pratāparudra (Vīrasiṃha). Secondary lit (Bhattacharyya, History of Tantric Religion; Scharfe, Education in Ancient India) confirms Mitramiśra, 17th c., composed the Vīramitrodaya digest. The Samayaprakāśa is one of its sections.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion_N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt; secondary:cleaned_Education In Ancient India_Scharfe 2002.txt
texts.note: Colophon names Mitramiśra; Vīramitrodaya is a large dharmanibandha, Samayaprakāśa = the kāṇḍa on calendrical/ritual time. Single author, secure.

texts.title: Yogasārasaṃgraha
texts.basis: Edition (Jha 1894) and standard catalogues (Literature about the Philosophical Systems, Potter EIP) list the Yogasārasaṃgraha among Vijñānabhikṣu's Yoga works alongside his Yogavārttika; Vijñānabhikṣu (16th c.) is the recognized Sāṃkhya-Yoga author. No competing attribution found.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web: edition Ganganatha Jha 1894
texts.note: A short prose epitome of Yoga doctrine by Vijñānabhikṣu; secure single authorship.

texts.title: Śaivasiddhāntaparibhāṣāmañjarī
texts.basis: Dagens (ed./trans. 1979, IFI 60) titles the work Śaivāgamaparibhāṣāmañjarī de Vedajñāna. Sanderson, The Śaiva Age: Vedajñānaguru II (= Nigamajñāna II), Cidambaram, second half of 16th c., author of the Ātmārthapūjāpaddhati and other Śaivasiddhānta works; nephew/disciple of Vedajñāna I. Web (academia/IFP) confirms Vedajñāna II as author.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_The Śaiva Age.txt; secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism_Goudriaan 1992.txt; web: edition Bruno Dagens 1979
texts.note: Proper title is Śaivāgama-paribhāṣāmañjarī (workitem 'Śaivasiddhānta-' is a variant). Author = Vedajñānaguru II / Nigamajñāna II of Cidambaram, 16th c. Secure.

texts.title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 1. Tattvasandarbha
texts.primary_author: Jīva Gosvāmī (redactor/author); based on Gopāla Bhaṭṭa's original notes
texts.basis: The Tattvasandarbha itself opens by acknowledging that Gopāla Bhaṭṭa (kinsman of Rūpa and Sanātana) had 'vivicya vyalikhad granthaṃ' — extracted and wrote the original draft from the ancients — which Jīva then rewrote in proper order (GRETIL header content; web: Sat Sandarbhas Wikipedia, jiva.org, bhaktitattva.com; Freschi & Maas, Adaptive Reuse). Standard scholarship (Elkman, Jiva Gosvamin's Tattvasandarbha) treats authorship as Gopāla Bhaṭṭa's preliminary compilation expanded/systematized by Jīva. GRETIL text carries Baladevavidyābhūṣaṇa's Ṭippanī (later commentary).
texts.source: gretil-header; web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sat_Sandarbhas; secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse.txt; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Conflation of roles: original notes/draft by Gopāla Bhaṭṭa Gosvāmī, rewritten and expanded into the systematic Sandarbha by Jīva Gosvāmī (who is the substantive author). Edited text also bundles Baladeva's Ṭippanī (commentator), a distinct hand.

texts.title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 4. Kṛṣṇasandarbha
texts.primary_author: Jīva Gosvāmī (redactor/author); based on Gopāla Bhaṭṭa's original notes
texts.basis: Same Ṣaṭsandarbha provenance: Gopāla Bhaṭṭa Gosvāmī made the initial collection of references which Jīva Gosvāmī rewrote and completed into the six Sandarbhas (web: Sat Sandarbhas Wikipedia, jiva.org; Freschi & Maas, Adaptive Reuse). The Kṛṣṇasandarbha (4th) is substantively Jīva's composition. Has a commentary by Jīva himself/successors noted in catalogues (Literature about the Philosophical Systems).
texts.source: web:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sat_Sandarbhas; secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse.txt; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Same conflation pattern as Tattvasandarbha: Gopāla Bhaṭṭa's foundational notes vs Jīva Gosvāmī's authorial redaction/composition. Jīva is the substantive author of this volume.

texts.title: Tattvacintāmaṇi (Śrītattvacintāmaṇi), chapters 16 to end
texts.basis: Edition (Sāṅkhyatīrtha, Calcutta Sanskrit Series 1936-37) attributes the Śrītattvacintāmaṇi to Pūrṇānanda, with a separate commentary by Cintāmaṇi Bhaṭṭācārya. Web (wisdomlib; Banglapedia/Tantra) confirms Pūrṇānanda, celebrated Bengali Tantric sādhaka (disciple of Brahmānanda, late 16th c.), author of the Śrītattvacintāmaṇi and the Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa. Workitem covers only the root text (chs 16-end), i.e. Pūrṇānanda alone, not the commentary.
texts.source: web:https://www.wisdomlib.org/concept/swami-purnananda; web: Banglapedia Tantra; edition Bhuvanmohan Sāṅkhyatīrtha 1936-37
texts.note: Distinct from Gaṅgeśa's Nyāya Tattvacintāmaṇi (title collision, not same work). Root text by Pūrṇānanda; the edition's commentary (Cintāmaṇi Bhaṭṭācārya) is a separate hand but not part of this workitem's chapters 16-end scope. Secure for the root text.

texts.title: Tantrarājatantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (revealed Āgama; Manoramā commentary by Subhagānandanātha)
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib/sanskritebooks): the root Tantrarāja is an anonymous revealed Tantra of the Śrīcakra/Kādimata tradition; the running Sanskrit commentary Manoramā is by Subhagānandanātha of the Kashmir school. Edition is the Tantric Texts series (Avalon/Woodroffe), ed. Lakṣmaṇa Śāstrī.
texts.note: Root tantra anonymous (traditionally a divinely-revealed Āgama); named human author belongs to the Manoramā commentary, not the work item (root text).

texts.title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 2. Bhāgavatasandarbha
texts.primary_author: Jīva Gosvāmī (author/redactor of final form, based on preliminary notes of Gopāla Bhaṭṭa)
texts.basis: Secondary (Freschi & Maas, Adaptive Reuse): 'Jīva mentions that his Sandarbhas are based on Gopāla Bhaṭṭa['s notes]'. Web (Wikipedia Sat Sandarbhas; jiva.org): Gopāla Bhaṭṭa did preliminary/incomplete compilation; Jīva reorganized, completed, expanded and published the six Sandarbhas. GRETIL colophon names Jīva.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse_Aspects of Creativity in South Asian Cultural History.txt
texts.note: Single title conflates two persons: Gopāla Bhaṭṭa (original/preliminary compilation) and Jīva Gosvāmī (final author-redactor). Jīva is the dominant author but the work explicitly builds on Gopāla Bhaṭṭa's material.

texts.title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 3. Paramātmasandarbha
texts.primary_author: Jīva Gosvāmī (author/redactor of final form, based on preliminary notes of Gopāla Bhaṭṭa)
texts.basis: Same as Bhāgavatasandarbha: the entire Ṣaṭsandarbha set was begun/compiled in preliminary form by Gopāla Bhaṭṭa and completed, reordered and expanded by Jīva Gosvāmī (Freschi & Maas; Wikipedia; jiva.org). GRETIL colophon names Jīva.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse_Aspects of Creativity in South Asian Cultural History.txt
texts.note: Conflation as for part 2: Gopāla Bhaṭṭa (preliminary compilation) + Jīva Gosvāmī (final author-redactor). A commentarial tradition (Gopālatoṣaṇī etc.) is later and not part of this work item.

texts.title: Tattvacintāmaṇi (Śrītattvacintāmaṇi), chapters 11-15
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib; MLBD; Google Books 'Pūrṇānanda's Śrītattvacintāmaṇi'): a 26-chapter (Prakāśa) Tantric verse work by the 16th-c. Bengali sādhaka Paramahaṃsa Pūrṇānanda; ed. Bhuvanmohan Sāṅkhyatīrtha with the comm. by Cintāmaṇi Bhaṭṭācārya. Distinct from Gaṅgeśa's Navya-Nyāya Tattvacintāmaṇi.
texts.note: Secure. The editor's modern commentary/notes (Cintāmaṇi Bhaṭṭācārya, Bhuvanmohan Sāṅkhyatīrtha) are editorial apparatus, not co-authorship of the root text. Not the homonymous Nyāya work by Gaṅgeśa.

texts.title: Sāṅkhyasāra
texts.basis: GRETIL header/content colophon: 'iti śrīvijñānabhikṣuviracite sāṅkhyasāre' throughout, confirming authorship. Editio princeps by Fitzedward Hall (Bibliotheca Indica, 1862). Well-known 16th-c. Sāṃkhya-Yoga author.
texts.note: Secure single authorship; colophon repeatedly names Vijñānabhikṣu. Two-part structure (pūrva-/uttara-bhāga) is internal, not multi-author.

texts.title: Gopālacampū: Uttaracampū, 5-6
texts.basis: GRETIL header/colophon attributes the Gopālacampū (Uttaracampū) repeatedly to Jīva Gosvāmin. Secondary (Encyclopedia of Tantra): 'The Gopāla-campū of Jīva Gosvāmin'. Standard Kāvyamālā 79 edition (Durgāprasāda & Paraba, 1892).
texts.note: Secure. Single-author campū kāvya by Jīva Gosvāmin (same author as the Sandarbhas, but here without the Gopāla Bhaṭṭa preliminary-notes layer, so no conflation).

texts.title: Gopālacampū: Uttaracampū, 1-3
texts.basis: GRETIL header titles it śrī-śrī-gopāla-campūḥ/uttara-campūḥ and credits Jīva Gosvāmin; secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra) refers to 'the Gopāla-campū of Jīva Gosvāmin'. The Uttaracampū is the second of the work's two campū halves, both by Jīva.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt
texts.note: 16th-c. Gauḍīya. Single-author prose-verse campū; no commentary layer in this file.

texts.title: Gopālacampū: Uttaracampū, 29
texts.basis: GRETIL header credits Jīva Gosvāmin; chapter colophon 'iti śrī-śrīmaduttara-gopāla-campūmanu ... ekonatriṃśaṃ pūraṇam ||29||' confirms it is pūraṇa 29 of the same Uttaracampū. Same author as the rest of the campū.
texts.note: Chapter 29 (bhāvi-kathā-pramāṇa-prathanam) of Jīva's Uttaracampū; single author.

texts.title: Saṅkalpakalpadruma
texts.basis: GRETIL file (named jivskd, placed under Jīva Gosvāmin) opens with śrī-rādhā-dāmodarābhyāṃ namaḥ. A homonymous Saṅkalpa-kalpadruma is widely attributed to Viśvanātha Cakravartī (Amazon/Internet Archive eds., ed. Kṛṣṇadeva Sarvabhauma); both authors composed works of this title (Jīva's described as a aṣṭa-kālīya-līlā saṅkalpa-prayer). GRETIL/edition assigns this text to Jīva.
texts.note: Title is shared by two distinct Gauḍīya authors (Jīva Gosvāmin and Viśvanātha Cakravartī); not a single conflated work but a homonym. This file follows the Jīva attribution; alternate Viśvanātha attribution noted.

texts.title: Tarkasaṃgraha
texts.basis: GRETIL file (input Takuya Ono) is the root Tarkasaṃgraha ALONE, no Dīpikā; opening 'bālānāṃ sukhabodhāya kriyate tarkasaṃgrahaḥ' and closing colophon 'annaṃbhaṭṭena viduṣā' name Annambhaṭṭa. Secondary lit (Ganeri 2001; Bilimoria; Matilal) uniformly attribute the Tarkasaṃgraha to Annambhaṭṭa.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Philosophy in Classical India. The Proper Work of Reason_Jonardon Ganeri, 2001.txt
texts.note: Root text only in this file; ca. 17th c. Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika primer. No commentary conflated here.

texts.title: Tarkasaṃgraha
texts.primary_author: Annambhaṭṭa (mūla + Dīpikā); Nīlakaṇṭha (Dīpikāprakāśikā); N.S. Rāmānuja Tātācārya (Bālapriyā); Kṛṣṇa Tātācārya (Prasāraṇā)
texts.basis: GRETIL file (Sansknet) bundles the root Tarkasaṃgraha + Annambhaṭṭa's own auto-commentary Dīpikā + Nīlakaṇṭha's Dīpikāprakāśikā + N.S. Rāmānuja Tātācārya's (Navalapakkam) Bālapriyā + Śrī Kṛṣṇa Tātācārya's (Tiruppukkuli) Prasāraṇā sub-commentary (dated 1980). Secondary lit (Literature on Phil. Systems) lists exactly this edition: 'Edited with Dīpikā, Nīlakaṇṭha's Prakāśikā, N.S. Rāmānuja Tātācārya's Bālapriyā, and Tiruppukkuli Svāmi's...'.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: current_author 'Annambhaṭṭa' undercounts: this file conflates mūla + auto-Dīpikā (Annambhaṭṭa) with three later sub-commentaries by Nīlakaṇṭha, N.S. Rāmānuja Tātācārya, and Kṛṣṇa Tātācārya.

texts.title: Liṅgadhāraṇacandrikā
texts.basis: Muktabodha Vīraśaiva collection. Secondary lit (Literature on Phil. Systems) lists 'Liṅgadhāraṇacandrikā (Vīraśaiva)' ed./tr. M.R. Sakhare, and separately 'Commentary on Nandikeśvara's Liṅgadhāraṇacandrikā', confirming the root work is traditionally ascribed to Nandikeśvara. Web search confirms it as a Vīraśaiva treatise on liṅgadhāraṇa but did not independently re-confirm the author name.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt; web:https://www.indica.today/reviews/a-handbook-of-virashaivism-by-dr-sc-nandimath/
texts.note: Traditional Vīraśaiva ascription to Nandikeśvara (NB: this is the Vīraśaiva author, distinct from the legendary Nandikeśvara of nāṭyaśāstra/grammar). No source_edition; no colophon inspected (Muktabodha portal, not a plain e-text).

texts.title: Jñānaprakāśācārya: Pauṣkaravṛtti
texts.basis: Web (OCHS/Ganesan): Jñānaprakāśa of Śālivāṭi, Jaffna (c. late 16th–early 17th c.) is a known commentator on the Pauṣkarāgama jñānapāda; the Pauṣkaravṛtti is his Śaivasiddhānta commentary. Sanderson confirms the Pauṣkara(pārameśvara) as an anonymous scripture (the base text), distinct from this commentary. Item is the vṛtti only.
texts.note: vṛtti is by Jñānaprakāśa; the commented Pauṣkarāgama scripture itself is anonymous/revealed and not counted here since the item is the commentary. Crit. ed. Ganesan (IFP).

texts.title: Gūḍhārthadīpikā

authors.name: Kuśalamiśra (great-grandson of Kṣemaṅkara, son of Bālakṛṣṇa)
Kālidāsa (traditional ascription of the base Ghaṭakharparakāvya)

texts.basis: GRETIL header (Slaje 1993): commentary author is Kuśalamiśra, self-identified in colophon as 'Kṣemaṅkaraprapautreṇa Bālakṛṣṇasutena ca Kuśalākhyena'; base text Ghaṭakharparakāvya ascribed to mahākavi Kālidāsa (traditional). Colophon also names scribe Rājānāndarāma (Śaka 1748).
texts.note: Confirms Kuśalamiśra as commentary author (resolves 'uncertain' hint). Distinct base kāvya traditionally ascribed to Kālidāsa; scribe Rājānāndarāma named in colophon.

texts.title: Jñānaprakāśa (Śālivāṭipuranivāsi): Śivāgamādimāhātmya
texts.basis: Web: published as 'Śivāgamādimāhātmyasaṃgraha of Śālivāṭi Jñānaprakāśa' (Academia.edu/Scribd ed.); same Śaiva author of Jaffna (c. 1600). The -saṃgraha title marks it as a compilation/digest, hence compiler role.
texts.note: Same Jñānaprakāśa of Śālivāṭi as the Pauṣkaravṛtti; work is a māhātmya-saṃgraha (compilation).

texts.title: Tattvayāthārthyadīpana
texts.primary_author: Bhāvāgaṇeśa Dīkṣita (commentary); Tattvasamāsa pseudo-ascribed to Kapila (base)

authors.name: anonymous (Tattvasamāsa sūtras, pseudepigraphically ascribed to Kapila)

texts.basis: GRETIL header + web: Tattvayāthārthyadīpana is Bhāvāgaṇeśa's commentary on the Tattvasamāsa. Eliade/Sinha (secondary) and Potter note the base Tattvasamāsa is attributed to Kapila but is actually late (14th–15th c.), i.e. anonymous/pseudepigraphic. Commentary and root sūtras are by different hands.
texts.note: Commentary securely Bhāvāgaṇeśa; the commented Tattvasamāsa is pseudo-Kapila (anonymous, late). Flagged conflation of root text (pseudepigraphic) + vṛtti.

texts.title: Kathāratnākara
texts.basis: Mylius, Geschichte der altindischen Literatur: the Kathāratnākara ('Märchenmeer', 258 stories) is by Hemavijaya, c. 1600. Dundas (Jains) corroborates Hemavijaya as a Śvetāmbara Jain author (Vijayapraśasti etc.).
texts.note: Single secure author Hemavijayagaṇi, Śvetāmbara Jain, c. 1600.

texts.title: Kṛṣṇānanda Āgamavāgīśa: Bṛhattantrasāra
texts.basis: Goudriaan/Padoux (Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism): Kṛṣṇānanda's Tantrasāra (= Bṛhattantrasāra), 17th c., 'mahāmahopādhyāyaśrīkṛṣṇānandavāgīśabhaṭṭā(cārya)'; a Bengali tantric digest quoting many scriptures, hence compiler role.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Ritual and Speculation in Early Tantrism_Goudriaan
texts.note: Single secure author/compiler Kṛṣṇānanda Āgamavāgīśa, Bengal, 17th c.; work is a tantric digest (sāra).

texts.title: Rādhākṛṣṇāṣṭakālīyalīlāsmaraṇakramapaddhati
texts.basis: GRETIL header names Dhyānacandra Gosvāmī; closing colophon: 'iti śrī-dhyāna-candra-gosvāmi-viracitā ... smaraṇa-krama-paddhatiḥ'. Single 17th-c. Gauḍīya author, no commentarial layering.
texts.note: Colophon self-ascription confirms single author; part of his Gaura-Govindārcana-smaraṇa-paddhati.

texts.title: Rājānaka Lakṣmīrāma: Parātriśikāvivṛti
texts.primary_author: Rājānaka Lakṣmīrāma (vivṛti); Abhinavagupta (laghuvṛtti); anonymous root Parātriśikā (Rudrayāmala)
texts.basis: Edition title (KSTS 68-69, ed. Jagaddhara Zādoo) is 'Parātriśikā-laghuvritti by Abhinavagupta [+] Parātriśikā-vivriti of Rājānaka Lakṣmīrāma' (confirmed WorldCat). Secondary lit (Lit. about Phil. Systems) lists 'Vivṛti on Abhinavagupta's Paratrimsikalaghuvrtti, NCat I 202' under Lakṣmīrāma. Three layers: anonymous root Parātriśikā (concluding portion of Rudrayāmala-tantra), Abhinavagupta's Laghuvṛtti, Lakṣmīrāma's sub-commentary.
texts.note: current_author credits only Lakṣmīrāma; the published unit conflates the anonymous root verses, Abhinavagupta's Laghuvṛtti, and Lakṣmīrāma's vivṛti (sub-commentary on Abhinavagupta).

texts.title: Laghukaumudīvyākaraṇa
texts.basis: Varadarāja (pupil of Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita) authored the Laghu(siddhānta)kaumudī as an abridgement of his teacher Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita's Siddhāntakaumudī (Wikipedia/Siddhantakaumudi; sanskritfromhome). Same grammar as varlghku.
texts.note: Work = Varadarāja's abridgement; underlying Pāṇinian material/source work is Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita's Siddhāntakaumudī. Listed Bhaṭṭoji as source-author but not a conflation (abridgement is Varadarāja's distinct work).

texts.title: Nyāyaprakāśa (Mīmāṃsānyāyaprakāśa / Āpadevī)
texts.basis: Secondary lit (Alper, Understanding Mantras): 'Mīmāṃsānyāyaprakāśa of Apadeva (early seventeenth century)', a Kumārila/Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā introduction (= the Āpadevī). Lit. about Phil. Systems lists it under Bhaṭṭa school and notes P.K. Gode's study of Āpadeva the author.
texts.note: Single author Āpadeva, ca. early 17th c.; the bare title 'Nyāyaprakāśa' is ambiguous (a distinct Dvaita Nyāyaprakāśa exists), but this is the Bhāṭṭa-Mīmāṃsā Āpadevī. Attribution firm, not merely 'most likely'.

texts.title: Laghusiddhāntakaumudī [Laghukaumudī]
texts.basis: Same work as n3011u. Varadarāja's Laghusiddhāntakaumudī is the 'laghu' abridgement of Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita's Siddhāntakaumudī (he made madhya/laghu/sāra versions). Confirmed by Wikipedia (Varadarāja, Siddhantakaumudi) and Ballantyne ed. 'The Laghu Kaumudi of Varadaraja'.
texts.note: Varadarāja the abridger; source work by his teacher Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita (listed as source-author, not a conflation).

texts.title: Sādhanādīpikā
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon: 'iti ... rādhākṛṣṇa-dāsodīritā sādhana-dīpikā dvitīyā kakṣā' -> Rādhākṛṣṇadāsa Gosvāmī (mahanta of Govindadeva temple, Vrndavana, early 17th c.); web (Chakra 4, 2005 / Vrindavan Research) concurs. BUT a printed Hindi-translation edition (archive.org) is published as 'Sri Sadhana Dipika of Sri Narayana Bhatta Vrajacharya', and Kusumasarovara ed. (samvat 2022) names 'Vrajacarya Srisrinarayanabhattaji' as composer.
texts.note: Genuine attribution dispute: GRETIL colophon and most scholarship support Rādhākṛṣṇadāsa Gosvāmī; some Vraja editions ascribe it to Nārāyaṇa Bhaṭṭa. Not a multi-author conflation, but rival single-author ascriptions.

texts.title: tārārahasyavṛtti 2
texts.basis: Edition cited is the Tārārahasya of Brahmānandagiri (KSS 199, ed. Sarayuprasād Śāstrī). shivashakti.com and Encyclopedia of Tantra confirm the Tārārahasya is a tantric Tārā digest (4 paṭalas) compiled by Brahmānandagiri, of whom little is known; it is a compilation drawing on ~15 source tantras, not a verse+autocommentary structure. The 'vṛtti N' labels here index sections of that digest, not a separate commentator. CAUTION: a DISTINCT work titled Tārārahasyavṛtti is attributed by Sanderson (Śaiva Age) to Gauḍīya Śaṅkara (composed 1630) and noted in LVP — do not conflate it with this Brahmānandagiri text.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Tantra. 5 volumes_Sadhu Santideva, 1999.txt; web:https://shivashakti.com/tararas; secondary:cleaned_Sanderson_The Śaiva Age.txt
texts.note: Brahmānandagiri biographically obscure (hence traditional, not secure). Same-title homonym Tārārahasyavṛtti by Gauḍīya Śaṅkara (1630) is a different work; keep separate.

texts.title: Sudhālaharī
texts.basis: GRETIL header gives author 'paṇḍitarājaśrījagannāthaviracitā' and closing colophon 'iti paṇḍitarājaśrījagannāthaviracitā sudhālaharī samāptā'. Single-author stotra; no commentary embedded; attribution undisputed.
texts.note: Colophon explicitly names Paṇḍitarāja Jagannātha as composer; matches current attribution.

texts.title: Kaliviḍambana
texts.basis: GRETIL colophon 'iti Nīlakaṇṭhaviracitaṃ Kaliviḍambanaṃ saṃpūrṇam' and final verse 'kavinā nīlakaṇṭhena ... racitaṃ'. Bendall's BM Catalogue: 'Nilakantha Dikshita. Kalividambana'; Olivelle/CSL Three Satires attributes Kaliviḍambana to Nīlakaṇṭha. Single-author satirical śataka; undisputed.
texts.note: Author = Nīlakaṇṭha Dīkṣita (17th c.); colophon and catalogues agree.

texts.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 3, Adhikaraṇa 2
texts.primary_author: Khaṇḍadeva (Bhāṭṭadīpikā, on Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra; with Śambhubhaṭṭa's Prabhāvalī)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Khaṇḍadeva's Bhāṭṭadīpikā 'with Śambhubhaṭṭa's Prabhāvalī (subcomm.)'. E-text content embeds Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra root sūtras (e.g. Jaim_1,3.1) inside Khaṇḍadeva's running commentary; the edition includes Prabhāvalī. D.R. Bhandarkar Volume: Khaṇḍadeva (d. Benares 1665), author of Bhāṭṭadīpikā; pupil Śambhubhaṭṭa wrote the Prabhāvalī. Freschi/Maas discuss Khaṇḍadeva's Bhāṭṭadīpikā. Distinct authorial layers in one file.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_D. R. Bhandarkar Volume_Bimala Churn Law, ed., 1940.txt; secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse.txt
texts.note: Bhāṭṭadīpikā secure to Khaṇḍadeva, but e-text interweaves Jaimini's sūtras (root) and the Prabhāvalī sub-commentary (Śambhubhaṭṭa) — multi-author transmission.

texts.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 1, Adhikaraṇa 3
texts.primary_author: Khaṇḍadeva (Bhāṭṭadīpikā, on Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra; with Śambhubhaṭṭa's Prabhāvalī)
texts.basis: Verified e-text content of jsbh1-3u: contains Jaimini Mīmāṃsāsūtra root (Jaim_1,3.1, 1,3.3), Khaṇḍadeva's Bhāṭṭadīpikā commentary, and edition includes Śambhubhaṭṭa's Prabhāvalī sub-commentary (header). Adhikaraṇa markers 'iti prathamaṃ smṛtyadhikaraṇam', 'iti dvitīyaṃ virodhādhikaraṇam'. Same author triad as other Bhāṭṭadīpikā files (Bhandarkar Vol.; Freschi/Maas).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_D. R. Bhandarkar Volume_Bimala Churn Law, ed., 1940.txt; secondary:cleaned_Freschi and Maas_Adaptive Reuse.txt
texts.note: Same as 3.2: Khaṇḍadeva commentary embedding Jaimini's sūtras plus Śambhubhaṭṭa's Prabhāvalī apparatus.

texts.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 1, Adhikaraṇa 4
texts.primary_author: Khaṇḍadeva (Bhāṭṭadīpikā, on Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra; with Śambhubhaṭṭa's Prabhāvalī)
texts.basis: Same edition/series as sibling Bhāṭṭadīpikā files (Khaṇḍadeva, ed. Ananta Krishna Sastri, with Śambhubhaṭṭa's Prabhāvalī). E-text format embeds Jaimini's Mīmāṃsāsūtra root sūtras within Khaṇḍadeva's commentary. Khaṇḍadeva (d. 1665) author per D.R. Bhandarkar Volume; Śambhubhaṭṭa = his pupil and Prabhāvalī author.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_D. R. Bhandarkar Volume_Bimala Churn Law, ed., 1940.txt
texts.note: Multi-author layered text: Jaimini (sūtra) + Khaṇḍadeva (Bhāṭṭadīpikā) + Śambhubhaṭṭa (Prabhāvalī).

texts.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 2, Adhikaraṇa 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header: 'śrīmatkhaṇḍadeva­viracitā bhāṭṭadīpikā' + 'śrīkhaṇḍadevāntevāsika...śaṃbhubhaṭṭaviracita­prabhāvalī'; file content includes both root commentary and sub-commentary. Secondary (Lit. about Phil. Systems 22.1.34) confirms 'Khandadeva's Bhattadipika and Sambhubhatta's Prabhavali'.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: E-text conflates two authors. Bhāṭṭadīpikā (Mīmāṃsā commentary on Jaimini's sūtra) by Khaṇḍadeva (17th c.); Prabhāvalī sub-commentary by Śambhubhaṭṭa, pupil of Khaṇḍadeva. current_author omits Śambhubhaṭṭa.

texts.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 3, Adhikaraṇa 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header explicitly: 'Bhāṭṭadīpikā... with Sambhubhatta's Prabhavali (subcomm.)'; file content carries both. Secondary lit confirms the pairing of Khaṇḍadeva's root and Śambhubhaṭṭa's Prabhāvalī.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Same conflation as the other adhikaraṇa files: Khaṇḍadeva (author) + Śambhubhaṭṭa (Prabhāvalī ṭīkā). current_author lists only Khaṇḍadeva.

texts.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 2, Adhikaraṇa 3
texts.basis: Same GRETIL edition/header structure (Khaṇḍadeva's Bhāṭṭadīpikā with Śambhubhaṭṭa's Prabhāvalī); edition title confirms both texts are present. Secondary lit corroborates authorship.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Conflates root author Khaṇḍadeva and sub-commentator Śambhubhaṭṭa (his pupil). current_author omits Śambhubhaṭṭa.

texts.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 2, Adhikaraṇa 2
texts.basis: Same GRETIL edition (Bhāṭṭadīpikā with Prabhāvalī of Śambhubhaṭṭa); header/content include both works. Secondary lit confirms Khaṇḍadeva (root) and Śambhubhaṭṭa (Prabhāvalī).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Conflation of Khaṇḍadeva (author) and Śambhubhaṭṭa (Prabhāvalī). current_author lists only Khaṇḍadeva.

texts.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 2, Adhikaraṇa 2
texts.basis: Same GRETIL edition (Khaṇḍadeva's Bhāṭṭadīpikā with Śambhubhaṭṭa's Prabhāvalī). Filename jsbh1- vs displayed title 'Adhyāya 2' shows a labeling inconsistency in the workitem, but authorship structure is identical. Secondary lit confirms both authors.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Conflates Khaṇḍadeva (author) + Śambhubhaṭṭa (Prabhāvalī ṭīkā). NB: workitem title says Adhyāya 2 Adh. 2 but filename is jsbh1-2u (Adhyāya 1) — title/file mismatch, does not affect authorship.

texts.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 1, Adhikaraṇa 1
texts.basis: GRETIL header (this file): 'śrīmatkhaṇḍadevaviracitā bhāṭṭadīpikā' for the root and 'śrīkhaṇḍadevāntevāsika­kavimaṇḍana­śaṃbhubhaṭṭaviracita­prabhāvalī' for the sub-commentary; file includes both. Secondary lit (22.1.34) confirms Khaṇḍadeva's Bhāṭṭadīpikā + Śambhubhaṭṭa's Prabhāvalī, ed. N.S. Anantakrishna Sastri.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Explicit dual colophon attribution. Khaṇḍadeva = author of Bhāṭṭadīpikā (Mīmāṃsā, 17th c.); Śambhubhaṭṭa = author of Prabhāvalī, his disciple. current_author omits Śambhubhaṭṭa.

texts.title: Bhāṭṭadīpikā: Adhyāya 2, Adhikaraṇa 4
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Bhāṭṭadīpikā by Khaṇḍadeva, with Prabhāvalī subcommentary by Śambhubhaṭṭa. Secondary (D.R. Bhandarkar Vol. 1940; Potter EIP vol.1): Khaṇḍadeva (fl. ~1630, d. 1665) wrote the Bhāṭṭadīpikā (a Mīmāṃsā commentary on Jaimini's sūtra); his pupil Śambhubhaṭṭa wrote the Prabhāvalī commentary (1708).
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_D. R. Bhandarkar Volume_Bimala Churn Law, ed., 1940.txt; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 1 - Potter.txt
texts.note: Root Mīmāṃsā work by Khaṇḍadeva; the edition/etext bundles the Prabhāvalī of his pupil Śambhubhaṭṭa, a distinct author (flag set to separate root vs commentary). The text proper of this item is Khaṇḍadeva's.

texts.title: Rasagaṅgādhara
texts.basis: GRETIL header (inputter T.C. Cahill): Rasagaṅgādhara by Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja; this etext (Ānana 1) is the root work, no commentary embedded though the edition prints Nāgeśa Bhaṭṭa's and Mathurānāth Śāstrī's commentaries. Secondary (Potter EIP vol.5; Bendall catalogue; de Bary): Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja securely named as author of the Rasagaṅgādhara; Nāgeśa Bhaṭṭa wrote the Marmaprakāśa/commentary.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 5 - Potter 1990.txt; secondary:cleaned_Bendall_Catalogue...txt
texts.note: Root poetics (alaṃkāra) treatise; this item is the mūla by Jagannātha (mid-17th c.). Commentaries by Nāgeśa Bhaṭṭa and Mathurānāth Śāstrī belong to the edition but are not this etext's content, so no conflation flag.

texts.title: Gheraṇḍa-Saṃhitā
texts.primary_author: anonymous (traditionally ascribed to the sage Gheraṇḍa)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: no external authorial attribution; within the dialogue the teacher Gheraṇḍa instructs the student Caṇḍakāpāli. Secondary (White, Yoga in Practice: late/compilatory haṭhayoga text, early modern; Bharati, Tantric Tradition: also loosely ascribed to Matsyendranātha): the text is an anonymous compiled haṭhayoga saṃhitā framed as Gheraṇḍa's teaching, not authored by a historical Gheraṇḍa.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:cleaned_White_Yoga in Practice.txt; secondary:cleaned_Tantric Tradition_Agehananda Bharati, 1965.txt
texts.note: Pseudepigraphic frame: 'Gheraṇḍa' is the legendary in-text teacher, not a real author. Genuinely anonymous compilation (c. late 17th c.); traditional ascription to sage Gheraṇḍa recorded but no historical author.

texts.title: Devīnāmavilāsa
texts.basis: Source URL (Muktabodha devanagari etext) returned HTTP 404, so header not fetched. Secondary (Bharati, Tantric Tradition 1965; Potter EIP vol.1 lists Hanneder, 'Sāhib Kaul's presentation of Pratyabhijñā philosophy in Devīnāmavilāsa'): Devīnāmavilāsa is securely by the Kashmiri Śaiva/Śākta author Sāhib Kaul (17th c.). Bharati notes 'Sāhib Kaul' combines an Arab-Muslim title with the Kashmiri Brahmin name Kaul.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Tantric Tradition_Agehananda Bharati, 1965.txt; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 1 - Potter.txt
texts.note: Attribution to Sāhib Kaul confirmed by secondary literature (also editor Madhusudan Kaul Shastri, KSTS 63). Bharati flags that 'Sāhib Kaul' may be a high tantric title rather than strictly a proper name, but it is the consistently used author designation.

texts.title: Muktivāda
texts.basis: source_url is the GRETIL index (no dedicated page). Secondary: a Sanskrit etext colophon (cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt) reads 'atha śrīmahāmahopādhyāya-gadādhara-bhaṭṭācārya-viracito muktivādaḥ prārabhyate', explicitly naming Gadādhara Bhaṭṭācārya as author. Potter EIP vol.1 and Indian-philosophy bibliography list the Muktivāda among Gadādhara's vāda-grantha works (with later Vivṛti/Kroda/Ṭīkā commentaries by others).
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt; secondary:cleaned_Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. 1 - Potter.txt
texts.note: Navya-nyāya vāda by Gadādhara Bhaṭṭācārya (17th c.), confirmed by colophon. Numerous later commentaries (Mathurānātha's gloss, Vivṛti, Kroda, Ṭīkā) exist by other authors but are not this root text.

texts.title: Śrīvidyārṇavatantra
texts.basis: No source_url. Web (Kamakoti Mandali; manasataramgini blog; Exotic India): the encyclopedic Śrīvidyārṇava is traditionally ascribed to Śrī Vidyāraṇya Yati, but the identification is contested. South Indian Śrīvidyā tradition equates him with the famous Śṛṅgerī Vidyāraṇya (Sāyaṇa's brother, guru of Harihara/Bukka, fl. 1380s); other scholars argue for a DISTINCT northern/Himalayan Vidyāraṇya (lineage Viṣṇuśarman->Pragalbhācārya->Vidyāraṇya, dated as early as ~1070 CE).
texts.note: Author name 'Vidyāraṇya' is retained but the WHICH-Vidyāraṇya identification is disputed: likely NOT the celebrated Śṛṅgerī Advaitin commonly assumed, but a distinct (probably northern) tantric Vidyāraṇya Yati. Status downgraded from implied-secure to disputed; flagged as changed for this nuance.

texts.title: Merutantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (late Tantra, no historical author named)
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text gives no historical author (revelatory Tantra). Bhattacharyya, History of the Tantric Religion: 'Of the most recent Tantras, the Merutantra is an extensive' work that even mentions London/the English, i.e. an 18th-19th c. compilation with no named human author; cited repeatedly only as a scriptural source.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_History of the Tantric Religion ... N. N. Bhattacharyya 2006.txt
texts.note: Anonymous revelatory Tantra; very late (mentions the English/London). Traditionally framed as Siva's revelation; no individual author.

texts.title: Mokṣopāyaṭīkā
texts.basis: GRETIL header (ed. W. Slaje) gives self-naming verse 'avatārakaṇṭhaputraḥ pautro vaiḍūryakaṇṭhapādānām / bhāskarakaṇṭho racayati ... vyākhyām' and colophon 'iti śrībhāskarakaṇṭhaviracitāyāṃ śrīmokṣopāyaṭīkāyāṃ'. NCat / Potter bibliography lists 'Moksopayatika' and Slaje's edition (Bhaskarakantha's Moksopayatika) under the same Kashmirian author (18th c.).
texts.note: This is the commentary (ṭīkā) on the Mokṣopāya/Yogavāsiṣṭha, not the root; root Mokṣopāya is a separate anonymous work. Bhāskarakaṇṭha = son of Avatārakaṇṭha, grandson of Vaiḍūryakaṇṭha; ~early 18th c. Kashmir.

texts.title: Jānakī Pariṇaya
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib / Tanjore Descriptive Catalogue): Jānakīpariṇaya nāṭaka by Rāmabhadra Dīkṣita of the Kauṇḍinya family, son of Yajñarāma Dīkṣita, of Kaṇḍaramāṇikkā (Tanjore dist.), fl. late 17th c. under Śahaji of Tanjore; has commentary by Śvetāraṇya Nārāyaṇa Śarmā.
texts.note: Title is the nāṭaka by Rāmabhadra Dīkṣita; distinct from the Jānakīpariṇaya campū of Cakrakavi (separate work, not a co-authored conflation). NB: the GK19 source_url is a generic GRETIL index and the snippet content corresponds to Yogavāsiṣṭha verses, not the nāṭaka — attribution here follows the item title (Rāmabhadra Dīkṣita's nāṭaka).

texts.title: Narasiṃhaṭhakkura: Tārābhaktisudhārṇava
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text colophon: 'iti mahāmahopādhyāya ṭhakkura śrīnarasiṃha viracite tārābhakti sudhārṇave'. Sanderson, The Śaiva Age: 'the Tārābhaktisudhārṇava, a work in some 11,000 verses composed by Nṛsimha Thakkura c. 1688'; bibliography lists 'Tārābhaktisudhārṇava of Nṛsimha Thakkura'.
texts.note: Single author Nṛsiṃha/Narasiṃha Ṭhakkura, c. 1688. Editors (Pañcānana Tarkaratna under Arthur Avalon/Woodroffe) are modern, not authors. Confirms current attribution.

texts.title: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinīvyākhyā
