texts.current_author: uncertain (a commentator on the Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī; possibly the Bhāskarī of Bhāskarakaṇṭha)
texts.basis: Edition title 'Bhāskarī. A Commentary on the Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī of Abhinavagupta' (Iyer & Pandey). NCat / Potter bibliography (entry 1441) lists under one author: 'Bhaskari on Abhinavagupta's Isvarapratyabhijnavimarsini (NCat II, 276)' and 'Moksopayatika' — i.e. the Bhāskarī commentator is the same Bhāskarakaṇṭha who wrote the Mokṣopāyaṭīkā.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Resolves the 'uncertain' status: the vyākhyā is the Bhāskarī of Bhāskarakaṇṭha (early 18th c. Kashmir). The base text it comments on (Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī) is by Abhinavagupta, which itself comments on Utpaladeva's kārikās — but this item is specifically the sub-commentary (Bhāskarī), authored by Bhāskarakaṇṭha alone.

texts.title: Tantracintāmaṇi
texts.basis: Muktabodha e-text opening verse names 'navamī siṃhastvamātyaḥ' (Navamīsiṃha, minister of King Bhūpālendra) as compiler of the Śrītantracintāmaṇi; header alternate name 'ādyānanda'. Bharati, Tantric Tradition: 'Navasimha (also called Ādyānanda), Tantracintamani. The author was supposed to be a minister of the Nepalese King Bhūpālendra ... in the late' 17th c.
texts.note: Compendium (40 prakāśas) of Tantra excerpts compiled by Navamīsiṃha/Navasiṃha alias Ādyānanda, minister of Bhūpālendra Malla of Kathmandu (c. 1689-1694). Confirms current attribution.

texts.title: Kulamuktikallolinī
texts.basis: Muktabodha header: author Navamīsiṃha, alias Ādyānanda; text colophon-verse self-identifies as Ādyānanda ('ādyānanda ity eva khyātanāmā guror mukhāt'). Navamīsiṃha names himself elsewhere (Tantracintāmaṇi) as minister of King Bhūpālendra Malla of Nepal (late 17th c.). The two names denote one person.
texts.note: Navamīsiṃha and Ādyānanda are the same author (proper name vs initiatory name); not a conflation. Floruit late 17th c. Nepal/Kathmandu.

texts.title: Paribhāṣenduśekhara
texts.basis: GRETIL header opening verse names Nāgeśa ('nāgeśaḥ kurute sudhīḥ ... paribhāṣenduśekharam') and colophon reads 'iti śrīnāgeśabhaṭṭaviracite paribhāṣenduśekhare'. Bronkhorst 2003 monograph treats it throughout as Nāgeśa's work. The mūla text is by Nāgeśa alone; later commentaries (e.g. Bālamanoramā in the cited Kāśī ed.) are separate works not part of this e-text.
texts.source: gretil-header + secondary:cleaned_Tradition and Argument in Classical Indian Linguistics ... Bronkhorst 2003.txt
texts.note: Author of the mūla secure. Kielhorn/ Kāśī editions bundle commentaries by other hands, but this GRETIL e-text is the Nāgeśa mūla only.

texts.title: Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇavatantra with the commentary called Setubandha
texts.current_author: Commentary (Setubandha) by Bhāskararāya Makhin; mūla tantra anonymous/traditional
texts.primary_author: Bhāskararāya Makhin (Setubandha commentary); mūla Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava anonymous (part of Vāmakeśvaratantra)
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib, Wikipedia) and skt-en-mono: the Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava is the first part of the Vāmakeśvaratantra (with Yoginīhṛdaya as second part), an anonymous Śrīvidyā mūlāgama. The Setubandha is Bhāskararāya's commentary on it (17th-18th c.). Encyclopedia of Tantra repeatedly cites 'Setubandha commentary on the Nityaṣoḍaśikārṇava'. Anandāśrama ed. (Āgāśe 1908) prints mūla + Setubandha together.
texts.note: Two distinct authorial layers: anonymous root tantra (Vāmakeśvara) vs Bhāskararāya's Setubandha ṭīkā. Current_author already separates roles; conflation flagged because one title combines them.

texts.title: Bhāskararāya: Mahāyāgakrama
texts.basis: Title itself names Bhāskararāya as author; Mahāyāgakrama is a standard ritual manual attributed to Bhāskararāya Makhin (c.1690-1785), the prolific Śrīvidyā author also credited with Setubandha, Varivasyārahasya, etc. (Wikipedia/Bhaskararaya; D.R.Bhandarkar vol. dates his career early 18th c. to c.1768).
texts.note: Muktabodha source_url 404; attribution rests on title and well-attested Bhāskararāya corpus. Initiatory name Bhāsuranandanātha.

texts.title: Varivasyārahasya
texts.basis: Adyar ed. (Subrahmanya Sastri) = 'Varivasyā-rahasya of Bhāskararāya Makhin with his own commentary Prakāśa'. skt-en-mono cites 'Prakasa on Varivasya-rahasya' and 'Sri Bhaskararaya Maklin, Varivasya-Rahasya and its commentary Prakasa'; Yajneshwara Shastri and D.R.Bhandarkar vol. attribute it to Bhāskararāya.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt + secondary:cleaned_Traverses on Less Trodden Path ... Yajneshwara Shastri.txt + edition
texts.note: Both the verses (varivasyārahasya) and the auto-commentary Prakāśa are by Bhāskararāya; single author, two roles (kārikā + auto-vṛtti). Not a multi-author conflation.

texts.title: Gurustutiratnāvalī
texts.basis: Web (McGill eScholarship 'Govinda Kaula's Gurustutiratnāvalī and a Lineage of Devotion'; Brill IIJ 64.4 2021 'The Guru as Śiva'): 18th-c. Sanskrit hymn by Govinda Kaula of Kashmir praising Sāhib Kaula; Govinda Kaula was a pupil of Jyotiṣprakāśa Kaula (himself a pupil of Sāhib Kaula) and scribe of Śāradā mss in Berlin.
texts.note: Muktabodha source_url 404; attribution confirmed by recent scholarship (Brill IIJ 2021). 18th-c. Kashmiri Śaiva/Śākta author.

texts.title: Bodhasāra
texts.primary_author: Narahari (mūla); Divākara (commentary Dīpti, not in e-text)
texts.basis: GRETIL header: Bodhasāra by Narahari, ed. Svāmī Dayānanda; commentary Dīpti/Arthadīpa by Narahari's pupil Paṇḍit Divākara (NOT included in the GRETIL e-text). Local lit (Literature about Philosophical Systems) lists 'Arthadīpa on Narahari's Bodhasāra'.
texts.source: gretil-header; secondary:reference-literature/cleaned_Literature about the Philosophical Systems and Indian Philosophy.txt
texts.note: Root Vedānta treatise by Narahari (secure). The printed edition pairs it with the pupil Divākara's commentary (Dīpti/Arthadīpa) — distinct author; the GRETIL e-text contains only the mūla, no commentary.

texts.title: Mahānirvāṇa Tantra
texts.primary_author: anonymous (tantra); Hariharānanda Bhāratī (commentary, in printed ed.)
texts.basis: Web (Hindupedia, Avalon ed. prefaces): the Tantra itself is an anonymous/traditional revealed text (Śiva-Pārvatī dialogue), conventionally undated (often placed 18th c.); the printed Tantrik Texts ed. carries the Sanskrit commentary of Hariharānanda Bhāratī (preceptor of Rammohan Roy). Some scholars suggest the commentator may have reshaped/revised the text toward Vedānta.
texts.note: Tantra is anonymous (no historical author; traditionally revelation). Edition conflates the anonymous mūla with Hariharānanda Bhāratī's commentary — distinct, datable person; possible 18th-c. redaction by the commentarial milieu.

texts.title: Puraścaryārṇava, volume 1
texts.current_author: Pratāpasiṃha Śāha Deva (Pratap Singh Shah, King of Nepal)
texts.primary_author: Pratāpasiṃha Śāhadeva (royal author/patron of the digest)
texts.basis: Edition title (Muralīdhara Jhā ed.) and Wellcome catalogue: 'Nepālamahārājādhirāja-pratāpasiṃhasāhadeva-viracitaḥ'. Web: 18th-c. Nepālī tantric digest (paddhati) attributed to King Pratāpasiṃha Śāha; as a vast compendium of ritual it compiles/quotes many sources, the king being the named royal author-patron (work likely executed with court pandits).
texts.note: Royal-author/patron attribution (viracita = composed by King Pratāpasiṃha). Genre is a tantric digest, so much material is compiled from earlier tantras; authorship is single-named and traditional rather than a multi-author conflation.

texts.title: Puraścaryārṇava, volume 3
texts.current_author: Pratāpasiṃha Śāha Deva (Pratap Singh Shah, King of Nepal)
texts.primary_author: Pratāpasiṃha Śāhadeva (royal author/patron of the digest)
texts.basis: Same work as vol. 1, Part III (Muralīdhara Jhā ed., 1904). Attributed by edition title and catalogues to King Pratāpasiṃha Śāhadeva of Nepal; 18th-c. tantric ritual digest.
texts.note: Volume 3 of the same digest; same single traditional royal attribution. Compendium genre (quotes many sources) but conventionally a one-author work.

texts.title: Puraścaryārṇava, volume 2
texts.current_author: Pratāpasiṃha Śāha Deva (Pratap Singh Shah, King of Nepal)
texts.primary_author: Pratāpasiṃha Śāhadeva (royal author/patron of the digest)
texts.basis: Same work as vols. 1 and 3, Part II (Muralīdhara Jhā ed., 1902). Attributed by edition title and catalogues to King Pratāpasiṃha Śāhadeva of Nepal; 18th-c. tantric ritual digest (paddhati).
texts.note: Volume 2 of the same digest; same single traditional royal attribution.

texts.title: Prāṇatoṣiṇī (Kāmyakāṇḍa)
texts.basis: Edition: compiled by Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya (Vidyālaṅkāra), Calcutta, early 19th c. Secondary lit (Encyclopedia of Tantra): 'Ramatosana, author of the Pranatosini, states that he wrote it under the patronage of Pranakrsna Biswas, a landlord of Khardah near Calcutta' — confirming named compiler and patron.
texts.note: Tantric digest (nibandha) securely compiled by Rāmatoṣaṇa; it quotes many tantras but the compiler is a single named person (self-attested), so not an author-conflation.

texts.title: Rāmatoṣaṇa (compiler): Prāṇatoṣiṇī, part 6
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib): Prāṇatoṣiṇī is a comprehensive tāntric digest compiled by Rāmatoṣaṇa Śarman c.1820-1821; it anthologizes quotations from many distinct tantras under one compiler. Secondary (Encyclopedia of Tantra, Santideva) cites it as a standard Tantric source/digest. Compiler role already in current_author.
texts.note: Compilation/digest of many tantras; named person is the compiler/redactor, not author of quoted source tantras. As a nibandha its individual quotations are by various (mostly anonymous) tantric authors, but the work itself is securely Rāmatoṣaṇa's compilation; not treated as multi-author conflation since it is explicitly a digest.

texts.title: Prāṇatoṣiṇī (Dharmakāṇḍa)
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib): Prāṇatoṣiṇī compiled by Rāmatoṣaṇa Śarman c.1821; Dharmakāṇḍa is one section (kāṇḍa) of the digest. Compiler role secure and already in current_author.
texts.note: One kāṇḍa of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī digest; quoted tantra material is by various original authors but the work is securely Rāmatoṣaṇa's compilation.

texts.title: Prāṇatoṣiṇī (Arthakāṇḍa)
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib): Prāṇatoṣiṇī compiled by Rāmatoṣaṇa Śarman c.1821; Arthakāṇḍa is one section of the digest. Compiler role secure and already in current_author.
texts.note: One kāṇḍa of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī digest; compiled tantra quotations by various original authors, work securely Rāmatoṣaṇa's compilation.

texts.title: Rāmatoṣaṇa (compiler): Prāṇatoṣiṇī, Bhaktikāṇḍa
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib): Prāṇatoṣiṇī compiled by Rāmatoṣaṇa Śarman c.1821; Bhaktikāṇḍa is one section of the digest. Compiler role secure; title and current_author already mark compiler.
texts.note: One kāṇḍa of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī digest; compiled quotations by various original authors, work securely Rāmatoṣaṇa's compilation.

texts.title: Prāṇatoṣiṇī (Sargakāṇḍa)
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib): Prāṇatoṣiṇī compiled by Rāmatoṣaṇa Śarman c.1821; Sargakāṇḍa is one section of the digest. Compiler role secure and already in current_author.
texts.note: One kāṇḍa of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī digest; compiled quotations by various original authors, work securely Rāmatoṣaṇa's compilation.

texts.title: Śrītattvanidhi
texts.current_author: Kṛṣṇarāja Wodeyar III (Mummaḍi Kṛṣṇarāja Woḍeyar) of Mysore
texts.primary_author: Kṛṣṇarāja Wodeyar III of Mysore (compiler/patron, traditional)
texts.basis: Web (wisdomlib; Wikipedia): Śrītattvanidhi is a 19th-c. iconographic/encyclopedic compendium in nine nidhis ascribed to Maharaja Krishnaraja Wodeyar III (1794-1868) of Mysore; wisdomlib notes it draws on source texts (e.g. Haṭhābhyāsapaddhati) so the king functioned as compiler/synthesizer, not sole original author. Secondary (Ngai 2010) confirms KW III as a royal patron-author ('Abhinava Bhoja') with ~50 works ascribed to him, many produced at/by his court. Secondary (White, Yoga in Practice) treats it as a Mysore royal-tradition text.
texts.source: web:https://www.wisdomlib.org/definition/shritattvanidhi | secondary:cleaned_From entertainment to enlightenment..._May-Ying Mary Ngai, 2010.txt
texts.note: Royal/nominal authorship: like King Bhoja's corpus, court-produced compendium compiled under (and ascribed to) the Maharaja; draws on earlier source texts. Status downgraded from secure to traditional and role refined to compiler; no distinct named co-author found so not flagged as conflation.

texts.title: Bhāvanāsārasaṃgraha
texts.basis: GRETIL header states 'Compiled by Śrī Śrī Siddha Kṛṣṇadāsa Tātapāda'; a Gauḍīya līlā-saṃgraha (Niśānta-/Prātar-līlā) drawing extensively on Govinda-līlāmṛta and Kṛṣṇa-bhāvanāmṛta, with verse-level attributions to those sources. No conflicting attribution in local literature.
texts.note: Devotional anthology/compilation; Kṛṣṇadāsa functions as compiler drawing on earlier līlā works. Attribution traditional within Gauḍīya tradition; not critically verified outside the edition.

texts.title: Devanandanasiṃha: Śāktapramoda

authors.name: Rāja Deva Nandan Singh (Devanandana Siṃha / Rājanandana Siṃha)

texts.basis: Sanderson (The Śaiva Age): 'Śāktapramoda, compiled by Rāja Devanandan Singh'. Encyclopedia of Tantra (Santideva): 'Saktapramoda by King Rajanandanasimha' (royal author). Web (Madhu Khanna 2013 ed., Exotic India/DK Printworld): it is a paddhati-style compendium of sixteen independent ritual manuals (ten Mahāvidyā Tantras + Kumārī, Gaṇeśa, Viṣṇu, Śiva, Durgā, Sūrya tantras), first published by Rāja Deva Nandan Singh, zamindar of Muzaffarpur, 19th c.
texts.note: Not an original single-author work: it is a compilation/digest of sixteen distinct (largely anonymous, traditionally Śākta/Tantric) ritual manuals. Deva Nandan Singh is the compiler/redactor, not the author of the constituent Tantras. Name also given as Rājanandana/Rajanandan Siṃha.

texts.title: Harināmacintāmaṇi
texts.basis: GRETIL header: author Kedaranatha Datta / Bhaktivinoda (1838-1914), title śrī-harināma-cintāmaṇi; no separate editor/scribe colophon. Local literature confirms Bhaktivinoda Ṭhākura as a prolific Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava author of this period (multiple commentarial works attributed to him).
texts.note: Single, securely attributed modern (late 19th c.) Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava author. No conflation; no dispute.

texts.title: Śvānastavakāvya
texts.basis: Dimitrov/Roesler/Steiner eds., Śikhisamuccayaḥ (2002), Bhikkhu Pāsādika's edition 'Poem in Praise of a Dog. Contemporary Sri Lankan Sanskrit Poetry: The Śvānastavakāvyam by Davuldeṇa Jñāneśvara Mahāsthavira' explicitly names him as the author, 'regarded as the foremost contemporary Sri Lankan Sanskrit poet'. Also indexed in Dan Martin's bibliography under his name.
texts.source: secondary:cleaned_Indian and Tibetan Studies (Collectanea Marpurgensia Indologica et Tibetica)_Dragomir Dimitrov, et.al. ed., 2002.txt
texts.note: Contemporary (20th c.) named Sri Lankan Sanskrit poet; secure single authorship, edited/translated by Bhikkhu Pāsādika (editor, not author).
